values: Fintechs Seek Growth Capital; CBN Says It Will Broker, Not Bankroll
CBN Draws the Line on Direct Funding, Pushes Blended Finance Instead

As fintech funding tightens in Nigeria, the CBN has ruled out running a growth fund but will help connect startups to development finance and private capital through guarantees and risk-sharing models. For founders, the path forward lies in structured finance, not direct central bank cheques.

Nigeria’s fintech founders are calling for a dedicated growth fund or credit guarantee scheme to unlock capital for innovation and scale. But the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) says it cannot run venture-style financing and will instead act as a matchmaker between startups, development financiers, and private capital.

The position was outlined in the CBN’s latest fintech sector report, informed by a quantitative survey of fintech executives, a closed-door workshop (June 2025), and the CBN fintech roundtable (October 2025).

 

Why Fintech Funding Is Tight

Access to finance emerged as one of the sector’s biggest constraints:

  • Macroeconomic volatility, regulatory delays affecting FDI approvals, and currency risk are making capital harder to secure.

  • Deal flow is down: Startup funding to Nigeria fell 17% to $343m in 2025.

  • Industry consensus: 87.5% of fintech executives support a fintech-specific growth fund or credit guarantee as equity cheques shrink.

 

Why the CBN Won’t Lend Directly

The CBN drew a clear line against direct funding, citing lessons from past interventions, most notably the Anchor Borrowers’ Programme (ABP). Launched in 2015, ABP disbursed ₦1.1trn by 2023 but faced weak monitoring and repayment issues; auditors report ₦629.04bn remains unrecovered. The regulator has since refocused on core monetary policy.

 

What the CBN Will Do

Instead of lending, the CBN says it will convene partners to structure:

  • Blended finance

  • Credit guarantees

  • Risk-sharing models

Potential partners include the Development Bank of Nigeria (DBN) and InfraCredit, aligned with its Payments System Vision 2025 (PSV2025).

 

Government-Backed Capital Is Growing

Even without direct CBN funding, public-backed capital is becoming more visible:

  • iDICE (launched 2023) with $617.7m to support digital and creative enterprises, backed by the Federal Government (via BOI), African Development Bank, Agence Française de Développement, and Islamic Development Bank.

  • In November 2025, iDICE backed Ventures Platform’s $64m fund, signalling stronger institutional appetite for early-stage innovation.

  • Nigeria’s Startup Act also provides for a government-backed seed fund of up to ₦10bn.

 

Liquidity, Markets, and Confidence

Stakeholders also proposed a secondary market for fintech debt, which the CBN says aligns with efforts to deepen domestic capital markets and reduce concentration risk.

External confidence is improving too. In October 2025, Nigeria exited the Financial Action Task Force (FATF) grey list, after being added in February 2023, easing cross-border transactions and supporting capital inflows, according to Finance Minister Wale Edun.

Action step: Capital is tightening, but the rails are being laid. Founders who adapt to structured finance and leverage improving regulatory credibility will be best placed to scale. Position for DFI-backed instruments, explore debt plus guarantees, and align pitches with national fintech priorities.

1. Expect facilitation, not cheques: The CBN will broker capital via guarantees and blended finance, not run venture funds.
2. More structured capital: Credit guarantees and risk-sharing can unlock longer-term debt when equity is scarce.
3. Improving signal to investors: FATF exit and AML progress strengthen Nigeria’s risk profile.
Trump Renews AGOA, Restoring Duty-Free Access for African Exports to the US
African exporters regain temporary trade relief

The United States has renewed the African Growth and Opportunity Act (AGOA) for one year, restoring duty-free access for thousands of African exports to the US market through December 31, 2026. The move stabilises trade flows after months of uncertainty and is especially significant for exporters in countries like Nigeria and South Africa.

The African Growth and Opportunity Act (AGOA), first enacted in 2000, allows eligible sub-Saharan African countries to export over 1,800 products to the United States duty-free. After expiring on September 30, 2025, the programme has now been retroactively reinstated through the end of 2026.

The extension was signed into law by Donald Trump, according to the US Trade Representative. Jamieson Greer confirmed that the renewal restores benefits from the lapse date and that discussions are underway with Congress to modernise AGOA in line with the administration’s America First trade agenda.


Key Facts at a Glance

  • Coverage: 1,800+ products exported duty-free to the US

  • Eligibility conditions: Progress toward market-based economies, rule of law, reduced barriers to US trade and investment, poverty reduction, anti-corruption measures, and human rights protections

  • 2024 trade value: $8.23 billion exported under AGOA

    • South Africa: ~50%

    • Nigeria: ~20%

The renewal comes amid strained US–South Africa relations, though South Africa’s Trade Minister has welcomed the extension. While the US House approved a longer extension, the final agreement limits AGOA to one year, underscoring ongoing policy uncertainty.

African exporters should leverage the restored access immediately, lock in US orders, optimise compliance, and diversify markets while preparing for potential rule changes as AGOA is reviewed.

Market certainty: Exporters regain duty-free access to the US, reducing costs and improving competitiveness.
Jobs and investment: The lapse last year disrupted trade and threatened thousands of jobs; the renewal helps stabilise supply chains.
Short runway: With only a one-year extension, businesses should plan proactively while monitoring potential reforms.
CBN to Fast-Track Open Banking and Enable Cross-Border Expansion for  Nigerian Fintechs
CBN Moves to Unlock Data, Cut Red Tape, and Support Pan-African Fintech Expansion

Nigeria’s Central Bank is accelerating open banking implementation and exploring regulatory passporting deals with other African countries to remove technical barriers holding back fintech growth. Backed by its Fintech Report 2025, the reforms promise clearer standards, faster market entry across Africa, and stronger digital infrastructure, positioning Nigerian fintechs to scale regionally with lower compliance costs and greater investor confidence.

Nigeria’s fintech ecosystem is set for a major regulatory boost. The Central Bank of  Nigeria (CBN) has announced plans to fast-track open banking implementation and  pursue regulatory passporting agreements with other African countries, moves aimed  at removing long-standing technical bottlenecks and supporting regional growth by local  fintech firms. 

The plans were outlined in the CBN’s newly released Fintech Report 2025, which  confirms that an open banking implementation roadmap will be issued within three  months. 

What’s Changing and Why It Matters 

Open banking allows banks and licensed third parties to securely share customer data  via APIs, enabling better digital products, stronger competition, and broader access to  financial services. Although Nigeria introduced its open banking framework in 2021 and  issued operational guidelines in 2023, adoption has lagged due to unresolved technical  and governance gaps.

 

The CBN says it is now prioritising: 

∙ Clear technical standards 

∙ Robust governance and dispute-resolution frameworks 

∙ Consumer education to build trust and adoption

This push reflects strong market demand. An ecosystem survey cited in the report  shows that 25% of fintech executives see open banking APIs as the single most  critical infrastructure for future growth. 

Regulatory Passporting: Easing African Expansion 

Alongside open banking, the CBN is developing a Regulatory Passporting  Programme to support Nigerian fintechs expanding across Africa. With 62.5% of  Nigerian fintechs planning regional expansion, the regulator is exploring mutual  licence recognition to reduce duplicated compliance and speed up market entry. 

Initial bilateral talks are planned with regulators in Ghana, Kenya, and Senegal. 

What to Expect: The Reform Timeline 

Immediate actions (next 3 months): 

∙ Launch of a CBN-led fintech engagement forum 

∙ Release of the open banking implementation roadmap 

∙ Technical scoping for a single regulatory window and smart licensing  gateway 

Near-term actions (within 9 months): 

∙ A new regulatory sandbox focused on AI and regtech 

∙ Guidance on data portability and consumer protection under open finance 

∙ Operationalisation of a fintech credit guarantee window with development  finance partners 

The CBN also plans to shift its inclusion strategy, moving away from expanding payment  service bank mandates toward authorising dedicated digital banking licences as a  more scalable way to serve underserved populations. 

AI, Security, and Global Alignment 

The report highlights the growing role of AI in fintech security, noting that 87.5% of  Nigerian fintechs already use AI primarily for fraud detection, underscoring ongoing  concerns around digital financial crime.

Globally, open banking is increasingly a regulatory tool for competition and consumer  protection. The European Union’s Revised Payment Services Directive (PSD2) and  Brazil’s central bank-led open finance regime are cited as reference points. The CBN  

says Nigeria’s phased approach will align with global best practice while reflecting local  realities. 

The regulator also plans deeper engagement with ECOWAS and the African Union to  help shape continent-wide fintech standards

For founders and investors, this signals a shift toward clearer rules, lower expansion  friction, and stronger digital infrastructure. Faster open banking rollout means more  room to build data-driven products, while regulatory passporting could significantly cut  the cost and time of scaling across Africa. 
In short: Nigeria is positioning its fintech sector not just for local growth, but for regional  leadership.
Nigeria’s Rising Political Noise, Security Gaps, and Governance Strain: What It  Means for Entrepreneurs Ahead of 2027
Election Politics, Security Risks, and Weak Institutions Are Raising the Cost of Doing Business

As Nigeria approaches the 2027 elections, rising political propaganda, persistent insecurity, and weak governance are increasing uncertainty for businesses. For entrepreneurs, the environment calls for sharper risk management, credible information sources, and stronger internal governance to navigate volatility and protect long-term growth.

As Nigeria edges toward the 2027 general elections, political activity is intensifying but not necessarily in ways that strengthen governance or economic confidence. A growing culture of political propaganda, unresolved security challenges, and weak institutional accountability is shaping an environment that African entrepreneurs cannot ignore. 

The Rise of Political “Vuvuzelas” 

As election season approaches, paid political commentators, popularly described as  “human vuvuzelas,” are flooding traditional and social media with aggressive messaging.  Often operating outside formal party structures, these actors amplify misinformation,  conduct character attacks, and increasingly deploy artificial intelligence tools to manipulate narratives. 

Why it matters for business: 

This noise distorts public discourse, heightens uncertainty, and distracts from policy debates that investors and founders rely on, such as jobs, infrastructure, taxes, and regulation. As campaigns heat up, separating signal from noise will become harder,  increasing reputational and market risks. 

Terrorism, Trust Deficit, and Policy Inconsistency

Despite repeated promises to “name and shame” terrorism financiers and sympathisers,  the Federal Government has yet to demonstrate decisive action. Public frustration persists over perceived tolerance for voices seen as sympathetic to insurgents, while reports of negotiated releases and amnesties fuel a trust deficit. 

State-level responses, such as negotiated settlements and prisoner exchanges, have also drawn criticism for potentially incentivising criminality. 

Why it matters for business: 

Persistent insecurity raises operating costs, disrupts supply chains, and dampens investor confidence. Inconsistent policy signals undermine trust in institutions, an essential ingredient for long-term capital and regional expansion. 

Governance Fatigue and Weak Oversight 

Many Nigerians believe governance outcomes have steadily declined since 1999. While successive administrations promise reform, critics point to “motion without movement” activity without impact. Under the current administration of Bola Ahmed Tinubu,  concerns persist that misgovernance remains entrenched. 

The National Assembly is widely viewed as underperforming its oversight role,  contrasting sharply with earlier periods of more robust legislative leadership. At the state level, governance quality varies widely, often weakened by money-driven political recruitment. 

Past assurances by Muhammadu Buhari and his aides that history would judge his tenure kindly have been challenged by both critics and allies, reinforcing public skepticism about political accountability. 

Why it matters for business: 

Weak oversight and poor governance reduce policy predictability. Entrepreneurs face higher regulatory risk, slower reforms, and limited protection against arbitrary decisions,  key barriers to scaling and attracting investment. 

As politics grows louder, founders and investors must double down on risk management, credible information sources, and scenario planning. In uncertain environments, resilience, regional diversification, and strong governance within businesses become competitive advantages, not luxuries.

For African entrepreneurs, Nigeria’s current climate underscores three realities: 
1. Political noise will rise ahead of 2027, expect more misinformation and  volatility. 
2. Security and governance gaps remain material risks to operations and  investment.
3. Institutional strength matters, markets reward transparency, accountability,  and policy clarity.
African FX Outlook: Naira and Kwacha Gain Momentum, Ghana’s Cedi Faces Pressure
Diverging Currency Trends Are Reshaping Costs, Risk, and Expansion Decisions

African currencies are moving in different directions, with the Nigerian naira and Zambian kwacha strengthening, Kenya and Uganda remaining stable, and Ghana’s cedi under pressure. For entrepreneurs, these shifts affect pricing, funding strategy, and market expansion, making FX risk management and country-specific planning increasingly important.

Currencies across Africa are moving in different directions as macro conditions, export earnings, and policy signals diverge. According to traders quoted by Reuters, the Nigerian naira and Zambian kwacha are likely to strengthen further against the US dollar, while Kenya and Uganda remain broadly stable and Ghana’s cedi comes under renewed pressure.

 

Country-by-Country Snapshot

Nigeria: Naira on a firmer footing

Improving FX supply boosted by strong oil receipts and renewed foreign investor inflows chasing high government debt yields is supporting the naira.

  • Spot: ₦1,364/$ (stronger than ₦1,388 a week earlier)

  • Parallel market: ~₦1,445/$
    Traders point to clearer FX rules and elevated oil prices. Strong demand at upcoming bond auctions could tighten liquidity further and push the currency toward ₦1,350/$.

Zambia: Kwacha extends gains

The kwacha is benefiting from improving macro conditions and resilient copper prices.

  • Spot: 19.09/$ (from 20.29 a week earlier)
    Recent central bank measures limiting foreign-currency usage are adding support.

Kenya: Shilling steady

Dollar demand from manufacturers and oil marketers is largely offset by tourism and horticulture inflows.

  • Banks: 128.90/129.10 per dollar (unchanged week-on-week)

Uganda: Shilling range-bound

Trading is subdued ahead of a central bank rate decision.

  • Banks: 3,553/3,563 per dollar (vs. 3,561/3,571 last week)

Ghana: Cedi under pressure

Persistent hard-currency demand, especially from the energy sector continues to outweigh supply.

  • Spot: 10.95/$ (slightly weaker than 10.90 a week earlier)
    Unfilled demand at recent FX auctions and a firm dollar suggest further near-term weakness.

Short-term FX conditions favor Nigeria and Zambia, while Ghana faces headwinds. Founders should adjust pricing, hedge where possible, and align funding plans with country-specific currency trends.

Pricing & costs: FX moves directly affect import costs, margins, and consumer pricing.
Funding strategy: Stronger currencies can lower FX risk on dollar-denominated debt; weaker ones raise hedging needs.
Expansion timing: Stable or strengthening currencies (Nigeria, Zambia) may improve planning certainty; pressure points (Ghana) call for tighter cash management.
Policy watch: Bond auctions, rate decisions, and FX rules can quickly shift market dynamics.
Why the Rio Tinto–Glencore Mega-Merger Fell Apart and What It Signals for African Businesses
Why Scale Alone Isn’t Enough in High-Value Deals

The proposed $200bn Rio Tinto–Glencore merger collapsed over valuation and control disputes, with Glencore opting to pursue its standalone growth strategy. The key lesson for African entrepreneurs: growth should be driven by strategic fit, fair value, and strong governance, not size for its own sake.

Plans to create the world’s largest mining company, valued at over $200 billion (≈ R3.2 trillion) have been shelved after Rio Tinto decided not to proceed with a merger with Glencore. The decision followed weeks of talks and ultimately hinged on valuation and control concerns raised by Glencore’s leadership.

 

What Was on the Table

In January, both companies confirmed discussions around a potential all-share combination involving some or all of their businesses. The deal would have created the world’s largest miner and marked another major copper-focused consolidation, following the recent Anglo American–Teck Resources merger.

Under UK takeover rules, Rio Tinto had until February 5 to make a formal offer or step back for six months. On the deadline, Rio announced it would not proceed.

 

Why Glencore Walked Away

Glencore, led by South African CEO Gary Nagle, said the proposed terms undervalued its business, particularly its copper assets and growth pipeline. Key sticking points included:

  • Leadership structure: Rio Tinto retaining both Chair and CEO roles.

  • Ownership split: A pro forma ownership structure that Glencore said did not reflect its relative value.

  • No control premium: The proposal failed to include a sufficient acquisition premium.

Glencore stated that the terms did not adequately account for synergy potential or its long-term, through-the-cycle value.

 

Glencore’s Standalone Strategy

Rather than pursue the merger, Glencore reiterated confidence in its independent growth path:

  • A diversified commodity portfolio backed by a leading global marketing business.

  • A strong position in energy transition commodities, especially copper.

  • Simplified operations improving accountability and execution.

  • On track to meet full-year production guidance for a second consecutive year.

  • A large copper asset base that could elevate Glencore into the top tier of global copper producers over the next decade.

Nagle, who took over in 2021 after fellow South African Ivan Glasenberg, had previously described a Rio tie-up as one of the most obvious deals in mining but coal exposure and valuation differences proved decisive.


The collapse of this mega-deal highlights a core business lesson for African founders and investors growth must be strategic, fairly valued, and aligned with long-term objectives, not driven by scale alone.

Valuation discipline matters: Bigger is not always better. Walking away can protect long-term value.
Control and governance are deal-breakers: Leadership structure and decision rights are as critical as price.
Commodity strategy is evolving: Copper and energy-transition minerals remain central to future growth.
Strong standalone cases win leverage: Companies with clear strategy and execution don’t need to merge on unfavourable terms.
Top 10 States by FAAC Allocation in 2025: What Nigeria’s Revenue Map Tells Entrepreneurs
Oil Still Dominates, but Commerce and Digital Activity Are Closing the Gap

Nigeria’s 2025 FAAC allocations were led by oil-producing states and major commercial hubs, with Delta, Rivers, and Lagos on top. For entrepreneurs, the data shows where public spending power and consumer demand are strongest and why states driven by VAT, population, and digital transactions are becoming increasingly attractive markets.

Nigeria’s Federation Account Allocation Committee (FAAC) disbursements in 2025 once again favoured oil-producing states and major commercial hubs, with Delta State, Rivers State, and Lagos State leading by total receipts.
The data, covering statutory allocations, 13% derivation, VAT, and the Electronic Money Transfer Levy (EMTL), highlights how oil revenue still dominates, while consumption, digital transactions, and population size are increasingly shaping outcomes.

 

How FAAC Was Distributed in 2025

Five components determined each state’s take:

  • Net statutory allocation

  • 13% derivation (for oil producers)

  • VAT

  • EMTL

  • Other federally shared inflows

Oil producers benefited disproportionately from derivation, while urbanised, high-commerce states captured larger VAT and EMTL shares. Together, these streams widened fiscal gaps, with the Top 10 accounting for a significant share of total FAAC inflows.

 

Top 10 States by Net FAAC Allocation (2025)

  1. Delta State — ₦649.67bn
    Driven by massive derivation (₦458.65bn). Oil output continues to confer a structural advantage.

  2. Rivers State — ₦526.30bn
     Strong mix of oil derivation, ports, and industrial-scale VAT inflows.

  3. Lagos State — ₦514.56bn
     Zero derivation, yet third nationally powered by Nigeria’s largest VAT share (₦459.87bn) and EMTL (₦33.74bn).

  4. Akwa Ibom State — ₦494.39bn
     High oil production and derivation (₦303.86bn).

  5. Bayelsa State — ₦488.08bn
     Derivation-heavy (₦320.45bn), reflecting oil dependence relative to population size.

  6. Kano State — ₦270.86bn
     Top non-oil state, lifted by population and trade-driven VAT (₦146.81bn).

  7. Oyo State — ₦213.75bn
     Non-oil growth story fueled by urbanisation and strong consumption (VAT ₦146.06bn).

  8. Anambra State — ₦199.88bn
     Strong commercial activity, rising VAT, plus ₦20.74bn in derivation.

  9. Borno State — ₦198.75bn
     Gains driven by higher statutory inflows and VAT (₦92.18bn).

  10. Ondo State — ₦198.42bn
     Boosted by stronger statutory revenue, VAT (₦87.17bn), and EMTL.

 

Key Insights Beneath the Rankings

  • Oil still rules: Derivation revenue decisively reshapes fiscal rankings for producing states.

  • VAT is rising fast: Lagos shows how commerce and digital payments can rival oil in FAAC outcomes.

  • Urbanisation pays: Oyo, Anambra, and Kano demonstrate that population density and trade lift allocations even without oil.

  • Digital economy matters: EMTL growth tracks rising electronic transactions nationwide.

The 2025 FAAC map confirms a dual reality, oil still dominates, but commerce, population, and digital activity are closing the gap. For founders, the best opportunities sit where consumption is growing, transactions are digitising, and fiscal capacity supports sustained demand.

Public spending capacity: FAAC-rich states have greater fiscal room for infrastructure, contracts, and social programmes that affect local markets.
Market selection: High-VAT states signal stronger consumption and digital transaction volumes, key for scaling.
Risk management: Oil-dependent states remain exposed to crude price volatility; diversified economies offer steadier demand.
Policy direction: States that grow taxable activity and internal revenue can reduce reliance on federal transfers.
Nigeria’s Treasury Bills Attract ₦4.59trn in Bids: What 300% Oversubscription Signals for Entrepreneurs
Strong Liquidity Is Pushing Investors Toward Safe, Long-Term Government Returns

Nigeria’s Treasury Bills auction attracted ₦4.59 trillion in bids, nearly triple the amount offered, driven by heavy demand for one-year bills. For entrepreneurs, the surge signals abundant liquidity, falling long-term yields, and a tougher environment for attracting capital away from risk-free government securities.

Nigeria’s Treasury Bills (NTB) market saw a sharp surge in demand at the latest auction, with ₦4.59 trillion in total subscriptions, nearly three times the ₦1.15 trillion on offer. The primary market auction, conducted on February 4, 2026, underscores strong investor appetite for government securities amid ample system liquidity.

Despite the flood of bids, the Debt Management Office (DMO) exercised restraint, allotting ₦952.60 billion across the 91-day, 182-day, and 364-day tenors.

 

Where the Money Went

Investor demand was overwhelmingly concentrated in the 364-day bill, reflecting a preference to lock in longer-term yields.

364-day NTB

  • Offer: ₦800.0bn

  • Subscriptions: ₦4.40trn

  • Allotment: ₦808.78bn

  • Stop rate: 16.99% (down 137bps from 18.36% in January)

182-day NTB

  • Offer: ₦200.0bn

  • Subscriptions: ₦123.41bn

  • Allotment: ₦80.61bn

  • Stop rate: 16.65% (unchanged)

91-day NTB

  • Offer: ₦150.0bn

  • Subscriptions: ₦66.05bn

  • Allotment: ₦63.21bn

  • Stop rate: 15.84% (unchanged)

Shorter tenors struggled to meet offer sizes, while the one-year paper was heavily oversubscribed, giving the DMO room to cut borrowing costs on longer maturities.

 

What the Data Is Saying

  • Persistent liquidity is chasing risk-free assets.

  • Investors are rotating into longer-dated bills, signalling confidence in government securities and a desire to lock in returns.

  • The DMO’s pricing power increased, enabling a meaningful drop in the 364-day yield while keeping shorter-term rates stable.

A near 300% oversubscription shows cash is abundant and seeking safety. For entrepreneurs, this environment rewards prudent cash management and highlights the importance of offering compelling risk-adjusted returns to compete with government securities.

Cost of capital signals: Falling long-term government yields can eventually ease benchmark borrowing costs across the economy.
Investor behaviour: Strong preference for NTBs suggests risk appetite remains conservative equities and private ventures must work harder to attract capital.
Treasury management: Founders with surplus cash may find one-year NTBs attractive for predictable returns.
Macro read-through: The shift from January’s rate-hike pressure to an issuer-friendly outcome points to improving market depth and confidence.
Delta’s Lagos Route Struggles as Low Passenger Demand Hits Expansion Plans
Weak load factors on the New York–Lagos route may slow airline expansion, pressure fares, and complicate Nigeria’s push for direct US connectivity.

A new aviation report shows Delta’s New York–Lagos route recording one of the lowest load factors in its global network, raising concerns about route sustainability, fare pressure, and Nigeria’s ambition for more direct US flights.

A report by Simple Flying has identified the New York (JFK) to Lagos route as one of the weakest-performing services in Delta Air Lines’ global network, with a load factor of just 56.7%, ranking second lowest across all Delta routes.

Reacting to the development, the Director of Research at Zenith Travels, Olumide Ohunayo, said the low passenger numbers could discourage Nigerian airlines planning to launch US routes under reciprocity agreements.

He explained that reduced demand would also limit the ability of American carriers to expand services on the route, noting that the situation could indirectly affect Nigeria’s efforts to achieve Category 1 status, which would allow Nigerian airlines to operate direct flights to the United States.

According to Ohunayo, the airlines most exposed to the downturn are not US carriers, but foreign airlines that rely on their hubs to connect passengers between Nigeria and the US.

“Those that would feel the impact more are not US airlines but airlines that use their hub to ferry passengers between Nigeria and the US. It is those airlines that would lose more passengers,” he said.

He added that declining passenger volumes would likely lead to lower fares on the route, making direct flights more attractive than journeys routed through third countries.

“With reduced passenger numbers, fares are expected to drop, and direct flights would be favoured over connecting services due to weaker demand,” Ohunayo noted.

While American airlines are unlikely to exit the route entirely, he said they may be forced to manage capacity more cautiously.

“US carriers would likely remain on the route but may be unable to expand or increase frequencies. Instead, they may maintain current operations or deploy smaller aircraft to reduce exposure,” he concluded.

Lower passenger demand on the New York–Lagos route could put downward pressure on airfares, making direct US travel more affordable for business owners, investors, and exporters. However, reduced capacity and slower route expansion may affect trade logistics, diaspora business travel, and cross-border dealmaking, signalling the need for entrepreneurs to plan travel and market access more strategically.
Malawi’s Finance Bank Wins in Court but Compensation Remains Uncertain
Supreme Court Ruling Favors Finance Bank, but Valuation and Payment Could Take Months

Malawi’s top court has ordered the central bank to compensate Finance Bank of Malawi for losses dating back to 2005, but the Reserve Bank of Malawi (RBM) says payment will only follow a formal compensation assessment, meaning the legal win may take time to translate into cash.

The Supreme Court of Appeal has ruled that RBM must compensate Finance Bank of Malawi (FBM) for lost business and profits after its licence as an authorised dealer bank was revoked in 2005. However, RBM has made clear it will wait for an official assessment of the compensation before taking any action.

According to RBM spokesperson Boston Banda, the central bank is awaiting the registrar’s valuation. This means FBM’s courtroom victory is only the first step; converting it into actual payment remains uncertain and potentially protracted.

FBM’s legal team has engaged financial experts to calculate losses spanning nearly two decades. Those figures will be submitted to the court, a process that could take months or longer, given the complexity and scope of the claims.

The dispute dates back to May 2005, when RBM suspended FBM’s foreign currency operations over alleged regulatory breaches and later recommended licence revocation to the Ministry of Finance. FBM challenged both actions, arguing they were unlawful and caused significant financial damage.

While the High Court initially sided with RBM and dismissed FBM’s counterclaim, the Supreme Court of Appeal has now overturned that decision, ordering compensation but leaving the amount unresolved and open to further scrutiny or challenge.

 FBM’s win is a landmark legal moment, but the road to compensation is still long. For African founders, the case is a reminder to factor regulatory risk, dispute timelines, and enforcement realities into long-term business strategy.

Regulatory risk can be long-lasting: Even when businesses win in court, enforcement and compensation can take years.
Legal victories don’t equal liquidity: Cash flow outcomes often depend on lengthy valuation and compliance processes.
Policy certainty matters: Prolonged disputes with regulators can stall growth, investment, and market confidence.
Kenya Unveils ₦220bn Infrastructure Push to Transform Nairobi
Housing, Water, Roads, and Waste Projects Aim to Fix City Bottlenecks and Unlock Growth

Kenya’s government plans to invest Sh220 billion in Nairobi to upgrade housing, water supply, roads, waste management, and public services, an ambitious push President William Ruto says is critical to national transformation. For entrepreneurs, the programme signals new opportunities in construction, services, logistics, and urban infrastructure.

Speaking in Nairobi, President Ruto said the capital must lead Kenya’s development, outlining a broad plan to tackle water shortages, poor roads, unreliable power, waste management, slums, and insecurity. He emphasized that a functional capital is essential to achieving first-world standards.

What’s in the Plan

  • Affordable housing: Sh220bn to build 169,000 housing units, 16 modern markets, and student hostels for 14,000 learners, with a long-term goal to eradicate slums within 15 years.

  • Water supply: Completion of the Northern Water Collector Tunnel now adds 140 million litres/day to Nairobi. Construction of the Maragua 4 Dam (one of 50 planned nationally) is set to begin this year to further boost supply.

  • Roads: Each ward will get at least one priority road, identified by county assembly members, to improve mobility across the city.

  • Waste management: From April 1, 2026, comprehensive garbage collection will roll out citywide. A Dandora treatment plant is under construction to process waste, produce fertiliser, and generate energy from next year.

  • Lighting & safety: Expanded public lighting to reduce crime.

  • Healthcare: Over 2.5 million Nairobi residents (about 57%) are registered with the Social Health Authority, a fivefold increase over the former NHIF.

  • Education: 700 new classrooms in Nairobi’s public schools (23,000 built nationally).

Nairobi Governor Johnson Sakaja welcomed the plan, citing job creation through initiatives like Climate Worx and improved water access. Trade Cabinet Secretary Lee Kinyanjui urged focus on delivery over politics.

Nairobi’s Sh220bn upgrade is a catalyst for urban growth. Founders positioned in infrastructure-linked value chains and ready to partner with government stand to benefit as the city’s basics improve and economic activity accelerates.

Demand surge: Large-scale public spending will lift demand for construction, materials, utilities, waste services, security, and logistics.
Urban markets: New housing and markets expand consumer bases and retail opportunities.
Reliability dividend: Better water, roads, power, and safety lower operating costs and improve service delivery.
Partnerships: Scope for PPPs and supplier contracts at city and national levels.
South Africa Eyes ECB Repo Lines to Support Trade and Financial Stability
Cheaper Euro Liquidity Could Cushion Shocks as Rate Cuts Continue

South Africa’s central bank says it would welcome access to new European Central Bank (ECB) repo lines, a move that could strengthen euro liquidity and underpin trade with Europe. The signal comes as inflation cools, the rand stabilises, and policymakers keep the door open to further interest-rate cuts.

South Africa would be keen to use new European Central Bank repo liquidity lines if they become available, central bank governor Lesetja Kganyago said, noting the country’s deep trade and investment ties with Europe.

ECB President Christine Lagarde recently said the bank plans to make its repo lines cheaper and easier to access to boost the euro’s global role. These facilities allow foreign central banks to borrow euros against euro-denominated collateral, primarily during periods of market stress.

Speaking on the sidelines of the Warwick Economics Summit, Kganyago said such a line would help “underpin” trade flows with Europe and would be “a welcome development.”

Rates, Inflation, and the Rand: South Africa’s benchmark rate stands at 6.75%, which Kganyago said remains “distant from the terminal rate.” While policymakers want to see inflation slow further, current projections point to two 25-basis-point cuts this year and another next year, a path he stressed is a guideline, not a commitment.

A stronger rand has helped tame inflation over the past year. Although the currency has recently softened amid global market jitters, Kganyago said overall volatility has declined, reflecting improved policy credibility.

Dollar Dominance and BRICS: On global finance, Kganyago pushed back against claims that emerging markets are trying to dethrone the dollar. Instead, he said countries are seeking protection from the “weaponisation” of the financial system, citing Russia’s exclusion from SWIFT.

He downplayed prospects of a BRICS currency, arguing it would require a BRICS central bank, something he does not see happening, despite the issue likely featuring at the next BRICS summit.

South Africa’s reserves remain about 60% in dollars, a mix Kganyago said mirrors trade patterns and would only change if those flows shift. He added that faster cross-border payment interoperability is about cutting costs and friction, especially in Africa, where non-convertible currencies force dollar invoicing through complex banking chains.

Access to ECB liquidity lines, alongside easing rates and payments reforms, could strengthen South Africa’s financial buffers, creating a more predictable environment for businesses trading with Europe and operating across Africa.

Trade resilience: ECB repo access could stabilise euro funding, supporting exporters and importers linked to Europe.
Lower volatility: Easing inflation and steadier currencies improve planning and pricing.
Cheaper finance ahead: A gradual rate-cut cycle can reduce borrowing costs over time.
Payments opportunity: Pushes for interoperable fast payments open space for fintech solutions that cut cross-border costs.
Nigeria Resumes Petrol Imports to Address Refinery Shortfalls
Fuel Imports Return as a Safety Valve for Nigeria’s Energy Market

The Nigerian government has reinstated petrol and diesel import licences to prevent fuel shortages while domestic refineries, including Dangote, continue to scale up. Regulators and industry experts frame this move as a temporary stabilisation tool, not a reversal of Nigeria’s long-term refining ambitions under the Petroleum Industry Act. Imports help close supply gaps, manage daily demand, and limit sudden price shocks, even as they introduce foreign exchange pressure and possible pump price increases.

The Nigerian Midstream and Downstream Petroleum Regulatory Authority (NMDPRA) has approved new petrol and diesel import licences for marketers. The move is designed to prevent fuel shortages while domestic refineries, including the Dangote Petroleum Refinery, expand their operations.

Industry stakeholders emphasise that imports are necessary to maintain a stable market. “The Dangote Refinery’s expansion will take years to reach full capacity,” an energy expert noted. “Until then, imports are critical to avoid supply gaps and price spikes.”

Why Imports Are Needed

  • Domestic production constraints: Dangote is currently processing semi-finished feedstock rather than producing fully refined fuel.

  • Constant demand: Nigeria’s daily fuel consumption leaves no room for supply delays.

  • Market stability: Controlled import licences ensure fuel availability and prevent abrupt price increases.

Economic Implications

  • Foreign exchange pressure: Dependence on imported fuel affects currency stability and inflation.

  • Potential cost impact: Additional logistics may increase pump prices by up to N75 per litre.

  • Policy context: Imports address immediate supply needs but do not alter the government’s refining strategy under the Petroleum Industry Act.

Dangote Refinery Update


The Dangote Refinery maintains it continues operations without interruption, processing imported feedstocks into finished products. Management warns that reliance on imported fuel could increase consumer costs if logistical expenses are passed on.


 The resumption of petrol imports highlights Nigeria’s ongoing challenge of balancing short-term supply demands with long-term refining goals. While domestic capacity expands, imports remain essential to maintain fuel availability and market stability.

Fuel is the silent engine behind most small and medium-sized businesses. Stable supply means predictable transport, production, and operating costs. For African SMSEs, especially in logistics, manufacturing, agribusiness, and retail, import-backed fuel availability reduces the risk of sudden disruptions that can halt operations overnight. However, higher import and logistics costs may squeeze margins if pump prices rise. In effect, imports buy time and stability, but SMSEs still need to plan for cost volatility until domestic refining fully comes online. Reliable energy keeps the lights on, but the bill still matters.
Gold Surges Past $5,000 as Buyers Return After Last Week’s Selloff
Gold’s Rebound Signals Renewed Faith in Safe Havens Amid Global Uncertainty

Gold prices have climbed back above $5,000 per ounce after a sharp selloff, as investors cautiously re-enter the market and central bank demand remains strong. Strategic buying at lower prices, combined with sustained purchases by institutions such as China’s central bank, helped drive the rebound. While volatility remains elevated across precious metals, gold’s safe-haven appeal continues to be supported by inflation concerns, geopolitical tensions, and currency risks.

Gold prices climbed above $5,000 per ounce on Monday, rebounding from a sharp decline last week. Investors are cautiously returning, driven by persistent demand from both markets and central banks, while silver also recovered after significant losses.


Gold rose as much as 1.7% in Asian trading, reaching $5,009 per ounce, recovering over half of last week’s losses. Silver surged 5.8% to $82.32, while platinum remained steady and palladium inched higher. The rebound follows a steep selloff that reversed gains from the record highs of late January, when gold briefly topped $5,400 per ounce.

Market analysts attribute the bounce to strategic buying at lower levels and resilient official demand, including continued purchases by China’s central bank for the 15th consecutive month. According to the Securities Times, these purchases help diversify reserves without triggering excessive price swings.

What Investors Should Know

  • Volatility remains high: Gold fell over 10% and silver nearly 30% last week, highlighting speculative pressures.

  • Buying opportunities emerge: Market dips are attracting cautious buyers looking to enter at lower prices.

  • Official demand is steady: Central bank purchases, particularly from China, continue to provide support.

  • US monetary policy matters: Upcoming employment and inflation data, plus debate over Fed independence, could influence short-term pricing.

  • Safe-haven appeal persists: Geopolitical tensions, inflationary pressures, and currency concerns continue to drive interest in precious metals.

Global Context

Precious metals have surged since 2024 due to currency depreciation concerns, geopolitical tensions, and inflationary pressures. Central banks, including the Bank of Ghana and others, continue adding gold to reserves, reinforcing its role as a strategic asset.

Impact for Nigeria


Nigeria holds approximately 21.38 metric tonnes of gold (around 687,402 troy ounces), valued at roughly N3.7 trillion at current prices. Rising global gold prices can affect reserves valuation and national economic strategy.

 Despite last week’s sharp correction, gold’s rebound underscores its enduring appeal as a safe-haven asset. Investors are strategically re-entering positions, while central bank demand continues to underpin a broader bullish trend.

For African SMSEs, rising and volatile gold prices are more than a headline. They influence currency stability, inflation, and input costs across the economy. Strong gold prices can bolster national reserves and investor confidence, supporting macroeconomic stability that small businesses rely on for planning and access to finance. At the same time, volatility can spill into exchange rates and borrowing costs, affecting import-dependent SMSEs. In essence, when gold glitters on the global stage, it often reflects deeper economic uncertainty that small businesses must navigate with sharper pricing, hedging, and cash-flow discipline.
South Africa Commands Over 95% of Africa’s $29 Billion REIT Market
South Africa Sets the Pace as Africa’s REIT Market Slowly Broadens

South Africa dominates Africa’s REIT landscape, controlling over 95% of the continent’s estimated $29 billion market and boasting a mature, diverse, and high-performing ecosystem. With more than 30 listed REITs and strong year-to-date returns that outperform global peers, the country sets the benchmark for property-backed investments in Africa. Meanwhile, Kenya, Morocco, and Nigeria are steadily developing their own REIT frameworks, supported by institutional reforms, niche property focus, and growing investor participation.

South Africa dominates Africa’s Real Estate Investment Trust (REIT) sector, holding more than 95% of the estimated $29 billion market. The country’s mature REIT ecosystem continues to deliver strong returns, while other African nations, including Kenya, Morocco, and Nigeria, are gradually developing their own REIT frameworks.


 According to a recent Fortren & Company report, South Africa remains the continent’s most advanced REIT market, with over 30 listed vehicles on the Johannesburg Stock Exchange. The sector’s diversity—spanning retail, office, industrial, residential, and logistics properties—combined with consistent performance, continues to attract attention.

Notable returns include a 46.2% year-to-date total return by late 2025, outperforming global benchmarks such as the US, UK, and the Global REIT Index. Growthpoint Properties, with a portfolio of over 450 assets, exemplifies the sector’s breadth, while Redefine Properties focuses on logistics and industrial assets alongside retail and office investments.

Emerging REIT Markets in Africa

  • Kenya: Features Income REITs (I-REITs) and Development REITs (D-REITs), targeting niche sectors such as student housing. Acorn Student Accommodation REIT and LAPTRUST Imara I-REIT provide exposure across commercial, residential, and retail properties.

  • Morocco: Benefits from low construction costs and stable economic conditions. Immorente Invest focuses on offices, logistics, and industrial assets in Casablanca, while retail properties maintain strong occupancy.

  • Nigeria: The sector is gaining momentum with institutional support and government initiatives. Key listed vehicles include SFS REIT, UPDC REIT, and UH REIT. The MOFI Real Estate Investment Fund Series 2, listed in November 2025, increased total REIT assets in Nigeria to N483.06 billion, representing 6.3% of the N7.67 trillion mutual fund market.

What You Should Know

  • South Africa sets the benchmark: Its mature REIT ecosystem offers size, diversity, and strong returns

  • Emerging markets present opportunities: Kenya, Morocco, and Nigeria are building REIT frameworks that provide new avenues for growth and investment.

  • Performance varies: While some funds show high yields, concentration and market size can affect risk and returns.

  • Macro factors influence outcomes: Interest rates, inflation, and housing demand remain key drivers for the performance and prospects of REITs across the continent.

    South Africa continues to lead Africa’s REIT market, offering robust returns and a wide range of investment options. Emerging REIT markets are gaining traction, creating opportunities for market participants to explore property investments, partnerships, and strategic growth across the continent.

A thriving REIT market shapes the spaces where small businesses operate. For African SMSEs, mature and emerging REIT ecosystems mean more professionally managed retail, office, industrial, and logistics properties, often with better infrastructure and predictable lease structures. In countries like Nigeria and Kenya, expanding REIT activity can unlock new commercial spaces, partnerships, and financing models that lower entry barriers for growing businesses. Over time, stronger REIT markets can translate into improved urban development, more reliable business locations, and broader access to capital tied to real assets, turning property from a constraint into a catalyst for SMSE growth.
EU Warns Meta Over WhatsApp AI Restrictions, Signals Possible Interim Penalties
EU Tightens the Leash on Big Tech as AI Competition Heats Up

The European Union has stepped up antitrust action against Meta, accusing the company of restricting competition by limiting WhatsApp’s AI features to its in-house Meta AI. Regulators warn that interim measures could be imposed to prevent Meta from entrenching an unfair advantage while investigations continue. The case builds on earlier findings under the Digital Markets Act and signals a more aggressive enforcement phase against dominant digital platforms integrating proprietary AI.

The European Union has intensified its antitrust scrutiny of Meta Platforms, warning the tech giant that it may face temporary measures after restricting access to WhatsApp for third-party AI services. The move underscores Brussels’ willingness to act swiftly to prevent anti-competitive practices in the fast-growing digital AI market.


The European Commission formally accused Meta of violating EU competition rules by allowing only its in-house AI assistant, Meta AI, to operate on WhatsApp, effectively blocking rival AI developers. The Commission sent Meta a “statement of objections”, outlining preliminary findings and giving the company an opportunity to respond before a final decision.

EU regulators warned that interim measures may be applied to prevent “serious and irreparable harm” to the market, reflecting concern that delays in a full investigation could allow Meta to entrench an unfair advantage. With WhatsApp’s hundreds of millions of users across Europe, the Commission says the restriction could harm competition immediately and over the long term.

Regulatory Context


This latest probe builds on ongoing scrutiny of Meta’s data and advertising practices. In April 2025, the European Commission found Meta in breach of the Digital Markets Act (DMA), a law aimed at limiting the market power of dominant digital platforms, or “gatekeepers”. Following that decision, Meta pledged to give EU users more control over how their data is used for advertising across Facebook and Instagram.

EU regulators have long raised concerns that Meta’s data-driven advertising and platform dominance give it an unfair edge while also posing privacy risks for users.

Potential Implications

  • Market access: If interim measures are imposed, Meta may have to allow third-party AI services on WhatsApp while the investigation continues.
    Financial risk: A final ruling could lead to substantial fines and enforce behavioural remedies.

  • Industry precedent: The case may set a benchmark for how dominant platforms can integrate proprietary AI without breaching competition laws.

  • Regulatory trend: Recent DMA fines for Apple (€500 million) and Meta (€200 million) indicate a stricter enforcement phase for digital gatekeepers in Europe.


The EU’s action against Meta highlights growing regulatory scrutiny of AI integration on major digital platforms. Companies operating in Europe or developing AI-powered services should monitor these developments closely, as they may reshape competitive dynamics and compliance expectations across the tech sector.

For African SMSEs building or relying on digital and AI-powered tools, this case sets an important precedent. If regulators force platforms like WhatsApp to open up to third-party AI services, smaller businesses and startups gain fairer access to customers without being locked into one dominant provider. Stronger competition can lower costs, expand choice, and create room for African developers to plug innovative AI solutions into global platforms. In the long run, tighter rules on digital gatekeepers could turn global tech infrastructure from a closed garden into a shared marketplace where smaller African firms can grow and compete.
Angola Targets Minority Stake in De Beers as Diamond Market Faces Headwinds
Southern African Nations Explore Collaboration Amid Global Diamond Challenges

Angola is seeking a 20%-30% interest in De Beers, Anglo American’s diamond unit, joining regional discussions with Botswana, Namibia, and South Africa on shared strategies for diamond ownership. The bid comes as global diamond prices fall and synthetic alternatives gain traction.

Angola initially proposed a full stake in De Beers in October 2025 but adjusted its plan to a minority holding to manage market risk.

Paulo Tanganha, Angola’s National Director of Mineral Resources, said:
"Taking a majority stake in luxury commodities is very risky. A 20%-30% share is sustainable for our economy, and we are satisfied with this approach."

The government plans to acquire the stake through Endiama, its state-owned mining company, and Sodiam, the national diamond marketing firm. While funding details remain undisclosed, Tanganha confirmed alternative financial sources are available.

Regional Coordination


Botswana, which currently owns 15% of De Beers, has indicated ambitions to acquire a majority stake. Angola, Botswana, Namibia, and South Africa are holding private discussions to align on how each country might benefit from ownership. "We’re stronger together. Regional cooperation ensures no country is left behind," Tanganha said.

Market Context


De Beers, with operations in Botswana, Namibia, Angola, South Africa, and Canada, has faced declining rough diamond production and increased competition from synthetic diamonds. Despite these challenges, Angola’s joint venture with De Beers recently discovered a new kimberlite cluster, the first in 30 years, underscoring the country’s untapped geological potential.


Angola’s minority bid in De Beers reflects a strategic effort to secure a foothold in a major global diamond player while balancing market risk. The outcome of regional negotiations could influence diamond ownership structures across Southern Africa and present long-term opportunities for economic growth and investment in the sector.
Indian Oil Turns to West Africa and the Middle East Amid Shift Away from Russian Crude
Nigeria and Angola Benefit as India Rebalances Energy Sourcing During US Trade Talks

India’s largest refiner has purchased six million barrels of crude from West Africa and the Middle East, including Nigerian and Angolan grades, as Indian refiners avoid Russian oil amid broader trade and geopolitical considerations. The move highlights growing opportunities for African oil exporters in a shifting global energy market.

India’s Indian Oil Corporation (IOC) has bought six million barrels of crude oil from West Africa and the Middle East, according to traders cited by Reuters. The purchases come as Indian refiners steer clear of Russian crude in the context of New Delhi’s push for a trade deal with the United States.

Traders said Indian refiners are not taking Russian crude offers for March–April delivery and may continue avoiding such supplies for some time.

Breakdown of the Purchases

West Africa:

- Angola’s Pazflor crude

-Nigeria’s Agbami, Akpo, and Bonny Light grades
These were sourced from TotalEnergies (via its trading arm Totsa) and Shell, traders said.

Middle East:

  • Two million barrels of Upper Zakum crude from the United Arab Emirates, purchased from Mercuria.

The cargoes were bought through tenders issued last week and are scheduled for April delivery. Indian companies typically do not comment on spot tenders due to confidentiality clauses. As India recalibrates its energy sourcing, African oil producers are emerging as key beneficiaries. For entrepreneurs across the energy value chain, the moment underscores the importance of export readiness, reliability, and strategic market positioning in a rapidly changing global landscape.

Rising demand for African crude: India’s pivot boosts the strategic relevance of Nigerian and Angolan oil in Asian markets.
Trade diversification: Reduced reliance on Russian supplies creates room for African exporters to deepen long-term relationships with major refiners.
Logistics and services opportunity: Increased flows support demand for shipping, storage, and energy services linked to oil exports.
Geopolitical spillovers: Shifts in global trade policy can quickly reshape commodity demand, creating winners among agile suppliers.
CBN Positions Nigeria to Shape the Future of Global Digital Finance
Nigeria’s growing fintech ecosystem and regulatory reforms aim to move the country from fintech adoption leader to global rule-setter.

Brief Summary

Nigeria’s Central Bank (CBN) is positioning the country to play a stronger global role in digital finance, building on rapid fintech growth, expanding real-time payments infrastructure, and increased financial inclusion efforts. The CBN’s latest fintech report highlights opportunities for innovation, investment, and financial inclusion, while stressing the need for stronger regulation, infrastructure, and domestic funding to sustain long-term growth.

Nigeria has emerged as one of Africa’s most vibrant fintech ecosystems, driven by regulatory reforms, expanding digital payments infrastructure, and a fast-growing community of innovators. The Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) now aims to move beyond fintech adoption toward shaping global standards for digital finance.

This ambition was outlined in the CBN Fintech Report titled “Shaping the Future of Fintech in Nigeria: Innovation, Inclusion and Integrity.” According to CBN Governor Olayemi Cardoso, Nigeria has the potential to transition from fintech frontrunner to fintech rule-setter if reforms continue and collaboration across the ecosystem deepens.

The report emphasises fintech’s role in accelerating financial inclusion, strengthening economic resilience, and positioning Nigeria within the global digital economy. With mobile phone access far exceeding traditional banking reach, fintech remains a critical tool for extending financial services to underserved populations.

However, challenges persist. Weak identity verification systems, affordability barriers, infrastructure gaps, and heavy reliance on foreign investment continue to limit broader growth. The CBN notes that addressing these issues will be essential for sustaining innovation and ensuring inclusive expansion.

Nigeria’s Fintech Progress in Numbers

  • Nigerian startups raised over $520 million in equity funding in 2024 out of a continental total of $2.2 billion.

  • Earlier momentum was stronger, with $747 million raised in 2019, representing about 37% of Africa’s startup funding.

  • Real-time payments remain a major strength, with nearly 11 billion transactions recorded in 2024, up from five billion in 2022.

  • More than 25% of electronic transactions in Nigeria now occur through real-time payment channels.

These developments reinforce Nigeria’s position as both a fintech innovation hub and a testing ground for digital finance solutions across emerging markets.

Progress has been supported by stronger identity systems such as the Bank Verification Number (BVN), integration with the National Identification Number (NIN), adoption of ISO 20022 payment standards, and improved consumer protection frameworks.

The CBN continues to stress that innovation must be balanced with strong governance, risk management, and consumer trust.

Looking Ahead

The CBN emphasises continued collaboration between regulators, financial institutions, and fintech innovators as essential to sustaining growth. By strengthening infrastructure, improving regulation, and promoting inclusion, Nigeria aims to elevate its role not only within Africa but in shaping the future of global digital finance.

Nigeria's fintech leadership push could unlock opportunities across payments, lending, and digital banking while expanding financial access for SMEs. By developing local funding channels and shaping continental standards, the country may accelerate African financial integration and reduce dependence on foreign capital.
Ethiopia Signs Debt Restructuring Deal with France
First bilateral agreement under the G20 Common Framework unlocks $3.5bn relief and fresh reform financing.

Ethiopia has signed its first bilateral debt restructuring deal with France since defaulting in December 2023. The agreement forms part of the G20 Common Framework process and is expected to deliver $3.5 billion in cash flow relief, alongside €81.5 million in additional French financing to support economic reforms.

Ethiopia has taken a major step in resolving its external debt crisis by signing a bilateral restructuring agreement with France.

The deal marks the first country-specific agreement since Ethiopia reached a broader restructuring understanding with its official creditors last July under the G20’s Common Framework initiative.

What the Deal Includes

  • $3.5 billion in cash flow relief under the official creditors’ committee

  • €81.5 million in new financing from France to support economic reforms and technical assistance

The official creditors’ committee, co-chaired by China and France had previously rejected an initial draft agreement between Ethiopia and its $1 billion Eurobond holders, citing failure to meet the “Comparability of Treatment” principle under the G20 framework.

Meanwhile, some Eurobond holders are considering legal action over delays in reaching a separate agreement.

Ethiopia’s agreement with France is a significant milestone in its post-default recovery strategy.

If broader restructuring talks progress smoothly, the country could stabilise its fiscal outlook and rebuild investor confidence, creating renewed opportunities across trade, manufacturing, and infrastructure-led sectors in East Africa.

Debt relief reduces Ethiopia’s short-term repayment pressure, potentially freeing up government resources for infrastructure, reform initiatives, and broader economic recovery. Additional financing from France also signals growing international confidence in the country’s reform agenda, offering a positive outlook for investors and private-sector players. However, unresolved negotiations with private bondholders remain a key watchpoint, as outcomes could influence currency stability and overall financing conditions for businesses.
Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries: Nigeria Misses Oil Quota for Sixth Straight Month
January output falls to 1.478m bpd, widening gap with OPEC target and raising fiscal concerns.

Nigeria produced 1.478 million barrels per day (bpd) in January 2026, about 80,000 bpd below its OPEC quota of 1.5 million bpd. The shortfall marks the sixth consecutive month of underperformance, driven by infrastructure bottlenecks, crude theft, and operational challenges.

Nigeria has again fallen short of its oil production target, according to OPEC’s latest monthly report.

Crude output declined to 1.478 million bpd in January, down 1.27% from 1.497 million bpd in December 2025, based on secondary source data.

Even figures submitted directly by Nigeria, which showed a slight improvement to 1.459 million bpd, remained below the 1.5 million bpd quota, leaving a gap of roughly 80,000 bpd.

Despite the shortfall, Nigeria maintained its position as Africa’s largest oil producer, ahead of Libya (1.304 million bpd).

 

What’s Behind the Gap?

Persistent constraints continue to limit production capacity:

  • Oil theft and pipeline vandalism

  • Security challenges in producing regions

  • Infrastructure bottlenecks

  • Operational inefficiencies

At the wider group level, total crude production among OPEC+ members averaged 42.448 million bpd in January, down 440,000 bpd month-on-month.

Nigeria’s repeated failure to meet its OPEC quota underscores structural challenges in its oil sector.

For business leaders, the key takeaway is clear: oil output remains a major driver of fiscal stability and currency strength, making diversification, export growth, and risk management increasingly critical for long-term resilience.

Nigeria’s heavy dependence on oil revenue means lower production could strain government budgets and tighten foreign exchange liquidity, increasing pressure on public spending. Reduced oil earnings may also pose risks to naira stability, affecting import costs, pricing strategies, and cross-border trade for businesses. At the same time, the persistent production gap underscores investment opportunities in infrastructure security, oilfield services, pipeline monitoring technology, and operational efficiency solutions within the energy sector.
BUA Group’s Rabiu Urges Africa to Move from Raw Material Export to Industrial Value Addition

Abdul Samad Rabiu, Founder and Executive Chairman of BUA Group, has called for a decisive shift in Africa’s economic strategy—from raw material extraction to large-scale industrial processing and value addition. Speaking at the Africa Finance Corporation (AFC) forum during Mining Indaba 2026, Rabiu emphasised that Africa’s abundant resources are underutilised, with much of the continent’s minerals and agricultural produce exported in raw form, capturing only a fraction of the potential economic value.

Drawing from BUA Group’s experience, Rabiu explained how the company transitioned from importing cement to locally producing it over 16 years ago. At the time, Nigeria imported cement despite being rich in limestone, and BUA faced challenges including high capital requirements and foreign exchange constraints. Today, the company mines 40,000 tonnes of limestone daily and produces approximately one million tonnes of cement monthly, transforming Nigeria from a net importer into a net exporter and saving billions of dollars in foreign exchange annually. Rabiu stressed that such industrial transformation requires strategic vision, long-term commitment, and disciplined execution.

The Role of Patient Capital

Long-term financing from development finance institutions, particularly the AFC, was central to BUA’s industrial growth. The company received over $400 million in financing for its cement and industrial operations, much of which has been repaid, demonstrating that well-structured African industrial projects can be both developmental and commercially viable. Rabiu stressed that patient capital enables entrepreneurs to invest in capital-intensive sectors, scale operations, and create sustainable industrial value chains.


Strategies for Driving Industrial Growth in Africa

Africa must adopt deliberate and coordinated strategies to translate industrialisation from vision into reality. Governments are encouraged to implement policies that support local production, including targeted tax incentives, support for capital-intensive industries, and investments in reliable power and transport infrastructure. The private sector should explore strategic partnerships, joint ventures, and the development of local supply chains, while development finance institutions and commercial financiers need to scale patient capital for high-impact industrial projects. By aligning the three pillars of policy, finance, and enterprise, Africa can systematically transform its natural resource wealth into industrial capacity and sustainable economic growth.

Rabiu emphasised that industrialisation is a deliberate process, not an accident. Africa’s opportunity lies in aligning private enterprise, patient capital, and supportive policy to transform natural resource wealth into industrial growth, shared prosperity, and economic resilience. By moving from extraction to value creation, African businesses can drive innovation, create jobs, and ensure that the continent captures a greater share of the wealth generated by its own resources.

Nigeria's industrialization focus creates opportunities for MSMEs to process materials locally rather than export raw commodities. Development finance and industrial clusters can support entrepreneurs in capturing more value through supply chain integration, while enabling access to broader markets and reducing commodity dependence.
Central Bank of Nigeria Approves $150,000 Weekly FX Sales to BDCs
New directive aims to boost dollar liquidity, narrow parallel market gap, and stabilise Nigeria’s retail FX market.

The Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) has allowed licensed Bureau De Change (BDC) operators to purchase up to $150,000 weekly from the official FX market. The move seeks to improve retail dollar supply, reduce the widening gap between official and parallel rates, and strengthen transparency through stricter compliance rules.

The CBN has approved the participation of licensed BDCs in the Nigerian Foreign Exchange Market (NFEM), permitting each operator to buy up to $150,000 per week through authorised dealer banks.

The directive, dated February 10, 2026, was signed by Dr. Musa Nakorji, Director of the Trade and Exchange Department.

Why It Matters

The decision comes as the gap between official and parallel market rates widened by over ₦90, the largest spread in three years. By increasing retail FX liquidity, the CBN aims to ease dollar scarcity and reduce speculative pressure.

Key Conditions

  • Banks must conduct full KYC and due diligence before selling FX to BDCs.

  • Unused dollars must be resold within 24 hours, no hoarding allowed.

  • All transactions must pass through licensed settlement accounts.

  • Third-party transactions are prohibited.

  • Cash settlements are capped at 25% per transaction.

  • Mandatory electronic reporting to the CBN.

This marks a significant shift from earlier restrictions, including the previous $25,000 weekly limit per bank and the suspension of direct dollar access that forced BDCs to rely on walk-in customers.

The CBN is balancing broader FX access with stricter controls. If effectively implemented, the policy could ease retail dollar pressure and improve confidence in Nigeria’s FX market, a critical factor for trade, manufacturing, and cross-border business growth.

Improved foreign exchange inflows could enhance dollar availability for SMEs that rely on FX for imports, travel, tech subscriptions, and raw materials. A narrower gap between official and parallel market rates may also reduce pricing uncertainty and support better cost planning. However, increased regulatory oversight signals a tighter compliance environment, requiring businesses to maintain accurate documentation and transparent transaction records to avoid disruptions.
Nigeria Attracts $21bn in Capital Inflows as Trade Hits ₦113tn in 2025 - Jumoke Oduwole
Surge in foreign investment and record trade surplus signal renewed investor confidence and stronger export performance.

Nigeria recorded $21 billion in capital importation within the first 10 months of 2025, up sharply from $12 billion in 2024 and under $4 billion in 2023. Total trade reached ₦113 trillion, while exports rose 11% year-on-year to a record $6.1 billion. The government credits targeted reforms, investor engagement, and policy execution for the rebound.

Nigeria is witnessing a significant rebound in foreign capital inflows and trade performance, according to Minister of Industry, Trade and Investment, Jumoke Oduwole.

Speaking during the ministry’s 2026 budget defence before the House of Representatives Joint Committee on Commerce, Industry and SMEs, Oduwole revealed that $21 billion in capital was imported into the country between January and October 2025, nearly double 2024’s total and more than five times 2023’s figure.

Capital importation refers to foreign funds invested into a country for business expansion, manufacturing, trade financing, and other productive activities.

 

What Drove the Surge?

The minister attributed the growth to deliberate efforts to convert policy into measurable economic outcomes. Key interventions included:

  • Curation of over $5 billion in bankable projects

  • Creation of sector-specific “deal rooms”

  • Hosting Nigeria’s first domestic investors’ summit

  • Resolution of approximately 50 investor bottlenecks

  • Over 100 bilateral investment engagements

Strategic partners included the UAE, Brazil, Japan, United States, and United Kingdom. Notably, UK investors accounted for about 65% of total foreign capital inflows in 2025, boosted by the Nigeria–UK Economic and Trade Partnership launched in Q2 2024.

 

Trade Performance: Record Highs

Beyond capital inflows, Nigeria recorded a trade surplus in 2025.

  • Total trade: ₦113 trillion (first three quarters)

  • Exports: Up 11% year-on-year to $6.1 billion, the highest on record in value and volume

This marks a strong improvement in non-oil trade performance and export diversification.

Nigeria’s $21 billion capital inflow milestone and record trade performance point to renewed economic momentum.

For entrepreneurs, the key opportunity lies in aligning with sectors prioritised under national trade and industrial policies, particularly export-led, manufacturing, and high-growth investment corridors.

If sustained, the current trajectory could translate into stronger funding pipelines, improved trade access, and a more stable operating environment for African businesses.

A surge in foreign capital inflows reflects growing global confidence in Nigeria’s economic direction, creating stronger funding and partnership opportunities for startups and SMEs. With $5 billion in structured, investment-ready projects, entrepreneurs in priority sectors may benefit from clearer financing pathways and government-backed deal facilitation. An 11% rise in exports also signals expanding demand for Nigerian goods, encouraging businesses in manufacturing, agro-processing, and value-added services to target regional and global markets. Backed by the Nigeria Trade Policy (2023–2027), Nigerian Investment Policy (2023–2027), and Nigerian Industrial Policy (2025–2035), the government’s 2026 agenda aims to deepen industrialisation, boost non-oil exports, and strengthen the investment climate, all critical to SME growth and competitiveness.
Dangote Refinery Hits Full Capacity at 650,000 bpd
Africa’s largest refinery reaches record output, reshaping Nigeria’s fuel market and opening new opportunities for regional trade.

Dangote Refinery has reached its full 650,000 barrels-per-day capacity after completing performance optimisation tests. The $20 billion facility can now supply up to 75 million litres of petrol daily, potentially ending Nigeria’s dependence on imported fuel and positioning the country as a refining hub for West Africa.

Africa’s most ambitious industrial project has reached a defining milestone. The $20 billion Dangote Refinery is now operating at its full nameplate capacity of 650,000 barrels per day (bpd) following successful optimisation of key processing units.

Industry observers describe the achievement as a world-scale benchmark for a single-train refinery.

According to the refinery’s CEO, David Bird, the facility can now supply up to 75 million litres of petrol daily to Nigeria’s domestic market, up from 45–50 million litres during the recent festive period.

What Made This Possible?

The refinery completed a 72-hour performance validation in collaboration with UOP, a Honeywell technology licensor. The tests covered critical gasoline-production infrastructure, including:

  • Crude Distillation Unit (CDU)

  • Naphtha Hydrotreater

  • Isomerisation Unit

  • Reformer Unit

All parameters reportedly met international efficiency standards. Phase 2 optimisation of additional processing units is scheduled to begin next week, suggesting further output stability ahead.

Challenges to Watch

Despite the milestone, some structural issues remain:

  • Difficulty securing consistent crude oil supply from domestic producers, forcing imports.

  • Ongoing questions around pricing and distribution within Nigeria’s evolving fuel subsidy framework.

How these issues are resolved will shape the refinery’s long-term impact on market pricing and competitiveness.

 

The Bigger Picture

Construction began in 2016 and faced pandemic delays, FX shortages, and technical hurdles. Its successful ramp-up signals a major shift in Africa’s industrial capacity at a time when global refining markets are under pressure from energy transition policies.

For African entrepreneurs, this is more than an oil story. It is a signal that large-scale industrial bets on the continent can reach execution and unlock entire ecosystems of opportunity.

If sustained at full capacity, Dangote’s refinery could mark the beginning of a new era: from crude exporter to refining powerhouse.

Nigeria has long relied on imported refined petroleum products despite being Africa’s largest oil producer, largely due to the chronic underperformance of its four state-owned refineries. If sustained at full capacity, the Dangote Refinery could significantly reduce or even eliminate fuel imports, easing foreign exchange pressure and minimizing supply disruptions that impact businesses nationwide. A more stable domestic fuel supply would improve cost predictability, reduce scarcity-related shutdowns, and support SMEs in transportation, manufacturing, agriculture, and other energy-intensive sectors. Beyond meeting local demand, the refinery also positions Nigeria as a potential net exporter to West Africa under AfCFTA, opening new opportunities in distribution, storage, shipping, and petrochemicals. With additional outputs such as diesel, aviation fuel, and polypropylene, the facility further strengthens Nigeria’s industrial value chain, creating upstream and downstream opportunities across plastics, packaging, aviation, and heavy industry.
Nigerian National Petroleum Company (NNPC) Posts $4.2bn Profit in 2025
State oil firm reports ₦5.76 trillion after-tax profit as production averages 1.62m barrels per day.

NNPC recorded an after-tax profit of ₦5.76 trillion ($4.26 billion) in 2025, with total revenue of ₦60.52 trillion. Average crude and condensate output stood at 1.62 million barrels per day, while gas supply remained strong and refinery fuel availability improved. Figures remain provisional pending reconciliation.

Nigeria’s state-owned energy giant, NNPC, reported a ₦5.76 trillion ($4.26 billion) after-tax profit for 2025, underscoring improved operational performance in Africa’s largest oil-producing nation.

Total revenue for the year reached ₦60.52 trillion, with ₦14.71 trillion paid in statutory obligations to government agencies and partners. Average crude and condensate production stood at 1.62 million barrels per day, although December output dipped to 1.54 million bpd due to scheduled maintenance and unplanned outages. December revenue alone reached ₦4.82 trillion.
