Natural gas supply averaged 6.914 billion standard cubic feet per day in December, while downstream performance strengthened, with Nigerian Refining Limited stations achieving 65% petrol availability. Upstream pipeline infrastructure reported full operational availability.

NNPC also advanced key gas projects, completing mainline welding on the Ajaokuta-Kaduna-Kano pipeline and progressing drilling on the Obiafu-Obrikom gas pipeline. All financial figures remain provisional.

NNPC’s 2025 results reflect a profitable year marked by solid production, improved downstream metrics, and ongoing infrastructure expansion. For businesses operating in or alongside the energy value chain, the company’s performance remains a key indicator of Nigeria’s broader economic momentum.

NNPC’s profitability signals stronger cash flow into Nigeria’s energy sector and potentially higher government revenues, which can influence fiscal stability and infrastructure spending. Improved pipeline performance, refinery availability, and gas infrastructure development point to gradual strengthening of domestic energy supply, a critical factor for manufacturers, logistics operators, and industrial SMEs. For investors and founders, sustained operational gains in oil and gas could improve foreign exchange liquidity, support energy reliability, and create downstream opportunities across gas distribution, petrochemicals, and industrial services.
South Africa Pushes Ahead with Eskom Break-Up Plan
Government confirms independent transmission company as part of broader power sector reform to unlock private investment.

President Cyril Ramaphosa has reaffirmed plans to unbundle Eskom and establish a fully independent, state-owned transmission company to run South Africa’s electricity grid and market. The move is aimed at attracting private investment, improving energy reliability, and addressing infrastructure funding gaps estimated at 390 billion rand over the next decade.

South Africa is moving forward with a major reform of its electricity sector, confirming plans to separate Eskom’s transmission business into an independent entity.

President Ramaphosa announced that a task team under the National Energy Crisis Committee will oversee the restructuring and present a detailed implementation plan within three months. The decision reverses earlier suggestions that transmission assets would remain an Eskom subsidiary, a proposal that had raised concerns among investors and business leaders.

Unbundling Eskom is designed to open the sector to competition and private capital after decades of monopoly control. Private renewable energy investors have already mobilised more than 200 billion rand and added 6,000 megawatts of capacity without increasing Eskom’s debt burden.

However, South Africa requires an estimated 390 billion rand in transmission infrastructure investment over the next ten years, funding Eskom cannot provide alone due to financial strain. The International Monetary Fund has urged faster electricity reforms, including the separation of generation and transmission units, development of a wholesale power market, and the use of risk-sharing tools such as an International Finance Corporation-backed credit guarantee vehicle.

Years of rolling blackouts have constrained Africa’s most industrialised economy, with ageing plants and weak finances limiting Eskom’s ability to meet demand. Transmission capacity remains one of the biggest bottlenecks to expanding electricity supply.

South Africa’s commitment to unbundling Eskom marks a decisive step toward modernising its electricity market. If backed by clear timelines and execution, the reform could attract fresh capital, ease infrastructure bottlenecks, and restore investor confidence in one of Africa’s largest economies.

For entrepreneurs, credible electricity reform signals potential improvements in power reliability, investment inflows, and long-term energy stability, all critical for manufacturing, tech, mining, and services businesses. Opening the transmission grid to structured private participation could accelerate renewable energy growth, reduce load-shedding risks, and create new opportunities across energy infrastructure, project finance, engineering, and clean power solutions. If successfully implemented, the reform could strengthen South Africa’s competitiveness and unlock broader private sector expansion across the region.
Democratic Republic of the Congo Moves Toward First-Ever International Bond
$750 million debut bond aims to fund infrastructure, build investor confidence, and unlock private sector capital.

The Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) is preparing to issue its first international bond, expected to raise about $750 million. Proceeds will finance roads, hydropower plants, airport upgrades, and rural infrastructure. Officials say the goal is not only to raise funds but to establish a sovereign yield curve that enables private sector borrowing and investment.

The DRC is edging closer to launching its debut international bond as it seeks to capitalise on strong global appetite for emerging market debt and renewed geopolitical interest in Africa’s critical minerals.

Finance Minister Doudou Fwamba Likunde said the planned $750 million issuance will help the country access global capital markets and create a pricing benchmark that allows Congolese companies to raise funds internationally.

While timing depends on market conditions, the bond could be launched around April, following signals from the central bank.

What the Funds Will Support

Bond proceeds are expected to finance major infrastructure projects, including the modernisation of N’djili International Airport in Kinshasa, road upgrades, hydropower plants, and rural infrastructure development.

Despite ongoing internal conflict, the DRC is benefiting from strong global demand for copper, cobalt, and lithium, minerals central to the energy transition. S&P Global Ratings recently placed the country’s B- credit rating on a positive outlook, although analysts expect the bond to carry double-digit interest rates.

The country’s external debt remains relatively low at 18.5% of GDP, with most of it on concessional terms.

In parallel, the U.S.-backed Lobito Corridor railway project, linking mineral-rich regions of the DRC, Angola, and Zambia to the Atlantic coast continues to advance. The U.S. International Development Finance Corporation has committed $530 million in financing to support the project through a private operator selected via tender.

The DRC’s move toward its first international bond reflects growing investor interest in frontier African markets, driven by infrastructure demand and critical mineral supply chains. If successfully executed, it could mark a turning point in the country’s access to global finance, with ripple effects for regional business expansion and private sector development.

For African entrepreneurs, the DRC’s planned bond signals deeper integration into global capital markets and increased infrastructure investment, both of which can unlock private sector growth. A sovereign yield curve could make it easier for local companies to attract foreign capital, while improved transport and energy infrastructure, especially through projects like the Lobito Corridor may reduce logistics costs, strengthen mineral value chains, and create new opportunities in construction, services, manufacturing, and export-driven industries across Central and Southern Africa.
US Judge Blocks Termination of Deportation Protections for South Sudanese Nationals
Court halts Trump administration’s attempt to end Temporary Protected Status, securing work rights for hundreds pending further legal review.

A U.S. federal judge has blocked the Trump administration from ending Temporary Protected Status (TPS) for South Sudanese nationals, a designation that allows eligible migrants to live and work in the United States without fear of deportation. The ruling came after the administration sought to terminate the program, but the court found the justification likely unlawful and part of a broader pattern of ending TPS protections for multiple countries.

A federal judge in Boston has ruled that the U.S. government cannot strip deportation protections from South Sudanese nationals currently living in the United States. The judge concluded that the Department of Homeland Security’s attempt to end TPS was likely unlawful and based on misleading reasoning, noting a broader effort to terminate protections for multiple countries, including Haiti, Venezuela and Ethiopia.

Under TPS, first granted to South Sudanese in 2011 due to ongoing conflict, beneficiaries receive work authorization and protection from deportation. The court’s decision maintains these rights for around 232 individuals currently covered, along with additional pending applicants, until further legal proceedings are complete.


The court’s decision preserves protections for South Sudanese nationals in the U.S., maintaining work and residency rights for now. For African entrepreneurs, the ruling underscores the importance of understanding how legal and policy changes abroad can impact talent mobility, remittances and diaspora-linked business opportunities, all of which are crucial in today’s interconnected economy.

For African entrepreneurs, the ruling preserves workforce stability and income flows within the South Sudanese diaspora, helping maintain remittances, cross-border trade links, and investment ties between the U.S. and African markets. Continued legal work status for affected nationals supports talent mobility, strengthens diaspora-driven business networks, and reduces uncertainty that could disrupt partnerships, staffing, or international expansion plans.
Ghana Cuts Cocoa Farmgate Price, Launches Bond-Backed Financing Model
Government aligns cocoa prices with global market, clears farmer arrears, and introduces domestic cocoa bonds to stabilise the sector.

Ghana has reduced its cocoa farmgate price from 58,000 cedis to 41,392 cedis per metric ton for the 2025/26 season, aligning it with falling global prices now around $4,000 per ton. The government will repay affected farmers and introduce a bond-backed financing model to fund cocoa purchases, while targeting 50% local processing by 2026/27.

Ghana, the world’s second-largest cocoa producer, has cut the price paid to farmers in response to a sharp decline in global cocoa demand and prices.

The new farmgate price is 41,392 cedis ($3,580) per metric ton, down from 58,000 cedis (about $5,300). International cocoa prices have fallen by nearly half over the past year to around $4,000 per ton, making Ghana’s previous pricing uncompetitive.

Officials said the higher domestic price discouraged international buyers, leaving large volumes of unsold beans and many farmers unpaid.

What’s Changing?

• Immediate repayment of outstanding arrears to cocoa farmers.

• Introduction of domestic cocoa bonds, issued by regulator Cocobod, to finance bean purchases within the same crop year.

• A proposed law to link farmgate prices directly to global markets, guaranteeing farmers at least 70% of the Free on Board (FOB) price.

• Target to increase local cocoa processing from 30–40% to at least 50% by the 2026/27 season, including reviving the state-owned Cocoa Processing Company (CPC).

Ghana is restructuring its cocoa pricing and financing system to restore competitiveness and protect farmers.

For African entrepreneurs, the key signal is clear: commodity-dependent economies are shifting toward market-linked pricing and domestic financing, while accelerating value addition at home.

For entrepreneurs, Ghana’s cocoa reforms signal a shift toward greater market realism, financing innovation, and value addition. Aligning farmgate prices with global trends should restore buyer confidence and stabilise export flows, while the new cocoa bond model highlights how domestic capital markets can support commodity financing. Most importantly, the push to increase local processing to 50% creates new opportunities across manufacturing, agro-processing, logistics, cosmetics, and food production, strengthening SME participation in higher-value segments of the cocoa value chain.
Ecowas' Unified Monetary Scheme Takes Centre Stage
Liberia Leads Regional Talks to Advance West Africa’s Single Currency Plan

Liberia hosted the 58th meeting of the West African Monetary Zone Technical Committee in Monrovia, bringing together key regional financial institutions and ECOWAS representatives to move discussions forward on launching the single currency, the Eco, by 2027.

During the meeting, Liberia highlighted its strong 2025 economic performance, including 5.1% growth, reduced inflation, improved revenue generation, and adherence to key macroeconomic convergence criteria. Delegates reviewed reports on fiscal stability, financial supervision, payment systems integration, and policy coordination. The session marked a major step toward aligning member states for deeper economic integration and a unified monetary system across West Africa.

Liberia decisively positioned itself at the core of regional economic integration on Thursday, February 5, 2026, by hosting the 58th meeting of the West African Monetary Zone (WAMZ) Technical Committee in Monrovia.

The meeting, held at the Central Bank of Liberia, drew high-level officials from across West Africa, including representatives from the ECOWAS Commission, the West African Monetary Institute (WAMI), the West African Monetary Agency (WAMA), the West African Institute for Financial and Economic Management (WAIFEM), and key international organisations.

Hon. Augustine Kpehe Ngafuan, Minister of Finance and Development Planning, delivered the welcome statement, while Hon. Dehpue Y. Zuo, Deputy Minister of Economic Management, emphasised Liberia's commitment to regional cooperation and the critical goal of launching the ECOWAS single currency, the Eco, in 2027.

"Liberia is deeply honoured to host this august gathering as we deliberate on critical issues concerning the consolidation of the ECOWAS Monetary Cooperation Programme and the deepening of regional economic integration, including the much-anticipated launch of our single currency, the Eco," Zuo said.

He stated that Liberia had originally planned to hold this gathering in 2022 but had to postpone it due to the country's bicentennial anniversary. "It is therefore a great pleasure that we are now able to welcome you physically to Monrovia, reaffirming Liberia's enduring commitment to regional cooperation and solidarity," the prime minister said.

Zuo gave an assessment of Liberia's recent economic performance, describing it as both a national achievement and a contribution to regional stability. He emphasised the country's robust 5.1% growth in 2025, which exceeded expectations and included substantial expansions in mining and panning operations, as well as advances in electricity and water services. Inflation, he added, had dropped drastically to 4% by December 2025, the lowest level in over two decades.

"These gains reflect prudent fiscal and monetary policies, the easing of global food and fuel prices, and investments in critical infrastructure," Zuo told reporters. On fiscal performance, he noted record domestic revenue of $847.7 million, which exceeded the objective by more than $43 million. He stated that this achievement illustrates Liberia's ability to fund its own development and reduce reliance on external donors.Domestic revenue has increased by US$235.7 million in the last two years, owing to stronger fiscal consolidation, digitalisation of revenue systems, and improved compliance, he added. Zuo also emphasised Liberia's ARREST Agenda for Inclusive Development, a $8.4 billion framework that prioritises Agriculture, Roads, Rule of Law, Education, Sanitation, and Tourism, providing a clear route to long-term growth, poverty reduction, and shared prosperity.

Zuo also emphasised Liberia's adherence to the ECOWAS Macroeconomic Convergence Criteria, stating that the country had met five key indicators by the end of 2025, including budget deficit control, exchange rate stability, debt-to-GDP ratio, reserve accumulation, and overall fiscal discipline. He emphasised that Liberia's achievements strengthen its policy credibility and position it as a model for resilience and economic transformation in West Africa.Your thorough review of these topics will be important in building the next phase of our regional monetary and financial integration," he remarked, emphasising the significance of technical rigour and collaborative effort in achieving the Eco agenda.

Following the opening remarks, the Chairman of the WAMZ Technical Committee, Mr. Alhaji Abu Komeh, Acting Director, Ministry of Finance, Sierra Leone, welcomed delegates and thanked Liberia for hosting the meeting. Komeh noted the region's unfavourable global economic situation in 2025, which will be characterised by policy uncertainty, protectionist trade policies, and falling commodity prices. Despite these hurdles, he observed that real GDP growth in the West African Monetary Zone is expected to reach 5% in the first half of 2025, up from 3.7% in 2024.

Komeh also cited Sierra Leone's economic performance as a major criterion for convergence, citing GDP growth of 4.4% in 2025, a sharp drop in inflation to 4.4% by December, and a lower public debt-to-GDP ratio of 40.9%, down from 42.3% in 2024. He applauded member states for strengthening budgetary discipline, increasing domestic revenue mobilisation, and improving public sector reforms. "We will continue to implement medium-term revenue mobilisation strategies, improve compliance, and modernise customs procedures to ensure efficiency in revenue administration and trade facilitation," Komeh stated. He emphasised that these steps were crucial to satisfying convergence standards and assuring preparation for the Eco launch.

Dr. Abdulsalam Abidemi Sikiru, Director-General of the West African Monetary Institute (WAMI), shared these comments and congratulated Liberia on its smooth 2025 presidential elections and its position as a stabilising force in the region. "Our presence here provides not only an opportunity for productive relations but also a chance to appreciate the history, resilience, and enduring optimism of the great nation whose commitment to regional solidarity continues to inspire us all," he told reporters.

Dr. Sikiru emphasised the necessity of technical preparation for the monetary union. "Key reports and policy papers, including the General Progress Report, Macroeconomic Development and Convergence Reports, and supervisory reports on banking and financial institutions, will form the basis of our deliberations during the days ahead," according to him. He congratulated all member states, the ECOWAS Commission, WAMI, WAIFEM, and development partners for their continued cooperation and support.

Throughout the week, delegates reviewed crucial issues such as remittance inflows, development financing, assistance implications, legal frameworks for the monetary union, capital market growth, payments system integration through PAPSS, and banking supervision. These negotiations are critical for ensuring that the Eco is launched on time and that the area benefits from greater economic cooperation.

Liberia's hosting of the WAMZ session also gave the country an opportunity to highlight its economic resilience and reform plan, which includes infrastructure investment, digitalisation of public services, and fiscal discipline. Zuo emphasised that the ECO's success is dependent on both regional technical collaboration and strong national underpinnings.

"Liberia stands ready to fully support the implementation of the decisions and recommendations that will emerge from your deliberations," the prime minister added. "Through your technical rigour, policy creativity, and collective resolve, we can advance an agenda that promotes sustainable growth, macroeconomic stability, and shared prosperity across our region."

The 58th WAMZ Technical Committee session in Monrovia is largely regarded as a watershed moment in the region's progress toward the ECOWAS Monetary Union. The summit is an important step in the long-awaited launch of the Eco in 2027 because it consolidates reforms, ensures convergence, and fosters policy coordination among member states.

As the region faces difficult global economic issues, the technical committee's discussions highlight the value of regional cooperation, good macroeconomic policies, and inclusive development initiatives. For Liberia, hosting this gathering demonstrates the country's leading position in developing West Africa's monetary and economic landscape.

By the end of the four-day meeting, delegates are expected to have reviewed and validated key reports, approved policy recommendations, and strengthened regional coordination mechanisms, laying the groundwork for a single currency that promises to boost trade, investment, and economic stability across West Africa.

In a region where economic integration has long been a strategic goal, Liberia's central role in hosting the WAMZ Technical Committee, combined with recent macroeconomic achievements, signals both national resilience and regional commitment to the successful realisation of the ECO and the larger vision of an "ECOWAS of the People".

For African Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises, the proposed Eco currency could be transformative:

Easier Cross-Border Trade: A single currency reduces exchange rate barriers, making it simpler for MSMEs to sell goods and services across West African countries.

Lower Transaction Costs: Businesses would avoid multiple currency conversions and associated banking fees.

Price Stability & Predictability: Stronger macroeconomic coordination can reduce inflation volatility, helping small businesses plan better.

Access to Larger Markets: A unified monetary zone creates a broader, more integrated regional marketplace.

Improved Financial Systems: Integration of payment platforms and banking supervision strengthens trust and efficiency in financial transactions.

In simple terms, if implemented successfully, the Eco could make it easier for an MSME in Lagos, Monrovia, Accra, or Freetown to trade across borders without currency headaches slowing growth.
2026 Appropriation Bill - Senate Defends Borrowing to Fund N25.91 Trillion Deficit, Pushes Power Subsidy Exit.
Senate Defends N25 Trillion Deficit as Borrowing Becomes Central to Nigeria’s 2026 Budget Plan

Nigeria’s Senate has defended the federal government’s decision to finance a projected N25.91 trillion deficit in the proposed N58.47 trillion 2026 budget through continued borrowing, citing unpredictable revenues and pressing development needs. Lawmakers emphasised that borrowing itself is not the problem, but how the funds are utilised, stressing the need for fiscal discipline, asset optimisation, subsidy reforms, and stronger revenue mobilisation.

While the government plans to reduce excessive domestic borrowing to avoid crowding out private sector credit, concerns were raised about rising debt servicing costs and the sustainability of mounting deficits. At the same time, the Minister of Industry, Trade, and Investment warned that the ministry’s proposed N2.72 billion capital allocation is insufficient to drive industrialisation, expand trade, and attract investment at the scale required to meet Nigeria’s trillion-dollar economy ambition. Policymakers agreed that budget implementation must shift from allocation-focused planning to measurable impact, ensuring that borrowed funds translate into infrastructure, job creation, and economic growth.

The Senate yesterday strongly defended the federal government's continued use of borrowing, declaring that deficit financing is unavoidable in order to fund the proposed N58.47 trillion 2026 Appropriation Bill, which has a deficit of approximately N25.91 trillion due to persistent revenue constraints and vast development needs.

This comes as the Minister of Industry, Trade, and Investment, Mrs Jumoke Oduwole, expressed concern yesterday about the proposed N2.72 billion capital allocation for the Ministry in 2026, warning that the amount was insufficient to deliver the scale of programmes required to drive Nigeria's industrialisation, trade expansion, and investment attraction agenda.

At a public hearing on the 2026 budget proposal held at the National Assembly, Senator Solomon Adeola, Chairman of the Senate Committee on Appropriations, stated that Nigeria could not realistically meet its infrastructure, security, and social obligations without borrowing but emphasised that the era of wasteful, consumption-driven deficits must end.

Adeola emphasised that the main issue was not borrowing itself, but rather how deficits are funded and borrowed resources are exploited.

"Nigeria cannot help but continue borrowing because revenue inflows are unpredictable and development needs are enormous." "What matters is how we borrow, how we fund deficits, and what we do with the borrowed resources," he said.

He revealed that, while predicted revenue for 2026 is N33.19 trillion, total expenditure is estimated at N58.47 trillion, leaving a deficit of N25.27 trillion, with debt service set to consume over N15.90 trillion.

According to him, the numbers highlight the importance of revenue growth, asset optimisation, and tighter budgetary discipline.

The congressman stated that the federal government is consciously avoiding excessive domestic borrowing, which might crowd out private sector credit, in favour of a combination of foreign finance, asset sales, privatisation, Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs), and infrastructure concessions.

Adeola emphasised the need to completely eliminate electricity subsidies to free up resources for development. He cautioned that partial reforms in the power sector would still deplete state budgets. We must finish the unbundling and subsidy elimination in the electrical sector. States are now authorised to generate electricity, although subsidies in that sector remain a significant economic burden. "It must be fully addressed," he stated.

He remembered that trillions of naira had previously been spent on gasoline subsidies, which were mostly supported by borrowing, and described President Bola Tinubu's decision to abolish the subsidies as a watershed moment that laid the groundwork for continued fiscal reforms.

Adeola announced that the National Assembly will no longer allow budget extensions, citing bad budget outcomes from previous rollovers. The National Assembly will never again adopt budget extensions. "We must discipline our budgeting process, enforce timelines, and improve coordination between policy design and implementation," he stated.

Godswill Akpabio, President of the Senate, and Deputy Senate President, Senator Barau Jibrin, described the 2026 budget plan as a moral and historical test for the nation. They urged lawmakers and the executive to guarantee that borrowing yields concrete advantages for Nigerians. A budget is a moral document. It expresses our priorities and ideals. It is not enough to simply allocate monies; we must turn budgets into tangible benefits for Nigerians," Akpabio stated.

Dr Doris Nkiruka Uzoka-Anite, Minister of State for Finance, stated that the proposed budget for 2026 is intended to coincide with government priorities, deepen ongoing reforms, and guarantee that limited national resources are used efficiently and effectively.

She acknowledged public dissatisfaction with rising living costs, characterising Nigeria's economic recovery as fragile but cautiously optimistic.

Dr Olatilewa Adebajo, a fiscal policy specialist, has cautioned that Nigeria's mounting deficit may become unsustainable unless urgent actions are taken to increase revenue mobilisation and enforce fiscal laws.

He advocated for a thorough study and stronger execution of the Fiscal Responsibility Act (FRA), citing it as a valuable but underutilised measure. We need to examine and aggressively enforce the Fiscal Responsibility Act. Revenue leakages remain substantial, particularly in sectors such as solid minerals, where Nigeria gets very little from large extraction activities," Adebajo claimed, saying that foreign interests continue to exploit the industry with limited returns to the government.

The Accountant General of the Federation, Shamseldeen Olujimi, called for a transition from allocation-driven budgeting to impact-focused implementation.

For too long, we have excelled in budget development but struggled with budget translation. "The real question is not how much we allocate, but what changes in Nigerians' lives as a result of these allocations," he remarked.

Olujimi emphasised that success should be judged in terms of running schools, operable health centres, reliable power supply, and job creation, rather than budget size or speed of passage.

Adeola guaranteed that all monies, including service-wide votes and special interventions, would continue to be subject to National Assembly supervision and warned Ministries, Departments, and Agencies (MDAs) that failing to defend their budgets would result in reallocation.

Mrs Jumoke Oduwole, Minister of Industry, Trade, and Investment, also expressed worry over the projected N2.72 billion capital allocation for her ministry, stressing that it was badly inadequate.

Oduwole, who was defending the ministry's 2026 budget plan before the combined Senate Committees on Trade and Investment and Industry, urged the National Assembly to enhance capital funding in a targeted manner.

She emphasised that without enough resources, the ministry's ability to support Tinubu's Renewed Hope Agenda and the drive for a trillion-dollar economy would be severely limited.

She credited the Ministry of Industry, Trade, and Investment with diversifying the economy away from oil, increasing non-oil exports, promoting domestic production, and attracting local and global investment.

The planned capital allocation of N2.72 billion will not be sufficient to meet the entire demands of our programmes and capital projects," Oduwole informed legislators.

She went on: "Given the scope of our responsibilities, we respectfully seek the committee's support for targeted enhancement of our capital allocation to enable us to effectively deliver on our mandate."

Senator Umar Sadiq, Chairman of the Senate Committee on Trade and Investment, emphasised the ministry's strategic relevance to the administration's economic vision.

He emphasised that establishing a $1 trillion GDP would be difficult without strong performance in the industry, trade, and investment sectors.

Sadiq stated, "We are all aware of the renewal agenda of Mr President, which is essentially to ensure that we have a trillion-dollar economy." Outside of the oil sector, the Ministry of Industry, Trade, and Investment is an important partner in attaining this goal.

Also addressing the Senate, Senator Francis Fadahunsi, Chairman of the Senate Committee on Industry, encouraged the ministry to clearly illustrate the impact of its agencies on Nigerians' lives, particularly in terms of job creation, export growth, and industrial development.

In her presentation, Oduwole detailed the ministry's accomplishments over the last two years, claiming that the results thus far justified further capital support.

She revealed that Nigeria spent approximately $21 billion on capital imports in the first ten months of 2025, compared to $12 billion in 2024 and less than $4 billion in 2023.

She credited the improvement to purposeful ministry actions, such as the creation of almost $5 billion in bankable investment projects, sector-specific deal rooms, and Nigeria's inaugural Domestic Investor Summit.

The minister stated that the ministry had also handled more than 50 key investor bottlenecks and had over 100 bilateral investment discussions with nations including the United Kingdom, the United States, the United Arab Emirates, Brazil, and Japan.

For African Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises, this budget debate is not abstract policy. It directly shapes their operating environment.

1. Access to Credit:
If government borrowing leans heavily on domestic banks, MSMEs may struggle to access affordable loans due to reduced private sector liquidity.

2. Infrastructure & Power Costs:
How borrowed funds are invested determines whether MSMEs benefit from improved roads, electricity, and digital infrastructure or continue operating under high overhead costs.

3. Industrial & Trade Support:
Limited capital allocation to the Ministry of Industry, Trade, and Investment could restrict export promotion programmes, SME financing schemes, and investment facilitation that small businesses rely on.

4. Policy Stability & Inflation:
Rising deficits and debt servicing pressures can influence inflation and exchange rate stability, both of which directly affect input costs and pricing for small businesses.

5. Market Expansion Opportunities:
If reforms succeed and investment inflows continue to grow, MSMEs could gain access to new supply chains, partnerships, and export markets.
Cedi stability depends on fiscal discipline, say economists to the government
Experts Urge Fiscal Discipline to Protect Cedi Stability After IMF Exit

Three Ghanaian economic experts have advised the government to maintain strict budgetary discipline and continue ongoing reforms to sustain the recent stability of the cedi as Ghana prepares to exit its IMF programme later this year.

During a policy discussion, the experts highlighted the importance of adhering to the Public Finance Management Act, including maintaining fiscal surpluses, capping public debt, and strengthening oversight mechanisms. While acknowledging progress in stabilising domestic economic conditions, they cautioned that external pressures, particularly Ghana’s dependence on imports like petroleum, remain a risk to currency stability.
They also emphasised the need to minimise sharp currency fluctuations, diversify the economy, and improve the business environment to support long-term economic resilience. According to the panel, sustained reforms and structural improvements are essential to keeping the cedi stable and strengthening Ghana’s post-IMF recovery.

Three economic experts have advised the government to maintain budgetary discipline and continue with existing economic reforms to stabilise the cedi.

The experts--Dr Theo Acheampong, Technical Advisor at the Ministry of Finance; Professor Agyapomaa Gyeke-Dako, economist at the University of Ghana Business School; and Mr Nicholas Issaka Gbana, development economist and chartered accountant--agreed that continuing these measures is critical to maintaining the cedi's recent performance and strengthening Ghana's economy after the country exits the International Monetary Fund (IMF) programme later this year.

Their comments came during an online panel debate on NovanReports' X Space on Sunday, titled "Cedi Stability in 2026: Can Ghana Hold the Line After the IMF?"

Dr Acheampong voiced confidence in Ghana's ability to sustain cedi stability post-IMF, citing the government's commitment to budgetary discipline and ongoing reforms. He emphasised the importance of strictly adhering to the Public Finance Management (PFM) Act, which includes the requirement for an annual fiscal surplus of at least 1.5 percent of GDP, a cap on public debt of 45 percent of GDP by 2035, and the Fiscal Council's oversight role, in keeping the cedi stable.

He also refuted charges that the government was underspending, stating that public funds are being focused toward productive areas. "What happens externally and what we do domestically are both very important in ensuring cedi stability as we exit the IMF programme," he added.

Mr Gbana praised Ghana's success in controlling domestic economic variables, but warned that external pressures, particularly the country's reliance on imported commodities such as petroleum, continue to be a source of concern. He recommended Small and Medium-Sized Enterprises (SMEs) to implement sensible pricing and inventory strategies to deal with foreign exchange volatility, recognising that many SMEs cannot afford formal hedging mechanisms.

Professor Gyeke-Dako stressed the necessity of minimising excessive cedi swings, claiming that relative stability enables enterprises to prepare properly. She also advocated for economic diversification and a more supportive business environment, citing structural obstacles that continue to impede local industry. "Until we fix the unfriendly business environment, local goods will remain more expensive than imported ones," she made clear.

The experts agreed that preserving fiscal discipline, enacting economic reforms, and resolving structural difficulties are critical to the cedi's survival and Ghana's long-term growth.

For African Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises, currency stability is not just a macroeconomic headline. It shapes daily survival and growth.

1. Predictable Costs:
A stable cedi reduces sudden spikes in the price of imported inputs such as fuel, machinery, and raw materials, allowing MSMEs to price products more confidently.

2. Better Planning:
Reduced exchange rate volatility enables small businesses to forecast cash flow, manage inventory, and plan expansion without fear of sudden currency shocks.

3. Access to Finance:
Strong fiscal discipline can improve investor confidence, potentially lowering borrowing costs and expanding financing options for SMEs.

4. Competitive Local Production:
If reforms improve the business environment and reduce structural bottlenecks, locally produced goods can become more competitive against imports, benefiting MSMEs in manufacturing and trade.

5. Resilience Against External Shocks:
Stable macroeconomic policies cushion small businesses from global commodity price swings and external financial pressures.
US-Backed Consortium Secures 40% Stake in Two DR Congo Mines
US-Backed Consortium Moves to Secure 40% Stake in Strategic DR Congo Copper and Cobalt Mines

The Orion Critical Mineral Consortium, backed by the US government through the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation and led by Orion Resource Partners, has signed a non binding agreement with Glencore to acquire a 40 percent stake in two major Democratic Republic of the Congo mining assets: Mutanda Mining and Kamoto Copper Company. The combined enterprise value of the assets is estimated at about 9 billion dollars.

The proposed deal forms part of a broader US-DRC Strategic Partnership aimed at strengthening bilateral ties and securing reliable supply chains for critical minerals such as copper and cobalt. These minerals are essential for energy transition technologies, electric vehicles, advanced manufacturing, defense systems, and digital infrastructure. If finalized, Orion CMC will appoint non executive directors and help direct the sale of its share of mineral production to designated buyers, while Glencore will continue operating the mines. The transaction remains subject to due diligence and regulatory approvals.

High level engagements, including participation by President Felix Tshisekedi in Washington business forums, signal the geopolitical and economic weight of the agreement.

The Orion Critical Mineral Consortium (Orion CMC), led by the private equity fund formed in October by Orion Resource Partners and the United States International Development Finance Corporation (DFC), intends to acquire a 40% stake in two mining ventures in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, according to a statement released Tuesday. The US government's investment in Orion CMC is "aimed at strengthening bilateral ties and securing critical mineral supply chains essential to advancing U.S. economic growth, security, and innovation in energy and advanced technologies," according to DFC, which founded the fund.

Glencore, the mine operations owner, made the news one day before the inaugural Critical Minerals Ministerial at the State Department in Washington, DC, which was hosted by US Secretary of State Marco Rubio. President Felix Tshisekedi of the Democratic Republic of the Congo and his mining minister, Louis Watum Kabamba, are scheduled to attend, as are ministers from Guinea and Kenya, as well as representatives from several mineral-rich and industrialised countries. On Friday, Tshisekedi will appear at a high-level U.S.-DRC Presidential Business Roundtable at the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, alongside Glencore DRC President Marie-Chantal Kaninda. The following is the text of Glencore and Orion CMC's announcement from today.

Glencore and the Orion Critical Mineral Consortium ("Orion CMC") have signed a non-binding Memorandum of Understanding regarding the potential acquisition by Orion CMC of a 40% stake in Glencore's interests in its Democratic Republic of Congo ("DRC") assets, Mutanda Mining ("Mumi") and Kamoto Copper Company. The merger is estimated to give Mumi and KCC a combined enterprise value of roughly $9 billion.

In accordance with the U.S.-DRC Strategic Partnership Agreement, Orion CMC will be able to appoint non-executive directors for the assets and direct the sale of the relevant share of production from the assets to nominated buyers, securing critical minerals for the United States and its partners. Mumi and KCC would continue to be handled by the Glencore Group.

Glencore and Orion CMC will also search for chances to grow and develop Mumi and KCC, collaborating with the DRC government and Gécamines, Glencore's current KCC partner, as well as acquiring more significant mineral projects and assets in the DRC and the African copper belt more broadly.

Orion CMC, founded in October 2025 and led by Orion Resource Partners in collaboration with the US government, is a mission-driven consortium dedicated to assisting the US and its allies and partners in developing secure, responsible, and resilient supply chains for critical minerals that will underpin future economic growth and security.

The proposed deal is still subject to due diligence, the execution of legally binding documentation, and any necessary regulatory approvals.

U.S. Deputy Secretary of State Christopher Landau stated, "The United States government is fully committed to the Washington Accords and ensuring peace, stability, and prosperity throughout the Great Lakes region." This proposed transaction between Glencore and the US-backed Orion Critical Minerals Consortium reflects the core objectives of the US-DRC Strategic Partnership Agreement, which include encouraging increased US investment in the DRC's mining sector and promoting secure, reliable, and mutually beneficial flows of critical minerals between our two countries. We look forward to continuing close collaboration with the DRC government to execute this deal in a way that benefits both our peoples and sets a good model for future US investment."

DFC CEO Ben Black stated, "This proposed partnership between Orion CMC and Glencore has the potential to generate significant returns for both the United States and the DRC." The possible investment by Orion CMC would highlight the strengthening partnership between the United States and the Democratic Republic of the Congo, assist secure a reliable source of vital minerals for the United States and our partners, and generate economic opportunity and regional stability in the DRC."

Gary Nagle, CEO of Glencore, stated, "We are pleased that the US government and Orion CMC have recognised Glencore's role as the sole major Western producer of copper and cobalt in the DRC, through our high-quality assets, Mumi and KCC. We would be able to support the aspirations of the US government and business sector through this relationship by supplying two key minerals."

"The proposed transaction also represents a significant vote of confidence in the DRC government's ongoing efforts to attract major foreign investment to the country and further develop its mining industry. As the world's largest producer of cobalt and one of the largest copper producers, the DRC plays a vital role in supporting the technology, defense and critical infrastructure roll-out of today and the future."

Oskar Lewnowski, Founder and CEO of Orion Resource Partners LP, stated, "This proposed transaction is exactly what Orion CMC was established to achieve: securing long-term, high-quality production of critical minerals while supporting resilient supply chains for the United States and its allies. It emphasises both the scope of the opportunity and the strategic importance of the Consortium's purpose, and this collaboration will provide a solid foundation for Orion CMC to further develop vital mineral assets and pursue further investment possibilities. We are excited to collaborate with Glencore and the US DFC to accomplish our common goals."

This is more than a mining deal. It is a tectonic plate shifting beneath Africa’s industrial landscape.

1. Supply Chain Openings
Large scale foreign investment often expands demand for local suppliers. African MSMEs in logistics, catering, equipment maintenance, security, ICT services, environmental services, and workforce training can plug into these expanding mining operations and their value chains.

2. Infrastructure Spillovers
Major mining investments typically accelerate improvements in roads, power, and connectivity around mining corridors. MSMEs benefit from improved infrastructure that lowers operating costs and opens access to new markets.

3. Access to Finance and Partnerships
US backed involvement can raise governance and compliance standards. That can attract additional international investors and development finance institutions, creating blended finance and subcontracting opportunities for credible local enterprises.

4. Industrialization Potential
Copper and cobalt are the backbone of batteries and renewable energy systems. If African governments leverage this momentum wisely, it could encourage more local processing, battery assembly, and downstream manufacturing. That is where MSMEs in fabrication, engineering, packaging, and tech innovation can thrive.

5. Stability and Investor Confidence
Strong bilateral agreements may improve political and regulatory stability. MSMEs operate best when the ground beneath them is steady, not shifting sand.
Uganda's central bank maintains its benchmark rate, urging prudence.

Uganda's central bank held its main lending rate constant on Monday for the sixth consecutive meeting, citing an uncertain economic climate as a reason for caution.

Since October 2024, the Bank of Uganda's Central Bank Rate, or UGCB, has been 9.75%.

Governor Michael Atingi-Ego told a press conference that the current policy stance was still adequate to boost economic activity while keeping inflation around the bank's 5% medium-term target.

In January, inflation increased to 3.2% year on year, up from 3.1% the previous month.

Atingi-Ego predicted that inflation will remain slightly below target in 2026, ranging between 3.8% and 4.3%, before stabilising at 5%.

Economic growth is expected to be between 6.5% and 7% in the current fiscal year that ends in June, rising to an average of 8% in the medium term due to strong levels of public investment and oil-related infrastructure investments, the governor stated.

The East African country is planning to begin pumping commercial quantities of crude oil later this year.
Zimbabwe’s ZiG and the Crisis of Credibility
Zimbabwe’s Economic Data Claims Clash with Market Reality as ZiG Currency Remains Largely Absent from Circulation

In February 2026, Zimbabwean authorities released a series of economic statements that sharply contradicted on-the-ground conditions. Government announcements of bumper maize and wheat harvests followed earlier claims of food self-sufficiency, despite continued large-scale imports, empty silos reported by millers, and persistent shortages in retail markets. Officials explained wheat imports as necessary for blending soft local wheat with hard wheat, but analysts noted that import volumes far exceeded blending requirements, raising concerns about overstated production figures or inefficiencies within the supply chain.

At the same time, the Ministry of Finance announced that inflation had fallen to 4.1 percent, the lowest level since 1997. The figure was attributed to price stability in the ZiG currency, which is tightly controlled by the state and pegged to the US dollar. However, the ZiG remains largely absent from everyday commerce, with limited availability in stores and no use in major transactions such as fuel, transport, healthcare, or international payments. Labour unions, including the Zimbabwe Congress of Trade Unions, accused the government of relying on manipulated statistics to undermine wage negotiations, urging workers and employers to disregard inflation figures based on the ZiG.

Despite official narratives, consumer prices in US dollars continue to rise, and the lack of physical ZiG currency in circulation has left businesses and consumers operating almost entirely outside the framework used to calculate official inflation.

In February 2026, Zimbabwean propaganda reaches weird new heights, painting a vision of a country that just does not exist. We were still digesting the news that Zimbabwe had a bumper maize harvest in 2025, despite milling companies reporting empty silos, supermarkets running out of stock, and us importing maize. It made no sense, but shortly after, the Ministry of Lands and Agriculture announced that we had also harvested a bumper crop of wheat, the largest since commercial production began in 1966.

But why are we importing wheat? many wondered. Authorities explained that Zimbabwe grows soft wheat and has to import 30% hard wheat with a high gluten level for usage in bread, biscuits, and baked products. According to experts, this explanation "collapses under mathematical scrutiny" because we import so much wheat that it is more than seven times the amount required for blending. Zimbabwe's NewsDay newspaper reported that "the volumes [of imports] far exceed blending needs, pointing to waste, re-exports, or overstated domestic production."

Then, last week, the Minister of Finance made the oddest revelation of all: inflation had fallen to 4.1%, the lowest since 1997. That figure appears to refer to 'price stability in the local currency. ' No one understands how to quantify inflation with a currency that is mostly invisible. The Ministry of Finance controls the ZiG currency and its exchange rate to the US dollar. A currency that no one carries in their pockets and is not available at most store checkouts. How can you even have a currency that cannot be used to purchase an airline ticket, a passport, fuel, or medicine? It doesn't make sense.

The Zimbabwe Congress of Trade Unions (ZCTU) accused Finance Minister Mthuli Ncube of using "severely manipulated" inflation numbers to disregard workers' pay demands. They stated that assertions of 'currency stability' were "cooked up". “Workers must earn salaries above the poverty datum line in a functional and stable currency,” they said. ZCTU Secretary General Mr Marimo said, “We urge all workers and negotiators to disregard the Minister’s misguided comments, which appear designed to pressure employers into paying salaries in the unstable ZiG.”

In the end, despite the mountains of propaganda and spin, it all boils down to data and what we see with our own eyes. This week, a 1-kilogram package of oats in the store cost US$4.35; in December, the same size and type of oats in the same shop cost US$3.50. Paying with a five-dollar bill, the teller asked, "Can I give you a ballpoint pen or a chocolate to bring the total up to five dollars?" "No," I answered, asking her to give me Zigs as change, which would only be approximately 20 Zigs, but she said, "Sorry, we don't have any Zigs at all."

This is exactly how it has been since April 2024, when Finance Minister Mthuli Ncube unveiled the ZiG: there is none in our pockets, in the tills, or in public circulation. Whether or not there is inflation, the Zig is little more than a mirage on the horizon.

This is not just a Zimbabwean story. It is a warning signal for MSMEs across Africa.

Pricing and Cost Planning Risks
MSMEs depend on credible inflation and currency data to price goods, forecast costs, and manage cash flow. When official figures are disconnected from transaction realities, small businesses absorb the shock through shrinking margins and unstable pricing.

Wage Negotiation and Labour Relations
Inflation data influences wage expectations and negotiations. Disputed or non-credible statistics heighten tensions between employers and workers, placing MSMEs—often operating on thin margins—under intense pressure.

Currency Availability and Liquidity Constraints
A functional currency must circulate. When businesses cannot access local currency for change, payments, or basic transactions, MSMEs are forced into informal workarounds that increase operational risk and reduce transparency.

Supply Chain Distortions
Overstated production figures mask real shortages. MSMEs in agriculture, food processing, retail, and logistics are exposed when import dependency is downplayed and supply risks are misrepresented.

Decision-Making Under Uncertainty
For MSMEs, unreliable macroeconomic signals make long-term planning nearly impossible. Expansion, hiring, investment, and inventory decisions become guesswork rather than strategy.
South Sudan faces renewed conflict as UN funding declines.
Rising Insecurity and Political Gridlock in South Sudan Threaten Peace Gains and Economic Stability

South Sudan is facing renewed instability as conflict, political deadlock, and a worsening humanitarian crisis converge. According to the UN peacekeeping chief, recent clashes involving government forces, opposition groups, and intensified aerial attacks have displaced more than 280,000 people, raising serious concerns about a relapse into widespread conflict. The situation has been compounded by restrictions on humanitarian access and funding cuts that have weakened UN operations, even as the country grapples with a severe cholera outbreak.

Political tensions continue to undermine progress on the 2018 peace agreement. Unilateral proposals to delay constitution-making until after elections risk eroding the agreement’s authority, while ongoing judicial actions against opposition leader Riek Machar have deepened mistrust. The UN has warned that without sustained international support, peacekeeping efforts critical to civilian protection and national stability could be severely compromised.

South Sudan has been plagued by conflict, political gridlock, and an escalating humanitarian crisis, while funding cuts have undermined UN operations tasked with protecting people.

Jean-Pierre Lacroix, UN peacekeeping chief, told the Security Council that clashes had displaced over 280,000 people, raising fears of a return to past conflict. The government troops, opposition groups, and intensified aerial attacks all led to the rise in insecurity. Furthermore, limits on assistance access aggravated an already severe cholera outbreak.

He also warned that unilateral efforts to change the 2018 peace agreement, such as suggestions to postpone constitution-making until after elections, risk jeopardising the accord's supremacy. Political concerns slowed progress on the 2018 peace accord and prompted the continuation of judicial procedures against opposition leader Riek Machar. Lacroix sought continued international support, warning that the mission's presence was critical to civilian protection and stability.

This crisis highlights how fragile peace directly shapes the business environment for MSMEs across Africa.

Business Continuity Risks
Insecurity and displacement disrupt local markets, supply chains, and workforce availability. MSMEs are often the first to shut down when violence escalates, losing assets, customers, and livelihoods.

Investor and Partner Confidence
Political uncertainty and weakened peace agreements deter investment and partnerships. MSMEs struggle to access capital, insurance, and long-term contracts in environments where rules and institutions are unstable.

Health Crises and Productivity Losses
Outbreaks such as cholera strain already fragile systems, increasing absenteeism, healthcare costs, and operational downtime for small businesses.

Dependence on Stability Infrastructure
Many MSMEs rely indirectly on peacekeeping missions and humanitarian operations for security, infrastructure support, and local demand. Funding cuts weaken these stabilizing forces, exposing businesses to higher risk.

Regional Spillover Effects
Instability in one country affects trade corridors, cross-border MSMEs, and regional markets, particularly in East and Central Africa where informal and small-scale trade is vital.
The Global Africa Investment Summit was launched in Dubai to transform Africa's investment landscape.
Global Africa Investment Summit Launches to Unlock Sovereign Assets and Channel Private Capital into Africa

The Global Africa Investment Summit (GAIS) has been officially launched as a new platform designed to connect Africa’s underutilised sovereign assets with global private investment. Co-founded by former African Development Bank President Dr. Akinwumi Adesina and APCO founder Margery Kraus, GAIS aims to accelerate Africa’s shift from aid dependence to investment-led growth by transforming public assets into bankable, investment-ready portfolios.

Positioned as a structured bridge between African governments and global investors, GAIS seeks to reduce long-standing barriers to capital flows such as fragmented markets, high transaction costs, and limited transparency. The platform will focus on strategic sectors including energy, critical minerals, agriculture, digital innovation, healthcare, and infrastructure. Through de-risked asset structuring, transaction support, and cross-border collaboration, GAIS aims to unlock large-scale investments capable of driving sustainable growth across the continent.

Backed by strong political and institutional support from African heads of state and international leaders, the initiative reflects a shared recognition that Africa’s vast assets—land, minerals, infrastructure, and human capital—remain significantly undervalued. The inaugural Global Africa Investment Summit is scheduled to take place in Luanda, Angola, in 2027, bringing together governments, institutional investors, CEOs, and development partners to advance investment-ready deals at scale.

The Global Africa Investment Summit (GAIS), a ground-breaking platform designed to connect Africa's underutilised sovereign assets with global private finance, was officially inaugurated today. Dr. Akinwumi Adesina, former president of the African Development Bank Group, and Margery Kraus, founder and executive chair of APCO, co-founded the platform, which is positioned to serve as a catalyst for Africa's transition from aid dependency to investment-driven prosperity.

At the launch, Dr. Akinwumi Adesina, co-founder and executive chairman of GAIS, emphasised the importance of this effort:

"In the next years, Africa will need to unlock its massive sovereign assets to drive global economic growth. The Global Africa Investment Summit platform is the reliable, structured institution required to connect Africa's enormous potential with global private investments. Through this platform, we are establishing the conditions for megadeals that will convert potential into bankable investments at scale, driving transformative growth throughout and beyond the continent."

Margery Kraus, co-founder and vice chair of GAIS, highlighted the summit's gathering power:

"The African economic environment is at a critical moment. The Global Africa Investment Summit will serve as a catalyst, bringing together governments, investors, and private sector groups to develop revolutionary relationships. It's an initiative that benefits from Dr. Adesina's knowledge and trust, as his experience driving economic progress in Africa is unparalleled. I am happy to support this project as a dependable partner in Africa's investment journey."

As Dr. Adesina's partner in this effort, APCO is dedicated to fostering strategic discourse and catalysing transformative partnerships that help Africa reach its full potential. “GAIS represents a bold step forward in reimagining Africa’s economic future,” said Kraus. “Powered by APCO’s global footprint and more than 40 years of experience working at the intersection of business and government, the Global Africa Investment Summit will bring together leaders from across the globe to create a platform where ambition meets action and where investment becomes the catalyst for a new era of prosperity across Africa.”

Unleashing Africa's Investment Potential

The Global Africa Investment Summit aims to establish Africa as a top global investment destination by transforming public assets into low-risk, investment-ready portfolios. The platform would aggressively remove constraints that have previously hampered Africa's access to global private capital, such as fragmented markets, high transaction costs, and inadequate transparency.

Africa, the world's youngest continent, has over 1.3 billion people and 12 of the world's 20 fastest-growing countries, placing it at the forefront of global economic considerations. The Global Africa Investment Summit aims to attract global private finance and leverage Africa's core sectors, including energy and critical minerals, agriculture, digital innovation, and health care, in order to allow long-term, sustainable development.

The platform will give governments, private sector leaders, and institutional investors with the following:

  • De-risked, investment-ready portfolios: Assisting governments in structuring and promoting sovereign assets.

  • Access to transaction support: Enabling transparent and efficient matchmaking between African countries and international investors.

  • Opportunities for Collaboration: Encourage cross-border investment to unlock regional potential.

At the launch, H.E. João Manuel Gonçalves Lourenço, president of Angola and chairperson of the African Union, stated that while Africa is rich in opportunities, the perception of risk has deterred many investors. The Global Africa Investment Summit is a widely recognised and well-organised bridge that connects Africa with global investors. It is precisely what Africa has been looking for, and what the world requires to realise Africa's full potential."

A New Platform for Africa's Future.

The Global Africa Investment Summit is more than just a single event; it represents a movement to reshape Africa's role in the global economy. "This summit is a game changer," said Dr. Akinwumi Adesina, former president of the African Development Bank Group. "It will attract global capital to unlock value in Africa's assets and create opportunities for our people."

The job of preparing assets for investment has already begun. The inaugural summit will be held in Luanda, Angola, in 2027, with participation from heads of state, global investors, CEOs, and development partners.

Voices of Leadership: A Common Vision for Africa

African heads of state, global leaders, and CEOs emphasise the importance and opportunity of this time for the continent:

Mozambique's president, H.E. Daniel Chapo, stated, "Africa is redesigning how capital arrives, where it goes, and how it delivers results, rather than asking whether it will come." Our continent has tremendous sovereign assets, such as energy, infrastructure, land, water, minerals, and people capital. However, too many of these assets remain undervalued, poorly structured, and underleveraged—not because to a lack of potential, but because they have not been properly organised, controlled, and risk-mitigated to satisfy global investment standards. This is the gap that the Global Africa Investment Summit aims to fill.

Tanzania's president, H.E. Samia Suluhu Hassan, stated that investors prioritise trust in institutions, policy continuity, and reliable partners. Tanzania is determined to be such a partner. We value relationships based on credibility, accountability, and shared success. In this environment, the Global Africa Investment Summit marks a significant shift—from fragmented participation to structured collaboration; from aid dependence to investment-led growth; and from isolated initiatives to bankable portfolios matched with national priorities.

Angola's president, H.E. João Manuel Gonçalves Lourenço, stated: "We are witnessing a great game changer on how to attract investment for Africa." Africa needs to unlock the potential of its sovereign assets to drive development and achieve the Africa 2063 goal. And this is exactly what the Global Africa Investment Summit will assist African countries with. This effort serves as an institutional bridge. We can connect Africa with global investors in a safe, sustainable, and mutually beneficial way."

Ghana's president, H.E. John Dramani Mahama, stated, "The global landscape is rapidly shifting." Geopolitical fragmentation, reorganisation of supply networks, the decline of traditional aid, and fierce competition for capital and resources necessitate a new approach. Africa cannot afford to remain a passive participant in the new global environment. The establishment of the Global Africa Investment Summit is more than just a symbolic gesture; it is a tangible step towards shaping how the world interacts with us.

H.E. William Ruto, president of the Republic of Kenya:

“The Global Africa Investment Summit is a timely, structured, credible platform that converts African assets into investment-ready portfolios and connects them to global capital because the next chapter of our development will not be financed by debt but by innovative monetization of African assets.”

H.E. Denis Sassou-Nguesso, President of the Republic of the Congo

"Africa's growth trajectory will only continue to improve if it can unleash the value of its considerable sovereign assets through international investment. The Global Africa Investment Summit is a realistic venue for achieving win-win agreements for the continent.

H.E. Giorgia Meloni, Prime Minister of the Republic of Italy, stated that Africa offers tremendous economic opportunities. The Global Africa Investment Summit is a one-of-a-kind opportunity to connect African assets with global capital. Italy's Mattei Plan for Africa is prepared to collaborate with the Global Africa Investment Summit to assist unlock investment prospects in Africa for economic growth, job creation, and shared prosperity."

Former UK Prime Minister Tony Blair praised Akinwumi Adesina and Margery Kraus, co-founders of the Global Africa Investment Summit, for their global reputation and trustworthiness. I am certain that the Global Africa Investment Summit will hasten the unlocking of Africa's tremendous assets. It will assist to change the continent's relationship with global investors."

Former UN Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon stated, "The world needs Africa, and Africa needs the world." With its abundant natural resources, Africa holds the key to world wealth and security. The Global Africa Investment Summit will bridge the gap between vision and execution, connecting governments' strategic priorities to resources and partnerships that can achieve long-term impact."

Aliko Dangote, founder and president of the Dangote Group, believes Africa's vast resources will shape the global energy revolution. The Global Africa Investment Summit establishes the framework and alliances required to realise their full potential, transforming assets into riches and prospects into bankable investments."

According to Robert Gumede, chairman and founder of the Guma Group, investing in Africa has the potential to generate considerable riches through global capital. The Global Africa Investment Summit platform provides investors and governments with the resources they need to plan and execute investment-grade transactions on a large scale and with confidence."

Mohammed Dewji, CEO of MeTL Group, highlights Africa's untapped potential and the hurdles faced by local entrepreneurs in accessing global funding. The Global Africa Investment Summit is a game-changing effort that will not only open up new prospects for African enterprises, but will also ensure that investments support long-term development, job creation, and economic expansion. I'm excited to make a difference as part of this platform."

This is not just a high-level investment platform—it reshapes the environment MSMEs operate in.

Expanded Market Opportunities
Large-scale investments in energy, infrastructure, agriculture, and digital sectors create downstream demand for local suppliers, contractors, service providers, and innovators—where MSMEs are best positioned to participate.

Improved Access to Capital Ecosystems
By raising governance and transparency standards, GAIS can attract development finance institutions and global investors whose presence often unlocks blended finance, subcontracting, and partnership opportunities for credible local enterprises.

Stronger Value Chain Integration
As sovereign assets are commercialised, MSMEs can integrate into regional and cross-border value chains in logistics, manufacturing, technology services, processing, and maintenance.

Infrastructure and Productivity Gains
Investment-driven growth typically accelerates improvements in power, transport, and digital infrastructure, lowering operating costs and expanding reach for small businesses.

Shift from Aid to Enterprise-Led Growth
GAIS signals a structural shift toward investment-led development. For MSMEs, this means growth driven by markets, demand, and competitiveness rather than short-term aid cycles.
African Development Bank Allocates $200 Million to Nigeria’s Climate-Smart Agriculture

The African Development Bank Group has approved a $200 million loan to support technology-driven and climate-smart agriculture in Nigeria. The financing will fund the second phase of the Federal Government’s National Agricultural Growth Scheme – Agro-Pocket (NAGS-AP), providing farmers nationwide with access to quality inputs, modern technologies, and data-driven farming methods. Scheduled to run for four years starting in March 2026, the programme aims to strengthen food security, improve agricultural productivity, and enhance value chains for priority crops.

The new funding builds on the Bank’s earlier African Emergency Food Production Facility, which helped address food insecurity challenges and laid the groundwork for scaling up agricultural interventions. Phase I of NAGS-AP delivered measurable results, including the cultivation of 118,000 hectares of wheat during the 2023/2024 dry season, a tripling of national wheat output to an estimated 500,000 metric tons in 2024, and direct support to roughly 650,000 smallholder farmers producing wheat, rice, cassava, maize, sorghum, and millet. Over 600 agro-dealers nationwide supplied certified seeds, fertilisers, and crop protection products, leveraging an ICT-based input distribution system that improved accessibility and efficiency.

Expanding Impact Through Climate-Smart Farming

The second phase aims to scale up these successes while placing a stronger emphasis on climate resilience and technological adoption. The programme will support five core pillars under Nigeria’s National Agricultural Technology and Innovation Policy framework: improving input access, strengthening value chains, revitalising extension services, promoting digital agriculture, and enhancing agricultural data systems. By integrating modern technologies with sustainable farming practices, the initiative seeks to increase wheat output fivefold and raise rice production by 20%.

Abdul Kamara, Director General for Nigeria at the African Development Bank, said the programme is designed to empower farmers to adopt digital tools and climate-smart methods, which are essential for improving productivity, building resilience against environmental shocks, and reducing reliance on food imports. He added that the programme will contribute to inclusive growth by creating opportunities for smallholder farmers and agribusinesses to expand, innovate, and integrate into national and regional value chains.

The $200 million AfDB financing underscores the importance of modern, climate-smart, and technology-driven agriculture in driving Nigeria’s food security and economic growth. By expanding access to inputs, digital tools, and resilient farming practices, the programme not only benefits smallholder farmers but also strengthens opportunities for MSMEs to integrate into more efficient, productive, and profitable agricultural value chains. Its focus on scale, sustainability, and innovation marks a significant step toward building a stronger and more inclusive agricultural sector in Nigeria.

For MSMEs in Nigeria’s agricultural and agri-processing sectors, this initiative represents both opportunity and guidance for strategic growth. By improving access to modern inputs, digital tools, and climate-resilient practices, the programme strengthens supply chains and stabilises production costs. This creates a more predictable environment for businesses involved in food processing, input distribution, and farm-to-market services. Enhanced productivity and value addition across key crops also offer opportunities for MSMEs to scale operations, expand into regional markets, and participate in emerging export opportunities. Ultimately, the programme aligns with broader economic goals by reducing import dependency, boosting local production, and fostering an ecosystem where small and medium enterprises can thrive sustainably.
CBN Inflation Expectations Index Dips as Energy and Exchange Rates Remain Top Concerns

Nigeria’s Central Bank of Nigeria inflation expectations index edged down to 41.4 points in January 2026 from 41.7 points in December 2025, signaling a slight improvement in how businesses and households perceive future price movements. The January Inflation Expectation Survey reflects a nuanced picture: while overall sentiment shows mild optimism, structural cost pressures remain elevated, particularly in energy, transportation, and foreign exchange. The survey offers a forward-looking lens for policymakers, investors, and businesses navigating a complex economic environment.

Insights from the January Survey

The decline in the index is largely driven by improved perceptions among businesses, while households continue to expect relatively high inflation. Key findings include:

  • Business sentiment: Overall business inflation perception fell from 48.3% in December to 45.2% in January. Microbusinesses reported the highest inflation expectations at 51.2%, reflecting their exposure to rising input and logistics costs, while small businesses were more resilient at 40.8%.

  • Household sentiment: Rural households maintained higher inflation expectations (55.0%) compared with urban households (53.9%), reflecting geographic disparities in access to goods, infrastructure, and energy.

  • Forward-looking expectations: Both businesses and households largely anticipate inflation to remain stable over the next one, three, and six months, suggesting that short-term expectations are contained despite persistent structural challenges.

Key Drivers Shaping Inflation Perceptions

The survey highlights several critical factors influencing expectations:

  • Energy costs: Fuel and electricity remain the most cited drivers of price perceptions. Rising energy costs directly increase production, distribution, and operational expenses across sectors.

  • Transportation: Higher fuel prices and vehicle operating costs continue to feed into consumer prices, particularly for goods that require extensive logistics or inter-state transport.

  • Exchange rate movements: Depreciation in parallel markets and volatility in official FX channels put pressure on businesses that rely on imported inputs and finished products, affecting pricing decisions.

  • Insecurity: Supply chain disruptions caused by insecurity, particularly in agricultural production zones, impact availability and cost of key commodities.

  • Interest rates: Elevated borrowing costs constrain business expansion and household purchasing power, influencing expectations of price stability.

These drivers underscore the interconnectedness of macroeconomic factors, with energy, transport, and currency dynamics directly shaping both operational costs for businesses and household consumption patterns.

While the January 2026 CBN Inflation Expectations Index shows only a modest decline, the survey offers valuable insights into the evolving dynamics of Nigeria’s economy. Energy prices, transportation costs, exchange rate fluctuations, insecurity, and interest rates continue to shape inflation perceptions, highlighting key operational and strategic risks for businesses and households alike. Understanding these drivers allows MSMEs to plan proactively, manage costs effectively, and navigate the challenges and opportunities presented by Nigeria’s shifting price environment.

For MSMEs operating in Nigeria, these inflation expectations offer crucial guidance for strategic decision-making. Slight moderation in the index suggests that businesses may plan for relatively stable short-term costs, yet the persistence of structural pressures requires careful risk management. Enterprises reliant on energy-intensive production, imported inputs, or logistics must monitor fuel and exchange rate trends closely, while agribusinesses should consider security and supply chain risks in cost projections. The survey reinforces the importance of forward-looking planning, as expectations often influence wage negotiations, pricing strategies, and investment timing. MSMEs that anticipate and adapt to these drivers can better stabilise operations, maintain margins, and seize opportunities in a market where cost pressures remain unevenly distributed.
Naira Gap Narrows as BDCs Prepare for Dollar Access
Official and parallel market convergence gains momentum amid renewed FX supply and positive policy signals.

The Naira’s gap between the official and parallel foreign exchange markets contracted further to ₦65 on Friday, reflecting growing convergence as Bureau De Change (BDC) operators prepare to access fresh dollar supply from banks. The spread has narrowed from ₦92 on Wednesday, supported by Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) reforms, positive communication, and underlying macroeconomic stability, including rising crude oil prices and strengthening external reserves, which reached $47.53 billion as of February 10, 2026.

The naira closed at ₦1,355.42 per dollar in the official market, up ₦10.77 week-on-week, while the parallel market rate strengthened by ₦10 to ₦1,420 per dollar. Analysts attribute the narrowing spread to both policy signalling and expectations of increased retail FX availability, highlighting a stabilising trend in Nigeria’s currency markets.

Operational and Regulatory Context

The convergence is partly driven by CBN measures allowing duly licensed BDCs to purchase foreign exchange directly from authorised dealer banks at prevailing market rates. Under the new framework:
