  • Licensed BDCs must complete full know-your-customer (KYC) and due diligence processes with banks.

  • Dollar sales are strictly for eligible retail transactions, with a weekly cap of $150,000 per bureau.

  • The framework aligns with broader efforts to formalise retail FX supply, promote transparency, and reduce speculative activity in the parallel market.

This follows the November 2025 rollout of revised licensing for 82 BDC operators, enhancing formalisation of retail FX operations and clarifying the role of BDCs in the CBN’s transmission mechanism. Market participants have responded positively, with BDCs proactively engaging banks ahead of dollar allocations.

Drivers Behind the Naira’s Performance

Several factors underpin the recent appreciation and convergence:

  • Policy communication: Forward guidance from the CBN has influenced pricing expectations, reducing speculative premiums.

  • Crude oil prices: Oil prices exceeding $70 per barrel toward January month-end supported external liquidity.

  • Exchange rate management: Slight naira depreciation in the official market was offset by gains week-on-week, reflecting disciplined interventions.

  • Market confidence: Positive sentiment among BDC operators and foreign investors, encouraged by clear operational guidelines, reinforces stability.

The contraction of the naira gap underscores the impact of regulatory clarity, policy signalling, and macroeconomic fundamentals in stabilising Nigeria’s FX market. As BDCs gain access to the official market, further convergence and improved price discovery are expected. For African MSMEs, these developments offer tangible benefits in cost management, planning, and market confidence, marking a positive step toward a more resilient and predictable financial environment.

A stable and converging FX market allows MSMEs to plan better, manage import costs, set predictable prices, and operate with greater financial confidence.  Reduced gaps between official and parallel rates also support cross-border trade and enhance investment planning.
Nigeria Inflation Drops to 15.10 Percent in January 2026 Amid Easing Price Pressures

Nigeria’s headline inflation declined slightly to 15.10 percent in January 2026, defying earlier analyst expectations of an increase following the recent Consumer Price Index normalisation. The moderation reflects easing food and core inflation pressures, improved supply conditions, relative currency stability, and softer demand conditions, suggesting a gradual easing in price pressures across key sectors of the economy.

Inflation Trend and Latest Data
Data released by the National Bureau of Statistics shows inflation edged down from 15.15 percent in December 2025 to 15.10 percent in January 2026, contrary to projections that inflation could rise toward 18 to 19 percent after the statistical base adjustment.

  • Food inflation slowed to about 8.89 percent.

  • Core inflation eased to roughly 17.72 percent.

  • Month on month inflation recorded negative 2.88 percent, indicating slower price increases compared with December.

The figures suggest improving price stability, particularly in essential consumer goods and services that strongly influence household spending patterns.

Drivers Behind the Decline
Several factors contributed to the moderation in inflationary pressure:

  • Post holiday demand normalisation supported softer consumer price growth.

  • Improved domestic supply conditions helped stabilise staple food prices.

  • Naira appreciation reduced imported cost pressures across manufacturing and retail sectors.

  • Energy price stability limited cost pass through to transport, logistics, and production.

Analysts at Meristem also noted sustained cooling in staple commodities such as maize and sorghum, although stronger export demand kept some oilseed prices relatively elevated.

Market Expectations and Analyst Perspectives
Prior to the data release, analysts at FBNQuest projected inflation could temporarily rise due to base effects. Despite the unexpected moderation, expectations remain for a gradual disinflation trend through 2026 if exchange rate stability, agricultural output improvements, and energy cost moderation are sustained.

Additional supportive factors include:

  • Petrol price stability linked to adjustments from the Dangote Refinery.

  • Naira appreciation of about 7.82 percent in January, averaging roughly N1,416.52 per dollar in the official market.

  • Reduced import related cost pressures supporting manufacturing and consumer goods sectors.


The latest inflation data suggests cautiously improving price stability in Nigeria. While structural economic risks remain, continued currency stability, improved agricultural supply, and stable energy costs could sustain gradual disinflation and support broader economic recovery.

Moderating inflation improves cost predictability, pricing stability, and investment planning for MSMEs. Easing food, energy, and import costs can strengthen margins, support consumer demand, and enhance business resilience across African markets.
Economic Reforms Drive Surge in Foreign Investment in Nigeria

Nigeria recorded its highest foreign capital inflows in five years during the first nine months of 2025, reflecting renewed investor confidence driven by foreign exchange reforms, improved liquidity conditions, and broader macroeconomic policy adjustments. The recovery marks a notable shift after several years of subdued inflows and signals gradual re engagement of global investors with Africa’s largest economy.

Investment Trends and Capital Composition
Data from the National Bureau of Statistics shows total capital importation for the period exceeded the entire 2024 inflow of $12.32 billion and far surpassed the $3.90 billion recorded during the same period in 2023.

  • Foreign Portfolio Investment dominated inflows, rising more than 226 percent year on year to approximately $14.25 billion.

  • Foreign Direct Investment remained relatively modest at about $565 million, highlighting ongoing caution toward long term fixed investments.

  • Higher domestic interest rates created attractive yields that encouraged short term offshore capital participation.

This pattern suggests stronger financial market confidence, though portfolio flows typically remain more sensitive to global monetary shifts and policy signals.

Reform Measures Driving Investor Sentiment
The rebound in capital inflows is closely linked to policy reforms initiated since 2023 aimed at restoring macroeconomic stability and investor confidence.

  • Removal of costly fuel subsidies helped improve fiscal sustainability.

  • Liberalisation of the foreign exchange market improved transparency and access to foreign currency.

  • Ongoing tax reforms are designed to enhance compliance, revenue mobilisation, and investor clarity.

These measures have strengthened perceptions of economic policy direction, improved liquidity conditions, and reduced uncertainty for international investors assessing entry into Nigerian markets.

Sectoral Distribution and Sources of Capital
Financial services remained the primary destination for imported capital, reflecting continued interest in Nigeria’s banking and financing sectors.

  • The banking sector recorded the largest inflows, exceeding $3 billion in the third quarter of 2025.

  • Financing and related services followed with substantial investment activity.

  • Major inflows originated from the United Kingdom and the United States, underscoring sustained engagement from established global financial centres.

Among financial institutions, Standard Chartered Bank Nigeria Limited, Stanbic IBTC Bank Plc, and Citibank Nigeria Limited recorded notable capital inflows, reflecting continued investor interest in Nigeria’s banking ecosystem.

The surge in foreign capital inflows highlights improving investor sentiment toward Nigeria’s reform trajectory and macroeconomic outlook. Sustaining this momentum will likely depend on policy consistency, exchange rate stability, and stronger real sector investment capable of translating financial inflows into sustainable economic growth, job creation, and industrial expansion.

Stronger foreign investment inflows can stabilise financial markets, improve access to capital, and support economic confidence. For African MSMEs, this can translate into better financing conditions, increased business opportunities, and a more predictable operating environment.
Kenya Commercial Banks Adjust Lending Rates Following Central Bank Rate Cut

Kenya Commercial Banks have adjusted their lending rates in response to the recent cut in the Central Bank Rate. This adjustment reflects changes in monetary policy aiming to stimulate borrowing and economic activity.

Leading commercial banks in Kenya lowered their base lending rates following the Central Bank of Kenya's (CBK) reduction of the Central Bank Rate (CBR) from 9.00% to 8.75%. This 25 basis point cut, part of a ten-consecutive reduction strategy, aims to stimulate credit growth as inflation declines to 4.4%, fostering economic activity. Major banks have updated their variable-rate loan pricing to the new benchmark. New loans from mid-February will use this rate plus a risk margin, while existing loans will adjust after a 30-day notice. The rate cuts are expected to lower borrowing costs, enhance access to affordable credit for businesses and consumers, and support economic expansion and resilience for small and medium-sized enterprises.

Leading commercial banks in Kenya have lowered their base lending rates after the Central Bank of Kenya (CBK) reduced the Central Bank Rate (CBR) from 9.00 percent to 8.75 percent this week. The adjustment reflects the central bank’s continued efforts to stimulate credit growth and reduce borrowing costs across the economy.

Central Bank Rate Reduction


The CBK’s Monetary Policy Committee announced a 25 basis point cut, marking the tenth consecutive reduction, aimed at encouraging credit expansion and supporting economic activity. The decision follows a decline in inflation to 4.4 percent in January, providing the central bank with room to maintain a supportive monetary stance. Analysts note that the cut is part of a broader strategy to sustain economic momentum amid stable price conditions and moderate global shocks.

Bank Responses


Major lenders, including KCB Bank Kenya, Equity Bank Kenya, NCBA Bank Kenya, and Family Bank Kenya, have updated their variable-rate loan pricing to align with the new 8.75 percent benchmark under the Risk-Based Credit Pricing Model.

  • New variable-rate loans issued from mid-February will be charged at the base rate plus a customer-specific risk margin.

  • Existing loans will have their benchmarks adjusted following a mandatory 30-day notice period.

  • Older facilities will transition to the updated pricing system by March 2026

The rate cuts are expected to reduce borrowing costs for both businesses and consumers. Monthly repayments for variable-rate loans will depend on individual risk margins and loan arrangements, giving entrepreneurs more flexibility in managing debt and operational cash flow. Lower borrowing costs may also encourage investment in expansion projects, technology adoption, and working capital improvements.

The continued reduction in Kenya’s base lending rates signals a supportive lending environment and provides businesses with greater access to affordable credit. By easing financial pressures on entrepreneurs, the policy may strengthen business resilience, encourage formal sector growth, and improve access to financing for small and medium-sized enterprises.

Lower lending rates can ease financing costs and improve access to credit for small and medium-sized enterprises. Businesses may leverage this opportunity to restructure loans, invest in growth, and strengthen cash flow management.
Afreximbank Backs $100 Million Dollars Road Infrastructure Upgrade in The Bahamas
Afreximbank is supporting a $100 million update to The Bahamas' road infrastructure.

The African Export–Import Bank (Afreximbank) has approved a $100 million Receivables Discounting Facility for Bahamas Striping Group of Companies Ltd. (BSGC), facilitating the rehabilitation and construction of over 200 kilometers of road infrastructure in The Bahamas. This financing is part of Afreximbank's Global Africa strategy and aims to enhance trade-enabling infrastructure. Issued under a Framework Agreement with the Bahamian government in 2025, the facility allows BSGC to access advance funding against government contracts, enabling faster project execution. The upgrades are expected to improve logistics efficiency, tourism access, and community connectivity, while also supporting local employment and supply chains.

The African Export–Import Bank (Afreximbank) has approved a $100 million Receivables Discounting Facility for Bahamas Striping Group of Companies Ltd. (BSGC) to support the rehabilitation and construction of over 200 kilometres of critical road infrastructure across The Bahamas. The financing forms part of Afreximbank’s Global Africa strategy and is aimed at strengthening trade-enabling infrastructure in strategic partner regions.

Transaction Overview

The $100 million facility was issued under a Framework Agreement signed with the Government of The Bahamas in 2025 on the sidelines of the 48th CARICOM Conference of Heads of Government in Bridgetown, Barbados. Structured as a receivables discounting arrangement, the facility allows BSGC to access advance funding against certified government contracts, enabling faster project execution and improved cash flow management. Afreximbank acted as the lender, financing completed and approved works such as asphalt paving, road striping, safety enhancements, and ongoing road maintenance across multiple islands within the Bahamian archipelago.

Strategic Significance

According to Afreximbank’s Acting COO for CARICOM, Okechukwu Ihejirika, the transaction underscores the Bank’s commitment to supporting Global Africa partners through innovative structured finance solutions that enable sustainable development, boost trade, and strengthen cross-regional economic linkages.

Local Economic Impact

The road infrastructure upgrades are expected to enhance:

  • logistics efficiency

  •  tourism access 

  •  and community connectivity;

while supporting employment and empowering local supply chains. BSGC, an indigenous Bahamian-owned company founded in 2010, has completed over 200 projects across Nassau, Exuma, and Eleuthera and employs over 300 people, including subcontractors.

This transaction demonstrates how receivables-based financing can unlock working capital for infrastructure and service-oriented businesses. It also highlights Afreximbank’s expanding Global Africa mandate, creating indirect opportunities for African MSMEs in cross-border partnerships, subcontracting, and knowledge transfer within infrastructure value chains.
BRVM Stocks Rally as Regional Market Hits Multi-Year Highs
BRVM stocks rally as regional market reaches multi-year highs.

NDPC Launches Investigation into Temu Over Alleged Data Protection Breaches

The National Data Protection Commission (NDPC) has launched an investigation into e-commerce platform Temu over potential violations of Nigeria’s Data Protection Act, focusing on data collection, transparency, and cross-border transfers. The probe highlights the growing regulatory scrutiny on foreign digital platforms and the need for strict compliance with local data protection laws. For African entrepreneurs and MSMEs, this underscores the importance of safeguarding user data to maintain trust, avoid legal risks, and support sustainable digital growth.

The National Data Protection Commission (NDPC) has ordered an immediate probe into the e-commerce platform Temu following concerns that its data handling practices may violate Nigeria’s Data Protection Act (NDPA). The investigation underscores Nigeria’s growing focus on digital compliance and the regulatory expectations for foreign platforms operating in the country.


Vincent Olatunji, NDPC National Commissioner and CEO, revealed that initial inquiries flagged potential breaches in several areas, including:

  • Excessive online surveillance and data collection beyond necessity

  • Weak accountability and governance mechanisms for personal data

  • Insufficient transparency in how user information is stored, used, and shared

  • Duty of care lapses in protecting sensitive user data

  • Unauthorized cross-border transfers of personal data

Temu reportedly manages information for approximately 12.7 million Nigerian users and 70 million daily active users globally. The size and scope of its operations mean any breach could have widespread implications for users, regulators, and the broader e-commerce ecosystem.

Regulatory Implications
The NDPC cautioned that processors acting on behalf of data controllers who fail to verify compliance with the NDPA may face enforcement actions. Babatunde Bamigboye, Head of Legal, Enforcement & Regulations at NDPC, emphasized that non-compliance could result in fines, suspension of operations, or other legal measures. This reinforces the need for companies, especially foreign operators, to implement robust compliance programs that align with Nigeria’s data protection laws.

Industry Context
The move comes amid growing scrutiny of foreign e-commerce platforms handling sensitive consumer data. Cross-border data flows, combined with the rapid expansion of online marketplaces in Nigeria, have raised concerns over privacy, security, and accountability. Regulators are increasingly focused on ensuring that digital businesses adhere to international best practices, particularly in protecting users’ personal and financial information.

Temu has yet to respond to the NDPC directive. Analysts note that the outcome could influence broader regulatory behavior in Nigeria, potentially affecting investment decisions, platform credibility, and operational models for digital businesses. The investigation also serves as a signal to African startups and MSMEs that data protection is not only a legal requirement but also a competitive differentiator in the digital economy.

In conclusion, the NDPC’s probe into Temu highlights the critical role of data protection in Nigeria’s digital economy. Ensuring compliance with the Data Protection Act is not only a legal obligation but also a strategic advantage for businesses seeking consumer trust and long-term growth.

African entrepreneurs and digital businesses must prioritise data protection to avoid legal risks and maintain consumer trust. Compliance with data privacy standards enhances credibility, supports long-term growth, and ensures that businesses can safely leverage technology for scaling operations.
Nigeria’s Grid Crisis Deepens as More Companies Turn to Private Power
Nigeria's grid crisis worsens as more companies switch to private power.

Nigeria's unreliable electricity supply is prompting many businesses to rely on self-generated power, leading to increased production costs and financial strain in the power sector. Frequent grid failures, highlighted by two nationwide blackouts in a short span, underscore structural weaknesses in the electricity system. Over 60% of businesses had already reduced their reliance on the grid by early 2025, investing in diesel generators, gas plants, and solar energy. This shift threatens Nigeria's economic reform agenda, with the Manufacturers Association calling for a forensic audit of the grid. Experts warn that the exit of large consumers could worsen financial challenges for the power sector, increase prices, and undermine investor confidence without significant infrastructure investment and governance reforms.

Nigeria’s unreliable electricity supply is pushing more businesses to abandon the national grid in favour of self-generated power, raising concerns about rising production costs, weakened industrial competitiveness, and financial strain across the country’s power sector. Frequent grid collapses and prolonged outages are increasingly forcing companies to rely on diesel generators, gas plants, and solar solutions to maintain operations.

Rising Grid Failures Undermining Industrial Confidence

Persistent disruptions in Nigeria’s transmission network continue to affect manufacturing and commercial activity, with the latest nationwide blackout reported as the second within two weeks. Although authorities confirmed power restoration, stakeholders argue that the frequency of outages highlights deep structural weaknesses in the electricity system.

Between January and September 2025, at least 20 companies reportedly disconnected from the national grid due to unreliable supply and repeated system failures. The trend threatens Nigeria’s broader economic reform agenda, which identifies improved power supply as a key driver of growth.

Businesses Increasingly Shifting to Captive Power

Companies across sectors are rapidly reducing dependence on public electricity:

  • More than 60% of Nigerian businesses had already scaled back grid reliance by early 2025.

  • Firms are investing heavily in diesel generators, gas-fired plants, and solar energy systems.

  • Some regions experience outages lasting days or even weeks, disrupting production cycles.

  • Large industrial users cite high tariffs combined with poor supply reliability as a major concern.

The Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN) has called for an independent forensic audit of the grid infrastructure and management framework, warning that frequent collapses are eroding the competitiveness of local industries.

Financial Risks to Nigeria’s Power Sector

Experts caution that the exit of large electricity consumers could worsen liquidity challenges within the Nigerian Electricity Supply Industry (NESI). Industrial customers generate significant revenue for the sector, and their departure may deepen existing debt and operational inefficiencies.

Stakeholders also warn that:

  • Reduced industrial demand could force generation companies to cut output.

  • Higher private energy costs will likely translate into increased prices for goods and services.

  • Investor confidence in Nigeria’s economy could weaken if power reliability does not improve.

Consumer advocacy groups argue that without major infrastructure investment and governance reforms, the country risks a broader electricity market crisis.

Unreliable electricity increases operating costs, reduces productivity, and limits growth opportunities for small businesses. As more firms move toward private energy solutions, MSMEs may need to explore alternative power options, energy efficiency strategies, and cooperative energy models to remain competitive in challenging operating environments.
AfCFTA Could Add $1.4 Trillion to Africa’s Economy by 2045 — Ruto
AfCFTA may add $1.4 trillion to Africa's economy by 2045, says Ruto.

Africa's largest trade agreement, the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA), is progressing towards implementation to enhance economic opportunities. At a recent meeting in Addis Ababa, leaders, including Kenya's President William Ruto, stressed the importance of executing the agreement to benefit Africa's 1.4 billion citizens. They highlighted potentials such as a $3 trillion increase in intra-African trade and a $1.4 trillion GDP boost by 2045. A new leadership committee will oversee these efforts to improve coordination and accountability, focusing on private sector involvement, especially small businesses and entrepreneurs, to foster economic growth and innovation.

Africa’s largest trade agreement is entering a critical phase as leaders call for faster implementation to unlock economic opportunities across the continent. Speaking at a high-level meeting, William Ruto emphasised that the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) must move beyond negotiations toward practical execution to deliver tangible benefits for businesses, industries, and citizens across Africa’s 1.4 billion-person market.

The remarks were made during the inaugural committee meeting on AfCFTA implementation held in Addis Ababa, with participation from multiple African leaders and the AfCFTA Secretariat.

AfCFTA’s Economic Potential Remains Massive

Leaders highlighted the transformative impact the agreement could deliver if effectively implemented:

  • Potential to increase intra-African trade by up to $3 trillion

  • Projected to boost Africa’s combined GDP by about $1.4 trillion between 2021 and 2045

  • Expected to accelerate industrialisation, value addition, and regional supply chains

  • Designed to create a more competitive and resilient continental market

The agreement aims to integrate African economies by reducing trade barriers and improving market access across borders.

New Leadership Committee to Drive Execution

A newly established committee of African heads of state will oversee implementation efforts, with Kenya’s president serving as chair and Botswana’s president as deputy. The group’s mandate includes:

  • Strengthening coordination between governments and regional economic communities

  • Ensuring accountability and measurable progress

  • Aligning national policies with AfCFTA trade instruments

  • Promoting collaboration with the private sector

Leaders stressed that sustained political commitment will be essential to turn policy frameworks into real economic outcomes.

Private Sector and MSMEs at the Centre of the Strategy

The initiative places strong emphasis on private sector participation, particularly small businesses, manufacturers, and entrepreneurs. Policymakers acknowledged that Africa’s private sector already drives the majority of employment, investment, and production across the continent.

Key priorities include:

  • Making cross-border trade easier for MSMEs

  • Reducing regulatory and logistical barriers

  • Supporting youth- and women-led enterprises

  • Encouraging innovation and manufacturing growth within Africa

Effective AfCFTA implementation could open access to larger regional markets, reduce trade costs, and create new export opportunities for small businesses. For MSMEs, this means greater potential for growth, partnerships, and scaling beyond domestic borders within a unified African marketplace.
Naira Strengthens to N1,390 on Black Market as FX Gap Narrows 2.5%

The naira strengthened sharply to N1,390 per dollar on Monday in the black market, reflecting continued convergence between Nigeria’s parallel and official foreign exchange markets. The move reduced the gap between the parallel market and the official FX window by 2.5 percent, signalling increased market confidence amid recent Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) interventions and improved macroeconomic fundamentals.

Recent Market Movements
Data from street traders showed that the naira appreciated by 2.16 percent on Monday morning, gaining N30 from N1,420 quoted on Friday in the parallel market. On Friday, the gap had already decreased to N65 as Bureau De Change (BDC) operators prepared to access fresh dollar supply from banks. This followed the CBN’s reopening of the retail FX window for BDCs, part of broader reforms aimed at formalising the FX market and enhancing liquidity at the retail level. Meanwhile, the official market recorded a marginal depreciation, with the naira slipping by N1.76 to N1,355.42 per dollar on Friday, reflecting minor daily volatility in line with normal market adjustments.

Factors Supporting the Naira
Several drivers underpin the recent strengthening and convergence trend:

  1. Policy interventions: The CBN’s reopening of the retail FX window and clearer operational guidelines for BDCs have improved confidence and reduced speculative premiums.

  2. Market sentiment: Positive expectations from traders and investors have encouraged early adjustments ahead of actual dollar allocations.

  3. Macroeconomic fundamentals: Rising crude oil prices, coupled with stronger external reserves, have reinforced the naira’s stability and provided liquidity support for the FX market.

Reduced parallel market distortions: As BDCs gain access to official supply, reliance on the unregulated market diminishes, curbing extreme fluctuations in the black market rate.

The narrowing of the naira gap underscores the effectiveness of regulatory clarity, policy signalling, and favourable macroeconomic conditions in stabilising Nigeria’s FX market. Continued convergence is expected as BDCs fully tap into the official window, providing more predictable pricing and supporting smoother capital flows.

A stabilising naira and narrowing FX gap enhance predictability in import and export costs, enabling MSMEs to plan operations, manage budgets, and make informed pricing decisions. Reliable access to official FX supply also reduces exposure to black market volatility, supporting sustainable business growth.

  • Performance in US Dollars: In US dollar terms, the increase is even more pronounced at 27.7%.

  • Long-Term Growth: Over the past 12 months, the shares have skyrocketed by 81.3%, with an astonishing 70.6% gain recorded in just the last six months.

  • Trading Activity: The bank has become the most actively traded stock on the BRVM in the past three months, boasting a market capitalization of approximately 524 billion XOF, which accounts for 3.51% of the exchange.

Financial Performance and Balance Sheet Strength

The recent rally in Ecobank's share price can be attributed to several key factors, particularly improved earnings and robust balance sheet fundamentals:

  • Revenue Growth: The bank reported trailing twelve-month revenue of $1.96 billion, reflecting an 11.35% increase year-on-year.

  • Net Income: Ecobank's net income reached $411.34 million, indicating strong profitability.

  • Total Assets: Total assets stood at an impressive $32.4 billion, showcasing the bank's substantial asset base.

  • Liquidity Position: The bank holds $4.3 billion in cash against $1.9 billion in debt, underscoring its enhanced liquidity and disciplined capital management.

  • Return on Equity: With a return on equity of 29.39%, Ecobank demonstrates effective capital deployment and operational efficiency.

Valuation and Market Context

Despite its strong financial performance, Ecobank continues to trade at relatively low valuation multiples:

  • Price-to-Earnings Ratio: The bank has a price-to-earnings ratio of 1.80, which is significantly lower than many of its peers.

  • Price-to-Sales Ratio: With a price-to-sales ratio of 0.39, this further indicates that regional banking stocks are undervalued compared to counterparts in other emerging markets, where banks typically trade at earnings multiples ranging from six to ten times.

Implications for Regional Markets

Ecobank’s recent rally reflects a broader trend of repricing banking stocks on the BRVM. This shift is attributed to:

  • Improved Cost Management: Banks are managing their costs more effectively, enhancing profitability.

  • Higher Asset Quality: Strengthened asset quality has increased investor confidence.

  • Stronger Capital Buffers: Following previous currency and credit cycles, banks have fortified their capital positions.

  • Increased Liquidity: Enhanced liquidity on the exchange has facilitated better price discovery.

  • Stable Dividend Expectations: With more predictable dividends, banks are increasingly viewed as income-generating and value-focused investments rather than speculative assets.

Stronger banks with healthier balance sheets are better positioned to extend credit, support trade finance, and finance MSME growth. As capital markets mature and banking fundamentals improve, African MSMEs benefit from more stable financial institutions, improved access to financing, and a more resilient regional financial ecosystem.
Gourmet Egypt Shares Surge on EGX Debut After $28M IPO
Gourmet Egypt.com Food: Initial Public Offering Marks a New Era for Egypt’s Equity Market

Gourmet Egypt Food has successfully launched its initial public offering (IPO) on the Egyptian Exchange, officially listed on February 10, 2026. The IPO, which combined a public offer with a private placement, raised around 1.31 billion Egyptian pounds (approximately US$28 million) through the issuance of 190.5 million shares, accounting for 47.6% of the company's total share capital, thus valuing the company at about 2.76 billion Egyptian pounds. Strong demand, particularly from institutional and qualified investors, led to the oversubscription of shares, with the stock rising nearly 24% on its debut day. This listing is seen as a vital boost to Egypt's capital market, which is undergoing revitalization, and highlights the appeal of food and consumer staples in emerging markets. Future performance will rely on profit growth, input cost management, and consumer purchasing power amidst challenges like food inflation.

Gourmet Egypt Food, a notable agri-food company, has made a significant entrance onto the Egyptian Exchange following the successful completion of its initial public offering (IPO). This IPO, which creatively combined a public offer with a private placement, was officially listed on February 10, 2026. This milestone not only represents a pivotal moment for Gourmet Egypt but also signifies a resurgence of activity within Egypt’s primary equity market, which has been working towards revitalization.

IPO Structure and Pricing

The structure of the IPO was meticulously designed to optimize capital raising while ensuring robust investor participation. The offering comprised a total of 190.5 million shares, which accounts for approximately 47.6% of the company’s total share capital. Each share was priced at 6.90 Egyptian pounds, leading to a total capital raise of around 1.31 billion Egyptian pounds, equivalent to approximately US$28 million. At the time of listing, this valuation positioned Gourmet Egypt at about 2.76 billion Egyptian pounds.

Following the IPO, Gourmet Egypt now boasts 400 million shares outstanding, marking a significant expansion in its shareholder base and enhancing its visibility in the market. This strategic move is expected to facilitate future growth and investment opportunities for the company.

Investor Demand and Market Response

Prior to the listing, the Financial Regulatory Authority granted approval for the offering, setting the stage for a successful debut. The demand for shares was exceptionally strong, particularly within the institutional and qualified investor tranche, which was significantly oversubscribed. This oversubscription is a clear indicator of robust investor confidence and enthusiasm for the company’s prospects.

Retail investors also exhibited considerable interest in the public tranche, further reflecting a broad-based appeal among various investor demographics. On its inaugural day of trading, Gourmet Egypt’s stock experienced a remarkable surge, climbing nearly 24% in early sessions. This impressive performance underscores the positive sentiment surrounding food and consumer staples companies on the exchange, suggesting that investors are increasingly optimistic about the sector’s potential.

Market Context and Outlook

The successful listing of Gourmet Egypt adds vital momentum to the ongoing recovery of Egypt’s capital market. In recent times, market authorities have been actively working to deepen market participation and attract foreign investments, aiming to foster a more dynamic and inclusive financial environment.

Food and consumer staples businesses, like Gourmet Egypt, typically resonate well with investors in emerging markets. These sectors are often seen as relatively defensive and stable, benefiting from consistent demand regardless of economic fluctuations.

However, the sustainability of Gourmet Egypt’s performance will depend on several critical factors, including:

  • Profit Growth: Continued expansion in profit margins is essential for maintaining investor interest and ensuring long-term viability.

  • Input Cost Management: Effectively managing input costs will be crucial, especially in the context of fluctuating commodity prices that can impact profitability.

  • Consumer Purchasing Power: In an environment where food inflation has posed significant challenges, understanding and adapting to shifts in consumer purchasing power will be imperative for sustained success.

Successful IPOs signal improving investor confidence and healthier capital markets, which benefit MSMEs through stronger financing ecosystems. For food and agri-business MSMEs, the listing highlights growing investor appetite for resilient consumer sectors and underscores the potential of scaling operations toward public or institutional funding as markets mature.
South Africa Expands Diesel Refunds to 100% for Key Industries from April 2026
Policy change aims to reduce fuel cost pressures for farming, forestry, and mining businesses

The South African Revenue Service (SARS) has announced that from April 1, 2026, onland diesel users in the farming, forestry, and mining sectors will be able to claim 100% of eligible diesel used in qualifying activities, an increase from the previous 80%. This change follows a proposal by Minister of Finance Enoch Godongwana in the 2025 Budget, aimed at providing relief after a fuel levy hike. The move is projected to cost R1 billion in refunds. SARS is updating its systems to reflect this new rate from the May 2026 VAT return, ensuring claimants are not disadvantaged during the transition. The revenue service has also initiated digital modernization efforts for the Diesel Refund System to improve efficiency and reduce fraud. Existing filing regulations remain unchanged, including the requirement to file claims within two years of purchase and maintain records for five years.

The South African Revenue Service (SARS) has announced a major adjustment to diesel refund claims that will allow eligible on-land users in the farming, forestry, and mining sectors to claim back 100% of qualifying diesel usage from April 1, 2026. The increase from the previous 80% claim rate follows a policy decision introduced in the 2025 national budget by Finance Minister Enoch Godongwana, aimed at cushioning industries affected by rising fuel levies.

What the Policy Change Means

The Diesel Refund Scheme enables qualifying businesses to recover part or all of the general fuel levy and Road Accident Fund (RAF) levy paid on diesel used for approved activities.

Key updates include:

  • Claim rate increasing from 80% to 100% of eligible diesel purchases and use

  • Effective date: April 1, 2026 (reflected in VAT returns submitted from May 2026)

  • Estimated fiscal cost to government: R1 billion in additional refunds

  • Applies to on-land users in agriculture, forestry, and mining sectors

The adjustment is designed to align the scheme with its original policy objective of supporting production sectors facing high operating costs.

System Modernisation and Claims Process Improvements

SARS has also been upgrading the diesel refund system to improve efficiency and reduce fraud risks:

  • A new standalone digital platform launched in December 2025 for registration and claims

  • Separation from the traditional VAT system to streamline processing

  • Improved automation and risk profiling for faster approvals

Authorities confirmed that claimants will not be disadvantaged during system upgrades, as VAT submissions are typically due later in the following month.

Compliance Requirements Remain in Place

Despite the increased refund rate, existing compliance rules still apply:

  • Claims must be submitted within two years of diesel purchase

  • Businesses must retain logbooks and purchase records for five years

  • Records must clearly distinguish eligible and non-eligible diesel usage

  • Incorrect claims may result in repayment, penalties, and interest

These measures are intended to maintain transparency and accountability while expanding financial relief.

Lower fuel costs can significantly improve profitability for small and medium enterprises operating in agriculture, logistics, and resource value chains. The policy also signals how targeted tax incentives can support productive sectors, offering a model other African countries may adopt to strengthen MSME competitiveness.
AfDB President Debuts Strategic Vision at AU Summit to Boost Africa’s Financing and Infrastructure
New “Four Cardinal Points” agenda aims to close Africa’s funding gap and accelerate development priorities

Dr. Sidi Ould Tah, President of the Bank Group, will lead a delegation to the African Union's 39th Assembly in Addis Ababa, marking his first visit since his presidency began on September 1, 2025. He aims to reinforce the Bank Group’s commitment to African priorities and present the "Four Cardinal Points" strategy, which focuses on bridging the funding gap, ensuring financial sovereignty, harnessing the demographic dividend, and building resilient infrastructure. The Bank's New African Financial Architecture (NAFA) will support these initiatives. Notably, the African Development Fund (ADF) recently achieved $11 billion in pledges for its replenishment. During the Summit, Dr. Ould Tah will connect these priorities with the AU's Agenda 2063 and engage in bilateral talks to strengthen strategic partnerships. Events will include discussions on health financing and nutrition, with social media coverage of the Bank's initiatives at the Summit.

The President of the African Development Bank Group, Sidi Ould Tah, is leading a high-level delegation to African Union headquarters in Addis Ababa for the AU’s 39th Assembly of Heads of State and Government. The visit marks his first participation in the summit since assuming office in September 2025 and signals the Bank’s renewed commitment to financing Africa’s structural transformation and long-term growth.

The Bank’s New Strategic Direction: Four Cardinal Points

At the summit, the AfDB President is presenting a new strategy called the “Four Cardinal Points", designed to tackle Africa’s most pressing development challenges:

  • Bridging Africa’s financing gap

  • Strengthening financial sovereignty across countries

  • Leveraging Africa’s demographic dividend

  • Building resilient infrastructure through value addition of natural resources

The strategy aligns closely with the AU’s long-term development blueprint, Agenda 2063, and positions the Bank as a central driver of continental transformation.

New African Financial Architecture (NAFA) to Mobilise Capital

A key pillar of the strategy is the New African Financial Architecture (NAFA), a framework aimed at mobilising large-scale funding to support economic growth and infrastructure development across Africa.

Progress under this approach already includes:

  • A historic $11 billion replenishment of the African Development Fund (ADF)

  • Contributions from 24 African countries, including 19 first-time contributors

  • Increased support for low-income and fragile economies across the continent

This reflects growing African ownership and participation in financing development initiatives.

High-Level Engagements and Strategic Partnerships

Beyond the main summit sessions, the Bank will participate in several major continental initiatives and bilateral meetings with global partners.

Key engagements include:

  • Presentation of the 10th African Visa Openness Index

  • Health financing discussions with Africa CDC leaders

  • Participation in the Italy–Africa Summit reviewing the Mattei Plan

  • The African Leaders for Nutrition high-level dinner focused on child nutrition

These engagements aim to strengthen partnerships and mobilise additional resources for Africa’s priority sectors.

Expanded development financing and infrastructure investment can unlock new opportunities for MSMEs through improved access to markets, funding, and supply chains. Stronger regional financial systems and industrial growth also create a more supportive environment for small businesses to scale and compete globally.
Swiss Private Banking Giant Pictet Enters South Africa as Global Banks Reshape African Presence
New market entry signals rising wealth opportunities and evolving financial competition across Africa

Banque Pictet & Cie has opened its first office in South Africa, marking its entry into the African market after 220 years. The South African Reserve Bank authorized this expansion, coinciding with a projected 65% rise in African millionaires over the next decade. Meanwhile, Revolut is also entering the market, seeking a banking licence to enhance its services and grow its client base significantly. In contrast, international banks like HSBC and BNP Paribas are withdrawing operations from South Africa, facilitating client transitions to local institutions like FirstRand. This reflects a shifting landscape in the financial sector, as some banks exit while others seek growth opportunities in the region.

Swiss private banking group Banque Pictet & Cie has opened its first African office in South Africa, marking a historic expansion into the continent after more than 220 years of operations. The move follows regulatory approval from the Prudential Authority under the South African Reserve Bank, positioning the Geneva-based institution to serve high-net-worth individuals and institutional clients in one of Africa’s largest financial markets.

With approximately 724 billion Swiss francs (about R15 trillion) in assets under management by the end of 2024, the Pictet Group is one of Switzerland’s largest financial institutions and Europe’s biggest privately owned financial firm.

Why South Africa? Growing Wealth and Financial Opportunity

The expansion reflects expectations that Africa’s wealthy population will grow significantly in the coming years. Wealth forecasts suggest the number of African millionaires could rise by 65% over the next decade, increasing demand for sophisticated wealth management and investment services.

Key facts about the new entry:

  • First African presence in Pictet’s history

  • Representative office approval granted by South African regulators

  • Focus on wealth management, asset management, and asset servicing

  • Target clients include high-net-worth individuals and institutional investors

Fintech Challenger Revolut Also Targets the Market

Pictet’s entry coincides with expansion plans by global fintech firm Revolut, which has applied for a banking licence in South Africa as part of its strategy to grow its customer base from 65 million to 100 million by 2027.

Revolut views South Africa as a strategic gateway into Africa due to:

  • A diversified economy

  • Rising digital adoption

  • Growing demand for innovative financial services

The company aims to position itself as a leading digital banking platform for millions of African users.

Global Banks Exit While New Players Enter

The evolving banking landscape also includes exits by several multinational banks:

  • HSBC exited South Africa after 30 years, transferring operations to FirstRand

  • BNP Paribas closed its corporate banking operations in 2024

  • Other global lenders, including Barclays and Standard Chartered, have reduced African exposure

This shift highlights a transition where traditional banks scale back while niche financial players and fintech firms expand.

Greater financial competition can improve access to funding, investment products, and digital banking tools for African businesses. As new global players enter African markets, MSMEs may benefit from more innovative financial solutions, partnerships, and cross-border investment opportunities.
Nigerian Defense-Tech Startup Terra Raises $22M Follow-On Round to Scale Security Solutions Across Africa
Rapid funding momentum positions the company as Africa’s most capitalised military technology startup

Terra Industries, a Nigerian defense-technology startup specializing in drone manufacture and location monitoring software, has secured $22 million in funding shortly after an $11.8 million round, led by Lux Capital with contributions from existing and new investors. The funds will enhance production, accelerate deployments across Africa, and recruit experts in key locations. Co-founder Nathan Nwachuku emphasized the necessity of addressing insecurity in Africa for its industrial growth. Terra aims to become the continent's leading military technology provider, offering locally integrated solutions for security challenges in infrastructure, which is crucial given Africa's vast mineral resources and significant investment in vulnerable areas. Founded in 2024, Terra develops various autonomous defense technologies linked through its proprietary ArtemisOS, with claims of managing assets worth $11 billion.

Nigerian defense-technology startup Terra Industries has secured an additional $22 million in funding just one month after raising $11.8 million, marking one of the fastest follow-on investment rounds in Africa’s startup ecosystem. The new capital will support production expansion, deployments across multiple African countries, and recruitment of senior engineering and commercial talent in Africa, London, and San Francisco.

The round was led by Lux Capital, with participation from returning investors 8VC, Nova Global, and Silent Ventures, alongside new backers including funds linked to Olugbenga Agboola, CEO of Flutterwave, as well as angel investors and global personalities.

Africa’s Security Gap Drives Investment Interest

Terra’s rapid funding reflects growing demand for locally built security infrastructure across Africa, where critical assets such as mines, power facilities, and transport corridors often operate in high-risk environments.

Key company insights:

  • Founded in 2024 by Nathan Nwachuku and Maxwell Maduka

  • Develops autonomous drones, sentry towers, and unmanned ground vehicles

  • Uses proprietary ArtemisOS software for real-time monitoring and coordinated response

  • Claims infrastructure monitoring contracts worth tens of millions of dollars across several African countries

  • Currently monitoring assets valued at approximately $11 billion

The company aims to build Africa’s first vertically integrated defense technology provider, similar to U.S. firms like Anduril Industries and Palantir Technologies, but tailored to African operational realities.

Strategic Focus: Local Solutions for Local Challenges

Africa holds more than 30% of the world’s critical mineral reserves and invests roughly $100 billion annually in infrastructure, much of it located in remote or insecure regions. Governments and operators often rely on imported security systems, which can be costly and vulnerable to geopolitical risks.

Terra’s model focuses on:

  • Locally manufactured hardware integrated with proprietary software

  • Autonomous threat detection and response capabilities

  • Reduced reliance on foreign defense imports

  • Scalable monitoring across land, air, and maritime environments

Improved security infrastructure can reduce operational risks for businesses operating in agriculture, mining, logistics, and industrial sectors. Local defense innovation also creates new supply chains, skilled jobs, and partnership opportunities for African MSMEs within the growing technology and manufacturing ecosystem.
Chams Holding Company Plc Launches AI & Data Infrastructure Subsidiary to Drive Digital Economy Expansion
With new subsidiary, Chams joins Nigeria’s growing AI infrastructure race

Chams Holding Company Plc, a Nigerian ICT firm, has established a new subsidiary, ChamsCorp Plc, to explore opportunities in artificial intelligence (AI) and data center infrastructure. This subsidiary will focus on digital device manufacturing, data center operations, and AI systems, representing a strategic shift from the company's legacy in payments and identification. Chams aims to enhance its position in the digital economy by investing in foundational technologies needed for future digital services. The company is led by Olufemi Oyenuga, effective February 1, 2026, although specific details regarding its operational strategy remain unclear. Chams is also planning to raise $5.52 million for projects in Africa's digital payments and identity management sectors. This move signals Chams' commitment to pivot towards technology sectors crucial for the future economy.

Chams Holding Company Plc has established a new subsidiary, ChamsCorp Plc, as part of a strategic shift into artificial intelligence (AI) and data center infrastructure. The move signals the Nigerian technology firm’s transition from its traditional focus on payments and identity solutions toward becoming a foundational player in Africa’s emerging digital economy.

The development was disclosed in a regulatory filing, with the company highlighting ambitions to invest across AI infrastructure, intelligent systems, and digital device manufacturing.

Strategic Pivot Into High-Value Digital Infrastructure

The creation of ChamsCorp represents a deliberate effort by the company to move up the technology value chain. Instead of primarily processing transactions, Chams is positioning itself to own and enable the computing power, storage systems, and intelligence platforms that will underpin future digital services.

Key focus areas for the new subsidiary include:

  • Digital device manufacturing and infrastructure development

  • Data center design, implementation, and operations

  • AI infrastructure and intelligent systems deployment

  • Technology-driven enterprise solutions

This shift aligns with broader global trends where technology companies are investing heavily in core infrastructure to capture long-term growth opportunities.

Leadership Appointment and Market Opportunity

ChamsCorp will be led by Olufemi Oyenuga, an information management and data analytics specialist with experience spanning healthcare, telecommunications, ICT, and manufacturing sectors. His appointment took effect on February 1, 2026.

The opportunity ahead is significant. According to projections by Mordor Intelligence, Nigeria’s data center market is expected to reach approximately $782.82 million by 2031, reflecting rising demand for cloud services, AI computing, and digital transformation across industries.

Investment Plans and Corporate Structure

While the company has not yet disclosed the full investment size or operational roadmap, it confirmed that further updates will be provided in line with the disclosure requirements of the Nigerian Exchange Group.

Chams Holdings currently operates multiple subsidiaries, including:

  • ChamsCorp Plc

  • Chams Access Limited

  • ChamSwitch Limited

  • Chams Mobile Limited

  • Card Centre Nigeria Limited

The AI and data infrastructure push marks one of the company’s most significant strategic evolutions since its founding.

Stronger local AI and data infrastructure can reduce costs, improve access to digital tools, and enable African businesses to adopt cloud, automation, and analytics solutions more easily. For MSMEs, this could translate into faster innovation, better customer insights, and improved competitiveness in both local and global markets.
Nigeria Confirms Arrival of 100 U.S. Military Personnel for Training Mission
Deployment focused on training, intelligence sharing, and technical support to strengthen Nigeria’s counterterrorism capacity.

Nigeria has confirmed the arrival of about 100 U.S. military personnel in the North-East to provide advisory support, specialised training, and intelligence cooperation. Officials say the mission is non-combat and conducted under Nigerian authority, aimed at enhancing national security and counterterrorism operations.

Nigeria’s Defence Headquarters has confirmed the arrival of approximately 100 United States military personnel in the country’s North-East, marking a new phase in bilateral security cooperation. According to officials, the deployment is strictly for training, technical support, and intelligence collaboration, not combat operations.

The troops and associated equipment landed at Bauchi Airfield following a formal request by the Nigerian government for targeted military capacity support.

 

What the Deployment Involves

The Defence Headquarters clarified that the U.S. personnel will operate strictly in an advisory and training capacity under Nigeria’s authority and control.

Key details include:

  • Around 100 U.S. military personnel deployed

  • Mission focused on training, technical assistance, and intelligence sharing

  • No direct combat role

  • Joint engagements to strengthen counterterrorism capabilities

  • Activities coordinated under Nigerian military leadership

Officials say the collaboration aims to enhance Nigeria’s ability to detect and neutralise extremist groups while improving protection for vulnerable communities.

The deployment builds on an existing small U.S. advisory presence and follows recent diplomatic and security engagements between both countries.

 

Context Behind the Move

The security partnership comes amid heightened international scrutiny of Nigeria’s counterterrorism efforts. While some external voices have framed violence in religious terms, Nigerian authorities and observers maintain that insecurity affects communities across faith lines.

Public reaction within Nigeria has been mixed, with some citizens expressing concern about foreign military presence. The Defence Headquarters has reiterated that the arrangement fully respects Nigeria’s sovereignty. No timeline has been disclosed for how long the U.S. personnel will remain.

 

Security and Economic Stability

Security remains a key factor influencing investment, infrastructure development, and business continuity across Nigeria, particularly in the North-East and North-West regions. Strengthened intelligence-sharing and training could improve operational effectiveness against insurgent groups, potentially supporting broader economic stability.

Improved security cooperation can reduce operational risk, protect supply chains, and strengthen investor confidence, especially in regions previously affected by instability. For MSMEs, enhanced national security capacity supports a more predictable environment for trade, logistics, agriculture, and regional expansion.
South African Rand Slips Ahead of Q4 Unemployment Data
Currency dips 0.4% as investors await Q4 unemployment figures expected near 32%.

The South African rand weakened to 16.0375/$ ahead of key labour market data, with economists projecting unemployment to remain largely unchanged around 31.7%–32% in Q4 2025. Investors are watching the release for signals on economic stability and consumer demand.

The South African rand weakened 0.4% to 16.0375/$ in early Tuesday trade as investors positioned ahead of fourth-quarter labour market data. Markets are watching closely for signals on employment stability in South Africa, Africa’s most industrialised economy, where joblessness remains among the highest globally.

The data is expected to influence near-term currency direction, bond yields, and broader investor sentiment.

Market Snapshot

  • Rand at 16.0375/$ (0.4% weaker from prior close)

  • Unemployment data due at 0930 GMT

  • Current official unemployment rate: 31.9%

  • Economists forecast Q4 rate near 31.7%–32%

  • 2035 government bond yield steady at 7.945%

The relatively stable bond yield suggests fixed-income investors are cautious but not yet repositioning aggressively ahead of the data.

What Analysts Are Saying

Economists expect unemployment to remain broadly unchanged in Q4 2025, reflecting a mixed economic environment.

Domestically oriented sectors may have benefited from:

  • Improved electricity supply compared to prior years

  • Easing logistics bottlenecks

  • Resilient consumer spending

However, export-driven industries are reportedly more cautious. Higher global tariffs and weaker external demand have slowed capacity expansion, limiting hiring momentum in manufacturing and trade-linked sectors.

South Africa’s unemployment rate, hovering near 32%, remains structurally high and continues to constrain long-term growth. Persistent joblessness limits household income growth, dampens domestic demand, and weighs on fiscal sustainability.

Currency movements around labour data are significant because employment trends shape expectations around:

  • Consumer spending

  • Tax revenues

  • Interest rate policy

  • Foreign capital flows

A stronger-than-expected labour report could support the rand and reduce pressure on policymakers. Conversely, weaker data may renew concerns about growth prospects and currency volatility.

Why Markets Care

Labour market stability is a key indicator of economic resilience. If employment shows signs of improvement, it could bolster investor confidence and support capital inflows. If conditions deteriorate, markets may price in slower growth and heightened fiscal risk.

For MSMEs, currency stability directly affects import costs, pricing decisions, and cross-border competitiveness. Employment trends also shape consumer purchasing power and demand across retail, services, and manufacturing. A stable labour market supports predictable planning, while volatility can tighten margins and slow expansion decisions.
Light Rains Boost Ivory Coast’s Cocoa Mid-Crop Outlook
Above-average rainfall strengthens prospects for April–September cocoa harvest.

Light but mostly above-average rains across Ivory Coast’s key cocoa regions are supporting pod development ahead of the mid-crop season. Farmers say tree conditions are stable, and consistent rainfall through mid-March could result in a stronger harvest than last year.

Favourable rainfall across Ivory Coast’s main cocoa-growing regions is strengthening expectations for the April–September mid-crop harvest. Farmers report healthy tree conditions and improved moisture levels, raising hopes that output could surpass last season’s performance if rains continue consistently through mid-March.

 

Rainfall Trends Across Key Regions

Ivory Coast, the world’s largest cocoa producer, is currently in its dry season (mid-November to March). However, light but mostly above-average rains last week supported crop development across several regions.

Key highlights:

  • Yamoussoukro: 14.5 mm rainfall (7.9 mm above five-year average)

  • Agboville: 22.8 mm rainfall (14.5 mm above five-year average)

  • Bongouanou: Above-average rainfall

  • Daloa, Soubre, Divo: Below-average rainfall, but trees remain stable

  • Abengourou: Above-average rainfall

  • Temperatures: 27.9°C to 31.7°C

Farmers report no signs of moisture stress and say numerous small pods are developing well. Many expect a gradual pickup in harvesting from May through September, despite a likely modest start in April.

 

Mid-Crop Outlook

Growing conditions are described as better than last season so far. If rainfall remains consistent until mid-March, farmers believe the mid-crop harvest could exceed last year’s output.

Stronger cocoa output could stabilise raw material supply for processors, exporters, and agribusiness SMEs across West Africa. Improved harvest prospects also support rural incomes and local spending, which benefits logistics, packaging, and food manufacturing businesses linked to the cocoa value chain.
Worker Strike Disrupts Flights at Kenya’s Main Airport
Aviation workers’ strike triggers air traffic delays at Nairobi’s key regional hub.

Flights at Jomo Kenyatta International Airport were delayed following a strike by aviation union members over unresolved labour disputes. The disruption is affecting arrivals and departures, raising concerns about potential impacts on regional travel and cargo operations.

Operations at Jomo Kenyatta International Airport faced delays on Monday following a strike by members of Kenya’s aviation workers’ union. The disruption affects one of Africa’s busiest air transport hubs, with airlines reporting delays in departures and arrivals.

The strike stems from a labour dispute between the aviation workers’ union and the Kenya Civil Aviation Authority over unresolved grievances, including failure to conclude a collective bargaining agreement.

Key developments:

  • Union issued a seven-day strike notice last week

  • Civil aviation authority sought a court order to block the strike

  • Union leadership says no court order halting the strike has been served

  • Air traffic control delays impacting flights

Kenya Airways and Precision Air confirmed that delays are affecting both arrivals and departures.

 

Regional Transport Impact

As a major regional hub, JKIA connects East Africa to Europe, Asia, and the Middle East. Any prolonged disruption could affect passenger travel, cargo shipments, and time-sensitive exports such as fresh produce and flowers.

Airport disruptions can delay cargo exports, increase logistics costs, and affect supply chains, especially for SMEs reliant on air freight. For businesses in agriculture, e-commerce, tourism, and regional trade, operational stability at key transport hubs is critical for predictable revenue and market access.
Dangote Group Signs $400m Deal with XCMG to Accelerate Refinery Expansion
Heavy equipment partnership supports refinery expansion to 1.4m bpd and major petrochemical capacity growth.

Dangote Group has signed a $400 million equipment deal with China’s XCMG to accelerate refinery expansion and boost petrochemical output. The project aims to raise capacity across fuel, fertilizer, and industrial inputs, strengthening regional supply chains and reducing reliance on imports.

Nigeria’s Dangote Group has signed a $400 million equipment agreement with China’s XCMG to fast-track expansion of its refinery toward a planned capacity of 1.4 million barrels per day (bpd). The investment supports broader industrial growth across refining, petrochemicals, agriculture, and infrastructure.

The move strengthens Dangote’s long-term ambition to become a $100 billion enterprise by 2030.

 

Deal Overview

The agreement enables Dangote to acquire heavy-duty construction and industrial machinery to accelerate ongoing projects, including refinery expansion.

Key highlights:

  • $400 million equipment deal

  • Target refinery capacity: 1.4 million bpd

  • Completion timeline: within three years

  • Equipment to support refining, petrochemicals, agriculture, and infrastructure

The refinery, valued at $20 billion, began operations in 2024 and is expected to significantly reduce Nigeria’s reliance on imported fuel.

 

Expansion Impact on Industrial Output

The expansion programme will substantially increase production across multiple segments:

  • Polypropylene: Rising to 2.4 million tons/year (from 900,000 tons)

  • Urea: Tripling Nigeria’s output to 9 million tons/year, alongside a 3 million-ton plant in Ethiopia

  • Linear Alkyl Benzene (LAB): Increasing to 400,000 tons/year, making Dangote Africa’s largest supplier

  • Additional base oil capacity planned

These upgrades position Dangote as one of the world’s largest urea producers and a dominant player in key industrial inputs across Africa.

 

Strategic Significance

The equipment deal signals continued momentum behind Africa’s largest single private-sector industrial investment. By scaling refining and petrochemical capacity, Dangote aims to strengthen regional supply chains, deepen local manufacturing, and reduce foreign exchange pressure from fuel and fertilizer imports.

Expanded petrochemical and fertilizer production could lower input costs for manufacturers, agriculture-focused businesses, and industrial SMEs. Greater local supply of fuel and industrial raw materials improves price stability, reduces import dependence, and creates new opportunities across logistics, packaging, agro-processing, and consumer goods value chains.
South Africa’s Unemployment Rate Falls to 31.4% in Q4
Job gains in social services, construction, and finance offset losses in trade and manufacturing.

South Africa’s unemployment rate edged down to 31.4% in Q4, its lowest level in over five years. While employment improved in several sectors, significant informal job losses kept overall joblessness high, reflecting a slow and uneven recovery.

South Africa’s official unemployment rate declined modestly to 31.4% in the fourth quarter (October–December), down from 31.9% in the previous quarter. While this marks the lowest level in over five years, joblessness remains among the highest globally, underscoring persistent structural challenges in Africa’s most industrialised economy.

 

Key Labour Market Highlights

  • Official unemployment rate: 31.4% (down from 31.9%)

  • Expanded unemployment rate: 42.1%

  • Sectors adding jobs: 7 of 10 industries tracked

  • Sectors losing jobs: 3 industries

Employment gains were recorded in:

  • Community and social services: +46,000 jobs

  • Construction: +35,000 jobs

  • Finance: +32,000 jobs

Job losses were recorded in:

  • Trade: -98,000 jobs

  • Manufacturing: -61,000 jobs

  • Mining: -5,000 jobs

A significant factor keeping unemployment elevated was the loss of 293,000 informal jobs, partly linked to the removal of informal traders in Johannesburg ahead of the November G20 summit.

 

Economic Context

Unemployment has remained above 30% since the COVID-19 pandemic, despite multiple government initiatives to stimulate job creation. Although electricity supply has stabilised and logistics constraints have eased, the broader economic recovery has yet to translate into sustained hiring momentum.

Analysts had expected little change in the labour market, citing global economic uncertainty and cautious business expansion strategies.

A modest drop in unemployment suggests gradual stabilisation but highlights ongoing demand-side pressure. MSMEs may benefit from improved power supply and construction activity, yet weak informal sector performance and cautious hiring signal that consumer spending growth may remain constrained in the near term.
Nigeria’s $14bn Portfolio Inflows Highlight Weak Long-Term Investment
High-yield portfolio inflows boost reserves, but weak FDI raises concerns over long-term growth.

Nigeria attracted $14.3 billion in portfolio inflows in the first nine months of 2025, far outweighing $566.2 million in foreign direct investment. While strong capital inflows have stabilised reserves and the naira, analysts warn that reliance on short-term funds leaves the economy vulnerable and underscores the need for sustained, job-creating investment.

Nigeria’s investment rebound is being powered largely by short-term portfolio inflows rather than sustained foreign direct investment (FDI), raising concerns about the durability of the country’s economic recovery.

While FDI more than doubled to $566.2 million in the first nine months of 2025, its highest level in four years, it was overshadowed by $14.3 billion in foreign portfolio investment (FPI), reflecting strong demand for high-yield financial assets rather than long-term industrial commitments.

 

Investment Breakdown

  • FDI (Jan–Sept 2025): $566.2 million

  • Portfolio inflows: $14.3 billion

  • Policy rate: 27%

  • Foreign reserves: ~$49 billion

  • GDP size: ~$300 billion

High interest rates and a more market-determined exchange rate have made Nigerian bonds and treasury instruments attractive to offshore investors seeking yield in frontier markets.

 

Why the Composition Matters

Analysts warn that portfolio flows can reverse quickly during global volatility, unlike FDI, which typically funds factories, infrastructure, and job creation.

Key concerns include:

  • Persistent insecurity

  • Infrastructure gaps

  • High energy costs

  • Policy inconsistency

Before the 2014 oil price crash, Nigeria routinely attracted several billion dollars annually in FDI, particularly into energy, telecoms, and consumer sectors. Current FDI levels remain modest relative to the size of the economy and its population of over 230 million.

 

Currency and Reserve Impact

Improved capital inflows have helped:

  • Boost foreign reserves to about $49 billion

  • Stabilise the naira within a narrower trading band

  • Ease imported inflation pressures

However, reliance on portfolio capital increases vulnerability to shifts in global risk appetite and U.S. interest rate changes. A reversal could pressure the naira and complicate reserve management.

Outlook for Direct Investment

Economists note that macroeconomic reforms, exchange rate liberalisation, and improving inflation trends are rebuilding confidence. Still, long-term investors remain cautious until structural constraints are sustainably addressed.

Sustained growth above 3–4% and meaningful job creation will depend more on durable FDI than short-term liquidity inflows.

Short-term capital inflows can stabilise currency markets and improve liquidity, but only sustained FDI drives factory expansion, infrastructure development, and job creation. For MSMEs, stronger long-term investment would translate into deeper supply chains, improved infrastructure, and broader market opportunities.
MTN Group to Acquire Full Control of IHS Holding in $6.2bn Deal
Telecom giant moves to regain control of nearly 29,000 African towers to strengthen digital infrastructure strategy.

MTN Group has agreed to acquire full ownership of IHS Holding in a $6.2 billion deal, paying $8.50 per share. The transaction will return direct control of key tower assets to MTN, reduce long-term leasing costs, and support faster 5G and fibre expansion across Africa.

MTN Group has agreed to buy the approximately 75% stake in IHS Holding that it does not already own, in a transaction valuing the tower company at about $6.2 billion. The move marks a strategic shift, returning MTN to direct ownership of critical telecom infrastructure across Africa.

Once completed, IHS will be taken private and become a wholly owned subsidiary of MTN.

 

Deal Structure

MTN will pay $8.50 per share in cash, representing:

  • A 36% premium to IHS’ 52-week volume-weighted average price

  • A 3% premium to its February 4 closing price

The acquisition will be funded through:

  • Rollover of MTN’s existing ~24% stake

  • Approximately $1.1 billion in cash from MTN

  • Around $1.1 billion from IHS’ balance sheet

  • No more than existing IHS debt

IHS’ board has unanimously approved the deal and will recommend it to shareholders. Major investor Wendel, which holds 19%, has agreed to vote in favour and will receive approximately $535 million.

 

Strategic Shift in Infrastructure Control

The deal reverses a decade-long industry trend where telecom operators outsourced tower ownership to specialist firms. MTN originally built many of these towers before selling them to IHS.

Key implications include:

  • Regaining control of nearly 29,000 African towers

  • Reducing long-term rental and leasing costs

  • Greater flexibility in 5G and fibre rollout

  • Strengthened control over digital infrastructure strategy

Earlier the same day, IHS agreed to sell its Latin American tower operations in Brazil and Colombia to Macquarie Asset Management, narrowing its focus to Africa under MTN ownership.

Stronger infrastructure control could accelerate 5G expansion, improve network reliability, and support digital services growth across Africa. For MSMEs, enhanced connectivity underpins e-commerce, fintech, logistics, and remote service delivery, making telecom infrastructure a critical foundation for business scale and competitiveness.
Namibia to Benefit From China's New Zero-Tariff Policy
China Expands Zero-Tariff Policy to 53 African Countries to Boost Trade Access

Namibia is one of 53 African nations benefiting from China's expanded zero-tariff trade policy, effective May 1. This policy removes tariffs on imports from nearly all African countries with diplomatic ties to Beijing, significantly broadening a previous arrangement that included only 33 nations. Announced during a ministerial meeting on the Forum on China-Africa Cooperation (FOCAC), the policy aims to enhance economic ties and increase African exports to China, one of the world's largest consumer markets. Key features include tariff elimination on all lines for eligible nations, additional trade facilitation measures for least developed countries, and alignment with China's broader economic cooperation objectives, such as industrialisation and infrastructure development. The tariff removal may create opportunities for African exporters across various sectors, improving competitiveness in the Chinese market.

Namibia is among 53 African nations set to benefit from an expanded zero-tariff trade policy announced by China, aimed at strengthening economic ties and increasing African exports to the Chinese market. The policy, which took effect from May 1, removes tariffs on imports from nearly all African countries with diplomatic relations with Beijing, significantly broadening an earlier arrangement that covered only 33 nations.

The announcement followed the Ministerial Meeting of Coordinators on the implementation of follow-up actions under the Forum on China-Africa Cooperation (FOCAC) held in Changsha, where Chinese leadership reaffirmed commitments to deepen trade cooperation with African partners.

What the Zero-Tariff Policy Includes

According to Chinese authorities, the expanded policy will:

  • Eliminate tariffs on all tariff lines for eligible African countries

  • Cover 53 African nations with diplomatic relations with Beijing

  • Expand a previous zero-duty arrangement that applied to 33 countries

  • Introduce additional trade facilitation measures to support exports from Africa’s least developed countries

The initiative is designed to reduce trade barriers and encourage African producers to increase exports into one of the world’s largest consumer markets.

Strategic Objectives Behind the Policy

Chinese officials say the move aligns with broader economic cooperation goals between China and Africa, including:

  • Accelerating implementation of partnership commitments under FOCAC

  • Supporting industrialisation and economic growth across African economies

  • Promoting joint modernisation and infrastructure development

  • Strengthening long-term China–Africa economic relations

Chinese President Xi Jinping emphasised that the policy is part of efforts to build what Beijing describes as an “all-weather China–Africa community with a shared future for the new era.”

Opportunities for African Exporters

The removal of tariffs could create new opportunities across multiple sectors, including:

  • Agriculture and agro-processing products

  • Textiles and garments

  • Manufactured goods

  • Minerals and natural resources

  • Value-added industrial exports

For African businesses, particularly exporters, lower trade barriers may improve competitiveness and market entry into China.

Duty-free access to China can significantly reduce export costs for African small businesses, opening doors to larger markets and new revenue streams. MSMEs involved in agriculture, manufacturing, and processing stand to benefit most if they can meet export standards and scale production.
UAE Reaffirms Investment Drive in Africa's Energy and Trade Sectors
UAE Commits Over $110 Billion to Africa, Targeting Energy, Trade, and Infrastructure Growth
