By adopting a flexible, trading led merchant model, the Dangote Refinery is positioning Nigeria as an active participant in global refined product markets rather than solely a crude exporter. While the model enhances operational agility and market integration, its success will depend on sustained utilisation, regulatory clarity, and effective risk management in volatile global energy markets.

A globally integrated refinery model can stabilise domestic fuel supply, improve product quality, and reduce reliance on imports. For MSMEs, this supports energy cost predictability, strengthens supply chains, and enhances competitiveness across manufacturing, transport, and trade sectors.
South Sudan Replaces Finance Minister Again Amid Ongoing Leadership Reshuffles
Frequent cabinet changes signal continued political and fiscal uncertainty in Africa’s youngest nation.

South Sudan’s President Salva Kiir has dismissed Finance Minister Bak Barnaba Chol after just three months in office, marking the ninth change in the role since 2020. Economist Salvatore Garang, who previously served from 2018 to 2020, has been reappointed amid broader administrative changes.

South Sudan has appointed a new finance minister for the ninth time since 2020, as President Salva Kiir continues a pattern of rapid cabinet changes. The latest reshuffle, announced on state television, saw Finance Minister Bak Barnaba Chol dismissed just three months after taking office, underscoring ongoing political and economic volatility in the world’s youngest nation.

 

What Happened

  • Bak Barnaba Chol was appointed in November 2025 and removed in February 2026.

  • He had replaced Athian Diing Athian, who himself lasted only two months in the role.

  • President Kiir has now changed finance ministers nine times since 2020.

  • Economist Salvatore Garang has been reappointed as Finance Minister.

Garang previously served in the role from 2018 to 2020, a period marked by fiscal reforms and severe budgetary pressure. During that time, local media reported corruption allegations, including claims he allocated $100,000 in public funds for his son’s funeral. He has not publicly responded to those claims.

 

Broader Administrative Shake-Up

The decree also removed several senior officials, including the commissioner general of the National Revenue Authority, signaling a wider restructuring within key fiscal institutions.

Analysts note that President Kiir frequently reshuffles senior government and military officials to consolidate authority, particularly amid:

  • Ongoing armed tensions

  • Political fragility

  • Speculation about succession planning

 

Economic Context

South Sudan’s economy remains heavily dependent on oil revenues, with persistent challenges including:

  • Limited revenue diversification

  • Currency instability

  • Fiscal deficits

  • Institutional fragility

Frequent leadership turnover in the finance ministry adds further uncertainty to economic management and reform continuity.

Policy instability in oil-dependent economies affects regional trade flows, currency stability, and cross-border investment confidence. For African MSMEs engaged in regional trade, infrastructure, or energy supply chains, leadership volatility can increase risk exposure and payment uncertainty.
Stability in fiscal leadership remains critical for predictable market conditions across East Africa.
Bitcoin Falls Below $63,000 as Geopolitical Tensions Trigger Risk Sell-Off
Cryptocurrency slides over 5% amid tariff concerns and escalating Middle East tensions.

Bitcoin fell over 5% to below $63,000 amid rising geopolitical tensions and tariff concerns. Analysts attribute the decline to broader risk aversion and tightening global liquidity, with the cryptocurrency now down 27% year-to-date.

Bitcoin dropped more than 5% on Tuesday, falling below $63,000 as investors reduced exposure to risk assets amid rising geopolitical tensions and trade policy uncertainty. The world’s largest cryptocurrency briefly traded at $62,964.64, extending a broader market downturn that has persisted since late last year.

Analysts say the sell-off reflects a broader risk sentiment reset rather than a crypto-specific shock.

 

What’s Driving the Decline

Market pressure intensified following:

  • Escalating tariff tensions

  • Heightened geopolitical risks in the Middle East

  • Comments from U.S. President Donald Trump regarding potential military action involving Iran

Washington has continued deploying military assets in the region, contributing to global market uncertainty.

 

Market Snapshot

  • Bitcoin price: Fell over 5% to below $63,000

  • Year-to-date decline: Down 27%

  • Drop from October peak ($125,000): Down roughly 50%

  • Ether: Down over 1% to $1,831.52

  • Gold: Slipped around 1%

Investment strategists note that Bitcoin remains highly sensitive to global liquidity conditions and shifts in financial tightening expectations.

 

Investor Sentiment

Market analysts describe the move as:

  • A “risk-sentiment reset”

  • Tactical de-risking rather than structural market exit

  • A reflection of tightening global financial conditions

When investors anticipate stricter trade policies or financial tightening, higher-volatility assets like cryptocurrencies are often among the first to react.

Crypto volatility directly impacts fintech platforms, cross-border payment solutions, and digital asset startups across Africa. For MSMEs using cryptocurrency for payments, treasury management, or remittances, heightened price swings increase financial risk and liquidity uncertainty, reinforcing the need for prudent risk management strategies.
West African Development Bank Clarifies Bond Investment Strategy Amid Surge in WAEMU Debt Issuance
BOAD explains treasury investments in member-state bonds as regional borrowing is set to rise nearly 28% in 2026.

BOAD has clarified that its investment in WAEMU sovereign bonds is part of treasury management, not budget support, as regional debt issuance is projected to rise nearly 28% in 2026. Growing borrowing by countries like Ivory Coast and Senegal underscores shifting fiscal dynamics in West Africa.

The West African Development Bank (BOAD) has clarified its strategy of investing in sovereign bonds issued by member countries, stating that the purchases are part of its treasury and asset management operations, not direct budget support.

The clarification comes as countries within the West African Economic and Monetary Union (WAEMU) ramp up borrowing from regional markets to meet fiscal needs.

 

BOAD’s Position

In a note to investors, the Togo-based multilateral lender explained that:

  • It invests part of its available liquidity in WAEMU sovereign bonds.

  • These investments help optimise treasury management and reduce cost of carry.

  • Bond purchases are separate from budget support loans and grants.

BOAD emphasized that, in over 50 years of operations, it has not recorded any sovereign payment default within the region.

 

Portfolio Growth

BOAD’s June 2025 financial statements show:

  • A nearly 360% increase in its Ivorian bond holdings over six months.

  • A 65% rise in its Senegalese bond portfolio over the same period.

The increase reflects growing regional reliance on domestic debt markets.

 

Regional Debt Outlook

According to UMOA-Titres, WAEMU debt issuance is projected to:

  • Rise 27.7% in 2026

  • Reach 15.143 trillion CFA francs (~$27.86 billion)

Planned 2026 borrowing includes:

  • Ivory Coast: 4.221 trillion CFA francs (~$7.69 billion)

  • Senegal: 4.132 trillion CFA francs (~$7.54 billion)

Senegal’s debt burden has risen sharply to 132% of GDP at the end of 2024, up from 78% in 2023, prompting the IMF to freeze its lending programme following debt misreporting concerns.

So far this year, Senegal has raised 590 billion CFA francs (~$1.06 billion) through five regional issuances.

 

Market Implications

With member states increasingly tapping regional markets, BOAD’s participation provides liquidity support and signals institutional confidence. However, rising sovereign debt levels across WAEMU are drawing attention from investors and multilateral lenders.

Higher sovereign borrowing can influence interest rates, liquidity conditions, and access to credit within regional markets. For MSMEs, sustained debt issuance may affect lending costs and public spending priorities, making it essential to monitor fiscal stability and regional capital market trends.
Migration Is Economic Expansion, Not Capital Drain — Optiva Capital
Firm argues structured migration boosts remittances, investment, and long-term wealth creation.

Optiva Capital’s chairman has argued that rising Nigerian migration represents capital expansion rather than capital flight. He highlighted remittances, cross-border investments, and global competitiveness as key economic benefits of structured mobility.

The Chairman of Optiva Capital Partners, Franklin Nechi, has rejected claims that rising migration among Nigerians amounts to capital flight. Speaking in Lagos, he argued that global mobility strengthens economic resilience by expanding access to opportunity, foreign exchange inflows, and cross-border investment.

According to him, structured migration, when properly managed, can enhance both individual wealth and national economic growth.

 

Mobility as Economic Expansion

Nechi emphasised that:

  • Business is increasingly global, not local.

  • Nigerians are competing in international markets.

  • Migration decisions are driven by education, healthcare, and opportunity, not resource drain.

He noted that remittances remain a key economic pillar, supporting:

  • Family welfare

  • Real estate investments

  • Small business development

  • Education and healthcare spending

Funds earned abroad, he said, often return to Nigeria through property acquisition and enterprise creation, reinforcing domestic economic activity.

 

Remittances and Resilience

Diaspora remittances have historically provided significant foreign exchange support to Nigeria’s economy. Nechi argued that outward mobility can:

  • Increase foreign currency inflows

  • Expand global networks

  • Improve access to international markets

  • Strengthen domestic investment capacity

Rather than shrinking the local economic base, he maintained that migration can diversify income sources and enhance resilience.

 

Policy and Planning Implications

With rising visa restrictions globally, Nechi advised families to integrate mobility into long-term wealth planning:

  • Start early

  • Remain compliant with international regulations

  • Diversify access across jurisdictions

He also called on policymakers to strengthen local institutions, noting that improvements in healthcare, education, and economic stability would reduce migration pressure over time.

 

Brain Drain vs. Human Capital Expansion

While acknowledging concerns about brain drain, Nechi argued that structured and strategic migration can enhance:

  • Human capital development

  • Global competitiveness

  • Knowledge transfer

“Global mobility is not capital flight; it is capital expansion,” he said.

Diaspora networks and remittances can unlock cross-border partnerships, funding opportunities, and market access for MSMEs. Structured global mobility may enhance foreign exchange inflows and knowledge transfer, strengthening entrepreneurial ecosystems across Africa.
Nigerian Communications Commission Telecom Sector Rebounds After Clearing ₦300bn USSD Debt Overhang
Debt resolution, tariff reform, and improved forex liquidity restore investor confidence.

Nigeria’s telecom sector is regaining investor confidence after resolving a ₦300 billion USSD debt dispute. Improved forex conditions and tariff reforms are strengthening operator balance sheets and supporting renewed infrastructure investment across the digital ecosystem.

Investor confidence is returning to Nigeria’s telecommunications sector following the resolution of a long-standing ₦300 billion USSD debt dispute between telecom operators and banks. Industry leaders say the settlement removes a major financial overhang that had constrained cash flow, delayed investment, and weakened sentiment across the digital ecosystem.

The breakthrough, alongside improved forex liquidity and tariff adjustments, signals a shift from crisis management to cautious expansion.

 

USSD Debt Dispute Resolved

At its peak, unpaid USSD charges incurred by banks for mobile banking services approached ₦300 billion.

Key outcomes:

  • Full resolution of outstanding USSD liabilities

  • Migration to an end-user billing framework

  • Improved clarity and sustainability in telecom-bank relations

Industry stakeholders say structured regulatory engagement played a critical role in resolving the impasse.

 

Improved Financial Stability

The sector has also benefited from:

  • Improved foreign exchange liquidity

  • Reduced exposure to legacy debt

  • Greater predictability in cash flow management

For years, forex scarcity forced operators, who earn in naira but settle many obligations in foreign currency to delay capital expenditure and accumulate foreign liabilities.

That pressure is now easing.

 

Tariff Adjustments Support Sustainability

Regulators approved the first major telecom tariff review in 13 years earlier this year.

Operators had argued that:

  • Inflation

  • Energy costs

  • Currency depreciation

had eroded margins and threatened infrastructure investment.

Industry leaders maintain that pricing adjustments were necessary to sustain network quality and prevent service deterioration.

 

Renewed Investor Interest

Early signs of recovery include:

  • Increased interest in tower infrastructure

  • Renewed focus on fibre expansion

  • Stronger capital positioning among operators

While operational risks such as fibre vandalism persist, the overall sector outlook has shifted from defensive survival to growth-oriented planning.

Telecom stability underpins fintech, e-commerce, digital payments, and broadband-driven businesses. Stronger operator balance sheets and renewed infrastructure investment improve service reliability and digital access critical foundations for MSME growth in Africa’s expanding digital economy.
Standard Chartered Posts 16% Profit Growth, Falls Short of Analyst Forecasts
Emerging markets lender benefits from strong wealth inflows and global banking activity.

Standard Chartered reported a 16% increase in full-year pretax profit to $6.96 billion, driven by strong global banking and wealth inflows, though results slightly missed analyst forecasts. The performance reflects continued strength in emerging markets, including Africa.

Standard Chartered reported a 16% rise in full-year pretax profit, driven by solid performance in global banking and continued strength in wealth management. However, the results fell slightly short of market expectations, reflecting cautious investor sentiment.

The bank, which generates most of its revenue across Asia and Africa, posted full-year pretax profit of $6.96 billion, up from $6.0 billion the previous year. Analysts had forecast an average of $7.2 billion.

 

Key Financial Highlights

  • Full-year pretax profit: $6.96 billion

  • Previous year: $6.0 billion

  • Growth rate: +16% year-on-year

  • Analyst expectation: $7.2 billion

The earnings boost was supported by:

  • Strong global banking performance

  • Increased wealth management inflows

  • Continued expansion in emerging markets

 

Market Reaction and Context

While profitability improved significantly, the slight miss relative to analyst expectations suggests that investor optimism remains measured, particularly amid global economic uncertainty and evolving interest rate conditions.

Standard Chartered’s exposure to high-growth but volatile emerging markets continues to shape both its opportunities and risk profile.

Stronger bank profitability signals improved financial sector resilience, which can support lending, trade finance, and cross-border investment. For MSMEs, healthy banking institutions enhance access to credit, international payment systems, and wealth management services critical for expansion in regional and global markets.
Nigeria’s External Reserves Hit 13 Year Peak at 50.45 Billion Dollars

Nigeria’s external reserves have reached 50.45 billion dollars, the highest level in 13 years, strengthening the country’s foreign exchange buffers and providing nearly 10 months of import cover. The growth reflects stronger oil earnings, improved capital inflows, and tighter monetary policy. The development enhances macroeconomic stability and supports currency confidence.

Nigeria’s external reserves have risen to 50.45 billion dollars, marking their highest level in 13 years, according to the Central Bank of Nigeria. The milestone signals a significant strengthening of the country’s foreign exchange buffers and reinforces confidence in Nigeria’s external position following a period of exchange rate volatility and macroeconomic adjustment.

Governor Olayemi Cardoso disclosed the update at the conclusion of the 304th Monetary Policy Committee meeting in Abuja, noting that the current reserve level provides approximately 9.68 months of import cover. This exceeds conventional adequacy benchmarks and positions Nigeria more comfortably against external shocks, including commodity price swings and global financial tightening.

What Is Driving the Reserve Growth

The accumulation of reserves reflects a combination of structural reforms and improved inflows into the economy.

Key contributing factors include:

  • Improved oil receipts supported by better production levels and relatively stable global prices

  • Increased foreign portfolio investment driven by higher domestic interest rates

  • Strong remittance inflows from the diaspora

  • Greater foreign exchange market transparency following exchange rate reforms

  • Tighter monetary policy aimed at restoring macroeconomic stability

The central bank had projected reserves would reach 51.04 billion dollars in 2026. The latest figure suggests that Nigeria is steadily approaching that target, indicating stronger external liquidity conditions than seen in recent years.

Implications for Currency and Monetary Stability

Higher reserves enhance the central bank’s capacity to intervene in the foreign exchange market when necessary. This helps to:

  • Reduce speculative pressure on the naira

  • Improve foreign exchange liquidity for importers and manufacturers

  • Support orderly market operations

  • Strengthen Nigeria’s credit profile in international markets

In recent years, reserve levels were closely watched as a signal of Nigeria’s ability to meet external obligations and stabilise the currency. The current buildup suggests improved coordination between fiscal reforms, oil revenue management, and monetary tightening.

Broader Economic Context

Nigeria is emerging from a period marked by subsidy removal, exchange rate unification, and aggressive monetary tightening. These reforms initially contributed to inflationary pressure and currency volatility but are now beginning to reflect in stronger external buffers and renewed investor confidence.

Sustaining reserve growth will depend on consistent oil output, continued foreign inflows, disciplined fiscal spending, and effective management of external debt obligations.

The rise in Nigeria’s external reserves to a 13 year high marks a notable improvement in the country’s macroeconomic resilience. While challenges remain, particularly around inflation and growth momentum, stronger reserve buffers provide policymakers with greater flexibility to manage external shocks and maintain exchange rate stability.

Stronger reserves improve currency stability and reduce foreign exchange uncertainty. For MSMEs engaged in trade, manufacturing, and cross border transactions, this supports better planning, more predictable input costs, and improved access to foreign currency for business operations.
Fixed-Income Yields Drive Investor Rush as CBN Rate Cut Looms

Nigeria’s fixed-income market is seeing record oversubscriptions in FGN bonds and corporate Commercial Papers as investors anticipate a rate cut by the CBN. N2.699 trillion poured into government bonds, while high-grade corporate CPs were heavily oversubscribed. This surge reflects confidence in monetary easing, a search for higher yields, and strategic positioning ahead of expected MPR reduction.

The Nigerian fixed-income market is experiencing unprecedented demand as institutional and retail investors rush to lock in high yields on Federal Government Bonds and Commercial Papers ahead of the anticipated Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) meeting. With a widely expected rate cut tomorrow, investors are front-running the market, signaling strong market consensus that the 27 percent Monetary Policy Rate (MPR) era may be coming to an end.

Record Subscriptions in Government Bonds and Commercial Papers

On the eve of the MPC meeting, the Debt Management Office (DMO) reported N2.699 trillion in subscriptions for FGN bonds, nearly four times the N800 billion offered. High-grade corporate Commercial Papers (CPs) from top-tier firms like MeCure Industries Plc and Coleman Technical Industries Limited are similarly oversubscribed, reflecting aggressive demand for double-digit yields.

  • MeCure Industries issued a N40 billion CP at 21.75 percent, closing two days early due to high demand.

  • Coleman Technical Industries offered N50 billion at 20.50 percent, also heavily oversubscribed.

  • Long-dated FGN bonds, including the 10-year FEB 2034 paper, recorded almost ten times oversubscription, with N972 billion in bids against a N100 billion offer.

According to Oise Ajayi, portfolio manager at Tangerine Life, “Over subscription is killing allotment for these CPs. Everyone is trying to lock in yields above 20 percent before the MPC cuts rates. No one wants to be left holding cash as yields tumble.”

Drivers Behind the Surge

Several factors are contributing to the market frenzy:

  • Cooling headline inflation, which fell to 15.1 percent in January 2026, making the current MPR increasingly expensive for investors.

  • Compressed Treasury Bill yields, with one-year bills recently touching 18.9 percent, pushing investors toward higher-yield CPs and longer-duration bonds.

  • A preference for 180-day and 270-day tenors to hedge against reinvestment risk when rates normalize later in 2026.

  • Strong investor confidence in corporate credit, as oversubscriptions indicate a willingness to assume some corporate risk in search of superior returns.

Implications for Yield and Liquidity

The rush to invest has already impacted interest rates in the fixed-income market:

  • Long-dated FGN bonds that cleared near 17.50 percent in January have fallen to 15.50 percent.

  • Stop rates for one-year Treasury Bills declined to 15.90 percent, reflecting aggressive bidding and expectations of a gradual yield moderation.

  • Oversubscription also indicates a concentration of liquidity among institutional players and a preference for longer-duration instruments.

While the surge signals strong market liquidity and investor appetite, it carries potential risks. If the CBN decides to hold rates to maintain inflationary control, early investors could be locked into corporate risk while government securities continue to offer attractive yields.

The massive oversubscriptions across government and corporate debt reflect broad market anticipation of an imminent rate cut by the CBN. Investors are aggressively reallocating liquidity into high-yield fixed-income instruments to protect returns ahead of expected monetary easing, demonstrating both confidence in the market and strategic portfolio positioning.

For MSMEs, the potential CBN rate cut and resulting yield compression could reduce borrowing costs and improve access to credit. Observing fixed-income market trends helps businesses anticipate interest rate movements, manage financing strategies, and plan for investment or expansion in a shifting macroeconomic environment.
Nigeria’s Naira Set to Strengthen to N1,000 per Dollar, Tinubu Says

President Tinubu announced that the naira could reach N1,000 per US dollar in the coming weeks, citing economic reforms and CBN interventions. He stressed that fiscal discipline, subsidy removal, and the 2026 budget are key drivers of stabilization. The Renewed Hope Ambassadors were tasked with engaging communities to ensure understanding, transparency, and feedback.

President Bola Tinubu has projected that the Nigerian naira could exchange at N1,000 to the US dollar within weeks, citing the positive effects of his administration’s broad economic reforms and timely interventions by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN). Speaking at the Renewed Hope Ambassadors summit in Abuja, Tinubu highlighted moderating inflation, rising oil revenues, and a resilient currency as key indicators that Nigeria’s economy is stabilizing after years of structural imbalances.

The President noted that without recent CBN interventions aimed at maintaining market stability, the N1,000-to-dollar exchange rate could have been achieved even sooner. “For the purpose of market stability, the CBN generously intervened yesterday,” he said, adding that the interventions also serve as a warning to those stockpiling dollars, which could distort market dynamics.

Economic Reforms and Fiscal Strategy
Tinubu emphasized that structural reforms—including tax policy adjustments, foreign exchange liberalization, removal of fuel subsidies, and streamlined revenue collection—have strengthened fiscal credibility, improved government revenue flows, and laid the foundation for sustainable economic growth.

He underlined that the N58.18 trillion 2026 budget is strategically designed to accelerate capital expenditure, modernize national security infrastructure, and create an enabling environment for private-sector participation. The reforms, he said, have also improved investor confidence: “Of the seven major investment decisions made last year in Africa, five were in Nigeria,” reflecting the country’s enhanced attractiveness as a business destination.

These reforms are further supported by tighter fiscal management, enhanced transparency in revenue reporting, and policy measures aimed at reducing leakages and improving allocation efficiency. By ensuring that funds are disbursed predictably and linked to priority sectors, the government aims to avoid previous implementation gaps that limited the impact of prior budgets.

Civic Engagement and National Unity
The President tasked the Renewed Hope Ambassadors with bridging the gap between government policies and citizens. He stressed that public understanding and participation are critical for reform success. Ambassadors were urged to explain the rationale behind difficult policy decisions, counter misinformation, and provide feedback from communities to inform policy adjustments.

Highlighting Benue State as an example, Tinubu emphasized the role of dialogue and collaboration in promoting peace and development. He encouraged local engagement, urging ambassadors to extend their efforts beyond conferences into wards, markets, campuses, and farms, ensuring that reforms translate into tangible benefits on the ground.

Market Implications and Forward Outlook
With targeted CBN interventions, subsidy reforms, and improved fiscal management, Nigeria’s currency outlook signals a potential period of macroeconomic stability. A stronger naira could reduce import costs, moderate inflationary pressures, and improve predictability for businesses and investors.

For entrepreneurs and MSMEs, these reforms provide a clearer operating environment, with more stable input costs and access to financing in a less volatile currency context. At the same time, investor-friendly policies and fiscal discipline may attract foreign investment, creating opportunities for partnerships, technology transfer, and market expansion.

Nigeria’s currency outlook, supported by structural reforms and targeted CBN interventions, signals a shift toward macroeconomic stability. With fiscal prudence and proactive community engagement, the government aims to ensure that economic reforms benefit all Nigerians, providing opportunities for entrepreneurs and businesses to thrive in a more predictable financial landscape.

A stronger, stable naira lowers import costs, improves pricing predictability, and boosts investor confidence. Combined with fiscal and currency reforms, these measures create a more predictable business environment, enabling MSMEs to plan operations, access financing, and scale sustainably.
Senegal Flags Key Policy Gaps as IMF Programme Negotiations Continue
Government rejects debt restructuring as negotiations continue over new IMF programme.

Senegal says key differences remain in talks with the IMF over a new lending programme, despite resolving past debt misreporting issues. The government rejects debt restructuring and maintains that long-term economic indicators are improving as negotiations continue.

Senegal’s Finance Minister, Cheikh Diba, has told lawmakers that “fundamental differences” remain between the government and the International Monetary Fund in ongoing discussions for a new lending programme. The country is seeking IMF support to stabilise public finances after its debt burden surged above 130% of GDP following previously unreported borrowing by the former administration.

While negotiations are continuing, Dakar has pushed back against what it describes as overly pessimistic economic assumptions by the Fund.

 

Key Issues in the Talks

According to the finance minister:

  • All misreporting concerns with the IMF have been resolved.

  • An agreement has been reached on auditing and correcting past statistics.

  • Senegal disagrees with the IMF’s economic projections.

  • The government opposes restructuring its debt.

Diba emphasised that long-term indicators are improving and expressed confidence in achieving sustainable debt levels without harming creditor relationships.

 

Debt Context

Senegal’s public debt has climbed to over 130% of GDP, up sharply from previous levels, largely due to undisclosed borrowing by the prior administration.

The new IMF programme is intended to:

  • Restore fiscal credibility

  • Improve investor confidence

  • Support macroeconomic stability

However, differences over fiscal outlook assumptions and debt management strategies remain unresolved.

 

Negotiations Continue

Despite the disagreements, talks between Senegal and the IMF are ongoing. The government maintains that it can stabilise the economy without resorting to debt restructuring.

IMF negotiations often shape fiscal policy, interest rates, and public spending priorities. For MSMEs in Senegal and the wider region, the outcome will influence credit conditions, government procurement opportunities, currency stability, and overall investor confidence.
South African rand Holds Steady Ahead of National Budget Announcement
Markets watch for fiscal consolidation signals as investors assess debt, deficit and reform plans.

The South African rand remained steady ahead of the national budget, with investors expecting continued fiscal consolidation and deficit reduction. A credible fiscal update could strengthen currency stability, while global risk factors remain a key watchpoint.

The South African rand traded largely flat on Tuesday as investors awaited the national budget speech by Finance Minister Enoch Godongwana. Markets expect the government to maintain its fiscal consolidation path, with analysts forecasting further progress in deficit reduction and debt stabilisation.

By mid-afternoon trading, the rand hovered around 16.02 per U.S. dollar, reflecting cautious positioning ahead of policy clarity.

 

Budget in Focus

The upcoming budget is expected to outline:

  • Fiscal priorities of the ruling coalition

  • Strategies to manage national debt

  • Plans to reduce the budget deficit

  • Ongoing structural and economic reforms

Analysts anticipate:

  • Faster deficit reduction

  • A widening primary surplus

  • Stabilising public debt levels

A credible fiscal update could support the rand and reinforce confidence in South Africa’s macroeconomic direction.

 

Market and Economic Signals

Additional indicators shaping sentiment include:

  • South African Reserve Bank’s leading business cycle indicator, which fell 1.0% month-on-month in December

  • Producer price inflation data due later this week

  • Upcoming releases on money supply, private sector credit, trade balance and budget balance

Meanwhile:

  • The Johannesburg Stock Exchange Top-40 index declined 0.4%

  • The 2035 government bond yield rose to 7.945%, reflecting mild pressure in the fixed-income market

Analysts warn that while fiscal discipline could support the currency, renewed global trade tensions or risk aversion may reverse recent gains.

Fiscal consolidation influences interest rates, taxation, government spending and access to credit. For MSMEs, a stable rand and disciplined budget framework can improve investor confidence, lower borrowing risks and create a more predictable business environment across Southern Africa.
Central Bank of Nigeria Cuts Benchmark Rate to 26.5% as African Easing Cycle Deepens
Nigeria joins six African peers in lowering rates amid easing inflation and growth concerns.

Nigeria has cut its benchmark rate to 26.5%, joining six other African economies in an easing cycle as inflation moderates. While the move signals improved macro stability, structural constraints may limit immediate relief for businesses seeking affordable credit.

Nigeria has reduced its benchmark interest rate by 50 basis points to 26.5%, marking its second cut in five months and the lowest level since June 2024. The decision, announced by CBN Governor Olayemi Cardoso at the first Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) meeting of 2026, signals a gradual shift from aggressive tightening toward supporting economic growth as inflation moderates.

Headline inflation eased to 15.10% in January, its tenth consecutive monthly decline, according to the National Bureau of Statistics.

 

Nigeria’s Monetary Policy Shift

Key highlights from the MPC decision:

  • Policy rate (MPR): Reduced to 26.5%

  • Asymmetric corridor: Retained at +50/-450 basis points

  • Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR):

    • 45% for deposit money banks

    • 16% for merchant banks

  • Liquidity ratio: Maintained at 30%

This follows a prior cut in September 2025 to 27%, after years of cumulative tightening totalling 875 basis points.

 

Part of a Broader African Easing Trend

Nigeria is among seven of 13 African central banks that have cut rates this year, including:

  • Kenya

  • Egypt

  • Angola

  • Ghana

  • Mozambique

  • Zambia

Five of the easing countries rank among Africa’s 10 largest economies, reflecting a broad policy pivot across the continent.

Notably:

  • Ghana has cut rates by 250 basis points to 15.5%

  • Egypt and Angola each reduced rates by 100 basis points

  • Zambia trimmed rates to 13.5%

  • Kenya and Mozambique delivered modest 25 basis point cuts

However, countries such as South Africa, Uganda, Tanzania, Namibia and Botswana have held rates steady, citing inflation and currency risks.

 

Will Businesses Feel the Impact?

Despite the rate cut, borrowing costs remain high due to structural constraints, including:

  • Elevated CRR levels

  • High deposit costs

  • Risk premiums tied to macroeconomic uncertainty

  • Government borrowing pressures

  • High banking sector operating costs

Analysts caution that unless these rigidities ease, the transmission of lower policy rates into affordable business loans may remain limited.

Lower benchmark rates can improve investor confidence and gradually ease financing conditions. However, the real benefit for MSMEs depends on whether commercial banks pass through lower rates into reduced borrowing costs for manufacturers, agribusinesses and SMEs.
Dangote Petroleum Refinery to Supply Up to 65 Million Litres of Dangote Refinery Scales to 65 Million Litres Per Day, Creating Downstream Growth Opportunities for Businesses Petrol Daily
Nigeria moves toward fuel self-sufficiency with surplus capacity for exports.

Dangote Refinery plans to supply up to 65 million litres of petrol daily, exceeding Nigeria’s domestic demand and enabling exports of up to 20 million litres. The move marks a major shift toward fuel self-sufficiency and improved energy stability.

Nigeria’s downstream petroleum landscape is entering a new phase as Dangote Petroleum Refinery & Petrochemicals announces plans to supply between 60 and 65 million litres of Premium Motor Spirit (PMS) daily to the domestic market. The move is expected to meet national demand while enabling exports of up to 20 million litres in surplus output.

Aliko Dangote, President of the Dangote Group, confirmed that a structured offtake agreement has been concluded with selected marketers to ensure nationwide distribution and supply stability.

 

Domestic Demand Fully Covered

Key figures:

  • Planned daily PMS supply: 60–65 million litres

  • Nigeria’s estimated daily consumption: 50–60 million litres

  • Surplus for export: 15–20 million litres

The refinery’s output exceeds current domestic demand, marking a decisive shift from decades of petrol import dependence.

 

Structured Distribution Model

Under a revised framework endorsed by the Nigerian Midstream and Downstream Petroleum Regulatory Authority, supply will be channelled through major marketing companies, including:

  • Nigerian National Petroleum Company Limited Retail

  • MRS Oil Nigeria Plc

  • 11 Plc

  • TotalEnergies Marketing Nigeria Plc

  • Ardova Plc

  • Rainoil, Northwest, Bovas, AA Rano, AYM Shafa, Conoil, Masters Energy and others

The model aims to:

  • Eliminate supply bottlenecks

  • Reduce speculative practices

  • Improve nationwide distribution efficiency

 

Operational Performance Exceeds Expectations

The refinery, designed for 650,000 barrels per day (bpd), is reportedly operating above expectations.

According to NNPC Limited’s Group CEO, Bayo Bashir Ojulari:

  • Live operational output reached 661,000 bpd

  • Performance exceeded initial industry expectations

Industry observers describe the facility as a transformative national asset capable of strengthening energy security and industrial capacity.

 

Macroeconomic Impact

With local refining now exceeding national demand, Nigeria could:

  • Reduce billions of dollars in annual fuel import costs

  • Ease pressure on foreign exchange reserves

  • Strengthen the naira

  • Improve trade balance stability

The development represents one of the most significant structural shifts in Nigeria’s fuel supply chain in decades.

Reliable fuel supply reduces logistics disruptions, stabilises operating costs and improves business planning. For MSMEs in transport, manufacturing, agriculture and distribution, sustained local refining capacity could enhance cost predictability and reduce exposure to forex-driven fuel price volatility.
Securities and Exchange Commission Nigeria: Capital Market Value Jumps 125%, GDP Contribution Rises to 33%
Market capitalisation surpasses ₦123.9 trillion as regulators push for deeper liquidity and broader participation.

Nigeria’s capital market has grown 125% in two years, with its GDP contribution rising from 13% to 33%. While growth signals strong investor confidence, regulators are now prioritising liquidity, broader participation and structural reforms to sustain momentum.

Nigeria’s capital market has expanded significantly over the past two years, with market capitalisation rising by 125% from approximately ₦55 trillion in April 2024 to over ₦123.93 trillion. According to SEC Director-General Emomotimi Agama, the market’s contribution to national GDP has also climbed from 13% to 33%, underscoring its growing role in economic development.

The announcement was made during the inauguration of the Capital Market Working Group on Market Liquidity in Lagos.

 

Strong Growth, Expanding Economic Role

Key highlights:

  • Market capitalisation: ₦55 trillion (April 2024) → ₦123.93 trillion

  • GDP contribution: 13% → 33%

  • Growth reflects improved investor confidence and resilience

Agama noted that while growth figures are impressive, long-term sustainability depends on improving market depth and liquidity.

 

Liquidity Remains a Priority

Despite rapid expansion, structural challenges persist:

  • High transaction impact costs for institutional investors

  • Trading concentration in a limited number of large-cap stocks

  • Shallow participation across broader segments

The SEC warns that insufficient liquidity may discourage investors concerned about exiting positions without price distortions.

 

Reform Agenda and Market Deepening

To address these issues, the SEC has inaugurated a multi-stakeholder Working Group including:

  • Exchanges

  • Custodians

  • Fund managers

  • Dealing members

  • Other market operators

The group’s mandate includes:

  • Reviewing trading and settlement infrastructure

  • Identifying structural bottlenecks

  • Enhancing transaction speed and efficiency

  • Aligning settlement cycles with global standards

  • Expanding retail investor participation

The SEC aims to onboard up to 20 million new investors through digital platforms, dematerialisation of share certificates and fintech partnerships.

 

Digital Assets and Product Innovation

The newly enacted Investments and Securities Act (ISA) 2025 expands regulatory oversight to include digital assets, creating pathways to formalise speculative activity into regulated investment channels.

Additionally, the SEC plans to accelerate:

  • Development of derivatives markets

  • Introduction of new asset classes

  • Broader hedging instruments

These measures are intended to deepen liquidity and enhance price discovery.

A deeper and more liquid capital market improves access to funding for businesses, supports infrastructure financing and strengthens investor confidence. For MSMEs, sustained market growth can translate into expanded financing options, stronger equity markets and improved long-term capital formation.
Nigerian Exchange Limited Retreats 0.92% After MPC Rate Cut
Equities pause record rally as investors reassess positions following monetary policy easing.

Nigeria’s stock market declined 0.92% after the MPC cut interest rates to 26.50%, ending its recent rally. While investors turned cautious in the short term, analysts expect continued sector rotation and volatility amid ongoing monetary easing.

Nigeria’s stock market snapped its winning streak on Tuesday, declining 0.92% after the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) reduced the benchmark rate by 50 basis points to 26.50%. The pullback follows a record-breaking run that saw the NGX All-Share Index (ASI) cross the 194,000-point mark earlier this year.

Investors adopted a cautious stance, triggering profit-taking in large-cap and consumer-facing stocks despite expectations that lower rates could support equities over the medium term.

 

MPC Decisions at a Glance

At its 304th meeting (February 23–24), the MPC:

  • Cut the Monetary Policy Rate (MPR) to 26.50%

  • Adjusted the asymmetric corridor to +50/-450 bps

  • Retained CRR at:

    • 45% for Deposit Money Banks

    • 16% for Merchant Banks

  • Maintained the Liquidity Ratio at 30%

The move signals gradual monetary easing amid moderating inflation.

 

Market Reaction

  • NGX ASI: Fell to 194,484.61 points

  • Market capitalisation: Declined to ₦124.827 trillion

  • Year-to-date return: Moderated to 24.98%

Sector performance diverged:

  • Banking and Industrials: Showed resilience

  • Consumer Goods and Insurance: Led the decline

Trading activity was mixed:

  • Total value traded: Rose 92.58% to ₦53.35 billion

  • Total volume traded: Fell 4.94% to 1.14 billion units

Analysts suggest investors are rotating capital into dividend-yielding and value stocks while locking in gains from the market’s 17.6% month-to-date rally.

 

Outlook: Consolidation Phase?

Market watchers expect short-term volatility as traders balance:

  • Profit-taking after strong gains

  • Sector rotation strategies

  • Anticipation of dividend announcements

  • Reaction to policy easing

Some analysts note that lower interest rates could, over time, increase equity allocations as investors seek stronger real returns compared to fixed-income assets.

Equity market performance reflects broader investor confidence and liquidity conditions. A stable and growing stock market can improve access to capital, strengthen investor sentiment and support business expansion opportunities for MSMEs.
National Pension Commission: FGN Securities Drive Pension Assets to ₦27.5 Trillion
Rising yields and government borrowing lift pension fund investments in Federal Government bonds.

Nigeria’s pension assets rose 22% to ₦27.45 trillion, driven by increased investment in Federal Government securities, which now account for nearly 60% of total assets. Rising yields and regulatory guidelines continue to channel pension funds into low-risk government bonds.

Nigeria’s pension fund assets have surged to ₦27.45 trillion, up 22% from ₦22.51 trillion in December 2024, largely driven by increased investments in Federal Government of Nigeria (FGN) securities. Data released by the National Pension Commission (PenCom) highlights strong growth in fixed-income allocations amid a high-interest-rate environment.

The expansion reflects both regulatory investment limits and the attractiveness of government bonds as low-risk instruments.

 

Key Growth Figures

  • Total Assets Under Management (AuM):

    • ₦27.451 trillion (Dec 2025)

    • Up from ₦22.512 trillion (Dec 2024)

  • Investment in FGN securities:

    • ₦16.334 trillion

    • Up 15.7% Year-on-Year

  • Share of total pension assets in government securities:

    • 59.5%

 

Fixed-Income Allocation Trends

Further breakdown shows:

  • Treasury bills holdings:

    • Increased 8% YoY to ₦761.094 billion

  • Sukuk bonds (HTM & AFS):

    • Declined 9.2% YoY to ₦85.017 billion

Analysts attribute the growth in government securities to:

  • Elevated interest rates in 2025

  • Increased government borrowing to finance budget deficits

  • Regulatory guidelines mandating substantial allocation to FGN instruments

FGN securities are considered gilt-edged (low-risk) assets, making them well-aligned with pension fund safety requirements.

 

Why Pension Flows Matter

As pension assets expand, their concentration in government debt instruments strengthens demand for sovereign bonds and supports fiscal financing.

However, the high allocation to government securities also reflects structural portfolio constraints under PenCom guidelines, which prioritise capital preservation and risk management.

Strong pension fund growth enhances domestic capital market depth and government financing stability. However, heavy concentration in government securities may limit the pool of long-term capital available for private-sector and MSME financing, underscoring the need for balanced market development.
Lafarge Africa Plc Honours Top Trade Partners to Drive 2026 Growth
Building materials leader rewards distributors and transporters as it expands retail reach and low-carbon product lines.

Lafarge Africa Plc honoured its top-performing distributors and transporters at its 2025 trade partner awards, highlighting retail expansion and new low-carbon product launches. The initiative reinforces supply chain strength and sets the stage for stronger growth in 2026.

Lafarge Africa Plc has recognised its top-performing trade partners at its 2025 annual awards ceremony in Lagos, celebrating customers and transporters who played a key role in nationwide distribution and business growth. The event also set the tone for stronger collaboration and performance in 2026.

The gathering brought together customers, logistics partners, government representatives and key stakeholders from across Nigeria’s construction value chain.

 

Strengthening Partnerships for Growth

Company leadership highlighted the strategic role of partners in sustaining nationwide supply and market expansion.

Key 2025 milestones shared at the event include:

  • Strengthening of the ready-mix concrete business

Top Award Winners

  • First Runner-Up:

    • C.C. Umeh and Sons Limited – Toyota Prado

Logistics and Distribution as Strategic Drivers

The awards were positioned not only as recognition but as motivation to enhance performance, service quality and market penetration in 2026.

Strong distribution networks are essential for scaling manufacturing and construction businesses across Africa. Recognising and incentivising trade partners strengthens supply chains, improves reliability and encourages higher performance standards, key factors for MSMEs operating in competitive, logistics-sensitive markets.
Lagos State Government of Nigeria Lists ₦244.82bn Bonds on FMDQ Securities Exchange Limited
Landmark green and fixed-rate bond issuance strengthens sustainable infrastructure financing.

Lagos State has listed ₦244.82 billion in green and fixed-rate bonds on FMDQ, supporting sustainable infrastructure and long-term development. The issuance strengthens sub-national capital market participation and advances Nigeria’s green finance agenda.

Lagos State has secured approval to list ₦244.82 billion in dual-series bonds on the FMDQ Securities Exchange platform, marking one of the most significant sub-national debt market transactions in Nigeria’s history. The listing includes a ₦14.82 billion 5-Year 16.00% Series 3 Green Bond and a ₦230 billion 10-Year 16.25% Series 4 Fixed Rate Bond, issued under the State’s ₦1 trillion Debt and Hybrid Instrument Issuance Programme.

The approval underscores Lagos State’s continued leadership in sustainable finance and long-term infrastructure funding.

 

Bond Structure at a Glance

Series 3 Green Bond

  • ₦14.82 billion

  • 5-year tenor

  • 16.00% fixed rate

  • Proceeds dedicated exclusively to eligible green projects

Series 4 Fixed Rate Bond

  • ₦230 billion

  • 10-year tenor

  • 16.25% fixed rate

  • Designed for stable long-term financing

The issuance was sponsored by Chapel Hill Denham Advisory Limited, a registration member (Listings) of FMDQ Exchange.

 

Focus on Sustainable Infrastructure

Proceeds from the Green Bond will finance projects aligned with global sustainability standards, including:

  • Renewable energy

  • Energy efficiency

  • Clean transportation

  • Sustainable water management

  • Climate resilience

  • Environmentally responsible urban infrastructure

The broader bond issuance will support critical infrastructure and strategic development programmes aimed at improving public service delivery across Lagos State.

 

Strategic Importance

As Nigeria’s commercial hub, contributing roughly 30% of national GDP, Lagos State relies heavily on long-term capital to sustain growth, urban development, and public infrastructure for its more than 20 million residents.

This dual-series listing:

  • Reinforces FMDQ’s role as a trusted capital market platform

  • Demonstrates sub-national access to transparent long-term funding

  • Strengthens Nigeria’s sustainable finance ecosystem petitive, logistics-sensitive markets.

Infrastructure financing improves transportation, energy reliability, water systems, and urban development, all critical to business productivity. For MSMEs, sustainable capital mobilisation at the sub-national level creates a more stable operating environment, enhances logistics efficiency, and unlocks long-term economic growth opportunities.
NGX Extends Bearish Run as Market Capitalisation Sheds N73.43bn

The Nigerian Exchange closed marginally lower, with market capitalisation declining by N73.43 billion as profit-taking in banking and insurance stocks outweighed gains in consumer counters. Trading volume increased, but overall value declined, reflecting cautious investor sentiment. Market breadth remained negative, with sell pressure dominating despite selective buying interest in food and agribusiness stocks.

Trading on the Nigerian Exchange Limited (NGX) closed on a mildly negative note on Wednesday, as profit-taking in bellwether banking and insurance stocks offset pockets of bargain hunting across consumer and select oil and gas counters. The All-Share Index (ASI) declined 0.06 percent to 194,370.20 points from 194,484.61 points, reflecting a modest contraction in equities valuation and extending the market’s cautious tone this week.

Correspondingly, market capitalisation eased to N124.75 trillion, down from N124.83 trillion, representing a loss of N73.43 billion in investors’ wealth. The decline extends the exchange’s recent bearish streak, as investors continue to rebalance portfolios following prior gains in key sectors, particularly financial services, which had outperformed in recent sessions.

Activity metrics were mixed. Total volume traded rose 21.0 percent to 1.4 billion units, suggesting heightened trading interest and increased retail participation. However, total value traded declined 13.4 percent to N46.2 billion, indicating reduced participation from large institutional investors and fewer block trades.

FTG Insure led the volume chart with 193.7 million units traded, accounting for 14.1 percent of total turnover. Meanwhile, Zenith Bank topped the value chart with N11.1 billion worth of transactions, representing 24.0 percent of total traded value, underscoring sustained investor interest in tier-1 banking stocks despite sector-wide profit-taking.

Sectoral Performance Shows Divergence

The NGX Banking Index retreated as profit-taking weighed on tier-1 lenders, with investors locking in gains following the sector’s earlier rally driven by strong earnings expectations and attractive dividend yields. Similarly, the NGX Insurance Index recorded broad-based losses across underwriting stocks, contributing significantly to the market’s negative breadth as sell pressure intensified in small- and mid-cap insurers.

The NGX Oil & Gas Index edged lower to 4,066.48 points, reflecting mild profit-taking in energy counters despite selective buying interest in downstream players amid stable crude oil price outlooks.

In contrast, the NGX Consumer Goods Index appreciated, supported by renewed demand in food and beverage stocks. Gains in BUA Foods and Dangote Sugar Refinery Plc provided partial cushioning to the broader market decline, as investors rotated into defensive consumer names viewed as relatively resilient in uncertain market conditions.

The NGX Industrial Index eased marginally as weakness in cement majors tempered recent upward momentum in the sector, reflecting cautious positioning ahead of macroeconomic data and corporate earnings releases.

Market breadth closed negative, with 22 gainers against 54 losers, underscoring the dominance of sell pressure despite selective advances in mid- and large-cap stocks.

Advancers were led by Jaiz Bank Plc, Okomu Oil Palm Company Plc, and Trans-Nationwide Express Plc, each posting near double-digit gains on renewed buying interest.

On the downside, ABC Transport Plc, RT Briscoe Plc, and Skyway Aviation Handling Company Plc recorded the steepest declines, while weakness in financial services and diversified industrial counters amplified the market’s subdued tone.

The NGX’s continued bearish tilt reflects cautious sentiment as investors rebalance portfolios and take profits in key sectors. While consumer stocks provided limited support, persistent sell pressure in financial and insurance counters suggests that near-term market direction will depend on macroeconomic signals, interest rate expectations, and upcoming corporate earnings performance.

Stock market performance often signals broader investor confidence and liquidity conditions within the economy. Sustained bearish trends can affect capital raising opportunities, investor appetite, and access to financing for growing businesses. However, sectoral resilience—particularly in consumer goods—may indicate pockets of strength that MSMEs can leverage through partnerships, supply chains, and distribution networks.
Nigeria Needs 12% Annual Growth to Achieve $1tn Economy Target – Minister

Nigeria requires sustained annual growth of 10 to 12 percent over the next decade to reach a one trillion dollar economy, according to Finance Minister Doris Uzoka Anite. The government is implementing fiscal restructuring, infrastructure expansion, domestic value addition, and credit reforms to support long term growth. Complementary initiatives in housing, energy, and digital infrastructure are expected to strengthen economic fundamentals.

Nigeria requires sustained annual economic growth of between 10 and 12 percent over the next decade to achieve the one trillion dollar economy target set by President Bola Tinubu, Minister of State for Finance, Doris Uzoka-Anite, has said.

Speaking at the 2026 Annual General Meeting of the Finance Correspondents Association of Nigeria in Abuja, the minister, represented by the Assistant Director of Information and Public Relations at the Federal Ministry of Finance, Mrs Uloma Amadi, stated that Nigeria’s gross domestic product currently stands at about 375 billion dollars.

“To reach one trillion dollars requires sustained GDP growth of between 10 and 12 percent annually over the coming decade,” she said, describing the target as a clear and measurable destination supported by structural reforms designed to lay the foundation for long term expansion.

Reforming the Economic Framework

Uzoka Anite noted that when the current administration assumed office in 2023, Nigeria’s economic fundamentals were structurally distorted. She cited fuel subsidy payments exceeding five trillion naira annually and a multiple exchange rate regime that encouraged rent seeking and weakened investor confidence.

She pointed to recent macroeconomic improvements, including a positive outlook revision by S&P Global Ratings, which affirmed Nigeria’s B slash B credit ratings and cited progress in fiscal, monetary, and external indicators.

On fiscal management, the minister said the Federal Government has restructured its budget framework to treat investment expenditure as a distinct pillar separate from recurrent spending. According to her, this approach encourages discipline by shifting focus from the volume of spending to the value created through public investment.

She disclosed that the second phase of reforms is anchored on the Disinflation and Growth Acceleration Strategy, developed jointly with the Central Bank of Nigeria. The strategy operates under a nine pillar implementation framework aimed at delivering growth above seven percent by 2027 without reigniting inflation.

The pillars include capital mobilisation through development finance instruments, sectoral acceleration in agriculture, energy, technology, manufacturing, and creative industries, and a nationwide energy expansion programme covering oil, gas, solar, hydro, and emerging fuels. Other components focus on digital infrastructure such as broadband and data centres, as well as technical and vocational training targeting over 10 million young Nigerians annually.

Domestic Value Addition and Infrastructure Push

Uzoka Anite observed that Nigeria imports about 70% of its industrial raw materials, limiting control over long term production costs. She cited the Dangote Refinery as an example of domestic value addition capable of boosting employment, tax revenues, and household income.

She also highlighted Nigeria’s removal from the Financial Action Task Force grey list, describing it as evidence of strengthened anti money laundering and counter terrorism financing frameworks. In addition, Nigeria has submitted its ECOWAS Tariff Offer to the African Continental Free Trade Area Secretariat, providing zero duties on 90% of goods traded within Africa.

In a related development, the Ministry of Finance Incorporated unveiled details of a one trillion naira private sector driven programme to transform the mortgage market. The initiative, registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission, has already deployed 75 billion naira from an initial 250 billion naira tranche.

Mortgages under the scheme are offered at 9.75% interest for up to 20 years, with participating institutions including Sterling Bank and Stanbic IBTC Bank.

Meanwhile, the Nigeria Infrastructure Fund under the Nigeria Sovereign Investment Authority said Nigeria requires between 100 billion and 150 billion dollars annually in infrastructure investment to close existing gaps and support the one trillion dollar ambition.

Nigeria’s one trillion dollar economy target reflects a structured growth agenda built on fiscal discipline, private sector mobilisation, and infrastructure development. The pace and consistency of reform implementation will determine how quickly the country can translate policy ambition into measurable and inclusive economic expansion.

Sustained high growth would expand market size, improve consumer purchasing power, and deepen access to financing. Reforms focused on infrastructure, energy, housing, and domestic production could lower operating costs and strengthen supply chains, creating broader opportunities for MSMEs across Nigeria and the African continent.
Wage Arrears: Labour Issues Friday Ultimatum to FG

Labour unions in Nigeria’s federal public service have set a Friday deadline for the Federal Government to release three months of unpaid wages and other pending allowances. Failure to comply will prompt decisive actions, potentially including industrial strikes, as government agencies are reportedly ready to process payments but are constrained by the Ministry of Finance.

Organised labour in Nigeria’s federal public service has issued a stern ultimatum to the Federal Government, demanding the immediate release of funds to clear three months of unpaid wages and other pending allowances. The Joint National Public Service Negotiating Council (Trade Union Side) conveyed the notice in a letter to the Federal Ministry of Labour and Employment, warning that failure to comply by Friday, February 27, 2026, will compel eight unions within the civil service to take decisive action.

The unions accused the government of deliberately withholding funds, despite agencies being ready to process payments once the Ministry of Finance releases the allocations. They described the prolonged delay as deeply unfair to workers, who have already endured over two years of disrupted payments.

Persistent Wage Disputes and Delayed Allowances

The dispute stems from the implementation of the N70,000 minimum wage following the removal of fuel subsidies. Labour leaders highlighted that while partial payments were made under pressure, three months remain outstanding since July 2024. The unions argued that such delays violate workers’ rights and undermine morale in the federal workforce.

The unions also listed other pending obligations requiring urgent attention, including:

  • Promotion arrears for workers elevated more than three years ago

  • Salary arrears for employees recruited between 2015 and 2024

  • Implementation of a 40 percent peculiar allowance linked to the N70,000 minimum wage

Threat of Industrial Action

In their letter, the unions warned that if funds are not released by the deadline, they would take “appropriate measures” to ensure compliance, signalling potential industrial action. Copies of the letter have been sent to the Office of the Head of the Civil Service of the Federation, Nigeria Labour Congress, Trade Union Congress, security agencies, and affiliate unions.

The unions stressed that workers’ entitlements should not be treated lightly, and delaying payments further exacerbates economic hardship for public servants and their families.

Resolving the wage arrears promptly is crucial to maintaining public sector stability and sustaining economic confidence. Swift action by the Federal Government would prevent workforce unrest, protect household incomes, and ensure that public spending continues to support broader market activity, including MSME development across Nigeria.

Delayed public sector wage payments can significantly affect African MSMEs. Workers unable to access salaries reduce spending on goods and services, impacting local businesses’ revenue and cash flow. Timely payments enhance consumer purchasing power, stimulate demand in local markets, and strengthen financial systems that MSMEs rely on for credit and growth.
Nigeria Advances Air Cargo Reforms to Boost Non-Oil Exports

Nigeria’s Federal Airports Authority has launched air cargo reforms to expand non-oil exports. Key initiatives include new cargo terminals, improved handling procedures, stakeholder coordination, digital reforms, and revenue collection adjustments. The reforms aim to reduce logistical bottlenecks, enhance export efficiency, and position Nigeria as a regional cargo hub.

Nigeria is intensifying efforts to expand non-oil exports through strategic air cargo reforms led by the Federal Airports Authority of Nigeria (FAAN). The initiative, which includes new infrastructure, stakeholder engagement, and revenue reforms, aims to position Nigeria as a regional export hub and reduce logistical bottlenecks for perishable agricultural goods.

For years, Nigeria’s agricultural potential has been hindered by logistical challenges that prevent goods from reaching international markets efficiently. Perishable produce such as mangoes, okra, and leafy vegetables often spoil before leaving the country due to inadequate cold chains and fragmented cargo systems.

Recognising this, the Honourable Minister of Aviation and Aerospace Development, Festus Keyamo, established a dedicated Cargo Development Directorate in December 2024. Under the leadership of Director Lekan Thomas, the directorate is spearheading efforts to streamline air cargo operations, modernise infrastructure, and improve export logistics.

Significant progress includes the commissioning of a domestic cargo terminal at the General Aviation Terminal in Lagos in May 2025. This facility is designed to reduce bottlenecks, improve handling efficiency, and attract national and international freight operators. Plans are underway to replicate similar facilities in Abuja and Kano, creating a nationwide network to support export growth.

Stakeholder Coordination and Digital Reforms

The directorate is engaging stakeholders across the supply chain, including the Nigeria Customs Service, freight forwarders, cooperatives, and regulatory agencies, to remove bureaucratic hurdles and improve efficiency. Digital reforms, such as single-window clearance systems and integrated cold-chain solutions, are also being prioritised to align Nigeria with global export standards.

Revenue collection reforms have resumed after a 15-year hiatus, with FAAN directly collecting cargo fees at the Murtala Muhammed International Airport. While adjustments to tariffs—from N7 to a compromise of N15 per kilogram—have faced opposition, authorities argue that sustainable revenue is critical for ongoing cargo infrastructure improvements.

Expert Insights and Ongoing Challenges

Aviation expert Alex Nwuba sees the reforms as part of a long-term strategy to elevate Nigeria from fourth to first in Africa’s air cargo ranking. He highlighted the importance of reducing post-harvest losses and coordinating states’ infrastructure with national export goals.

However, John Ojikutu, CEO of Centurion Security Limited, cautions that reforms must be backed by verifiable traffic and export data. Without clear statistics on cargo throughput, he warns, the initiative risks misallocation of resources and limited impact on actual exports. Ojikutu advocates for concessioning terminals to private operators to improve efficiency and revenue collection.

Despite these challenges, the reforms signal a paradigm shift for Nigeria’s air cargo sector, transforming airports from passive property managers into active logistics enablers that support agricultural exports and economic diversification.

While Nigeria’s cargo reforms face challenges around infrastructure, stakeholder coordination, and reliable data, the strategic focus on modern terminals, integrated supply chains, and export facilitation demonstrates a commitment to economic diversification. Successful implementation could transform Nigeria into a leading exporter of perishable goods and strengthen the country’s position in regional and global trade.

Efficient air cargo infrastructure directly benefits African MSMEs by lowering transportation costs, reducing product spoilage, and enabling faster access to international markets. Reliable logistics can increase competitiveness, stimulate trade, and expand market opportunities for small- and medium-sized agricultural producers.
Microfinance Banks Lead Credit and Digital Payments in Nigeria’s Financial Inclusion Drive

Microfinance banks account for 95% of total lending and control 85% of POS terminals in Nigeria. Industry leaders are emphasising capacity building, corporate governance, and regulatory compliance as key drivers of sustainable growth. The sector remains central to financial inclusion, particularly for low-income individuals and small businesses.

Microfinance banks (MFBs) remain at the forefront of Nigeria’s financial inclusion drive, accounting for 95% of total lending in the sector and controlling 85% of point-of-sale (POS) terminals nationwide, Francis Akie, Chairman of the National Association of Microfinance Banks, FCT Chapter, has said.

Akie, who is also Chief Executive Officer of Prudent Microfinance Bank, spoke with journalists in Abuja on Wednesday during a corporate governance training for directors and managing directors of microfinance banks in the Federal Capital Territory.

The training session, Akie explained, was aimed at enhancing the capacity of microfinance directors, board members, and managing directors to ensure compliance with regulatory standards and sustain sector growth.

“We have gathered all the directors, chairmen, and MDs of various microfinance banks in the FCT to enhance their capacity. We have discovered that without capacity building, we cannot move forward,” Akie said.

He emphasised the role of corporate governance in maintaining regulatory compliance and operational stability. “Without corporate governance in this system, in our microfinance ecosystem, we will not be able to comply with basic rules and regulations of the Central Bank of Nigeria,” he stated.

Dr Victor Dare, founder and CEO of Better Ways, led discussions on governance and compliance, highlighting that weak oversight and poor board-management relations have caused failures in some financial institutions. “Corporate governance goes beyond routine monitoring. It is the foundation of sustainability, profitability, and the going concern of any bank,” he said.

Microfinance Banks and Financial Inclusion

Akie highlighted that MFBs remain central to reaching low-income Nigerians. “We are in the forefront of the financial inclusion drive because we deal with the people at the bottom of the pyramid. We know where they are and what they need from us,” he said.

He noted that loan recovery requires clear processes before credit disbursement, underscoring the need for strong internal controls.

Dr Dare also stressed that Nigeria is utilising less than 30 per cent of the potential of microfinance institutions. He warned that applying the same regulatory standards used for commercial banks could limit MFBs’ ability to serve low-income clients while remaining profitable.

Recent data underscores the sector’s dominance. As of September 2024, 6,253 out of 6,537 total bank debtors borrowed from MFBs, according to The PUNCH. This highlights the pivotal role of microfinance banks in providing credit to individuals and small businesses.

MFBs also control a significant share of the country’s POS ecosystem. Nigerians conducted transactions worth N85.91 trillion through POS terminals in the first half of 2024, compared to N12.21 trillion through ATMs. POS transaction values grew 77 per cent year-on-year, while transaction volumes rose 31 per cent, reflecting a strong shift toward digital payments.

Nigeria’s microfinance banks are proving critical to the financial ecosystem, combining credit provision and digital payment infrastructure. Strengthened governance, regulatory compliance, and capacity development will be essential to sustain growth, enhance financial inclusion, and support the country’s small businesses.

Strong MFBs improve access to credit for micro, small, and medium enterprises, helping them expand operations, meet working capital needs, and grow local economies. Greater POS coverage also enables MSMEs to participate in the digital economy, reducing reliance on cash and improving transaction transparency.
Seplat Energy Plc Grows FY2025 Pre-Tax Profit by 86.7% as Revenue Surges to $2.73bn
Strong offshore performance, gas expansion and cost discipline drive record earnings and higher shareholder returns.

Seplat Energy grew FY2025 pre-tax profit by 86.7% to $497.8 million as revenue climbed to $2.73 billion. Strong offshore operations, gas expansion and cost efficiency positioned the company for continued growth and higher shareholder returns in 2026.

Seplat Energy Plc has reported a strong financial performance for the year ended December 2025, with profit before tax rising 86.7% to $497.8 million, up from $266.7 million in 2024. Revenue more than doubled to $2.726 billion, reflecting a full year contribution from offshore assets and expanded gas operations.

The results, released on both the Nigerian and London Stock Exchanges, underscore Seplat’s growing scale as an integrated African energy player.

 

Financial Performance Highlights

  • Revenue: $2.726 billion (+144.2% YoY)

  • Gross Profit: $904.5 million (+156.4%)

  • Adjusted EBITDA: $1.275 billion (+137%)

  • Cash from Operations: $1.165 billion (+276%)

  • Net Debt: $673.3 million (down 25% YoY)

  • Net Debt/EBITDA: 0.53x

Unit production operating costs declined 5% to $15.7/boe, reflecting improved efficiency.

The company declared a total dividend of 25.0 cents per share for 2025, representing a 52% increase year-on-year.

 

Production and Growth Outlook

  • Average production: 131,506 boepd (+148% YoY)

  • 2026 production guidance: 135–155 kboepd

  • Gas production: +30% YoY

Key operational developments:

  • First gas achieved at the ANOH Gas Plant (50–70 MMscfd currently stable)

  • Oso-BRT project on track to double offshore gas sales to 240 MMscfd by 2026

  • 17 new wells planned (15 onshore, 2 offshore)

  • First offshore jack-up rig arriving 3Q 2026

Initial 2026 capex guidance stands at $360–440 million.

 

Operational Efficiency and Sustainability

  • Emissions intensity for onshore assets reduced 24% YoY

  • Successful idle well restoration added 48.6 kboepd capacity

  • Offshore NGL recovery peaked at ~33 kboepd in early 2026

The company maintained strong safety performance, recording one Lost Time Injury in 2025 with over 11 million hours worked without incident since September 2024.

 

Strategic Direction

Seplat reaffirmed its ambition to “Build an African Energy Champion,” targeting 200 kboepd production by 2030 and $1 billion cumulative capital returns to shareholders.

Management also highlighted reduced borrowing costs and a strengthened balance sheet, providing additional flexibility for long-term growth.

Energy sector expansion strengthens supply chains, drives gas-to-power reliability and supports industrial productivity. For MSMEs, improved domestic gas infrastructure and energy stability can reduce operating costs, enhance manufacturing capacity and stimulate broader economic activity.
Payaza Africa Limited Targets ₦50bn via Commercial Paper Issuance
Fintech firm seeks short-term capital under ₦150bn programme to strengthen working capital and scale merchant services.

Payaza Africa Limited is raising ₦50 billion through Series 1 and 2 Commercial Papers to fund working capital under its ₦150 billion programme. With a strong repayment record, upgraded credit ratings and expanding cross-border presence, the fintech firm is positioning itself for deeper growth in Africa’s digital payments ecosystem.

Payaza Africa Limited has launched the issuance of Series 1 and Series 2 Commercial Papers (CPs) to raise approximately ₦50 billion under its ₦150 billion Commercial Paper Issuance Programme. The offer opened on February 23 and closes on February 26, with proceeds earmarked to fund the company’s working capital requirements.

The move reflects continued expansion in Nigeria’s fast-growing digital payments ecosystem and Payaza’s strategy to deepen its merchant infrastructure across markets.

Issuance Details

• Target raise: ₦50 billion

• Programme size: ₦150 billion CP Issuance Programme

• Offer window: February 23 – February 26

• Purpose: Working capital financing

Since inception, Payaza has raised over ₦70 billion across six CP series, repaying ₦32.53 billion in full, while remaining obligations are within scheduled maturity.

Financial Track Record & Credit Profile

• Demonstrated history of timely repayment

• Current credit ratings:

o GCR: A3

o DataPro: A1 (upgraded from BBB+)

The company says it remains committed to maintaining strong investor confidence and capital market discipline.

Business Model & Market Footprint

Payaza operates a merchant-enabling payment platform that integrates:

• Card payments

• USSD

• QR codes

• Virtual accounts

• Mobile money

The platform supports both online and offline merchants and enables seamless integration across digital channels.

Key operational highlights:

• Certified partner for Mastercard and Visa

• Presence in 19+ countries across Africa, Asia, the Middle East and North America

• T+0 settlement time (versus industry average T+3)

• Registered payment gateway for global brands including Dunkin’ Donuts

The company holds multiple operational licenses and certifications across jurisdictions to ensure compliance with international standards.

Strategic Positioning

Payaza describes itself as one of the continent’s most compliance-focused payment gateways, positioning its CP issuance as both a funding strategy and a signal of growth ambition within Africa’s digital finance sector.

The working capital raise is expected to support transaction volume growth, merchant acquisition and operational scale.

Stronger fintech infrastructure improves cash flow speed, reduces settlement delays and enhances access to digital payment tools for SMEs. As platforms like Payaza scale, African businesses benefit from faster transactions, broader payment options and improved financial inclusion.
Germany’s Rheinland-Pfalz Targets Nigeria for Manufacturing and Renewable Energy Partnerships
German state signals shift from export-only trade to joint production and clean energy collaboration with Nigeria.
