Germany’s Rheinland-Pfalz state is positioning Nigeria as both a production base and renewable energy partner. The move signals a shift toward joint manufacturing, clean energy cooperation and deeper business-to-business trade ties that could benefit Nigeria’s industrial and SME ecosystem.

The German state of Rheinland-Pfalz has identified Nigeria as a strategic partner for expanded bilateral trade, local production and renewable energy cooperation. Leading an eight-company delegation to Lagos, state officials said the engagement marks a pivot from viewing Nigeria purely as a market to positioning it as a production base and energy transition partner.

The visit reflects growing European subnational interest in Nigeria’s industrial capacity, energy reforms and regional economic influence.

 

Expanding Trade Beyond Exports

Rheinland-Pfalz, one of Germany’s 16 federal states, has:

  • A population of approximately 4 million

  • A GDP of roughly €185 billion

  • Over €1.5 billion in trade with Nigeria

  • Ranked Nigeria as its fourth-largest African trading partner

Total trade between Germany and Nigeria reached about €3 billion in 2024, with officials noting strong potential for deeper engagement in:

  • Industrial manufacturing

  • Pharmaceuticals and chemicals

  • Construction materials and equipment

  • Renewable energy systems

The delegation includes firms in machinery, construction, wine production and technical equipment.

 

Renewable Energy Cooperation in Focus

A key area of interest is renewable energy integration and decentralised power systems.

Rheinland-Pfalz generates about 60% of its electricity from renewable sources, providing experience in:

  • Grid integration of intermittent energy

  • Decentralised clean energy deployment

  • Reliable technical and economic energy systems

Officials emphasised that renewable adoption must ensure consistent, round-the-clock reliability, an area where German firms believe they can contribute technical expertise.

 

Nigeria as a Production Base

Beyond trade, German officials indicated openness to:

  • Local manufacturing or assembly partnerships

  • Joint production models

  • Industrial cooperation serving West African markets

The approach prioritises business-to-business partnerships first, with government agreements expected to follow if necessary.

Plans are also underway to host Nigerian government representatives in Rheinland-Pfalz to deepen institutional engagement.

 

Strategic Context

The engagement signals a broader evolution in international trade relations:

  • Shift from export-only models to co-production

  • Increased focus on energy transition partnerships

  • Recognition of Nigeria’s growing regional economic influence

Global firms headquartered in Rheinland-Pfalz include BASF, Boehringer Ingelheim and BioNTech, reflecting the state’s strong industrial base.

If structured effectively, production partnerships and renewable collaborations can unlock technology transfer, supply-chain integration and export opportunities for Nigerian SMEs. Joint ventures and decentralised energy solutions could reduce power costs, expand manufacturing capacity and strengthen Nigeria’s role as a regional industrial hub.
UK-Funded Programme (Propcom+) Boosts Livestock Feed Production in Jigawa to Ease Farmer–Herder Tensions
Propcom+ backs commercial alfalfa production with irrigation, solar power and green logistics to strengthen livestock value chains.

A UK-funded programme is supporting large-scale alfalfa production in Jigawa through irrigation, solar power and green logistics investments. The initiative aims to stabilise livestock feed supply, boost smallholder incomes and reduce farmer–herder tensions while promoting climate-resilient agriculture.

A UK-funded agricultural development programme is supporting large-scale livestock feed production in Jigawa State as part of efforts to address Nigeria’s recurring farmer–herder conflicts and strengthen rural livelihoods.

Propcom+, funded by the UK Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO), has provided catalytic support to Matura Agro Farms to scale up commercial alfalfa production, targeting improved fodder availability, supply chain stability and climate-resilient agriculture.

 

Strengthening Nigeria’s Livestock Feed System

The intervention focuses on closing supply gaps in quality fodder by enabling year-round production and processing capacity.

Key investments include:

  • Installation of a 55-hectare centre-pivot irrigation system for continuous alfalfa cultivation

  • Deployment of a 100-kilowatt solar power system at the processing plant

  • Expansion of hay processing capacity from 10 tonnes to 80 tonnes per day

  • Introduction of electric three-wheelers to pilot green logistics

The solar upgrade addressed unreliable electricity that previously limited processing output.

 

Inclusive Supply Chain Model

The programme also enhances:

  • Aggregation systems for outgrower farmers

  • Access to improved inputs and technical knowledge

  • Guaranteed market linkages

  • Supply chain coordination for smallholders

This structure is designed to increase productivity and income for both commercial producers and smallholder farmers.

 

Climate and Conflict Dimension

Livestock production contributes to global methane emissions, making feed efficiency and sustainability critical policy areas.

By improving commercial fodder production:

  • Grazing pressure on farmland may reduce

  • Competition over land resources could ease

  • Livestock productivity can improve without expanding grazing territory

Propcom+ operates as an eight-year rural market development programme (2023–2030) and aims to impact 3.79 million smallholder farmers and entrepreneurs through income growth and enhanced climate resilience.

Reliable livestock feed supply strengthens Nigeria’s meat, dairy and agribusiness value chains, creating opportunities for input suppliers, processors, transporters and agro-SMEs. Improved irrigation, solar-powered processing and structured aggregation models also demonstrate how climate-smart agriculture can drive commercial growth while reducing rural conflict risks.
Nigeria Moves to Enforce Sachet Alcohol Ban as Lawmakers Review Tobacco Control Framework
Health ministry backs NAFDAC’s enforcement authority while House of Representatives considers reforms to regulate emerging nicotine products.

Nigeria’s Health Ministry has affirmed NAFDAC’s authority to enforce the sachet alcohol ban, while lawmakers move to review tobacco laws to regulate emerging vape products. The developments point to tighter enforcement and higher compliance standards across regulated consumer goods sectors.

Nigeria’s Federal Ministry of Health and Social Welfare has reaffirmed that the National Agency for Food and Drug Administration and Control (NAFDAC) has full legal authority to enforce the ban on alcoholic beverages packaged in sachets, PET bottles and glass containers below 200ml.

The clarification was made in a counter-affidavit filed before the Federal High Court in Lagos, as lawmakers simultaneously signalled plans to update tobacco control laws to address the rise of e-cigarettes and vape devices.

 

NAFDAC’s Enforcement Authority

According to the ministry:

  • NAFDAC derives its powers from Sections 5 and 30 of the NAFDAC Act

  • It is a statutory regulatory agency with defined enforcement responsibilities

  • The Ministry of Health does not interfere in enforcement decisions

  • No extension has been granted on the moratorium for enforcing the sachet alcohol prohibition

The suit (FHC/L/CS/2568/25) was filed by the Socio-Economic Rights and Accountability Project (SERAP) against the Minister of Health and the Attorney-General of the Federation. A hearing date has not yet been fixed.

 

Regulatory Scope: Alcohol Packaging Ban

The enforcement covers:

  • Alcoholic beverages in sachets

  • PET bottles below 200ml

  • Glass containers below 200ml

The policy aims to address public health concerns associated with cheap, high-volume access to alcohol.

 

House of Representatives to Review Tobacco Control Act

Separately, the House of Representatives has initiated plans to review the National Tobacco Control Act in response to:

  • Growth of electronic cigarettes and vape devices

  • Gaps in current regulatory frameworks

  • Concerns over illicit drug and nicotine product trafficking

During a visit to the Nigeria Customs Service (NCS), lawmakers emphasised the need to:

  • Strengthen border controls

  • Improve compliance monitoring in bonded warehouses

  • Enhance enforcement capacity

Customs confirmed:

  • Mandatory drug testing for personnel

  • Signed Memoranda of Understanding with NAFDAC and the NDLEA

  • Ongoing collaboration to curb illicit pharmaceutical and tobacco imports

 

Broader Regulatory Context

The combined actions reflect Nigeria’s tightening regulatory stance across:

  • Alcohol distribution

  • Tobacco and nicotine products

  • Illicit pharmaceutical trade

For manufacturers, importers and distributors, compliance oversight is expected to intensify.

Stronger enforcement and regulatory reviews signal increased compliance requirements for beverage, retail and import businesses. MSMEs operating in alcohol, tobacco or pharmaceutical supply chains must reassess packaging standards, licensing, import documentation and regulatory exposure to avoid disruptions.
International Monetary Fund Mission Begins Talks with Kenya on New Loan Programme
Government seeks successor deal to strengthen fiscal reforms and investor confidence after $3.6bn programme expiry.

An IMF mission is in Kenya to negotiate a new loan programme following the expiry of its $3.6 billion facility. The government hopes the successor deal will reinforce fiscal reforms, stabilise the economy and strengthen investor confidence amid high debt servicing pressures.

An International Monetary Fund (IMF) staff mission is in Kenya this week to negotiate a potential new lending programme following the expiration of the country’s previous $3.6 billion facility in April.

The discussions, running from February 24 to March 4, 2026, aim to align any new IMF-supported arrangement with Kenya’s fiscal consolidation plans, macroeconomic stability goals and investor confidence strategy.

 

Background: Previous Programme Expired

Kenya’s earlier $3.6 billion IMF programme ended in April 2025. Since then:

  • Multiple rounds of talks have taken place in Nairobi and Washington

  • The government has formally requested a successor programme

  • IMF funding has not yet been factored into the current or upcoming fiscal budgets

The Kenyan government confirmed the mission in a Eurobond prospectus linked to its recent $2.25 billion bond issuance.

 

Objectives of the New IMF Engagement

According to official statements, the new arrangement is expected to:

  • Support key policy reforms

  • Strengthen fiscal discipline

  • Reinforce macroeconomic stability

  • Bolster investor confidence

Kenya continues to grapple with elevated debt servicing costs and has explored securitisation of revenue streams to fund development projects, a strategy that initially complicated IMF negotiations.

 

Debt and Fiscal Context

Kenya faces:

  • High debt servicing obligations

  • Ongoing fiscal consolidation pressures

  • Continued need for external financing

Securing a new IMF programme could provide:

  • Policy credibility

  • External financing backstop

  • Improved access to international capital markets

An IMF-backed programme can stabilise currency markets, reduce fiscal uncertainty and restore investor confidence, factors that directly affect borrowing costs, inflation and access to credit. For Kenyan MSMEs, macroeconomic stability improves planning certainty, investment appetite and overall business confidence.
Uganda Plans Tanzania Rail Link to Open New Export Corridor
Proposed railway connection to Dar es Salaam could reduce transport costs and unlock mineral trade across East and Central Africa.

Uganda is exploring a railway link to Tanzania’s network to create a new export corridor to Dar es Salaam. The proposed project could cut logistics costs, strengthen mineral exports and deepen regional trade integration across East and Central Africa.

Uganda is seeking to connect its planned Standard Gauge Railway (SGR) to Tanzania’s rail network, creating a new export route to the port of Dar es Salaam. The proposal, outlined in a Ministry of Works and Transport document, could diversify Uganda’s logistics options and lower export costs for key commodities including gold, copper and iron ore.

The move signals a broader regional infrastructure push aimed at strengthening trade corridors across East and Central Africa.

 

Project Overview

According to the government document:

  • The railway would link Uganda’s SGR to Tanzania’s network

  • It would run from the Uganda–Tanzania border through southern and southwestern Uganda

  • The line would terminate at Mpondwe, near the border with the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC)

  • The corridor is designed to connect mineral-rich regions to the port of Dar es Salaam

Uganda currently exports most goods through Kenya’s port of Mombasa and is also progressing plans to connect its SGR to Kenya’s network.

 

Strategic Objectives

The proposed Tanzania link aims to:

  • Reduce transport time and logistics costs

  • Provide an alternative export route

  • Improve regional trade integration

  • Strengthen mineral export capacity

The document indicates that the African Development Bank (AfDB) may fund preparatory studies, with potential financing subject to project bankability.

 

Regional Impact Potential

The project could extend beyond Uganda and Tanzania:

  • The DRC may later seek to connect to the corridor

  • Mineral-rich inland regions could gain improved port access

  • Regional trade routes may become more diversified

If implemented, the link would create a multi-country logistics chain connecting Uganda, Tanzania and potentially the DRC.

 

Financing Considerations

The AfDB has confirmed it is reviewing a request to fund preparation activities. Full financing would depend on:

  • Feasibility studies

  • Commercial viability

  • Long-term economic sustainability

Improved rail infrastructure lowers transport costs, shortens delivery timelines and enhances cross-border trade reliability. For MSMEs in mining, agriculture, logistics and manufacturing, diversified export corridors can improve competitiveness and reduce dependency on single-port routes.
Uganda Raises 2026/27 Budget to $21.78bn, Prioritises Oil and Infrastructure Projects
Spending revision signals stronger focus on energy, minerals and transport infrastructure ahead of crude production.

Uganda has raised its 2026/27 spending projection by 12.7% to $21.78 billion, prioritising oil pipeline completion, mineral development and railway construction. The expansion signals increased infrastructure activity and potential business opportunities across energy and transport value chains.

Uganda has increased its projected public spending for the 2026/2027 financial year by 12.7% to 78.2 trillion shillings ($21.78 billion), up from an earlier estimate of 69.4 trillion shillings.

The revised figure, announced by the Ministry of Finance, reflects a significant expansion in fiscal planning as the country prepares for major oil, mineral and infrastructure milestones.

 

Revised Spending Projection

  • New budget projection: 78.2 trillion shillings ($21.78 billion)

  • Previous estimate: 69.4 trillion shillings

  • Increase: 12.7%

While the ministry did not specify detailed reasons for the upward revision, the government has outlined key strategic priorities for the upcoming fiscal year.

 

Top Spending Priorities

Uganda’s 2026/27 budget will focus on:

  • Completion of the East African Crude Oil Pipeline (EACOP)

  • Facilitating the start of commercial crude oil production

  • Mineral quantification for iron ore, gold and copper deposits

  • Development of a domestic refinery

  • Continued construction of the Standard Gauge Railway (SGR)

These projects are central to Uganda’s long-term industrialisation and export diversification strategy.

 

Strategic Context

The increased spending aligns with Uganda’s ambition to transition into an oil-producing economy while strengthening transport corridors and mineral value chains.

Infrastructure investments, particularly in pipelines and rail networks, are expected to enhance logistics efficiency and attract foreign direct investment.

Higher public spending on energy and transport infrastructure can stimulate supply chains, create subcontracting opportunities and improve market access for MSMEs. Oil, mining and railway projects often generate demand for local services, construction, logistics and manufacturing inputs.
Nigeria’s Shea Export Restriction Drives 5% Rise in Local Processing
Crude shea oil exports edge up as policy shift pushes value addition and domestic industrialisation.

Nigeria’s policy restricting raw shea nut exports has driven a 5% increase in crude shea oil exports, signalling early gains in domestic value addition. While still small in scale, the shift supports Nigeria’s broader industrialisation strategy and opens new opportunities across the shea value chain.

Nigeria’s restriction on raw shea nut exports is beginning to yield measurable results, with crude shea oil (karite oil) exports rising 5% year-on-year, according to a new industry report titled “Keeping Value at Home.”

Exports increased from ₦1.25 billion in Q3 2024 to ₦1.31 billion in Q3 2025, signalling early momentum in the country’s push to retain more value through domestic processing rather than exporting raw commodities.

 

Policy Objective: Move Up the Value Chain

The export restriction policy is designed to:

  • Limit raw shea nut exports

  • Encourage local processing into butter and oil

  • Retain higher-margin activities within Nigeria

  • Align with Nigeria’s National Industrial Policy

The strategy seeks to reduce dependence on exporting unprocessed agricultural commodities and instead capture greater value across the supply chain.

 

Profit Distribution Across the Shea Value Chain

The report highlights structural imbalances in the global shea market:

  • Rural collectors (mostly women): 19.45% of total profit

  • Primary processors: 13.55%

  • Foreign marketers and retailers: 67%

Exporting raw nuts transfers downstream, high-margin processing profits abroad. Analysts estimate that shifting from raw nut export to butter and oil processing can increase margins by 19%–21% compared to simple aggregation.

 

Industrialisation Beyond Primary Processing

While initial gains are visible, scaling further requires:

  • Capital-intensive refining plants

  • Technology transfer

  • Fractionation into shea stearin and shea olein

These higher-value derivatives are widely used in:

  • Confectionery manufacturing (as cocoa butter equivalents)

  • Premium cosmetics

Such segments command significantly stronger margins than crude butter production.

 

Policy Consistency Key to Investment

Industry experts stress that long-term investor confidence will depend on:

  • Consistent enforcement of export restrictions

  • Clear industrial policy signals

  • Infrastructure support for processing clusters

Without policy stability, large-scale refining investment may remain limited.

 

Strategic Significance

The shea sector is being positioned as a pilot case for Nigeria’s broader industrialisation agenda, mirroring strategies in oil refining, cocoa processing and timber manufacturing, where domestic value addition multiplies export revenue potential.

Although the 5% increase is modest relative to global shea trade volumes, it suggests a structural shift is beginning.

Value addition in shea processing creates opportunities for agro-processors, packaging firms, exporters and women-led cooperatives. As local refining expands, MSMEs can capture higher margins, build brands and integrate into global cosmetic and food supply chains rather than remaining raw commodity suppliers.
Lithium Prices Surge in China After Zimbabwe Halts Raw Exports
Africa’s largest lithium producer suspends exports, raising supply concerns amid global energy storage demand.

China’s lithium prices surged after Zimbabwe suspended exports of raw lithium minerals and concentrates. As Africa’s largest producer tightens supply, global battery markets face potential disruptions, while local value addition strategies could reshape opportunities across the continent’s mineral value chains.

Lithium prices in China jumped sharply after Zimbabwe announced an immediate suspension of raw mineral and lithium concentrate exports. The move has triggered supply concerns in a market already buoyed by rising global demand for electric vehicles (EVs) and energy storage systems.

Zimbabwe, Africa’s largest lithium producer, has become a critical supplier to China, making the export halt a significant development for global battery supply chains.

 

Market Reaction in China

Following the announcement:

  • The most traded lithium carbonate contract on the Guangzhou Futures Exchange rose 6.07% to 178,020 yuan ($26,043) per metric ton

  • Prices briefly spiked over 9% to 187,700 yuan during the session

The surge reflects fears of tighter supply, particularly as demand for battery-grade lithium continues to grow.

 

Zimbabwe’s Strategic Move

Zimbabwe suspended exports of:

  • Raw lithium minerals

  • Lithium concentrates, including spodumene

In 2025, Zimbabwe exported 1.128 million tons of spodumene concentrate, up 11% year-on-year, with the majority shipped to China.

The country has attracted significant investment from Chinese mining firms, including Zhejiang Huayou Cobalt and Sinomine, positioning it as a key node in the global battery value chain.

 

Supply Chain Implications

The export ban comes amid:

  • Expanding global EV production

  • Accelerating deployment of grid-scale energy storage systems

  • Strong lithium price recovery since the second half of 2025

By restricting raw exports, Zimbabwe may be seeking to encourage domestic value addition and local processing, mirroring similar industrial policies seen in other resource-rich nations.

 

Global Battery Market Context

Lithium is a core component in:

  • Electric vehicle batteries

  • Renewable energy storage systems

  • Consumer electronics

Any disruption in upstream supply can have ripple effects across battery manufacturers, automakers and clean energy projects.

Rising lithium prices and supply chain shifts create opportunities for African processors, logistics providers and battery-related manufacturers. If resource-rich countries prioritise local processing, MSMEs can integrate into higher-value segments of the energy transition supply chain rather than remaining raw mineral exporters.
Nigeria Advances $2bn China-Backed Plan to Revive Ajaokuta Steel Company Limited
Production-sharing model aims to restart 1.3m-tonne capacity plant and scale output to 10m tonnes annually.

Nigeria is advancing a $2 billion Chinese-backed plan to revive Ajaokuta Steel under a production-sharing model that preserves national ownership. If implemented, the project could restore domestic steel capacity, reduce imports and strengthen Nigeria’s industrial base while creating new opportunities across the manufacturing value chain.

Nigeria’s federal government has progressed negotiations with Chinese investors to revive the long-dormant Ajaokuta Steel Company under a proposed $2 billion investment framework. The plan adopts a production-sharing model designed to restore large-scale steel manufacturing without direct federal funding.

The initiative is positioned as a strategic pillar in Nigeria’s industrialisation agenda, targeting reduced steel imports, stronger value addition and improved trade balance with China.

 

Background: A 42-Year-Old Industrial Asset

Built in 1984 with an installed capacity of 1.3 million metric tonnes per annum, Ajaokuta has remained largely non-operational due to legal disputes, policy inconsistencies and concession challenges.

Nigeria currently consumes about 10 million metric tonnes of steel annually, but produces only 1.2 million metric tonnes, mostly from scrap-based recycling.

 

Proposed Investment Model

Under the proposed framework:

  • Chinese partners will inject $2 billion to rehabilitate and expand the complex

  • Financing will be privately raised, subject to approval by China’s National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC)

  • Nigeria retains ownership of the plant

  • Investors recover capital through a sliding-scale production-sharing arrangement

The investor may initially receive 60–70% of output, declining over 5–10 years until Nigeria assumes full production control.

 

Operational Roadmap

  • Rolling mill operations could resume within six months of commencement

  • Immediate restart of existing 1.3m-tonne rolling mill

  • Planned expansion toward 10 million tonnes annual capacity

A technical team of 20 engineers has already assessed the plant, concluding that core infrastructure remains viable despite outdated equipment.

 

Supply Chain and Infrastructure Integration

The government plans to:

  • Integrate iron ore supply from Itakpe and other deposits

  • Bring steel and iron ore assets under a coordinated holding structure

  • Upgrade logistics corridors including Warri Port, rail links and inland waterways

Officials argue Nigeria’s iron ore (34–44% purity) remains commercially viable with beneficiation.

 

Technology Transfer and Skills Development

The framework includes:

  • Training of Nigerian engineers in China

  • Structured capacity-building programmes

  • Emphasis on sustainable knowledge transfer

The aim is to avoid past scenarios where industrial assets lacked local technical expertise.

 

Broader Trade and Industrial Strategy

The Ajaokuta revival forms part of Nigeria’s wider industrial cooperation with China. Bilateral trade currently stands at $23 billion, with Nigerian exports accounting for about $2 billion and imports exceeding $20 billion.

Government targets:

  • Expand trade to $40 billion within five years

  • Increase Nigerian exports to at least $15 billion

  • Shift from raw material exports to value-added production

Reviving large-scale steel production can lower input costs for construction, manufacturing and fabrication businesses. MSMEs in engineering, logistics, mining services and industrial supply chains stand to benefit from increased local sourcing, job creation and reduced import dependence
Nigeria Accelerates Cassava Bioethanol Strategy to Drive Industrial Growth
Federal Government targets fuel blending, value addition and smallholder integration under bio-economy agenda.

Nigeria is advancing its cassava bioethanol programme to support industrial growth, reduce fuel imports and build a circular bio-based economy. By moving beyond food production into energy and industrial applications, the initiative aims to unlock new value chains and job opportunities across agriculture and manufacturing.

The Federal Government has intensified efforts to develop Nigeria’s cassava bioethanol value chain as part of a broader strategy to stimulate industrial expansion, reduce fuel imports and strengthen economic stability.

The renewed push was highlighted at a stakeholder capacity-building workshop in Abeokuta focused on the Cassava Bioethanol Value Chain Development Project for the South-West zone, aligning with the administration’s economic transformation agenda.

 

From Food Crop to Industrial Resource

Although Nigeria is the world’s largest producer of cassava, policymakers say the crop’s industrial potential remains underutilised.

The new strategy seeks to:

  • Expand cassava use beyond food consumption

  • Promote large-scale bioethanol production

  • Integrate cassava into industrial and energy value chains

  • Support Nigeria’s National Bio-Economy Policy

Bioethanol blending with Premium Motor Spirit (PMS) is expected to reduce fuel import dependence and conserve foreign exchange.

 

Economic and Energy Impact

Government projections suggest that scaling bioethanol production could:

  • Save billions of naira in fuel import costs

  • Support naira stability through reduced FX demand

  • Strengthen energy security

  • Create employment opportunities across agriculture and processing

The initiative also aims to promote a circular bio-based economy by unlocking value from:

  • High-quality starch

  • Carbon dioxide captured during fermentation

  • Animal feed derived from distillery grains

 

Value Chain Development and Skills Transfer

Stakeholders were encouraged to build capacity in:

  • High-yield cassava cultivation

  • Efficient bioethanol processing

  • Sustainable resource management

Experts described the bio-economy as a model that leverages renewable biological resources, scientific innovation and industrial processing to drive inclusive growth.

 

Strategic Context

The cassava bioethanol drive aligns with Nigeria’s broader industrialisation goals, which prioritise domestic value addition, rural income growth and energy transition.

By linking agriculture with energy production and industrial processing, the government aims to reposition cassava as a cornerstone of Nigeria’s bio-resource economy.

Expanding cassava-based bioethanol production creates new opportunities for agro-processors, equipment suppliers, logistics providers and smallholder cooperatives. MSMEs can benefit from higher-value processing, stronger market linkages and diversified income streams within a growing bio-economy.
Weak Gender Law Enforcement Constrains Nigeria’s Growth — World Bank
Low implementation scores and absence of parenthood protections limit women’s workforce participation.

The World Bank says weak enforcement of gender equality laws and the absence of parenthood policies are limiting Nigeria’s workforce participation and economic growth. Closing the gap between legislation and implementation could unlock significant productivity gains and strengthen MSME performance.

Nigeria’s economic growth potential is being undermined by weak enforcement of gender equality laws and the absence of structured parenthood support policies, according to the World Bank’s Women, Business and the Law 2026 report.

While Nigeria scores moderately on gender equality legislation, the report highlights a significant gap between laws on paper and their practical implementation — a disconnect that is limiting women’s participation in the workforce and weakening overall productivity.

 

Nigeria’s Gender Equality Scores

The report reveals a sharp contrast between legal provisions and enforcement systems:

  • 50/100 – Gender equality laws on paper

  • 21.7/100 – Systems required to implement those laws (funding, services, institutional support)

  • 0/100 – Parenthood policies

The zero score reflects the absence of:

  • Federally mandated at least 14 weeks of paid maternity leave

  • Paid paternity leave

  • Explicit protections against dismissal of pregnant workers

  • National policies guaranteeing access to affordable, quality childcare

 

The Implementation Gap

Globally, the World Bank notes:

  • Average score of 67/100 for gender equality laws

  • Enforcement score drops to 53/100

  • Implementation systems score falls further to 47/100

  • Only 4% of women globally live in economies with near-full legal equality

The Bank warns that gaps between legislation and enforcement create “huge opportunity gaps” that directly reduce economic output in developing economies.

 

Economic Impact on Nigeria

The absence of structured childcare, parental leave and enforceable equal pay protections has several economic consequences:

  • Lower female labour force retention

  • Reduced productivity

  • Constrained household income growth

  • Underutilisation of Nigeria’s demographic dividend

Nigeria also lacks tax incentives or government-administered financial support systems to help families balance work and caregiving responsibilities.

Women make up a significant share of Nigeria’s entrepreneurial and SME workforce. Weak parenthood protections and limited support systems reduce talent retention, productivity and long-term business stability. For MSMEs, stronger gender-inclusive policies could expand the labour pool, boost incomes and unlock broader consumer spending power.
CAC Digital Reforms Accelerate Business Registration as Ambassador Urges Stronger Crypto Oversight
Instant AI-powered registration boosts entrepreneurship, but calls grow for tighter regulation of digital asset platforms.

Nigeria’s AI-powered business registration system is driving faster company formation and boosting revenue generation. At the same time, stakeholders are calling for stronger crypto regulation to protect investors and sustain confidence in the digital economy.

Nigeria’s business registration landscape has entered a new phase of digital acceleration, with the Corporate Affairs Commission (CAC) deploying an AI-powered portal that enables entrepreneurs to register companies in as little as 10 minutes.

However, stakeholders are simultaneously calling for stronger regulatory oversight in the cryptocurrency sector to protect investors and preserve confidence in Nigeria’s digital economy.

 

Instant Registration Drives Entrepreneurial Growth

At a media briefing in Lagos, CAC Ambassador Michael Nwabufo described the commission’s digital transformation as a milestone in Nigeria’s economic development.

Key highlights of the reform include:

  • AI-powered online registration platform

  • Business certificates issued in record time (subject to NIN verification)

  • Significant surge in business registrations

  • Billions of naira in increased revenue inflows to government

According to Nwabufo, Nigeria has moved beyond merely aligning with global standards and is now competing in instant, technology-driven business registration.

 

Call for Stronger Crypto Regulation

While commending CAC reforms, Nwabufo raised concerns over the rise of unregulated cryptocurrency and digital asset platforms operating without adequate oversight.

He warned that:

  • Many Nigerians have suffered financial losses from non-compliant crypto entities

  • Weak oversight damages trust in Nigeria’s digital financial ecosystem

  • Regulatory discipline is critical to sustaining innovation

He urged digital asset vendors to comply strictly with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) regulatory framework to enhance accountability and investor protection.

 

Balancing Innovation and Regulation

The developments reflect a broader economic shift:

  • Government institutions are accelerating digital reforms

  • Business formalisation is becoming faster and more accessible

  • Emerging sectors like cryptocurrency require stronger compliance systems

As digital entrepreneurship expands, regulatory clarity is becoming increasingly important to safeguard market stability.

Faster business registration lowers entry barriers for startups and SMEs, improving formalisation and access to finance. However, MSMEs operating in fintech or digital assets must prioritise regulatory compliance to avoid legal risks and protect customer trust in a tightening oversight environment.
UBA Moves Diaspora Banking Beyond Remittances to Structured Investment
New integrated platform targets Africa’s $100bn annual remittance flows, converting transfers into long-term capital formation.

UBA has launched an integrated diaspora banking and investment ecosystem aimed at converting Africa’s $100bn annual remittances into structured, long-term capital. By prioritising trust, regulation and multi-sector integration, the bank is seeking to move diaspora engagement from transfers to wealth creation and institutional growth.

African diaspora remittances now exceed $100 billion annually, accounting for roughly 6% of the continent’s GDP and surpassing foreign direct investment in several countries. Yet most of these inflows remain consumption-driven rather than investment-led.

United Bank for Africa (UBA) is seeking to change that dynamic. The bank has launched a comprehensive diaspora banking and investment ecosystem designed to transform remittances from short-term transfers into structured, professionally managed wealth and capital market participation.

 

Africa’s Diaspora Capital: Scale and Opportunity

Key figures underscore the magnitude of the opportunity:

  • $100bn+ in annual diaspora remittances across Africa

  • Remittances exceed FDI in multiple countries

  • Nigeria received $21bn in 2024, projected to rise to $23bn

  • Egypt, Kenya, Ghana, Tunisia and Algeria also rely heavily on diaspora inflows

While remittances support household consumption and foreign exchange stability, only a small fraction is channelled into formal investment vehicles.

 

UBA’s Integrated Diaspora Ecosystem

Rather than launch a standalone product, UBA introduced a multi-institution financial architecture under the theme “Beyond Banking: Powering the Global African Lifestyle.”

The platform integrates:

  • Banking and cross-border payments

  • Asset management and securities services

  • Digital investment management

  • Pension solutions

  • Insurance protection

  • Structured property investment

  • Cross-border healthcare access

The goal is to centralise diaspora financial needs into a unified, regulated framework, reducing fragmentation across providers and jurisdictions.

 

Closing the Trust Gap

A key barrier to diaspora investment has been institutional trust. Many investors lost track of equity holdings due to weak record-keeping or defunct intermediaries.

UBA’s model emphasises:

  • Regulated stockbroking and custodial services

  • SEC-aligned compliance frameworks

  • Portfolio reconciliation and recovery of dormant assets

  • Improved transparency and reporting

Industry estimates suggest tens of billions of naira remain in unclaimed dividends due to inactive accounts.

 

From Consumption to Capital Formation

Executives argue that diaspora flows must shift from consumption support to productive capital deployment.

Potential impact of structured allocation:

  • Deepening local capital markets

  • Strengthening liquidity and FX stability

  • Expanding pension and insurance penetration

  • Financing infrastructure-linked instruments

  • Reducing reliance on volatile foreign portfolio flows

Even a modest reallocation of remittance flows into managed investment vehicles could materially alter Africa’s funding structure.

 

Distribution Strategy: Digital + Advisory

UBA is combining:

  • Digital onboarding and identity verification

  • Cross-border account integration

  • Certified physical advisory support

With operations in 20 African countries and relationships in the US and UK, the bank is positioning trust, regulatory compliance and presence as core pillars of diaspora engagement.

 

Strategic Context: Africapitalism in Action

The initiative aligns with the broader philosophy of Africapitalism, the idea that African private capital should drive long-term development while generating returns.

The strategy reframes diaspora capital as:

  • Growth equity, not charity

  • Structured investment, not informal transfers

  • Institutional financing, not episodic remittance inflow

Structured diaspora investment can unlock deeper liquidity for SMEs through capital markets, pension funds and managed investment vehicles. If even a fraction of annual remittances shifts into formal investment channels, MSMEs could gain improved access to financing, stronger currency stability and expanded domestic capital pools.
Nigerian Manufacturers Lose Up to 15% Energy to Weak Maintenance – MAN
Assessment reveals major efficiency gaps, with compressed air, steam systems and poor monitoring driving industrial energy waste.

A nationwide assessment shows Nigerian manufacturers are losing up to 15% of energy due to weak maintenance and poor monitoring systems. By adopting structured energy management standards, industries could cut costs by up to 25%, boosting competitiveness and sustainability.

Nigeria’s industrial sector is losing between 10% and 15% of energy to weak maintenance practices, while most factories lack sub-metering systems to properly track consumption, according to findings by the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN).

The findings emerged from a Cleaner Production Assessment (CPA) conducted across 42 industries nationwide under the GEF-UNIDO Industrial Energy Efficiency and Resource Efficiency Cleaner Production Project.


Key Findings from the Assessment

The study covered sectors including food and beverages, metals, textiles, wood processing and petrochemicals. It revealed significant energy efficiency gaps across facilities.

Major sources of energy loss include:

  • Compressed air systems – 25% of electricity losses (leaks and misuse)

  • Steam systems – 30% of losses

  • Lighting inefficiencies – 18%

  • Poor insulation and flue gas losses in boilers and furnaces

  • Motors running inefficiently at partial loads

  • Absence of Variable Speed Drives (VSDs)

  • Idle equipment in textile and leather plants

In some facilities, optimising compressed air systems allowed operators to shut down an entire compressor.

 

Data and Monitoring Gaps

A major structural issue identified was the absence of sub-metering systems.

  • Most facilities cannot track departmental energy consumption

  • Water usage is rarely measured, despite heavy borehole reliance

  • Lack of data makes waste management and optimisation difficult

Experts emphasised that “what is not measured cannot be managed.”

 

Potential Savings and Impact

The CPA estimates that manufacturers could achieve:

  • 20–25% reduction in energy use

  • Approximately 500 megawatt-hours of savings per plant annually

These savings translate directly into lower production costs, improved margins and reduced carbon emissions.

A Lagos-based food and beverage plant successfully reduced compressed air leaks by 20% following optimisation efforts.

 

ISO Standards as a Solution

MAN urged manufacturers to adopt:

  • ISO 50001 – Energy Management Systems

  • ISO 14001 – Resource Efficiency and Cleaner Production

These frameworks institutionalise structured energy monitoring, process optimisation and sustainability practices.

Industry leaders stressed that top management commitment is critical to driving adoption and closing the efficiency gap.

 

Hidden Financial Leakages

Industrial leaders also highlighted:

  • Waste heat recovery opportunities from generators

  • Commercialisation of industrial waste streams

  • Recycling water to reduce operational costs

Energy inefficiency not only increases costs but also raises emissions and reduces competitiveness in export markets.

Energy costs are one of the largest operational burdens for manufacturers. Closing a 15–25% efficiency gap can significantly improve profit margins, pricing competitiveness and resilience amid grid instability. MSMEs that institutionalise energy management early will gain a structural cost advantage in increasingly competitive markets.
EU Commits €63 Million in New Humanitarian Aid for Somalia
Funding targets urgent health and nutrition needs amid conflict and drought pressures.

The European Union has pledged €63 million in humanitarian funding to Somalia, focused on urgent health and nutrition services. The support adds to more than €750 million in EU aid since 2017 and comes amid conflict, drought and growing food insecurity concerns.

The European Union has announced a fresh €63 million ($74.39 million) humanitarian assistance package for Somalia, aimed at addressing worsening health and nutrition challenges driven by ongoing conflict and climate shocks.

The funding comes as Somalia continues to grapple with fighting between government forces and al-Shabaab militants, alongside prolonged drought conditions that have strained food systems and displaced vulnerable communities.

 

What the Funding Covers

According to the EU, the new allocation will prioritise:

  • Life-saving health services

  • Emergency nutrition support

  • Assistance for vulnerable and displaced populations

The EU noted that this latest commitment brings its total humanitarian support to Somalia to over €750 million since 2017.

 

Humanitarian Context

Somalia faces compounded crises:

  • Persistent armed conflict disrupting livelihoods

  • Recurrent droughts impacting agriculture and pastoral systems

  • Food insecurity affecting millions

Earlier in February, the United Nations World Food Programme warned that its life-saving food and nutrition programmes in Somalia could be suspended by April without additional funding.

The EU did not specify a timeline for disbursement of the new funds.

 

Broader Economic Implications

While humanitarian in nature, the funding plays a stabilising role by:

  • Supporting public health systems

  • Reducing immediate food insecurity risks

  • Helping maintain basic social and economic functionality

Sustained humanitarian financing is often critical to preventing deeper economic collapse in fragile markets.

Stability in fragile economies like Somalia directly affects regional trade, supply chains and cross-border commerce in East Africa. Humanitarian support helps sustain consumer purchasing power, workforce health and basic economic activity, key foundations for MSME resilience and recovery.
Enugu Targets Digital Economy Leap with 2026 Tech Festival
State positions itself as West Africa’s emerging innovation hub as 50,000+ participants expected for four-day global tech gathering.

Enugu will host the 2026 Tech Festival, expecting nearly 50,000 participants as part of a broader strategy to reposition the state as a digital innovation hub. With large-scale skills training, startup investment forums and ecosystem expansion, the initiative signals a long-term commitment to technology-led economic growth.

Enugu State is set to host the second edition of the Enugu Tech Festival (ETF) from 24–27 February 2026, with nearly 50,000 participants expected at the Enugu International Conference Centre. The event signals a broader strategy to reposition the state from its historic “Coal City” identity into a competitive digital innovation hub.

The festival builds on ongoing infrastructure upgrades, digitisation of public services and technology ecosystem development under Governor Peter Mbah’s administration.

 

From Coal to Code: A Strategic Rebrand

The 2026 theme, “Coal to Code: Energy in New Form,” reflects Enugu’s shift toward a knowledge-driven economy.

Key reforms supporting this transition include:

  • Expansion of broadband infrastructure

  • Digitisation of government services

  • Development of Smart Green Schools

  • Upgrading of healthcare and transport systems

  • Innovation-friendly policy frameworks

The government’s focus extends beyond hosting events to embedding long-term digital capacity.

 

Scale and Scope of the Festival

The four-day festival will convene:

  • Startup founders and developers

  • Venture capitalists and corporate tech leaders

  • Policymakers and regulators

  • Students and academics

  • Delegations from across Africa and international innovation ecosystems

Programming spans high-growth sectors such as:

  • Artificial Intelligence

  • Blockchain

  • Cybersecurity

  • Robotics

  • Fintech

  • Data Science

  • Cloud Computing

  • Internet of Things (IoT)

 

Large-Scale Digital Skills Training

A standout feature of ETF 2026 is a three-day mass training initiative targeting over 67,000 participants.

Participants will receive free instruction in:

  • Data analytics

  • Software development

  • Artificial intelligence

  • Digital project management

Trainers from the United Kingdom, including partners from 1Goal Analytics, will issue internationally recognised certifications.

This initiative aims to address youth unemployment and align local talent with global standards.

 

Ecosystem Growth Since ETF 2025

Following the inaugural edition in 2025, Enugu has recorded:

  • Launch of 10 new tech hubs, including a Blockchain Hub

  • Increased startup relocation into the state

  • Stronger collaboration between investors and founders

  • Expanded incubation and mentorship platforms

The 2026 edition will feature a Startup Showcase and Investment Forum, connecting entrepreneurs directly with investors and strategic partners.

Selected startups will receive post-event incubation and advisory support.

 

High-Level Participation

The festival will include participation from:

  • Federal and state government officials

  • Communications and digital economy policymakers

  • Youth development stakeholders

  • International diplomatic representatives

  • Leading private-sector executives

Discussions will focus on enabling policies, innovation financing and ease-of-doing-business reforms.

 

Strategic Significance

Enugu’s strategy extends beyond event hosting. The goal is to institutionalise innovation by ensuring:

  • Skills training translates into employment

  • Tech hubs mature into sustainable enterprises

  • Public-private collaboration drives long-term growth

The festival represents an economic positioning move, not merely a branding exercise.

Technology-driven ecosystems create new markets for SMEs in fintech, agritech, healthtech, logistics and digital services. Access to investors, certification programmes and innovation hubs can accelerate startup formation and improve funding opportunities for African MSMEs.
AEO-Certified Firms Cut Cargo Clearance Time to 41 Hours — Nigeria Customs
Authorised Economic Operator programme delivers 77% efficiency gains, lower demurrage costs and higher compliance revenue.

Nigeria Customs reports that AEO-certified firms now clear cargo in 41 hours, down from seven days, cutting costs and boosting compliance. The programme has improved trade efficiency by over 77% and strengthened revenue performance, positioning compliant traders for faster, lower-cost operations.

Companies certified under the Nigeria Customs Service (NCS) Authorised Economic Operator (AEO) Programme are now clearing cargo in an average of 41 hours, down from 168 hours (seven days) prior to certification.

According to the NCS Monitoring and Evaluation Report, the improved turnaround time reflects the programme’s push for digitalisation, simplified procedures and enhanced compliance standards.

 

What the AEO Programme Delivers

Launched from February 2025 to January 2026 to replace the defunct Fast-Track Scheme, the AEO Programme rewards highly compliant traders with trade facilitation benefits.

Certified operators receive:

  • Faster cargo clearance

  • Reduced physical inspections

  • Pre-arrival processing

  • Targeted risk-based screening

  • Simplified documentation procedures

The initiative is designed to ease cross-border trade while strengthening compliance and transparency.

 

Efficiency and Cost Impact

Among the 51 fully certified firms:

  • Trade efficiency improved by 77.11%

  • Operating costs declined by 57.2%

  • Demurrage payments dropped by 90%

Reduced demurrage significantly limits capital outflows to foreign-owned port service providers and improves liquidity for importers.

 

Revenue and Compliance Gains

The programme also boosted Customs revenue performance:

  • N1.585 trillion generated by AEO firms (up from N1.222 trillion pre-certification)

  • Represented 21.77% of N7.28 trillion total Customs revenue in 2025

  • Customs duties paid by AEO firms rose 85.66%

The increase is attributed to stronger compliance and expanded legitimate trade volumes.

Additionally, several companies voluntarily remitted over N1 billion into the Federation Account following internal compliance reviews, reflecting stronger post-clearance audit mechanisms.

 

Certification Status and Enforcement

Of 584 applications received before the January 31, 2026 deadline:

  • 51 firms achieved full certification

  • 46 firms remain on probation

  • 154 applications are pending validation

One fully certified operator was suspended over false declaration following a compliance breach, reinforcing the programme’s strict oversight framework.

Certified firms must renew status every three to five years, depending on their category. Companies that lose certification revert to standard Customs processing and forfeit expedited clearance benefits.

 

Strategic Implications

The AEO Programme reflects a broader trade modernisation strategy aimed at:

  • Reducing port congestion

  • Lowering transaction costs

  • Strengthening risk-based customs controls

  • Encouraging voluntary compliance

The model aligns Nigeria more closely with global trade facilitation standards under the World Customs Organization framework.

Faster clearance and reduced port charges directly improve cash flow, inventory turnover and cost predictability for import-dependent MSMEs. However, the benefits depend on strict compliance. SMEs that invest in transparent documentation, digital systems and regulatory discipline may gain significant competitive advantage in cross-border trade.
Nigeria’s Economy Rebounds to Three-Year High on Reform Gains

Nigeria’s economy grew 3.87 percent in 2025, its strongest performance in three years, driven by agriculture, industry, and services. Fourth quarter growth reached 4.07 percent year on year, while oil’s share of output remained modest, reflecting gradual diversification.

Nigeria’s economy expanded at its strongest pace in three years in 2025, supported by improved output in agriculture and industry, as policymakers work to consolidate reforms in Africa’s largest economy.

Gross domestic product grew 4.07 percent year on year in the fourth quarter of 2025, higher than the 3.76 percent recorded in the same period of 2024, according to data released by the National Bureau of Statistics. For the full year, growth stood at 3.87 percent, up from 3.38 percent in 2024.

The latest figures mark Nigeria’s strongest annual performance since 2022 and extend the recovery from the pandemic induced contraction of 6.96 percent in 2020. Growth rebounded to 0.95 percent in 2021, accelerated to 4.32 percent in 2022, slowed to 3.04 percent in 2023, and has now strengthened for two consecutive years.

Broad Based Expansion

The fourth quarter expansion was driven by gains across key sectors. Agriculture grew 4 percent, up from 2.54 percent a year earlier, while industry expanded 3.88 percent compared with 2.49 percent in the fourth quarter of 2024.

Services, which account for more than half of total output, rose 4.15 percent. Although slightly lower than the 4.75 percent recorded a year earlier, the sector still contributed 55.92 percent of total GDP, underscoring its dominant role in the economy.

In nominal terms, GDP at basic prices increased 17.55 percent to 122.8 trillion naira in the fourth quarter, compared with 104.5 trillion naira in the same period of 2024, reflecting both real expansion and elevated price levels.

The structure of growth continues to reflect gradual diversification. Crop production, trade, real estate, telecommunications, and construction were among the leading contributors. Crude petroleum and natural gas accounted for just 2.87 percent of real GDP in the quarter, highlighting a reduced dependence on oil relative to previous years.

Reform Momentum and Outlook

The stronger performance comes as the World Bank and the International Monetary Fund project further acceleration. Both institutions forecast growth of about 4.4 percent in 2026 and 2027, which would represent the fastest expansion in roughly a decade if sustained.

That outlook depends on continued reform momentum, improved oil production, exchange rate stability, and easing inflation. Nigeria has faced persistent price pressures and currency volatility in recent years, limiting household purchasing power even as headline growth improved.

Recent macroeconomic adjustments, including the removal of fuel subsidies and the move toward a more market driven exchange rate regime, have begun to stabilise conditions. Inflation has eased to around 15 percent, and the naira recorded gains last year after a prolonged period of weakness.

Growth Versus Living Standards

Despite the improved macro indicators, living conditions remain challenging. More than 60 percent of Nigeria’s estimated 230 million people live in extreme poverty, and the cost of living remains elevated.

Adetilewa Adebajo, Chief Executive Officer of CFG Advisory, said stronger and more inclusive growth is required for reforms to translate into tangible improvements in living standards.

“To see the impact on the lives of Nigerians, we need to translate the gains of these reforms into sustainable, solid growth and productivity. The economy has to grow sustainably by 8 to 10 percent,” he said.

Nigeria’s latest growth figures signal a strengthening recovery and improved macroeconomic stability. The challenge ahead lies in sustaining reform momentum and lifting annual expansion to levels that meaningfully reduce poverty and deliver broad based prosperity.

Stronger GDP growth can improve consumer demand, support business expansion, and enhance access to finance for micro, small, and medium enterprises. However, sustained high growth is essential for translating macroeconomic gains into broader employment opportunities and improved purchasing power.
Gas Shortage Plunges Nigeria Into Prolonged Blackouts

Nigeria’s ongoing nationwide blackouts are driven by severe gas shortages affecting thermal power plants. With less than 43 percent of required gas supply available, electricity generation has fallen to about 4,300 megawatts, prompting nationwide load shedding to stabilise the grid.

Nigeria’s lingering nationwide power outages are primarily the result of inadequate gas supply to thermal power plants, the Nigerian Independent System Operator said on Friday, attributing the sustained decline in electricity generation to severe fuel constraints affecting the national grid.

In a statement titled Declining Power Output Attributable to Generation Shortfalls and Gas Supply Limitations, released on its official X handle, the operator disclosed that average available generation currently stands at about 4,300 megawatts, far below installed capacity and significantly below national demand.

Gas Disruptions Trigger Supply Shock

The outages began in early February following scheduled maintenance on key gas supply infrastructure by Nigerian National Petroleum Company Limited and Seplat Energy. The maintenance temporarily disrupted gas deliveries to several thermal plants, leading to an immediate drop in power generation.

However, the situation has persisted due to ongoing gas supply constraints, deepening electricity shortages across the country.

According to NISO, thermal plants require an estimated 1,629.75 million standard cubic feet of gas per day to operate at optimal capacity. As of February 23, 2026, actual supply stood at approximately 692.00 million standard cubic feet per day.

This means less than 43 percent of required gas volumes are currently available, creating a substantial supply deficit.

“Given that thermal plants account for the dominant share of Nigeria’s generation mix, any disruption or limitation in gas supply directly affects available generation capacity and overall grid output,” the operator stated.

Load Shedding to Stabilise the Grid

With generation levels significantly below demand, NISO confirmed it has implemented load shedding nationwide to prevent grid instability.

“When total system generation drops significantly, the Independent System Operator must implement load shedding across the system, while dispatching available energy in line with the NERC MYTO allocation percentages across all distribution networks to maintain grid stability and prevent system disturbances,” the statement said.

Nigeria’s electricity system remains heavily dependent on gas fired thermal plants, which account for more than 70 percent of grid power, with hydropower providing the balance. This structural reliance means any disruption in gas production, pipeline infrastructure, pricing arrangements, or payment flows within the electricity value chain can quickly trigger widespread blackouts.

Gas supply shortages in the power sector have historically stemmed from upstream production challenges, pipeline vandalism, legacy debts owed by generating companies to gas suppliers, foreign exchange constraints, and infrastructure bottlenecks.

Liquidity challenges also persist. Generating companies have repeatedly warned that inadequate remittances from Distribution Companies weaken their ability to meet financial obligations to gas suppliers, creating a cycle of underperformance.

Although recent reforms separated the system operator from the Transmission Company of Nigeria to enhance transparency and grid management, generation capacity remains tightly linked to fuel availability.

With national peak demand estimated at well above 20,000 megawatts, the current 4,300 megawatt average highlights the scale of Nigeria’s electricity deficit.

Nigeria’s power crisis underscores the structural vulnerability of a generation system heavily dependent on gas supply. Sustainable improvement in electricity delivery will require stable gas supply arrangements, improved liquidity across the value chain, and continued reforms to strengthen operational efficiency. Until fuel flows normalise, businesses and households are likely to face continued power rationing.

Reliable electricity is critical for micro, small, and medium enterprises across Nigeria. Prolonged outages increase operating costs as businesses rely on diesel generators, erode profit margins, disrupt production schedules, and reduce competitiveness. Persistent power instability also discourages investment and weakens overall economic productivity.
MTN Nigeria Records N5.2tn Service Revenue

MTN Nigeria recorded 5.2 trillion naira in service revenue in 2025 and invested 1 trillion naira in capital expenditure to expand network infrastructure. The company returned to profitability and reinforced its role as a major contributor to tax revenue, digital connectivity, and investor wealth creation.

MTN Nigeria has reaffirmed its position as a major driver of Nigeria’s non oil economy after reporting service revenue of 5.2 trillion naira in its audited 2025 financial results.

The strong performance reflects not only operational recovery but also the expanding role of telecommunications in national output. As one of Nigeria’s largest corporate taxpayers, the company said its profitability directly supports public revenues used to fund infrastructure, social services, and broader economic development.

The 2025 financial year was described as a period of resilience and strategic turnaround, following macroeconomic pressures including currency volatility, elevated inflation, and higher operating costs that affected earnings in previous periods.

Chief Executive Officer, Dr Karl Toriola, described 2025 as a turning point marked by a return to profitability and a stronger balance sheet. According to him, improved cost discipline, pricing adjustments, and operational efficiency contributed to stabilising margins and restoring investor confidence.

The recovery also reflects the growing demand for data services, digital payments infrastructure, enterprise connectivity, and broadband expansion across Nigeria’s rapidly evolving digital landscape.

A stronger balance sheet enabled the company to accelerate capital deployment without significantly increasing leverage, positioning it to fund expansion sustainably while maintaining financial flexibility.

N1tn Capital Investment and Digital Infrastructure Expansion

MTN disclosed that it invested 1 trillion naira in capital expenditure during the year, one of the largest single year investments in the country’s telecom sector.

The investment covered expansion of 4G and 5G coverage, additional base station rollout, fibre backbone extension, network modernisation, and improved capacity to handle rising data traffic. With internet penetration and smartphone adoption increasing, demand for reliable connectivity continues to surge.

By reinvesting earnings locally, MTN is strengthening the backbone of Nigeria’s digital economy. Telecommunications infrastructure plays a foundational role in enabling fintech growth, e commerce platforms, digital education, remote work, health technology services, and enterprise productivity.

MTN acknowledged recent economic reforms, noting that greater macroeconomic clarity and improved policy direction supported its return to stability. Exchange rate reforms and broader fiscal adjustments have helped restore a degree of predictability for capital intensive sectors such as telecommunications.

As one of the most capitalised firms on the Nigerian Exchange Limited, the company emphasised its role in deepening local capital markets. With millions of direct and indirect Nigerian shareholders, MTN positions itself as both an infrastructure provider and a vehicle for domestic wealth creation.

Management expressed confidence that sustained capital investment, operational discipline, and regulatory stability will continue to enhance shareholder value while expanding national digital capacity.

Telecoms as a Non Oil Growth Engine

The results underscore the structural shift in Nigeria’s economy toward services and technology driven growth. Telecommunications has become one of the most resilient and scalable sectors, contributing significantly to GDP and supporting diversification away from oil dependency.

The ability of telecom operators to maintain heavy capital investment while restoring profitability signals improved sector stability and long term growth potential.

Stronger telecommunications infrastructure improves internet access, digital payments, e commerce, and enterprise productivity for micro, small, and medium enterprises. Reliable connectivity enables MSMEs to reach customers, manage operations efficiently, and integrate into regional and global value chains.
Analysts Support CBN’s 0.5% Interest Rate Cut

The Central Bank of Nigeria reduced its benchmark interest rate by 50 basis points to 26.5 percent, citing sustained disinflation and stronger external buffers. Analysts view the move as a cautious signal of policy normalisation while maintaining a restrictive monetary stance.

Economic analysts have endorsed the decision of the Monetary Policy Committee of the Central Bank of Nigeria to reduce the benchmark interest rate by 50 basis points, describing the move as a cautious step toward policy normalisation rather than the beginning of an aggressive easing cycle.

At the conclusion of its first meeting in 2026, the MPC cut the Monetary Policy Rate to 26.5 percent. The committee retained the Standing Facilities Corridor at plus or minus 450 basis points around the MPR, kept the Cash Reserve Ratio for Deposit Money Banks at 45 percent, Merchant Banks at 16 percent, and maintained 75 percent for non TSA public sector deposits.

According to the committee’s communiqué, the decision followed sustained disinflation, stronger external buffers, and continued banking sector resilience. Foreign reserves have climbed to a 13 year high, while headline inflation slowed to 15.1 percent.

Analysts at Coronation Merchant Bank said the modest reduction signals responsiveness to improving macroeconomic conditions while safeguarding hard won gains on inflation and exchange rate stability.

Professor Uche Uwaleke, President of the Capital Market Academics of Nigeria, described the move as deliberate and strategic.

He noted that inflation has declined for eleven consecutive months, with food inflation easing sharply and month on month inflation turning negative. However, he warned that monetary policy works with significant lags, meaning much of the current disinflation reflects earlier tightening measures.

According to him, cutting rates more aggressively at this stage could undermine the credibility the central bank has built during its tightening cycle. In his view, the 50 basis point reduction should be seen as a signalling move rather than a pivot toward rapid easing.

Exchange Rate Stability and Capital Flows

Exchange rate management remains central to policy considerations. Uwaleke pointed to the recent intervention in which the central bank mopped up about 190 million dollars to moderate naira appreciation. He said this unusual move signals strong foreign inflows from oil earnings, remittances, and portfolio investors.

A sharp rate cut, he cautioned, could trigger unwinding of carry trade positions and renewed dollar demand, potentially destabilising the fixed income market.

Analysts at Analysts Data Services and Resources also stressed that despite the rate cut, monetary policy remains tight. With inflation at 15.10 percent and the MPR at 26.50 percent, real interest rates remain elevated, indicating a restrictive stance.

They added that while recent inflation moderation partly reflects consumer price index rebasing, underlying disinflation is also supported by tighter financial conditions and the fading effects of earlier exchange rate and energy price adjustments.

Uwaleke further highlighted political economy risks, noting that 2026 is a pre election year. Potential expansionary fiscal spending could create upward pressure on prices, requiring monetary authorities to remain vigilant.

A gradual easing path, he said, gives the central bank flexibility to respond to evolving fiscal dynamics and external shocks without compromising macroeconomic stability.

Coronation Merchant Bank analysts echoed this view, stating that real interest rates remain around 11 percent and that further recalibration will depend on sustained moderation in inflation and exchange rate stability. They also interpreted the MPC’s decision to schedule its next meeting in three months, rather than the recent two month rotation, as a signal of confidence in current macroeconomic conditions.

The MPC’s measured rate cut reflects a careful balancing of inflation control, exchange rate stability, and growth support. Analysts agree that while the easing cycle has begun, the central bank is prioritising credibility and macroeconomic stability, ensuring that any further adjustments remain data driven and gradual.

Interest rate adjustments directly influence borrowing costs, credit availability, and investment decisions for micro, small, and medium enterprises. While rates remain high, gradual easing could lower financing costs over time, improve liquidity, and stimulate business expansion if macroeconomic stability is sustained.
NSC, ECOWAS Boost Trade Facilitation at Seme Border

The Nigerian Shippers’ Council and ECOWAS are strengthening the use of the Border Information Centre at the Seme corridor to formalise cross border trade. The initiative provides traders with reliable information on tariffs, procedures, and regulations, while supporting broader trade facilitation and regional integration efforts.

The Nigerian Shippers’ Council and the ECOWAS Commission have intensified efforts to formalise cross border trade through the Border Information Centre at the Seme corridor, positioning it as a practical tool to unlock value in West Africa’s largely informal trade ecosystem.

At a multi stakeholder sensitisation and dialogue session along the Seme Krake axis, regulators, Customs authorities, and regional trade institutions described the BIC as a frontline business support platform for traders, farmers, and logistics operators navigating Nigeria’s land sea trade interface with Benin and the wider ECOWAS market.

The programme, themed “Trade Now: Empowering Cross Border Traders through the Trade Information Desk,” drew participation from the Nigeria Customs Service, the International Trade Centre, Deutsche Gesellschaft für Internationale Zusammenarbeit, private sector groups, and cross border trade associations.

Director of Consumer Affairs at the Nigerian Shippers’ Council, Ify Okolue, described the BIC as a strategic response to one of the most persistent bottlenecks in cross border commerce: limited access to reliable trade information.

She explained that the centre provides traders with clear guidance on tariffs, documentation requirements, import and export procedures, standards, and sanitary and phytosanitary measures. By centralising this information, the BIC aims to reduce misinformation, curb extortion, and lower transaction costs.

Okolue said Seme was deliberately chosen because of its economic importance as a gateway linking Nigerian seaports to inland markets in Benin and across the ECOWAS sub region.

Beyond Seme Krake, the Council has established BICs at Jibiya Maradi, Illela Sokoto, and Mfum, with a new centre at Idiroko expected to become operational before the end of the second quarter of 2026.

She added that the initiative aligns with the Council’s mandate as port economic regulator, extending oversight beyond seaports into inland corridors where logistics inefficiencies directly increase trade costs.

Complementing the border desks, the Council highlighted investments in inland dry ports at Kaduna, Dala, and Funtua to decentralise shipping services and strengthen transit trade to land linked countries such as Niger, Chad, and Mali.

Plans are also underway to develop vehicle transit areas along trade corridors to improve road safety and logistics efficiency. According to Okolue, driver fatigue and unsafe practices have contributed to avoidable accidents, and structured rest areas are expected to improve safety and cargo reliability.

Representing the Comptroller General of Customs, the Customs Area Controller of Seme Area Command, Wale Adenuga, said the engagement aligns with Customs’ renewed focus on trade facilitation. He assured legitimate traders of expedited clearance and emphasised coordination with Beninese authorities under a unified command structure to enhance efficiency and enforcement.

From a regional perspective, ECOWAS officials said non tariff barriers and limited awareness of trade rules remain major obstacles to intra regional commerce.

Speaking on behalf of the ECOWAS Director of Trade, Sarah Opofure said Trade Information Desks are essential to reducing harassment and preventing informal charges faced by traders, many of whom are women and youth operating at small scale levels.

Despite frameworks such as the ECOWAS Trade Liberalisation Scheme and the Protocol on Free Movement of Persons, informal traders often struggle with compliance due to poor access to accurate information.

ECOWAS sensitisation caravans along key corridors, including the Lagos Abidjan axis, have reportedly reached over 1,800 women and young traders, while a joint agricultural trade project with GIZ has provided targeted training and mobility support to Nigerian women traders.

Executive Secretary of the Nigerian Shippers’ Council, Pius Akutah, noted increasing uptake of the BIC facility and urged traders to take fuller advantage of the platform to improve compliance and efficiency.

Deepening the use of the Border Information Centre at Seme reflects a broader push to translate regional trade frameworks into practical results on the ground. By improving transparency, coordination, and trader awareness, stakeholders aim to enhance corridor competitiveness and unlock greater value within West Africa’s cross border economy.

Many cross border traders in West Africa operate informally and face high transaction costs due to information gaps and non tariff barriers. Access to structured trade information can reduce compliance risks, lower costs, and expand market access for micro, small, and medium enterprises, particularly women led businesses active in regional trade.
AfDB, AU Urge Mobility Reform to Boost Intra African Trade

African leaders and development institutions are pushing for visa free travel across the continent to unlock the full potential of AfCFTA. With most African travellers still requiring visas for intra continental trips, policymakers say mobility reform is essential for boosting trade, investment, and regional integration.

African policymakers and development institutions have renewed calls for visa free travel across the continent, describing free movement of people as critical to unlocking economic transformation under the African Continental Free Trade Area.

The renewed push followed a high level symposium co convened by the African Development Bank Group and the African Union Commission on the margins of the African Union summit in Addis Ababa. Participants argued that restrictive visa regimes remain a major obstacle to intra African trade, services growth, investment flows, tourism, and labour mobility.

According to data cited at the event, only five African countries currently offer visa free access or visas on arrival to citizens of all African states. These include Seychelles, Mozambique, Rwanda, Comoros, and Madagascar. By contrast, countries such as the Democratic Republic of Congo, Equatorial Guinea, São Tomé, and Sudan require citizens from every African country to obtain a visa before travel.

On average, African citizens require visas to visit about 60 percent of African countries. The rate ranges from a high of 84 percent for Somalia to a low of 41 percent for The Gambia, underscoring uneven progress toward continental mobility.

Participants at the symposium described this fragmentation as a structural constraint on the success of AfCFTA, which seeks to create a single African market for goods and services.

Mobility as an Economic Enabler

Alex Mubiru, Director General for Eastern Africa at the African Development Bank Group, said visa free travel, interoperable digital systems, and integrated markets are practical enablers of enterprise development and regional value chains.

“The evidence is clear. The economics support openness. The human story demands it,” he said, urging countries to move from incremental reforms to transformative change.

Amma Twum Amoah, Commissioner for Health, Humanitarian Affairs and Social Development at the African Union Commission, called for faster implementation of continental frameworks, describing visa openness as a strategic lever for strengthening regional markets and improving collective responses to economic and humanitarian shocks.

Former AU Commission Chairperson Nkosazana Dlamini-Zuma reiterated that free movement remains central to the African Union’s long term development blueprint, Agenda 2063. She urged member states to operationalise initiatives such as the African Passport and the Free Movement of Persons Protocol.

Ghana’s Trade and Industry Minister, Elizabeth Ofosu Adjare, shared her country’s experience with open visa policies, citing increased business travel, tourism inflows, and investor interest as early benefits.
