The Chief Executive Officer of Ethiopian Airlines, Mesfin Bekele, called for full implementation of the Single African Air Transport Market, arguing that aviation connectivity and visa liberalisation must advance together to ensure seamless movement.

Regional leaders also emphasised the importance of strengthening border management, digital identity systems, and information sharing to build trust among member states while facilitating mobility.

Participants concluded that achieving a visa free Africa will require policy alignment across migration systems, digital infrastructure investment, and sustained political commitment at national and regional levels.

Visa liberalisation is increasingly seen as an economic reform rather than simply a migration policy issue. By reducing barriers to mobility, African countries can deepen integration, accelerate trade under AfCFTA, and enhance long term competitiveness in a rapidly evolving global economy.

For micro, small, and medium enterprises, visa restrictions increase transaction costs, delay business travel, and limit market access. Easier movement across borders can support cross border trade, services expansion, tourism ventures, and participation in regional value chains, directly strengthening MSME competitiveness.
Oil Surges 10% Amid Iran Conflict, Analysts Warn of $100 per Barrel Risk
Brent climbs to $80 as fears grow over potential Strait of Hormuz disruption.

Oil prices surged 10% to $80 per barrel following escalating conflict involving Iran, with analysts warning prices could approach $100 if the Strait of Hormuz is disrupted. The development raises inflation and cost risks globally, particularly for fuel-dependent businesses and import-driven economies.

Global oil prices jumped 10% to around $80 per barrel over the weekend following U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran, escalating tensions in the Middle East. Analysts now warn that prices could climb toward $100 per barrel if supply disruptions intensify, particularly if the Strait of Hormuz is affected.

The surge reflects growing concern that a broader regional conflict could disrupt one of the world’s most critical oil transit routes.

 

What Is Driving the Spike

Key developments influencing prices include:

  • U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran

  • Escalation into a broader regional conflict

  • Fears of disruption to the Strait of Hormuz, a major global oil shipping route

  • Heightened geopolitical risk premiums

Brent crude had already rallied to $73 per barrel on Friday, its highest level since July, before the weekend escalation.

Energy analysts suggest that prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz could trigger a sharp supply shock.

 

Strait of Hormuz: The Critical Risk Factor

The Strait of Hormuz handles a significant portion of global crude exports. If shipping is disrupted:

  • An estimated 8–10 million barrels per day (bpd) could be temporarily lost

  • Even with diversion via Saudi Arabia’s East-West pipeline and Abu Dhabi infrastructure, global supply would be materially affected

Rystad Energy estimates prices could initially rise by about $20 per barrel, pushing Brent toward $92, while some analysts see upside risk beyond $100 if disruption persists.

 

OPEC+ Output Increase Offers Limited Relief

OPEC+ agreed to increase production by 206,000 bpd from April, representing less than 0.2% of global demand, a modest adjustment unlikely to offset a major shipping disruption.

Meanwhile:

  • RBC analysts warn prices could exceed $100 per barrel

  • Rabobank projects near-term levels above $90

  • Asian refiners are reviewing stockpiles and alternative supply routes

  • India may increase imports of Russian crude to compensate for potential Middle East shortages

 

Market and Economic Implications

Higher oil prices typically translate into:

  • Increased fuel and transportation costs

  • Upward pressure on inflation

  • Higher input costs for manufacturers

  • Currency pressure for oil-importing countries

For oil-exporting African economies, short-term fiscal revenues may improve, but domestic fuel subsidies and price controls can complicate the net benefit.

Rising oil prices directly affect logistics, production costs and consumer purchasing power. MSMEs in transport, manufacturing and agriculture may face margin compression if fuel prices climb. Businesses should prepare for potential cost volatility and review pricing, supply chains and energy efficiency strategies.
Niger Delta Host Communities Push for 13% Derivation Share as 155 PIA Trusts Take Off
Communities seek direct funding from state allocations while NUPRC reports progress on Host Community Development Trusts.

Host communities in the Niger Delta are demanding a direct share of the 13% oil derivation fund, even as the NUPRC confirms the incorporation of 155 Host Community Development Trusts under the PIA. While implementation is advancing, governance and funding debates continue to shape the future of community-based energy development.

Agitations for a direct share of Nigeria’s 13 percent oil derivation fund resurfaced over the weekend as host communities in the Niger Delta called on state governors to channel part of the allocation to Host Community Development Trusts (HCDTs).

The renewed demand comes as the Nigerian Upstream Petroleum Regulatory Commission (NUPRC) announced the incorporation of 155 HCDTs under the Petroleum Industry Act (PIA), with 79 already funded through mandatory oil company contributions.

 

The Core Demand: 13% Derivation for Communities

At a town hall meeting in Rivers State involving regulators, oil companies and community representatives, host community leaders argued that:

  • The 13% oil derivation fund was intended to benefit oil-producing communities, not state governments alone

  • Governors should remit part of the allocation directly to HCDTs

  • Community-level trusts can complement the 3% OPEX contributions mandated under the PIA

Leaders framed the demand as part of a broader push for resource equity and inclusive development in the Niger Delta.

 

Progress Under the Petroleum Industry Act (PIA)

The NUPRC reported measurable implementation milestones:

  • 155 Host Community Development Trusts (HCDTs) incorporated

  • 79 trusts funded via mandatory 3% operational expenditure (OPEX) contributions from oil companies (settlors)

  • 663 projects currently under execution across the region

  • Launch of a digital monitoring platform, “HostComply,” to track compliance and reporting in real time

The Commission noted that the success of the framework depends on collective responsibility among government, oil firms and host communities.

 

Spending Rules Under the PIA

Community leaders also cautioned trust executives to comply strictly with statutory spending limits:

  • 75% of HCDT funds must go to projects

  • 20% allocated to investments

  • 5% capped for administrative expenses

Misallocation, they warned, could undermine long-term development outcomes.

 

Governance Challenges Identified

Despite progress, the NUPRC acknowledged ongoing issues, including:

  • Governance and accountability concerns

  • Bureaucratic bottlenecks

  • Internal community disputes

  • Capacity gaps within trust management structures

To address these, the Commission outlined measures such as:

  • Capacity building for trust boards

  • Structured dispute resolution mechanisms

  • Improved stakeholder engagement

  • Independent monitoring frameworks

 

Economic and Investment Implications

The PIA’s host community framework is designed to reduce conflict, improve local development outcomes and create a more stable operating environment for oil and gas investors.

However, the renewed call for access to the 13% derivation fund signals potential policy debates between state governments and host communities over resource control and revenue allocation.

Stable host community relations are critical for oil, gas and energy value chains. Improved transparency and structured community funding can reduce disruptions and create local procurement opportunities for MSMEs in construction, services, logistics and infrastructure projects tied to HCDTs.
Wema Bank Unveils Top 10 Nominees for 2026 SARA Gives to Empower Her Award
Women-focused initiative recognises leaders driving impact and empowerment across Nigeria.

Wema Bank has unveiled the Top 10 nominees for its 2026 SARA Gives to Empower Her Award, recognising women who actively empower others. Five winners will be announced on March 4, reinforcing the bank’s commitment to advancing women-led economic growth and enterprise development.

Wema Bank has announced the Top 10 nominees for the 2026 edition of its SARA Gives to Empower Her Award & Grant, a flagship initiative under its women-focused platform, SARA by Wema.

The award celebrates women who have built a legacy of empowering other women socially, professionally and economically. Five winners will be selected and announced during the bank’s International Women’s Day (IWD) event on March 4, 2026.

 

About the Initiative

Launched in 2024, the She Empowers Her Award, now in its second edition is designed to:

  • Spotlight women creating measurable impact

  • Encourage peer-to-peer empowerment

  • Provide recognition and grant support

  • Strengthen networks of women-led influence

The 2026 edition aligns with the global IWD theme, “Give to Gain,” and Wema Bank’s local theme, “When She Gains, We Grow.”

 

Top 10 Nominees

The shortlisted nominees represent diverse sectors including entrepreneurship, nonprofit leadership, coaching, marketing and advocacy.

They include:

  • Bosede Nwachi (Social Entrepreneur)

  • Chigozie Blessing (NGO Founder)

  • Ekwutosi Cynthia (Customer Success Leader)

  • Franca Ozini (Coach & Public Speaker)

  • Hassanat Abubakar (TEDx Speaker)

  • Ikepo Osawaye (Mentor)

  • Jennifer Edem Ekong (Marketing Professional)

  • Ambassador Mabel Abel-Onaiwu (World Safety Ambassador)

  • Oluwaseun Joan Stephen (Founder, Patrick Stephen Purple Heart Foundation for Widows)

  • Victoria Ezechukwu-Obiya (Virtual Assistant Coach)

 

Selection Process

The bank implemented a structured and transparent evaluation system, including:

  • Public nominations (February 12–22, 2026)

  • Independent audit vetting

  • Internal jury review

  • Public voting

A total of:

  • 2,332 nominations were received

  • Covering 351 women

  • 10 finalists were selected based on impact and track record

Voting is currently open to determine the Top 5 winners.

 

Award Ceremony Details

  • Date: March 4, 2026

  • Venue: Lagos

  • Live stream: Wema Bank’s official YouTube channel

  • Time: 10:00 a.m.

The event will form part of the bank’s International Women’s Day activities.

 

Broader Economic Context

Women-led businesses and enterprises contribute significantly to Nigeria’s SME ecosystem. However, access to visibility, funding, mentorship and structured recognition remains a challenge for many.

Initiatives like SARA by Wema aim to:

  • Expand access to capital and networks

  • Encourage inclusive entrepreneurship

  • Promote women-led economic growth

Women-owned MSMEs are central to Africa’s economic expansion. Recognition platforms combined with grant support can unlock growth opportunities, partnerships and increased market credibility. Strengthening women-led enterprises directly contributes to broader economic resilience and job creation.
Zamfara Abolishes Cash Revenue Collection to Strengthen Fiscal Transparency
Governor Dauda Lawal mandates full digital payments across MDAs to curb leakages and boost internally generated revenue.

Zamfara State has abolished cash-based revenue collection, mandating full digital payments across all government agencies. The reform aligns with national tax restructuring efforts and aims to curb leakages, boost IGR and improve transparency, strengthening the state’s fiscal and business environment.

Governor Dauda Lawal has ordered the immediate abolition of cash-based revenue collection across Zamfara State, directing all Ministries, Departments and Agencies (MDAs) to transition to digital payment systems. The move is aimed at enhancing transparency, eliminating revenue leakages and strengthening fiscal accountability under Nigeria’s new tax reform framework.

The directive was announced during a town hall sensitisation on the Nigeria Tax Reform Acts 2025, organised by the Zamfara State Internal Revenue Service in Gusau.

 

Key Policy Shift: From Cash to Digital

Under the new directive:

  • All cash revenue collections across MDAs are abolished

  • Payments must be made through approved digital platforms

  • Real-time monitoring and inter-agency verification systems will be strengthened

  • Revenue databases across MDAs will be harmonised

The government stated that informal collections, duplication and revenue leakages will no longer be tolerated.

 

Alignment with National Tax Reform

Governor Lawal noted that the Nigeria Tax Reform Acts 2025 have:

  • Clarified tax administration responsibilities

  • Standardised procedures nationwide

  • Improved coordination among federal, state and local governments

Zamfara’s revised Consolidated Revenue Law has also:

  • Strengthened the authority of the State Internal Revenue Service

  • Unified tax and non-tax revenue collection under a single legal framework

  • Centralised assessment, collection and accounting processes

 

Revenue Targets and Institutional Accountability

The state aims to achieve an Internally Generated Revenue (IGR) target of:

  • ₦38–₦42 billion in 2025

To support this, MDAs have been directed to:

  • Review existing laws and procedures

  • Eliminate overlaps and ambiguities

  • Ensure prompt remittance through approved channels

Outstanding revenue remitters were recognised at the town hall, reinforcing a culture of compliance and performance.

 

Economic and Governance Implications

The shift to digital revenue systems is expected to:

  • Reduce cash-handling risks

  • Improve audit trails and compliance

  • Increase investor confidence

  • Enhance fiscal discipline

For states seeking to diversify revenue streams beyond federal allocations, digitalisation is increasingly becoming a governance priority.

A transparent, digital revenue system reduces informal charges and unpredictability for businesses. MSMEs operating in Zamfara can expect clearer payment channels, improved documentation and reduced exposure to unofficial levies, factors that improve ease of doing business and investment confidence.
OpenAI Secures $110bn Funding Round, Expands Strategic Alliances with Amazon and Nvidia
Record private financing values AI leader at $730bn as infrastructure spending accelerates toward 2030 goals.

OpenAI has closed a record $110 billion funding round led by Amazon, Nvidia and SoftBank, raising its valuation to $730 billion. The company is expanding cloud and GPU partnerships while targeting $600 billion in compute spending by 2030, reinforcing AI’s central role in the global economy.

OpenAI has closed a historic $110 billion funding round more than double its previous raise, cementing its position as the most highly valued private technology company globally. Backed by Amazon, Nvidia and SoftBank, the deal pushes OpenAI’s pre-money valuation to $730 billion and signals intensified global investment in artificial intelligence infrastructure.

The financing underscores the scale of capital required to power next-generation AI systems and the growing competition in the enterprise AI market.

 

Breakdown of the Funding Round

Key investors include:

  • Amazon: $50 billion

  • Nvidia: $30 billion

  • SoftBank: $30 billion

The valuation marks a sharp increase from OpenAI’s $500 billion secondary valuation in October. Additional investors may join as the round progresses.

 

Strategic Partnerships and Infrastructure Expansion

Beyond capital, the deal deepens operational alliances:

Amazon Partnership

  • Multi-year strategic collaboration

  • Development of customised AI models for Amazon applications

  • Expansion of existing $38 billion AWS agreement by an additional $100 billion over eight years

  • AWS to serve as exclusive third-party cloud provider for OpenAI’s enterprise platform, Frontier

Amazon’s $50 billion investment will be structured as:

  • $15 billion initial commitment

  • $35 billion contingent on milestone conditions

Nvidia Collaboration

  • Expansion of long-standing GPU partnership

  • Access to:

    • 3 gigawatts of inference capacity

    • 2 gigawatts of training capacity

    • Powered by Nvidia’s Vera Rubin systems

 

Massive Compute and Revenue Targets

OpenAI has revised its infrastructure roadmap:

  • Targeting $600 billion in total compute spend by 2030

  • Previously floated infrastructure ambitions of up to $1.4 trillion

  • Projecting $280 billion in annual revenue by 2030

    • Roughly split between consumer and enterprise segments

The company faces growing competition from:

  • Google’s Gemini

  • Anthropic (enterprise AI)

  • xAI

 

Microsoft Partnership Remains Intact

OpenAI clarified that the new funding and partnerships do not alter its existing long-term relationship with Microsoft, which remains a core strategic backer.

 

Market Implications

This marks the largest private financing round in history, surpassing OpenAI’s previous $40 billion raise and exceeding recent funding rounds by major AI competitors.

The scale of investment highlights:

  • Intensifying AI infrastructure arms race

  • Rising global demand for GPU capacity

  • Shift toward enterprise AI monetisation

  • Increasing capital requirements for advanced model development

AI is rapidly becoming embedded across finance, healthcare, logistics, marketing and customer service. As global AI infrastructure expands, MSMEs will gain greater access to scalable AI tools but competition and digital capability gaps may widen. Businesses that invest early in AI adoption and digital skills development will be better positioned to compete in an AI-driven economy.
Malawi’s Public Debt Surpasses 90% of GDP, Government Seeks IMF Support
Fiscal deficit projected at 11.9% as authorities pursue debt restructuring and economic stabilisation.

Malawi’s public debt has risen above 90% of GDP, prompting the government to seek a new IMF support programme and pursue debt restructuring. With inflation above 20% and a fiscal deficit near 12%, authorities aim to restore stability while targeting 3.8% economic growth in 2026.

Malawi’s public debt has climbed to unsustainable levels above 90% of gross domestic product (GDP), raising concerns over fiscal stability and long-term economic growth. The announcement was made by Finance Minister Joseph Mwanamvekha during the country’s annual budget presentation.

With inflation above 20% since mid-2022 and a widening fiscal deficit, the government is seeking a new support programme with the International Monetary Fund (IMF) to restore macroeconomic balance.

 

Debt Position and Fiscal Outlook

Key figures presented in the budget include:

  • Public debt stock: 23.9 trillion kwacha ($13.92 billion)

  • Debt-to-GDP ratio: Above 90%

  • Domestic debt share: Approximately 65% of total debt

  • Current fiscal deficit: 11.9% of GDP

  • Target deficit (next fiscal year): Around 9% of GDP

The government also reiterated its economic growth forecast of 3.8% for 2026.

 

Debt Restructuring and IMF Engagement

To create fiscal space and stabilise the economy, Malawi plans to:

  • Restructure both domestic and external debt

  • Negotiate a new IMF-supported programme in the short to medium term

  • Implement measures to narrow the budget deficit

The move reflects broader efforts to restore investor confidence and improve donor support in a country heavily reliant on external financing.

 

Macroeconomic Pressures

Malawi’s economy has faced prolonged challenges, including:

  • Persistent inflation above 20%

  • High public spending relative to revenue

  • Limited fiscal buffers

  • Pressure on currency and foreign reserves

High domestic borrowing levels may also crowd out private sector credit and raise borrowing costs for businesses.

 

Regional and Investment Implications

For Southern African markets, Malawi’s fiscal adjustment path will influence:

  • Sovereign risk perceptions

  • Access to concessional financing

  • Exchange rate stability

  • Business operating conditions

An IMF programme could provide policy credibility and unlock additional development funding.

High public debt and inflation affect interest rates, currency stability and consumer purchasing power. MSMEs in Malawi may face tighter credit conditions and higher operating costs. Fiscal reforms and IMF support, if successfully implemented, could stabilise the business environment and improve medium-term investment prospects.
Nigeria, Cameroon Sign Landmark Aviation Safety and Search & Rescue Pact
New bilateral agreement strengthens cross-border airspace safety and emergency response coordination.

Nigeria and Cameroon have signed a bilateral agreement to enhance aviation search and rescue operations, strengthening cross-border airspace safety and emergency coordination. The pact supports regional integration and reinforces aviation reliability for trade and business activities.

Nigeria and Cameroon have signed a Bilateral Agreement on Technical Aeronautical Search and Rescue Operations, marking a significant step toward enhanced aviation safety across their shared airspace.

The agreement, signed in Yaoundé by Nigeria’s Minister of Aviation and Aerospace Development, Festus Keyamo, and Cameroon’s Minister of Transport, Jean Ernest NgalNgallé Bibehe, establishes a framework for faster and more coordinated emergency responses to aviation incidents along both countries’ borders.

 

What the Agreement Covers

The pact is designed to:

  • Improve cross-border search and rescue coordination

  • Enable quicker response to aviation emergencies

  • Strengthen airspace monitoring and operational collaboration

  • Align technical standards and communication protocols

The Nigerian delegation included senior aviation regulators and operators to ensure seamless implementation, including:

  • Director General, Nigeria Civil Aviation Authority (NCAA)

  • Managing Director, Nigerian Airspace Management Agency (NAMA)

  • Key ministry officials

 

Strategic and Regional Significance

The agreement supports broader regional aviation integration and aligns with Nigeria’s aviation reform and safety agenda.

Key objectives include:

  • Institutional strengthening

  • Regional cooperation in airspace management

  • Protection of lives and property

  • Increased operational resilience

By formalising joint search and rescue protocols, both countries aim to enhance confidence in West and Central Africa’s aviation ecosystem.

 

Economic and Industry Implications

Safer and more coordinated airspace operations can:

  • Reduce aviation risk premiums

  • Improve airline operational efficiency

  • Strengthen regional connectivity

  • Enhance investor confidence in aviation infrastructure

Improved emergency preparedness is also a critical compliance requirement under international aviation safety standards.

Reliable and safe aviation systems support trade, tourism, logistics and business travel. MSMEs that depend on air transport for cargo, supply chains or executive travel benefit from stronger safety coordination and reduced operational risks across regional routes.
Vodafone Partners Amazon’s LEO Satellites to Connect Remote 4G/5G Masts in Europe and Africa
Satellite backhaul deal aims to cut fibre costs and expand network coverage in hard-to-reach regions.

Vodafone has partnered with Amazon’s Low Earth Orbit satellite network to connect remote 4G and 5G masts across Europe and Africa. The deal enables high-speed satellite backhaul, reducing fibre costs and accelerating digital connectivity in underserved regions.

Vodafone has signed a strategic agreement with Amazon’s Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite network to connect 4G and 5G mobile base stations in remote areas across Europe and Africa.

The partnership will enable high-speed satellite backhaul connectivity for masts located in regions where fibre infrastructure is costly or difficult to deploy.

 

What the Deal Includes

Under the agreement:

  • Amazon LEO satellites will provide connectivity of up to:

    • 1 Gbps download speeds

    • 400 Mbps upload speeds

  • Satellite links will connect Vodafone’s core network to remote mobile masts

  • Deployment begins in Germany and other European markets this year

  • Gradual rollout will extend to Africa via Vodafone’s subsidiary, Vodacom

Amazon’s LEO constellation currently has over 200 satellites in orbit, with hundreds more prepared for launch.

 

Why Satellite Backhaul Matters

Traditional fibre installation in remote or rural regions is often:

  • Capital-intensive

  • Logistically complex

  • Time-consuming

Satellite backhaul offers:

  • Faster deployment

  • Lower infrastructure costs

  • Expanded coverage in underserved communities

  • Improved reliability in challenging terrains

 

Africa Rollout via Vodacom

Following European deployment, Vodafone plans to extend the solution across African markets through Vodacom, potentially enhancing connectivity in:

  • Rural communities

  • Mining zones

  • Agricultural regions

  • Border and remote commercial corridors

Improved connectivity is expected to support digital inclusion and mobile broadband expansion across underserved markets.

 

Additional Satellite Plans

Separately, Vodafone is working with AST SpaceMobile to offer direct satellite connectivity to customers using standard smartphones, although service launch dates have not yet been announced.

 

Industry and Market Implications

The partnership reflects a broader shift toward satellite-enabled telecom infrastructure, as global operators:

  • Seek alternatives to fibre-heavy models

  • Accelerate rural network expansion

  • Compete in high-growth emerging markets

  • Leverage space-based broadband for resilience

Low Earth Orbit networks are increasingly viewed as a strategic layer in next-generation telecom ecosystems.

Expanded rural and remote connectivity unlocks digital access for SMEs in agriculture, mining, trade and services. Reliable broadband enables mobile payments, e-commerce, logistics tracking and remote operations, helping MSMEs scale beyond urban centres and participate in digital markets.
South Africa’s Manufacturing PMI Slips Further Below Growth Threshold in February
Business activity and employment decline as sector struggles with weak demand and operational bottlenecks.

South Africa’s manufacturing PMI fell to 47.4 in February, signalling continued contraction as business activity and employment declined. Persistent port delays, power disruptions and weak demand are weighing on the sector’s recovery prospects.

South Africa’s manufacturing sentiment weakened in February, with the headline Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI) falling further below the 50-point neutral mark. The seasonally adjusted PMI, sponsored by Absa, declined to 47.4 points from 48.7 in January, signalling continued contraction in the sector.

A PMI reading below 50 indicates a deterioration in business conditions.

 

Key Highlights from the February PMI

  • Headline PMI: 47.4 (down from 48.7 in January)

  • Business activity: Weakened

  • Employment: Continued to decline

  • Most subcomponents: Largely unchanged

  • Expected business conditions sub-index: Improved slightly

The data suggests that an early-year rebound in production was not sustained.

 

Challenges Facing Manufacturers

Ongoing structural and operational pressures include:

  • Delays in shipments at South African ports

  • Localised electricity disruptions

  • Weak domestic and external demand

  • Stop-start production cycles

According to Absa, the inconsistent output pattern is limiting:

  • Long-term capacity expansion

  • Investment growth

  • Job creation

 

Economic and Investment Implications

The manufacturing sector plays a critical role in:

  • Export earnings

  • Industrial employment

  • Supply chain stability

  • GDP contribution

Sustained PMI contraction may dampen investor confidence and slow industrial recovery, especially amid broader economic headwinds.

Manufacturing slowdown affects suppliers, distributors and service providers across the value chain. MSMEs linked to industrial production may face weaker demand, delayed payments and cautious investment behaviour. Monitoring PMI trends helps businesses anticipate shifts in production cycles and plan inventory, staffing and financing accordingly.
Petrol Prices May Hit ₦1,000/Litre as Dangote Refinery Raises Gantry Rate
Global crude surge pushes gantry price to ₦874/litre, with retail pump prices expected to rise nationwide.

Dangote Refinery has increased its gantry petrol price to ₦874 per litre, potentially pushing pump prices above ₦1,000 nationwide. The move reflects global crude oil volatility linked to Middle East tensions, underscoring Nigeria’s continued exposure to international energy market fluctuations despite growing domestic refining capacity.

Petrol prices across Nigeria may soon climb to between ₦980 and over ₦1,000 per litre, following a fresh increase in the gantry price by the Dangote Petroleum Refinery. The refinery raised its ex-depot price from ₦774 to ₦874 per litre, citing volatility in global crude oil markets and rising replacement costs.

Industry marketers say the adjustment will ripple through the downstream value chain, potentially pushing retail pump prices above ₦1,000 in some locations depending on logistics and distribution costs.

 

What Triggered the Price Hike

Key drivers behind the adjustment include:

  • Surge in global crude oil prices above $80 per barrel

  • Heightened geopolitical tensions in the Middle East

  • Shipping disruptions and rising insurance premiums

  • Replacement cost pressures in the downstream market

Petrol loading at the refinery was temporarily suspended after crude prices spiked, although diesel supply continued. Several depot owners also paused sales to reassess pricing amid market uncertainty.

 

Global Oil Market Volatility Intensifies

Escalating tensions involving the United States, Israel and Iran have triggered sharp oil price movements:

  • Brent crude rose nearly 9% to around $79 per barrel

  • WTI gained over 7% to trade above $72

  • Shipping activity through the Strait of Hormuz declined by about 70%

  • War-risk insurance premiums increased by up to 50%

The Strait of Hormuz remains a critical global energy chokepoint, handling approximately 20–21 million barrels per day, nearly 20% of global oil consumption.

JPMorgan projects that Brent crude could rise to $120 per barrel if disruptions persist, raising the risk of further domestic fuel price increases in Nigeria.

 

Domestic Impact and Market Response

With the revised gantry price at ₦874 per litre:

  • Retail prices are expected to range from ₦980 to over ₦1,000

  • Location and logistics costs will influence final pump prices

  • Independent marketers confirm likely upward adjustments nationwide

Despite Nigeria’s expanding domestic refining capacity, petrol pricing remains closely tied to global crude fundamentals.

 

Dangote’s Broader Industrial Strategy

Beyond refining, Dangote Group has announced plans to expand into:

  • Electricity generation

  • Steel production

  • Port infrastructure development

The 650,000-barrel-per-day refinery is currently operational, with plans to double output within three years. The group also intends to list refinery shares on the Nigerian stock exchange to broaden local participation.

Dangote has emphasised that reliable power supply and industrial expansion are central to reducing import dependence, strengthening energy security and creating employment.

 

Economic Implications

Rising petrol prices may:

  • Increase transportation and logistics costs

  • Raise production expenses for manufacturers

  • Worsen inflationary pressures

  • Affect consumer purchasing power

Even with domestic refining, Nigeria remains exposed to global oil price volatility due to crude-linked pricing structures.

Fuel costs directly impact transport, distribution, manufacturing and service delivery. MSMEs should anticipate higher operating expenses and review pricing strategies, supply chains and energy alternatives to manage margin pressures during periods of oil market volatility.
Israel–Iran Crisis Spurs US Security Alert in Nigeria as Protests Emerge Nationwide
Peaceful demonstrations in Lagos and northern states follow reported killings of Iranian leaders amid escalating Middle East conflict.

Escalating Israel–Iran tensions have led the US to issue a security alert to its citizens in Nigeria, while peaceful Shi’ite protests occurred across several states. Although no violence was reported, the situation highlights how global conflicts can influence local security conditions and economic stability in Africa’s largest economy.

Rising tensions between the United States, Israel, and Iran have triggered diplomatic and security ripples in Nigeria. The US government has issued a security advisory to its citizens in Nigeria, while members of the Islamic Movement in Nigeria (IMN) staged peaceful protests in Lagos and several northern states following reports that Iran’s Supreme Leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, was killed in coordinated US–Israeli strikes.

Although demonstrations ended without major incidents, security agencies heightened monitoring across affected cities.

 

US Issues Security Advisory

The US government warned its citizens in Nigeria and globally to exercise increased caution following the launch of US combat operations involving Iran.

Key advisory points:

  • Americans should monitor updates from US embassies and consulates.

  • Possible travel disruptions due to airspace closures.

  • Enrollment in the Smart Traveler Enrollment Program (STEP) was encouraged.

  • Citizens were advised to follow official security update channels.

The alert follows Iranian media reports confirming the death of Ayatollah Khamenei and other senior officials in recent airstrikes. Iran has reportedly retaliated with missile strikes targeting US bases in Gulf countries.

 

Protests Across Lagos and Northern States

Peaceful demonstrations were recorded in:

  • Lagos (Maryland axis)

  • Kano

  • Bauchi

  • Yobe (Potiskum, Bade, Nguru)

  • Gombe

Protesters carried placards and portraits, expressing solidarity with Iran and opposition to US and Israeli actions. Movement leaders emphasised that the demonstrations were peaceful and intended to express grievances rather than incite violence.

In Kano and other states, police confirmed:

  • Rapid deployment of officers based on intelligence

  • Escorting protesters to prevent unrest

  • No reported breakdown of law and order

Security agencies remained on standby after the demonstrations concluded.

 

Broader Geopolitical Context

The developments follow:

  • Reported coordinated US–Israeli airstrikes in Tehran

  • The reported death of Ayatollah Khamenei and other senior Iranian figures

  • Retaliatory missile launches by Iran targeting US military installations

  • Heightened global diplomatic tensions

The crisis has already influenced global oil prices and international travel advisories.

 

Local Security and Economic Implications

While protests in Nigeria were largely peaceful, international crises can have domestic spillover effects, including:

  • Heightened security surveillance in major cities

  • Diplomatic sensitivity

  • Oil price volatility

  • Temporary disruptions in travel or business operations

Nigeria’s economy remains highly sensitive to geopolitical developments, particularly those affecting global energy markets.

Geopolitical instability can affect fuel prices, logistics costs, investor confidence, and security perceptions. MSMEs should monitor developments closely, strengthen contingency planning, and prepare for potential cost pressures tied to global energy and political volatility.
Nigeria’s Net FX Reserves Climb to $34.8bn as Reforms Strengthen External Buffers
CBN reports sharp rise in net reserves, citing stronger inflows, improved transparency, and restored currency market confidence.

Nigeria’s net foreign exchange reserves surged to $34.8 billion in 2025, up sharply from previous years, reflecting stronger inflows and ongoing CBN reforms. The rise strengthens external buffers, supports currency stability, and signals improving macroeconomic resilience.

Nigeria’s net foreign exchange (FX) reserves rose significantly to $34.8 billion by the end of 2025, according to Central Bank Governor Olayemi Cardoso. The increase marks a substantial recovery from $3.99 billion two years earlier, reflecting what the CBN describes as strengthened external fundamentals and the impact of ongoing forex market reforms.

The latest figures underscore renewed confidence in Nigeria’s currency management framework and improved resilience against external shocks.

 

Key Reserve Figures

The CBN disclosed the following updates:

  • Net FX reserves:

    • $34.8 billion (end-2025)

    • Up from $23.11 billion a year earlier

    • Up from $3.99 billion two years ago

  • Gross reserves:

    • $45.71 billion (end-2025)

    • Up from $40.19 billion the previous year

    • Recently reported at $50.45 billion as of February 16

The rise in net reserves which represent liquid, usable external assets after short-term liabilities, signals stronger real buffers available to defend the currency and meet external obligations.

 

What Drove the Increase

Governor Cardoso attributed the improvement to:

  • Stronger forex inflows

  • Enhanced reserves management

  • Clearer and more transparent FX rules

  • Reduced market distortions

  • Renewed investor confidence

Since his appointment in 2023, the CBN has implemented reforms aimed at stabilising the naira, improving transparency, and unwinding multiple exchange rate distortions.

 

Implications for Currency Stability

Higher net reserves improve Nigeria’s ability to:

  • Meet external debt obligations

  • Intervene in the FX market when necessary

  • Manage exchange rate volatility

  • Strengthen sovereign credibility

Cardoso emphasised that the CBN will maintain robust external buffers and continue orderly forex market operations.

 

Broader Economic Significance

A stronger reserve position enhances Nigeria’s macroeconomic standing at a time of:

  • Global oil price volatility

  • Currency reform transitions

  • External financing pressures

  • Inflation management challenges

Sustained reserve growth could further support investor confidence and capital inflows.

FX stability directly affects import costs, pricing strategies, raw material sourcing, and cross-border trade. Stronger reserves may reduce currency volatility and improve access to foreign exchange, helping MSMEs plan better, manage risk, and protect margins.
Shoprite First-Half Profit Rises 7.7% on Strong Festive Season Sales
South Africa’s largest retailer reports solid revenue growth despite lower inflation and slight margin pressure.

Shoprite reported a 7.7% rise in first-half profit, driven by strong festive season demand and steady sales growth. Despite slightly lower margins due to reduced inflation, the retailer maintained earnings momentum and expects low single-digit price inflation to continue into the second half of 2026.

Shoprite Holdings, Africa’s largest grocery retailer, posted a 7.7% increase in first-half profit, driven by strong festive season demand and broad-based sales growth across its supermarket chains.

For the six months ended December 28, 2025, the group delivered higher earnings and revenue despite operating in a low-inflation environment that slightly pressured margins.

 

Key Financial Highlights

  • Headline earnings per share (HEPS):

    • 710.5 cents

    • Up from 659.8 cents (restated) a year earlier

  • EBITDA:

    • 12.4 billion rand ($767 million)

    • Up 6.7% year-on-year

  • Group sales:

    • 136.8 billion rand

    • Up 7.2% overall

  • South African supermarket sales:

    • Up 7.1%

  • Interim dividend:

    • 307 cents

    • Up 7.7%

 

Margin Pressure from Lower Inflation

While revenue expanded, gross margins declined slightly:

  • Gross margin: 23.8%

  • Down 10 basis points

The company attributed this to low selling price inflation, which has now shifted toward mild deflation in the current trading period.

For the first month of the second half:

  • South African supermarket sales rose 7.5%

  • Internal selling price inflation measured 0.7%

  • Compared with 3.1% inflation in January 2025

Shoprite expects low single-digit price inflation to continue through the remainder of the second half of 2026.

 

What’s Driving Performance

Shoprite’s results reflect:

  • Strong festive season consumer spending

  • Consistent traffic across food and non-food chains

  • Broad-based growth across formats

  • Continued operational discipline

The performance suggests resilient consumer demand despite a shifting inflation environment.

 

Retail Sector Implications

Lower inflation can benefit consumers but compress retailer margins if cost management does not keep pace. Shoprite’s results demonstrate the importance of:

  • Efficient supply chains

  • Volume-driven growth

  • Pricing discipline

  • Diversified retail formats

The company’s ability to maintain earnings growth amid declining inflation highlights operational strength in a competitive market.

Strong retail growth signals resilient consumer demand, especially during peak trading periods. MSMEs supplying large retailers or operating in fast-moving consumer goods should monitor inflation trends closely, as lower price growth may tighten margins but boost volume opportunities.
Nedbank Annual Profit Edges Up 2% as Impairment Charges Fall
Improved loan quality and reduced credit losses support earnings growth despite modest revenue expansion.

Nedbank posted a 2% rise in annual headline earnings to 17.2 billion rand, supported by an 18% reduction in impairment charges. While revenue growth was moderate, improved loan quality and lower credit losses strengthened profitability and position the bank for stable performance in 2026.

Nedbank Group reported a 2% rise in full-year headline earnings, driven primarily by a significant reduction in impairment charges, even as overall revenue growth remained moderate.

For the year ended December 31, headline earnings increased to 17.2 billion rand ($1.07 billion), up from 16.9 billion rand the previous year.

 

Key Financial Highlights

  • Headline earnings: 17.2 billion rand (up 2%)

  • Impairment charge: 6.6 billion rand (down 18%)

  • Net interest income: 42.9 billion rand (up 3%)

  • Non-interest revenue: Up 4%

  • Group credit loss ratio: Down 68 basis points

  • Return on equity (ROE): 15.4% (from 15.8%)

  • Final dividend: 11.04 rand per share

 

Improved Asset Quality Drives Gains

The reduction in impairment charges reflects:

  • Derisking strategies

  • Improved front-book loan origination quality

  • Lower bad loan levels

The decline in the group’s credit loss ratio signals stronger loan book performance and improved borrower stability.

 

Revenue Growth Remains Modest

While profit rose, revenue growth was relatively restrained:

  • Net interest income increased by 3%

  • Non-interest revenue grew 4%, supported by stronger commissions, fees, and insurance income

Despite a slight dip in ROE to 15.4%, the figure remains above the bank’s 2025 cost of equity target of 14.6%. CEO Jason Quinn indicated that ROE for 2026 is expected to exceed 15%.

 

What This Signals for the Banking Sector

Nedbank’s results suggest:

  • Stabilising credit conditions

  • Improved risk management in lending

  • Resilience amid a challenging macroeconomic environment

  • Continued shareholder returns through dividends

The improved asset quality indicates reduced stress within segments of the South African economy.

Healthier bank balance sheets can support stronger lending capacity and improved access to credit. As credit risks decline and profitability stabilises, MSMEs may benefit from more predictable financing conditions and sustained financial sector stability.
Nigeria Pharma Profits Hit Decade High on Tax Waivers and FX Stability
Executive Order on raw material imports and a steadier naira drive earnings rebound across listed drug makers.

Nigeria’s listed pharmaceutical firms delivered decade-high profits in 2025, driven by VAT and duty waivers on 875 raw materials and improved FX stability. While structural import dependence persists, the reform-driven rebound is accelerating local capacity expansion and strengthening sector profitability.

Nigeria’s listed pharmaceutical manufacturers have posted their strongest earnings in over a decade, reversing years of currency-driven losses and margin pressure. Combined full-year profits more than doubled from ₦6.5 billion in 2024 to ₦14.8 billion in 2025, supported by tax waivers on imported raw materials and improved foreign exchange (FX) stability.

The turnaround marks a structural shift in an industry long constrained by heavy import dependence and volatile input costs.

 

Earnings Surge Across Major Players

Key performance highlights include:

  • Neimeth International Pharmaceuticals

    • Swung from ₦885.3 million loss (2024) to ₦982.1 million profit (2025)

    • Gross profit rose to ₦3.3 billion

    • Cost of sales fell to ₦2.5 billion (from ₦4.0 billion)

    • Recorded FX gain of ₦48.1 million versus over ₦2 billion loss in 2024

  • Fidson Healthcare Plc

    • Profit after tax up 61% to ₦9.31 billion

    • Gross profit increased to ₦49.1 billion

  • May & Baker Nigeria Plc

    • Profit after tax rose to ₦4.45 billion

    • Gross profit climbed to ₦13.1 billion

Sector leaders directly attributed the rebound to policy relief and improved macroeconomic conditions.

 

Tax Waiver Reshapes Cost Structure

At the centre of the recovery is a Presidential Executive Order signed in June 2024 and implemented in March 2025, which:

  • Exempted 875 pharmaceutical raw materials from VAT and import duties

  • Removed tariffs and excise duties on specified machinery and inputs

  • Covered Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients (APIs), excipients, syringes, diagnostic kits, and essential medical supplies

For an industry that imports approximately 95% of its APIs, the policy immediately reduced production costs and improved margin resilience.

 

FX Stability Supports Bottom Line

Beyond fiscal relief, 2025 saw relative stability in the naira, which closed around ₦1,448 per dollar.

This reduced:

  • Translation losses

  • Volatility in import costs

  • FX-related balance sheet pressure

The combined effect allowed savings from duty exemptions to flow directly into profits, a dynamic largely absent during the 2024 currency crisis.

 

Investment and Capacity Expansion Accelerate

With the waiver scheduled through March 2027, companies are converting short-term relief into long-term expansion:

  • Fidson: ₦7.83 billion invested in WHO-compliant facility expansion

  • Neimeth: ₦20 billion capital raise approved for Amawbia plant completion

  • May & Baker: ₦6.37 billion invested in property, plant, and equipment

Regulatory reforms, including NAFDAC’s “5+5” localisation policy, are also encouraging importers to manufacture locally.

 

Structural Risks Remain

Despite record profits:

  • Nigeria still relies heavily on imported APIs

  • Public health capital releases lag budget allocations

  • Full pharmaceutical self-sufficiency remains a work in progress

Dependence on imports remains a strategic vulnerability, even as localisation efforts improve.

Policy consistency, FX stability, and targeted tax incentives can dramatically reshape industry profitability. MSMEs in manufacturing, healthcare supply chains, and import-dependent sectors should monitor policy reforms closely as fiscal relief and currency stability can unlock margin expansion and investment growth.
Uganda Central Bank to Begin Domestic Gold Buying Programme
Bank of Uganda targets at least 100kg between March and June 2026 as global gold demand surges.

Uganda’s central bank will begin purchasing domestically produced gold this month, targeting at least 100kg by mid-2026. The move aims to diversify reserves, strengthen economic buffers, and capitalise on rising global gold demand, while supporting local mining and refining industries.

Uganda’s central bank will launch its long-planned domestic gold purchasing programme this month, aiming to strengthen national reserves and shield the economy from global financial volatility. The move aligns Uganda with a growing list of countries increasing gold holdings amid record-high bullion prices and geopolitical uncertainty.

If implementation proceeds as scheduled, the Bank of Uganda expects to acquire at least 100 kilograms of gold between March and June 2026.

 

Why Uganda Is Buying Gold

The programme, first announced two years ago, is designed to:

  • Boost foreign exchange reserves

  • Diversify reserve assets away from traditional currencies

  • Reduce exposure to global financial market shocks

  • Strengthen macroeconomic stability

Executive Director for Research and Economic Analysis, Adam Mugume, confirmed that arrangements with contracted refineries are being finalised to meet required purity standards through fire assaying and refining.

 

Global Gold Rally Adds Momentum

Gold prices have reached record highs amid global tensions:

  • Spot gold rose more than 2% to $5,395.99 per ounce

  • Prices are being driven by geopolitical risks and safe-haven demand

  • Other African central banks, including Kenya and the Democratic Republic of Congo, are also increasing gold reserves

The recent surge reflects heightened political and economic uncertainty globally.

 

Uganda’s Expanding Gold Sector

Uganda’s gold industry has grown rapidly:

  • $5.8 billion in gold exports last year (up 76% year-on-year)

  • Artisanal miners still dominate production

  • First large-scale gold mine commissioned last year

    • Chinese-owned facility

    • Processes 5,000 metric tons of ore daily

    • Expected output: 1.2 tons of refined gold annually

The central bank plans to purchase gold from:

  • Artisanal miners

  • Medium-scale producers

  • Large-scale operators

Uganda established its first bullion processor, Africa Gold Refinery, in 2017, with additional refineries now operating and processing both local and regional supply.

 

Economic and Policy Implications

Domestic gold purchasing could:

  • Increase formalisation within the artisanal mining sector

  • Improve traceability and quality control

  • Strengthen Uganda’s external reserves

  • Support local value addition through refining

By sourcing domestically, the central bank also injects liquidity into the mining ecosystem while enhancing reserve resilience.

Gold reserve diversification strengthens currency stability and reduces exposure to global shocks. For MSMEs, especially those in mining, refining, logistics, and financial services, this programme could stimulate domestic value chains and improve macroeconomic predictability.
Dangote Expands Industrial Ambition into Steel, Power and Ports
Africa’s largest industrial conglomerate signals deeper push into heavy manufacturing and infrastructure.

Aliko Dangote has announced plans to expand into steel, electricity and port infrastructure, building on the success of his operational refinery. The strategy aims to accelerate Africa’s industrialisation, create jobs, and strengthen manufacturing competitiveness across the continent.

President of Dangote Group, Aliko Dangote, has unveiled plans to expand into steel production, electricity generation and port development, marking a new phase in the conglomerate’s industrial strategy. The move builds on the operational launch of the Dangote Petroleum Refinery & Petrochemicals and signals a broader push to accelerate Africa’s manufacturing capacity.

Dangote said the long-term objective is to deepen the continent’s industrial base and reduce reliance on imports by investing in critical sectors that enable large-scale production and trade.

 

From Refining to Heavy Industry

Dangote’s refinery, currently producing about 650,000 barrels of refined products daily, is expected to double output within three years as expansion progresses.

However, refining is only part of a larger industrial blueprint. According to Dangote:

  • Steel production will support infrastructure and construction growth

  • Electricity generation will address persistent power shortages

  • Port development will ease logistics bottlenecks and improve trade efficiency

Industry observers note that these sectors are foundational to sustained industrial growth.

 

Strategic Rationale

The expansion targets long-standing structural gaps in Nigeria and across Africa:

  • Steel: Critical for housing, rail, bridges, manufacturing and heavy industry

  • Power: Essential for productivity, competitiveness and industrial reliability

  • Ports: Vital for exports, imports and supply chain efficiency

Dangote referenced India’s Tata Group as a model of diversified industrial expansion, highlighting how multi-sector manufacturing can transform emerging economies.

 

Job Creation at the Core

Employment generation remains central to the strategy. With Nigeria projected to require 40–50 million new jobs by 2030, Dangote emphasised that large-scale industrial projects are necessary to absorb the country’s growing youth population.

Beyond refining, the group already operates in:

  • Cement

  • Sugar

  • Salt

  • Fertiliser

  • Petrochemicals

The planned expansion further strengthens its footprint across the industrial value chain.

 

Broader Economic Implications

If executed at scale, the initiative could:

  • Reduce import dependence

  • Retain more economic value within Africa

  • Improve infrastructure capacity

  • Strengthen manufacturing competitiveness

  • Attract long-term investment

The integration of steel, power and port infrastructure alongside refining reflects a vertically aligned industrial strategy.

Expanded steel production, improved electricity supply and stronger port infrastructure can lower operating costs and improve supply chain reliability for SMEs. A more industrialised ecosystem creates downstream opportunities in manufacturing, logistics, construction, and services across Africa.
Naira Gains 4.31% in February Despite CBN Intervention

The naira appreciated 4.31 percent in February despite late-month dollar purchases by the Central Bank aimed at moderating gains. Stronger oil prices and improved external reserves supported the rally, although short-term volatility and inflation risks remain.

Nigeria’s naira strengthened by 4.31 percent in February, appreciating across both the official and parallel markets despite late-month dollar purchases by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) aimed at moderating the rally.

According to a report by the Financial Market Dealers Association (FMDA), the currency firmed across the Nigerian Foreign Exchange Market (NFEM) and the parallel market, even as the apex bank absorbed foreign exchange liquidity to prevent what policymakers viewed as excessive appreciation.

The development reflects improving foreign exchange sentiment supported by stronger oil prices, rising external reserves, and relatively improved investor confidence in Nigeria’s reform trajectory.


The CBN’s intervention toward the end of February was aimed at preventing a sharp currency rally that could distort fixed-income investor positioning. Many investors entered local debt markets when the naira traded between ₦1,400 and ₦1,500 per dollar. A rapid strengthening could alter yield expectations and trigger portfolio rebalancing.


Despite the liquidity mop-up, the naira maintained upward momentum, buoyed by improving foreign exchange supply conditions. Crude oil prices climbed toward $80 per barrel amid escalating geopolitical tensions between the United States and Iran, strengthening Nigeria’s export earnings outlook.

Higher oil prices typically improve dollar inflows into the economy, bolstering reserves and easing pressure on the exchange rate. Analysts suggest that if oil prices continue to rise, the CBN may sustain measured interventions to prevent overheating while preserving exchange rate stability.

However, the oil-driven support comes with potential risks. A prolonged spike in crude prices could lift domestic fuel and import costs, feeding into inflation in an economy already contending with elevated price levels.

Nigeria’s gross external reserves rose to $49.69 billion as of February 27, 2026, strengthening the country’s external buffers and improving its capacity to manage exchange rate pressures.

The reserve build-up enhances the CBN’s ability to defend the currency against speculative attacks, smooth volatility, and maintain orderly market conditions.

Nevertheless, short-term fluctuations remain evident. The naira recently recorded daily losses at both the official and parallel markets, reflecting ongoing liquidity adjustments and global market sensitivities. These movements underscore that while structural sentiment has improved, the currency remains exposed to oil price swings and global financial conditions.


The naira’s February appreciation signals improving foreign exchange fundamentals and stronger reserve buffers. While oil prices have provided a temporary cushion, long-term currency stability will require structural economic diversification, sustained capital inflows, and prudent liquidity management.

Nigeria’s ability to balance currency strength with inflation control will remain central to maintaining investor confidence and supporting broader economic recovery.

Exchange rate stability is critical for Nigeria’s macroeconomic outlook. A firmer and more predictable naira reduces cost uncertainty for import-dependent sectors, including manufacturing, pharmaceuticals, technology, and consumer goods.
For SMEs and digital enterprises, improved currency stability enhances pricing confidence, supports cross-border transactions, and lowers foreign input costs.
Nigeria’s VAT Revenue Hits N2.28tn in Q3 2025, Up 10.66% — NBS

Nigeria’s VAT revenue reached N2.28 trillion in Q3 2025, rising 10.66 percent quarter-on-quarter and 28.10 percent year-on-year. Manufacturing, ICT, and mining were the largest contributors, while administrative services recorded the fastest growth.

Nigeria’s Value Added Tax (VAT) revenue rose to N2.28 trillion in the third quarter of 2025, representing a 10.66 percent increase from N2.06 trillion recorded in Q2 2025.

The figures were disclosed in the Sectorial Distribution of VAT Q3 2025 report published by the National Bureau of Statistics (NBS).

On a year-on-year basis, VAT collections increased by 28.10 percent compared to Q3 2024, underscoring sustained improvement in non-oil tax mobilisation amid ongoing fiscal reform efforts. The increase reflects both expanded economic activity in key sectors and improvements in tax compliance and enforcement.

The steady upward trajectory suggests that domestic consumption, corporate transactions, and import-related activities remained relatively resilient during the quarter, despite broader macroeconomic pressures including inflation and currency volatility.

Sectoral Drivers and Revenue Composition

A breakdown of collections shows that local VAT payments accounted for N1.12 trillion, representing the largest component of total receipts. Foreign VAT contributed N680.23 billion, while import VAT added N479.79 billion during the quarter.

The significant share of foreign VAT highlights the growing impact of cross-border digital services, offshore platforms, and multinational corporate activity within Nigeria’s tax net. Import VAT performance, meanwhile, reflects sustained trade flows and consumer demand for foreign goods, even amid exchange rate adjustments.

In terms of sectoral contributions, manufacturing led with 25.89 percent of total VAT revenue, followed by information and communication at 18.77 percent, and mining and quarrying at 14.85 percent. These three sectors collectively underscore the expanding role of industry, telecommunications, and extractive activities in strengthening Nigeria’s tax base.

The strong showing from information and communication aligns with rising broadband penetration, fintech growth, digital payments expansion, and increased enterprise connectivity — all of which contribute to taxable transactions within the formal economy.

Quarter-on-quarter growth was strongest in administrative and support services, which expanded by 89.28 percent. Arts, entertainment and recreation followed with 82.49 percent growth, while human health and social work activities grew by 32.40 percent. These gains suggest improving activity in service-oriented segments that had previously faced contractionary pressures.

Conversely, real estate activities recorded the steepest contraction at –51.33 percent, potentially reflecting slower property transactions, financing constraints, and elevated construction costs. Activities of households as employers declined by –36.22 percent, while other service activities fell by –20.30 percent, highlighting uneven recovery dynamics across sectors.


The sustained increase in VAT revenue signals gradual strengthening of Nigeria’s non-oil revenue base. With crude oil earnings remaining volatile and exposed to global price swings, VAT has become an increasingly critical pillar of fiscal sustainability.

A broader and more efficient VAT system reduces dependence on hydrocarbons and supports more predictable government revenue streams. This is particularly important as public spending demands remain elevated in infrastructure, healthcare, education, and social protection.

The strong performance of manufacturing and information and communication also reflects structural shifts toward services and value-added industrial activity. Rising digital adoption, telecom expansion, improved payment systems, and formalisation of business operations continue to widen the VAT net.

In addition, improved compliance mechanisms, digitised tax administration platforms, and tighter enforcement efforts may be contributing to higher collections, especially within foreign VAT and import VAT segments.

The Q3 2025 VAT figures reflect improving revenue resilience in Africa’s largest economy. While sectoral performance remains uneven, sustained growth in manufacturing, ICT, and industrial activity signals a gradual deepening of Nigeria’s non-oil fiscal capacity.

Maintaining this trajectory will require continued reforms in tax administration, expanded formalisation of economic activity, and policies that stimulate productive sectors. As Nigeria seeks to strengthen fiscal buffers and reduce oil dependency, VAT growth will remain central to building a more diversified and sustainable public finance framework.

Stronger VAT collections enhance fiscal space and reduce reliance on oil revenue. Improved non-oil tax mobilisation supports public spending on infrastructure, healthcare, and social services without increasing debt pressures.
For businesses, sectoral VAT trends also signal which industries are expanding, formalising, and contributing more significantly to the economy. A broader tax base strengthens macroeconomic stability and supports long-term economic diversification.
Banks Disburse N36.4tn to Trade Sector Amid Interest Rate Cuts

Banks disbursed N36.39 trillion to Nigeria’s Trade and General Commerce sector in the first nine months of 2025, slightly above 2024 levels. Lending accelerated in Q3, aligning with a modest policy rate cut to 26.5 percent.

Nigerian banks disbursed N36.39 trillion in credit to the Trade and General Commerce sector between January and September 2025, reflecting steady support for business activity despite elevated borrowing costs.

Figures from the Central Bank of Nigeria show the amount represents a 0.96 percent increase from N36.05 trillion recorded during the same period in 2024.

While the growth appears modest, it signals resilience in commercial lending at a time when tight monetary conditions and high interest rates have constrained private sector expansion.


Credit distribution gathered momentum in the third quarter of 2025, offsetting a relatively slow first half. August recorded the highest monthly disbursement at N5.06 trillion, followed by September at N4.85 trillion and July at N4.51 trillion. By contrast, January and February posted the lowest figures at N3.48 trillion and N3.54 trillion respectively.

The late-year acceleration suggests improving business confidence and stronger working capital demand, particularly in wholesale and retail trade. On average, banks extended N4.04 trillion monthly in 2025, slightly higher than the N4.01 trillion monthly average recorded in the corresponding period of 2024.

The rebound also coincided with the Monetary Policy Committee’s decision to cut the benchmark Monetary Policy Rate by 50 basis points to 26.5 percent — the first sign of easing after an extended tightening cycle that pushed rates to historic highs.

Credit Conditions and Transmission Challenges

Despite the policy rate adjustment, commercial lending rates remain elevated, in some cases approaching 30 percent. Business leaders argue that meaningful private sector expansion will depend on whether banks transmit the lower benchmark rate into reduced borrowing costs.

Chinyere Almona of the Lagos Chamber of Commerce and Industry described the rate cut as a potential bridge from reform to results, urging stronger credit flows to productive sectors and infrastructure.

Similarly, Segun Ajayi-Kadir of the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria noted that lower financing costs could enable firms to expand production capacity, hire more workers, and increase SME performance — provided inflation and exchange rate pressures remain contained.

However, Muda Yusuf of the Centre for the Promotion of Private Enterprise cautioned that Nigeria has historically experienced weak transmission between monetary policy adjustments and actual lending rates. Structural challenges including high operating costs, risk premiums, and infrastructure deficits continue to constrain affordable credit expansion.

Trade and General Commerce form the backbone of Nigeria’s domestic economic activity, linking manufacturers, importers, distributors, and retailers across value chains. Sustained credit allocation to this segment is therefore essential for maintaining supply chain stability, moderating price pressures, and supporting employment across micro, small, and medium-sized enterprises.

However, the relatively modest growth in credit also underscores the cautious stance of banks in a high-risk macro environment. Financial institutions continue to balance asset quality concerns with regulatory capital requirements and liquidity management obligations.

If policy rate reductions are gradually transmitted into lower borrowing costs, credit expansion could accelerate, amplifying multiplier effects across logistics, manufacturing, agriculture, and services. Conversely, weak transmission and persistent structural challenges could dampen the intended stimulus impact of monetary easing.


The N36.4 trillion credit allocation underscores the banking sector’s continued engagement with Nigeria’s commercial backbone, even under tight monetary conditions.

The key test now lies in whether recent rate cuts can meaningfully reduce financing costs and unlock stronger private sector investment. Coordinated reforms, infrastructure improvements, and macroeconomic stability will be essential to transform stable credit flows into accelerated economic growth.

Trade and general commerce are central to Nigeria’s domestic supply chains, SME ecosystem, and consumer markets. Sustained credit flow supports inventory financing, distribution networks, and retail expansion.

If lower benchmark rates translate into cheaper loans, businesses could scale operations, boost employment, and stimulate multiplier effects across the economy. However, weak policy transmission and structural bottlenecks may limit the full impact of monetary easing.
FG Approves ₦48bn to Upgrade Engineering Faculties in 12 Universities
TETFund-backed intervention targets practical training, innovation, and industry alignment across six geopolitical zones.

The Federal Government has approved ₦48 billion to modernise engineering faculties in 12 universities, reinforcing practical training and innovation capacity. Meanwhile, NELFUND has extended its loan application window after disbursing ₦183 billion to nearly one million students, strengthening access to higher education nationwide.

The Federal Government has approved ₦48 billion to upgrade Faculties of Engineering and Technology in 12 federal and state universities, aiming to strengthen practical training, applied research, and innovation capacity. The intervention, executed in partnership with TETFund, seeks to bridge the gap between classroom theory and industry-ready skills.

At the same time, the Nigerian Education Loan Fund (NELFUND) has extended its student loan application deadline, having already disbursed ₦183 billion to 983,706 students across 265 institutions.

 

Breakdown of the ₦48bn Engineering Upgrade

The funding will support:

  • Rehabilitation and equipping of engineering workshops

  • Construction of new workshops where required

  • Compliance monitoring and procurement transparency

  • Digital project tracking and periodic reporting

A Ministerial Monitoring, Evaluation and Implementation Committee has been inaugurated to oversee execution, chaired by the President of the Nigerian Society of Engineers.

The 12 beneficiary institutions span all six geopolitical zones and include federal and state-owned universities such as:

  • Federal University of Technology, Minna

  • Abubakar Tafawa Balewa University, Bauchi

  • Federal University of Technology, Owerri

  • Delta State University, Abraka

  • Federal University of Technology, Akure

  • Nigeria Army University, Biu

  • African Aviation University, Abuja

  • Others across Enugu, Jigawa, Akwa Ibom, Osun, and Sokoto

The intervention is separate from TETFund’s 2026 allocation of about ₦20 billion for additional engineering workshop upgrades.

 

Strategic Objectives

The Minister of Education stated that the programme aims to:

  • Transform universities into hubs of practical learning and innovation

  • Enhance graduate employability

  • Restore industry confidence in Nigerian engineering graduates

  • Improve Nigeria’s competitiveness in global technology markets

Officials emphasised that funding alone is insufficient without strong governance, transparency, and accountability.

 

NELFUND Extends Student Loan Portal

In a related development:

  • NELFUND has disbursed ₦183 billion to nearly one million students

  • The application deadline has been extended due to high demand

  • The extension accommodates new academic sessions and pending institutional submissions

  • Institutions yet to begin the 2025/2026 session must formally request additional time

The Fund reiterated its commitment to inclusive and equitable access to higher education financing.

 

Economic and Industrial Implications

Upgrading engineering infrastructure could:

  • Strengthen Nigeria’s technical workforce pipeline

  • Support industrialisation and manufacturing growth

  • Reduce skills mismatch in technology and engineering sectors

  • Stimulate research-driven entrepreneurship

Improved student financing access further strengthens long-term human capital development.

Stronger engineering faculties mean better-trained graduates, improved innovation capacity, and more industry-ready talent for SMEs. Combined with expanded student financing, this intervention supports long-term productivity, manufacturing growth, and technology-driven enterprise development across Nigeria.
Cooking Gas Prices Rise ₦100/kg in Nigeria as Middle East Tensions Shake Energy Markets
LPG depot prices climb amid global oil rally triggered by U.S.–Iran conflict.

Cooking gas prices in Nigeria have risen by about ₦100 per kilogram as global oil prices surge amid Middle East tensions. The increase highlights Nigeria’s vulnerability to international energy shocks, with higher LPG costs impacting households and small businesses alike.

Liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), widely used for cooking across Nigeria, has increased by an average of ₦100 per kilogram, following rising global energy prices linked to escalating tensions between the United States and Iran. Depot owners and distributors have adjusted prices upward as geopolitical disruptions in the Middle East ripple through international oil markets.

The increase reflects the strong connection between global crude oil prices and Nigeria’s domestic LPG market, where most supply is priced based on international parity.

 

New Depot Price Levels

Major LPG distributors have updated their depot prices:

  • Nipco Plc: ₦950 per kilogram

  • Navgas Limited: ₦900 per kilogram

  • Techno Oil Limited: ₦885 per kilogram

These prices represent a notable increase from the previous market average of around ₦800 per kilogram.

 

Global Oil Rally Driving Price Hikes

Energy prices have surged following military strikes involving the United States, Israel, and Iran. Market benchmarks have responded sharply:

  • Bonny Light crude: rose to about $80 per barrel, up from $70

  • Brent crude: climbed to $79.08 per barrel

  • Murban crude: increased to $81.05 per barrel

  • West Texas Intermediate (WTI): jumped to $72.24 per barrel

The price rally reflects concerns about potential disruptions to crude flows from the Persian Gulf — a critical energy supply corridor.

 

Why Nigerian LPG Prices React Quickly

Nigeria’s domestic LPG prices are highly sensitive to global energy markets due to structural factors:

  • Heavy reliance on imported refined petroleum products

  • Pricing benchmarks linked to international parity

  • Exposure to global shipping and logistics costs

As a result, geopolitical crises in key oil-producing regions often translate directly into higher domestic fuel and gas prices.

 

Consumer and Market Impact

Retailers say rising import costs leave little room to absorb price shocks. As international crude prices climb, distributors pass on the additional costs to local markets, affecting households and businesses alike.

The latest increase highlights the broader economic implications of global energy volatility for import-dependent economies.

Rising cooking gas prices increase operating costs for small restaurants, food vendors, caterers, and other MSMEs dependent on LPG. Energy price volatility can squeeze margins and force price adjustments, underscoring the importance of stable energy supply and diversified local production.
Oando Strengthens Role in Nigeria’s Indigenous Oil Industry
Local producers consolidate control of upstream production as collaboration among Nigerian operators deepens.

Oando is reinforcing its role in Nigeria’s indigenous oil push as local producers now account for more than half of national crude output. Through collaboration with the Independent Petroleum Producers Group, the company aims to strengthen domestic industry leadership, improve policy engagement, and sustain long-term production growth.

Nigeria’s indigenous oil producers are gaining stronger influence in the country’s upstream sector, with Oando Energy Resources reinforcing its position as a key driver of the shift toward locally led production.

During a strategic meeting in Lagos with the leadership of the Independent Petroleum Producers Group (IPPG), Oando reaffirmed its commitment to strengthening collaboration among local operators and advancing a domestically driven oil industry.

The engagement reflects a broader transformation in Nigeria’s energy sector, where indigenous companies now account for a significant share of crude production.

 

Indigenous Producers Take the Lead

According to IPPG leadership:

  • Member companies now account for more than 50% of Nigeria’s oil production

  • Local operators have steadily increased their control of upstream assets over the past decade

  • Indigenous producers are shaping policy discussions and industry strategy

A decade ago, international oil majors dominated Nigeria’s upstream sector. Today, domestic companies are increasingly steering production and operational decisions.

 

Strategic Collaboration Within the Sector

The meeting between Oando and IPPG leaders focused on:

  • Strengthening cooperation among indigenous oil companies

  • Coordinating industry advocacy with regulators and policymakers

  • Enhancing institutional capacity within the sector

IPPG Chairman Adegbite Falade highlighted Oando’s role in advancing the collective voice of local operators and supporting the group’s long-term industry initiatives.

 

Oando’s Strategic Approach

Oando’s leadership emphasised three key priorities for sustaining indigenous growth in the sector:

  • Disciplined operational execution

  • Strong corporate governance

  • Coordinated industry advocacy

These pillars are seen as essential for maintaining production stability amid evolving regulatory frameworks and volatile global energy markets.

 

Policy and Industry Implications

Industry leaders say the next phase of development will require stronger engagement with policymakers to ensure that regulatory frameworks reflect the realities of a domestically led oil industry.

As more upstream assets shift from international oil majors to local companies, scrutiny has intensified around the operational capacity of indigenous operators to manage complex oil infrastructure at scale.

Strengthening institutional frameworks within IPPG is therefore viewed as critical to maintaining production efficiency and investor confidence.

A stronger indigenous energy sector can create more opportunities for local suppliers, service companies, and contractors across the oil and gas value chain. As domestic operators expand, MSMEs may benefit from increased participation in procurement, logistics, engineering, and energy services.
Nigeria Introduces New Presumptive Tax Rules to Simplify Compliance for MSMEs
Turnover-based tax framework aims to expand the tax base while easing compliance for small businesses.

Nigeria has introduced new presumptive tax regulations to simplify tax compliance for MSMEs. The framework exempts businesses earning under ₦12 million annually and applies a flat 1% rate for others, while banning cash collections and roadside enforcement to improve transparency and formalisation.

Nigeria’s Federal Government has introduced new presumptive tax regulations designed to simplify tax compliance for Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs) while expanding the country’s tax base.
