The policy, announced by the Federal Ministry of Finance Nigeria, focuses on reducing administrative burdens and bringing more informal businesses into the formal economy.

The reforms are part of broader fiscal measures under the Renewed Hope Agenda aimed at improving tax inclusion without increasing tax rates.

 

Key Features of the New Presumptive Tax Rules

The updated framework introduces a simplified approach to taxation for small businesses:

  • Turnover-based assessments replace complex accounting requirements

  • Nano and small enterprises earning below ₦12 million annually are exempt

  • Businesses above the exemption threshold face a flat 1% presumptive tax rate

  • Nationwide ban on cash tax collections to improve transparency

  • Elimination of roadside tax enforcement and informal levies

The policy aims to remove arbitrary tax practices and provide predictable compliance rules for small businesses.

 

Government’s Fiscal Strategy

According to Finance Minister Wale Edun, the reforms focus on expanding participation rather than raising taxes.

Key objectives include:

  • Expanding Nigeria’s tax base

  • Simplifying tax administration

  • Encouraging formalisation of informal businesses

  • Improving transparency and compliance

The government estimates that Nigeria’s informal sector accounts for more than 80% of national employment, highlighting the need for inclusive fiscal policies.

 

Pathway to Formalisation

By simplifying taxation, the government hopes to encourage more businesses to formalise operations.

Formalisation could enable MSMEs to access:

  • Bank credit and financial services

  • Insurance products

  • Government support programmes

  • Business development opportunities

A clearer tax framework is expected to improve trust between businesses and revenue authorities.

 

Economic Context

Nigeria’s tax reforms are part of broader efforts to strengthen non-oil revenue while supporting business growth. Simplified tax systems are widely used globally to encourage compliance among small enterprises with limited accounting capacity.

Reducing enforcement abuses and informal collections may also improve the ease of doing business.

Simpler tax rules reduce compliance costs and uncertainty for small businesses. By linking taxation to turnover and eliminating arbitrary enforcement, the reforms can help MSMEs formalise operations, access financing, and scale sustainably within Nigeria’s growing economy.
Foreign Investment in Nigeria’s Manufacturing Sector Drops to 2.76% of Capital Inflows
Despite a surge in overall capital importation, foreign investment into manufacturing declined sharply in 2025.

Nigeria’s manufacturing sector attracted just $463.5 million in foreign investment in the first nine months of 2025, a 54% drop from 2024. While overall capital inflows surged, manufacturing’s share fell to 2.76%, reflecting investor concerns about infrastructure, policy stability, and production costs.

Foreign investment flowing into Nigeria’s manufacturing sector fell sharply in the first nine months of 2025, even as overall capital inflows into the country surged. According to data from the National Bureau of Statistics, manufacturing attracted $463.52 million between January and September 2025, representing a 54.11% decline from the $1.01 billion recorded during the same period in 2024.

The drop significantly reduced the sector’s share of total capital importation, highlighting growing investor caution toward Nigeria’s production environment.

 

Manufacturing’s Shrinking Share of Capital Inflows

Key figures from the NBS report show:

  • Manufacturing capital inflows (9M 2025): $463.52 million

  • Manufacturing inflows (9M 2024): $1.01 billion

  • Year-on-year decline: 54.11%

At the same time:

  • Total capital importation (9M 2025): $16.78 billion

  • Total capital importation (9M 2024): $7.23 billion

  • Overall growth: 131.96%

As a result, manufacturing accounted for just 2.76% of total capital inflows in 2025, down from 13.97% in the same period of 2024.

 

Quarterly Investment Trends

Capital inflows into manufacturing fluctuated throughout the year:

2025 performance

  • Q1: $129.92 million

  • Q2: $72.25 million

  • Q3: $261.35 million (rebound)

2024 comparison

  • Q1: $191.92 million

  • Q2: $624.71 million

  • Q3: $189.22 million

The data shows a sharp contraction in mid-2025 before a modest recovery in the third quarter.

 

Overall Capital Inflows Surge

Nigeria’s broader investment environment saw strong growth:

  • Q3 2025 capital inflows: $6.01 billion

  • Up 380.16% year-on-year from $1.25 billion in Q3 2024

  • Up 17.46% quarter-on-quarter compared to Q2 2025

Despite this surge, analysts note that much of the inflow has gone into financial assets rather than the real sector.

 

Why Investors Are Avoiding Manufacturing

Industry leaders say structural challenges continue to deter long-term manufacturing investment.

According to the Manufacturing Association of Nigeria, key constraints include:

  • Foreign exchange volatility

  • Persistent inflation and rising input costs

  • Unreliable electricity supply

  • Poor transport infrastructure and inefficient ports

  • Policy inconsistency and regulatory uncertainty

  • High interest rates

  • Security concerns

These conditions increase operational risk, making other sectors more attractive to foreign investors.

 

Implications for Industrial Growth

Lower foreign investment in manufacturing may have broader economic consequences:

  • Slower industrial expansion

  • Reduced job creation in the real sector

  • Limited progress toward economic diversification

  • Continued dependence on imports

Closing the investment gap will likely require structural reforms in infrastructure, energy, and policy stability.

Manufacturing investment drives supply chains, creates jobs, and supports SME growth in logistics, services, and distribution. Declining foreign investment in the sector could limit opportunities for MSMEs, highlighting the importance of improving infrastructure, financing access, and policy stability.
Kenya Launches Tender for New Airport to Ease Capacity Pressure at JKIA
Government plans major aviation infrastructure expansion as Nairobi’s main airport exceeds passenger capacity.

Kenya has launched a tender to build a new airport and expand facilities at Jomo Kenyatta International Airport in Nairobi, which currently handles more passengers than its designed capacity. The project aims to modernise aviation infrastructure and strengthen Kenya’s position as a key regional transport hub.

Kenya has issued a tender for the construction of a new airport adjacent to Jomo Kenyatta International Airport (JKIA) in Nairobi, as part of efforts to ease congestion and modernise the country’s aviation infrastructure. The announcement was made by Davis Chirchir, Kenya’s Minister for Roads and Transport.

The project aims to expand passenger handling capacity and upgrade facilities at one of East Africa’s busiest aviation hubs, which is currently operating beyond its design limits.

 

Why Kenya Is Expanding Airport Capacity

According to the Kenya Airports Authority, Jomo Kenyatta International Airport handled around 9 million passengers in 2025, exceeding its 7.5 million annual capacity.

To address the pressure, the government has launched a tender to:

  • Build a new passenger terminal capable of handling 10 million passengers annually

  • Develop additional taxiways and aviation infrastructure

  • Upgrade existing facilities at JKIA to improve operational efficiency

The expansion is expected to strengthen Nairobi’s role as a regional aviation and logistics hub.

 

Background: Cancelled $2 Billion Airport Deal

The new tender follows the cancellation of a previous airport modernisation plan.

In November 2024, President William Ruto ordered the termination of a proposed $2 billion agreement with the Adani Group.

The deal would have:

  • Added a second runway at JKIA

  • Upgraded passenger terminals

  • Granted the company a 30-year lease to operate the airport

The decision came after legal issues involving Gautam Adani in the United States.

Kenya’s government reiterated that JKIA remains a national asset and is not for sale, while confirming the need for urgent infrastructure upgrades.

 

Regional Aviation Competition

Kenya’s expansion comes amid growing aviation infrastructure investment across Africa.

For example, Ethiopia is currently constructing a major new airport expected to become Africa’s largest by 2030, intensifying competition among regional aviation hubs.

Expanding JKIA’s capacity could help Kenya maintain its strategic position as a gateway for trade, tourism, and logistics in East Africa.

Improved airport infrastructure strengthens regional trade, logistics, and tourism sectors where many MSMEs operate. Expanded capacity at Nairobi’s main airport could create new opportunities for small businesses in supply chains, exports, travel services, and cross-border commerce.
Alibaba Launches $1.5 Million Grant Programme for African Startups
Africa’s Business Heroes initiative aims to fund and mentor entrepreneurs building solutions to the continent’s biggest challenges.

Alibaba Philanthropy and the Jack Ma Foundation have launched the 2026 Africa’s Business Heroes competition, offering $1.5 million in grants to African entrepreneurs. The programme supports startups across sectors and provides mentorship, international exposure, and growth opportunities for businesses shaping Africa’s future.

The Alibaba Philanthropy and the Jack Ma Foundation have announced $1.5 million in grant funding for African entrepreneurs through the Africa’s Business Heroes (ABH) programme.

The initiative, now in its 8th edition, aims to support startups developing innovative solutions across sectors including agriculture, fintech, healthcare, logistics, manufacturing, and emerging technologies.

 

Programme Theme and Focus

The 2026 edition, themed “Defining Africa’s Future Today,” is designed to empower entrepreneurs building businesses that address Africa’s most pressing economic and social challenges.

The programme provides:

  • $1.5 million in grant funding to top-performing entrepreneurs

  • Mentorship and strategic guidance from global business leaders

  • International exposure to investors and ecosystem partners

  • Growth support to scale high-impact African businesses

The initiative aims to accelerate startups capable of driving job creation and long-term economic transformation.

 

Expanded Opportunities for 2026

This year’s programme will broaden its reach across the continent.

Key updates include:

  • Expansion of recognition to the Top 100 finalists

  • Increased engagement in 10 African markets

  • Greater focus on underrepresented countries, including Namibia, Tunisia, and Zambia

  • Access for Top 10 finalists to a fully sponsored learning programme in Hangzhou, China

The learning programme takes place at Alibaba’s global campus, offering founders exposure to international business ecosystems.

 

Who Can Apply

Applications are open to entrepreneurs who meet the following criteria:

  • Must be African citizens or legal residents

  • Lead a registered business operating for at least three years

  • Demonstrate measurable social or economic impact

  • Show strong potential for scaling innovative solutions

Since its launch, the ABH programme has attracted over 30,000 applications annually, making it one of Africa’s most competitive entrepreneurship platforms.

 

Supporting Africa’s Entrepreneurial Ecosystem

According to Zahra Boateng-Baitie, Managing Director for Africa at the programme, the initiative aims to highlight entrepreneurs solving real problems across the continent.

The programme focuses on empowering founders who are building businesses that generate jobs, drive innovation, and contribute to inclusive economic growth.

Grant programmes like Africa’s Business Heroes provide vital funding, mentorship, and visibility for African startups. For MSMEs, access to such platforms can accelerate growth, attract investors, and unlock opportunities to scale solutions that address Africa’s development challenges.
Martech Africa Conference Highlights Growth Strategies for Startups and SMEs
Industry leaders in Lagos explore how marketing technology can help businesses acquire customers, improve engagement, and scale sustainably.

The Martech Africa 3.0 Conference in Lagos brought together industry leaders and startups to explore how marketing technology can drive business growth. The event highlighted affordable digital tools, partnerships, and data-driven strategies to help African businesses improve customer acquisition and scale sustainably.

Marketing and technology stakeholders gathered in Lagos for the Martech Africa 3.0 Conference, where experts discussed practical and affordable strategies to help startups and small businesses improve customer acquisition, engagement, and retention.

The event, themed “Mastering the Growth Loop: Redefining Customer Acquisition, Engagement and Retention through Martech,” focused on how marketing technology can help businesses achieve sustainable growth in increasingly competitive markets.

 

Driving Growth Through Marketing Technology

The conference brought together founders, marketers, and industry experts to explore how businesses can leverage digital tools to scale efficiently.

Key focus areas included:

  • Customer acquisition strategies using marketing automation

  • Data-driven engagement and retention tactics

  • Cost-effective digital marketing for startups

  • Building sustainable growth loops through technology

Participants emphasized that startups must adopt smarter marketing strategies as digital competition intensifies.

 

Supporting Startups with Tools and Knowledge

The event was convened by Shina Memud, founder of YourNotify, who highlighted the importance of accessible marketing tools for growing businesses.

Memud noted that many startups struggle with the rising cost of social media advertising and require more affordable alternatives.

Future initiatives announced at the conference include:

  • Launch of monthly and quarterly podcasts focused on growth strategies

  • Partnerships with venture studios to help startups access capital

  • Collaboration with the National Institute of Marketing of Nigeria to bridge traditional marketing practices with emerging technologies

 

Industry Leaders Share Insights

Speakers from across Nigeria’s business ecosystem shared insights on how technology can transform marketing performance.

Key perspectives included:

  • Raymond Ibileke, Head of Marketing and Communications at AB Microfinance Bank Nigeria, described the conference as a platform that broadens marketers’ understanding of customer experience.

  • Priscilla Otukoya, Head of Growth and Revenue at Tuteria, highlighted how marketing technology enables companies to optimise growth strategies and strengthen collaboration across sectors.

Speakers also stressed the importance of partnerships between businesses, education providers, and government to address structural challenges affecting innovation.

 

A Shift Toward Smarter Marketing

The conference underscored the growing importance of marketing technology in Nigeria’s evolving business landscape. With rising digital advertising costs and changing consumer behaviour, businesses must adopt more creative and data-driven marketing approaches.

Experts noted that combining technology with strategic storytelling and customer insights can significantly improve conversion rates and long-term brand loyalty.

Marketing technology helps small businesses compete with larger firms by automating campaigns, improving customer insights, and lowering marketing costs. Platforms and knowledge-sharing initiatives like Martech Africa provide MSMEs with practical tools to scale faster and reach wider markets.
Nigeria Customs Seizes 14,375 Litres of Petrol Bound for Cameroon
Anti-smuggling operation in Cross River targets illegal fuel exports threatening Nigeria’s domestic supply.

The Nigeria Customs Service has seized 14,375 litres of petrol valued at ₦14 million that were allegedly being smuggled from Cross River State into Cameroon. The interception, carried out under Operation Whirlwind, aims to protect Nigeria’s domestic fuel supply and disrupt illegal petroleum export networks.

The Nigeria Customs Service has intercepted and auctioned 14,375 litres of Premium Motor Spirit (PMS) in Calabar, Cross River State, after the fuel was allegedly being diverted for illegal export to Cameroon.

Authorities say the seizure forms part of intensified anti-smuggling operations designed to protect Nigeria’s domestic fuel supply and prevent economic losses linked to cross-border petroleum diversion.

 

Details of the Seizure

According to the Customs Service, the confiscated petrol, valued at about ₦14 million was recovered during coordinated enforcement operations across key border areas.

Items intercepted included:

  • 235 jerry cans containing 25 litres each

  • 40 drums containing 200 litres each

  • Total fuel volume: 14,375 litres of PMS

The operation targeted smuggling routes around Ikang, Bakassi, Ikom, and Ikot-Idareka, which have been identified as major flashpoints for illicit fuel exports.

 

Operation Whirlwind: Tackling Fuel Smuggling

The seizure was carried out under Operation Whirlwind, a special initiative established by the Nigeria Customs Service to curb petroleum smuggling.

According to Abubakar Aliyu, National Coordinator of the operation, the initiative focuses on:

  • Strengthening border surveillance and intelligence gathering

  • Disrupting smuggling networks moving petroleum products across borders

  • Protecting Nigeria’s energy security and domestic fuel availability

Authorities say the intercepted fuel was diverted for illegal export in violation of regulations governing petroleum distribution in Nigeria.

 

Why Fuel Smuggling Matters

Officials warn that petrol smuggling has broader economic consequences.

Key impacts include:

  • Artificial fuel scarcity in local markets

  • Loss of government revenue

  • Distortion of domestic supply chains

  • Expansion of criminal networks along border corridors

To prevent the seized fuel from returning to illegal channels, the Customs Service auctioned the product publicly so it could re-enter the legitimate domestic market.

 

Strengthening Enforcement at Border Corridors

The Customs Service says enforcement operations will continue across major smuggling routes, with increased collaboration among security agencies and improved intelligence tracking.

Authorities also warned individuals involved in petroleum smuggling to desist, stating that enforcement measures will remain proactive and intelligence-driven.

Fuel supply disruptions affect transportation, logistics, and production costs for small businesses. Stronger enforcement against fuel smuggling helps stabilise domestic energy supply, which is critical for MSMEs that depend on predictable fuel availability for operations and distribution.
Nigeria’s Money Supply Declines Slightly to ₦123 Trillion Amid Liquidity Tightening
Central bank data shows reduced banking system liquidity despite a modest drop in interest rates.

Nigeria’s money supply declined slightly to ₦123.4 trillion in January 2026, reflecting lower liquidity in the banking system despite falling interest rates on central bank instruments. The data also shows reduced credit to government and the private sector, signalling cautious financial conditions that could affect access to business financing.

Nigeria’s broad money supply (M²) declined slightly to ₦123.4 trillion in January 2026, down from ₦124.4 trillion in December 2025, according to new data from the Central Bank of Nigeria.

The 0.8% month-on-month drop signals tighter liquidity in the financial system, even as interest rates on central bank liquidity instruments declined during the same period.

 

Key Monetary Indicators

Recent monetary data highlights a mixed movement across key liquidity indicators:

  • Money supply (M²): ₦123.4 trillion in January 2026

  • Previous month: ₦124.4 trillion in December 2025

  • Month-on-month decline: 0.8%

At the same time, interest rates on the central bank’s Open Market Operations (OMO) fell:

  • OMO rate (Jan 2026): 17.2%

  • OMO rate (Dec 2025): 19.4%

  • Monthly decline: 2.2 percentage points

This suggests a moderate easing in monetary policy tools despite overall lower liquidity.

 

Movement Across Money Supply Components

Breakdown of the money supply components shows varying trends.

Declining components

  • Quasi money: fell 1.2% to ₦81 trillion

  • Currency outside banks: dropped 3.7% to ₦5.2 trillion

Rising components

  • Demand deposits: increased 1.14% to ₦37.12 trillion

Quasi money typically includes highly liquid financial assets such as savings deposits and time deposits that can easily be converted into cash.

 

Credit to Government and Private Sector Falls

The reduction in money supply was also reflected in domestic credit levels.

Key figures include:

  • Credit to government: ₦34.18 trillion (down 0.11%)

  • Credit to private sector: ₦75.2 trillion (down 0.79%)

  • Net domestic credit: ₦109.4 trillion (down 0.59% from ₦110.06 trillion)

Lower credit growth may indicate cautious lending activity within the banking system.

 

Fiscal Context and Debt Considerations

Nigeria’s public debt stood at ₦153.3 trillion as of September 2025, according to the Debt Management Office Nigeria.

Economic analysts note that while borrowing remains a legitimate fiscal tool, the sustainability of debt depends on whether funds are channelled into productive investments that expand economic output.

Experts have also urged governments to strengthen revenue generation and prioritise investments that support economic growth.

Changes in money supply and credit conditions influence lending rates and access to finance. For MSMEs, tighter liquidity and slower credit growth may affect business loans and working capital availability, making efficient financial planning and alternative funding strategies increasingly important.
Nigeria Strengthens Mining Reforms to Boost Global Competitiveness
Government aligns policies with continental mining standards to attract investment and strengthen the solid minerals sector.

Nigeria is strengthening reforms in its mining sector to improve governance, attract investment, and align with continental standards under the African Mining Vision. Officials say the reforms aim to boost investor confidence and position Nigeria as a competitive mining destination in Africa.

Nigeria’s Federal Government is advancing reforms in the mining sector aimed at improving governance, attracting investment, and strengthening the country’s position within Africa’s and the global mining industry. Officials say the reforms are focused on improving policy frameworks, regulatory compliance, and environmental standards across the sector.

The initiative was highlighted during a sector validation workshop in Abuja organised by the Federal Ministry of Solid Minerals Development Nigeria, where stakeholders discussed strategies to reposition Nigeria’s solid minerals industry for sustainable growth.

 

Aligning Nigeria with Continental Mining Standards

The reforms are designed to align Nigeria with Africa-wide mining frameworks, including the African Mining Vision, which promotes responsible and sustainable mineral development across the continent.

According to Faruk Yusuf Yabo, the engagement serves as a strategic platform to integrate Nigeria more effectively into both African and global mining ecosystems.

The government aims to:

  • Strengthen policy and regulatory frameworks

  • Improve compliance with environmental standards

  • Increase transparency and sector governance

  • Attract long-term financing into mining projects

 

Building Investor Confidence

Authorities say better governance and reliable sector data are critical for attracting investors.

Officials noted that ongoing reforms focus on:

  • Establishing credible regulatory systems for mining operators

  • Improving access to financing mechanisms for the sector

  • Providing reliable data and market information for investors

These steps are expected to make Nigeria’s mining industry more competitive compared with other African mineral-producing countries.

 

Role of Institutions and Development Partners

Key institutions and development partners are supporting Nigeria’s mining reforms.

The Mining Cadastre Office Nigeria emphasised that regulatory reforms and improved licensing systems have strengthened governance in the sector.

Development partners including the African Union and the Agence Française de Développement have also supported policy alignment and sector development initiatives.

 

Why the Mining Sector Matters

Nigeria’s solid minerals sector is widely seen as a key pathway for economic diversification beyond oil.

With vast reserves of minerals such as lithium, gold, and limestone, the sector has the potential to:

  • Increase export revenue

  • Create industrial supply chains

  • Generate employment

  • Support manufacturing and infrastructure development

Strengthening regulatory frameworks and attracting private investment are considered essential steps toward unlocking this potential.

Mining sector reforms can create new opportunities for MSMEs in equipment supply, logistics, mineral processing, and support services. A stronger regulatory environment and increased investment can stimulate local supply chains and expand business opportunities for entrepreneurs across the mining ecosystem.
Lagos Partners World Bank to Strengthen Disaster Risk and Emergency Management
Collaboration focuses on early warning systems, infrastructure resilience, and improved disaster response in Africa’s largest coastal city.

The Lagos State Government has partnered with the World Bank to strengthen disaster risk management and emergency preparedness. The initiative will focus on early warning systems, infrastructure resilience, and coordinated emergency response to reduce the impact of flooding and climate risks in the state.

The Lagos State Government has partnered with the World Bank to strengthen disaster risk management and emergency preparedness across the state.

The collaboration aims to improve Lagos’ resilience to flooding and other environmental risks through enhanced infrastructure, early warning systems, and coordinated emergency response strategies.

 

Why Lagos Is Strengthening Risk Management

Lagos faces unique environmental challenges due to its geography. According to state officials:

  • The state shares approximately 486 kilometres of coastline with the Atlantic Ocean

  • About 25% of Lagos’ land area is covered by water

  • Rapid urbanisation increases exposure to flash flooding and coastal risks

These factors make disaster preparedness a key priority for the state government.

 

Focus Areas of the Partnership

The collaboration will bring together experts from government institutions and the World Bank to develop stronger disaster response systems.

Key initiatives include:

  • Developing early warning systems for flooding and climate risks

  • Strengthening emergency response and disaster preparedness plans

  • Conducting risk assessments across vulnerable communities

  • Implementing public awareness campaigns on disaster prevention

The initiative will also involve improving coordination among emergency agencies and local authorities.

 

Infrastructure and Environmental Measures

Officials say Lagos has already taken several steps to reduce environmental risks.

Current actions include:

  • Regular dredging and clearing of drainage channels and manholes

  • Strengthening flood control infrastructure across the state

  • Issuing early warnings based on rainfall forecasts from Nigerian Meteorological Agency

  • Introducing environmental policies such as banning single-use plastics

Authorities say these measures help reduce the likelihood and impact of flooding in vulnerable communities.

 

Technical and Investment Support

The World Bank will provide technical expertise and support to strengthen disaster management systems.

The initiative includes:

  • Assessing the needs of emergency agencies

  • Improving equipment, facilities, and data systems

  • Enhancing information sharing across response teams

The partnership is expected to strengthen Lagos’ capacity to manage climate-related risks in one of Africa’s fastest-growing megacities.

Natural disasters and flooding can disrupt supply chains, transportation, and business operations. Improved risk management systems help protect infrastructure and economic activity, enabling MSMEs to operate more reliably in urban markets like Lagos.
Impala Platinum Boosts Dividend After Five-Fold Profit Surge
Higher platinum group metal prices drive strong earnings recovery for the South African mining giant.

Impala Platinum reported a five-fold increase in half-year profit to 9.3 billion rand, driven by a strong rise in platinum prices. The improved earnings allowed the company to restore shareholder dividends, highlighting renewed strength in the global platinum market.

South African mining company Impala Platinum Holdings has reported a five-fold increase in half-year profit, supported by a strong rally in platinum group metal (PGM) prices. The improved earnings performance has enabled the company to reinstate shareholder payouts through an interim dividend.

For the six months ending December 31, the Johannesburg-based miner recorded headline earnings of 9.3 billion rand ($567 million), compared with 1.9 billion rand during the same period a year earlier.

 

Strong Earnings Growth

The surge in profitability reflects improved market conditions for platinum group metals.

Key financial highlights include:

  • Headline earnings: 9.3 billion rand

  • Previous period earnings: 1.9 billion rand

  • Profit increase: Approximately five-fold growth

The company’s stronger earnings position follows a period of price recovery across global precious metals markets.

 

Dividend Restored for Shareholders

With profits improving, the company announced a renewed dividend distribution.

Key dividend details:

  • Interim dividend: 4.10 rand per share

  • Total payout to shareholders: 3.7 billion rand

Impala Platinum did not declare an interim dividend last year, but restored the payout following improved financial performance and stronger commodity prices.

 

Platinum Prices Drive Mining Profits

The company’s results were largely driven by a sharp increase in platinum prices.

Market trends include:

  • Spot platinum prices more than doubled in 2025

  • Prices reached over $2,700 per ounce in January

  • Gains were driven by tight supply and rising investment demand

Higher commodity prices typically boost margins for mining companies and can support expansion and investment in the sector.

 

Implications for the Mining Sector

The recovery in platinum prices signals renewed momentum in the global precious metals market. For mining companies, stronger commodity prices can improve balance sheets, increase dividends, and enable further capital investment.

South Africa remains one of the world’s largest producers of platinum group metals, making price trends in the sector particularly significant for the country’s economy.

Mining growth can stimulate opportunities for MSMEs in equipment supply, logistics, engineering services, and mineral processing. Strong commodity markets often lead to increased investment across the mining value chain, benefiting smaller businesses connected to the sector.
Nigeria’s Cargo Throughput Surges 24.8% to 129.3 Million Metric Tons
Strong export activity and rising container traffic drive significant growth across Nigeria’s port system.

Nigeria’s cargo throughput rose 24.8% to 129.3 million metric tons in 2025, driven by stronger exports and a surge in container traffic. The growth, led by Lekki, Onne, and Apapa ports, highlights Nigeria’s expanding role as a regional logistics hub and reflects broader improvements in maritime trade activity.

The Nigerian Ports Authority (NPA) has reported a 24.8% increase in cargo throughput, reaching 129.3 million metric tons in 2025, compared with 103.6 million metric tons in 2024.

The surge highlights growing trade activity through Nigeria’s ports, supported by increased exports, higher container volumes, and expanded maritime operations.

 

Exports Drive Trade Expansion

According to the NPA’s 2025 Operational Performance Report, export activity played a key role in boosting cargo movement.

Breakdown of total throughput shows:

  • Imports: 59.2% of cargo traffic

  • Exports: 39% of cargo traffic

  • Transshipment: 1.8%

Analysts say the rising share of exports reflects ongoing efforts by the Federal Government to diversify the economy and expand non-oil trade.

 

Container Traffic Shows Strong Growth

Containerised cargo, a key indicator of trade activity, recorded notable gains during the year.

Key figures include:

  • Total container traffic: Over 2.1 million Twenty-foot Equivalent Units (TEUs)

  • Growth rate: 25.7% increase year-on-year

  • Import containers: Up 32.8%

  • Export containers: Up 3.1%

  • Transshipment containers: Up 205.8%

The sharp rise in transshipment activity indicates Nigeria’s growing role as a regional logistics hub for West and Central Africa.

 

Leading Ports and Vessel Traffic

Several major ports drove cargo handling growth across the country.

Top contributors include:

  • Lekki Deep Sea Port: 40.6% of total cargo throughput

  • Onne Port: 19.1%

  • Apapa Port: 16.7%

Lekki Port also handled the largest vessels, recording an average Gross Registered Tonnage (GRT) of 55,712, slightly higher than Onne’s 53,022 GRT.

Overall ship traffic increased significantly:

  • Total ship calls: 4,477 vessels

  • Annual growth: Nearly 12%

 

Cargo Composition Across Ports

Different cargo categories contributed to total throughput.

Key distribution includes:

  • Liquid bulk cargo: 54.7% of total volumes

  • Containerised cargo: 24%

  • Remaining volumes consisted of dry bulk and general cargo

NPA officials say ongoing port modernisation projects and the planned rollout of a National Single Window system are expected to improve efficiency and reduce trade bottlenecks.

Rising cargo throughput signals stronger trade activity and improved logistics infrastructure. Efficient ports reduce shipping costs and delivery delays, enabling MSMEs to import raw materials, export products, and participate more effectively in regional and global supply chains.
FirstRand Reports 11% Earnings Growth on Strong Revenue and Improved Credit Performance
South Africa’s second-largest bank by assets posts solid half-year results driven by retail banking growth and higher fee income.

South Africa’s FirstRand reported 11% growth in half-year earnings, reaching 23.2 billion rand, driven by strong retail banking revenue and rising fee income. The bank also increased its dividend payout, reflecting improved financial performance and stable credit conditions across its operations.

South African banking group FirstRand Limited has reported an 11% increase in adjusted half-year earnings, supported by strong revenue growth and improved credit performance across its operations.

For the six months ending December 31, the lender posted normalised earnings of 23.2 billion rand ($1.41 billion), reflecting solid performance across its retail and corporate banking segments.

 

Key Financial Performance

FirstRand’s financial results show growth across major revenue streams.

Key highlights include:

  • Normalised earnings: 23.2 billion rand

  • Year-on-year growth: 11%

  • Net interest income: 48 billion rand (up 8%)

  • Non-interest revenue: 31.9 billion rand (up 12%)

Net interest income represents the difference between interest earned on loans and interest paid on customer deposits.

 

Drivers of Revenue Growth

The bank attributed its earnings growth to strong performance across several business segments.

Key contributors included:

  • Increased fee and commission income in the retail banking division

  • Recovery in global markets operations within the corporate banking arm

  • Growth in transaction and financial services activity

Analysts had forecast earnings growth of around 7.4%–7.9%, meaning the bank’s results exceeded expectations.

 

Credit Performance Across Markets

FirstRand operates in South Africa, other sub-Saharan African markets, and the United Kingdom.

Credit conditions improved in some markets but remained challenging in others.

Key credit indicators include:

  • Credit loss ratio: 86 basis points (up slightly from 84 basis points)

  • Improved credit performance in South Africa’s retail banking sector

  • Higher credit charges in broader African markets, particularly due to macroeconomic pressures in Botswana

The credit loss ratio measures the level of bad loans relative to total lending.

 

Dividend Increase for Shareholders

Following the strong earnings performance, the bank announced an increased dividend payout.

Dividend details:

  • Interim dividend: 259 cents per share

  • Dividend increase: 18%

The payout reflects the bank’s confidence in its earnings outlook and balance sheet strength.

Stronger bank earnings often signal improved lending capacity and financial stability within the banking sector. For MSMEs, this can translate into better access to credit, financing solutions, and banking services needed to support business growth across African markets.
NAICOM and BPP Sign Partnership to Strengthen Insurance Compliance in Public Procurement
New agreement aims to improve transparency, enforce insurance standards, and strengthen Nigeria’s public procurement system.

South Africa’s FirstRand reported 11% growth in half-year earnings, reaching 23.2 billion rand, driven by strong retail banking revenue and rising fee income. The bank also increased its dividend payout, reflecting improved financial performance and stable credit conditions across its operations

The National Insurance Commission (NAICOM) has signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with the Bureau of Public Procurement (BPP) to strengthen insurance compliance within Nigeria’s public procurement system.

The partnership aims to align insurance practices with procurement regulations, promote transparency in government contracting, and enhance risk management for public sector projects.

 

Strengthening Insurance Compliance in Public Contracts

Under the agreement, both agencies will collaborate to standardise insurance requirements across government procurement processes.

Key objectives of the partnership include:

  • Standardising insurance requirements for public procurement contracts

  • Ensuring the proper use of insurance bonds in government projects

  • Strengthening risk management and asset protection in public infrastructure

  • Improving transparency and accountability in procurement systems

The agreement also seeks to promote the use of insurance instruments to protect government investments and contractors.

 

Joint Monitoring and Implementation Framework

To support implementation, the agencies will establish joint oversight mechanisms.

Key measures include:

  • Creation of a joint technical working group

  • Periodic reviews and retreats to evaluate implementation progress

  • Development of sector-specific procurement templates and guidelines

  • Monitoring systems to verify insurance coverage for public procurement projects

Authorities say these measures will help ensure consistent compliance with procurement and insurance standards.

 

Supporting Nigeria’s Insurance Sector Reforms

The partnership aligns with broader reforms in Nigeria’s insurance industry following the Nigerian Insurance Industry Reform Act 2025.

The reforms include:

  • Industry recapitalisation initiatives

  • Regulatory modernisation

  • Improved policyholder protection

  • Increased insurance penetration across the economy

Officials say integrating insurance more deeply into public procurement will strengthen market confidence and support industry growth.

 

Digital Procurement and Local Industry Development

The Bureau of Public Procurement has also introduced digital processes to improve procurement oversight.

According to Adebowale Adedokun, the agency now operates a fully digital submission model designed to:

  • Speed up procurement approvals

  • Reduce corruption risks

  • Improve monitoring of contractors and service providers

The collaboration also supports government policies aimed at encouraging local industry participation, including procurement opportunities for women, youth, startups, and persons with disabilities.

Clear procurement rules and insurance requirements create more transparent government contracting opportunities. For MSMEs, stronger compliance systems can improve access to public contracts, reduce financial risks, and increase participation in government-funded projects.
ECOWAS Parliament Calls for Stronger Regional Trade Participation
West African businesses urged to leverage regional integration frameworks to unlock cross-border opportunities.

The ECOWAS Parliament has called on entrepreneurs and businesses to take greater advantage of regional trade frameworks designed to boost cross-border commerce. Through new partnerships, trade forums, and innovation initiatives, the organisation aims to deepen economic integration and unlock new growth opportunities for West African businesses.

The ECOWAS Parliament has called for stronger participation in regional trade across West Africa, urging entrepreneurs, traders, and businesses to fully leverage existing economic integration frameworks.

The call was made during a press briefing in Abuja announcing private sector activities to mark the Parliament’s 25th anniversary, with officials emphasising that cross-border trade and innovation must translate into tangible economic benefits for citizens across the region.

 

Regional Integration Frameworks Offer Untapped Opportunities

Officials highlighted several regional initiatives designed to promote trade and economic collaboration within the Economic Community of West African States.

Key frameworks include:

  • ECOWAS Trade Liberalisation Scheme, which allows duty-free movement of approved goods within member states

  • The ECOWAS Free Movement Protocol, enabling citizens to travel, work, and establish businesses across borders

  • Regional initiatives promoting innovation, entrepreneurship, and private sector collaboration

Despite these policies, awareness remains low among many traders and small businesses.

According to Kabeer Garba, greater participation by entrepreneurs is essential for regional integration to deliver meaningful economic benefits.

 

Private Sector Partnerships to Drive Regional Growth

Private sector leaders are partnering with ECOWAS institutions to convert policy frameworks into real economic opportunities.

According to Victoria Akai, the anniversary programme will focus on:

  • Strengthening regional trade and entrepreneurship

  • Promoting innovation-led economic growth

  • Connecting businesses, policymakers, and investors across West Africa

The initiative aims to encourage businesses to explore new markets within the ECOWAS region.

 

Regional Trade Events and Collaboration Platforms

The anniversary programme will include several activities designed to foster cross-border commerce and collaboration.

Planned initiatives include:

  • The ECOWAS Smart Challenge to support innovation-driven enterprises

  • Regional trade forums and policy dialogues

  • Business-to-business networking events

Events will take place across major cities, including:

Abidjan, Accra, Lomé, Freetown, Abuja, Lagos

These forums aim to strengthen collaboration among policymakers, entrepreneurs, financial institutions, and innovators.

 

Financial Institutions Expanding Trade Support

Banks and financial institutions are also increasing their support for regional trade.

According to Biodun Ariyo, financial institutions are partnering with regional bodies to:

  • Expand trade financing for cross-border transactions

  • Support businesses operating across multiple ECOWAS markets

  • Facilitate networking platforms connecting regional enterprises

Improved financial access is expected to play a key role in scaling regional trade.

Regional trade integration can significantly expand market access for African MSMEs. By leveraging ECOWAS trade frameworks, small businesses can reach over 400 million consumers across West Africa, diversify revenue streams, and scale beyond domestic markets.
South African Manganese Producers Plan Bid for New Export Terminal
Mining consortium aims to expand export capacity and improve logistics through a new port project at Ngqura.

A consortium of South African manganese producers plans to bid for a new export terminal at Ngqura Port in partnership with Transnet. The project could add 16 million tons of export capacity annually, helping reduce logistics bottlenecks and strengthen South Africa’s position in the global manganese market.

A consortium of South African manganese producers is preparing to bid for the construction and operation of a new manganese export terminal at Ngqura Port in the Eastern Cape. The project is expected to significantly expand export capacity and strengthen logistics infrastructure for one of the country’s key mineral industries.

The initiative is being driven by members of the Manganese Producers Consortium, which includes African Rainbow Minerals through its subsidiary Assmang.

 

New Terminal Could Add 16 Million Tons in Export Capacity

The proposed project, known as the Ngqura Manganese Ore Export Terminal, aims to expand South Africa’s mineral export capacity and ease existing logistics constraints.

Key details of the project include:

  • Planned export capacity increase of 16 million metric tons annually

  • Development through a public–private partnership with Transnet

  • Private sector involvement in design, construction, and operations of the terminal

  • Expected launch of the official bid process around April

The terminal will be located at Port of Ngqura, one of South Africa’s key deep-water ports.

 

Infrastructure Upgrade to Support Mining Exports

South Africa is the world’s leading producer of manganese and holds approximately 70% of global reserves. The mineral is a critical input in steel manufacturing, making reliable export logistics essential for the sector.

Recent export performance highlights the sector’s growth:

  • 26.2 million tons exported in 2025, a record level

  • Previous peak of 22.3 million tons in 2024

However, declining rail capacity and port bottlenecks have constrained export volumes in recent years. Opening parts of the logistics network to private sector investment is intended to restore efficiency and unlock growth in mineral exports.

 

Market Pressures Affect Manganese Producers

Despite strong export volumes, mining companies are facing price pressures in the global manganese market.

According to African Rainbow Minerals:

  • Headline earnings from manganese operations fell by 76%

  • The decline was driven by a 22% drop in the average dollar price for high-grade ore

However, the company’s overall performance remained stable due to gains in other commodities.

Key financial highlights:

  • Overall profit rose 10% to 1.67 billion rand in the six months to December 31

  • Higher platinum group metal prices offset weaker earnings from manganese, iron ore, and coal operations.

Improved port and rail infrastructure strengthens Africa’s export competitiveness and opens opportunities for logistics providers, equipment suppliers, engineering firms, and service SMEs that support the mining value chain.
Nigeria Expands Fisheries Sector to Boost Jobs and Regional Trade
New AfCFTA-backed programme aims to empower women and youth while strengthening Nigeria’s fisheries value chain.

Nigeria is working with international partners to expand its fisheries sector through a new AfCFTA-backed programme aimed at boosting trade, creating jobs, and strengthening food security. The initiative focuses on empowering women and youth while unlocking new regional market opportunities for the country’s growing aquaculture industry.

Nigeria is intensifying efforts to grow its fisheries and aquaculture sector through a new regional programme designed to boost trade, expand fish production, and create jobs for women and young entrepreneurs.

The initiative was highlighted during the Nigeria National Fisheries Stakeholders Forum in Lagos, where government officials and development partners discussed strategies to strengthen the country’s fisheries value chain and leverage opportunities under the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA).

 

Programme Targets Jobs and Market Expansion

The initiative is part of the Women and Youth Economic Empowerment in the Fisheries Sector project under the Inclusive Market Access Programme, a five-year initiative running from 2024 to 2029.

The programme is being implemented by the AfCFTA Secretariat in partnership with:

  • Mastercard Foundation

  • WorldFish

  • TradeMark Africa

The initiative focuses on improving market access, increasing production capacity, and unlocking economic opportunities for underrepresented groups in the fisheries industry.

 

Removing Barriers for Women and Youth

The Federal Government says the programme will address key challenges limiting participation in the fisheries sector.

Key focus areas include:

  • Improving access to financing for small-scale producers

  • Reducing trade and regulatory barriers

  • Strengthening compliance with regional market standards

  • Expanding cross-border trade opportunities

Officials say the programme is designed to make it easier for women and youth entrepreneurs to enter and grow within the fisheries value chain.

 

AfCFTA Opens New Export Opportunities

The AfCFTA agreement offers Nigerian fish producers access to a much larger continental market.

Key benefits include:

  • Expanded access to African consumer markets

  • Opportunities to scale production and exports

  • Reduced tariffs and trade barriers across member states

Stakeholders say the continental market could encourage greater investment in aquaculture and fisheries processing.

 

Reducing Fish Imports and Strengthening Local Production

Nigeria remains one of Africa’s largest fish consumers but still imports significant volumes of fish.

The programme aims to:

  • Increase domestic fish production

  • Strengthen the fisheries value chain from production to trade

  • Support youth-led aquaculture enterprises

According to WorldFish, building a more resilient fisheries industry could help reduce reliance on imports while improving food security.

 

Pan-African Programme Targets Job Creation

The Inclusive Market Access Programme is currently being implemented across several African countries, including:

  • Kenya

  • Uganda

  • Tanzania

  • Democratic Republic of the Congo

  • Zambia

  • Comoros

  • Cabo Verde

Across participating countries, the programme aims to create over 242,000 decent jobs, particularly for young people and women.

Expanding fisheries and aquaculture offers new opportunities for MSMEs in farming, processing, cold-chain logistics, and regional trade. With AfCFTA opening larger markets across Africa, small businesses in the fisheries value chain can scale production, access new buyers, and build export-driven growth.
Sunbeth Seeks ₦100bn Funding to Expand Africa’s Cocoa Trade Operations
Commodity trader plans major capital raise as West Africa’s cocoa market adjusts to changing global prices.

Sunbeth Global Concepts is seeking ₦100 billion through a commercial paper issuance as part of a ₦200 billion funding programme to expand its role in Africa’s cocoa trade. The move comes as West Africa’s cocoa market adjusts to changing global price dynamics, opening new opportunities across the commodity value chain.

Agro-commodities trading firm Sunbeth Global Concepts is seeking to raise ₦100 billion through a commercial paper issuance as part of a broader ₦200 billion funding programme aimed at expanding its footprint across Africa’s cocoa value chain.

The capital raise comes as the West African cocoa market adjusts to shifting price dynamics following reduced farmgate prices in key producing countries such as Côte d’Ivoire and Ghana, which together account for the majority of global cocoa production.

 

Cocoa Market Adjusts to Changing Global Prices

Both Côte d’Ivoire and Ghana recently reduced the prices paid to farmers in response to softer global cocoa prices. The adjustments reflect changing demand patterns and broader volatility in commodity markets.

Key market developments include:

  • Lower farmgate prices for cocoa farmers across West Africa

  • A shift in global cocoa supply and demand dynamics

  • Increased competition among commodity trading firms

Despite these challenges, industry players believe the region’s cocoa value chain continues to offer strong long-term opportunities.

 

Sunbeth Expands Its Position in Cocoa Trading

Sunbeth has been actively trading cocoa across the region and is now looking to scale its operations.

Key highlights of the company’s operations include:

  • Over 200,000 metric tons of cocoa exported since 2017

  • Expansion plans across commodity sourcing, trading, and export logistics

  • A ₦200 billion capital programme designed to support growth

According to Chuma Ajene, the funding will help the company strengthen its role in Africa’s evolving cocoa trade landscape.

 

Growing Opportunities Across the Cocoa Value Chain

West Africa remains the world’s leading cocoa-producing region, creating opportunities beyond farming alone.

Key areas of opportunity include:

  • Commodity trading and export logistics

  • Cocoa processing and value addition

  • Supply chain financing and storage infrastructure

  • Technology-driven commodity trading platforms

Industry experts say stronger financing options will allow trading companies to capture more value across the cocoa ecosystem.

Expanding investment in cocoa trading and processing creates new opportunities for African MSMEs involved in farming, logistics, warehousing, and commodity services. Improved financing across the supply chain can also strengthen market access for smallholder farmers and local agribusinesses.
Nigeria’s Textile Sector Output Declines as GDP Contribution Falls 3.6% in Two Years
Persistent structural challenges continue to weigh on Nigeria’s once-thriving cotton, textile, and garment industry.

Nigeria’s textile sector has continued to decline, with its GDP contribution falling 3.6% between 2023 and 2025. While structural challenges remain, new government policies and industry reforms aim to revitalise the sector and restore its role in Nigeria’s industrial development.

Nigeria’s cotton, textile, and garment industry continues to face significant economic pressure, with its contribution to the country’s Gross Domestic Product (GDP) declining steadily over the past two years.

According to data from the National Bureau of Statistics, the sector’s GDP contribution dropped to ₦4.384 trillion in 2025, reflecting a persistent downturn driven by structural and operational challenges.

 

Sector Output Shows Continuous Decline

Recent statistics indicate a gradual contraction in the industry’s economic output.

Key figures include:

  • ₦4.548 trillion contribution in 2023

  • ₦4.476 trillion contribution in 2024

  • ₦4.384 trillion contribution in 2025

This represents a 3.6% decline in sector output between 2023 and 2025, equivalent to roughly ₦164 billion in lost value over the two-year period.

Industry experts say the trend reflects broader competitiveness challenges facing domestic manufacturers.

 

Structural Challenges Continue to Pressure Manufacturers

Nigeria’s textile sector, once a major industrial hub with over 180 textile mills operating in the 1980s, has experienced a dramatic contraction.

Today, fewer than 20 mills remain operational.

Key factors driving the decline include:

  • High production and energy costs

  • Unstable electricity supply

  • Foreign exchange constraints affecting raw material imports

  • Influx of cheaper imported textiles competing with local production

These challenges have forced many manufacturers to scale down operations or run far below installed capacity.

 

Government Moves to Revive the Industry

The Federal Government has introduced policy measures aimed at revitalising the sector and strengthening domestic manufacturing.

Key initiatives include:

  • Implementation of the Nigeria Industrial Policy 2025

  • Establishment of a Cotton, Textile and Garment Development Board

  • Enforcement of Executive Order 003, which encourages government agencies to patronise locally produced textiles

Industry stakeholders say these measures could help restore competitiveness and stimulate investment in the sector.

 

Industry Stakeholders Support Reform Efforts

The National Union of Textile Garment and Tailoring Workers of Nigeria has welcomed the proposed reforms.

According to union leadership, the creation of a dedicated development board signals a renewed commitment by government to address longstanding structural challenges affecting the industry.

Stakeholders believe sustained policy implementation and investment could gradually restore Nigeria’s textile sector as a major contributor to industrial growth and employment.

A stronger textile industry can unlock opportunities for MSMEs across the value chain, from cotton farming and garment manufacturing to retail and fashion design. Reviving local production also reduces import dependence and strengthens Africa’s broader manufacturing ecosystem.
African Business Confidence Rises as Trade Conditions Improve
New Standard Bank report highlights stronger infrastructure, growing regional trade momentum, and improving market outlook.

The latest Standard Bank Africa Trade Barometer indicates rising business confidence and improving trade conditions across the continent. Stronger infrastructure and the growing impact of AfCFTA are creating new opportunities for businesses to expand cross-border trade within Africa.

Business confidence across Africa is strengthening as trade conditions improve, according to the latest Standard Bank Africa Trade Barometer.

The report shows that companies operating across ten major African markets are experiencing better infrastructure conditions, growing optimism about trade prospects, and stronger momentum around regional integration driven by the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA).

 

Improving Trade Environment Across African Markets

Businesses surveyed in the report reported positive shifts in several trade indicators.

Key improvements include:

  • Rising business confidence among firms across key African markets

  • Better logistics and infrastructure performance supporting trade activity

  • Increased participation in regional trade initiatives

The findings suggest gradual recovery and strengthening trade flows across the continent.

 

Regional Integration Driving New Opportunities

A major factor supporting improved business sentiment is the implementation of the African Continental Free Trade Area.

The agreement aims to:

  • Create a single African market for goods and services

  • Reduce tariffs and non-tariff barriers across member states

  • Facilitate cross-border investment and trade

Businesses increasingly view AfCFTA as a catalyst for expanding market access across Africa’s rapidly growing consumer base.

 

Infrastructure Improvements Supporting Trade Growth

Companies also reported improvements in infrastructure and logistics systems in several African economies.

Areas showing progress include:

  • Transport and logistics networks supporting cross-border commerce

  • Port and trade facilitation infrastructure

  • Increasing digitalisation of trade processes

Improved infrastructure is helping reduce delays and costs associated with moving goods across borders.

Improved trade conditions and regional integration expand market opportunities for African MSMEs. As infrastructure and trade policies improve, small businesses can access larger regional markets, scale operations, and participate more actively in continental supply chains.
FG Tax Reforms to Eliminate Multiple Levies on Businesses, Shettima Says

Vice President Kashim Shettima says the Federal Government’s proposed tax reforms will eliminate multiple levies on businesses and protect low income earners from excessive charges. The reforms are designed to simplify Nigeria’s tax system, improve compliance, and support economic growth while reducing the financial burden faced by small businesses across the country.

Vice President Kashim Shettima has reaffirmed the Federal Government’s commitment to implementing tax reforms aimed at eliminating multiple levies on businesses and easing the financial pressure on small enterprises and low income earners.

Speaking on behalf of President Bola Ahmed Tinubu at an interfaith breaking of fast organised for Ramadan and Lent at the State House in Abuja, the Vice President said the reforms were carefully designed to simplify Nigeria’s tax system and improve the economic welfare of citizens.

According to Shettima, the government remains focused on ensuring that the new tax framework supports economic growth while protecting vulnerable groups from excessive taxation.

He dismissed criticism suggesting that the reforms would worsen economic hardship for Nigerians, noting that the government would intensify public engagement to ensure citizens understand the purpose and expected benefits of the policy changes.

“The same people who are shouting hoarse that the tax reform is meant to pulverise further and pauperise the poor are far from the truth,” Shettima said.

“We have to educate them. We have to mount the pulpits and take our government to the Nigerian people and tell them the truth.”


The Vice President urged government officials and public sector leaders to actively communicate the benefits of the administration’s economic reforms and counter misinformation.

The event was attended by members of the Federal Executive Council, the Governor of the Central Bank of Nigeria, special advisers, senior special assistants, and heads of government agencies and parastatals.

Shettima stressed that the administration remained committed to policies that would improve economic stability while reducing poverty.

Highlighting the progress made under the current administration, he pointed to improvements in Nigeria’s foreign exchange reserves, the streamlining of the exchange rate system, and the removal of fuel subsidy as key reforms aimed at stabilising the economy and restoring investor confidence.

He also commended President Tinubu for taking difficult economic decisions that previous administrations had avoided, noting that such reforms were necessary to address long standing structural challenges within the Nigerian economy.

According to the Vice President, the removal of the fuel subsidy became unavoidable because the policy had continued to place a heavy strain on national finances.

“Three years down the road, the economy has bounced back,” he said.

He added that government officials must continue to engage citizens with factual information about ongoing reforms and demonstrate how these policies are designed to support long term economic stability.

The Federal Government’s tax reform agenda is part of broader economic changes aimed at stabilising the economy and improving the business environment in Nigeria. By reducing multiple levies and simplifying the tax structure, the government hopes to ease financial pressures on businesses and support economic growth.

While the reforms continue to attract public debate, officials say stronger communication and clearer implementation will be key to ensuring that businesses and citizens understand the long term benefits of the policy changes.

For small and medium sized businesses in Nigeria, multiple taxes and levies have long been a major challenge, often increasing operational costs and discouraging business expansion.

Simplifying the tax system and reducing overlapping charges can help create a more predictable and supportive business environment for entrepreneurs. This can encourage investment, improve compliance, and enable businesses to allocate more resources toward growth, employment, and innovation.

If effectively implemented, the proposed reforms could strengthen Nigeria’s business environment and improve the ability of small enterprises to compete within both domestic and regional markets.
FG Introduces Alphanumeric Postcode System to Modernise Postal Services

The Federal Executive Council has approved the implementation of a GIS- enabled alphanumeric digital postcode system aimed at improving the speed, accuracy, and reliability of mail and parcel processing across Nigeria. The initiative forms part of the government’s broader strategy to modernise communications infrastructure and strengthen the country’s digital economy.

The Federal Government has approved the implementation of a GIS enabled alphanumeric digital postcode system designed to improve mail and parcel processing across Nigeria.

The approval was announced by the Minister of Communications, Innovation and Digital Economy, Bosun Tijani, following the Federal Executive Council meeting presided over by President Bola Ahmed Tinubu in Abuja.

According to the minister, the new digital postcode system will introduce a modern geospatial addressing structure capable of improving the accuracy and efficiency of postal operations nationwide.

The project will be implemented in collaboration with the Nigerian Postal Service and the Office of the Postmaster General of the Federation, led by Tola Odeyemi.

Tijani noted that the initiative aligns with the ministry’s broader digital transformation strategy launched in 2023 under the blueprint titled “Accelerating Our Collective Prosperity through Technical Efficiency,” which aims to expand the use of information and communications technology across Nigeria.


Beyond improving postal operations, the new digital postcode system is expected to serve as a foundational infrastructure for several sectors of the economy.

According to the minister, the system will improve logistics efficiency, support the growth of e-commerce, and strengthen the delivery of government services across different parts of the country.

It will also enhance national planning and emergency response systems by providing more accurate location data for agencies and service providers.

Tijani explained that the geospatial addressing system will help connect communities, businesses, and institutions more efficiently, enabling smoother communication and service delivery nationwide.

“Beyond strengthening postal operations, the Digital Postcode System will also serve as an important national enabler supporting better national planning, improved emergency response, more efficient logistics and e-commerce, and the delivery of government services,” the minister said.

He added that the initiative represents another step in the government’s effort to create an enabling environment for a modern and globally competitive digital economy.


The approval of the GIS enabled digital postcode system marks a significant step toward modernising Nigeria’s postal and logistics infrastructure.

By improving address accuracy, strengthening delivery systems, and supporting digital commerce, the initiative is expected to contribute to a more connected and efficient economy. As Nigeria continues to expand its digital infrastructure, systems such as this will play an important role in improving services for businesses, government agencies, and citizens across the country.

For businesses, particularly small and medium enterprises, reliable addressing systems play an important role in logistics, delivery services, and customer reach.

A modern digital postcode system can significantly improve last mile delivery, reduce delays in shipping, and enable businesses to reach customers more efficiently. This is particularly important for e-commerce platforms, logistics providers, and businesses that rely on nationwide distribution networks.

Improved location accuracy can also help entrepreneurs operate more efficiently and expand into new markets.
CBN Strengthens Gold Holdings While Senate Pushes Fintech Regulation Reform

Nigeria’s gold reserves have reached $3.5 billion following continued purchases under the Central Bank’s Domestic Gold Purchase Programme. At the same time, the Senate is advancing legislative reforms that would position the CBN as the coordinating regulator of Nigeria’s rapidly growing fintech sector while strengthening efforts to curb fraudulent investment platforms

The Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) has expanded the country’s gold reserves to $3.5 billion, reflecting ongoing efforts to diversify Nigeria’s external reserves and strengthen macroeconomic stability.

CBN Governor Olayemi Cardoso explained that the bank’s strategy involves purchasing domestically refined gold without deploying foreign currency, enabling the country to build reserves while supporting the local mining value chain.

According to Cardoso, the initiative contributes to reserve accretion while reducing pressure on foreign exchange resources.

He noted that global reserve management strategies are increasingly shifting as central banks seek to strengthen economic resilience amid geopolitical uncertainty, inflation pressures, and financial market volatility.

Gold, he said, has regained importance as a strategic asset due to its role as a hedge against inflation and economic instability.

The workshop organised by the CBN’s Corporate Secretariat and Reserve Management Departments brought together stakeholders across the gold value chain to deepen collaboration and strengthen Nigeria’s gold sector.

Cardoso emphasised that Nigeria’s natural resource potential can only deliver long-term benefits through strategic coordination, transparency, and strong governance frameworks.


Industry stakeholders also highlighted the importance of the Domestic Gold Purchase Programme in formalising Nigeria’s gold sector and improving supply chain transparency.

The Executive Secretary of the Solid Minerals Development Fund, Fatima Umaru Shinkafi, said the delivery of London Bullion Market Association standard gold demonstrated the strength of Nigeria’s formalisation framework and due diligence processes.

Similarly, the World Gold Council commended Nigeria for aligning the programme with the London Principles for responsible artisanal and small-scale gold sourcing, which aim to ensure responsible and transparent gold production.

The President of the Africa Finance Corporation, Samaila Zubairu, stressed that improving mineral processing infrastructure and strengthening geological data would help attract greater investment into the sector while improving gold recovery rates.

Industry experts also called for stronger investment in exploration and transparency to support the development of strategic gold reserves that can contribute to long-term economic stability.


Meanwhile, the Nigerian Senate is considering amendments to the Banks and Other Financial Institutions Act (BOFIA) that would position the Central Bank as the lead coordinating regulator for Nigeria’s rapidly expanding fintech ecosystem.

The proposal emerged during a public hearing organised by multiple Senate committees, including those on banking, ICT and cybersecurity, capital markets, and anti-corruption.

Lawmakers said the amendment is necessary because fintech companies such as digital lenders, payment platforms, and mobile money operators have grown significantly in scale and influence.

According to Senator Mukhail Adetokunbo Abiru, chairman of the Senate Committee on Banking, the existing regulatory framework has not evolved at the same pace as the growth of digital financial platforms.

The proposed reforms would empower the CBN to:

  • Designate major fintech companies as Systemically Important Financial Institutions

  • Establish a national registry for improved transparency and ownership disclosure

  • Strengthen risk-based supervision tailored to digital financial services

  • Improve coordination among regulators in the financial ecosystem

Abiru argued that creating a separate fintech regulatory agency would lead to unnecessary duplication and bureaucratic overlap.

Instead, strengthening the CBN’s supervisory powers while encouraging collaboration with agencies such as the Securities and Exchange Commission, Nigerian Communications Commission, and the National Information Technology Development Agency would provide a more coherent regulatory framework.


In addition to fintech regulation, lawmakers also raised concerns over the growing prevalence of Ponzi schemes and fraudulent digital investment platforms.

The Senate cited the collapse of the Crypto Bullion Exchange (CBEX) as a recent example that exposed thousands of Nigerians to significant financial losses.

Lawmakers warned that such schemes undermine public confidence in legitimate financial institutions and expose the country to risks including money laundering and illicit financial flows.

The Senate said it will review regulatory gaps and enforcement challenges while considering stronger legislative measures to protect citizens from financial fraud.

Stakeholders participating in the hearing included representatives from the CBN, Nigerian Deposit Insurance Corporation, Economic and Financial Crimes Commission, Nigerian Communications Commission, and the Chartered Institute of Bankers of Nigeria.


Nigeria’s expanding gold reserves and ongoing fintech regulatory reforms reflect broader efforts to strengthen the country’s financial system in an increasingly digital and uncertain global economy.

While the CBN continues to diversify reserves and build resilience through strategic resource management, lawmakers are also working to modernise financial regulations to keep pace with technological innovation and protect citizens from financial exploitation.

For small businesses and startups, stronger fintech regulation and improved financial oversight could lead to greater trust in digital financial services, safer online transactions, and better consumer protection. This is important as many MSMEs rely on digital payments, mobile banking, and fintech platforms to run daily operations.

At the same time, the expansion of Nigeria’s gold reserves signals broader efforts to stabilise the economy and strengthen foreign reserves, which can influence exchange rates, investment flows, and overall economic confidence.
Middle East Crisis May Raise Nigeria’s Oil Earnings, Inflation Risks

Recent macroeconomic reports from Comercio Partners and Arthur Steven Asset Management suggest that a conflict in the Middle East would primarily affect Nigeria through global oil price shocks. Nigeria produces roughly 1.3–1.5 million barrels of crude per day, and the federal budget for 2026 is benchmarked at $65 per barrel.

A surge in Brent crude prices to $80–$100 per barrel could immediately increase government revenue, translating to billions in additional earnings annually. Higher oil prices would strengthen federally collected revenue, improve Federation Account Allocation Committee (FAAC) disbursements, and reduce fiscal deficit pressure.

FX inflows from crude sales could bolster external reserves and support the naira, provided production levels remain stable and leakages are contained. However, production constraints—due to pipeline outages, theft, or underinvestment—could limit the volume of oil captured, meaning Nigeria might benefit from higher prices but not from increased output.

Inflationary Pressures and Economic Risks

While higher oil prices can improve fiscal revenues, they come with economic trade-offs. Nigeria remains structurally dependent on imported refined petroleum products, meaning a global crude price spike could increase domestic fuel costs. The ripple effect would extend to transportation, logistics, food distribution, and manufacturing, putting upward pressure on consumer prices and eroding household purchasing power.

To manage inflationary pressures, the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) may tighten monetary policy, potentially raising interest rates and increasing borrowing costs for both households and businesses. While oil-linked equities and sectors directly tied to the oil economy could benefit from improved revenue expectations, heightened global risk aversion may reduce foreign portfolio inflows, resulting in volatility in Nigerian equities and fixed-income markets.

Analysts stress that the net impact on the economy would depend on how long higher oil prices persist, the degree of production stability, and the ability of policymakers to balance revenue gains with inflation control.


A Middle East crisis could be a double-edged sword for Nigeria: higher oil prices offer fiscal and external reserve relief, but risk intensifying inflation and tightening monetary conditions.

For policymakers, balancing revenue gains with inflation management will be crucial. For MSMEs, monitoring fuel prices, adjusting supply chains, and preparing for cost fluctuations will help mitigate risks while exploring potential opportunities from improved liquidity and government spending.

Small and medium enterprises in Nigeria are particularly sensitive to energy prices and inflation. Rising fuel costs increase transportation and logistics expenses, raising the cost of goods and services.

MSMEs reliant on imported raw materials or fuel-intensive operations may face reduced profit margins, while businesses serving local markets could see demand pressure due to higher consumer prices.

At the same time, improved FX liquidity and higher government revenue could indirectly benefit MSMEs through more stable payment systems, potential public sector contracts, and sustained subsidies or relief programs.
Nigeria Engages Foreign Investors at Presidential Villa as PEBEC Addresses Business Climate Concerns

Nigeria hosted foreign investors, diplomats, and regulators at the Presidential Villa for the third PEBEC Foreign Direct Investors Roundtable. Discussions focused on security, taxation, customs processes, and regulatory challenges affecting business operations. The government reaffirmed its commitment to reforms aimed at improving investor confidence and strengthening Nigeria’s investment climate.

Nigeria’s Federal Government has intensified engagement with foreign investors as part of efforts to strengthen confidence in the country’s business environment. Senior government officials, diplomats, and multinational business leaders convened at the Presidential Villa in Abuja for a high-level investment dialogue organised by the Presidential Enabling Business Environment Council (PEBEC).
