The meeting formed part of the Third Existing Foreign Direct Investors Roundtable, a platform designed to sustain direct communication between government regulators and international investors already operating in Nigeria.

Opening the session, Deputy Chief of Staff to the President, Ibrahim Hadejia, reaffirmed the administration’s commitment to building a stable and competitive investment environment. Speaking on behalf of Vice President Kashim Shettima, who chairs PEBEC, he said the government remains focused on implementing reforms aimed at strengthening investor confidence and driving sustainable economic growth.

He emphasised that foreign direct investment plays a critical role in expanding industrial capacity, creating jobs, and facilitating technology transfer across key sectors of the economy.


According to PEBEC Director-General Zahrah Mustapha Audu, the roundtable is part of a broader strategy to ensure that government reforms translate into practical improvements for businesses operating in Nigeria.

She noted that while attracting new foreign investment remains a priority, retaining and supporting existing investors is equally important for economic stability and long-term growth.

“Existing foreign investors are key partners in Nigeria’s development. Their investments contribute significantly to employment, capital inflow, technology transfer, and supply chain development,” Audu said.

The meeting also featured engagements with heads of major regulatory institutions, including:

  • Zacch Adedeji, Chairman of the Nigeria Revenue Service

  • Bashir Adewale Adeniyi, Comptroller-General of the Nigeria Customs Service

  • Olutunji Rilwan Disu, Inspector-General of the Nigeria Police Force

Also contributing virtually was tax policy expert Taiwo Oyedele, Chairman of the Presidential Committee on Fiscal Policy and Tax Reforms, who outlined ongoing fiscal reforms aimed at improving tax certainty, streamlining revenue administration, and strengthening Nigeria’s competitiveness as an investment destination.


During the dialogue, foreign investors raised several operational concerns affecting business activity in Nigeria, including:

  • Security challenges in certain regions

  • Tax administration and regulatory complexity

  • Customs clearance delays

  • Policy consistency and regulatory coordination

The interactive format allowed investors to engage regulators directly, ask questions, and seek clarification on reforms already underway to address these issues.

Representatives from diplomatic missions including the United States, Russia, France, Colombia, Denmark, and Pakistan attended the meeting alongside development partners such as the United Nations and the World Bank.

Participants commended the government for creating an open platform for engagement and encouraged authorities to maintain regular consultations with the private sector.


Analysts note that structured dialogue between investors and regulators is becoming increasingly important as Nigeria competes with other African economies for global investment capital.

Direct engagement with businesses already operating in the country allows policymakers to identify systemic challenges early and accelerate reforms aimed at improving the ease of doing business.

Through initiatives such as the PEBEC investor roundtable, the government hopes to strengthen trust with international investors and ensure that ongoing economic reforms translate into a more predictable and competitive operating environment.

Improving the ease of doing business and attracting foreign investment can expand supply chains, increase capital inflows, and create partnerships that benefit micro, small, and medium enterprises across Africa. Stronger investor confidence in Nigeria can also stimulate job creation, technology transfer, and broader economic growth.
Bank of Agriculture Expands Support for Women Entrepreneurs on International Women’s Day
Agricultural lender reinforces commitment to women-led businesses and food security through targeted financing programmes.

To mark International Women’s Day, the Bank of Agriculture has reaffirmed its commitment to empowering women entrepreneurs through targeted financing programmes. With over ₦8 billion already disbursed to women-led businesses, the initiative aims to strengthen food security, support agribusiness growth, and expand economic opportunities across Nigeria.

As the world marks International Women’s Day, the Bank of Agriculture (BOA) has reaffirmed its commitment to empowering women in agriculture and entrepreneurship, emphasizing that Nigeria’s economic growth depends significantly on women’s participation.

Under the global campaign theme “Give to Gain” and the United Nations call for “Rights, Justice, Action,” the bank said it is intensifying programmes that expand financial access, mentorship, and business opportunities for women across Nigeria’s agricultural value chain.

 

Women Seen as Drivers of Economic Growth

BOA noted that empowering women is both a social responsibility and a strategic economic investment capable of strengthening communities and improving national food security.

According to the bank’s Managing Director, Ayo Sotinrin, investments in women produce far-reaching economic benefits.

He noted that:

  • Supporting women entrepreneurs creates a multiplier effect across households and communities

  • Women-led enterprises play a key role in food production and rural economic growth

  • Inclusive participation strengthens national economic resilience

Through its various financial products, the bank has expanded access to credit for women farmers and agribusiness owners nationwide.

Key programmes supporting women include:

  • Grow and Earn More (GEM) programme

  • Micro Agric Loan scheme

  • Micro Non-Agric Loan facility

According to BOA, these initiatives have disbursed over ₦8 billion to women across Nigeria, supporting both individual entrepreneurs and organised women groups engaged in agriculture and agribusiness.

 

Alignment with Nigeria’s Agricultural Development Agenda

The bank said its empowerment strategy aligns with the economic priorities of the Bola Ahmed Tinubu administration, which places agriculture, food security, and inclusive growth at the centre of national development.

BOA also highlighted complementary national initiatives aimed at improving nutrition and welfare, including programmes championed by Oluremi Tinubu.

These initiatives aim to:

  • Increase women’s participation in agricultural production

  • Strengthen rural economies and agribusiness development

  • Improve food security and nutrition outcomes

 

Call for Broader Stakeholder Support

The bank urged stakeholders across government, the private sector, and civil society to deepen investment in women-led enterprises.

Recommended actions include:

  • Expanding financing for women entrepreneurs

  • Supporting farm mechanisation for female farmers

  • Strengthening leadership and mentorship opportunities for women

BOA emphasised that empowering women remains essential to achieving long-term economic development and sustainable food systems in Nigeria.

Expanding financing for women-led agribusinesses unlocks new opportunities across Africa’s agricultural value chain, from farming and processing to distribution and retail. Greater inclusion of women entrepreneurs also strengthens food systems and drives inclusive economic growth across rural economies.
CBN Agricultural Credit Scheme Expands Financing Access for Nigerian Farmers
Government-backed loan guarantee programme encourages banks to finance farmers and agribusiness entrepreneurs.

Nigeria’s Agricultural Credit Guarantee Scheme Fund is helping farmers access bank financing by reducing lending risks for financial institutions. Managed by the Central Bank of Nigeria, the programme supports farm expansion, agribusiness growth, and increased agricultural productivity across the country.

Farmers and agribusiness entrepreneurs in Nigeria can now access expanded financing through the Agricultural Credit Guarantee Scheme Fund (ACGSF), a Federal Government initiative designed to improve credit access in the agricultural sector.

The programme is managed by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) and provides guarantees on agricultural loans issued by banks. By reducing the lending risk for financial institutions, the scheme encourages banks to extend more credit to farmers and agribusinesses seeking capital to expand operations.

 

Loan Guarantees Designed to Unlock Agricultural Financing

The ACGSF works by guaranteeing a portion of loans provided by participating banks, making it easier for farmers to secure financing.

Key objectives of the scheme include:

  • Expanding credit access for farmers and agribusiness operators

  • Reducing lending risk for banks financing agricultural activities

  • Supporting investment in modern farming and agribusiness growth

The initiative forms part of broader government efforts to strengthen Nigeria’s agricultural economy and improve food security.

 

Funding Supports Farm Expansion and Productivity

Eligible farmers and agribusiness owners can access loans through participating financial institutions to support different aspects of agricultural production.

Funding under the scheme can be used for:

  • Expanding farmland and increasing crop production

  • Purchasing farming equipment and machinery

  • Scaling agribusiness operations and supply chains

  • Improving farm productivity and efficiency

The programme aims to help farmers move from small-scale production toward more commercially viable agricultural operations.

 

How Farmers Can Access the Programme

Farmers interested in the scheme can apply through participating banks.

Application steps typically include:

  • Visiting a commercial bank or microfinance bank participating in the programme

  • Requesting an agricultural loan under the ACGSF framework

  • Completing the required loan application forms and documentation

The CBN advises applicants to deal only with licensed financial institutions and avoid unofficial platforms requesting payments or personal information outside approved channels.

Further details on eligibility and application guidelines are available on the official website of the Central Bank of Nigeria.

Improved access to agricultural financing helps MSMEs in farming, food processing, and agribusiness expand production and invest in modern equipment. Strengthening credit access also boosts rural economies and supports Africa’s broader food security and agribusiness development goals.
South-East Development Commission Targets $200bn Regional Economy by 2035
New ₦140bn budget approved to drive industrialisation, infrastructure, agriculture, and innovation across Nigeria’s South-East.

The South East Development Commission has secured approval for a ₦140 billion budget and unveiled a long-term plan to expand the region’s economy from $40 billion to $200 billion. The strategy focuses on industrialisation, agriculture, infrastructure, and youth entrepreneurship to attract investment and accelerate economic growth across the South-East.

Nigeria’s South East Development Commission (SEDC) has unveiled an ambitious plan to grow the South-East economy from about $40 billion to $200 billion over the next decade, positioning the region as a major economic and industrial hub.

The commission’s Managing Director and CEO, Mark Okoye, disclosed the strategy while presenting the agency’s ₦140 billion 2026 budget proposal before the House of Representatives Committee on SEDC at the National Assembly of Nigeria.

The economic blueprint prioritises industrialisation, agriculture, technology, and infrastructure development to attract private investment and accelerate regional growth.

 

₦140bn Budget Approved for Regional Development

The House Committee on SEDC approved the ₦140 billion budget, expressing confidence that the proposed programmes could stimulate economic activity and investment across the five South-East states.

Key development priorities include:

  • Expanding industrial manufacturing and special economic zones

  • Promoting mechanised agriculture and agro-industrial hubs

  • Investing in technology and youth entrepreneurship

  • Developing critical infrastructure to attract investors

The commission emphasised that collaboration among state governments, private investors, and diaspora communities will be essential to achieving the region’s long-term economic target.

 

Infrastructure and Investment to Drive Economic Growth

A major component of the development plan is the creation of the South-East Investment Company Limited, an investment vehicle designed to mobilise private capital for large-scale infrastructure projects.

Priority infrastructure projects include:

  • Rail transport networks

  • Electric power generation facilities

  • Ports and logistics infrastructure

  • Gas pipelines and energy systems

To ensure projects are investment-ready, the commission also plans to establish a Project Preparation Facility that will fund feasibility studies, environmental assessments, and engineering designs.

 

Agriculture and Industrialisation at the Core

Agriculture will play a key role in the region’s economic expansion strategy.

Planned initiatives include:

  • Clearing farmland for mechanised agriculture projects

  • Establishing demonstration farms spanning 200–300 hectares

  • Creating agro-industrial hubs and produce aggregation centres

The South-East Industrialisation Programme will also support the development of special economic zones aimed at attracting manufacturing investments and strengthening regional supply chains.

 

Environmental and Security Challenges

Addressing environmental risks is also a priority. The South-East currently has more than 2,700 erosion sites, many of which threaten farmland, communities, and infrastructure.

According to the commission:

  • Repairing a single erosion site may cost ₦10 billion to ₦20 billion

  • Sustainable environmental interventions will be necessary to protect economic assets

The commission is also proposing a South-East Security Intervention Programme to strengthen regional security coordination and improve the investment climate.

 

Youth Entrepreneurship and Innovation

The development strategy includes initiatives aimed at supporting young entrepreneurs and technology startups across the region.

Planned programmes include:

  • Youth Entrepreneurship and Innovation Programme

  • Support for technology startups and digital businesses

  • Investment in grassroots sports infrastructure to nurture talent

These initiatives aim to stimulate job creation and encourage youth-led enterprise development.

Large-scale regional development programmes create opportunities for MSMEs in manufacturing, agriculture, logistics, technology, and construction. If successfully implemented, the South-East’s economic expansion strategy could unlock new markets, attract investment, and strengthen supply chains across Nigeria and West Africa.
Nigeria’s National Single Window Could Drive $3bn in New Investment
Trade digitisation platform expected to simplify compliance and boost investment in Nigeria’s import and export ecosystem.

Nigeria’s National Single Window trade platform could attract up to $3 billion in investment over the next five years by simplifying regulatory processes for importers and exporters. The digital portal will integrate over 20 government agencies into a single system, aiming to improve trade efficiency and strengthen Nigeria’s business environment.

Nigeria’s National Single Window (NSW) trade platform could attract between $2 billion and $3 billion in new investment within five years, according to economic analyst Dele Oye.

Speaking in an interview on ARISE TV, Oye described the initiative as one of the most significant pro-business reforms under the administration of Bola Ahmed Tinubu, noting that it could significantly reduce regulatory bottlenecks for importers and exporters.

The National Single Window is scheduled to launch its Phase One “go-live” on March 27, 2026, creating a unified digital portal for trade documentation and regulatory compliance.

 

Unified Platform to Simplify Trade Processes

The NSW aims to integrate multiple government agencies into a single digital trade portal, eliminating the need for businesses to navigate separate regulatory systems.

Agencies expected to be connected to the platform include:

  • Nigeria Customs Service

  • National Agency for Food and Drug Administration and Control (NAFDAC)

  • Nigerian Ports Authority (NPA)

Key objectives of the platform include:

  • Streamlining import and export documentation processes

  • Reducing regulatory delays and compliance costs

  • Improving trade transparency and efficiency

The system will allow businesses to complete multiple regulatory requirements through a single digital interface.

 

Potential to Attract Billions in Investment

According to Oye, the simplified regulatory process could significantly improve Nigeria’s investment climate.

Expected economic benefits include:

  • $2–$3 billion in potential investment inflows within five years

  • Reduced costs associated with regulatory compliance

  • Faster trade clearance and logistics operations

He emphasised that the digital portal could eliminate the long-standing practice of businesses moving “from ministry to ministry” to secure approvals.

 

Political Support Seen as Key to Success

Nigeria has attempted to implement a single-window trade system multiple times since 2009, but previous efforts stalled.

Industry analysts say the current initiative stands a stronger chance of success due to:

  • Direct presidential backing

  • Improved inter-agency coordination

  • Strong leadership driving implementation

Oye credited the project’s progress partly to the leadership of Zacch Adedeji, who has helped align participating government agencies.

 

Private Sector Participation Remains Critical

Despite optimism around the platform, Oye warned that the private sector must be actively involved in its governance and development.

Key recommendations include:

  • Including private sector representatives in decision-making

  • Aligning the platform with global trade facilitation standards

  • Ensuring continuous feedback from business users

He emphasised that successful trade reforms require strong collaboration between government and the business community.

A fully functional National Single Window can significantly reduce compliance costs and delays for small exporters and importers. By simplifying trade processes and improving transparency, the platform could help MSMEs participate more easily in international trade and regional supply chains.
West African Partners Advance Climate Justice and Energy Solutions
Ford Foundation-backed initiatives are empowering communities and supporting a fair energy transition across Nigeria, Senegal, and Ghana.

Ford Foundation partners across West Africa are advancing climate justice initiatives that support clean energy access, community rights, and environmental accountability. The collaborative efforts aim to ensure that the region’s energy transition promotes sustainable economic opportunities while protecting vulnerable communities.

Grantee partners of the Ford Foundation across West Africa are developing innovative initiatives to address the impacts of extractive industries, climate change, and energy transition on communities, livelihoods, and ecosystems.

The efforts were highlighted at a regional meeting hosted in Lagos, where organisations from Nigeria, Senegal, and Ghana shared practical solutions aimed at promoting climate justice, expanding energy access, and protecting community rights.

The meeting brought together civil society leaders and development partners, including Heather Gerken, to explore how local leadership can shape a more sustainable and inclusive energy future for the region.

 

Local Organisations Driving Climate and Energy Solutions

Participants highlighted the role of local organisations in advancing climate justice and protecting communities affected by fossil fuel extraction.

Key organisations contributing to these efforts include:

  • Spaces for Change

  • Sustainable Energy for All

  • Health of Mother Earth Foundation

  • Africa Centre for Energy Policy

Their work focuses on strengthening environmental governance, supporting clean energy adoption, and ensuring communities benefit from energy transition policies.

 

Energy Access Linked to Economic Opportunity

Experts emphasised that energy access is a key driver of economic growth and community resilience.

Initiatives highlighted during the meeting aim to:

  • Expand clean energy access for small businesses and rural communities

  • Support agricultural productivity through reliable power supply

  • Improve livelihood opportunities in underserved regions

According to Lanre Shasore, energy access is closely tied to economic opportunity, particularly for small enterprises and rural entrepreneurs.

 

Addressing Community Impacts of Extractive Industries

Participants also stressed the importance of addressing environmental damage caused by decades of fossil fuel extraction.

Advocacy groups are focusing on:

  • Restoring damaged ecosystems and livelihoods

  • Promoting transparent energy governance

  • Ensuring communities participate in energy policy decisions

Nnimmo Bassey noted that a just energy transition must address historical environmental harm and protect frontline communities.

 

Energy Poverty and Social Impacts

Research presented during the meeting highlighted the social impacts of energy poverty, particularly for women.

According to Victoria Ibezim-Ohaeri, limited access to energy in many communities increases economic vulnerability and social risks.

Key solutions discussed include:

  • Promoting clean cooking fuels

  • Supporting renewable energy alternatives

  • Expanding community energy infrastructure

Improved energy access and climate-focused investment can unlock major opportunities for MSMEs in agriculture, manufacturing, and services. Reliable and affordable power allows small businesses to increase productivity, reduce costs, and participate more effectively in Africa’s growing green economy.
Nigeria Pushes for Expanded GBV Support and Women’s Inclusion on International Women’s Day 2026
Government and development partners call for stronger protection services, greater political participation, and economic empowerment for women.

Nigeria is marking International Women’s Day 2026 with renewed commitments to expand GBV survivor services, increase women’s political participation, and strengthen economic empowerment programmes. Government officials and development partners say deeper investment and policy reforms are essential to advancing gender equality and inclusive growth.

As Nigeria marks International Women’s Day 2026, the Federal Government and development partners have called for the urgent expansion of services for survivors of gender-based violence (GBV), stronger enforcement of protection laws, and increased participation of women in political leadership.

The call was made during a high-level ministerial press briefing in Abuja led by Imaan Sulaiman-Ibrahim, as the country launched its national activities for International Women’s Day and prepared for participation in the 70th Session of the Commission on the Status of Women (CSW70).

 

Urgent Need to Expand GBV Support Services

The Minister noted that while Nigeria has strengthened legal frameworks and social protection programmes for women and families, significant gaps remain in protection services for survivors of violence.

Key statistics highlighted include:

  • Nigeria currently operates about 50 Sexual Assault Referral Centres (SARCs) nationwide

  • International standards recommend one centre per 400,000 people

  • With a population exceeding 200 million, Nigeria requires at least 500 centres

The government is therefore seeking increased investment and partnerships to expand survivor-centred services, improve prevention mechanisms, and ensure accountability for perpetrators of violence against women and girls.

 

Push for Greater Women’s Political Representation

Stakeholders also renewed calls for the passage of the Reserved Special Seats Bill, which aims to address the persistent underrepresentation of women in Nigeria’s elective offices.

According to the Minister:

  • Women now occupy several strategic leadership roles in government institutions

  • However, representation in elected positions remains significantly low

The proposed legislation seeks to create opportunities for more women to participate in governance and decision-making.

 

Government Programmes Supporting Women’s Economic Growth

The Federal Government highlighted several initiatives under its social and economic empowerment programmes designed to support women across the country.

Key focus areas include:

  • Women’s entrepreneurship and access to finance

  • Agricultural value chain programmes for female farmers

  • Clean energy initiatives supporting women-led businesses

  • Leadership development and mentorship programmes

According to Wale Edun, Nigeria’s social intervention programmes have already reached about nine million households, with plans to expand coverage to 15 million households nationwide.

 

Global Partners Call for Structural Reforms

Development partners emphasised that long-term progress requires addressing structural barriers affecting women’s participation in economic and political life.

Beatrice Eyong noted that Nigerian women remain among the most resilient and resourceful globally but continue to face challenges such as:

  • Limited access to finance and capital

  • Restricted leadership opportunities

  • Inadequate protection from gender-based violence

She stressed that advancing women’s rights requires measurable policy actions that translate into real improvements in the lives of women and girls.

Expanding economic opportunities for women can significantly strengthen Africa’s entrepreneurial ecosystem. Greater access to finance, leadership opportunities, and protection services enables women-led MSMEs to grow businesses, create jobs, and contribute more meaningfully to economic development.
Petrol Price Rises to ₦1,200/Litre as Market Volatility Intensifies
Global crude price spike and refinery adjustments drive sharp increase in fuel costs across Lagos.

Petrol prices in Lagos have climbed above ₦1,200 per litre in some locations as rising global oil prices and refinery adjustments push up domestic fuel costs. The increase, driven by geopolitical tensions and supply chain pressures, could raise operating expenses for businesses and households across Nigeria.

Petrol prices in parts of Lagos have surged past ₦1,200 per litre at some filling stations, marking one of the steepest short-term increases since Nigeria removed its long-standing fuel subsidy in 2023.

A market survey across Lagos revealed pump prices ranging from ₦1,020 to ₦1,080 per litre at major retail outlets, while some independent operators in high-demand areas have crossed the ₦1,200 threshold.

The price spike reflects global energy market pressures as crude oil prices rise amid escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East.

 

Global Oil Prices Driving Domestic Fuel Costs

The surge in pump prices is closely tied to rising international oil prices.

Key global developments include:

  • Brent crude rising to about $91 per barrel amid Middle East tensions

  • Conflict involving Israel and Iran increasing market uncertainty

  • Potential disruption of oil shipments through the Strait of Hormuz

Energy analysts warn that if tanker traffic through the strait is restricted, oil prices could climb significantly higher, with some projections reaching $150 per barrel in extreme scenarios.

 

Domestic Refinery Prices Increase

Nigeria’s domestic fuel market has also been affected by price adjustments from the Dangote Refinery, the country’s largest local fuel supplier.

Recent pricing changes include:

  • Ex-gantry price increase from ₦774 to ₦874 per litre

  • Subsequent adjustment to ₦995 per litre within days

  • Overall price rise of about 29% in less than a week

Retail fuel marketers have passed the increases directly to consumers to maintain operational margins.

 

Current Pump Prices Across Lagos

A survey of filling stations across Lagos revealed the following pump prices:

  • ₦1,040 per litre at Nigerian National Petroleum Company Limited retail stations in Ilasa, Apple Junction, and Ago Palace Way

  • ₦1,057 per litre at MRS Oil Nigeria stations

  • ₦1,050 per litre at a Techno Oil Limited outlet in Festac

Independent roadside operators in some locations are selling petrol for above ₦1,200 per litre, signalling potential future price levels if global crude prices remain elevated.

 

Alternative Fuels Begin to Emerge

As petrol prices rise, alternative fuel solutions are beginning to gain attention.

NIPCO Gas Limited has announced the rollout of 20 new compressed natural gas (CNG) stations nationwide, with prices around ₦380 per standard cubic metre.

However, adoption remains limited due to:

  • Low availability of CNG-powered vehicles

  • Limited fuel infrastructure nationwide

Rising petrol prices significantly increase transportation, logistics, and production costs for MSMEs. For many small businesses, especially in retail, agriculture, and delivery services, fuel price volatility directly impacts profitability and pricing strategies.
NIWIIT Launches Campus Tech Queens Programme to Grow Female Tech Talent
New national initiative aims to equip female university students with digital skills, mentorship, and innovation opportunities.

The Nigerian Women in Information Technology has launched the Campus Tech Queens Initiative to empower female students in Nigerian universities with digital skills, mentorship, and innovation opportunities. The programme aims to bridge the gender gap in technology while preparing young women for leadership roles in Africa’s growing digital economy.

The Nigerian Women in Information Technology (NIWIIT) has launched the Campus Tech Queens Initiative, a national programme designed to prepare female students in tertiary institutions for careers in the digital economy.

The mentorship-driven initiative was officially unveiled at Kogi State Polytechnic and aims to identify, train, and mentor female technology talents across universities and polytechnics nationwide.

According to Oluwakemi Ayodele, the programme seeks to close the gender gap in technology and empower young women to become innovators and leaders in the digital sector.

 

Programme Designed to Build Future Female Tech Leaders

The Campus Tech Queens Initiative will run through a structured development pathway designed to nurture female tech talent from early discovery to innovation leadership.

The programme includes six key stages:

  • Campus sensitisation and student registration

  • Digital capacity building and technical training

  • Mentorship and innovation ideation sessions

  • State-level hackathon competitions

  • National grand finale innovation challenge

  • Post-hackathon incubation and leadership development

The initiative aims to equip participants with both technical skills and entrepreneurial capabilities required in the global technology ecosystem.

 

Stakeholders Support Technology and Gender Inclusion

Government officials and technology leaders have welcomed the initiative as an important step toward building Nigeria’s digital workforce.

Key supporters include:

  • Helen Aderibigbe, who emphasised the role of broadband expansion and digital infrastructure in supporting such programmes.

  • Wemi Jones, represented by the Director of Tertiary Education, who reaffirmed the government’s commitment to initiatives that support youth and girl-child education.

  • Nigeria Computer Society, which described the initiative as a strategic pathway for young women to develop technology careers.

Academic institutions also pledged support, with leadership at Kogi State Polytechnic committing to provide an enabling environment for participating students.

 

Students Welcome the Opportunity

Participants at the launch highlighted the programme’s potential to transform career opportunities for young women in technology.

Students noted that the initiative would:

  • Provide hands-on digital skills and mentorship

  • Encourage confidence and leadership among women in tech

  • Open pathways into technology careers and innovation ecosystems

The programme also aims to strengthen collaboration between universities, industry stakeholders, and technology communities.

Expanding digital skills among young women strengthens Africa’s technology talent pipeline and supports innovation-driven entrepreneurship. As more women gain skills in software development, data, and digital solutions, MSMEs across sectors can benefit from new technology-driven services and startups.
WINBAFRICA Launches Initiative to Strengthen Youth Participation in Africa’s Entrepreneurial Ecosystem
Foundation marks 12th anniversary with a new push to empower young innovators, entrepreneurs, and future business leaders.

WINBAFRICA has launched a new initiative to strengthen youth participation in Africa’s entrepreneurial ecosystem during its 12th anniversary summit. The programme aims to empower young innovators and entrepreneurs through mentorship, education, and leadership development to support sustainable economic growth across the continent.

The African Women Foundation for Nation Building has launched a new initiative aimed at strengthening youth participation in Africa’s entrepreneurial ecosystem.

The announcement was made during the foundation’s Roundtable Summit marking its 12th anniversary, where business leaders, policymakers, development experts, and young innovators gathered to explore strategies for empowering the next generation of African entrepreneurs.

Founder Bukola Bello Jaiyesimi said the initiative reflects the foundation’s long-standing commitment to promoting inclusive leadership, economic participation, and community development across the continent.

 

Building Youth Leadership for Economic Growth

The anniversary summit, themed “Youth as Architects of Nation Building,” focused on expanding opportunities for young Africans to contribute to economic development and entrepreneurship.

Participants highlighted several priorities:

  • Increasing youth participation in entrepreneurship and leadership

  • Expanding access to mentorship, education, and innovation support

  • Strengthening institutions that enable transparent and merit-based advancement

  • Encouraging intergenerational collaboration in business and governance

Speakers emphasised that sustainable nation-building requires active engagement from both experienced leaders and emerging entrepreneurs.

 

Foundation’s Programmes Supporting Youth and Women

Since its establishment in 2014, WINBAFRICA has implemented several programmes focused on education, leadership development, and community impact across Africa.

Key initiatives include:

  • Education First Series – promoting learning opportunities for young people

  • Project Sapphire – supporting leadership and empowerment programmes

  • Orphanage Challenge – community-focused welfare initiatives

  • Project Back-to-School – education support for vulnerable children

  • WINBAFRICA Monthly Roundtable Discussions – policy and leadership dialogue

  • WINBAFRICA Food Bank – community relief support programmes

These initiatives aim to strengthen human capital development while encouraging youth participation in economic activities.

 

Focus on Inclusive Nation Building

Summit participants stressed that nation-building extends beyond government policy and includes:

  • Economic resilience and entrepreneurship

  • Infrastructure and institutional development

  • Cultural identity and social cohesion

  • Human capital and leadership development

The initiative also aims to move beyond symbolic recognition of women’s achievements by implementing practical strategies that promote education, mentorship, economic inclusion, and institutional support.

WINBAFRICA reaffirmed its commitment to empowering women and youth as catalysts for sustainable development across Africa.

Youth-driven innovation is essential for Africa’s startup and MSME ecosystem. Initiatives that expand mentorship, leadership development, and entrepreneurial opportunities help create new businesses, strengthen job creation, and accelerate inclusive economic growth across the continent.
Experts Call for Reforms to Expand Insurance Coverage in Nigeria
Industry leaders push for stronger regulation, digital distribution, and public awareness to unlock the sector’s economic potential.

Industry experts are calling for reforms to strengthen Nigeria’s insurance sector through improved regulation, digital distribution, and greater public awareness. With insurance penetration currently below 1% of GDP, stakeholders believe expanding coverage could significantly boost economic resilience, job creation, and investment across the country.

Industry leaders and financial experts have called for comprehensive reforms to deepen insurance penetration in Nigeria, highlighting the sector’s potential to strengthen economic resilience and support long-term national growth.

The call was made during the Insurance Sector Transformation Consultative Forum 2026, convened by EnterpriseNGR, where stakeholders gathered to explore strategies for rebuilding consumer trust and expanding insurance coverage across the country.

Speakers emphasised that stronger regulation, improved public awareness, and digital distribution platforms will be essential for increasing insurance adoption and strengthening the industry’s contribution to Nigeria’s economy.

 

Insurance Seen as Key Driver of Economic Growth

According to Aigboje Aig-Imoukhuede, vibrant financial and professional services sectors are critical for countries seeking to transition from emerging markets to advanced economies.

He noted that sectors such as:

  • Banking

  • Insurance

  • Asset management

  • Accounting

  • Legal services

  • Fintech

play a crucial role in mobilising domestic investment and attracting foreign capital.

However, Nigeria’s insurance industry currently contributes less than 1% of GDP, far below the 3% to 11% penetration levels recorded in several African markets.

 

New Reform Law to Modernise the Sector

Lawmakers say recent legislative reforms will help strengthen regulation and modernise insurance operations.

According to Tokunbo Abiru, the Nigerian Insurance Industry Reform Act 2025 was introduced to update outdated legislation governing the industry.

Key objectives of the reform include:

  • Modernising regulatory frameworks

  • Strengthening industry governance

  • Improving consumer protection

  • Expanding insurance access nationwide

Stakeholders stressed that effective implementation of the law will require strong collaboration between regulators, insurers, and industry operators.

 

Insurance Critical to Nigeria’s $1 Trillion Economy Goal

The Commissioner for Insurance and CEO of National Insurance Commission, Olusegun Omosehin, described the forum as a turning point for the industry.

He emphasised that insurance will be essential in supporting the Federal Government’s ambition of building a $1 trillion economy, noting that no country can achieve sustainable growth without a strong risk management framework.

Industry leaders also highlighted the sector’s potential for large-scale job creation, particularly through expanding the number of insurance agents nationwide.

Nigeria currently has fewer than 100,000 insurance agents, indicating significant room for workforce expansion.

Industry Collaboration Key to Sector Transformation

Stakeholders across the industry reaffirmed their commitment to supporting reforms aimed at expanding insurance coverage.

Notable contributions included:

  • Bola Odukale, who emphasised the need for strong collaboration during the sector’s reform phase.

  • Gboyega Lesi, who noted that the industry must move from the background to become a central pillar of Nigeria’s economic system.

Experts stressed that deeper penetration will ensure insurance services become more accessible to everyday Nigerians and businesses.

Expanded insurance coverage helps MSMEs manage risks such as asset loss, business interruption, and liability exposure. Stronger insurance penetration also improves access to financing, as lenders often require insured assets, making risk protection a key enabler of sustainable business growth.
Esso Expands Procurement Opportunities for Women-Owned Businesses in Nigeria’s Energy Sector
Supplier diversity engagement aims to strengthen inclusion and open oil and gas supply chains to women-led enterprises.

Esso Exploration and Production Nigeria Limited has expanded procurement opportunities for women-owned businesses through a supplier diversity engagement in Lagos. The initiative aims to increase participation of women-led enterprises in Nigeria’s oil and gas supply chain while strengthening local capacity and inclusive economic development.

Esso Exploration and Production Nigeria Limited has reinforced its commitment to inclusive sourcing by expanding procurement opportunities for women-owned businesses in Nigeria’s energy sector.

The company hosted a Supplier Diversity engagement session titled “Meet the Member Buyer” in Lagos, bringing together members of WEConnect International and certified women-owned businesses (WBEs) operating across different segments of the energy value chain.

The initiative aims to strengthen collaboration between buyers and suppliers while enabling women entrepreneurs to participate more actively in procurement opportunities within Nigeria’s oil and gas industry.

 

Driving Inclusion in the Energy Supply Chain

According to Etabuko Arbihire, the Supplier Diversity Programme is designed to move beyond policy commitments by creating practical pathways for women-owned enterprises to access industry contracts.

He noted that meaningful inclusion in a highly technical industry such as oil and gas requires:

  • Clear procurement requirements and standards

  • Strong partnerships between buyers and suppliers

  • Direct engagement and trust-building opportunities

  • Structured pathways for women-led businesses to participate

Arbihire emphasised that diverse supplier networks contribute to innovation, agility, and stronger value creation within the energy sector.

 

Continued Investment in Nigeria’s Energy Industry

Despite broader divestments from shallow-water assets by ExxonMobil affiliates, EEPNL remains actively invested in Nigeria through key offshore operations, including:

  • Usan Field

  • Erha Field

  • Owowo Field

These operations continue to create opportunities for local businesses and suppliers, reinforcing the company’s commitment to supporting domestic enterprise development.

 

Capacity Building Support for Women Entrepreneurs

The engagement also featured collaboration with the Nigerian Content Development and Monitoring Board, which reiterated its commitment to supporting women-owned enterprises across the energy value chain.

According to Alexis Emelle, the board provides support through:

  • Capacity development programmes

  • Access to funding opportunities

  • Assistance with documentation and compliance

  • Readiness training for meeting industry procurement standards

Participants engaged in discussions on procurement processes, regulatory requirements, and strategies for scaling businesses within the energy sector.

Expanding supplier diversity programmes helps small and women-owned businesses access high-value contracts in the energy sector. Greater participation in corporate supply chains can unlock new revenue streams, strengthen local capacity, and promote inclusive economic growth across Africa’s energy industry.
Nigeria Launches ₦10 Billion Fund to Accelerate New Sugar Industry Projects
Government and Bank of Industry partner to finance investor-ready sugar projects and strengthen domestic production.

Nigeria has launched a ₦10 billion Sugar Project Acceleration Fund to support the development of new sugar ventures and strengthen the country’s agro-industrial sector. The programme, managed by the Bank of Industry in partnership with the National Sugar Development Council, aims to help promising projects become investment-ready and attract large-scale financing.

Nigeria has launched a ₦10 billion Sugar Project Acceleration Fund aimed at accelerating the development of new sugar production projects and strengthening the country’s agro-industrial capacity.

The initiative is a partnership between the National Sugar Development Council and the Bank of Industry. The fund will provide financing and technical support to help greenfield sugar projects become investment-ready.

Officials say the programme is designed to improve Nigeria’s competitiveness in the sugar industry while creating a pipeline of bankable projects capable of attracting large-scale financing.

 

Addressing the Gap Between Capital and Bankable Projects

According to the Executive Secretary of the National Sugar Development Council, access to finance is not the main challenge facing Nigeria’s sugar sector.

Instead, the major barrier is the lack of well-structured, investment-ready projects that meet the standards required by financial institutions.

Experts explained that successful agro-industrial projects must include:

  • Detailed technical feasibility studies covering agronomy, water resources, and infrastructure

  • Strong financial models capable of testing different economic scenarios

  • Clear capital structures that distribute risk among investors

  • Secure land tenure arrangements

  • Structured outgrower schemes involving smallholder farmers

  • Credible management teams with proven track records

Without these elements, many early-stage agricultural ventures struggle to attract financing.

 

Fund Designed as a Pre-Investment Facility

The Sugar Project Acceleration Fund has been structured as a pre-investment development facility rather than a grant programme.

Its primary objective is to help promising projects reach bankable standards required by investors and development finance institutions.

The programme will provide:

  • Technical advisory support

  • Financial structuring assistance

  • Project development guidance

  • Capacity building for participating businesses

This approach aims to build a strong pipeline of commercially viable sugar ventures capable of scaling production.

 

Roles of Key Institutions

Under the initiative:

  • The Bank of Industry will manage the fund’s operational functions, including credit appraisal, loan disbursement, monitoring, and repayment management.

  • The National Sugar Development Council will provide sector leadership, technical oversight, and industry coordination.

The programme will also prioritise skills development and capacity building to strengthen project execution and long-term sustainability.

 

Early Projects Already Identified

Only companies engaged in sugar-related activities are eligible to participate in the programme.

Greenfield projects already represented in early discussions include:

  • Illaj Sugar

  • Brent Foods

  • Crystal Sugar

  • Legacy Sugar

  • Saro Sugar

  • Awaa

  • Ganic

  • Confluence Sugar

The initiative is expected to accelerate new sugar investments while strengthening Nigeria’s domestic supply chain.

A stronger domestic sugar industry can unlock opportunities for MSMEs in farming, logistics, processing, packaging, and supply-chain services. By supporting structured agribusiness projects, the initiative can also expand participation for smallholder farmers and local suppliers across the agricultural value chain.
UBA Launches “Gen W – The Evolved Woman” Business Series to Empower Female Entrepreneurs
Special edition of UBA Business Series will spotlight women leaders shaping Africa’s business and innovation landscape.

United Bank for Africa will host a special edition of its Business Series themed “Gen W – The Evolved Woman,” bringing together female entrepreneurs and leaders to share insights on leadership, business growth, and opportunity creation. The initiative aims to strengthen the role of women in driving entrepreneurship and economic development across Africa.

United Bank for Africa Plc is set to host a special edition of its UBA Business Series focused on empowering female entrepreneurs and professionals across Africa.

The event, themed “Gen W – The Evolved Woman,” will take place on March 12, 2026, at UBA House in Lagos, bringing together leading women in business, media, and entrepreneurship to share insights on leadership, ambition, and enterprise growth. The session will also be streamed live across UBA’s digital platforms, allowing broader participation from audiences across the continent.

The initiative aims to celebrate a new generation of African women who are not only pursuing opportunities but actively creating and shaping them.

 

Platform for Leadership and Entrepreneurial Growth

The special edition of the Business Series will focus on equipping women with practical strategies for navigating leadership and building resilient enterprises.

Key discussion themes will include:

  • Leadership and personal growth in evolving industries

  • Building sustainable businesses in Africa’s changing economy

  • Authenticity and confidence in professional spaces

  • Strategies for scaling entrepreneurial ventures

Panelists will share personal experiences and insights to help women strengthen their leadership and business capabilities.

 

Notable Speakers and Industry Voices

The event will feature a diverse lineup of accomplished female leaders and professionals from various sectors.

Speakers include:

  • Joycee Awosika – entrepreneur and business leader

  • Tomike Adeoye – entrepreneur and media personality

  • Olufunke Davies – entrepreneur and creative business owner

  • Ayo Mario-Ese – TV Anchor

The conversation will be hosted by media personality and actor Tobi Bakre.

 

Supporting Women as Drivers of Economic Growth

According to Alero Ladipo, the event reflects the bank’s commitment to supporting women as key drivers of economic development across Africa.

She noted that modern African women are increasingly bold, visionary, and intentional about leadership, and initiatives such as the UBA Business Series help create platforms for meaningful conversations around opportunity and enterprise growth.

The Business Series has become a prominent knowledge-sharing platform designed to equip entrepreneurs and professionals with tools for navigating Africa’s evolving business environment.

 

UBA’s Pan-African Reach

With operations across 20 African countries and multiple international markets, United Bank for Africa serves more than 45 million customers globally. The bank continues to promote financial inclusion while supporting entrepreneurial development across the continent.

Women-led businesses represent a significant share of Africa’s MSME sector. Platforms that provide mentorship, knowledge sharing, and networking opportunities can help female entrepreneurs access markets, finance, and partnerships that accelerate business growth.
Dangote Refinery Raises Petrol Price Above ₦1,000 per Litre After Third Increase in One Week
Rapid fuel price adjustments reflect rising supply costs and mounting pressure on Nigeria’s downstream energy market.

Dangote Petroleum Refinery has increased the ex-gantry price of petrol to ₦1,175 per litre, marking its third price adjustment in a week. The rapid increases have pushed retail petrol prices above ₦1,000 nationwide, adding pressure on businesses and consumers as energy costs continue to rise.

The Dangote Petroleum Refinery has increased the ex-gantry price of Premium Motor Spirit (PMS), commonly known as petrol to ₦1,175 per litre, marking the third price adjustment within one week.

The new price, communicated to fuel marketers on Monday, represents a ₦180 increase from the ₦995 per litre announced just days earlier, reflecting an 18.1% rise within three days.

The refinery also adjusted the gantry price of Automotive Gas Oil (diesel) to ₦1,620 per litre, adding further pressure to Nigeria’s energy and transportation costs.

 

Series of Rapid Fuel Price Adjustments

The latest price change follows a series of increases introduced by the refinery over the past week.

Recent pricing changes include:

  • ₦874 per litre – earlier gantry price for petrol

  • ₦995 per litre – revised price announced days later

  • ₦1,175 per litre – latest adjustment announced to marketers

These increases have pushed retail fuel prices above ₦1,000 per litre nationwide, depending on distribution margins and location.

 

Impact on Nigeria’s Downstream Market

The Dangote refinery plays a critical role in Nigeria’s fuel supply chain as the country’s largest domestic refining facility. Price adjustments at the refinery level often ripple through the downstream sector, influencing pump prices across filling stations.

Analysts note that refinery price movements can be influenced by several factors, including:

  • Global crude oil price fluctuations

  • Logistics and distribution costs

  • Foreign exchange pressures

  • Market demand dynamics within the downstream sector

 

Rising Energy Costs for Businesses and Consumers

Higher fuel prices are already affecting transportation, logistics, and operating expenses across the economy.

For many businesses, especially those relying on generators or fuel-powered transportation, petrol and diesel price increases significantly raise the cost of operations.

The rising energy costs also come at a time when businesses and households across Nigeria continue to adjust to the removal of the country’s fuel subsidy regime in 2023.

Fuel price increases directly impact MSMEs through higher transportation, production, and distribution costs. Small businesses in logistics, agriculture, retail, and manufacturing may face tighter margins and may need to adjust pricing strategies to manage rising operating expenses.
Shell Nigeria Gas Partners Anambra Government to Expand Industrial Gas Energy
New collaboration aims to help manufacturers shift from diesel to natural gas to cut costs and improve power reliability.

Shell Nigeria Gas and the Anambra State Government are collaborating to expand natural gas infrastructure for industries. The initiative aims to help manufacturers transition from diesel generators to cleaner and more cost-effective gas energy, improving industrial productivity and strengthening the state’s investment climate.

Shell Nigeria Gas has strengthened its partnership with the Anambra State Government to expand gas-based energy solutions for industries in the state.

The collaboration was highlighted at the Anambra Energy Stakeholders Forum in Awka, where manufacturers, energy executives, and policymakers discussed strategies to transition industries from diesel-powered systems to natural gas.

The initiative is expected to improve power reliability, reduce industrial energy costs, and support Anambra’s ambition to become a major manufacturing hub in Nigeria.

 

Energy Infrastructure Key to Industrialisation

Representing Governor Chukwuma Charles Soludo, Secretary to the State Government Solo Chukwulobelu said the administration has spent the past two years developing strategies to strengthen energy infrastructure.

The broader goal is to power industries and attract investment by ensuring businesses have access to reliable and affordable energy.

According to state officials, improved energy systems are critical to supporting manufacturing growth and long-term economic expansion.

 

Natural Gas Offers Lower-Cost Industrial Power

Industry stakeholders emphasised that switching from diesel generators to natural gas could significantly reduce production costs for manufacturers.

Key benefits of natural gas for industries include:

  • Lower and more stable energy costs

  • Reduced reliance on diesel generators

  • Improved operational efficiency

  • Cleaner and more sustainable energy use

Christian Udechukwu noted that the partnership aligns with Nigeria’s broader gas development strategy while supporting industrial productivity in the state.

 

Gas Infrastructure Expansion Plans

Speaking at the forum, Ralph Gbobo said the company is committed to expanding gas infrastructure to support Nigeria’s industrial transformation.

Shell Nigeria Gas plans to:

  • Extend gas distribution to industrial clusters

  • Provide stable fuel for manufacturing operations

  • Support businesses seeking to reduce energy price volatility

Company executives said reliable gas supply can help manufacturers manage operating costs and improve long-term business sustainability.

 

Strategic Energy Roadmap for Anambra

According to Feyi Fabunmi, many manufacturers currently depend on diesel generators due to unreliable electricity supply.

To address this challenge, Shell Nigeria Gas has delivered a comprehensive gas master plan to the Anambra State Government outlining the infrastructure required to expand gas distribution across the state.

The plan provides a roadmap for delivering energy solutions capable of supporting:

  • Manufacturing plants

  • Industrial boilers

  • Heat generation for production processes

Officials say the initiative could strengthen Anambra’s industrial ecosystem and attract new investments.

Reliable and affordable energy is one of the biggest challenges facing African MSMEs. Expanding access to natural gas can significantly reduce production costs for manufacturers and small industrial businesses, improving competitiveness and enabling local enterprises to scale.
Women Power Africa’s $59 Billion Creative Economy, New Report Finds
Growing youth population, digital platforms, and global demand are accelerating Africa’s creativity-led economic transformation.

Africa’s creative economy is now valued at $59 billion, with women playing a leading role in fashion, design, and digital content industries. With a young population and growing digital connectivity, the sector is expected to become a major driver of exports, entrepreneurship, and economic diversification across the continent.

Africa’s creative industries are emerging as a powerful driver of economic growth, with women playing a central role in innovation, entrepreneurship, and job creation across the sector.

A new report by Boston Consulting Group titled Africa’s Next Growth Frontier: Empowering Women in the Creative Industries estimates the continent’s creative economy—spanning fashion, music, film, design, and digital content—is currently valued at about $59 billion.

The report highlights how Africa’s youthful population and expanding digital connectivity are transforming creative industries into a major engine for economic diversification and global cultural influence.

 

Youth and Digital Connectivity Driving Growth

Africa’s demographic advantage is a key factor behind the sector’s expansion.

Key insights from the report include:

  • Africa has about 890 million people under the age of 25, the world’s youngest population

  • 300–400 million Africans actively use social media platforms

  • This represents around 40% of the continent’s population

These digital platforms allow creators to reach global audiences directly, develop new revenue models, and distribute creative content without traditional gatekeepers.

 

Women Leading Creative Industry Entrepreneurship

Women are playing a dominant role in many segments of Africa’s creative economy, particularly in fashion and design.

According to the report:

  • Africa’s fashion and design industry is valued at about $31 billion

  • Women account for more than 60% of the sector’s workforce

  • In countries such as Kenya and Madagascar, women represent over 80% of fashion industry workers

Experts say women-led creative enterprises are generating jobs, building supply chains, and reinvesting economic value within local communities.

 

Four Forces Accelerating Africa’s Creative Economy

The report identifies four key drivers shaping the continent’s creative growth:

  • Digital acceleration – increased internet access and mobile connectivity

  • Cultural intellectual property (IP) – global demand for African stories, designs, and artistic expression

  • African diaspora influence – more than 200 million people of African descent worldwide boosting demand for African cultural products

  • Demographic growth – a large youth population fueling creativity and consumption

According to Lisa Ivers, Africa’s creative economy offers a unique development model rooted in innovation and cultural identity.

 

Global Opportunity for African Creative Exports

Despite its rapid growth, Africa’s creative sector still accounts for less than 3% of the $2 trillion global creative economy.

However, the report suggests that if Africa doubles its global share to 6% by 2030, the continent’s creative exports could reach $150–160 billion.

This expansion could significantly support economic diversification and reduce reliance on extractive industries.

Creative industries are dominated by small businesses, freelancers, and micro-enterprises. As digital platforms expand global market access, African MSMEs in fashion, film, music, design, and content creation can scale internationally, create jobs, and strengthen local creative ecosystems.
Benin Republic Sees Rising Interest from Nigerian Manufacturers After Offering Tax Incentives
New investment incentives and simplified company registration are drawing early interest from Nigerian businesses.

Benin Republic is attracting early interest from Nigerian manufacturers after launching a campaign offering tax incentives and simplified business registration. With industrial zones near the Nigerian border and growing investment cooperation between the two countries, Benin aims to position itself as a competitive manufacturing base for businesses serving West Africa’s largest market.

The Agency for the Promotion of Investments and Exports says Nigerian manufacturers have begun exploring investment opportunities in Benin Republic, following the country’s recent campaign offering tax incentives and simplified business registration.

According to Eric Akoute, the agency has started receiving enquiries from Nigerian businesses interested in establishing manufacturing operations in Benin.

The interest comes shortly after the country promoted its investment incentives at the Benin–Nigeria Business Forum in Lagos, where officials highlighted benefits such as tax exemptions, lower labour costs, and faster cargo clearance processes.

 

Tax Incentives and Investment Benefits

Benin’s investment promotion strategy is built around its Investment Code, which offers generous incentives to attract new businesses.

Key benefits include:

  • Corporate Income Tax (CIT) exemptions

  • Value Added Tax (VAT) exemptions

  • Customs duty waivers

  • Tax incentives for up to 17 years on new investments

Officials say these incentives aim to position Benin as a lower-cost manufacturing base for companies targeting the Nigerian market.

 

Strategic Industrial Zone Near Nigeria

A central element of the investment pitch is an industrial zone located about nine kilometres from the Nigerian border.

The zone is designed to help manufacturers:

  • Produce goods close to Nigeria’s large consumer market

  • Reduce operational costs

  • Avoid some regulatory and logistics bottlenecks experienced in Nigeria

The initiative could allow companies to maintain market access while benefiting from a streamlined business environment.

 

Growing Cross-Border Investment Cooperation

Benin is also strengthening institutional collaboration with Nigerian agencies to promote cross-border investment.

Notable developments include:

  • A Memorandum of Understanding signed in January between Nigerian Investment Promotion Commission and APiEx

  • Plans to establish an investment information desk in Abuja to guide Nigerian investors

  • Ongoing discussions about creating a cross-border business facilitation centre

The Badagry Chamber of Commerce recently visited Benin’s industrial zones to explore collaboration opportunities.

 

Transport and Logistics Connectivity Plans

Officials are also exploring ways to improve transport links between the two countries.

Proposals under discussion include:

  • Direct flights between Cotonou and Lagos

  • Partnerships with airlines such as Air Peace and Ibom Air

  • Greater use of the Port of Cotonou as a logistics gateway

These improvements aim to strengthen trade flows and facilitate investment between the neighbouring economies.

Regional investment incentives and cross-border industrial zones create new opportunities for MSMEs to expand production, access new markets, and optimise operating costs. For Nigerian businesses, nearby manufacturing hubs could enable export-oriented growth and stronger participation in regional value chains.
Kigali Hosts Global Fintech Leaders to Advance Inclusive Finance
Inclusive FinTech Forum 2026 will bring together policymakers, innovators, and investors to accelerate financial inclusion across emerging markets.

Kigali will host the Inclusive FinTech Forum 2026, bringing together more than 3,000 global leaders to accelerate financial inclusion and fintech innovation across emerging markets. The forum highlights Africa’s growing role in digital finance and aims to connect policy, technology, and investment to expand access to financial services.

Kigali is set to host the third edition of the Inclusive FinTech Forum from March 10–12, bringing together more than 3,000 leaders from finance, technology, policy, and investment.

The event will take place at the Kigali Convention Centre and aims to move the global conversation on financial inclusion from ambition to implementation.

Hosted in partnership with the Kigali International Financial Centre, the National Bank of Rwanda, and the Global Finance & Technology Network, the forum focuses on aligning policy, technology, and capital to build inclusive financial systems.

 

Africa’s Fintech Boom Driving Global Attention

Africa’s fintech ecosystem has experienced rapid growth in recent years, becoming one of the continent’s most dynamic technology sectors.

Despite this progress:

  • Nearly 300 million adults in Africa remain unbanked

  • Mobile connectivity and digital platforms are expanding access to financial services

  • According to McKinsey & Company, Africa’s fintech revenues could grow from about $10 billion in 2023 to $47 billion by 2028

Experts say this expansion is creating new opportunities for digital payments, lending platforms, investment services, and financial infrastructure across emerging markets.

 

Key Themes Shaping the Future of Inclusive Finance

The 2026 forum will focus on the theme “Shaping the Future of Inclusive Finance: Innovation. Impact. Connection.”

Discussions will explore several emerging trends transforming financial services:

  • Development of digital currency corridors across Africa

  • Use of artificial intelligence to expand financial inclusion

  • Growth of open finance ecosystems

  • Rise of climate fintech solutions supporting sustainable investment

These topics reflect the growing convergence of technology, policy frameworks, and capital markets.

 

Rwanda’s Ambition as a Financial Innovation Hub

Rwanda’s hosting of the forum highlights the country’s broader ambition to become a regional centre for financial innovation.

According to Hortense Mudenge, the forum serves as a platform where investors, policymakers, and innovators collaborate to build scalable financial systems.

Meanwhile, Soraya Munyana Hakuziyaremye emphasised the need for balanced regulation that encourages innovation while safeguarding financial stability and consumer protection.

 

From Dialogue to Action

The forum is designed as a decision-oriented platform focused on measurable outcomes.

Key activities will include:

  • Policy dialogue sessions with regulators

  • Investor forums connecting startups with capital

  • Startup pitch competitions

  • Networking sessions linking fintech innovators with global partners

The previous edition attracted over 1,100 senior executives, 450 fintech companies, 120 investment firms, and representatives from 20 central banks, demonstrating the event’s growing influence.

Fintech innovation expands access to payments, credit, and digital financial services, critical tools for MSMEs across Africa. Improved financial inclusion can help small businesses access capital, reach customers digitally, and participate more effectively in regional and global markets.
Baobab MFB Launches Green Finance Project to Expand Solar Access for Lagos SMEs
New initiative will train renewable energy finance advisors to help small businesses adopt reliable solar power solutions.

Baobab Microfinance Bank Nigeria has launched a Green Finance Project to help Lagos SMEs adopt solar energy through professional advisory support. By training Green Energy and Finance Consultants, the initiative aims to improve access to reliable renewable energy while ensuring solar investments are technically suitable and financially sustainable for small businesses.

Baobab Microfinance Bank Nigeria has launched a Green Finance Project aimed at expanding access to solar energy for households and small businesses in Lagos as rising fuel costs and unreliable electricity continue to disrupt productivity.

The initiative focuses on building a new network of trained professionals who can guide small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) toward affordable and technically suitable renewable energy solutions.

With many businesses increasingly turning to solar power due to grid instability and expensive diesel and petrol generators, the programme seeks to improve the quality of solar adoption by ensuring businesses receive credible technical and financial advice before investing in solar systems.

 

Addressing Lagos’ Energy Challenges

Small businesses in Lagos face growing pressure from rising energy costs and unreliable electricity supply.

Solar energy is becoming a practical alternative, but adoption has often been hindered by limited guidance on system design and financing.

Common challenges include:

  • Solar systems that are poorly matched to business energy needs

  • Limited understanding of solar financing options

  • Lack of trained advisors capable of combining technical and financial expertise

The Green Finance Project aims to close this gap by training professionals who can provide independent, well-informed energy advisory services.

 

Training Green Energy and Finance Consultants

A key component of the initiative is the development of Green Energy and Finance Consultants who will support businesses and households in transitioning to renewable energy.

The training programme will equip participants with:

  • Technical knowledge of solar energy systems

  • Financial analysis skills to evaluate affordability and return on investment

  • Practical understanding of energy needs for Lagos-based SMEs

  • Advisory capabilities to recommend sustainable and scalable solar solutions

According to the bank, reliable solar adoption begins with accurate advice and realistic financial planning, rather than simply selling equipment.

 

Building Confidence in Renewable Energy Solutions

The programme is also designed to strengthen trust in the renewable energy market by encouraging long-term, sustainable installations rather than short-term sales-driven solutions.

The project has entered an active recruitment phase, with selected consultants receiving hands-on training focused on the realities of Lagos’ business and energy environment.

The initiative is supported by the European Investment Bank through its Financial Inclusion Fund, reflecting broader efforts to expand sustainable energy access and financial inclusion.

Reliable and affordable energy is critical for productivity, especially for small businesses operating in environments with unstable electricity supply. By improving access to solar advisory and financing solutions, initiatives like this can help MSMEs reduce operating costs, improve resilience, and transition toward cleaner energy systems.
Dangote Refinery Raises Petrol Ex-Gantry Price to N1,175 as Pump Price May Approach N1,500
Latest price adjustment marks the third increase in one week, raising fears that petrol pump prices across Nigeria could climb toward N1,500 per litre amid rising global oil prices.

Petrol prices in Nigeria may soon approach N1,500 per litre after the Dangote Petroleum Refinery increased its ex-gantry price to N1,175 per litre, the third adjustment within a week. Industry stakeholders warn that rising global oil prices and higher depot costs could push pump prices significantly higher, prompting calls for government intervention to improve crude oil supply to the refinery and ease pressure on consumers.

The pump price of Premium Motor Spirit (PMS), commonly known as petrol, could rise to about N1,500 per litre in the coming days following another upward adjustment in the ex-gantry price by the Dangote Petroleum Refinery.

The refinery recently increased its ex-gantry price of petrol by N180 to N1,175 per litre, marking the third price adjustment within a week and raising concerns about a potential surge in retail pump prices across the country.

The development was confirmed on Monday by Abubakar Garima, National President of the Independent Petroleum Marketers Association of Nigeria (IPMAN), who warned that the steady increase in the refinery’s pricing could soon translate to significantly higher prices for consumers at filling stations.

Garima explained that the refinery had earlier adjusted its petrol price on March 7, increasing the ex-gantry rate to N995 per litre from N874 per litre, a move that had already triggered price adjustments among independent marketers and private depot operators.

With the latest increase to N1,175 per litre, industry stakeholders say the cost of transporting, distributing, and retailing the product may push the final pump price close to N1,500 per litre in some parts of the country.

“This is the third increase within a short period, and it will inevitably affect the retail price of petrol nationwide,” Garima said. “If the current trend continues, Nigerians may soon be paying close to N1,500 per litre at filling stations.”

He also revealed that the refinery has adjusted its ex-gantry diesel price to N1,620 per litre, reflecting broader cost pressures within the downstream petroleum market.

According to Garima, petrol prices at private depots are already hovering around N1,200 per litre, indicating that market prices have begun reacting to the refinery’s latest adjustments.

Industry analysts note that the Dangote refinery, being the largest single supplier of refined petroleum products in the country, plays a significant role in shaping market prices, especially as Nigeria continues its transition from heavy dependence on imported fuel to locally refined products.

Garima called on the Federal Government to take urgent steps to stabilise the situation by improving the refinery’s access to crude oil, particularly through local supply arrangements.

“We appeal to President Bola Tinubu to intervene and ensure the refinery receives increased access to crude oil,” he said. “If crude supply improves, production costs may reduce and that could help moderate petrol prices for Nigerians.”

He noted that access to affordable crude oil remains a critical factor affecting the cost of refined petroleum products, particularly as international crude prices continue to fluctuate.

Meanwhile, David Bird, Chief Executive Officer of the Dangote Petroleum Refinery, disclosed that the company had temporarily paused petrol loadings at its gantry system to update its pricing structure following a sharp spike in global oil prices.

Speaking on the development, Bird explained that the refinery had to halt loadings at midnight in order to reflect the new pricing structure in its system.

“There is a gantry price movement, so we paused loadings as of midnight and are updating the system to the new gantry price following a spike in oil prices,” Bird said.

He added that the refinery remains fully operational and that fuel distribution will resume once the system update is completed.

“There is a new gantry price, and loadings will commence immediately after the system upload. Dangote Refinery remains fully open for business,” he stated.

Market watchers say the recent price adjustments are closely linked to developments in the global crude oil market, where prices have surged due to rising geopolitical tensions.

The ongoing conflict involving the United States, Iran, and Israel has contributed to volatility in global oil supply expectations, pushing crude prices higher within a short period.

Energy analysts report that crude oil prices increased significantly within the past 24 hours, forcing refiners and fuel suppliers around the world to review their pricing structures.

Because petrol production costs are directly tied to crude oil prices, increases in international oil benchmarks often translate into higher fuel prices for consumers.

For Nigeria, which recently removed petrol subsidies and transitioned to a market-driven pricing system, fluctuations in global oil prices now have a more direct impact on the retail price of fuel.

Economic experts warn that a potential increase in petrol prices to around N1,500 per litre could further raise transportation costs, increase the cost of goods and services, and place additional pressure on households already dealing with inflation.

Small businesses, logistics operators, and transport unions are also expected to feel the impact, as fuel costs remain one of the largest operating expenses across many sectors of the Nigerian economy.

Despite these concerns, industry stakeholders maintain that strengthening domestic refining capacity remains a long-term solution to stabilizing fuel supply and reducing reliance on imports.

The 650,000-barrel-per-day Dangote Petroleum Refinery, located in Lagos, is currently Africa’s largest refinery and is expected to play a central role in transforming Nigeria’s downstream petroleum sector.

However, experts note that stable crude supply, efficient distribution systems, and market transparency will be key factors in determining whether local refining can ultimately lead to more stable fuel prices for Nigerian consumers.

Rising operating costs: Higher petrol prices will increase transportation, logistics, and power generation costs for many MSMEs that rely on fuel for deliveries, generators, and daily operations.

Higher cost of goods and services: As fuel costs rise, suppliers and transporters may increase prices, which could push MSMEs to adjust their pricing or absorb reduced profit margins.
