Pressure on supply chains: Businesses that depend on frequent movement of goods—such as retail, agriculture, and manufacturing—may face disruptions or increased distribution expenses.

Impact on consumer spending: Higher fuel costs can reduce disposable income for households, potentially lowering demand for non-essential products and services offered by MSMEs.
Ebonyi’s Rice Boom Creating New Opportunities for Nigerian MSMEs Across the Agricultural Value Chain
From smallholder farmers to food traders and mill operators, Ebonyi’s expanding rice industry is driving jobs, entrepreneurship, and local supply chains across Nigeria.

Ebonyi State’s expanding rice industry is creating significant opportunities for Nigerian MSMEs across the agricultural value chain, from farming and milling to packaging, distribution, and retail. Government support, improved processing, and rising consumer preference for locally produced rice have helped transform the sector into a major source of jobs and entrepreneurship. However, stakeholders say addressing challenges such as infrastructure, storage, power supply, and rising input costs will be critical to sustaining growth and strengthening Nigeria’s food security.

The growing success of rice production in Ebonyi State is creating significant opportunities for Nigerian micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs), as the state emerges as one of the country’s most important hubs for locally produced rice.

Often referred to as the “Salt of the Nation,” Ebonyi is endowed with fertile plains suitable for large-scale cultivation of rice and other agricultural products. For decades, rice farming in communities such as Abakaliki, Izzi, Ezza, and Ikwo was largely manual and focused mainly on subsistence farming.

However, increasing national efforts to achieve food self-sufficiency and reduce reliance on imported rice have transformed the sector. Today, Ebonyi’s rice industry is not only supporting food security but also fueling entrepreneurship, job creation, and new business opportunities across Nigeria’s agricultural value chain.

Beyond Ebonyi, other notable rice-producing states in Nigeria include Kebbi, Jigawa, Kano, Ekiti, Benue, Kaduna, Niger, Cross River, and Ogun. Yet Ebonyi has increasingly become a standout example of how local agriculture can stimulate MSME growth.

What was once a small-scale rural activity has evolved into a thriving ecosystem involving farmers, millers, transporters, food traders, packaging businesses, and agricultural input suppliers.

Agriculture stakeholders say Ebonyi rice has transitioned from being a little-known local grain into a nationally recognized product that is now available in major markets and supermarkets in cities such as Lagos, Abuja, Enugu, Port Harcourt, and Asaba.

Unlike in the past, when locally milled rice was often criticised for containing stones and chaff, improved processing methods have transformed its quality and market perception.

Today, Ebonyi rice is increasingly sold in well-packaged branded bags labeled “Made in Nigeria,” reflecting the growing maturity of Nigeria’s domestic agricultural industry and the expanding role of MSMEs in food processing and distribution.

Industry observers say the transformation did not occur overnight. For many years, rice farmers in the state faced multiple challenges, including limited government support, poor access to modern farming technology, and high post-harvest losses that reduced profitability.

Many farmers were also dependent on middlemen who purchased their harvests at low prices, limiting the economic benefits that local producers could capture.

However, the narrative began to shift around 2015, when federal policies aimed at boosting local rice production and restricting rice imports created stronger demand for locally produced rice.

These policies helped stimulate investment across the agricultural value chain and encouraged more Nigerians to explore agribusiness opportunities.

Today, Ebonyi rice represents more than just a staple food; it has become a symbol of local enterprise and a growing source of income for thousands of small-scale entrepreneurs.

The Ebonyi State Government, led by Governor Francis Nwifuru, has declared an ambition to position the state as Nigeria’s rice capital, with the goal of attracting agricultural investment, increasing internally generated revenue, and creating jobs for the state’s growing youth population.

According to the State Ministry of Agriculture and Natural Resources, rice production has already generated more than 20,000 jobs across the value chain, including planting, harvesting, milling, transportation, packaging, and distribution.

The Commissioner for Agriculture and Natural Resources, Mrs Nkechinyere Iyioku, said the government remains committed to expanding rice production and encouraging young Nigerians to pursue careers in agriculture and agribusiness.

She noted that the state government, through the Ministry of Human Capital Development, has trained hundreds of young people in agribusiness areas such as rice production, poultry farming, fisheries, and cassava processing.

Beneficiaries of the programme also received startup support of up to N3 million each, enabling them to launch small agricultural enterprises and contribute to the growing food economy.

For many entrepreneurs, the rice sector has become a viable entry point into Nigeria’s agricultural MSME ecosystem.

Mr Linus Obeji, Executive Chairman of the Rice Mill Industry in Abakaliki, attributed the state’s progress to a combination of government support and farmer resilience.

According to him, federal initiatives such as the Anchor Borrowers’ Programme, alongside policies introduced by successive administrations, have provided smallholder farmers with access to loans, improved seedlings, and fertilizers.

He added that the Ebonyi government has also invested in modernizing rice mills, providing tractors, and strengthening farmer cooperatives, measures that have significantly increased production capacity.

“Ebonyi now produces hundreds of thousands of metric tonnes of rice annually,” Obeji said. “The state is feeding not only its own population but also supplying markets across Nigeria.”

Improved processing technology has also played a crucial role in enhancing consumer confidence in local rice.

Obeji explained that rice mills in the state now follow strict quality control procedures, including de-stoning, sorting, polishing, and proper packaging, ensuring the product meets modern consumer expectations.

“Ebonyi rice is known for its natural aroma, absence of chemical preservatives, and high nutritional value,” he said. “With modern processing, it now competes favorably with imported brands.”

For Nigerian MSMEs, the growth of Ebonyi’s rice sector is creating opportunities far beyond farming.

Food traders, distributors, packaging companies, logistics providers, and digital marketplaces are increasingly benefiting from the rising demand for locally produced rice.

Mr Mathew Nwoko, a rice dealer based in Port Harcourt, said Ebonyi rice has gained strong acceptance in markets across the country.

“In markets from Port Harcourt to Owerri, Asaba, and Lagos, Ebonyi rice is becoming a household name,” he said. “Consumers appreciate the quality and also like the idea of supporting local farmers.”

Similarly, Mrs Adaku Chukwubike, a Lagos-based food trader, said demand for local rice has grown significantly in her store.

“Since I started selling Ebonyi rice, customer demand has increased,” she said. “Many customers now specifically request locally produced rice.”

For young agripreneurs, the industry is also providing pathways to financial independence.

Mr Eze Nwube, a rice mill operator in Onueke, Ezza-South Local Government Area, said the business has transformed his life.

“People once mocked me for choosing farming,” he said. “But today I supply rice to supermarkets and online buyers.”

Despite the progress, experts say several challenges still threaten the sector’s growth.

These include poor road infrastructure, inadequate storage facilities, erratic electricity supply, insecurity in some farming areas, and climate change–related flooding.

Agricultural economist Mrs Grace Nwafor emphasized the need for greater investment in modern farming technology, irrigation systems, and improved seed varieties to help Nigerian farmers compete globally.

She also called for stronger youth-focused agricultural training programs that would prepare the next generation of agribusiness entrepreneurs.

Similarly, Dr Ignatius Unah, Chairman of the All Farmers Association of Nigeria (AFAN) in Ebonyi State, said rising input costs remain a major concern for many farmers.

He urged federal and state governments to expand subsidies on fertilizers and agrochemicals while strengthening access to mechanized farming equipment.

For farmers like Mr Nwokpor Nicholas, however, rice farming has already become a life-changing opportunity.

“Rice farming now supports our families,” he said. “Many farmers can send their children to school because of the income from rice.”

Recent market trends also suggest that increased local production may be helping to stabilize rice prices.

According to data from December 2025, the price of a 50kg bag of rice fell to between N30,000 and N45,000, compared with N65,000 to N90,000 recorded earlier in the year.

Stakeholders believe that if infrastructure, storage capacity, and mechanization continue to improve, Ebonyi’s rice industry could play a major role in strengthening Nigeria’s food security while expanding opportunities for MSMEs across the agricultural economy.

Expanding agribusiness opportunities: The growth of Ebonyi’s rice industry is opening up new opportunities for MSMEs in farming, milling, packaging, logistics, food retail, and agricultural inputs across the value chain.

Stronger local supply chains: Increased local rice production reduces dependence on imports and strengthens domestic supply chains, allowing small businesses to source products locally and operate more sustainably.

Job creation and youth entrepreneurship: Government-backed training and startup support in agribusiness are enabling more young entrepreneurs to launch small agricultural enterprises and participate in Nigeria’s growing food economy.

Market opportunities for food traders: Rising consumer preference for locally produced rice is creating new demand for retailers, wholesalers, and distributors, especially in major urban markets.

Potential for export and regional trade: As production capacity improves, locally produced rice could open new opportunities for MSMEs to participate in regional food trade across Africa.
Oyo State Launches 2026 Farming Season with 50% Tractor Subsidy for Farmers
New mechanisation subsidy and climate advisory aim to improve productivity and reduce farming risks.

Oyo State has launched the 2026 farming season with a 50% tractor subsidy to expand mechanised agriculture and improve productivity. The government is also encouraging farmers to rely on seasonal climate forecasts from NiMet to guide planting decisions and reduce weather-related farming risks.

The Oyo State government has officially launched the 2026 farming season, introducing a 50 percent tractorisation subsidy to help farmers expand mechanised agriculture and improve productivity.

Announcing the initiative in Ibadan, the Commissioner for Agriculture and Rural Development, Olasunkanmi Olaleye, said the programme will also encourage farmers to rely on seasonal weather forecasts from the Nigerian Meteorological Agency to guide planting decisions.

The combined approach improving access to farm equipment and strengthening climate-informed farming, aims to increase crop yields while reducing the risks associated with changing weather patterns.

 

50% Tractor Subsidy to Expand Farm Mechanisation

A central component of the programme is the tractor subsidy scheme, designed to make mechanised farming more affordable for farmers.

Key objectives include:

  • Reducing the cost of tractor services by 50 percent

  • Expanding mechanised farming among smallholder farmers

  • Increasing farm productivity and efficiency

  • Supporting the state’s broader food security strategy

Officials say many farmers struggle to access mechanised equipment due to high costs, limiting their production capacity.

 

Using Weather Forecasts to Guide Planting Decisions

Farmers were also advised to integrate climate and rainfall forecasts into their agricultural planning.

Experts from NiMet presented rainfall projections during the launch event and encouraged farmers to adopt climate-smart farming practices.

Recommendations include:

  • Monitoring seasonal rainfall forecasts before planting

  • Using drought-resistant crop varieties

  • Aligning planting schedules with stable rainfall periods

Agricultural experts also warned farmers not to plant prematurely due to early rainfall in some areas.

According to NiMet representatives, planting should ideally begin toward the end of April, when rainfall patterns are expected to stabilise.

 

Strengthening Agricultural Planning and Training

The launch event also included a sensitisation programme designed to help farmers and stakeholders understand how to apply seasonal climate predictions in their operations.

According to Abosede Owoeye, the initiative is aimed at improving decision-making across the agricultural value chain.

Participants included:

  • Farmers and agribusiness operators

  • Agricultural extension officers

  • Financial institutions supporting agricultural finance

  • Policymakers and sector stakeholders

The programme seeks to ensure that agricultural planning aligns with climate conditions and modern farming practices.

Agriculture remains one of the largest sectors for MSMEs across Africa. Mechanisation subsidies and climate-informed farming support can help smallholder farmers increase yields, reduce production risks, and strengthen agribusiness supply chains that feed local markets and agro-processing industries.
Why AI Is Becoming a Critical Tool for MSMEs in Nigeria and Africa   (Opinion Ed)
As digital tools become more accessible, AI is emerging as a practical growth engine for small businesses across the continent.

Artificial intelligence is rapidly becoming a practical tool for small and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs) across Nigeria and Africa. From automating customer communication and marketing to improving financial tracking and data analysis, AI is helping entrepreneurs operate more efficiently and make smarter decisions. As digital tools become more accessible, businesses that adopt AI early are likely to gain a competitive advantage in an increasingly technology-driven marketplace.

Why AI Is Becoming a Critical Tool for MSMEs in Nigeria and Africa


Across Nigeria and much of Africa, small and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs) have always relied on adaptability to survive and grow. From navigating currency fluctuations to managing unpredictable supply chains, entrepreneurs often operate with limited resources but high resilience. Now, a new tool is beginning to shape how these businesses compete and scale: artificial intelligence (AI).

Once viewed as a technology reserved for large corporations and global tech companies, AI is gradually becoming a practical resource for entrepreneurs across sectors—from retail and logistics to agriculture and creative industries. For many MSMEs in Nigeria and across Africa, AI is shifting from an abstract concept to a strategic business tool.

A New Layer of Business Productivity

For years, discussions about AI were mostly confined to innovation forums and technology conferences. Today, however, AI is embedded in many of the digital tools entrepreneurs already use.

Email platforms now suggest responses, accounting software categorises expenses automatically, and marketing platforms analyse customer behaviour. Instead of being a complex technical project, AI is increasingly functioning as a productivity layer that helps businesses operate more efficiently.

For MSMEs that often operate with lean teams, this shift is significant. According to Nigeria’s Small and Medium Enterprises Development Agency (SMEDAN), MSMEs account for more than 96% of businesses in Nigeria and contribute nearly 48% of the country’s GDP. Many of these businesses are run by small teams where the owner handles everything from marketing to financial tracking.

AI-driven tools can help reduce that operational burden.

How African MSMEs Are Already Using AI

In Nigeria, several small businesses have begun integrating AI-powered tools into their daily operations.

Retail entrepreneurs who sell on platforms like Jumia and Instagram increasingly use AI-driven marketing tools to generate product descriptions, social media content, and targeted advertising campaigns. This allows them to maintain a consistent online presence without hiring dedicated marketing teams.

Customer communication is another area where AI is proving useful. Many small businesses now use automated messaging systems on WhatsApp Business to respond to frequently asked questions, confirm orders, and provide product information instantly. For businesses that rely heavily on social commerce, faster response times often translate into higher sales conversions.

Across Africa, similar trends are emerging. In Kenya, logistics startup Sendy has explored data-driven technologies to optimise delivery routes and improve operational efficiency. In agriculture, platforms like Twiga Foods in Kenya use data analytics and digital tools to streamline food distribution between farmers and urban retailers.

In South Africa, small retailers increasingly rely on AI-enabled financial software to manage bookkeeping and monitor cash flow—an area where many small businesses traditionally struggle.

These examples illustrate a broader shift: AI is gradually becoming accessible to smaller businesses, not just large enterprises.

Improving Decision-Making for Small Businesses

Beyond automation, one of the most significant advantages of AI for MSMEs lies in data analysis.

Historically, detailed business analytics were tools primarily used by large corporations with dedicated research teams. Today, AI-powered platforms can analyse sales trends, customer preferences, and purchasing patterns within minutes.

For a small fashion brand in Lagos, this could mean identifying which products sell best during certain seasons. For a small food processing business in Ghana, it might mean analysing customer demand to determine which products should be produced in larger quantities.

These insights allow entrepreneurs to move from reactive decision-making to more strategic planning.

A Competitive Advantage for Early Adopters

For many MSMEs, the biggest challenge is not just running a business but keeping pace with competitors. Businesses that adopt digital tools, including AI, often gain an operational advantage.

They respond to customers faster, analyse market trends earlier, and streamline internal processes more effectively. Over time, these incremental improvements can translate into stronger customer relationships and more sustainable growth.

Business analysts increasingly see AI as part of the next wave of digital transformation for African businesses—similar to how mobile payments reshaped commerce across the continent.

In Nigeria, the rapid adoption of platforms like Flutterwave, Paystack, and OPay showed how quickly technology can reshape everyday business operations. AI may follow a similar trajectory.

Technology Still Needs Human Vision

Despite the growing influence of AI, experts emphasise that the technology does not replace entrepreneurial leadership.

Business vision, relationship-building, and understanding customer needs remain fundamentally human capabilities. AI simply acts as a support system, handling repetitive tasks and analysing data so entrepreneurs can focus on strategy and growth.

For many MSME founders, this balance is critical. Technology may enhance productivity, but the direction of the business still depends on the entrepreneur.

Starting Small

For small business owners curious about AI, experts often recommend a gradual approach.

Instead of attempting to overhaul all operations at once, entrepreneurs can begin with one area that consumes significant time. This might include automating customer inquiries, improving bookkeeping, or generating marketing content.

Even modest improvements—such as saving a few hours of administrative work each week, can free up valuable time for product development, customer engagement, or market expansion.

The Next Phase of Business Transformation

African entrepreneurship has always evolved alongside technology. Businesses that embraced mobile phones, digital payments, and social media early gained significant advantages in the past decade.

Now, artificial intelligence appears to represent the next stage of that transformation.

For Nigeria and Africa’s millions of MSMEs, the real opportunity lies not in viewing AI as a futuristic concept, but as a practical tool for improving efficiency, strengthening decision-making, and unlocking new growth opportunities in an increasingly competitive marketplace.

Across Nigeria and much of Africa, small and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs) have always relied on adaptability to survive and grow. From navigating currency fluctuations to managing unpredictable supply chains, entrepreneurs often operate with limited resources but high resilience. Now, a new tool is beginning to shape how these businesses compete and scale: artificial intelligence (AI).

Once viewed as a technology reserved for large corporations and global tech companies, AI is gradually becoming a practical resource for entrepreneurs across sectors—from retail and logistics to agriculture and creative industries. For many MSMEs in Nigeria and across Africa, AI is shifting from an abstract concept to a strategic business tool.

A New Layer of Business Productivity

For years, discussions about AI were mostly confined to innovation forums and technology conferences. Today, however, AI is embedded in many of the digital tools entrepreneurs already use.

Email platforms now suggest responses, accounting software categorises expenses automatically, and marketing platforms analyse customer behaviour. Instead of being a complex technical project, AI is increasingly functioning as a productivity layer that helps businesses operate more efficiently.

For MSMEs that often operate with lean teams, this shift is significant. According to Nigeria’s Small and Medium Enterprises Development Agency (SMEDAN), MSMEs account for more than 96% of businesses in Nigeria and contribute nearly 48% of the country’s GDP. Many of these businesses are run by small teams where the owner handles everything from marketing to financial tracking.

AI-driven tools can help reduce that operational burden.

How African MSMEs Are Already Using AI

In Nigeria, several small businesses have begun integrating AI-powered tools into their daily operations.

Retail entrepreneurs who sell on platforms like Jumia and Instagram increasingly use AI-driven marketing tools to generate product descriptions, social media content, and targeted advertising campaigns. This allows them to maintain a consistent online presence without hiring dedicated marketing teams.

Customer communication is another area where AI is proving useful. Many small businesses now use automated messaging systems on WhatsApp Business to respond to frequently asked questions, confirm orders, and provide product information instantly. For businesses that rely heavily on social commerce, faster response times often translate into higher sales conversions.

Across Africa, similar trends are emerging. In Kenya, logistics startup Sendy has explored data-driven technologies to optimise delivery routes and improve operational efficiency. In agriculture, platforms like Twiga Foods in Kenya use data analytics and digital tools to streamline food distribution between farmers and urban retailers.

In South Africa, small retailers increasingly rely on AI-enabled financial software to manage bookkeeping and monitor cash flow—an area where many small businesses traditionally struggle.

These examples illustrate a broader shift: AI is gradually becoming accessible to smaller businesses, not just large enterprises.

Improving Decision-Making for Small Businesses

Beyond automation, one of the most significant advantages of AI for MSMEs lies in data analysis.

Historically, detailed business analytics were tools primarily used by large corporations with dedicated research teams. Today, AI-powered platforms can analyse sales trends, customer preferences, and purchasing patterns within minutes.

For a small fashion brand in Lagos, this could mean identifying which products sell best during certain seasons. For a small food processing business in Ghana, it might mean analysing customer demand to determine which products should be produced in larger quantities.

These insights allow entrepreneurs to move from reactive decision-making to more strategic planning.

A Competitive Advantage for Early Adopters

For many MSMEs, the biggest challenge is not just running a business but keeping pace with competitors. Businesses that adopt digital tools, including AI, often gain an operational advantage.

They respond to customers faster, analyse market trends earlier, and streamline internal processes more effectively. Over time, these incremental improvements can translate into stronger customer relationships and more sustainable growth.

Business analysts increasingly see AI as part of the next wave of digital transformation for African businesses—similar to how mobile payments reshaped commerce across the continent.

In Nigeria, the rapid adoption of platforms like Flutterwave, Paystack, and OPay showed how quickly technology can reshape everyday business operations. AI may follow a similar trajectory.

Technology Still Needs Human Vision

Despite the growing influence of AI, experts emphasise that the technology does not replace entrepreneurial leadership.

Business vision, relationship-building, and understanding customer needs remain fundamentally human capabilities. AI simply acts as a support system—handling repetitive tasks and analysing data so entrepreneurs can focus on strategy and growth.

For many MSME founders, this balance is critical. Technology may enhance productivity, but the direction of the business still depends on the entrepreneur.

Starting Small

For small business owners curious about AI, experts often recommend a gradual approach.

Instead of attempting to overhaul all operations at once, entrepreneurs can begin with one area that consumes significant time. This might include automating customer inquiries, improving bookkeeping, or generating marketing content.

Even modest improvements such as saving a few hours of administrative work each week can free up valuable time for product development, customer engagement, or market expansion.

The Next Phase of Business Transformation

African entrepreneurship has always evolved alongside technology. Businesses that embraced mobile phones, digital payments, and social media early gained significant advantages in the past decade.

Now, artificial intelligence appears to represent the next stage of that transformation.

For Nigeria and Africa’s millions of MSMEs, the real opportunity lies not in viewing AI as a futuristic concept, but as a practical tool for improving efficiency, strengthening decision-making, and unlocking new growth opportunities in an increasingly competitive marketplace.

Artificial intelligence can help African MSMEs operate more efficiently by automating routine tasks, improving customer engagement, and providing insights that support better business decisions. For small businesses that often operate with limited staff and resources, AI tools can reduce operational costs and free up time for entrepreneurs to focus on growth.
As digital competition increases across African markets, MSMEs that adopt AI early are likely to gain a stronger competitive advantage, scale faster, and access new opportunities in the continent’s growing digital economy.
Nigeria Launches $500 Million SPIN Project to Boost Irrigation, Food Production, and Hydropower
World Bank-backed initiative aims to modernise irrigation systems, strengthen dam safety, and improve agricultural productivity.

Nigeria has launched the $500 million World Bank-funded SPIN Project to modernise irrigation systems, improve dam safety, and increase hydropower generation. The initiative aims to strengthen food production, enhance climate resilience, and support millions of farmers while boosting the country’s agricultural and energy infrastructure.

The Federal Government of Nigeria has launched the $500 million Sustainable Power and Irrigation for Nigeria (SPIN) Project, a major initiative funded by the World Bank to strengthen irrigation systems, improve dam safety, and boost agricultural production.

The project was officially flagged off in Abuja by Joseph Utsev, who said the initiative represents a critical step toward improving food security, energy generation, and climate resilience.

Officials say the SPIN project will modernise irrigation infrastructure, enhance water governance, and support millions of farmers while also improving hydropower generation across the country.

 

Strategic Investment in Food and Energy Security

The SPIN initiative is designed to strengthen Nigeria’s agricultural and energy infrastructure through improved water management.

Key objectives include:

  • Expanding modern irrigation systems to support year-round farming

  • Improving dam operations and safety across the country

  • Increasing hydropower generation capacity

  • Strengthening institutional capacity for water resources management

According to government officials, the project supports Nigeria’s strategy to move beyond reliance on rain-fed agriculture and adopt climate-resilient farming systems.

 

Four Core Components of the SPIN Project

The programme will be implemented through four major components:

  • Institutional strengthening and capacity building for water resources management

  • Irrigation modernisation to improve agricultural productivity

  • Dam safety and operational improvements

  • Project coordination and governance systems

These components aim to ensure that water infrastructure can support both agricultural expansion and reliable power generation.

 

Building on Previous Irrigation Reforms

The SPIN project builds on the earlier Transforming Irrigation Management in Nigeria programme, which focused on improving irrigation management and agricultural productivity.

According to Mathew Verghis, the new initiative will rehabilitate about 14,000 hectares of irrigated farmland while also attracting private investment in irrigation development.

Meanwhile, Saroj Kumar Jha highlighted the need to improve Nigeria’s water storage systems.

He noted that the country has more than 400 dams, many of which require rehabilitation to better manage flooding, drought, and irrigation needs.

 

Supporting Nigeria’s Food Security Strategy

Officials say the project aligns with the government’s broader economic agenda and the national push to improve food security.

With increasing climate variability, population growth, and rising food demand, authorities say modern irrigation systems are essential for stabilising agricultural output.

The project is expected to strengthen the livelihoods of farmers while also supporting broader economic growth through improved infrastructure and energy supply.

Reliable irrigation and energy infrastructure are essential for agricultural productivity and agro-processing businesses. Investments in water management and irrigation can help farmers increase yields, stabilise food supply, and create new opportunities for agribusiness MSMEs across agricultural value chains.
Côte d’Ivoire Sells Over 400,000 Tons of Mid-Crop Cocoa Export Contracts
Sales surge after regulator and exporters reach agreement to revive mid-crop market activity.

Côte d’Ivoire has sold over 400,000 tons of cocoa export contracts for the 2025/26 mid-crop season shortly after exporters resumed purchases. The sales rebound follows an agreement between regulators and exporters and a reduction in farm-gate prices aimed at boosting competitiveness in the global cocoa market.

Côte d’Ivoire the world’s largest cocoa producer, has sold more than 400,000 metric tons of cocoa export contracts for the 2025/26 mid-crop season within just ten days of reopening export purchases.

According to officials from the Coffee and Cocoa Council, the strong sales followed an agreement reached in late February between the regulator and exporters to resume mid-crop buying activity.

The rapid sales mark a significant turnaround after months of slow trading caused by falling global cocoa prices and unsold stocks accumulating in warehouses and ports.

 

Export Sales Rebound After Market Agreement

The recovery in cocoa export activity was triggered by a deal between the CCC and the exporters’ association Groupement Professionnel des Exportateurs de Café et de Cacao.

Members of the exporters’ group include major global commodity companies such as:

  • Barry Callebaut

  • Olam Group

  • Cargill

Before the agreement, only 10,000 tons of cocoa had been sold for the mid-crop season due to weak global demand and pricing challenges.

Officials say the rapid sale of over 400,000 tons since purchases resumed represents a strong recovery in export activity.

 

Farm-Gate Price Adjustment to Stimulate Sales

To improve market competitiveness, the government recently reduced the fixed farm-gate price paid to cocoa farmers.

Key adjustments include:

  • New farm-gate price: 1,200 CFA francs ($2.13) per kilogram

  • Change implemented at the start of the mid-crop season on March 1

  • Price cut aimed at boosting export sales and clearing accumulated stock

Officials say the move was necessary to align domestic pricing with global market conditions.

Mid-Crop Volume Nearly Fully Sold

Industry sources estimate that the mid-crop harvest totals around 400,000 tons, meaning most of the available export contracts have already been allocated.

The mid-crop also began about one month earlier than usual, allowing exporters to access additional volumes sooner.

However, falling global prices had previously made Ivorian cocoa relatively expensive, leading to unsold beans piling up in warehouses and ports before the recent sales surge.

Cocoa remains a major source of income for millions of farmers and agribusinesses across West Africa. Stable export demand helps sustain smallholder farmers, traders, and processors, while highlighting the need for greater local value addition to reduce exposure to global commodity price fluctuations.
Agrofood Nigeria 2026 Set to Showcase Investment Opportunities in Nigeria’s Fast-Growing Food and Beverage Industry
Major industry trade fair to bring global technology providers, investors, and food industry stakeholders to Lagos as Nigeria’s agrifood market expands rapidly.

agrofood Nigeria 2026 will bring together global and local stakeholders in Lagos to explore investment and technology opportunities in Nigeria’s rapidly expanding food and beverage sector. As demand for food production, processing, and packaging continues to grow, the event aims to connect businesses, investors, and technology providers across the agrifood value chain to support industry development and reduce Nigeria’s reliance on food imports.

Stakeholders in Nigeria’s food and beverage industry are set to converge in Lagos as agrofood Nigeria 2026, one of West Africa’s leading trade exhibitions for the agrifood and packaging sectors, returns to the Landmark Centre, Victoria Island, from March 24 to 26, 2026.

The annual event comes at a time when Nigeria’s food industry is experiencing rapid growth driven by rising population, urbanisation, and changing consumer demand.

Industry projections show that Nigeria’s food market could reach $233.53 billion in 2025, with the sector expected to expand at an annual growth rate of 10.76 percent between 2025 and 2030.

Despite this growth potential, Nigeria continues to face a significant food trade imbalance. According to the World Trade Organization (WTO), the country imported $6.1 billion worth of food products in 2023, while food exports stood at $2.2 billion, highlighting the need for increased domestic production and processing capacity.

Organisers say agrofood Nigeria has grown into one of the most important platforms for connecting local industry players with global technology providers, investors, and suppliers.

Now in its 11th edition, the event brings together stakeholders across the entire food and beverage value chain, including agricultural producers, food processors, packaging companies, equipment manufacturers, and distributors.

The trade show gained increased prominence in 2024, when former President Olusegun Obasanjo delivered the opening address, a move widely interpreted as recognition of the sector’s importance to Nigeria’s economic diversification and industrial development.

Over the years, the agrofood brand has expanded its presence across Africa, with similar exhibitions now held in countries such as Côte d’Ivoire and Ethiopia.

This year’s edition is expected to attract a larger international presence, with The Netherlands participating as the Guest of Honor.

The designation reflects growing cooperation between Nigeria and the Netherlands in areas such as horticulture, dairy technology, and sustainable food systems.

Several other countries will also participate through national pavilions, including Belgium’s Flanders Region, China, Germany, India, and South Africa, turning the exhibition into a global marketplace for food processing technologies, packaging innovations, and agricultural solutions.

Industry analysts say Nigeria remains one of the most attractive markets for food production and processing technologies in West Africa.

According to data from the German Mechanical Engineering Industry Association (VDMA), Nigeria imported €265 million worth of food and packaging technology in 2024, with packaging technology accounting for €121 million of that figure.

These imports reflect the country’s growing need for modern equipment to support its expanding food production and processing industry.

Nigeria’s population, estimated at about 238 million in 2025, is projected to reach 411 million by 2050, significantly increasing demand for food, beverages, and processed products.

Urbanisation and the growth of the middle class are also driving demand for packaged foods, branded consumer goods, and convenience products, creating opportunities for businesses across the agrifood value chain.

Experts say addressing the country’s food demand will require large-scale investment in areas such as food processing, cold chain infrastructure, logistics, packaging technology, and ingredient sourcing.

At the agricultural production level, Nigeria has also seen increased interest in agritech innovations.

According to Disrupt Africa, Nigerian agritech startups raised over $100 million in venture capital funding between 2024 and 2025, with innovations ranging from drone-based crop monitoring and solar-powered irrigation systems to precision farming tools.

Government initiatives such as the National Agricultural Technology and Innovation Policy are also supporting mechanisation and efforts to reduce post-harvest losses.

However, significant gaps remain between agricultural production and industrial processing.

The Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) estimates that Nigeria loses more than 30 percent of its perishable agricultural products after harvest, largely due to inadequate storage, logistics, and processing facilities.

Industry experts say these losses represent both a challenge and an opportunity for technology providers and investors looking to improve Nigeria’s food supply chain.

Demand for food services is also rising rapidly.

Nigeria’s hospitality and food service sectors are expanding, particularly in major urban centres such as Lagos, where new hotels, quick-service restaurants, and catering businesses are emerging to serve a growing young and urban population.

The International Hotel and Restaurant Association projects that Nigeria’s food service market will grow by 8 to 9 percent annually through the end of the decade, further increasing demand for reliable food supply chains and industrial food preparation equipment.

Organisers say agrofood Nigeria stands out among industry events due to its strong institutional support.

The exhibition is backed by more than 20 organisations, including international chambers of commerce, Nigerian government agencies, and global industry associations such as VDMA and the Nigerian Association of Chambers of Commerce, Industry, Mines and Agriculture (NACCIMA).

These partnerships help position the event as a major business platform for connecting global suppliers with African buyers and investors.

Held in Lagos, the commercial hub of West Africa, the exhibition also attracts participants from across the ECOWAS region, reflecting the growing integration of food markets within the sub-region.

Manufacturers, processors, distributors, and technology providers from multiple countries are expected to attend the event to explore new partnerships and investment opportunities.

Organisers have also announced that entry to agrofood Nigeria 2026 will be free for professional visitors, a move aimed at encouraging broader participation from industry stakeholders.

They say the initiative will enable more Nigerian businesses to access global technologies and innovations that can improve efficiency across the food production and processing value chain.

Industry stakeholders believe that as Nigeria works to close its food supply gap and strengthen local production, platforms such as agrofood Nigeria will play an increasingly important role in facilitating technology transfer, industry partnerships, and investment in the agrifood sector.

For African MSMEs in agriculture, food processing, packaging, and logistics, agrofood Nigeria provides direct access to global technologies, suppliers, and business partners that can help them scale operations and improve productivity. It also opens opportunities for small businesses to connect with investors, distributors, and regional buyers across the ECOWAS market. As demand for processed and packaged foods continues to rise, MSMEs that adopt modern production, storage, and packaging solutions will be better positioned to compete in both local and regional markets.
Nigeria Moves to Establish Grid Asset Management Company to Address Power Transmission Challenges
Only eight of more than 30 power stations connected to the national grid are currently generating electricity, highlighting deep structural issues in the power sector.

Nigeria plans to establish a Grid Asset Management Company (GAMCO) to manage and upgrade the country’s electricity transmission infrastructure in an effort to address persistent power supply challenges. The move comes as data shows that only eight of more than 30 power stations connected to the national grid are currently generating electricity. Industry experts say broader reforms, improved infrastructure, and stronger private sector participation will be necessary to restore efficiency and attract investment to the power sector.

The Federal Government is planning to establish a Grid Asset Management Company (GAMCO) to manage and modernise Nigeria’s electricity transmission infrastructure as part of broader efforts to address longstanding challenges in the country’s power sector.

The proposed company will be responsible for improving the efficiency and reliability of the national transmission grid, which has struggled with operational inefficiencies and inadequate infrastructure for years.

The move comes amid new data from the Nigerian Independent System Operator (NISO) showing that out of more than 30 power stations connected to the national grid, only eight are currently generating electricity, underscoring the structural weaknesses affecting Nigeria’s electricity supply.

Industry stakeholders say the situation reflects persistent challenges across the entire power value chain, including gas supply, power generation, transmission infrastructure, and electricity distribution.

Speaking on the development, Chukwueloka Umeh, Chief Executive Officer of Century Power Generation, said Nigeria’s electricity challenges extend beyond a single segment of the sector.

“The entire sector is broken from gas production to power generation, transmission, and distribution,” Umeh said during an interview on CNBC Africa.

According to him, the creation of the Grid Asset Management Company could represent an important step toward addressing the country’s transmission bottlenecks if implemented effectively.

However, he raised concerns about the composition of the committee responsible for overseeing the new entity, warning that excessive political influence could limit its effectiveness.

Umeh said the involvement of numerous political appointees in the management structure could undermine the operational independence required for such an initiative to succeed.

Industry analysts have long argued that political interference has been a recurring obstacle to sustainable reforms within Nigeria’s electricity sector.

Nigeria continues to face widespread power shortages despite having abundant natural resources capable of supporting electricity generation.

Experts point to the country’s more than 210 trillion cubic feet of proven natural gas reserves, which could provide a strong foundation for reliable power generation if effectively harnessed.

Umeh described the situation as a paradox, noting that a country with significant energy resources continues to struggle with electricity supply challenges.

He emphasised that addressing Nigeria’s electricity crisis would require more comprehensive reforms beyond the creation of a new transmission management entity.

According to him, deeper structural changes such as expanded private sector participation and improved regulatory frameworks are necessary to attract investment into the power sector.

“We need to prioritise the power industry because without power, economic development becomes extremely difficult,” Umeh said.

He added that a fully functional and competitive electricity market could help unlock both domestic and foreign investments needed to upgrade the country’s ageing power infrastructure.

In addition to institutional reforms, experts say Nigeria must also invest heavily in modernising its electricity transmission network.

Umeh proposed the development of a super-grid system, which would involve upgrading the country’s current 330 kilovolt transmission infrastructure to a 500 kilovolt network.

Such an upgrade could significantly reduce transmission losses and improve the capacity of the national grid to transmit electricity from power plants to distribution networks.

He also suggested expanding the use of regional mini-grids connected to major grid nodes, which could help distribute electricity more efficiently while allowing integration of diverse energy sources such as hydroelectric and solar power.

Although Nigeria has introduced new legislation, including the Electricity Act, aimed at reforming the sector and decentralising electricity generation and distribution, implementation challenges remain.

Stakeholders say persistent regulatory uncertainties, security concerns, and weak enforcement of market rules continue to discourage private investment in the industry.

Another major challenge is the accumulation of legacy debts within the electricity sector.

Power generation companies have repeatedly complained about delayed payments and unpaid subsidies owed by the government, which have constrained their ability to maintain operations and invest in infrastructure upgrades.

Umeh noted that addressing these financial obligations would be essential for restoring investor confidence and ensuring long-term sustainability within the sector.

Despite these challenges, industry observers say the proposed Grid Asset Management Company could play a key role in improving the performance of Nigeria’s electricity transmission system if it operates with transparency, professional management, and clear regulatory oversight.

Nigeria’s electricity demand continues to rise as the country’s population grows and industrial activities expand.

However, experts warn that without decisive reforms and infrastructure investments, the power sector could remain a major constraint on economic growth and industrial development.

The proposed initiative is expected to form part of the government’s broader efforts to improve electricity reliability and support Nigeria’s long-term economic development goals.

Reliable electricity is critical for MSMEs across Africa, particularly in sectors such as manufacturing, agro-processing, retail, and digital services that depend on consistent power supply. Improvements in Nigeria’s power transmission infrastructure could reduce operational costs for small businesses that currently rely heavily on generators. A more stable electricity system would also support productivity, enable business expansion, and attract investment into industries where MSMEs play a key role in job creation and economic growth.
African Financial Leaders Call for Unified Capital Markets to Boost Investment and Economic Growth
Stakeholders at Cape Town summit highlight reforms, local currency financing, and AfCFTA as key drivers of financial integration across the continent.

African financial leaders are calling for stronger integration of the continent’s capital markets to increase investment and support long-term economic growth. Discussions at the LMA–ICMA summit in Cape Town highlighted the role of local currency financing, financial sector reforms, and initiatives such as the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) in improving capital flows and strengthening regional economic integration across Africa

Financial leaders and policymakers from across Africa are advocating for stronger integration of the continent’s capital markets to unlock investment and support long-term economic development.

The call was made during a summit jointly organised by the Loan Market Association (LMA) and the International Capital Market Association (ICMA), currently taking place in Cape Town, where industry leaders are discussing ways to strengthen Africa’s loan and debt capital markets.

The event brings together financial institutions, investors, and policymakers to explore how more integrated financial markets can mobilise capital for sustainable development across Africa.

Speaking on the sidelines of the summit, Raymond Ofungi, Head of Structured Sales for Africa at Nedbank Corporate and Investment Banking (CIB), said the continent is gradually moving toward more unified capital markets despite structural challenges.

According to Ofungi, one of the key shifts currently taking place in African financial markets is the growing interest in using local currencies rather than relying heavily on the U.S. dollar, which has historically dominated financial transactions on the continent.

“African markets have traditionally been tied to dollar-based transactions because many of our exports are commodities priced in dollars,” Ofungi said.

“However, institutions are increasingly looking at local currencies as part of strengthening value chains and improving financial resilience.”

Experts at the summit noted that reliance on foreign currencies has often exposed African economies to exchange rate volatility and external economic shocks.

Encouraging greater use of local currencies could therefore help strengthen domestic financial systems and improve access to financing for businesses.

Despite these efforts, the development of unified capital markets across Africa remains complex due to the continent’s diverse economic structures and regulatory environments.

Ofungi explained that financial markets across regions—including Southern, Eastern, Western, and Northern Africa operate at different levels of maturity, making standardisation and integration more challenging.

However, he noted that recent economic reforms and increased political commitment are gradually improving the outlook for greater financial integration.

“Forging a unified capital market across Africa has always been complex because of the different political and economic dynamics across regions,” he said.

“But the reforms we are beginning to see are promising and signal progress toward a more connected financial system.”

A major driver of this integration effort is the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA), which aims to create a single market for goods and services across the continent.

The AfCFTA is expected to significantly expand intra-African trade and encourage cross-border investments by reducing trade barriers and improving economic cooperation among member states.

While the agreement has created new momentum for economic integration, analysts say several challenges remain.

Market fragmentation, logistical constraints, and policy inconsistencies continue to limit the flow of capital and investments between African countries.

Geopolitical shifts and global economic uncertainties have also prompted African governments and financial institutions to place greater emphasis on regional self-reliance and financial independence.

At the summit, industry stakeholders emphasised the importance of aligning Africa’s financial systems with global standards, a step considered necessary to attract international investors and strengthen the continent’s financial markets.

According to Ofungi, reforms within the financial sector are gradually addressing long-standing bottlenecks that have previously restricted capital flows across the continent.

“The finance space is undergoing significant reforms that are helping to dismantle some of the barriers that have limited capital movement within Africa,” he said.

However, participants also noted that central banks across the continent must play a stronger role in supporting financial integration.

In particular, financial leaders highlighted the importance of developing infrastructure that enables seamless cross-border payments.

One key initiative in this regard is the Pan-African Payment and Settlement System (PAPSS), which was created to simplify and speed up transactions between African countries by allowing payments to be processed in local currencies.

Experts say the success of such systems will depend largely on the willingness of central banks to adopt the required infrastructure and regulatory frameworks.

Beyond financial systems, participants also pointed to logistical and administrative barriers that continue to hinder deeper regional integration.

Restrictions on visa access, transportation challenges, and regulatory differences still make cross-border business operations difficult in many parts of the continent.

Nevertheless, stakeholders remain optimistic about the future of Africa’s financial integration.

They say the development of unified capital markets could significantly improve access to financing for businesses, stimulate investment, and support economic growth across the continent.

As discussions continue at the Cape Town summit, industry leaders say collaborative reforms among governments, financial institutions, and regulators will be essential to unlocking Africa’s vast economic potential and building stronger, more resilient financial markets.

Unified capital markets across Africa could significantly improve access to financing for MSMEs, which often struggle to secure affordable loans and investment. Stronger financial integration would allow businesses to tap into a broader pool of regional capital, reduce cross-border payment barriers, and make it easier for MSMEs to expand into new African markets under frameworks like AfCFTA. Ultimately, better access to capital and smoother regional trade can help small businesses scale, innovate, and compete more effectively across the continent.
South Africa’s Economy Expands by 1.1% in 2025, Missing Government Growth Forecasts
Slow growth highlights structural challenges as policymakers seek stronger investment and job creation in Africa’s most industrialised economy.

South Africa’s economy grew by 1.1% in 2025, falling short of government and central bank forecasts. While modest growth in consumer sectors and private investment supported the economy, several industries such as agriculture and manufacturing contracted. Economists say the slow growth rate is insufficient to address the country’s high unemployment and that stronger investment and structural reforms will be needed to boost long-term economic expansion.

South Africa’s economy grew by 1.1 percent in 2025, falling short of projections by the country’s central bank and National Treasury, according to data released on Tuesday by Statistics South Africa.

The growth rate underscores the ongoing economic challenges facing Africa’s most industrialised economy, where sluggish expansion over the past decade has limited job creation and investment.

According to the data, South Africa’s growth performance in 2025 was below the 1.3 percent expansion forecast by the South African Reserve Bank and the 1.4 percent growth projected by the National Treasury.

The latest figures come as the country’s coalition government seeks to accelerate economic growth and address persistent unemployment.

Over the past ten years, South Africa’s economy has grown at an average rate of less than one percent annually, a pace widely regarded by economists as insufficient to generate meaningful employment opportunities or reduce poverty.

Speaking during a press briefing in Pretoria, Statistician-General Risenga Maluleke cautioned against interpreting the modest growth as a positive economic turning point.

“Growth of around one percent should not create too much excitement,” Maluleke said.

“Such growth will not be able to contain the joblessness that we see in the country.”

South Africa continues to face one of the highest unemployment rates in the world, with millions of people struggling to find work despite gradual improvements in some sectors of the economy.

Economic activity in the fourth quarter of 2025 showed some improvement but remained below expectations.

The economy expanded by 0.4 percent in the fourth quarter compared with the previous quarter, slightly above economists’ expectations.

However, on a year-on-year basis, fourth-quarter growth stood at 0.8 percent, below the median forecast in a Reuters survey of economists.

Sectoral performance during the period revealed mixed economic activity.

Out of the ten sectors monitored by Statistics South Africa, five recorded growth while the remaining five experienced contraction during the fourth quarter.

Economic expansion was largely concentrated in consumer-driven sectors, including industries linked to household spending.

In contrast, primary sectors such as agriculture and mining, as well as secondary industries like manufacturing, recorded weaker performance.

Analysts say this imbalance reflects ongoing structural weaknesses within the country’s production and industrial sectors.

Despite the modest growth figures, there were some positive developments in investor sentiment during the year.

Investor confidence improved in 2025 following signs of fiscal stability and the government’s commitment to maintaining low and stable inflation.

Economic analysts say these factors helped stabilise financial markets and support limited investment growth.

Data released by Statistics South Africa also showed a moderate increase in gross fixed capital formation, a key indicator of investment in infrastructure, equipment, and productive assets.

Gross fixed capital formation rose by 1.3 percent in the fourth quarter, marking the second consecutive quarter of growth.

The increase was driven largely by private sector investment, suggesting that businesses are gradually expanding spending on capital projects.

However, economists say the level of investment remains far below what is needed to significantly boost economic growth.

Sustained investment in infrastructure, energy, and industrial development is widely seen as essential to unlocking South Africa’s long-term growth potential.

South Africa’s economic recovery has been constrained by several structural challenges, including persistent power supply disruptions, infrastructure bottlenecks, and regulatory constraints.

Electricity shortages in particular have weighed heavily on industrial output and business activity in recent years.

The government has introduced several reforms aimed at improving energy supply, encouraging private investment in electricity generation, and modernising infrastructure.

Policy analysts say these reforms could gradually support stronger economic growth if effectively implemented.

In addition, policymakers are focusing on measures to attract foreign investment and expand industrial capacity in sectors such as manufacturing, renewable energy, and technology.

Economists note that stronger growth will likely require deeper structural reforms aimed at improving productivity, reducing business costs, and strengthening the country’s investment environment.

The government has also emphasised the importance of expanding opportunities for small and medium-sized enterprises as part of its economic development strategy.

Experts say sustained economic growth above three percent annually would likely be required to significantly reduce unemployment and support inclusive development.

For now, however, the latest economic data suggests that South Africa’s recovery remains gradual and uneven.

While modest improvements in investment and consumer activity offer some optimism, economists warn that the country will need stronger policy reforms and higher levels of investment to achieve the sustained growth required to address its longstanding economic challenges.

South Africa is one of Africa’s largest economies and a major trading hub for the continent. Slow economic growth can limit market demand, investment opportunities, and cross-border trade for MSMEs operating in or exporting to the South African market. For small businesses across Africa, stronger growth in South Africa would mean larger consumer markets, improved regional supply chains, and increased opportunities for partnerships, investment, and expansion within the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA).
PalmPay Launches Initiative to Train and Mentor Women for Careers in Nigeria’s Fintech Industry
Programme offers digital skills training, mentorship, and internship opportunities to increase women’s participation in technology and financial services.

PalmPay Nigeria has launched the third edition of its Purple Women programme to equip young women with fintech, digital, and leadership skills aimed at increasing female participation in the technology sector. The initiative, unveiled during the Global Women’s Masterclass in Lagos, provides mentorship, training, and internship opportunities, with 10 participants selected for a six-month internship at the company to gain practical industry experience.

PalmPay Nigeria has reaffirmed its commitment to promoting gender inclusion in the technology sector through a programme designed to equip young women with the skills needed to succeed in the financial technology industry.

The digital financial services company announced the third edition of its Global Women’s Masterclass, organised in partnership with the Global Women’s International Campaign Nigeria to mark International Women’s Day.

The initiative, known as the Purple Women Programme, focuses on providing women with training in digital skills, business development, and leadership, alongside mentorship and employment opportunities within the fintech ecosystem.

Speaking at the event in Lagos, Managing Director of PalmPay Nigeria, Chika Nwosu, said the programme was created to address the limited representation of women in the fintech and broader technology sectors.

According to him, the initiative is aimed at developing a pipeline of female professionals who can take on leadership roles in the rapidly growing digital economy.

“We launched this initiative because we noticed that there are not many women in fintech and the wider technology industry,” Nwosu said.

“Our goal is to bridge that gap and ensure that more women are empowered to succeed and take leadership positions within the sector.”

He added that empowering women in technology and finance can have broader economic benefits for society.

“When women gain access to financial tools and career opportunities, the positive impact often extends to families and communities,” he said.

The Purple Women programme, now in its third edition, combines mentorship, professional training, and career development support for participants.

While the masterclass was hosted physically in Lagos, organisers said women across Nigeria were able to participate through an online registration platform.

The programme also includes internship opportunities designed to help participants transition from training into professional employment.

At the end of the masterclass, 10 participants were selected for a six-month internship programme at PalmPay, where they will gain hands-on experience across several departments.

These include marketing, human resources, administration, product development, sales, and business intelligence.

According to Anthony Iwuala, Head of Human Resources at PalmPay, the selection process was conducted through a merit-based evaluation.

Participants attended training sessions and completed assessments, after which the highest-performing candidates were selected for the internship programme.

“For us at PalmPay, equity and fairness are very important,” Iwuala said.

“The participants attended classes and wrote tests after each session. Many performed well, but we had to rank them and select the top ten candidates.”

He explained that each intern will be paired with experienced mentors within the organisation who will guide them throughout the six-month training period.

The programme is designed not only to provide learning opportunities but also to create employment pathways.

“We are not just training participants; our aim is to train them to become employable professionals,” Iwuala said.

He noted that participants from the previous Purple Women 2.0 edition were offered permanent employment with PalmPay and continue to work within the organisation.

Industry leaders who spoke at the event encouraged women to pursue careers in technology development and digital innovation.

Kemi Okusanya, Chief Executive Officer of Hydrogen, highlighted the importance of including female perspectives in the design and development of technology products.

According to her, greater participation by women can help ensure that digital products better reflect the needs of diverse users.

“The only thing that can stop you is yourself,” Okusanya told participants.

“From a product perspective, the world needs more women building technology. If only men develop products used by women, there will always be gaps.”

She encouraged participants to build technology solutions locally while maintaining the ambition to scale them globally.

Also speaking at the event, Dr. Nneka Okekearu, Director of the Enterprise Development Centre at Pan-Atlantic University, delivered a session on personal branding and self-development.

She emphasised the role of confidence, self-awareness, and mindset in career advancement.

According to Okekearu, many women face barriers rooted in unconscious biases that shape career choices from an early age.

“A lot of women have so much potential but are limited by unconscious bias,” she said.

“Self-worth is about recognising your abilities and having the confidence to own them.”

While acknowledging progress in gender inclusion within Nigeria’s financial sector, Okekearu noted that women still face career progression challenges.

She pointed out that more than 30 percent of commercial banks in Nigeria are now led by female chief executives, indicating improved representation at senior leadership levels.

However, she highlighted what she described as the “missing middle” challenge, where many women leave the workforce during key life stages such as marriage or childbirth.

Okekearu called for systems and policies that allow women to return to the workforce without losing career momentum.

PalmPay said it plans to expand the Purple Women initiative in future editions as part of its broader strategy to support a more inclusive digital economy.

Nwosu said the company intends to grow the programme and reach more women in the coming years.

“The fourth edition will definitely happen, and we plan to make it bigger and better,” he said.

“Our long-term vision is to see more Nigerian women occupying leadership roles across industries driven by technology and innovation.”

Increasing women’s participation in fintech and technology expands the pool of skilled talent available to African MSMEs operating in the digital economy. As more women gain digital, financial, and entrepreneurial skills, they can start businesses, lead tech-driven startups, and support innovation across sectors such as e-commerce, financial services, and digital payments. Greater gender inclusion in technology also helps MSMEs design products and services that better reflect the needs of diverse consumers, strengthening growth and competitiveness across African markets.
South African Rand Hits Three-Month Low as Rising Oil Prices Trigger Inflation and Market Concerns
Currency weakness and rising energy costs could increase operating pressures for African MSMEs linked to trade, manufacturing, and supply chains.

The South African rand fell to a three-month low as global oil prices surged amid rising geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, increasing concerns about inflation and economic growth. The currency’s decline reflects growing investor caution and could influence the South African Reserve Bank’s upcoming interest rate decision. Analysts warn that continued volatility in oil prices and financial markets may raise operating costs and create economic uncertainty for businesses, including MSMEs across Africa.

The South African rand weakened sharply at the start of the week, falling to its lowest level in three months as global oil prices surged and investors grew increasingly cautious about the potential impact of rising energy costs on inflation and economic growth.

In early trading on Monday, the rand was valued at 16.85 against the U.S. dollar, representing a decline of about 2 percent from its previous close. The currency hovered near its weakest level since mid-December, reflecting growing global market volatility.

The currency’s decline came as investors reduced exposure to risk-sensitive assets amid rising geopolitical tensions and fears of prolonged disruption to global oil supply.

South Africa’s financial markets also reflected the shift in investor sentiment. The country’s benchmark 2035 government bond weakened in early trading, with its yield rising 1.5 basis points to 8.505 percent, signalling increased risk perceptions among investors.

Analysts attribute the market reaction largely to the recent surge in crude oil prices, which have risen by more than 25 percent in recent days.

The price spike has been driven by escalating tensions involving the United States, Israel, and Iran, which have raised concerns about disruptions to oil supply routes in the Middle East.

Some major oil producers in the region have reportedly reduced production amid fears that conflict could affect shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world’s most critical energy transport corridors.

Financial analysts say the developments have increased global risk aversion, prompting investors to shift capital away from emerging markets such as South Africa.

The rand, which is considered one of the most liquid emerging market currencies, is particularly sensitive to global risk sentiment and commodity price fluctuations.

Because South Africa imports a significant portion of its energy needs, higher oil prices often translate into increased economic pressure through rising fuel costs and inflation.

Analysts warn that the currency may remain under pressure if oil prices continue to rise or geopolitical tensions intensify.

“The stars have aligned for the rand to come under considerable pressure,” analysts at ETM Analytics said in a market note.

They added that the South African Reserve Bank (SARB) could respond to currency volatility and capital outflows by tightening monetary policy.

According to the analysts, one possible response by the central bank could be raising interest rates to stabilise the currency and attract investor confidence.

Higher interest rates can encourage foreign investment in government bonds and financial assets, but they may also slow domestic economic activity.

Last week, the governor of the South African Reserve Bank told Reuters that the central bank is reviewing potential risk scenarios ahead of its next monetary policy meeting.

The bank is scheduled to announce its next interest rate decision on March 26.

In January, policymakers decided to keep the country’s main lending rate unchanged at 6.75 percent, following a split vote within the central bank’s policy committee.

At the time, officials indicated that they wanted to see further improvements in inflation expectations before making adjustments to interest rates.

Market analysts say the recent rise in oil prices could complicate the central bank’s policy outlook, as higher energy costs tend to feed into inflation across transportation, manufacturing, and food supply chains.

Meanwhile, the Johannesburg Stock Exchange (JSE) also recorded losses in early trading, with the Top-40 index declining by 0.6 percent.

Investors are closely monitoring domestic economic indicators this week for further signals about the health of South Africa’s economy.

Statistics South Africa is expected to release fourth-quarter gross domestic product (GDP) figures, as well as January mining and manufacturing data, which could provide insight into economic momentum in Africa’s most industrialised economy.

Currency strategists say markets are likely to remain volatile until there is greater clarity regarding the geopolitical situation and its impact on global energy supply.

“Until there is clarity on the duration and scale of the conflict, markets are likely to remain volatile with investors favouring defensive positioning,” said Andre Cilliers, currency strategist at TreasuryONE.

Economic observers note that fluctuations in the rand and rising energy costs could have ripple effects across African economies.

South Africa plays a central role in regional trade, manufacturing, and financial markets, meaning currency volatility and inflationary pressures may affect businesses across the continent.

For African micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs), especially those involved in cross-border trade, manufacturing, and logistics, a weaker rand can create both challenges and opportunities.

Rising fuel prices and transportation costs could increase operational expenses for businesses that rely on imported inputs or regional supply chains.

At the same time, currency depreciation can sometimes make South African exports more competitive in regional and international markets.

However, analysts say sustained currency volatility combined with rising energy costs could strain businesses already operating in difficult economic conditions.

Many MSMEs across Africa depend on stable supply chains, affordable energy, and predictable exchange rates to maintain profitability.

As global markets continue to react to geopolitical tensions and energy price fluctuations, economists say policymakers will need to carefully balance inflation control with economic growth to protect businesses and maintain financial stability across the region.

Fluctuations in the South African rand and rising global oil prices can increase operating costs for MSMEs across Africa, particularly those involved in manufacturing, transportation, agriculture, and cross-border trade. Higher fuel prices often lead to increased logistics and production expenses, which can reduce profit margins for small businesses. Currency volatility can also affect import costs and regional trade pricing, making it harder for MSMEs to plan, price goods competitively, and maintain stable supply chains across African markets.
Nigeria Urged to Expand Cashew Processing to Capture Global Market Value
Industry leaders warn Nigeria risks losing billions in value by exporting raw cashew instead of processing locally.

The National Cashew Association of Nigeria has called for greater investment in local cashew processing to prevent the country from losing value in the global market. With most cashew nuts currently exported raw, industry leaders say affordable financing, improved tree varieties, and expanded processing capacity are critical to unlocking Nigeria’s full potential in the rapidly growing global cashew industry.

The National Cashew Association of Nigeria has called for major investment in domestic cashew processing, warning that Nigeria is losing significant economic value by exporting most of its cashew nuts in raw form.

According to Ojo Ajanaku, Nigeria currently processes less than 10 percent of its annual cashew production, estimated at about 350,000 metric tons. Most raw cashew nuts are exported to countries such as India and Vietnam, where they are processed and later sold at significantly higher value in global markets.

Industry stakeholders say expanding local processing capacity could help Nigeria retain more value, create jobs, and strengthen its agro-processing sector.

 

Low Processing Capacity Limits Value Addition

Despite being one of the world’s major cashew producers, Nigeria’s processing industry remains relatively small.

Key industry figures include:

  • Nigeria produces about 350,000 metric tons of cashew annually

  • Less than 10% is processed locally

  • Only 25–30 processing companies can process 10 tons or more per day

  • Many small processors mainly serve local markets and lack export-scale capacity

As a result, the majority of Nigeria’s cashew harvest is exported as raw nuts.

 

High Financing Costs Hindering Industry Growth

Industry leaders say access to affordable financing remains one of the biggest obstacles to expanding processing capacity.

Cashew harvesting lasts only about three months each year, but processors must store raw materials to operate year-round.

However, processors currently face:

  • Interest rates as high as 42 percent

  • Limited access to long-term agricultural financing

  • High costs of equipment and storage infrastructure
