The association is therefore calling on the Federal Government to introduce a special financing facility or product paper that would allow cashew processors to access single-digit interest loans.

 

Replanting and Improved Varieties Needed

Production challenges are also emerging in several key cashew-growing areas, including Kogi State, Benue State, and parts of the former Kwara State.

Many cashew trees in these regions were planted decades ago and are reaching the end of their productive life cycles.

To address this, NCAN is working with partners to:

  • Establish cashew nursery farms nationwide

  • Promote high-yield varieties such as the Ogbomosho cashew variety

  • Support tree grafting and plantation renewal programmes

These efforts aim to improve productivity and ensure long-term sustainability of Nigeria’s cashew sector.

 

Untapped Opportunities in Cashew By-Products

Industry experts also highlight the potential to develop a broader cashew processing ecosystem.

Beyond kernels, cashew processing can generate valuable by-products including:

  • Cashew Nut Shell Liquid (CNSL) used in pharmaceuticals and paint manufacturing

  • Cashew oil with strong global demand

  • Additional value-added products for industrial and food markets

Developing these industries could significantly increase Nigeria’s share of the global cashew market, which is projected to grow from $7.8 billion to $11.8 billion in the coming years.

Expanding agro-processing industries allows countries to retain more value from agricultural commodities while creating jobs and new business opportunities. For MSMEs, stronger cashew value chains, from farming to processing and exports can unlock investment, support rural enterprises, and increase participation in global agricultural markets.
Nigeria’s Expanding Tax Net Is Increasing Levies on Everyday Transactions
Growing enforcement of transactional taxes is affecting digital payments, bank transfers, and small business operations.

Nigeria’s tax reforms are expanding the scope of transaction-based taxes, bringing everyday financial activities, such as bank transfers and digital payments into the formal tax net. While the move could increase government revenue, analysts warn it may also raise operating costs for businesses and affect the adoption of digital financial services.

Nigeria’s ongoing tax reforms are expanding the reach of transaction-based taxes, bringing a growing number of everyday financial activities, such as bank transfers, digital subscriptions, and electronic payments into the formal tax system.

For many consumers and small business operators, the impact is felt through multiple small deductions attached to routine transactions rather than a single large tax. Analysts say the trend reflects stronger enforcement and greater compliance following recent tax reforms aimed at increasing government revenue.

Operators within Nigeria’s rapidly expanding digital payment ecosystem, including Point-of-Sale (POS) agents, say these charges are becoming more noticeable in daily financial transactions.

 

Everyday Transactions Increasingly Subject to Small Levies

Many consumers now encounter a series of small deductions when conducting routine financial activities.

Examples of common transactional charges include:

  • Electronic transfer levies on bank transactions

  • Stamp duties on certain financial documents and transfers

  • Utility payment charges and service fees

  • Value Added Tax (VAT) applied to goods and services

For POS operators and small businesses handling large volumes of transactions, these cumulative charges can significantly increase the cost of operating within the digital payment ecosystem.

 

Rising VAT Compliance Among Small Businesses

Another visible shift is the growing enforcement of Value Added Tax (VAT) across Nigeria’s informal and digital economy.

According to tax professionals, many small vendors, especially online businesses previously did not include the 7.5% VAT on invoices. However, compliance has increased following reforms introduced under the 2019 Finance Act.

Key developments include:

  • More businesses adding VAT to invoices and digital transactions

  • Increased awareness of tax obligations among entrepreneurs

  • Greater scrutiny of tax compliance during audits

These changes reflect broader efforts by authorities to ensure that more economic activities fall within the formal tax system.

 

Government Revenue From Consumption Taxes Rising

The shift toward transaction-based taxation is also reflected in government revenue figures.

Recent data from the Federation Accounts Allocation Committee shows that Value Added Tax collections reached ₦1.08 trillion in January 2026, up from ₦913.96 billion in December 2025.

The increase highlights Nigeria’s growing reliance on consumption taxes as part of its broader fiscal strategy.

With tax revenue historically low relative to the size of the economy, policymakers are seeking ways to capture more taxable activity as economic transactions increasingly move into digital and traceable channels.

 

Concerns Over Business Costs and Financial Inclusion

While the expanding tax net could increase government revenue, experts warn that it may also place additional pressure on businesses and consumers.

Key concerns include:

  • Higher compliance costs for businesses managing tax obligations

  • Increased operating expenses for small enterprises and POS agents

  • Potential slowdown in digital financial adoption

Digital payments have been a major driver of financial inclusion in Nigeria over the past decade. However, rising levies attached to electronic transactions could discourage some users from fully participating in the formal financial system.

Experts say improving tax education and public awareness will be essential to ensure citizens understand how the evolving tax framework operates.

Small businesses rely heavily on digital payments, bank transfers, and online transactions to operate efficiently. As governments expand transactional taxes, MSMEs must understand new compliance requirements and manage rising transaction costs while continuing to leverage digital financial tools for growth.
Insecurity Increasingly Threatens Production and Investment in Nigeria, LCCI Warns
Business leaders say security challenges are raising costs, disrupting supply chains, and discouraging new investments.

The Lagos Chamber of Commerce and Industry has warned that insecurity is increasingly affecting production, investment, and business confidence in Nigeria. Business leaders say rising security risks are disrupting supply chains, raising operational costs, and discouraging new investments, highlighting the need for stronger collaboration between government and the private sector.

The Lagos Chamber of Commerce and Industry has warned that rising insecurity is increasingly affecting production, investment decisions, and overall business confidence in Nigeria.

Speaking at the 10th edition of the Security Meets Business Dialogue Series in Lagos, LCCI President Leye Kupoluyi said insecurity has become a significant economic burden that is disrupting business operations and discouraging long-term capital investment.

According to him, investors today evaluate not only fiscal policies and market size but also the predictability and stability of the security environment when making investment decisions.

 

Security Now a Key Economic Risk Factor

Business leaders say insecurity is now influencing multiple economic indicators and sectors.

Kupoluyi noted that security concerns affect:

  • Insurance premiums and operating costs for businesses

  • Logistics and transport efficiency, increasing delivery times

  • Tourism and trade flows

  • Confidence in digital finance systems

He added that threats such as kidnapping, terrorism, and cybercrime create what he described as a “fear premium” on business operations, increasing the cost of doing business across sectors.

 

Operational and Financial Impact on Businesses

The private sector is experiencing several operational disruptions due to worsening security conditions.

Key impacts include:

  • Reduced factory working hours in high-risk areas

  • Altered transport routes and fragmented supply chains

  • Higher spending on private security services

  • Increased insurance costs and borrowing rates

In some cases, businesses have been forced to scale down operations or shut down entirely, particularly in regions facing persistent security threats.

 

Threats to Agriculture, Trade, and Investment

Industry leaders also warned that insecurity in rural and transport corridors is affecting critical sectors of the economy.

For example:

  • Disruptions to farming activities are reducing food supply and contributing to inflation

  • Attacks on transport corridors delay the movement of goods

  • Cybercrime risks weaken trust in digital financial systems

Kupoluyi emphasized that major economic corridors should be treated as national security assets, given their importance to trade and economic productivity.

 

Calls for Stronger Public–Private Security Collaboration

Stakeholders at the dialogue called for greater cooperation between government institutions, security agencies, and the private sector.

Proposed measures include:

  • Intelligence-led security operations

  • Expanded digital surveillance capacity

  • Stronger coordination among security institutions and communities

  • Integration of private-sector security data into national intelligence systems

Representing Vice President Kashim Shettima, Tope Fasua said economic prosperity cannot be achieved without a stable security environment.

He noted that initiatives such as the African Continental Free Trade Area and Nigeria’s digital economy agenda depend on safe infrastructure, secure digital systems, and strong enforcement of the rule of law.

 

Veterans’ Welfare Bill Introduced

During the event, LCCI Director-General Chinyere Almona and business leader Julius Adelusi-Adeluyi unveiled the Veterans’ Welfare and Reintegration Bill 2026.

The proposed legislation aims to create a structured framework for the welfare, protection, and reintegration of veterans from Nigeria’s armed forces and police services.

Security stability is a critical factor for business growth and investment. For MSMEs, rising insecurity increases operating costs, disrupts supply chains, and discourages new investments, making it harder for businesses to expand, access markets, and attract capital.
BOI, Plateau State Launch ₦4 Billion Fund to Support Small Businesses
New matching fund will provide financing, training, and equipment support for MSMEs, with a focus on women and youth entrepreneurs.

The Bank of Industry and the Plateau State Government have launched a ₦4 billion matching fund to finance small businesses across the state. The initiative will provide capital, equipment support, and entrepreneurship training for MSMEs, particularly women and youth while strengthening local industries and job creation.

The Bank of Industry and the Plateau State government have signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) establishing a ₦4 billion matching fund to support Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs) across the state.

The fund, jointly financed by both parties, was formalised at the Government House in Jos. The initiative is designed to provide working capital, equipment financing, and business support services aimed at accelerating job creation and strengthening local value chains.

Officials say the programme will prioritise women and youth entrepreneurs, helping expand economic opportunities and stimulate inclusive growth in the state.

 

A Joint Fund to Expand MSME Financing

The ₦4 billion facility will be equally funded by BOI and the Plateau State Government.

The programme aims to:

  • Provide affordable financing for small businesses

  • Support equipment purchases and working capital needs

  • Strengthen local production and value chains

  • Stimulate employment and economic growth

Governor Caleb Mutfwang described the partnership as a significant step toward expanding the state’s economic contribution to Nigeria’s growth.

He noted that strengthening MSMEs, particularly in the informal sector is essential for increasing the state’s share of national economic output.

 

Focus on Women and Youth Entrepreneurs

The programme will prioritise women-led and youth-led enterprises, groups often facing barriers to accessing formal financing.

According to the governor, the fund will help identify and support high-potential businesses across the state, ensuring that financing opportunities reach entrepreneurs in multiple communities.

He added that the initiative is expected to serve as seed capital capable of generating broader economic activity and investment across Plateau State.

 

Training and Capacity Building for Beneficiaries

Beyond financing, the programme will also include entrepreneurship training and capacity development.

According to Olasupo Olusi, beneficiaries will receive training through accredited Entrepreneurship Development Centres (EDCs) to strengthen their business skills.

Training areas will include value addition in sectors such as:

  • Poultry production

  • Block making and construction materials

  • Oil and rice milling

  • Agro-mechanisation and packaging

  • Pharmaceutical production

  • Bakery and confectionery businesses

These sectors were identified for their potential to create local employment and supply chain opportunities.

 

Improving the Business Environment

BOI also acknowledged Plateau State’s improved ranking in the Ease of Doing Business index, as highlighted in reports by the Presidential Enabling Business Environment Council.

Officials say ongoing reforms aimed at improving the business environment have helped position the state as a growing destination for investment and private-sector activity.

Access to financing remains one of the biggest barriers for small businesses across Africa. Public–private funding partnerships like this matching fund can help entrepreneurs obtain capital, strengthen value chains, and create jobs while supporting regional economic development.
Uganda’s Coffee Export Earnings Hold Steady Despite Global Price Decline
Slight increase in export volumes helped offset lower international coffee prices in January.

Uganda earned $161 million from coffee exports in January, with export volumes rising slightly despite lower global prices. Higher shipments helped offset price pressures, underscoring the continued importance of coffee as a major source of foreign exchange and livelihood for millions of farmers.

Uganda’s coffee export earnings remained largely stable in January despite falling global coffee prices, according to a report from the Uganda Ministry of Agriculture Animal Industry and Fisheries.

The country generated $161 million in coffee export revenue, representing a modest 1.5 percent increase compared to the same period last year. Officials say the slight improvement was supported by higher export volumes, which helped offset the impact of declining global coffee prices.

Coffee remains one of Uganda’s most important export commodities and a major source of foreign exchange earnings, with the country currently ranking as Africa’s largest coffee exporter.

 

Higher Export Volumes Offset Price Pressures

Uganda exported 569,454 bags of coffee (60 kilograms each) in January, representing a 2 percent increase year-on-year.

Key export highlights include:

  • $161 million in total export earnings

  • 1.5% increase in export value compared with January of the previous year

  • 569,454 bags exported, up 2% year-on-year

The increase in shipment volumes helped cushion the effect of weaker global prices.

 

Global Supply Conditions Driving Lower Prices

The decline in coffee prices was largely attributed to improved global supply conditions.

Agriculture officials noted that heavy rainfall in Brazil, one of the world’s largest coffee producers, improved crop outlooks and eased concerns about global supply shortages.

This development has led to downward pressure on international coffee prices, affecting export earnings for producing countries.

 

Coffee Remains Critical to Uganda’s Economy

Coffee plays a central role in Uganda’s agricultural economy and export sector.

The industry:

  • Generates significant foreign exchange earnings

  • Supports millions of smallholder farmers

  • Contributes to rural employment and agricultural trade

Maintaining stable export performance remains important for the country as it navigates fluctuations in global commodity prices.

Coffee exports support thousands of smallholder farmers, traders, and agro-processing businesses across Africa. Stable export volumes, even amid price fluctuations, help sustain rural livelihoods and highlight the importance of value addition and diversified markets for agricultural MSMEs.
Lagos Trade Fair Traders Protest Planned Government Management Change, Shut Down Shops
Traders close shops and demand dialogue over fears of new levies and increased cost of doing business.

Traders at the Lagos International Trade Fair Complex shut down business activities to protest a proposed takeover of the market’s management by state and local government authorities. The protest disrupted operations across sections of the complex, including the Auto Spare Parts and Machinery Dealers Association market, as traders expressed concerns that the new arrangement could introduce additional levies and increase the cost of doing business for MSMEs. Meanwhile, Nigeria’s Minister of Industry, Trade and Investment, Jumoke Oduwole, visited the market to engage traders and encourage dialogue aimed at restoring normal commercial activities.

Traders at the Lagos International Trade Fair Complex on Thursday shut down business activities in protest against a proposed takeover of the market’s management by state and local government authorities.

The protest disrupted commercial activities across the complex as traders locked up their shops and stalls early in the morning to express concerns over the planned arrangement and its potential impact on small and medium-scale enterprises operating within the market.

Many traders gathered around parks and garages within the market premises while discussions with authorities continued.

According to traders, the proposed management change could introduce new levies and taxes that may increase the cost of doing business for micro, small and medium enterprises (MSMEs), which dominate trade activities within the complex.

“We are not against development, but we are concerned about the possible levies and taxes that may be introduced if the management structure changes,” a trader, Emeka Onu, said during the protest.

Traders also argued that the complex is a federal facility and called for broader consultations with stakeholders before any decision is taken regarding its management structure.

The shutdown affected several sections of the market, including the Auto Spare Parts and Machinery Dealers Association (ASPANDA) market, one of the largest spare parts trading hubs in Lagos and a key center for MSME activity in the automotive parts supply chain.

The market hosts thousands of small businesses involved in the sale and distribution of spare parts, machinery and other industrial goods, making it an important commercial hub for traders from across Nigeria and West Africa.

Eyewitnesses said the protest began early in the day as traders closed their shops to draw attention to their concerns and demand dialogue with authorities over the future management of the facility.

Meanwhile, Nigeria’s Minister of Industry, Trade and Investment, Jumoke Oduwole, visited the market as part of efforts to engage traders and encourage the reopening of the complex.

Before beginning her tour of the market, the minister called for the gates of the complex to be opened, explaining that her visit was aimed at interacting with stakeholders and supporting the smooth conduct of business activities.

The situation highlights ongoing concerns among MSMEs over regulatory changes and operational costs, particularly in major trading hubs that serve as critical platforms for small-scale entrepreneurs in Nigeria’s informal and semi-formal economy.

Discussions between government officials and market representatives were ongoing as authorities sought to restore normal commercial activities at the complex.

Traders at the Lagos International Trade Fair Complex shut down business activities to protest a proposed takeover of the market’s management by state and local government authorities. The protest disrupted operations across sections of the complex, including the Auto Spare Parts and Machinery Dealers Association market, as traders expressed concerns that the new arrangement could introduce additional levies and increase the cost of doing business for MSMEs. Meanwhile, Nigeria’s Minister of Industry, Trade and Investment, Jumoke Oduwole, visited the market to engage traders and encourage dialogue aimed at restoring normal commercial activities.
Ogun State Extends OSAMCA Loan Scheme to Civil Servants to Boost Agricultural Production
Ogun State Extends OSAMCA Loan Scheme to Civil Servants to Boost Agricultural Production

Ogun State has expanded the OSAMCA agricultural loan scheme to include civil servants engaged in farming, while reducing the interest rate to 15 percent. The initiative aims to increase food production, support agribusiness activities, and strengthen local agricultural value chains.

The Ogun State government has expanded the loan programme of the Ogun State Agricultural and Multi-Purpose Credit Agency to include civil and public servants as part of efforts to increase agricultural production and strengthen food security.

Approved by Governor Dapo Abiodun, the initiative aims to encourage more public sector workers to engage in farming and agribusiness activities.

Officials say the programme supports the state’s broader strategy to boost local food production, reduce poverty, and encourage supplementary income opportunities for workers.

 

Expanded Access to Agricultural Financing

The OSAMCA loan scheme was previously designed to support farmers and agribusiness operators. The new policy now allows eligible civil servants who are actively involved in agriculture to access financing.

According to a circular issued by the Kehinde Onasanya, applicants must meet the following conditions:

  • Be confirmed civil or public servants

  • Have 3–5 years of service history, or

  • Have at least five years remaining before retirement

The initiative is intended to support workers already engaged in farming or interested in expanding agricultural ventures.

 

Lower Interest Rate to Encourage Participation

To make the programme more accessible, the state government has reduced the loan interest rate from 18 percent to 15 percent.

Other features of the scheme include:

  • Self-guaranteed loan structure

  • Monthly repayment deductions from salaries

  • Financing for farm expansion and agricultural investments

Officials say the revised structure is designed to reduce financial barriers and encourage more workers to participate in farming.

 

Supporting Food Security and Rural Enterprise

By expanding access to agricultural financing, the state government hopes to stimulate local food production while supporting small-scale agricultural businesses.

The initiative is expected to:

  • Increase agricultural productivity within the state

  • Encourage more participation in farming and agribusiness

  • Strengthen local food supply chains

Officials say the programme could help build a stronger agricultural ecosystem involving farmers, suppliers, and agro-processing businesses.

 

Procurement Training to Strengthen Governance

In a separate initiative, the Ogun State Bureau of Public Procurement organised a two-day training programme for heads of procurement units across ministries, departments, and agencies.

The training focused on improving the use of Standard Bidding Documents to promote transparency and ensure compliance with procurement regulations.

According to Bolarinwa Adebowale, the programme aims to standardise procurement practices and reduce poorly executed or abandoned public projects.

Access to affordable financing remains a major challenge for small farmers and agribusinesses. Expanding agricultural credit schemes, especially those targeting workers and small-scale producers can increase food production, support rural enterprises, and strengthen agricultural value chains.
Fidelity Bank Launches Women Empowerment Initiative to Strengthen MSMEs
HerFidelity Apprenticeship Programme targets sustainable business growth for women across multiple sectors.

Fidelity Bank Plc has launched the “Give Her Power” initiative and signed partnership agreements with several organisations to support women’s economic empowerment and strengthen women-led MSMEs in Nigeria. The programme, introduced during activities marking International Women's Day, will provide vocational training, mentorship, financial knowledge, and business support through the bank’s HerFidelity Apprenticeship Programme. The initiative also includes the distribution of equipment and skills training across multiple sectors to help women build sustainable enterprises and expand their participation in Nigeria’s small business economy.

Fidelity Bank Plc has reinforced its commitment to supporting women-led businesses with the signing of strategic Memoranda of Understanding (MoUs) with several partner organisations under its “Give Her Power” initiative, a programme designed to equip Nigerian women with practical skills, mentorship, and financial knowledge to build sustainable enterprises.

The partnerships were formalised during activities marking the 2026 edition of International Women's Day and bring together stakeholders including LUSH Hair, Barbergirl Academy School of Barbering, Dee 'n' Ell the Shoe Architect, Inter-Bau Foundation, Innoson Vehicle Manufacturing, National Credit Guarantee Company, and One Universe.

The collaboration supports the bank’s HerFidelity Apprenticeship Programme, a structured platform aimed at providing vocational training, business support, and enterprise development opportunities for women across different sectors.

Speaking at the event, Managing Director and Chief Executive Officer of Fidelity Bank, Nneka Onyeali-Ikpe, said the initiative reflects the bank’s broader strategy of promoting economic inclusion and strengthening women’s participation in entrepreneurship.

According to her, empowering women through access to skills and financial knowledge is a critical step toward building resilient micro, small and medium enterprises (MSMEs) in Nigeria and across Africa.

“At Fidelity Bank, we strongly believe that empowering women is not only a social responsibility but also a powerful economic strategy.

Through the ‘Give Her Power’ initiative and the HerFidelity Apprenticeship Programme, we are providing women with practical tools, vocational skills and financial knowledge that will enable them to build sustainable businesses,” she said.

The programme will deliver several interventions throughout March, including the distribution of 1,000 sewing and grinding machines to support women-led microbusinesses. Participants will also receive vocational training in areas such as automobile mechanics, interior decoration, barbing, hair making, and shoe making.

Other activities include mentorship sessions with established female entrepreneurs, professional headshot sessions to strengthen personal and business branding, and hands-on skill acquisition training in makeup artistry, nail care, and traditional headgear tying.

Onyeali-Ikpe noted that improving women’s economic participation has far-reaching implications for business growth and economic development, particularly within the MSME sector which forms the backbone of many African economies.

She added that increasing women’s participation in entrepreneurship has the potential to strengthen households, create jobs, and contribute significantly to economic growth.

Also speaking at the event, Folashade Ambrose-Medebem, Lagos State Commissioner for Commerce, Cooperatives, Trade and Investment, commended the initiative, describing it as a strategic intervention that supports entrepreneurship and inclusive economic growth.

She said the programme aligns with the development priorities of the Lagos State Government to expand opportunities for women in business and enterprise development.

Ambrose-Medebem added that women continue to play a vital role in commerce across Lagos, from traditional market trading to emerging digital and creative sectors, noting that initiatives that strengthen their access to skills, mentorship and financing are essential to the growth of MSMEs and broader economic development in Africa.

The “Give Her Power” initiative by Fidelity Bank Plc supports African MSMEs by equipping women entrepreneurs with skills, mentorship, and financial knowledge needed to start, sustain, and scale small businesses that drive local economic growth.
Escalating U.S.–Israel–Iran Conflict Raises Concerns for Global Trade and African MSMEs
Rising oil prices and global supply disruptions threaten African MSMEs, highlighting the wider economic impact of the U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict.

The escalating U.S.–Israel military campaign against Iran has disrupted global trade and driven oil prices sharply higher, raising concerns for African MSMEs that depend on stable energy costs and supply chains, as rising commodity prices and inflation threaten small businesses across the continent.

A widening military confrontation between United States and Israel against Iran has triggered global economic concerns, with analysts warning that a prolonged conflict could disrupt international trade, drive energy prices higher, and place additional pressure on micro, small and medium enterprises (MSMEs) across Africa.

The conflict intensified on February 28 when the United States and Israel launched a coordinated military campaign against Iran, conducting hundreds of airstrikes targeting military infrastructure, air defence systems, and nuclear research facilities. The operation marked a dramatic escalation of tensions in the Middle East and followed months of deteriorating relations over Iran’s nuclear

programme.

Reports indicate that the initial bombardment targeted high-level meetings in Tehran, resulting in the death of Iran’s Supreme Leader, Ali Khamenei, alongside several senior military commanders. The strikes were part of a broader effort by Washington and Tel Aviv to dismantle Iran’s missile capabilities and prevent it from developing nuclear weapons.

Donald Trump, President of the United States, described the objective of the campaign as eliminating Iran’s missile industry and ensuring that the country does not achieve nuclear capability. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu also defended the strikes as a necessary action against what he described as an existential threat.

Iran has since retaliated with hundreds of ballistic missiles and drones targeting Israeli territory and expanding attacks to include civilian infrastructure in Gulf states such as Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, United Arab Emirates, and Saudi Arabia. Analysts say the attacks aim to pressure the United States and its allies into negotiating a ceasefire.

The latest escalation follows earlier hostilities that erupted in June 2025 when Israeli airstrikes targeted Iranian nuclear sites, including facilities at Natanz Nuclear Facility and Fordow Fuel Enrichment Plant. Iran responded with missile and drone attacks on Israeli cities, resulting in casualties on both sides.

International reactions to the conflict have been mixed. António Guterres, Secretary-General of the United Nations, condemned the strikes and warned that the growing confrontation could destabilise multiple countries in the region. Meanwhile, China and Russia have criticised the military campaign, while some European allies remain cautious or neutral.

Economic analysts warn that the conflict is already affecting global markets. According to reports, crude oil prices have surged by more than 20 per cent, exceeding 100 dollars per barrel, as fears of supply disruptions in the Gulf region intensify.

Rising energy prices and potential supply chain disruptions could have significant consequences for African economies, particularly for MSMEs that rely on stable fuel costs and predictable global trade conditions. Increased oil prices often lead to higher transportation and production costs, which can reduce profit margins for small businesses and increase the cost of goods for consumers.

In Nigeria and other African countries, the ripple effects are already emerging, with analysts warning of potential increases in commodity prices and inflation if the conflict persists.

While military analysts suggest the strikes have significantly weakened Iran’s air defence system, experts say the political outcome of the conflict remains uncertain. Many warn that a prolonged confrontation could strain global economic stability and create lasting disruptions for small businesses that depend on international trade and stable energy markets.

The conflict matters to African MSMEs because rising oil prices and global supply disruptions increase production and transportation costs, threatening the profitability and sustainability of small businesses across the continent.
South Korea and Ghana Strengthen Climate, Tech, and Maritime Partnerships to Support African MSMEs
Agreements on climate, technology, and maritime security aim to boost trade, innovation, and growth opportunities for African MSMEs.

South Korea and Ghana have signed agreements on climate cooperation, digital innovation, and maritime security, aiming to strengthen trade, technology access, and safe shipping routes—initiatives that could enhance growth opportunities and support African MSMEs across multiple sectors.

South Korean President Lee Jae Myung and Ghanaian President John Mahama held summit talks on Wednesday, agreeing to deepen cooperation in climate action, digital innovation, and maritime security, with potential benefits for African micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs).

During the summit, the two leaders witnessed the signing of three key agreements: a climate-change cooperation accord, a memorandum of understanding (MoU) on technology and digital innovation, and a maritime safety and security MoU between the

Korea Coast Guard and the Ghana Navy. The maritime agreement is aimed at enhancing coordinated policing and safeguarding regional shipping routes, which are critical for trade and commerce.

President Lee expressed gratitude to Ghana’s navy for its role in protecting South Korean nationals in the Gulf of Guinea, emphasizing that the maritime pact will strengthen joint security efforts in the region.

President Mahama highlighted Ghana’s rich mineral reserves, including bauxite, manganese, gold, lithium, and nickel, noting that collaboration with South Korea could enhance the exploration, processing, and value-chain integration of these resources. Such partnerships are expected to provide African MSMEs with improved access to technology, digital tools, and secure trade channels.

The leaders also explored broader cooperation in economic security, agriculture, education, culture, and critical minerals, and exchanged perspectives on pressing regional and global issues, including Middle East tensions. Analysts suggest that stronger ties between Ghana and South Korea could help MSMEs across Africa access innovative technologies, climate adaptation solutions, and safer maritime trade routes, supporting sustainable business growth and economic resilience.

By combining climate action, digital innovation, and maritime security, the partnership aims to bolster the operational capacity and market reach of African small businesses, creating opportunities for trade, technology transfer, and resource-based entrepreneurship.

As African MSMEs increasingly rely on international partnerships for growth, agreements like these highlight the importance of cross-continental cooperation in promoting sustainable business ecosystems, reducing operational risks, and enhancing competitiveness in global markets.

The partnership matters to African MSMEs because improved access to technology, secure maritime routes, and climate solutions can lower operational risks, expand market opportunities, and support sustainable business growth.
Nigeria Urges Gulf Oil Producers to Invest, Positioning Country as Alternative Supply Hub for African MSMEs
Minister urges Gulf investors to partner with Nigeria, highlighting stable fuel supply as vital for African MSMEs and economic resilience.

Nigeria’s Foreign Minister, Yusuf Tuggar, urged Gulf oil producers to invest in the country to diversify global supply amid the Iran conflict, highlighting Nigeria’s growing output, expanding domestic refining, and untapped reserves as a means to stabilize fuel access and support African MSMEs reliant on energy for operations and trade.

Nigeria’s Foreign Minister, Yusuf Tuggar, has called on Gulf oil and gas producers to view Nigeria as a strategic partner rather than a competitor, highlighting the country’s untapped reserves as a critical alternative source during global supply disruptions. Speaking to Reuters, Tuggar stressed that recent conflict in Iran, which has disrupted shipments through the Strait of Hormuz, underscores the need for diversified energy supply chains.

“The Middle East conflict shows why countries that might otherwise consider us competitors should partner with us and invest, so they can diversify market share and work with us,” Tuggar said.

Nigeria has increased total oil output to approximately 1.7 million barrels per day, up from 1.4 million at the start of President Bola Tinubu’s tenure in 2023, despite challenges such as underinvestment, pipeline vandalism, and theft. The minister noted that new investment in oil fields and pipelines could further expand production, providing stability for global supply and opening opportunities for domestic businesses, including MSMEs, that depend on fuel for operations and transport.

While some analysts caution that Gulf investors may delay African commitments due to regional instability, Tuggar suggested the opposite: “It could make them want to work with countries like Nigeria that are rich in gas and oil … to diversify market share for the benefit of both countries.”

The call for investment follows recent agreements, including the January Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement with United Arab Emirates, aimed at unlocking trade and energy sector investment. Additionally, Qatar-linked investors have announced intentions to invest in Nigeria’s gas sector, though specific timelines remain uncertain.

Analysts warn that investment pledges often face long approval cycles and execution risks in Nigeria, but Tuggar emphasized that Nigeria is increasingly positioned to absorb shocks from global supply disruptions. The country imports large volumes of refined products, which has raised transport and food prices, particularly during high-demand periods such as the Muslim fasting month of Ramadan.

However, expanding domestic refining capacity, including the privately owned Dangote Refinery operating at 650,000 barrels per day, is expected to stabilize the market and support African MSMEs that rely on reliable fuel for business operations.

Tuggar also underscored the continued global relevance of oil: “At the moment the world consumes about 105 to 106 million barrels per day. I don’t see that changing much anytime soon, so we need to work together to ensure enough hydrocarbons are available.”

Experts say that attracting Gulf investment not only strengthens global energy supply chains but also benefits local African businesses by ensuring stable fuel access, supporting transport, production, and operational costs for MSMEs across Nigeria and the continent.

By positioning Nigeria as a reliable alternative supplier, the minister’s appeal highlights the strategic role African energy resources can play in stabilizing markets and supporting small and medium enterprises that are highly sensitive to energy price fluctuations.

It matters to African MSMEs because stable fuel supply and increased investment in Nigeria’s oil and gas sector help lower energy costs, secure reliable transport and production inputs, and support the growth and sustainability of small businesses across the continent.
Nigeria Monitors Oil and Market Risks Amid Rising Middle East Tensions to Protect MSMEs
Rising Middle East tensions push oil prices higher, prompting Nigeria to safeguard MSMEs from fuel and commodity cost shocks.

Nigeria’s Finance Ministry is monitoring rising Middle East tensions and their impact on oil prices, capital flows, and logistics costs, taking steps to protect MSMEs from higher fuel, diesel, cooking gas, and fertiliser prices that could affect production, transport, and business operations.

Nigeria’s government is closely monitoring escalating tensions in the Middle East as it evaluates potential impacts on the country’s economic stability, particularly for micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs) that are sensitive to energy and market fluctuations.

The Finance Ministry announced on Wednesday that it is assessing risks from the ongoing U.S.–Israel conflict with Iran.

Finance Minister Wale Edun convened the Economic Management Team to review how the conflict could affect oil prices, capital flows, and logistics costs in Nigeria. Officials highlighted that volatility in global energy markets, including potential disruptions around the Strait of Hormuz, is already pushing crude oil prices higher.

Rising crude prices are expected to translate into increased domestic costs for fuel, diesel, cooking gas, and fertiliser, commodities that underpin daily operations for Nigerian MSMEs. Analysts warn that energy price spikes could strain production, transportation, and operating expenses for small businesses, particularly in sectors like agriculture, manufacturing, and retail.

The ministry emphasized that early monitoring and proactive policy measures are intended to mitigate risks to businesses and maintain market stability. By tracking global supply chain disruptions and capital market movements, the government aims to protect Nigerian MSMEs from sudden cost increases and potential shortages.

With Nigeria heavily reliant on oil for both government revenue and industrial inputs, officials said maintaining stable energy supplies is essential for economic resilience. MSMEs, which form the backbone of the country’s informal and semi-formal economy, could face significant challenges if fuel and commodity prices remain volatile.

The Economic Management Team is reportedly evaluating strategies including coordinated fuel pricing, strategic reserves management, and support for key supply chains to cushion the impact on small businesses. Finance Minister Edun noted that while global events are largely beyond Nigeria’s control, careful monitoring and timely interventions can reduce shocks to MSMEs and the broader economy.

As global oil markets react to geopolitical tensions, Nigerian MSMEs are expected to face higher operational costs, making government oversight and policy coordination critical to sustaining business activity and protecting livelihoods.

The ministry’s review underscores the importance of proactive economic management in times of international uncertainty, aiming to ensure that small and medium enterprises remain resilient despite external pressures on oil supply and commodity prices.

By keeping a close watch on market developments, Nigeria hopes to provide MSMEs with the stability needed to continue operations, safeguard jobs, and maintain supply chains, even as regional conflicts threaten to disrupt global energy flows.

It matters to MSMEs because rising oil and commodity prices directly increase production, transport, and operating costs, threatening the profitability and sustainability of small and medium-sized businesses across Nigeria.
Ghana Champions Intra-African Trade and Value Addition at 2026 Africa Trade Conference
Ghana promotes value-added supply chains and regional collaboration to boost intra-African trade and support MSME growth.

Ghana is using its role as AfCFTA Secretariat host to promote intra-African trade, value-added supply chains, and regional collaboration, aiming to attract investment and create growth opportunities for MSMEs across the continent.

Ghana is leveraging its position as host of the AfCFTA Secretariat to accelerate intra-African trade and industrialization, officials said at the 2026 Africa Trade Conference in Cape Town’s International Convention Centre. The event, which brings together business leaders and policymakers from across the continent, aims to boost Africa’s integration into global markets.

Speaking to CNBC Africa, Ghana’s Minister of Trade, Agribusiness and Industry, Elizabeth Ofosu-Adjare, highlighted the country’s efforts to shift away from raw material exports toward value-added supply chains, particularly in agribusiness. She emphasized that harmonizing policies across African nations and promoting investment in local processing are key to unlocking industrial growth.

Ofosu-Adjare stressed the importance of patient capital to support long-term industrialization projects across Africa, noting that collective prosperity depends on collaboration among African nations. According to the minister, initiatives that prioritize value addition, regional cooperation, and shared growth will help create stronger, more resilient markets that benefit small and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs) across the continent.

The conference also focused on enhancing trade infrastructure, streamlining regulatory frameworks, and promoting cross-border partnerships, which can provide MSMEs with better access to larger markets and investment opportunities. Ghana’s active role underscores the country’s commitment to fostering an African trade ecosystem that supports inclusive growth, industrialization, and the sustainability of small businesses.

By promoting intra-African trade, value addition, and industrial investment, Ghana aims to strengthen supply chains, reduce reliance on raw material exports, and provide MSMEs with opportunities to scale operations and access new markets across the continent.

It matters to MSMEs because stronger intra-African trade, value-added supply chains, and harmonized policies provide small businesses with greater market access, investment opportunities, and the ability to scale operations across the continent.
Sahara Group Urges African Youths to Turn Energy Challenges into MSME Opportunities
Sahara Group empowers youths to develop innovative energy solutions that create MSME opportunities and drive sustainable economic growth in Africa.

Sahara Group, through the 2026 EIBIC Programme at the University of Lagos, urged African youths to turn energy challenges into entrepreneurial opportunities, introducing solar, bioenergy, and cooling solutions that can help MSMEs access electricity, improve productivity, and drive sustainable economic growth.

Energy conglomerate Sahara Group has called on young Africans to transform the continent’s energy challenges into entrepreneurial opportunities, encouraging them to develop innovative solutions that address Africa’s development gaps. The appeal was made during the 2026 Entrepreneurship, Innovation and Business Certification (EIBIC) Programme hosted by the University of Lagos.

Now in its third year, the EIBIC Programme serves as a learning platform designed to instil an entrepreneurial mindset among students early in their academic journey. Sahara Group delivered a thought leadership session titled “Becoming a M.A.D. Entrepreneur: Powering Africa’s Energy Future,” framing entrepreneurship around the concept of Making A Difference.

The company was also honoured with the EIBIC Champion Award in recognition of its ongoing support and partnership with the university. Vice Chancellor Prof. Folasade Ogunsola commended Sahara Group for promoting innovation and enterprise among young Africans.

Sahara Group’s Head of Corporate Communications, Bethel Obioma, said the partnership aligns with the company’s “Beyond Energy” philosophy, which encourages youths to think differently, collaborate boldly, and act responsibly to address Africa’s energy poverty and sustainability challenges.

Director of Governance and Sustainability, Ejiro Gray, highlighted the need for responsible, inclusive innovation to close Africa’s energy access gap, the widest globally. He emphasised that solutions should be multidisciplinary, spanning policy, finance, healthcare, construction, and technology, to create lasting impact.

Students were introduced to practical energy solutions, including pay-as-you-go solar models, enabling households and micro-enterprises to access electricity despite low incomes, thereby improving productivity and participation in the formal economy. The session also showcased solar-powered cold storage and cooling technologies aimed at reducing food waste, protecting farmer livelihoods, and stabilising supply chains.

Additionally, participants explored circular bioenergy models that convert agricultural waste into electricity and clean cooking fuel, demonstrating how rural energy access can be expanded while generating local economic value.

Through the EIBIC Programme, Sahara Group is deliberately shaping young minds to see Africa’s energy challenges as opportunities for MSMEs, fostering resilience, sustainability, and innovation that can drive enterprise growth and long-term economic development across the continent.

It matters to MSMEs because innovative energy solutions like solar and bioenergy reduce operational costs, improve productivity, and expand access to electricity, enabling small businesses to grow sustainably and participate more fully in the economy.
Union Bank Launches ‘Give to Gain’ Campaign to Empower Women with Disabilities and Caregivers
Initiative provides mentorship, training, and financial support to help women with disabilities build sustainable businesses and economic opportunities.

Union Bank of Nigeria has launched the “Give to Gain” campaign to support women living with disabilities and caregivers through mentorship, business training, and educational support, aiming to expand economic opportunities and promote inclusive participation in entrepreneurship and professional development.

Union Bank of Nigeria has launched its ‘Give to Gain: Creating Pathways for Inclusion and Endless Opportunities’ campaign, a strategic initiative aimed at advancing gender equity by supporting women living with disabilities and those raising children with disabilities. The initiative coincides with International Women’s Month 2026 and underscores the bank’s commitment to fostering social and economic empowerment for historically marginalized groups.

The flagship launch event in Lagos brought together women with disabilities, caregivers, advocacy organisations, and key stakeholders to facilitate dialogue, mentorship, and access to essential resources. The campaign is designed to move beyond advocacy, creating deliberate systems of support to help women thrive in entrepreneurship, education, and professional careers.

Olufunmilola Aluko, Chief Brand and Marketing Officer at Union Bank, highlighted the importance of intentional inclusion, stating, “At Union Bank, inclusion is not an abstract ideal; it is a deliberate choice. Women living with disabilities and women raising children with disabilities are too often left out entirely. Opening access and support for them creates value that extends to families, communities, and society at large.”

The campaign features a multi-faceted approach, including tailored business sustainability training through the bank’s women-focused banking proposition, Alpher, and strategic partnerships with educational institutions. Mentorship programmes are being deployed to build confidence, enhance capabilities, and provide learning resources to female students with disabilities.

Within the bank, the employee-led WeHub network is supporting an inclusive corporate culture and professional growth, reinforcing the campaign’s internal and external impact.

Aligned with the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals, particularly Goal 5 (gender equality) and Goal 10 (reduced inequalities), the initiative aims to ensure women with disabilities have access to economic opportunities, entrepreneurial support, and pathways for sustainable growth.

By combining mentorship, training, and inclusive policies, Union Bank seeks to empower women with disabilities and caregivers, helping them overcome systemic barriers and participate fully in economic life, including entrepreneurship and professional development.

This initiative highlights the potential for financial institutions to foster inclusive economic ecosystems, creating opportunities for women whose contributions are often overlooked, while strengthening the broader social and economic fabric.

It matters to African MSMEs because inclusive training, mentorship, and financial support enable women with disabilities to start and grow small businesses, expanding entrepreneurship, job creation, and economic participation across the continent.
Financial Regulation Experts Urge Greater Women Participation in Capital Markets to Drive Enterprise Growth
Industry leaders call for stronger female participation in investment and capital markets to expand access to funding and support women-led enterprises.

Stakeholders at an Chartered Institute of Stockbrokers event marking International Women's Day highlighted the growing influence of women in Nigeria’s financial markets, urging greater participation in investment, capital formation, and strategic partnerships to strengthen women-led enterprises and market development.

Stakeholders at the 2026 International Women’s Day celebration organised by the Chartered Institute of Stockbrokers have highlighted the growing influence of women in Nigeria’s financial markets and the need to strengthen their participation in investment and capital formation.

The event, held under the theme “Give to Gain - Women Empowering Women Through Capital, Advisory & Investment,” brought together regulators, financial professionals, and industry leaders to discuss strategies for increasing women’s access to capital and long-term investment opportunities.

Discussions focused on the topic “Why Women Must Own the Capital Conversation,” with speakers emphasising the importance of adopting long-term investment perspectives and forming strategic partnerships to reduce the risks associated with single-owner enterprises.

Panellists noted that women-led companies listed on the Nigerian Exchange Limited are among the best-performing, demonstrating the potential of female-led enterprises to drive sustainable growth in the financial market.

Speakers encouraged women entrepreneurs and business leaders to leverage innovation and diversify investment opportunities to attract capital for business expansion. They also highlighted the role of collaboration, financial literacy, and structured corporate governance in strengthening women-led businesses.

The panel featured key industry figures including Jude Chiemeka, Chief Executive Officer of the Nigerian Exchange Limited; Adebola Oruma, Group Head of Gender Business at the Bank of Industry; Lilian Olubi, Co-founder of the Drug Aid Africa Initiative; Raliat

Oyetunde, Principal Consultant at Prinsult Global Consulting; and Tunji Andrews, Chief Executive Officer of Awabah. The discussion was moderated by Simisola Ojumu, Managing Director of Regius Asset Management.

Participants highlighted women’s increasing contributions to capital formation, investment decision-making, and financial leadership within Nigeria’s capital markets.

In his welcome remarks, the 13th President and Chairman of the institute, Oluropo Dada, commended women for their significant role in market development and their continued contributions to the growth of the capital market ecosystem.

The event also featured the participation of the institute’s leadership, including First Vice President Fiona Ahimie, Second Vice President Akeem Oyewale, Registrar and CEO Ayo Adeonipekun, Board of Fellows member Elizabeth Ebi, and Council Member Elile Olutimayin.

Organisers noted that the programme attracted strong participation both physically and virtually, reflecting growing interest in advancing women’s leadership in the financial sector.

Panellists concluded by calling for sustained efforts to cultivate long-term vision among women entrepreneurs, strengthen corporate structures, and support enterprises capable of building lasting value within Nigeria’s financial markets.

It matters to African MSMEs because greater participation of women in capital markets can expand access to funding, investment networks, and financial expertise needed to grow and sustain small businesses across the continent.
Matna Foods Partners Ondo State to Boost Youth and Women Participation in Cassava Farming
Initiative Targets Youth and Women to Strengthen Nigeria’s Cassava Value Chain

Matna Foods Company Limited, in partnership with the Ondo State Government and the Partnership Initiatives in the Niger Delta Foundation, has launched the Women in Sourcing and Enterprise Programme to empower 3,000 youth and women farmers in cassava production, providing training, inputs, and guaranteed market access to promote sustainable agricultural growth.

Matna Foods Company Limited has teamed up with the Ondo State Government to empower 3,000 outgrower farmers, focusing on youth and women, through the Women in Sourcing and Enterprise Programme, aimed at strengthening the cassava value chain.

The initiative targets primarily young women aged 18–35 and is implemented in partnership with the Partnership Initiatives in the Niger Delta Foundation. It provides farmers with improved inputs, technical training, and access to guaranteed markets via Matna Foods’ processing facilities, fostering sustainable agricultural development.

Speaking at a stakeholder session, Matna Foods’ Managing Director, Oska Aiyeleso, said the programme seeks to create long-term economic value while strengthening agricultural supply chains. “By connecting young farmers directly to structured markets, we are creating jobs and laying the foundation for inclusive agricultural growth that benefits communities across Ondo State,” he stated.

Government officials also emphasised the importance of collaboration. Olaleye Akinola highlighted agriculture as a key pillar of economic development, stressing that public-private partnerships can mobilise land, technical expertise, and extension services to ensure a steady raw material supply for agro-processing.

The Commissioner for Women Affairs, Seun Osamaye, praised the programme for prioritising women and vulnerable groups, saying it creates meaningful opportunities for women to achieve economic independence. Similarly, Segun Omoyofunmi noted that agriculture offers a pathway for youth empowerment and self-reliance.

The initiative will run for 39 months, supporting participants through two cultivation cycles. Beneficiaries start with 0.4 hectares of farmland and can expand as they gain experience. The programme combines farmer training, extension support, and structured market access to promote sustainable participation in the cassava value chain.

Other stakeholders, including the Ondo State Disability Board, reaffirmed commitment to inclusive development, ensuring that persons living with disabilities can also benefit.

Through this partnership, Matna Foods and the Ondo State Government aim to create a robust, inclusive agricultural ecosystem that empowers women and youth, strengthens rural livelihoods, and supports the growth of Nigeria’s agro-processing sector.

By connecting smallholder farmers to structured markets and technical support, the programme enhances the capacity, productivity, and income of agribusiness MSMEs, fostering inclusive economic growth across Africa’s agricultural sector.
UBA Launches ‘Gen.W’ Initiative to Empower Female Entrepreneurs Across Africa
UBA’s Gen.W Platform Provides Resources, Mentorship, and Financial Support to African Women Entrepreneurs

United Bank for Africa (UBA) launched its Gen.W initiative during its quarterly Business Series in Lagos, empowering female entrepreneurs through mentorship, financial support, and practical resources. The programme encourages women to take ownership of leadership, create their own opportunities, and build sustainable businesses, reinforcing UBA’s commitment to supporting women-led enterprises across Africa.

United Bank for Africa (UBA) Plc marked International Women’s Month with a special edition of its quarterly Business Series, themed around the “Evolved Woman,” at the UBA House in Lagos. The hybrid event brought together female leaders, entrepreneurs, and professionals to discuss redefining leadership, ambition, and success for modern African women.

Opening the session, UBA’s Group Head of Customer Experience, Michelle Nwoga, encouraged women to step forward without waiting for invitations to lead. “Women are now taking a stand and stepping up. They’re not waiting to be asked; they are volunteering and making themselves known,” she said.

The event, moderated by media personality Tobi Bakre, emphasized that an “Evolved Woman” embodies self-worth and communal support rather than following a checklist. “It is a woman who is grounded in her own self-worth and gives room for other women to be grounded in theirs,” Bakre remarked.

During a panel discussion, industry leaders, including Joycee Awosika, digital entrepreneur Tomike Adeoye, Fine-Funky founder Olufunke Davies, and broadcaster Ayo Mario-Ese, highlighted autonomy as a key aspect of female entrepreneurship. Awosika stressed, “That woman does not need to ask to sit at the table; she is creating her own table and adding value.” Adeoye added, “The evolved woman is now bringing their own table, being vocal about struggles, and setting her standards while pursuing her dreams.”

UBA also introduced the Gen.W platform, a lifestyle and business initiative providing women with practical resources, expert insights, financial support, loans, and discounted products regardless of banking status. Alero Ladipo explained, “Raising each woman up is not that hard when you leverage community and shared stories; women need to take a position to give to others.”

The event reinforced UBA’s commitment to fostering women-led businesses, providing tools and support to help female entrepreneurs grow, innovate, and strengthen their presence in Africa’s economic ecosystem.

By equipping female entrepreneurs with capital, skills, and mentorship, the Gen.W initiative strengthens women-led MSMEs, fostering innovation, economic inclusion, and sustainable growth within Africa’s entrepreneurial ecosystem.
Guinness Nigeria Highlights Women Empowerment as Catalyst for Business Growth
Promoting Mentorship, Inclusion, and Leadership Pathways for Women Entrepreneurs

Guinness Nigeria Plc celebrated International Women’s Day with a panel session emphasizing mentorship, inclusion, and women’s empowerment, highlighting how supporting female leaders strengthens businesses, industries, and communities while fostering sustainable growth for women-led enterprises and MSMEs.

Guinness Nigeria Plc has reinforced its commitment to advancing women’s leadership and entrepreneurship in the corporate and business sectors, using mentorship, inclusion, and collective empowerment as drivers for sustainable value creation.

The company marked International Women’s Day 2026 with a panel session at its Lagos headquarters, themed “Give to Gain: How We Create Value by Lifting Women.” The event brought together corporate leaders, development experts, and employees to explore strategies for strengthening leadership opportunities for women and building more inclusive institutions.

Speaking at the event, Managing Director and Chief Executive Officer, Girish Sharma, emphasised that empowering women is both a social responsibility and a business imperative. “Empowering women is not only the right thing to do, but it is also essential for building stronger organisations and communities. At Guinness Nigeria, we believe that when opportunities are shared and people are supported to grow and lead, businesses and societies thrive together,” Sharma said.

The event featured a keynote address by Olufunke Amobi, Executive Director, Operations at Stanbic IBTC Bank, and a panel discussion including IT professional Folorunsho Aliu, UN Women National Consultant Hansatu Adegbite, and Human Resources Director at Guinness Nigeria Plc, Ayodeji Ajibola. Panelists discussed the critical role of mentorship in developing leadership skills and highlighted the importance of women’s representation across Nigeria’s corporate landscape.

Amobi noted, “There are rooms for women to thrive everywhere in Nigeria, and we must continue to claim our place with confidence and preparation. When women are supported to grow, they not only lead organisations; they expand possibilities for others and create a cycle of impact that strengthens industries and communities alike.”

Panel discussions also explored how inclusive workplace policies, empathetic leadership, and structured mentorship can remove structural barriers that limit women’s advancement. The session reinforced the importance of organisations embedding gender inclusion in core strategies, providing mentorship networks, and recognising diverse perspectives across business operations.

Guinness Nigeria pledged to continue creating platforms that encourage women-led initiatives, expand leadership pathways, and amplify women’s voices within organisational hierarchies. The company stressed that cultivating women’s empowerment not only enhances corporate culture but also drives growth for the wider business ecosystem.

For African micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs), initiatives like Guinness Nigeria’s offer practical lessons on the value of mentorship, inclusion, and collaborative growth. By supporting female entrepreneurs, MSMEs can leverage increased skills, leadership opportunities, and networks, creating a multiplier effect that boosts productivity, innovation, and economic resilience across Africa’s business landscape.

The event demonstrates that deliberate efforts to uplift women in business translate into broader societal and economic benefits, strengthening the foundations for sustainable enterprise development and long-term growth for both corporations and MSMEs.

Empowering women through mentorship, inclusive policies, and leadership opportunities helps African MSMEs grow by boosting female entrepreneurship, improving business resilience, and expanding access to networks and markets.
NGO Launches Sustainable Fashion Initiative to Support Women in Circular Economy
The Oniparo Project highlights women’s role in clothing redistribution, environmental sustainability, and livelihoods in Western Nigeria.

EcoSmart Club has launched the Oniparo Project, a nine-month research initiative aimed at documenting and supporting women involved in traditional clothing trade. The project promotes sustainable fashion, circular economy practices, and livelihood preservation, while raising awareness about textile waste and environmental impacts.

EcoSmart Club, a youth-led climate action organisation, has unveiled the Oniparo Project, a nine-month initiative aimed at promoting sustainable fashion while empowering women engaged in traditional clothing trade across Western Nigeria.

The project highlights the work of women known as Oniparos, who collect and exchange used clothes through a barter-style trade system. This long-standing practice not only provides affordable clothing to low-income communities but also supports livelihoods for thousands of women and their families.

According to EcoSmart Club Founder Hannah Omokhaye, the initiative will document the environmental and economic contributions of these women, raising awareness about circular economy practices. “These women are pioneers of the circular economy in Nigeria. They reduce textile waste, create jobs, and provide affordable clothing while preserving cultural trade practices,” she said.

The Oniparo trade is increasingly threatened by economic changes, climate variability, and shifts in consumption patterns, including growing demand for disposable fashion. Omokhaye emphasised the importance of recognising and preserving this traditional business model.

Supported by the African Climate Alliance, the Oniparo Project will run from March to October 2026. The initiative will also engage the public in discussions about the environmental impact of textile waste, while advocating for policy recognition of informal women-led circular economy efforts.

Project Manager Oluwatoyin Ajao added, “Through inclusive storytelling, the project not only preserves culture but also showcases Afrocentric solutions to global challenges like textile waste and environmental sustainability.”

The project aligns with global concerns over the textile industry’s environmental footprint. The United Nations Environment Programme reports that approximately 92 million tonnes of textile waste are generated globally each year, with a large portion ending up in landfills or incinerated.

EcoSmart Club intends to complement the initiative with ongoing community programs, including recycling and upcycling projects, green skill development, and tree planting, reinforcing its commitment to environmental sustainability and women’s economic empowerment.

By focusing on women-led circular trade, the Oniparo Project creates a model for sustainable business practices that could inspire African MSMEs to adopt environmentally conscious approaches while supporting local livelihoods.

By supporting women-led clothing trade and promoting circular economy practices, the project provides African MSMEs with opportunities for sustainable business models, affordable supply chains, and inclusive local economic growth.
Unilever–UNICEF Programme Equips 900,000 Nigerian Youth with Digital and Employability Skills
Future-X Campus Ambassadors initiative uses digital learning and university networks to prepare young Nigerians for the modern workforce.

The Future-X Unilever Campus Ambassadors Programme, a collaboration between Unilever Nigeria and UNICEF, has trained more than 900,000 Nigerian youths in digital, entrepreneurial, and employability skills. The initiative combines digital platforms and campus networks to help students prepare for modern career opportunities and entrepreneurship.

A joint initiative between Unilever Nigeria Plc and UNICEF has helped equip more than 900,000 Nigerian youths with employability, digital, and entrepreneurial skills to improve their prospects in the evolving labour market.

The programme, known as the Future-X Unilever Campus Ambassadors Programme (FUCAP)—was launched about two and a half years ago to support young people transitioning from education into employment or entrepreneurship.

By combining digital learning platforms, campus engagement, and career mentorship, the initiative aims to bridge the gap between academic knowledge and industry expectations.

 

Building Skills for the Future Workforce

FUCAP focuses on preparing students for the rapidly changing global workplace by offering access to training and career development resources.

The programme provides:

  • Digital and technology skills training

  • Career guidance and employability workshops

  • Entrepreneurship and innovation education

  • Mentorship from industry professionals

Participants gain exposure to career pathways while building practical competencies needed for modern workplaces.

 

Leveraging Digital Platforms and Campus Networks

The programme operates through Youth Agency Marketplace, a digital platform designed to connect young people to skills training and career opportunities.

In addition to online learning, the initiative also uses:

  • Campus ambassador networks across universities

  • Social media engagement to reach students nationwide

  • Career seminars and mentorship events

According to Tobi Adeniyi, more than 85,000 students have participated in 30 physical career seminars organised across universities and polytechnics by trained campus ambassadors.

 

Part of a Global Youth Employment Initiative

The programme forms part of Generation Unlimited, a global public-private partnership aimed at connecting young people to education, skills development, and employment opportunities.

In Nigeria, the initiative is chaired by Kashim Shettima and focuses on improving pathways for youth employment and entrepreneurship.

According to Celine Lafoucriere, the programme currently operates in 20 universities, where students receive digital skills training, career guidance, and mentorship to support their transition into the workforce.

Africa’s youth population represents a major opportunity for entrepreneurship and innovation. Programmes that equip young people with digital, business, and employability skills can help create the next generation of MSME founders, innovators, and skilled professionals across the continent.
Centre for Enterprise Governance Urges Stronger Governance in Women-Led MSMEs
Structured advisory boards, internal controls, and risk management systems highlighted as key to scaling women-owned businesses in Nigeria.

The Centre for Enterprise Governance (CEG) has urged women-led MSMEs in Nigeria to strengthen governance systems, including advisory boards, internal controls, and risk management, to enable sustainable growth. Dr. Adeyinka Hassan, CEG’s founder, highlighted that weak governance limits access to finance and hinders scaling. The centre is promoting mentorship, capacity-building, and practical support to help women entrepreneurs transform their businesses from personality-driven ventures to structured, investment-ready enterprises. Financial institutions are also encouraged to support governance development alongside funding initiatives.

The Centre for Enterprise Governance (CEG) has called on women-led enterprises in Nigeria to strengthen governance systems, warning that weak structures continue to limit business growth and sustainability.

Dr. Adeyinka Hassan, Founder of CEG, explained that while women are highly active in the country’s micro, small, and medium enterprise (MSME) sector, many struggle to scale due to governance gaps. “Enterprise governance is fundamental to business sustainability. It transforms personality-driven ventures into structured organisations capable of attracting investment, strategic partnerships, and institutional financing,” Hassan said.

He highlighted common weaknesses in women-led MSMEs, including poor separation of personal and business finances, limited documentation of policies, absence of advisory boards, and weak risk management and performance monitoring systems. These gaps, Hassan noted, make it difficult for enterprises to grow or withstand external shocks.

“Women play a significant role across trade, services, agriculture, and the creative economy. However, their representation declines as businesses scale, and bridging this gap requires institutional support, access to capital, and robust governance frameworks,” he added.

CEG is raising awareness among women entrepreneurs about the importance of structured support for governance, through initiatives that include capacity-building, governance education, and access to practical resources. Hassan emphasised mentorship and advisory boards as critical tools for enhancing strategic oversight, decision-making, and business resilience.

“Mentorship provides guidance, confidence, and access to networks, while structured advisory systems introduce accountability and strategic discipline. When women entrepreneurs are supported by experienced advisors, they gain critical perspectives on strategy, risk management, financial planning, and growth opportunities,” Hassan explained.

He also called on financial institutions to integrate governance support into their services, linking access to finance with training in financial management and reporting structures. “Banks and development finance institutions can play a catalytic role by supporting governance development within women-led enterprises,” he said.

Hassan acknowledged progress in the banking sector, citing regulatory interventions that have increased women’s representation on boards, but stressed that ongoing governance capacity building remains critical for improving business resilience and access to finance.

“Empowering women-led MSMEs with stronger governance capabilities is not only a gender inclusion objective; it is an economic imperative. Strengthened governance improves resilience, enhances access to finance, and positions enterprises to contribute more meaningfully to national economic development,” Hassan said.

Stronger governance in women-led MSMEs improves resilience, access to finance, and growth potential, enabling these enterprises to create jobs, drive innovation, and contribute meaningfully to Africa’s economic development.
NGO Trains 450 Lagos Women in Trade Skills, Boosts Economic Independence
Humanity Family Foundation for Peace and Development, supported by the French Embassy, equips women with vocational tools, training, and financial literacy to expand businesses and livelihoods.

Over 450 women engaged in trade and vocational businesses in Lagos were empowered through a programme by Humanity Family Foundation for Peace and Development (HUFFPED) with support from the French Embassy. The initiative provided business-specific equipment, training on financial literacy, marketing, and Village Savings and Loan Association schemes, fostering autonomy and long-term sustainability. Beneficiaries reported improved business management, increased income, and enhanced capacity to plan for growth.

Over 450 women engaged in trade and vocational businesses across Lagos State have been empowered through a programme organised by the Humanity Family Foundation for Peace and Development (HUFFPED), with support from the French Embassy in Nigeria. The initiative, held at the Police Officers’ Wives Association Hall in Ikeja, forms part of activities marking International Women’s Day 2026.

The empowerment programme aimed to provide participants with vocational skills, business equipment, and financial literacy training to enhance their income-generating activities and foster sustainable economic independence. Beneficiaries received items tailored to their trades, including sewing machines, stonemasonry tools, hairdressing equipment, and materials for food vending.

Speaking at the event, Pierre Louis Bonnel, a representative of the French Embassy, highlighted the embassy’s ongoing support for civil society organisations working on gender equality and women’s economic empowerment. “The key is sustainability.
