These women are empowered to generate more income and expand their businesses independently,” he said, noting that the programme promotes autonomy rather than dependency.

HUFFPED’s Executive Director, Henry Adenigba, explained that the initiative is part of the Household Economy Strengthening and Resilience project, running for over a year and including capacity-building interventions and continuous monitoring. “We are providing support to boost the businesses these women are already running, helping them create opportunities to sustain their livelihoods and support their families,” Adenigba said.

Executive Director, Finance and Administration at HUFFPED, Adekemi Adeyeye, urged participants to protect their capital and grow their businesses. Beneficiaries also received training on record-keeping, marketing strategies, and financial literacy, including participation in Village Savings and Loan Associations (VSLA), which enable group-based saving and loan access.

Several participants shared their experiences. Oluwaseun Abibat, a barber, expressed gratitude for the financial literacy training that helped her manage her business more effectively. Food vendor Jimoh Rashidat said loans obtained through the VSLA scheme enabled her to expand her business, while Adeniran Funmilayo highlighted improved financial management for both business and family expenses.

The programme underscores the critical role of targeted skill development, financial empowerment, and community-based support in strengthening women-led businesses. By equipping women entrepreneurs with the tools, knowledge, and confidence to expand their enterprises, the initiative not only boosts individual livelihoods but also contributes to broader economic development in Lagos and beyond.

HUFFPED and its partners plan to continue monitoring progress and providing capacity-building support, ensuring that women participants can maintain business growth and economic resilience over the long term.

By equipping women entrepreneurs with skills, resources, and financial literacy, the programme strengthens the capacity of micro and small businesses, promotes local economic development, and encourages sustainable growth within African MSME ecosystems.
Stanbic Bank Foundation Drives Kisumu’s MSME Growth Through Capital and Training
Empowering Traders, Building Economies: How Kisumu’s Micro-Entrepreneurs Are Driving Inclusive Growth

The Stanbic Bank Foundation, in partnership with the Kisumu County Government and the Gates Foundation, has significantly boosted the MSME sector in Kisumu. Through targeted loans, financial literacy training, and digital skills support, over 7,000 traders have received capital, and more than 25,000 entrepreneurs have enhanced their business skills. The initiative strengthens small enterprises, promotes financial inclusion, and is being considered for regional expansion, demonstrating how micro-businesses can drive sustainable local economic growth.

The Stanbic Bank Foundation is transforming Kisumu’s micro, small, and medium enterprise (MSME) sector by providing financial support, training, and digital skills to low-income traders, strengthening the backbone of the city’s informal economy.

Speaking during a high-level courtesy call, Kisumu Deputy Governor Mathews Owili described the partnership as “a dependable ally in our mission to scale up the MSME sector,” highlighting the county’s strategy to prioritize small enterprises as drivers of inclusive growth.

Since its launch in 2024, the programme has injected KSh 13 million ($100,426) in working capital through Kisumu County’s Department of Trade. More than 7,000 traders have benefited from direct financial support, while over 25,000 low-income entrepreneurs have received structured training in enterprise development, financial management, and business planning.

The initiative, implemented in collaboration with the Gates Foundation, targets informal traders who often face barriers to conventional banking, such as lack of collateral or credit history. Mercy Githanji, a programme coordinator, emphasised that “financial inclusion must be accompanied by financial literacy. Capital alone does not guarantee enterprise sustainability.”

County officials noted the programme’s cross-sector benefits. Trade authorities see it as a stimulus for enterprise; finance officials view it as a pathway to broaden the tax base, and education leaders highlight the skills development opportunities.

Beyond finance, the Stanbic Bank Foundation has supported ICT training and donated computers to vocational training centres, equipping entrepreneurs with digital skills critical for mobile payments and online marketplaces. “Preparing the next generation of entrepreneurs for a digital economy is central to sustaining growth,” Githanji added.

Officials also revealed plans to evaluate the programme for regional expansion into Uganda and South Africa, aiming to replicate its inclusive urban enterprise model across the continent.

Dr. Owili remarked that empowering small traders “strengthens households, markets, and ultimately the local economy,” underscoring the pivotal role of micro-businesses in driving sustainable urban growth.

By aligning capital, capacity-building, and digital empowerment, the Stanbic Bank Foundation is helping Kisumu’s informal sector flourish, demonstrating how grassroots entrepreneurship can reshape local economies and serve as a model for MSME development across Africa.

For African MSMEs, access to both capital and structured training is often the key to survival and growth. The Kisumu model demonstrates that combining financial support with skills development enhances business sustainability, fosters entrepreneurship, and strengthens local economies. It offers a blueprint for other African cities, showing that empowering small-scale traders and informal enterprises can drive inclusive growth, reduce poverty, and create resilient economic ecosystems.
Kenya Launches Fursa Index to Track MSME Readiness for Structured Markets
First national assessment provides insights into MSME preparedness, highlighting gaps and opportunities for growth

Kenya has launched its first MSME Opportunity Readiness Index, the Fursa Index, to assess how prepared small and medium enterprises are to access structured markets. Drawing on insights from over 10,000 enterprises nationwide, the index highlights gaps in organisation, capacity, and access to economic opportunities. The launch, part of the SalesFest Africa - Chanuka Jipange Edition, emphasised the importance of structured systems, sector-specific solutions, and behavioural mindsets in enabling MSMEs to scale, attract investment, and compete globally.

Kenya has officially launched its first-ever Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprise (MSME) Opportunity Readiness Index, called the Fursa Index, during the SalesFest Africa - Chanuka Jipange Edition. The index evaluates how ready Kenyan MSMEs are to access economic opportunities and participate in structured markets, drawing on insights from over 10,000 enterprises across all 47 counties.

Speaking at the launch, Principal Secretary for MSME Development, Susan Mang’eni, emphasized the value of the findings for shaping national policies. She challenged stakeholders to consider how informal businesses, such as boda boda operators and hawkers, could integrate into organised systems. “The Fursa Index offers insights that can help shape policies responsive to the real experiences of entrepreneurs,” she said.

Behavioural scientist Irene Njoroge highlighted that structural barriers, rather than effort alone, often limit entrepreneurial growth. She identified three mindsets, survival, blame, and hustle, that can prevent MSMEs from scaling into structured markets.

Clement Tulezi, CEO of the Kenya Flower Council, showcased the flower industry as a model of structured operations, demonstrating how organisation can enhance market access and competitiveness. In contrast, livestock expert David Maina cited the sector’s lack of organisation, calling for grading systems and feeding hubs similar to the Galana Kulalu model to unlock economic potential for pastoralists and smallholder farmers.

The Fursa Index launch signals the start of a national conversation on creating structured opportunity systems that allow Kenyan MSMEs to grow sustainably, attract investment, and compete on global platforms. By identifying readiness gaps and sector-specific challenges, the initiative aims to guide targeted interventions and policy reforms that strengthen the country’s entrepreneurial ecosystem.

The Fursa Index provides a practical benchmark for African MSMEs to understand their readiness to participate in structured markets, access funding, and scale operations. By highlighting gaps in organisation, capacity, and market access, it helps entrepreneurs identify areas for improvement and equips policymakers to create supportive frameworks. For African MSMEs, such tools are critical in transforming informal businesses into resilient, competitive enterprises that can attract investment, formalise operations, and contribute to economic growth across the continent.
BoI, Plateau State Launch N4 Billion Fund to Boost MSMEs
Empowering Women and Youth-Led Businesses Through Affordable Financing

The Bank of Industry (BoI) and Plateau State Government have partnered to launch a N4 billion matching fund aimed at boosting MSMEs across the state. Each party contributes N2 billion to provide long-term, low-interest financing for equipment acquisition and working capital, particularly for women- and youth-led businesses. Loans have a maximum limit of N100 million, a tenor of up to five years, and a 12-month moratorium. The initiative also includes training and capacity-building through Entrepreneurship Development Centres, focusing on sectors like agro-processing, manufacturing, and value addition, while promoting product clusters for sustainable enterprise growth.

Plateau State has partnered with the Bank of Industry (BoI) to establish a N4 billion matching fund aimed at expanding affordable financing for Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs) across the state’s 17 local government areas.

The BoI-PLSG Matching Fund will see both the state government and BoI contributing N2 billion each to provide long-term financing at single-digit interest rates for equipment acquisition and working capital. The fund targets especially women- and youth-led businesses. Loans under the initiative have a maximum limit of N100 million per beneficiary, a repayment tenor of up to five years, and a moratorium of up to 12 months from disbursement.

Speaking at the signing ceremony at the Government House in Jos, Plateau State Governor Caleb Mutfwang said the partnership would stimulate MSME growth and enhance the state’s contribution to the national economy. “The Plateau State government and the Bank of Industry are contributing N2 billion each to facilitate this partnership aimed at stimulating MSMEs in the state, with focus on women and youths,” Mutfwang said, noting that the initiative aligns with national efforts to build a $1 trillion economy.

BoI Managing Director and CEO Olasupo Olusi highlighted the bank’s active role in Plateau since 2020, disbursing N8.46 billion to 257 enterprises across sectors. “These interventions span several programmes, including the Federal Government’s MSME and Manufacturing Intervention Funds, the Rural Area Programme on Investment for Development (RAPID), and BOI-funded projects,” he said.

Olusi also praised Plateau State’s business reforms, noting that its improved ranking in the Ease of Doing Business index reflects deliberate efforts to attract investment and private sector participation.

As part of the initiative, BoI will organise training and capacity-building programmes for beneficiaries through accredited Entrepreneurship Development Centres. The programmes will focus on value addition in sectors such as poultry, block making, oil and rice milling, agro-mechanisation, packaging, pharmaceuticals, and bakery and confectionery. Additionally, the partnership aims to encourage the development of product clusters to support specialised entrepreneurs and promote sustainable business growth in the state.

The BoI-PLSG Matching Fund is expected to strengthen local MSMEs, improve access to finance, and create jobs, particularly for women and youth, contributing to inclusive economic development in Plateau State.

This partnership provides African MSMEs with critical access to affordable capital, enabling them to expand operations, acquire essential equipment, and increase productivity. By coupling finance with capacity-building, the initiative strengthens business resilience, encourages formalisation, and enhances competitiveness. For women- and youth-led enterprises, it fosters inclusion, while promoting clustered development helps create sector-specific economies of scale that can drive broader economic growth across the continent.
Union Bank Launches ‘Give to Gain’ Campaign to Empower Women with Disabilities
New initiative provides training, mentorship, and education support to promote inclusive economic participation.

Union Bank has launched the “Give to Gain” campaign to support women living with disabilities and caregivers through entrepreneurship training, mentorship, and education support. The initiative aims to promote inclusive economic participation and expand opportunities for women often excluded from traditional business and career pathways.

Union Bank of Nigeria has launched the “Give to Gain” campaign, a new initiative designed to promote gender equity by empowering women living with disabilities and women raising children with disabilities.

The programme, unveiled during International Women’s Month 2026, aims to expand access to entrepreneurship support, professional development, and educational opportunities for women from historically marginalised communities.

Titled “Give to Gain: Creating Pathways for Inclusion and Endless Opportunities,” the initiative brings together advocacy organisations, mentors, and community stakeholders to create practical systems that help women thrive in business, education, and careers.

 

Expanding Economic Opportunities Through Inclusion

The campaign focuses on creating meaningful pathways for women with disabilities to participate more fully in economic and professional life.

Key objectives of the initiative include:

  • Promoting entrepreneurship and business sustainability skills

  • Expanding mentorship and professional development opportunities

  • Supporting education access for female students with disabilities

  • Encouraging inclusive corporate and business environments

The programme launch event in Lagos brought together women with disabilities, caregivers, advocacy organisations, and other stakeholders to foster dialogue and collaboration.

 

Training and Mentorship for Women Entrepreneurs

Through its women-focused banking platform Alpher, the bank is providing business sustainability training for women entrepreneurs with disabilities and caregivers.

The initiative also includes:

  • Mentorship programmes designed to build confidence and leadership capacity

  • Access to business and financial management training

  • Opportunities to connect with support networks and industry professionals

According to Olufunmilola Aluko, intentional inclusion is critical to unlocking the economic potential of marginalised groups.

She noted that empowering women with disabilities can create wider benefits for families, communities, and the broader economy.

 

Supporting Education and Inclusive Workplace Culture

The campaign also includes collaborations with educational institutions to provide learning materials and academic support for female students with disabilities.

Within the bank, the initiative is reinforced through WeHub, an employee-led platform focused on promoting inclusion, professional development, and gender equity in the workplace.

The programme aligns with the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals, particularly:

  • Goal 5 – Gender Equality

  • Goal 10 – Reduced Inequalities

Inclusive entrepreneurship initiatives help expand economic participation by enabling underrepresented groups to start and grow businesses. Supporting women with disabilities through training, mentorship, and financial access can unlock new MSME opportunities while promoting more inclusive economic growth.
Tech and Data Systems Could Unlock Agricultural Financing for Nigerian Farmers
Tech and Data Systems Could Unlock Agricultural Financing for Nigerian Farmers

Experts say technology-driven financing systems and reliable farmer databases could significantly improve access to agricultural funding in Nigeria. By using digital platforms, biometric identification, and structured aggregation models, investors and financial institutions can better connect with millions of smallholder farmers and support a more resilient agricultural value chain.

Agriculture experts and financial leaders say adopting technology-driven financing models and reliable farmer databases could significantly improve access to funding for Nigeria’s agricultural sector.

Speaking at BusinessDay Foundation Trade and Investment Series, industry stakeholders highlighted the need for digital systems that can help financial institutions identify farmers, assess risks, and structure investments more effectively.

Participants noted that without reliable data and technology-driven financing platforms, millions of farmers will continue to face challenges accessing funding from development finance institutions (DFIs), venture capital investors, and agricultural banks.

 

Technology as a Tool for De-Risking Agricultural Finance

According to Sean De Cleene, technology can help reduce risks across agricultural value chains by improving transparency and traceability.

He referenced an agricultural financing model implemented in Malawi, where digital technologies helped transform the country’s agricultural financing system.

Key innovations included:

  • Electronic financing systems for farmers

  • Biometric identification using fingerprint technology

  • Direct electronic bank transfers for agricultural subsidies

These tools allowed governments and financial institutions to reach large numbers of farmers efficiently while improving accountability within the value chain.

 

Reliable Farmer Data Key to Unlocking Investment

Industry experts stressed that accurate data is essential for connecting farmers with investors and financing opportunities.

Nigeria’s agricultural sector includes an estimated 38 million smallholder farmers, responsible for producing roughly 80 percent of the country’s food supply.

However, these farmers often operate on small, fragmented plots, creating challenges such as:

  • Limited access to formal financing and credit facilities

  • Difficulty achieving economies of scale

  • High post-harvest losses of 30–50 percent

  • Limited access to modern technology and markets

According to Adeyemi Aduwo, well-structured farmer databases would allow investors to locate producers and design financing models tailored to their needs.

He added that aggregators play a crucial role in linking smallholder farmers to financial institutions.

 

International Partnerships Supporting Agricultural Development

Nigeria’s agricultural sector is also attracting international interest.

According to Jette Bjerrum, the country plays a strategic role in **Denmark’s Africa-focused economic strategy launched in 2024.

Under this framework, Nigeria has been identified as one of four regional hubs for economic cooperation in Africa, covering areas such as trade, development finance, and agricultural collaboration.

Experts at the forum emphasized that building resilient agricultural value chains will require coordinated financing models that combine public institutions, private investors, and digital infrastructure.

Smallholder farmers and agribusiness SMEs form the backbone of Africa’s food systems but often lack access to financing. Digital platforms, farmer databases, and aggregation models can help unlock investment, improve productivity, and create stronger agricultural value chains across the continent.
Oil Prices Surge as Middle East Conflict Threatens Global Supply
Escalating attacks near the Strait of Hormuz push crude prices higher, raising concerns over global energy supply disruptions.

Oil prices climbed sharply as tensions in the Middle East intensified, threatening major shipping routes and global energy supplies. Analysts warn that prolonged disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz could drive crude prices even higher, with potential ripple effects on fuel costs and business operations worldwide.

Global oil prices rose sharply on Thursday after escalating attacks on oil and transport infrastructure across the Middle East heightened fears of supply disruptions through the critical Strait of Hormuz.

Brent crude futures rose $4.47 (4.86%) to $96.45 per barrel, after briefly hitting $100 per barrel earlier in the day. Meanwhile, U.S. West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude climbed $4.05 (4.64%) to $91.30 per barrel.

The surge follows rising tensions involving Iran, the United States, and Israel, with markets increasingly worried that the conflict could disrupt one of the world’s most important oil transit routes.

 

Geopolitical Tensions Drive Oil Market Volatility

Oil markets have become highly sensitive to developments in the Middle East, where shipping disruptions and attacks on energy infrastructure threaten global supply chains.

Key developments influencing the market include:

  • Brent crude previously surged to $119.50 per barrel, its highest level since mid-2022.

  • Iranian officials warned that prolonged instability could push oil prices toward $200 per barrel.

  • Analysts say the ongoing conflict continues to threaten oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz, which handles a significant portion of global oil shipments.

According to analysts at ING Group, sustained price declines are unlikely unless oil flows through the strategic waterway resume normally.

 

Attacks on Tankers Raise Supply Concerns

Security incidents have further intensified fears about global oil supply stability.

According to Iraqi authorities:

  • Two foreign tankers carrying Iraqi fuel oil were attacked in Iraqi territorial waters.

  • The vessels caught fire after being struck by explosive-laden boats, according to initial investigations.

  • The incident has raised concerns about the safety of energy transport across the Gulf region.

Such attacks highlight the vulnerability of global energy supply routes during geopolitical conflicts.

 

Global Measures to Stabilize Oil Markets

In response to rising prices, governments and international energy organizations are taking emergency measures to stabilize the market.

Key actions include:

  • International Energy Agency agreed to release 400 million barrels of oil from emergency reserves.

  • The United States will contribute 172 million barrels from its Strategic Petroleum Reserve.

  • China has imposed a temporary ban on refined fuel exports to safeguard domestic supply.

However, analysts warn that these interventions may only provide short-term relief if disruptions to Middle East oil flows continue.

Rising global oil prices directly increase fuel, transport, and production costs across Africa. For MSMEs, especially in logistics, manufacturing, and agriculture, sustained oil price spikes could intensify operating costs and inflation pressures, making energy efficiency and alternative energy solutions increasingly important.
Oil Prices Surge as Middle East Conflict Threatens Global Supply
Escalating attacks near the Strait of Hormuz push crude prices higher, raising concerns over global energy supply disruptions.

Oil prices climbed sharply as tensions in the Middle East intensified, threatening major shipping routes and global energy supplies. Analysts warn that prolonged disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz could drive crude prices even higher, with potential ripple effects on fuel costs and business operations worldwide.

Global oil prices climbed sharply on Thursday as escalating tensions in the Middle East raised concerns about potential disruptions to global energy supplies, particularly through the strategically important Strait of Hormuz.

Brent crude futures rose by $4.47, or 4.86 per cent, to $96.45 per barrel after briefly touching the $100 mark earlier in the day. U.S. West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude also gained $4.05, or 4.64 per cent, to trade at $91.30 per barrel.

The surge comes amid growing geopolitical tensions involving Iran, the United States, and Israel, with markets increasingly worried that the conflict could interfere with oil shipments through one of the world’s most critical energy transit routes.

Oil markets have remained highly sensitive to developments in the region, where attacks on energy infrastructure and shipping vessels are raising alarm over the stability of global supply chains. Analysts note that Brent crude had earlier surged to $119.50 per barrel, marking its highest level since mid-2022, while Iranian officials have warned that prolonged instability could push prices as high as $200 per barrel.

Industry experts say the continued threat to oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz—a key maritime corridor for global oil shipments, remains a major source of volatility in the market. Analysts at ING Group noted that sustained price declines are unlikely unless oil transport through the waterway resumes without disruption.

Concerns intensified following reports from Iraqi authorities that two foreign tankers carrying Iraqi fuel oil were attacked in Iraqi territorial waters. Initial investigations suggest the vessels were struck by explosive-laden boats, causing fires onboard. The incident has further highlighted the vulnerability of energy transport routes in the Gulf region during periods of geopolitical conflict.

In response to rising oil prices, governments and international energy institutions have begun implementing emergency measures aimed at stabilising the market. The International Energy Agency has agreed to release 400 million barrels of oil from global emergency reserves, while the United States is expected to contribute 172 million barrels from its Strategic Petroleum Reserve.

China has also introduced a temporary ban on refined fuel exports in an effort to safeguard domestic supply amid the ongoing uncertainty.

Despite these measures, analysts caution that such interventions may only offer short-term relief if disruptions in Middle Eastern oil flows persist. With the region accounting for a significant share of global oil production and exports, any prolonged conflict could have far-reaching implications for energy markets and global economic stability.

Rising global oil prices directly increase fuel, transport, and production costs across Africa. For MSMEs, especially in logistics, manufacturing, and agriculture, sustained oil price spikes could intensify operating costs and inflation pressures, making energy efficiency and alternative energy solutions increasingly important.

With an estimated 38 million smallholder farmers producing about 80 percent of the country’s food supply, the country’s agricultural workforce remains a key pillar of food security.

However, many of these farmers operate on small and fragmented plots of land, which creates challenges such as limited access to credit facilities, difficulties achieving economies of scale, and high post-harvest losses estimated at between 30 and 50 percent. Limited access to modern farming technologies and structured markets further compounds these challenges.

Adeyemi Aduwo noted that well-structured farmer databases would enable investors and financial institutions to locate producers more easily and design financing models suited to their needs. He added that aggregators play a critical role in linking smallholder farmers with financial institutions and investment opportunities.

Nigeria’s agricultural sector is also attracting growing international interest. According to Jette Bjerrum, the country plays a strategic role in Denmark’s Africa-focused economic strategy launched in 2024.

Under the framework, Nigeria has been identified as one of four regional hubs for economic cooperation in Africa, with focus areas including trade, development finance, and agricultural collaboration.

Experts at the forum concluded that unlocking large-scale agricultural financing in Nigeria will require coordinated efforts that combine public institutions, private sector investors, and digital infrastructure to build stronger and more resilient agricultural value chains.

Smallholder farmers and agribusiness SMEs form the backbone of Africa’s food systems but often lack access to financing. Digital platforms, farmer databases, and aggregation models can help unlock investment, improve productivity, and create stronger agricultural value chains across the continent.
Nigeria Targets 6 Million Youth Agripreneurs Through New Government–IITA Partnership
A new national initiative combines agribusiness training with land access to help young Nigerians launch scalable agricultural ventures.

The Nigerian government has partnered with the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture to train over six million youths in agribusiness while launching a Youth in Agribusiness Land Trust Fund to improve land access. The initiative aims to transform agriculture into a modern, youth-driven sector capable of creating jobs and boosting food production.

The Federal Government of Nigeria has signed a partnership with the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) to train more than six million Nigerian youths in modern agriculture and agribusiness.

The agreement, signed in Abuja, forms part of a broader strategy to expand youth participation in Nigeria’s agricultural value chain and strengthen the country’s food production systems.

The programme will equip young participants with technical farming skills, agribusiness training, mentorship, and access to resources required to build sustainable agricultural enterprises.

 

Youth in Agribusiness Land Trust Fund to Improve Land Access

A major component of the initiative is the launch of the Youth in Agribusiness Land Trust Fund, designed to address one of the biggest barriers facing young farmers—access to land.

The fund will leverage 42 National Youth Development Centres across Nigeria, providing land and supporting infrastructure for youth-led agricultural ventures.

Key objectives include:

  • Providing land access for young agripreneurs

  • Developing agriculture innovation hubs at youth centres

  • Supporting climate-smart farming practices

  • Connecting youth entrepreneurs with investors and technology

This approach aims to make it easier for young Nigerians to transition from training into real agribusiness operations.

 

Building a New Generation of Agribusiness Entrepreneurs

Under the three-year programme, IITA will help transform youth development centres into agriculture innovation hubs that link young farmers to modern tools, markets, and digital farming technologies.

According to the Federal Ministry of Youth Development, data from the national youth database shows that more than six million young Nigerians have already expressed interest in participating in agricultural value chains.

The initiative will provide participants with:

  • Climate-smart agriculture training

  • Business and enterprise development skills

  • Mentorship and incubation support

  • Access to digital agriculture tools

The programme also seeks to reposition agriculture as a profitable and innovation-driven sector, capable of attracting young entrepreneurs.

 

Agriculture as a Strategic Economic Opportunity

With nearly 70 percent of Nigeria’s population under the age of 30, policymakers believe youth-driven agribusiness could play a critical role in addressing unemployment while strengthening national food systems.

The government and its partners say empowering young agripreneurs will help:

  • Increase agricultural productivity

  • Stimulate rural economic growth

  • Create sustainable jobs

  • Strengthen Nigeria’s food security

Agriculture remains one of Africa’s largest opportunities for entrepreneurship and job creation. By improving land access, skills development, and agribusiness financing pathways, initiatives like this can help thousands of youth-led MSMEs emerge across the agricultural value chain, from farming and processing to logistics and export.
Nigeria Encourages Women to Expand Role in Trade and Entrepreneurship
Government calls on women to leverage business opportunities, digital innovation, and funding programmes to drive economic growth.

Nigeria’s Federal Government is urging women to actively participate in trade, entrepreneurship, and investment as part of efforts to strengthen economic growth. Officials say empowering women-led enterprises across sectors such as agribusiness, manufacturing, and export trade will play a key role in expanding the country’s productive capacity.

The Federal Government of Nigeria has encouraged women across the country to take greater advantage of opportunities in trade, entrepreneurship, and enterprise development to strengthen Nigeria’s economic growth.

The call was made by Jumoke Oduwole, Nigeria’s Minister of Industry, Trade and Investment, during activities marking International Women’s Day in Abuja.

Observed globally every March 8, International Women’s Day celebrates the achievements of women while promoting greater economic, social, and political inclusion. The 2026 theme, “Give to Gain,” highlights the importance of investing in women to unlock broader economic and social benefits.

 

Women Encouraged to Explore Business and Trade Opportunities

Speaking at the event, Oduwole emphasised that expanding women’s participation in business and trade is essential for Nigeria’s long-term economic development.

She urged women to position themselves to benefit from emerging opportunities by:

  • Building entrepreneurial and business management skills

  • Leveraging government grants and funding programmes

  • Exploring cooperative and enterprise development initiatives

  • Expanding into sectors such as agribusiness, manufacturing, and export trade

According to the minister, Nigeria’s economic future will depend significantly on how effectively women participate in key productive sectors.

 

Digital Skills and Innovation Key to Competitiveness

Oduwole also encouraged women entrepreneurs to adopt digital innovation and continuous learning to remain competitive in a rapidly evolving global economy.

Key strategies highlighted include:

  • Developing multiple income streams

  • Leveraging digital platforms for business growth

  • Building technology-driven enterprises

  • Strengthening financial literacy and investment knowledge

The Federal Ministry of Industry, Trade and Investment reaffirmed its commitment to supporting women through policies, capacity-building programmes, and entrepreneurship initiatives.

 

Government Expands Gender Inclusion Initiatives

According to Nura Rimi, Permanent Secretary of the ministry, government programmes have continued to prioritise gender inclusion despite funding constraints.

He noted that ongoing initiatives are designed to expand opportunities for women through:

  • Entrepreneurship training programmes

  • Policy awareness and mentorship initiatives

  • Enterprise development support

Meanwhile, Hauwa Sarki, Head of the Gender Unit in the ministry, said the programme aims to expose women to policy opportunities and mentorship platforms that can translate into meaningful economic empowerment.

Women entrepreneurs play a vital role in Africa’s MSME ecosystem, particularly in sectors such as agriculture, retail, and manufacturing. Expanding access to funding, training, and policy support for women-led enterprises can unlock new business growth, create jobs, and strengthen inclusive economic development across the continent.
LSETF Launches ‘Fund Her Future’ Programme to Support Female-Led Startups in Lagos
The Female Founders & Funders initiative expands funding access, mentorship, and investor networks for women building tech startups.

The Lagos State Employment Trust Fund and AfriGloCal VC have launched the third edition of the Female Founders & Funders programme to support women entrepreneurs in Nigeria’s technology ecosystem. The initiative focuses on improving access to capital, mentorship, and investment readiness for female-led startups.

The Lagos State Employment Trust Fund (LSETF), in partnership with AfriGloCal VC, has launched the third edition of the Female Founders & Funders (FFF) programme, an initiative designed to strengthen support systems for women building startups in Nigeria’s technology ecosystem.

Delivered under Lagos Innovates, LSETF’s technology ecosystem development platform, the programme focuses on expanding access to funding, mentorship, and startup ecosystem resources for early-stage ventures with at least one female founder.

Now in its third year, the programme continues to promote female entrepreneurship and innovation within Lagos’ rapidly growing tech sector.

 

Strengthening Funding Pathways for Female Entrepreneurs

Since its launch, the Female Founders & Funders programme has made measurable progress in supporting women in the startup ecosystem.

Key achievements include:

  • Supporting 33+ female founders building early-stage startups

  • Training over 24 aspiring female investors

  • Awarding more than ₦9 million in startup prizes

  • Connecting founders with 30+ industry mentors

The initiative aims to address the structural barriers that often limit women’s access to capital, mentorship, and investment networks in the startup ecosystem.

 

Dual-Track Model for Founders and Investors

A unique feature of the programme is its dual-track approach, designed to strengthen both sides of the startup funding pipeline.

The programme includes two key pathways:

  • Founder Track: Incubation and business development support for female entrepreneurs building technology-enabled startups

  • Investor Track: Training and exposure for women interested in participating in early-stage venture investment

This model helps increase both the number of women-led startups and the number of women participating in venture capital funding.

 

Six-Week Training and Investor Demo Day

Under the 2026 theme “Fund Her Future,” the programme will focus on improving investment readiness and strengthening connections between founders and investors.

Participants in the six-week cohort will receive training through masterclasses covering:

  • Business modelling and startup strategy

  • Product-led growth

  • Startup finance and legal frameworks

  • Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) strategy

  • Artificial intelligence integration

  • Investment readiness and fundraising

The programme will conclude with a Demo Day, where selected founders will pitch their startups to a curated audience of investors and ecosystem stakeholders.

Applications are open to women building or investing in tech-enabled and innovation-driven ventures within Lagos State, with submissions closing on April 4, 2026 via the Lagos Innovates platform.

Female founders remain underrepresented in venture capital funding across Africa. Programmes like Female Founders & Funders help close this gap by improving access to capital, mentorship, and investor networks, unlocking new growth opportunities for women-led startups and strengthening the continent’s innovation ecosystem.
Libya Restarts Mabruk Oilfield Amid Rising Global Prices
TotalEnergies-led operations resume at dormant site, boosting national output and regional energy prospects.

Libya has restarted production at the Mabruk oilfield, dormant since 2015 due to political instability. The move, led by TotalEnergies in partnership with the National Oil Corporation, adds 25,000 barrels per day and signals Libya’s oil sector recovery as global prices rise following the US-Israeli attack on Iran.

Libya has resumed operations at its long-dormant Mabruk oilfield, marking a critical step in revitalizing the country’s oil sector, according to the Libya Review on March 12. The field, inactive since 2015 due to security challenges, is now operational under the management of Mabruk Oil Operations—a joint venture including TotalEnergies, which holds a 37.5 per cent stake.

The restart coincides with rising global energy prices triggered by geopolitical tensions in the Gulf, including the US-Israeli attack on Iran. A newly constructed production unit at Mabruk can process 25,000 barrels of oil daily, with work on the facility beginning in May 2024 and official commissioning on 28 February 2026.

Libya, home to Africa’s largest proven crude reserves, has seen its oil revenues reach their highest level in over a decade in 2025.

National output currently stands at 1.4 million barrels per day, the highest in over ten years, with authorities aiming for 1.6 million barrels per day by the end of 2026.

The country has also attracted international interest, signing a $20 billion, 25-year agreement with TotalEnergies and ConocoPhillips earlier this year to expand production and secure long-term supply. Libyan crude, relatively cheap to extract, is strategically positioned to meet European demand as the continent seeks alternatives to Russian oil amid geopolitical tensions.

Despite the resurgence, Libya’s oil sector continues to face political and administrative challenges. Revenues remain a contentious issue between rival administrations in Tripoli and Benghazi, and security disruptions have historically hampered production.

The revival of Mabruk is part of a broader national effort to restore production capacity and attract foreign investment, signaling stability and growth potential in Libya’s energy industry.

The restart of Libya’s Mabruk oilfield has implications beyond the energy sector. Higher oil revenues can strengthen government finances, enabling investment in infrastructure, business incentives, and public services, all of which support MSMEs.
For African MSMEs, stable and increased oil production in Libya can help moderate regional fuel prices, reduce transportation and logistics costs, and improve the availability of energy-dependent inputs. Additionally, foreign investment in energy projects may open opportunities for local suppliers, contractors, and service providers, stimulating entrepreneurship and job creation across adjacent sectors.
Togo Seeks Increased Electricity Imports from Nigeria
Rising power demand drives push for expanded cross-border energy cooperation

The Republic of Togo has expressed interest in increasing electricity imports from the Niger Delta Power Holding Company to meet rising energy demand and support newly connected customers. The request was made during a meeting with Compagnie Energie Electrique du Togo, which currently imports about 75 megawatt-hours of electricity from Nigeria. Both parties reaffirmed their commitment to expanding cross-border power trade while establishing sustainable commercial arrangements to support long-term energy cooperation in West Africa.

The Republic of Togo has expressed interest in increasing its electricity imports from Niger Delta Power Holding Company to meet rising domestic demand and support the expansion of reliable electricity supply across the country.

The request was made during a strategic meeting between the management of NDPHC and a delegation from Compagnie Energie Electrique du Togo, the national electricity utility headquartered in Lomé.

According to NDPHC, the delegation was led by Débo-K’mba Barandao, who visited the company to strengthen existing cooperation and explore opportunities for expanding cross-border electricity trade.

Officials said CEET currently purchases about 75 megawatt-hours of electricity from NDPHC under a bilateral arrangement, which has helped maintain stable electricity delivery and support economic activities in Togo.

The imported electricity contributes to sustaining a reliable and affordable power supply for households, businesses, and public institutions across the country.

During the meeting, Barandao commended NDPHC for the consistency of its electricity supply, noting that the partnership has improved power reliability within Togo’s electricity network and strengthened regional energy cooperation.

He explained that CEET is experiencing a surge in electricity demand following the addition of new industrial and commercial customers, as well as ongoing efforts by the Togolese government to expand electricity access nationwide.

As a result, the utility company expressed strong interest in increasing the volume of electricity it purchases from Nigeria to support the country’s power expansion strategy and ensure stable electricity for newly connected consumers.

Responding, Jennifer Adighije reaffirmed the company’s readiness to deepen collaboration with CEET and continue supporting electricity exports to neighbouring countries in the West African sub-region.

She noted that NDPHC, which operates several power plants across Nigeria under the National Integrated Power Project, has the capacity to support regional electricity supply and promote energy integration across West Africa.

Adighije added that the partnership aligns with broader regional initiatives under the Economic Community of West African States aimed at strengthening electricity trade and improving power availability across member states.

However, she emphasised the need for bankable and sustainable commercial arrangements to guide future transactions, noting that credible financial guarantees and structured payment mechanisms would help mitigate payment risks often associated with cross-border electricity supply.

Both parties described the meeting as productive and reaffirmed their commitment to strengthening cooperation within the electricity sector. They also agreed to continue engagements aimed at developing workable frameworks that would support increased power supply from Nigeria to Togo.

If implemented, the proposed increase in electricity imports is expected to strengthen energy cooperation between Nigeria and Togo while supporting improved electricity access and economic development across the West African region.

Reliable electricity is essential for the growth and productivity of African MSMEs. Increased cross-border power trade between countries like Nigeria and Togo can help stabilise electricity supply, reduce outages, and lower the cost of doing business. For small businesses that depend on consistent power such as manufacturers, agro-processors, and digital enterprises improved energy access supports higher productivity, business expansion, and job creation. Stronger regional energy cooperation also strengthens economic integration across West Africa, creating a more enabling environment for MSMEs to grow and compete.
Government, Private Sector Intensify Collaboration Ahead of Nigeria’s Trade Digitalisation Launch
Stakeholder engagement across key cities prepares traders, customs officers, and industry groups for Nigeria’s upcoming National Single Window digital trade platform launch.

The National Single Window Secretariat, in collaboration with the Nigeria Customs Service, has intensified stakeholder engagement across Lagos, Port Harcourt, and Kano ahead of the official launch of the National Single Window platform. The initiative aims to digitise Nigeria’s trade processes by creating a single online entry point where traders can interact with multiple government agencies. The platform is expected to reduce delays, eliminate duplicate documentation, and improve transparency in import and export transactions while strengthening collaboration between government institutions and the private sector.

The National Single Window Secretariat, in collaboration with the Nigeria Customs Service, has intensified stakeholder engagement across key commercial hubs in Nigeria as preparations advance for the official launch of the National Single Window platform later this month.

According to a statement from the secretariat, sensitisation sessions held in Lagos, Port Harcourt, and Kano brought together Customs officers and major stakeholders within Nigeria’s trade ecosystem to strengthen awareness and readiness for the new digital trade system.

The engagements form part of the government’s efforts to build confidence in the platform and ensure a smooth transition to a technology-driven trade environment designed to reduce delays, improve transparency, and enhance Nigeria’s competitiveness in global commerce.

Speaking during the Lagos session, Director of the National Single Window Secretariat, Tola Fakolade, said the nationwide engagement tour was aimed at preparing both government institutions and private sector participants for the adoption of the integrated digital platform.

He described the National Single Window as a significant reform that will streamline trade procedures and simplify interactions between traders and regulatory agencies.

According to Fakolade, the platform will enable importers, exporters, and logistics operators to access multiple government services through a single digital entry point, eliminating repetitive documentation and administrative bottlenecks that often delay trade transactions.

He noted that the system has undergone extensive testing and consultations with stakeholders ahead of its official rollout scheduled for March 27.

Fakolade also addressed concerns about compatibility with existing systems, explaining that the National Single Window will work alongside the B’Odogwu platform rather than replace it.

Instead, he said the platform will serve as a central coordinating hub that integrates various trade-related systems and government agencies into one unified digital framework.

The engagement sessions were structured to support both operational and industry readiness. The first day focused on officers of the Nigeria Customs Service, with discussions centred on system integration, operational preparedness, and inter-agency coordination.

The second day brought together key players in the trade value chain, including licensed customs agents, freight forwarders, port operators, exporters, importers, and representatives of the organised private sector.

Industry associations such as the National Association of Government Approved Freight Forwarders, the Association of Nigerian

Licensed Customs Agents, and the Importers Association of Nigeria participated in the sessions and expressed support for the initiative.

Leaders of the associations commended the government for engaging stakeholders ahead of implementation and emphasised that sustained collaboration between public institutions and the private sector will be critical to the success of the digital trade platform.

The National Single Window initiative is expected to improve trade facilitation in Nigeria by reducing processing times, minimising paperwork, and strengthening coordination among regulatory agencies involved in import and export operations.

Officials say the reform represents an important step toward modernising Nigeria’s trade infrastructure and aligning the country’s systems with global best practices in digital trade management.

Trade digitalisation can significantly lower the barriers that MSMEs face in cross-border trade. A unified platform like the National Single Window simplifies documentation, shortens clearance times, and reduces administrative costs for exporters and importers. For African MSMEs especially those engaged in manufacturing, agriculture, and e-commerce this means faster market access, improved efficiency, and better opportunities to compete in regional and global trade.
Nigeria Urges Global Investment in Women at UN Gender Summit
Government officials and global leaders highlight women’s empowerment as a key driver of economic growth, inclusion, and sustainable development.

Federal Ministry of Women Affairs and Social Development hosted the second edition of Nigerian Women’s Day during the United Nations Commission on the Status of Women in New York City, bringing together global leaders, policymakers, and advocates to discuss gender equality and women’s empowerment. The event called for increased global investment in women and girls as a strategy for economic development and social progress.

The Federal Ministry of Women Affairs and Social Development convened the second edition of Nigerian Women’s Day during the 70th session of the United Nations Commission on the Status of Women in New York City, calling for increased global investment in women and girls.

The event, organised under the leadership of the Minister of Women Affairs and Social Development, Imaan Sulaiman-Ibrahim, brought together policymakers, diplomats, gender advocates, and entrepreneurs to advance discussions on gender equality and inclusive development.

Among the notable participants was Amina J. Mohammed, alongside ministers, civil society leaders, members of the Nigerian diaspora, and development partners. The full-day programme featured policy dialogue, cultural displays, and high-level engagements focused on strengthening women’s participation in economic and social development.

The event was themed “31 Years of Progress, Resilience, Impact and Renewed Hope,” highlighting Nigeria’s progress in advancing gender equality while reinforcing the administration of Bola Ahmed Tinubu on women’s rights, family resilience, and inclusive development.

Chair of the Board of Directors of Women Radio WFM 91.7, Toun Okewale Sonaiya, delivered the welcome address, outlining the day’s focus on women’s leadership, innovation, and global collaboration. The session also featured a specially commissioned film highlighting the role of women in leadership across institutions and international platforms.

In her keynote remarks, Sulaiman-Ibrahim called for stronger global collaboration to support women’s empowerment. She referenced

Nigeria’s Renewed Hope Social Impact Interventions 774 framework, which aims to reach women across all 774 local government areas through initiatives in energy access, agriculture, digital inclusion, health and protection, innovation, education, and leadership development.

She also presented a six-point call to action urging governments, development partners, and civil society organisations to increase funding and accelerate the implementation of gender equality commitments.

“Transforming women’s lives at scale requires sustained investment, strong partnerships, and coordinated action,” she said, stressing that empowering women is both an economic and development strategy.

A major highlight of the programme was a fireside conversation between Sulaiman-Ibrahim and the UN Deputy Secretary-General, which explored Nigeria’s national gender equality agenda within the broader global framework.

The event also featured panel discussions on global leadership, women’s economic participation, and the role of institutions in advancing gender equality. Cultural performances and artistic presentations, including a live demonstration by internationally recognised Nigerian artist Laolu Senbanjo, formed part of the programme.

Organisers said the event reaffirmed the importance of placing women, families, and children at the centre of economic policy and national development strategies.

Greater investment in women’s empowerment can significantly expand Africa’s MSME ecosystem. Women entrepreneurs operate a large share of small businesses across the continent, particularly in sectors such as retail, agriculture, and services. Increased funding, training, and policy support for women-led enterprises can improve business growth, job creation, and economic inclusion, helping MSMEs scale and contribute more effectively to national and regional development.
Strategic Policy Review Charts Path for Nigeria’s MSMEs 2026–2030
New report identifies systemic barriers and recommends reforms to strengthen enterprise growth, digital adoption, and industrial clusters.

A recent policy review titled “Reassessment of Nigeria’s National Policy on Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs), 2021–2025, and Strategic Directions for 2026–2030” highlights the structural challenges limiting the impact of MSME support programmes. Conducted by Onyinye Udoka Ozurumba of Africari Limited, the study recommends stronger policy coordination, cluster-based industrial development, digital sector prioritisation, and capacity-building initiatives to enable Nigerian MSMEs to scale sustainably.

A new policy review has outlined strategic priorities for Nigeria’s MSME framework from 2026 to 2030, noting that structural weaknesses have constrained the long-term effectiveness of government support programmes.

The study, conducted by Onyinye Udoka Ozurumba of Africari Limited, Scotland, evaluates the 2021–2025 MSME policy cycle and proposes reforms to strengthen Nigeria’s enterprise ecosystem. Data cited in the report show that MSMEs account for approximately 96.9% of registered businesses, contribute nearly 48% of GDP, and employ about 87.9% of the national workforce.

Despite these contributions, many small businesses continue to face low productivity, high mortality rates, and limited capacity to scale operations. The report argues that the central challenge is not the lack of government support, but the limited effectiveness of existing policy interventions in addressing structural constraints.

The review highlights a disconnect between increased access to credit and enterprise growth during the 2021–2025 cycle. While intervention funds and development finance schemes improved liquidity, most loans were used for short-term operational needs rather than long-term capital investment, machinery acquisition, or technology upgrades. Macroeconomic instability, including inflation, exchange-rate volatility, and rising energy costs, further discouraged investment.

Other systemic barriers identified include weak infrastructure, limited digital adoption, a large informal business sector, and a complex regulatory environment with multiple tax obligations. Informality limits access to financing, government support, and formal markets.

Despite challenges, MSMEs operating in industrial clusters and structured value chains demonstrated higher productivity and survival rates, benefiting from shared infrastructure, supplier networks, and knowledge exchange. The report underscores the importance of ecosystem-based approaches rather than isolated interventions.

For the 2026–2030 policy cycle, the study recommends establishing a unified national MSME delivery architecture with a central enterprise registry and interoperable data systems to improve programme coordination, targeting, and accountability. It also advocates for cluster-based industrial development, digital and creative sector prioritisation, and initiatives to improve MSMEs’ absorptive capacity, including diagnostics, capacity-building, mentorship, and market linkages.

The report concludes that strengthening policy coordination, reducing transaction costs, and enhancing business support systems will be critical to transforming Nigerian MSMEs from survival-focused enterprises to scalable, sustainable engines of economic growth.

The proposed reforms provide a roadmap for African MSMEs to thrive in competitive and resource-constrained environments. By improving policy coordination, access to long-term financing, digital adoption, and cluster-based development, MSMEs across Africa can achieve higher productivity, sustainability, and global competitiveness. A stronger enterprise ecosystem also supports job creation, innovation, and inclusive economic growth across the continent.
ECA Urges West Africa to Harness Innovation for Economic Growth
UN Economic Commission for Africa highlights digital transformation and frontier technologies as key drivers of structural transformation in the region.

The United Nations Economic Commission for Africa (ECA) has urged West African countries to leverage innovation, data systems, and emerging technologies to accelerate economic transformation and sustainable development. Speaking ahead of the Conference of African Ministers of Finance, Planning, and Economic Development, ECA’s Ngone Diop emphasized digital connectivity gains and projected growth in Africa’s internet economy, while highlighting the transformative potential of frontier technologies like Artificial Intelligence.

The United Nations Economic Commission for Africa (ECA) has called on West African nations to harness innovation, data, and frontier technologies to drive economic transformation and sustainable development.

Ngone Diop, Director of the ECA Sub-Regional Office for West Africa, made the remarks during a virtual pre-conference dialogue ahead of the Conference of African Ministers of Finance, Planning, and Economic Development scheduled in Tangier, Morocco, from March 28 to April 3.

Diop emphasized that innovation and digital transformation have become critical global drivers of economic growth. She stressed that Africa and West Africa must leverage these tools to accelerate structural transformation and achieve sustainable development goals.

According to the ECA director, the region has made notable progress in digital connectivity. Access to third-generation mobile networks in Africa increased from 54% in 2015 to 86% in 2024, with West Africa reaching 85.9% connectivity within the same period.

She highlighted the growing significance of Africa’s internet economy, projected to reach $180 billion by 2025, equivalent to 5.2% of the continent’s GDP. By 2050, the internet economy could expand to $712 billion, representing 8.5% of GDP.

Diop also noted that frontier technologies, including Artificial Intelligence, are already reshaping production systems, trade logistics, governance, and service delivery across Africa. She underscored the importance of integrating digital strategies into national economic plans to sustain competitiveness and growth.

The ECA reaffirmed its commitment to supporting member states in leveraging digital transformation to promote economic growth, innovation, and regional integration. Diop stressed that continued investment in technology, data systems, and innovation hubs is essential for West Africa to fully realize its economic potential.

For African MSMEs, the ECA’s call underscores the importance of adopting digital tools, data analytics, and emerging technologies to enhance productivity, market access, and competitiveness. Leveraging digital infrastructure and innovation ecosystems can enable small businesses to scale efficiently, access regional and global markets, and contribute to inclusive economic growth across the continent.
World Bank Approves $250 Million to Boost MSME Finance and Jobs in Niger
New IDA-supported project aims to expand access to financing for small businesses and stimulate job creation across Niger.

The World Bank has approved a $250 million grant through the International Development Association (IDA) to help Niger strengthen its financial sector and improve access to finance for micro, small, and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs). The initiative targets liquidity restoration, increased lending to viable businesses, and job creation, with special attention to women-led and climate-resilient enterprises.

The World Bank has approved a $250 million grant to support Niger in expanding access to finance for micro, small, and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs) and to strengthen the country’s financial sector.

The project, called the Support to the Financial Sector and Access to MSME Finance in Niger Project, is funded through the International Development Association (IDA). It is designed to address liquidity constraints affecting financial institutions and MSMEs, while promoting sustainable lending, job creation, and income generation.

The initiative is expected to benefit approximately 7,500 MSMEs, including women-led businesses and climate-resilient investments, and to create or sustain an estimated 58,000 jobs across Niger.

According to the World Bank, Niger’s private sector has been under pressure due to macroeconomic challenges and financial disruptions. Several banks and microfinance institutions are experiencing liquidity shortages, while structural weaknesses in public banks have restricted credit access for MSMEs, limiting their capacity to invest, maintain employment, and expand operations.

The project combines a range of financial tools, including liquidity support and risk-sharing instruments, to help viable MSMEs secure the financing needed to sustain and grow their businesses. Additionally, technical assistance and capacity-building measures will strengthen financial institutions’ ability to lend more effectively and sustainably, including to women-led enterprises.

A Contingent Emergency Response Component is included in the project to ensure a rapid response if future crises occur, while implementation support, monitoring, and evaluation measures aim to ensure the programme is executed efficiently.

By addressing liquidity challenges, improving financial intermediation, and broadening access to finance, the project seeks to restore confidence in Niger’s financial system and provide a platform for private sector growth. The initiative also aligns with broader national efforts to modernize the financial sector, stimulate entrepreneurship, and enhance job creation in the country.

The project underscores the importance of accessible financing and strong financial institutions for small businesses in Africa. For MSMEs in Niger and the wider region, improved access to credit, technical assistance, and risk-sharing mechanisms enables growth, resilience, and competitiveness. Supporting women-led enterprises and climate-resilient ventures also promotes inclusive economic development and job creation across local communities.
Beverage Giants Increase Prices Amid Rising Costs, Impacting MSMEs
Nigerian Breweries and Guinness announce price hikes on select products, reflecting broader economic pressures affecting small and medium-sized businesses.

Nigerian Breweries Plc and Guinness Nigeria are set to raise prices on certain products due to rising operational and input costs. The adjustments, effective March 20 for Nigerian Breweries and March 27 for Guinness, highlight the challenges that inflation and economic pressures pose for MSMEs involved in retail, distribution, and hospitality sectors.

Nigerian Breweries Plc has notified distributors of an upward adjustment in the prices of selected stock-keeping units (SKUs) to mitigate the impact of rising operational and input costs. The brewer, known for alcoholic products like Star Lager, Gulder, Legend Extra Stout, Heineken, Goldberg, Life, and Star Radler, as well as non-alcoholic beverages including Maltina, Amstel Malta, Fayrouz, Climax Energy Drink, and Malta Gold, said prevailing economic conditions necessitated the revision.

In a notice dated March 13 and signed by John Oloche Ademu, Zonal Business Manager (West), Nigerian Breweries stated that the price increases would take effect from March 20, 2026. The company assured that fully funded and confirmed orders placed before the effective date would be honored at existing prices.

Similarly, Guinness Nigeria has informed distributors of a planned price increase across select SKUs in its product range. The notice, dated March 14, stated that the revised pricing would take effect from March 27, 2026, with fully funded orders raised before this date honored at current prices.

Both companies attributed the price adjustments to the evolving economic environment, which has significantly increased the cost of doing business. They emphasized that these measures are necessary to maintain sustainable operations and ensure continued product availability for consumers.

The price increases by Nigerian Breweries and Guinness have implications for MSMEs across retail, hospitality, and distribution networks. Higher input costs can reduce profit margins for small businesses that rely on beverage sales, forcing them to adjust their pricing, manage inventory more carefully, and reassess cash flow strate
CBN Governor Calms Naira Devaluation Fears, Highlights FX Reforms
Yemi Cardoso assures stability in Nigeria’s currency and improved access to foreign exchange, emphasizing ongoing financial reforms.

The Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) Governor, Yemi Cardoso, has eased concerns over a potential Naira devaluation, citing reforms that have strengthened confidence in the currency and improved access to foreign exchange. He highlighted that inflation reduction and market transparency remain central to the CBN’s monetary strategy.

The Governor of the Central Bank of Nigeria, Yemi Cardoso, has reassured Nigerians that there are no immediate plans to devalue the Naira, attributing improved stability to ongoing monetary and foreign exchange reforms. Cardoso made the remarks during a keynote address at the Annual Distinguished Alumni Lecture for the Founders’ Day of the St. Gregory’s College Old Boys Association in Lagos.

“These reforms have restored pride in our currency and strengthened confidence in our financial system,” Cardoso said, stressing that the CBN remains focused on reducing inflation to single digits. He acknowledged that while achieving this goal will take time, it remains central to the bank’s policy objectives.

Cardoso noted that the apex bank’s transparency initiatives, including the elimination of multiple exchange rates, have broadened access to foreign exchange and enhanced market efficiency. “Some critics argue the exchange rate today appears higher than before the reforms. The key difference is accessibility and transparency. Foreign exchange is now accessible through formal channels,” he said.

The CBN Governor also highlighted improvements in the foreign exchange market, including the narrowed premium between official and parallel market rates, which declined from around 50% in 2022 to less than 2% in 2025. He further pointed out that Nigerians travelling abroad now benefit from direct access to foreign currency via naira-linked cards, reducing reliance on informal channels.

Stability in Nigeria’s currency and improved access to foreign exchange directly affect micro, small, and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs) that rely on imported inputs, raw materials, and international trade. Transparent FX policies reduce uncertainties, lower transaction costs, and make foreign currency more predictable and accessible.
MSMEs that import goods or export products can plan more effectively, secure financing in foreign currencies, and mitigate risks associated with informal FX markets. This strengthens business resilience and enables small enterprises to scale operations, invest in growth, and compete in regional and global markets.
The CBN’s reforms signal a more stable and predictable financial environment, offering MSMEs a foundation to make strategic investments and expand their role in Nigeria’s economy.
Egypt Caps Bread Prices Amid Rising Living Costs
Authorities impose price ceilings on bread to protect consumers as fuel costs surge and inflation remains high.

Egypt’s trade ministry has introduced price caps on bread and mandated bakeries to clearly display prices and volumes, following recent fuel price hikes. The government warned of strict penalties, including trials in military courts, for price manipulation, as Egyptians face rising living costs.

Egyptian authorities have imposed caps on the prices of bread to shield consumers from further increases in the cost of living. The measure was announced by the Ministry of Trade and Industry on Thursday, requiring bakeries to display both the price and volume of bread clearly at their storefronts. Overpricing may result in penalties, with President Abdel Fattah El-Sisi warning last week that offenders could face trials in military courts.

The price controls follow government adjustments to energy costs, with gasoline and cooking gas cylinders increased by up to 30% and 22%, respectively. These hikes come amid rising energy prices linked to geopolitical tensions, including the ongoing US-Israeli conflict involving Iran.

Egypt has seen inflation ease to 13.4% in February 2026, a significant drop from the record 40% in August 2024, which coincided with the peak of the country’s economic crisis. Despite this improvement, many Egyptians continue to cope with limited savings and rising daily expenses.

The government’s intervention aims to prevent further erosion of household purchasing power and ensure essential commodities, like bread, remain affordable.

Price controls on essential goods such as bread directly affect small and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs) in the food sector, including bakeries and distributors. Clear pricing guidelines and caps can stabilize operations, reduce uncertainty, and prevent unfair competition.

However, rising fuel and energy costs increase operational expenses for MSMEs, potentially compressing profit margins. Predictable price policies can help these businesses plan production, manage costs, and maintain supply, while ensuring they continue to serve local communities effectively.

For African MSMEs, this illustrates the broader challenge of operating in volatile markets where energy costs and inflation directly influence pricing, consumer demand, and business sustainability. Policies balancing affordability for consumers and viability for small enterprises are critical for economic resilience.
Three Women CEOs Leading Nigeria’s Trillion-Naira Companies
A new wave of female leadership is shaping the performance of some of Nigeria’s most valuable publicly listed companies.

Three female CEOs: Adaora Umeoji, Nneka Onyeali-Ikpe, and Uzo Oshogwe are leading companies valued at over ₦1 trillion on the Nigerian Exchange. Together, the firms they lead represent roughly ₦6.7 trillion in market value, highlighting a gradual but significant shift in corporate leadership within Nigeria’s top-tier companies.

Three women are currently leading some of Nigeria’s most valuable publicly listed companies, collectively overseeing businesses worth about ₦6.7 trillion in market capitalisation on the Nigerian Exchange Group (NGX).

The executives, Adaora Umeoji, Nneka Onyeali‑Ikpe, and Uzo Oshogwe are part of a growing leadership shift across Nigeria’s corporate sector, particularly among large firms valued above ₦1 trillion.

While the NGX trillion-naira club has expanded to 26 companies, it remains largely dominated by industrial manufacturers, telecom operators, and major banks sectors historically characterised by limited female representation at the top.

 

Adaora Umeoji: Leading Zenith Bank Through Recapitalisation

Adaora Umeoji became the first female Group Managing Director and Chief Executive Officer of Zenith Bank in June 2024.

Her appointment came during a critical period for Nigeria’s banking sector following recapitalisation requirements introduced by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN).

Key milestones under her leadership include:

  • Zenith Bank’s market capitalisation reaching about ₦3.78 trillion

  • A successful public offer oversubscribed by more than 160%

  • Capital base increasing to ₦614.65 billion

  • Total assets expanding to ₦31.18 trillion by September 2025

These developments strengthened investor confidence while ensuring the bank complied with the CBN’s directive requiring banks with international licences to raise their minimum capital to ₦500 billion.

 

Nneka Onyeali-Ikpe: Transforming Fidelity Bank

Nneka Onyeali‑Ikpe, Managing Director and CEO of Fidelity Bank Plc, has overseen a major transformation of the bank since assuming office in January 2021.

Under her leadership, Fidelity Bank transitioned from a mid-tier institution to one of Nigeria’s most competitive lenders.

Key developments include:

  • The bank crossing the ₦1 trillion market capitalisation mark during her tenure

  • A ₦127 billion capital raise, which was oversubscribed

  • Expansion into international markets through the acquisition of Fidelity Bank UK Limited

  • Strengthening its position in SME banking and diaspora financial services

Although the bank’s valuation recently adjusted to around ₦964 billion, analysts expect the lender to regain trillion-naira status as its growth strategy continues.

 

Uzo Oshogwe: Driving Growth at Transcorp Hotels

Uzo Oshogwe, Managing Director of Transcorp Hotels Plc, leads the only hospitality company currently valued above ₦1 trillion on the NGX.

Since assuming office in January 2025, Oshogwe has focused on expanding the company’s assets and improving operational efficiency.

Recent performance highlights include:

  • Market valuation of approximately ₦1.94 trillion

  • Revenue rising 38% to ₦97.04 billion in 2025

  • Operating profit increasing 35% to ₦35.24 billion

  • Profit after tax growing 47% to ₦21.85 billion

A major project under her leadership is the 5,000-seat Transcorp Centre in Abuja, designed to strengthen Nigeria’s capacity to host global conferences, diplomatic gatherings, and major business events.

Digital transformation initiatives have also helped expand the company’s gross margin to about 77%.

A Gradual Shift in Corporate Leadership

Despite the progress represented by these executives, women remain significantly underrepresented in senior corporate leadership across Nigeria.

However, the success of leaders like Umeoji, Onyeali-Ikpe, and Oshogwe demonstrates that female leadership at the highest levels can deliver:

  • Strong financial performance

  • Successful capital raising

  • International expansion

  • Improved investor confidence

Their leadership reflects a gradual shift toward greater diversity in Nigeria’s corporate boardrooms.

Women leaders in large corporations help reshape perceptions about female leadership across the broader business ecosystem. Their success strengthens investor confidence in women-led enterprises and inspires more female entrepreneurs and MSME founders across Africa to pursue leadership and scale their businesses.
Nasarawa State University Partners Vocational Institute to Train 500 Furniture Entrepreneurs
New collaboration combines technical skills and business training to strengthen Nigeria’s furniture and interior design industry.

Nasarawa State University has partnered with Prince Interior Vocational Institute to train over 500 furniture artisans through a hands-on entrepreneurship programme. The initiative aims to equip students with practical production skills and business knowledge to build sustainable enterprises in Nigeria’s growing furniture sector.

Nasarawa State University  Keffi  has entered into a strategic partnership with Prince Interior Vocational Institute to train more than 500 furniture artisans over the next two years.

The collaboration, formalised through a Memorandum of Understanding, is designed to strengthen entrepreneurship education, vocational training, and enterprise development within Nigeria’s furniture and interior design industry.
