The initiative aligns with national efforts to promote skills acquisition, local manufacturing, and youth entrepreneurship.

 

Hands-On Training Across the Furniture Value Chain

The programme will provide participants with practical, industry-relevant training covering the full furniture production and business lifecycle.

Key training areas include:

  • Raw material sourcing and selection

  • Furniture design and manufacturing

  • Finishing and quality control

  • Branding, marketing, and distribution

  • Business development and enterprise management

Participants will also be enrolled in certified training programmes designed to prepare them for launching and managing furniture businesses.

 

On-Campus Workshop to Support Practical Learning

As part of the partnership, a medium-scale furniture production workshop will be established within the university.

The facility will:

  • Provide access to modern production equipment

  • Enable hands-on learning and real-time manufacturing experience

  • Support the university in reducing furniture procurement costs

  • Serve as a hub for innovation and enterprise development

The university’s Entrepreneurship Development Centre also confirmed that students will have access to over 50 vocational skills programmes, further expanding opportunities for practical learning.

 

Strengthening Entrepreneurship and Local Manufacturing

University officials noted that the initiative aligns with guidelines from the Tertiary Education Trust Fund (TETFund) on entrepreneurship development in higher institutions.

The programme is designed to:

  • Promote self-employment and business creation

  • Strengthen Nigeria’s local furniture manufacturing ecosystem

  • Support the “Buy Naija” initiative encouraging local production

  • Build a pipeline of skilled artisans and interior design entrepreneurs

Graduates are expected to launch businesses that contribute to local production, job creation, and value chain development.

Skills-based entrepreneurship is critical to MSME growth across Africa. By combining vocational training with business development, initiatives like this help create skilled artisans who can build scalable enterprises, strengthen local manufacturing, and reduce reliance on imports across key sectors like furniture and interior design.
Abia Secures $200m Palm Oil Investment to Boost Agro-Industrial Growth
New partnership with Presco Plc aims to develop a large-scale plantation and processing hub to revive regional agriculture.

Abia State has secured a $200 million investment from Presco Plc to develop a 14,000-hectare oil palm plantation and processing facility. The project is expected to create jobs, strengthen agro-processing value chains, and position the state as a key palm oil production hub in Nigeria.

The Abia State Government, led by Governor Alex Otti, has secured a $200 million investment partnership with Presco Plc to transform palm oil production and drive agricultural development in the state.

The agreement, finalised following a high-level meeting in Nvosi, will establish a 14,000-hectare oil palm plantation and processing hub, positioning Abia as a major agro-industrial centre in South-East Nigeria.

The initiative is part of broader efforts to revive the state’s agricultural economy and restore its historic leadership in palm oil production.

 

Large-Scale Plantation and Processing Hub

The project is designed to build an integrated palm oil value chain—from cultivation to processing, leveraging Abia’s fertile agricultural land.

Key components include:

  • Development of a 14,000-hectare oil palm plantation

  • Establishment of a modern processing facility

  • Expansion of agro-industrial activities across the state

  • Creation of a regional palm oil production hub

The project will be implemented across key communities, including Ozuitem, Abam, and Ulonna, with government support for land access and infrastructure.

 

Job Creation and Rural Economic Development

The investment is expected to generate over 5,000 direct and indirect jobs across the value chain.

Key impact areas include:

  • Employment in plantation operations and processing

  • Growth in logistics and supply chain services

  • Opportunities for smallholder farmers and local suppliers

  • Reduced rural-to-urban migration through local job creation

The state government has also committed to ensuring adequate security and fair compensation for host communities involved in the project.

 

Unlocking Value Across the Palm Oil Ecosystem

Executives from Presco Plc noted that the project is designed to attract downstream industries that depend on crude palm oil as a key input.

Potential value chain opportunities include:

  • Food processing and manufacturing

  • Packaging and distribution services

  • Equipment supply and maintenance

  • Export-oriented agribusiness activities

The project has received support from Afrinvest, following due diligence under the state’s Public-Private Partnership framework.

If fully implemented, the investment is expected to contribute billions of naira annually to Abia’s Gross Domestic Product (GDP).

 

Reviving Agricultural Heritage and Competitiveness

Officials say the initiative aligns with efforts to restore Abia’s legacy as a leading agricultural hub, reminiscent of the era of Michael Okpara, when the region was known for strong agricultural output.

The project is expected to strengthen:

  • Agricultural productivity and exports

  • Local processing capacity

  • Nigeria’s competitiveness in palm oil production

Large-scale agricultural investments create ripple effects across the MSME ecosystem. From farming and logistics to processing and packaging, this project opens up new opportunities for small businesses to participate in and benefit from a growing palm oil value chain, driving inclusive economic growth.
Rising Energy Costs Threaten SME Profitability in Nigeria, CPPE Warns
Experts highlight mounting pressure on small businesses as fuel prices and operating costs surge.

The Centre for the Promotion of Private Enterprise (CPPE) has warned that rising energy prices could severely impact the survival of Nigerian SMEs. With fuel costs climbing and macroeconomic pressures intensifying, businesses are being urged to adopt cost-control strategies and rethink pricing models to remain viable.

The Centre for the Promotion of Private Enterprise (CPPE) has raised concerns that escalating energy prices could significantly undermine the profitability and sustainability of small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) in Nigeria.

According to CPPE CEO, Muda Yusuf, many businesses are already grappling with high inflation, rising interest rates, and weak consumer purchasing power, making the recent spike in energy costs an additional and critical burden.

The organisation warned that without timely intervention, rising operating expenses could erode margins and threaten the survival of thousands of SMEs.

 

Energy Costs Driving Up Business Expenses

The surge in energy prices, particularly petrol has sharply increased operating costs across sectors.

Key concerns highlighted include:

  • Rising fuel prices between ₦1,230 and ₦1,300 per litre nationwide

  • Increased costs for transportation, logistics, and production

  • Reduced profit margins for SMEs with limited financial buffers

  • Higher vulnerability for businesses dependent on generators and alternative power sources

The situation is being worsened by global oil market disruptions linked to geopolitical tensions involving Iran, Israel, and the United States.

 

Strategic Responses for SMEs

To navigate rising costs, CPPE advised businesses to adopt practical strategies that balance cost recovery with customer retention.

Recommended actions include:

  • Gradual price adjustments to reflect rising costs

  • Strengthening product value propositions

  • Introducing smaller product sizes or alternative packaging

  • Improving cost efficiency and operational discipline

These measures can help businesses maintain competitiveness while managing cost pressures.

 

Strengthening Financial Resilience

Beyond pricing strategies, CPPE emphasised the need for stronger financial management to withstand economic shocks.

Key recommendations include:

  • Reducing non-essential expenditures

  • Improving inventory and cash flow management

  • Renegotiating supplier payment terms

  • Maintaining liquidity buffers

The organisation noted that liquidity challenges are often more severe for SMEs due to limited access to affordable financing.

 

Calls for Policy and Infrastructure Support

Industry stakeholders have also called for government intervention to mitigate the impact of rising energy costs.

  • The Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC) has urged government action following fuel price increases

  • The Petroleum Products Retail Outlets Owners Association of Nigeria (PETROAN) has called for investments in gas infrastructure to provide cheaper energy alternatives

CPPE stressed that without policy support, rising energy costs could weaken business activity and slow overall economic growth.

Energy costs are one of the largest expenses for African MSMEs. Sustained increases in fuel prices can reduce profitability, limit growth, and force business closures. Adopting cost-efficient operations and exploring alternative energy solutions will be critical for resilience in a volatile energy environment.
APICES Finance Launches Gold-Backed Lending and Invoice Financing to Boost SME Liquidity
Fresh financial solutions aim to unlock dormant assets and improve cash flow for Nigerian businesses.

The Centre for the Promotion of Private Enterprise (CPPE) has warned that rising energy prices could severely impact the survival of Nigerian SMEs. With fuel costs climbing and macroeconomic pressures intensifying, businesses are being urged to adopt cost-control strategies and rethink pricing models to remain viable.

APICES Finance has entered Nigeria’s financial services market with innovative products including gold-backed lending and debt factoring. The solutions are designed to help SMEs unlock capital tied in assets and access immediate funding from unpaid invoices, addressing key liquidity challenges.

 

APICES Finance Company Limited has launched operations in Nigeria with a suite of innovative financial solutions aimed at addressing liquidity constraints for SMEs and youth entrepreneurs.

The company’s flagship offerings, gold-for-cash financing and debt factoring are designed to unlock dormant capital and improve cash flow for businesses, particularly contractors and suppliers facing delayed payments.

The initiative reflects a growing shift toward alternative financing models that better meet the needs of underserved businesses.

 

Gold-for-Cash: Unlocking Dormant Personal Assets

One of the company’s key innovations is its gold-backed financing model, which allows individuals and entrepreneurs to access liquidity without selling their assets.

According to Daniel Oniko, Group Managing Director of APICES Finance, the product targets “wealth under the pillow”, gold holdings that are not actively generating economic value.

Key features include:

  • Access to financing using gold as collateral

  • Ability to retain ownership of the asset’s value

  • Conversion of idle assets into business capital

  • Increased access to funding for informal and underserved entrepreneurs

This model aims to activate untapped wealth and channel it into productive economic activities.

 

Debt Factoring to Solve Cash Flow Challenges

APICES Finance is also introducing debt factoring services to address one of the biggest challenges facing SMEs, delayed payments.

Through this model, businesses can:

  • Receive immediate cash advances on unpaid invoices

  • Improve working capital and liquidity

  • Reduce dependency on traditional bank loans

  • Maintain operations despite long payment cycles

This is particularly relevant for contractors and suppliers working with large organisations or government entities.

 

Digital-First Approach to Financial Inclusion

The company is leveraging technology to expand access to its services, particularly among underserved populations.

According to Evie Ukodiko, Board Chairman (represented by Moses Chukwu), APICES is focused on:

  • Digitised application and processing systems

  • Expanding access to finance for youths and small businesses

  • Supporting skill-based entrepreneurship and income generation

  • Building a scalable financial ecosystem that drives shared prosperity

The firm aims to bridge financing gaps that prevent many skilled individuals and entrepreneurs from scaling their businesses.

 

A New Approach to SME Financing in Nigeria

With leadership experience rooted in traditional banking, APICES Finance is positioning itself as a more agile alternative focused on speed, accessibility, and innovation.

The company’s approach addresses two major gaps in Nigeria’s financial ecosystem:

  • Unlocking dormant personal and household assets

  • Providing fast liquidity for businesses with delayed receivables

Access to finance remains one of the biggest barriers for African SMEs. Innovative solutions like asset-backed lending and invoice financing can unlock new funding channels, improve cash flow, and enable businesses to scale without relying solely on traditional banking systems.
Nigeria Introduces 1% Presumptive Tax for Informal Businesses
New tax framework aims to simplify compliance, expand the tax base, and reduce informal levies on small businesses.

The Federal Government of Nigeria has introduced a 1% presumptive tax on informal businesses to simplify tax collection and improve compliance. While the policy may reduce multiple unofficial levies and improve transparency, concerns remain about its impact on SMEs operating with thin profit margins.

The Federal Government of Nigeria has introduced a 1% presumptive tax framework targeting businesses in the informal sector, as part of broader efforts to expand the tax base and reform revenue collection.

Implemented through the Federal Inland Revenue Service (FIRS), the policy applies a flat 1% tax on annual turnover for eligible small businesses, replacing more complex profit-based tax assessments.

The reform also introduces a ban on cash-based tax collection, aimed at improving transparency and reducing illegal levies imposed on small business operators.

 

How the Presumptive Tax System Works

The new framework is designed to simplify tax compliance for micro and small enterprises that often lack formal accounting systems.

Key features include:

  • 1% tax on annual turnover for eligible informal businesses

  • Elimination of profit-based tax calculations for small operators

  • Targeting businesses with limited or no formal financial records

  • Mandatory non-cash, traceable tax payments

The approach is expected to make it easier for traders, artisans, and small service providers to comply with tax requirements.

 

Potential Benefits for SMEs

The government says the reform could improve the business environment for small enterprises by reducing inefficiencies in tax collection.

Expected benefits include:

  • Reduction in multiple and unofficial tax levies

  • Improved transparency through digital payments

  • Lower risk of harassment by tax officials

  • Simpler and more predictable tax obligations

For many SMEs, this could mark a shift toward a more structured and accountable tax system.

 

Concerns Over Cost Burden on Small Businesses

Despite its simplicity, the new tax has raised concerns among SME operators.

Key challenges include:

  • Tax based on turnover rather than profit, affecting low-margin businesses

  • Increased pressure during periods of high inflation and rising operating costs

  • Risk of additional financial burden for already struggling enterprises

Analysts note that the overall impact will depend on how effectively the policy is implemented and whether it replaces existing informal levies.

 

A Step Toward Formalisation

The policy is part of Nigeria’s broader strategy to integrate informal businesses into the formal economy.

Potential long-term benefits include:

  • Improved access to government support programmes

  • Increased eligibility for financing and credit facilities

  • Better participation in formal markets and supply chains

As Nigeria seeks to strengthen non-oil revenue, reforms like the presumptive tax are expected to play a key role in shaping the SME landscape.

Simplified tax systems can improve compliance and reduce informal levies, but turnover-based taxes may strain low-margin businesses. For MSMEs, the key will be balancing compliance with profitability while leveraging formalisation to access funding, support programmes, and new market opportunities.
Oil Price Spike Raises Operating Costs for SMEs as Supply Tightens
Strait of Hormuz disruption fuels renewed price surge, raising global energy cost concerns.

Oil prices rebounded by about 3% amid escalating tensions in the Middle East and disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz. With supply routes constrained and production impacted, analysts warn that prolonged conflict could push energy prices even higher.

Global oil prices rose sharply as renewed supply concerns emerged from escalating conflict in the Middle East, particularly disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz, a critical channel for global energy flows.

Brent crude increased by 3.1% to $103.28 per barrel, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate (WTI) rose 3.6% to $96.85, reversing losses recorded in the previous trading session.

The rebound reflects growing fears that prolonged instability could significantly impact global oil supply and pricing.

 

Strait of Hormuz Disruptions Drive Supply Fears

The Strait of Hormuz, which handles approximately 20% of global oil and liquefied natural gas trade, has been severely affected by ongoing conflict involving Iran, Israel, and the United States.

Key developments include:

  • Partial shutdown of shipping routes through the strait

  • Reduced tanker movement due to security risks

  • Increased threat of attacks on oil infrastructure and vessels

  • Limited military support for escorting oil shipments through the region

Analysts warn that even minor disruptions could trigger further volatility in global oil markets.

 

Production Cuts and Infrastructure Risks Intensify Pressure

The supply situation has worsened due to production disruptions and security incidents across the region.

Notable developments:

  • The United Arab Emirates has reportedly reduced oil production by more than half

  • A drone attack triggered a fire in the Fujairah Oil Industry Zone

  • Middle East crude benchmarks have surged to record highs

  • Ongoing risks to oil transport and infrastructure across the Gulf

Market analysts note that continued instability could further tighten supply and sustain upward pressure on prices.

 

Global Response and Market Outlook

Efforts are underway to stabilise the oil market, but uncertainties remain.

  • The International Energy Agency (IEA) has already approved the release of 400 million barrels from strategic reserves

  • Additional releases may be considered if supply disruptions persist

  • Analysts project that oil prices could rise further, with some forecasts placing WTI at up to $124 per barrel

The duration of the conflict remains the key factor influencing market direction.

Rising oil prices directly increase fuel, logistics, and production costs for African businesses. For MSMEs, sustained energy price volatility can reduce margins, disrupt operations, and increase the cost of goods, making cost efficiency and alternative energy strategies increasingly critical.
MTN Delivers Strong 2025 Growth as Data and Fintech Power Performance
Telecom giant expands customer base, boosts earnings, and shifts toward a platform-driven future.

MTN Group reported strong financial and operational growth in 2025, driven by rising demand for data and fintech services across Africa. With over 307 million users and improved profitability, the company is positioning for long-term expansion under its Ambition 2030 strategy.

MTN Group recorded a strong performance for the year ended December 2025, with growth driven by increased data consumption, fintech adoption, and expansion across key African markets.

The company now serves 307.2 million customers across 16 markets, reinforcing its position as one of Africa’s largest digital and financial services platforms.

 

Strong Revenue and Profit Growth

MTN delivered solid financial gains across key metrics:

  • Service revenue: R218.5 billion (+22.9%)

  • EBITDA: R98.5 billion (+36.8%)

  • EBITDA margin: 44.5%

  • Headline earnings per share: 1,274 cents (up from 110 cents in 2024)

  • Free cash flow: R26.9 billion (+345.5%)

Growth was largely driven by strong performance in MTN Nigeria and MTN Ghana, with service revenue growth of 54.9% and 35.9% respectively (constant currency).

 

Data and Fintech Drive Customer Growth

Demand for digital services continues to shape MTN’s growth trajectory.

Key platform metrics:

  • Total subscribers: 307.2 million (+5.6%)

  • Active data users: 172.6 million (+9.4%)

  • Data revenue: R101.5 billion (+37.7%)

  • Data traffic: +27% to 24.7 petabytes

Fintech growth:

  • MoMo active users: 69.5 million (+10%)

  • Transaction volume: 23.3 billion (+14.9%)

  • Transaction value: $500.3 billion (+37.6%)

MTN continues to scale its fintech ecosystem despite rising competition and pricing pressures.

 

Stronger Balance Sheet and Investment Focus

MTN improved its financial position while continuing to invest in infrastructure:

  • Net debt-to-EBITDA: Reduced to 0.3x (from 0.7x)

  • Liquidity: R43.1 billion available

  • Capital expenditure: R38.5 billion (focused on network expansion)

Investments were concentrated on strengthening network capacity, especially in high-growth markets like Nigeria and Ghana.

 

Increased Shareholder Returns

Stronger earnings translated into higher investor payouts:

  • Final dividend: 500 cents per share (up from 345 cents)

  • Target payout: 40%–60% of equity free cash flow

  • Potential R6 billion share buyback programme (from 2026)

 

Strategic Shift to Ambition 2030

MTN is transitioning to a platform-led growth model under its Ambition 2030 strategy, focusing on:

  • Connectivity

  • Fintech

  • Digital infrastructure

Key strategic moves include:

  • Network-sharing agreements in Nigeria and Uganda

  • Structural separation of fintech businesses

  • Planned acquisition of remaining stake in IHS Holdings

 

Outlook and Market Risks

While growth remains strong, MTN highlighted potential risks:

  • Global geopolitical tensions affecting energy prices and inflation

  • Foreign exchange volatility across African markets

  • Need to improve performance in South Africa’s prepaid segment

Despite these challenges, the company expects continued growth driven by rising digital adoption across the continent.

MTN’s expansion in data and fintech strengthens digital infrastructure and financial access for small businesses across Africa. For MSMEs, this means better connectivity, easier payments, and more opportunities to scale through digital platforms and mobile financial services.
Cassava Positioned as a Strategic Driver of Africa’s Food Security and Industrial Growth.
Stakeholders highlight cassava’s expanding role in agro-processing, manufacturing, and economic development across Africa.

Industry leaders and policymakers have reaffirmed cassava’s importance to Africa’s food security and industrialisation. At the Africa Cassava Conference in Abuja, stakeholders outlined strategies to scale production, deepen processing, and unlock value across the cassava value chain.

Cassava has been identified as a critical asset for Africa’s food systems and industrial future, with stakeholders calling for increased investment in processing, technology, and value chain development.

Speaking at the Africa Cassava Conference in Abuja, the Director-General of the Pan-African Organisation for Small and Medium Industries (PAOSMI) emphasised that cassava remains both a staple food and a high-value industrial crop across the continent.

 

Cassava’s Dual Role: Food Staple and Industrial Input

Cassava continues to play a vital role in both household consumption and industrial production.

Key applications include:

  • Widely consumed foods such as garri, tapioca, and flour-based products

  • Industrial inputs including:

    • Ethanol

    • High-quality cassava flour (HQCF)

    • Sorbitol and sweeteners

This versatility positions cassava as a strategic crop for food security and manufacturing growth.

 

Conference Highlights and Strategic Focus

The three-day Africa Cassava Conference brought together policymakers, scientists, and industry players from across the continent to align on growth strategies.

Key outcomes include:

  • Participation from over seven African countries

  • Focus on policy alignment, research, and industrial utilisation

  • Development of strategies to scale processing and value addition

  • Increased collaboration between governments, financiers, and agribusiness stakeholders

The conference served as a platform to translate cassava’s potential into practical, scalable economic opportunities.

 

Key Challenges Facing the Cassava Value Chain

Stakeholders identified several barriers limiting the sector’s growth, particularly for smallholder farmers:

  • Limited access to affordable financing

  • Gaps in modern farming practices and mechanisation

  • Weak processing capacity and industrial standards

  • Fragmented market access and value chains

The involvement of institutions such as the Bank of Industry is expected to support the development of financing solutions for the sector.

 

Technology and Mechanisation as Growth Enablers

Experts emphasised the importance of technology in unlocking cassava’s full potential.

Key insights:

  • Mechanised solutions, including cassava harvesters, can enable large-scale production

  • Adoption of modern tools can improve efficiency, yield, and consistency

  • Technology integration is critical to positioning cassava within Africa’s industrial transformation agenda

 

Regional Collaboration and Knowledge Sharing

The conference highlighted growing cross-border collaboration in the cassava sector.

  • Countries such as South Sudan are looking to learn from Nigeria’s experience in production and processing

  • Shared innovations between countries like Ghana and Nigeria can accelerate sector growth

  • Stronger regional cooperation can improve food security, nutrition, and export potential

Cassava presents significant opportunities for MSMEs in farming, processing, packaging, and manufacturing. By investing in value addition and leveraging new technologies, small businesses can tap into a growing agro-industrial market and build scalable, export-ready enterprises.
iDICE Startup Bridge Offers $100,000 Investment and Grants to Nigerian Techpreneurs
New national programme provides structured funding, mentorship, and growth pathways for early-stage and scaling startups.

Nigeria has launched the iDICE Startup Bridge Programme to support tech entrepreneurs with grants, equity investment, and capacity building. The initiative offers up to $100,000 in funding for growth-stage startups and grants of up to ₦10 million for early-stage founders.

Nigeria has introduced a new pathway to support startup growth through the iDICE Startup Bridge Programme, a nationwide initiative designed to strengthen early-stage innovation and improve access to capital.

The programme operates under the Investment in Digital and Creative Enterprises (iDICE) Programme, implemented by the Bank of Industry (BOI) and supported by global partners including the African Development Bank (AfDB), Agence Française de Développement (AFD), and the Islamic Development Bank (IsDB).

The initiative targets entrepreneurs across all 36 states and the FCT, aiming to unlock job creation and scale Nigeria’s digital economy.

 

Two-Tier Support Model for Startup Growth

The iDICE Startup Bridge Programme is structured into two key pathways to support founders at different stages:

  1. Founders Lab (Early-Stage Startups):

  • 12-week capacity-building programme

  • Focus on idea validation, business models, and MVP development

  • 250 participants trained annually

  • 100 top participants receive grants of up to ₦10 million

  1. Growth Lab (Scaling Startups):

  • Targets startups with existing traction and revenue potential

  • Each selected startup receives up to $100,000 in equity investment

  • Includes support for scaling, governance, and fundraising readiness

 

Access to Capital and Investor Networks

Beyond training and funding, the programme provides structured access to capital markets.

Key benefits include:

  • Direct connection to institutional investors

  • Opportunity for follow-on funding

  • Access to match funding for startups that secure external investment

  • Support in refining investment strategy and business structure

Over time, the programme is expected to support more than 500 Nigerian startups, strengthening the country’s innovation pipeline.

 

Government and Institutional Backing

The programme is part of a broader national strategy to drive enterprise growth and youth employment.

  • Backed by the Federal Government of Nigeria

  • Delivered through BOI’s growing track record in SME financing

  • Builds on BOI’s ₦636 billion disbursement in 2025, including ₦43 billion to digital and creative sectors

Leaders say the initiative is designed to move startups from idea stage to scalable businesses.

 

Structured Pathway from Idea to Scale

Programme leaders emphasise a clear progression model:

  • Idea → Validation → Product → Funding → Scale

The Founders Lab supports early exploration, while the Growth Lab focuses on turning validated startups into investment-ready ventures capable of attracting institutional capital.

This programme expands access to funding, mentorship, and investor networks for African startups, key barriers to growth. For MSMEs, especially in tech and creative sectors, it creates a clearer pathway to scale, attract capital, and build sustainable, high-impact businesses.
Naira Gains Momentum Amid Improved FX Liquidity
Stronger reserves and market stability drive continued appreciation of Nigeria’s currency.

The naira continued its upward trend, appreciating to N1,355 per dollar at the official market, supported by improved foreign reserves and stable market conditions. The Central Bank of Nigeria attributes the gains to ongoing reforms and increased investor confidence.

The Nigerian naira continued its recovery on Monday, appreciating to N1,355 per dollar at the official foreign exchange market, up from N1,363.5/$ on Friday. This marks the currency’s strongest level since February 23, 2026, when it closed at N1,353.5/$.

According to data from the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), the naira has maintained a steady upward trend in recent days, supported by improved market conditions and liquidity. Earlier in the week, the currency moved as follows:

  • Tuesday: N1,390.5/$

  • Wednesday: N1,373.5/$

  • Thursday: N1,370/$

  • Friday: N1,363.5/$

During Monday’s trading session, the naira fluctuated between N1,365.35/$ and N1,354/$, indicating stable intraday activity and a healthier supply of foreign currency in the market.

The CBN attributed the gains to Nigeria’s improving external reserves, which stood at $34.8 billion in net foreign exchange reserves at the end of 2025. Gross external reserves also increased to $50.45 billion in February 2026, supported by higher oil revenues and stronger foreign inflows.

CBN Governor Olayemi Cardoso highlighted that ongoing monetary and foreign exchange reforms are aimed at boosting investor confidence and enhancing liquidity. The bank’s 2026 macroeconomic outlook projects that external reserves could rise further to $51.04 billion, largely driven by continued oil earnings and foreign capital inflows.

Global market dynamics also influenced local sentiment. Early trading in Asia saw the euro decline 0.12% to $1.1492, the British pound slip 0.1% to $1.33, and the Australian dollar weaken slightly ahead of a key interest rate decision. The U.S. dollar index remained largely unchanged at 99.913. Analysts said that geopolitical tensions, including developments involving Iran, have contributed to cautious movements in global currency markets.

Financial market experts note that the naira’s recovery could improve liquidity for businesses, reduce the local cost of imported goods, and enhance pricing predictability. This could be particularly beneficial for small and medium enterprises (SMEs) that rely on foreign inputs for production and trade.

While the naira’s gains are encouraging, market watchers continue to monitor global and domestic factors, including oil prices, foreign inflows, and the CBN’s ongoing reforms, for their potential impact on exchange rate stability in the coming months.

The appreciation of the naira and improved foreign exchange stability are critical for MSMEs across Nigeria and Africa, particularly those involved in importation, manufacturing, and cross-border trade. A stronger and more stable currency reduces the cost of importing raw materials, equipment, and goods, helping small businesses manage expenses and maintain pricing stability.
For export-oriented MSMEs, improved FX liquidity enhances access to formal foreign exchange channels, making international transactions more efficient and predictable. It also reduces reliance on parallel markets, lowering financial risk and uncertainty.
More broadly, currency stability strengthens investor confidence, which can increase access to funding, partnerships, and expansion opportunities for MSMEs. As Africa’s largest economy stabilizes its currency, the ripple effects can support regional trade, improve supply chains, and create a more enabling environment for small businesses to grow and compete.
Rising Food Costs Pressure Nigerian MSMEs as Inflation Returns to Double Digits
Rising input costs push Nigeria’s food inflation to 12.12%, increasing pressure on households and businesses.

Nigeria’s food inflation rose to 12.12% in February 2026, reversing January’s single-digit level due to higher input costs and rising prices of staple foods. While inflation remains lower than last year’s peak, short-term pressures on food prices are intensifying.

Nigeria’s food inflation returned to double digits in February 2026, climbing to 12.12% year-on-year from 8.89% in January, the National Bureau of Statistics (NBS) reported. This marks the first return to double-digit food inflation in over a decade following January’s brief slowdown.

Despite the sharp rise, the figure remains well below February 2025’s 26.98%, indicating a gradual easing of inflationary pressures over the past year. On a month-on-month basis, food prices increased by 4.69%, reflecting volatility in key food markets. Staples such as beans, cassava tubers, crayfish, millet flour, yam flour, ogbono, and cowpeas were among the items driving the surge.

Farmers and market stakeholders linked the spike to rising production costs, including seeds, fertilisers, and transportation. They have urged government support to stabilise input prices and ease pressure on both producers and consumers.

State-level data highlight significant regional differences in food inflation. Kogi State recorded the highest rate at 26.91%, followed by Adamawa at 23.12% and Benue at 21.89%. On the other hand, Katsina posted the lowest food inflation at 5.09%, while Bauchi and Imo recorded 7.09% and 7.65%, respectively. Such disparities underscore the uneven impact of rising food prices across the country, affecting MSMEs differently depending on their location.

Over the 12 months ending February 2026, the average food inflation rate declined to 19.08%, a significant drop from 37.40% in the same period of 2025. Nevertheless, food continues to be the largest contributor to headline inflation, accounting for 6.03 percentage points of the overall index. Headline inflation eased slightly to 15.06% in February from 15.10% in January, although monthly inflation rose by 2.01%, reflecting short-term price pressures.

The surge in food prices presents challenges for micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs), particularly those involved in food production, retail, and agro-processing. Rising procurement costs and reduced consumer spending power may squeeze profit margins for businesses such as local food vendors, restaurants, and small-scale processors. Market traders relying on daily sales are also likely to feel the impact.

Economists warn that continued increases in food prices could hinder MSME growth, urging targeted support measures such as subsidies on essential inputs, improved access to finance, and policies aimed at strengthening local supply chains. With food costs being a critical driver of operational expenses, the inflation trend remains a key concern for both businesses and policymakers.

Rising food inflation has direct implications for MSMEs across Nigeria and Africa, particularly those in agriculture, food processing, retail, and hospitality. Higher input costs, such as seeds, fertilizers, transportation, and energy reduce profit margins for small businesses and often force price increases that can weaken consumer demand.
Fuel Supply Arrivals Amid Rising Prices Add Pressure on Nigerian MSMEs
Incoming petrol and diesel supplies expected to ease availability concerns, even as costs surge nationwide.

Nigeria is receiving new shipments of petrol and diesel amid rising fuel prices, with authorities clarifying that imports are based on earlier licences. While supply is being maintained, high global oil prices continue to drive up local fuel costs.

As fuel prices climb across Nigeria, vessels carrying approximately 129,000 metric tonnes of Premium Motor Spirit (petrol) and Automotive Gas Oil (diesel) are expected to dock at Lagos ports between March 14 and 17, 2026, the Nigerian Ports Authority (NPA) reported.

On March 14, the vessel Mosunmola delivered 20,000 metric tonnes of petrol, while Kobe brought 22,000 metric tonnes of diesel to Tin Can Island Port. Additional shipments, including Bora with 27,000 metric tonnes of petrol and Ashabi carrying 30,000 metric tonnes of diesel, are scheduled to arrive in the coming days. Calabar ports have also received deliveries as part of ongoing supply efforts to meet domestic demand.

The arrivals coincide with retail petrol prices exceeding N1,200 per litre, following adjustments by the Dangote Petroleum Refinery, with some regions recording prices between N1,200 and N1,300 per litre. Analysts caution that continued global oil price volatility, influenced by geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, may push prices even higher in the coming weeks.

Rising fuel costs are already affecting micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs), particularly businesses reliant on transportation and logistics. Small-scale retailers, agro-processors, and delivery-based enterprises face increased operating costs, which are passed on to goods and services. Industry stakeholders have called for government interventions to mitigate the financial pressure on small businesses.

The Nigerian Midstream and Downstream Petroleum Regulatory Authority (NMDPRA) confirmed that no new petrol import licences were issued in the first quarter of 2026. Current imports are based on previously approved licences and delayed shipments.

Independent marketers have pledged to distribute available fuel to maintain supply and market competition, though experts note that prices are unlikely to fall significantly without a reduction in global crude oil rates.

Domestic refinery output is rising, with the Dangote refinery currently producing about 36 million litres per day. However, this remains below Nigeria’s estimated daily consumption of 56 million litres, leaving a persistent supply gap that continues to be bridged by imports.

For MSMEs across sectors, the combination of high fuel costs and fluctuating supply highlights the need for strategic planning, including budgeting for transport expenses and exploring alternative logistics solutions. Policymakers and industry players continue to monitor the situation closely, given its potential impact on inflation, consumer demand, and overall business sustainability.

Rising fuel prices have a direct and immediate impact on MSMEs across Nigeria and Africa. Increased transportation and logistics costs raise the cost of moving goods, affecting supply chains for retailers, manufacturers, and distributors.
For small businesses, especially those reliant on generators due to inconsistent power supply, higher diesel and petrol prices significantly increase operating expenses, reducing profit margins. Many MSMEs are forced to either absorb these costs or pass them on to consumers, which can reduce demand.
Nigeria Strengthens AfCFTA Integration, Opening Opportunities for MSMEs
Nigeria deepens continental trade participation with first AfCFTA shipment and strengthened export systems

Nigeria has made notable progress in implementing the AfCFTA, moving from planning to active trade participation. Key achievements include its first AfCFTA-compliant export, improved trade systems, stronger institutional coordination, and increased support for exporters and private sector engagement.

Nigeria has made measurable progress in implementing the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA), according to the latest scorecard from the Nigeria AfCFTA Coordination Office. The report, covering 2024–2025, highlights the country’s transition from preparatory work to active execution of its AfCFTA commitments.

The report credited the late National Coordinator, Mr. Olusegun Awolowo, for positioning Nigeria as an active participant in the continental trade agreement. Key achievements include Nigeria’s formal participation in AfCFTA trading arrangements, enhanced institutional coordination, and increased engagement with private sector stakeholders and development partners.

Dr. Olusegun Olutayo, Acting National Coordinator, said the report underscores the Federal Government’s commitment to maximising AfCFTA opportunities. The office plans to expand exporter participation, strengthen institutional systems, and boost Nigeria’s trade footprint across African markets.

A significant milestone highlighted in the report is Nigeria’s participation in the AfCFTA Guided Trade Initiative, a pilot programme designed to facilitate preferential trade among member countries. The country completed its first AfCFTA-compliant shipment, marking a major step toward continental integration.

The report also notes improvements in trade facilitation, capacity-building programmes for exporters, customs officials, and regulatory agencies, as well as efforts to identify export-ready products. Digitalisation initiatives have been introduced to integrate AfCFTA processes into national trade information systems, improving transparency and data exchange.

For micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs), these developments present new opportunities for regional expansion. Improved regulatory alignment, streamlined trade procedures, and digital tracking systems make it easier for MSMEs to access African markets, participate in regional value chains, and enhance export readiness.

The Coordination Office emphasised that ongoing engagement with government institutions, private enterprises, and trade groups will support deeper integration. As Nigeria continues to strengthen its AfCFTA framework, MSMEs are expected to benefit from broader market access, capacity-building support, and new cross-border trade opportunities.

AfCFTA implementation opens up a larger, tariff-reduced African market for MSMEs, making it easier for small businesses to export, scale operations, and reach new customers. With improved trade systems, capacity-building programmes, and clearer regulations, MSMEs can better compete, integrate into regional value chains, and drive intra-African trade growth.
Nigeria’s Internet Boom Creates New Opportunities for MSMEs
Rising data usage driven by infrastructure investment, broadband expansion, and growing digital demand

Nigeria’s internet usage has grown by 168% between January 2023 and January 2026, reaching over 1.38 million terabytes, according to data from the Nigerian Communications Commission (NCC). The steady annual growth of about 38–39% reflects increasing reliance on digital services, supported by over $1bn in telecom investments, expansion of network infrastructure, and rising broadband penetration, now at 53.07%.

Nigeria’s internet usage has surged by 168% over the past three years, reaching 1,385,536 terabytes (TB) in January 2026, up from 517,670 TB in January 2023, according to the Nigerian Communications Commission (NCC).

Data from the NCC shows consistent year-on-year growth: 721,522 TB in January 2024, representing a 39.4% increase; 1,000,931 TB in January 2025, up 38.7%; and a 38.4% rise by January 2026. Broadband penetration also expanded to 53.07%, equivalent to 115.04 million subscriptions, up from 112.67 million in December 2025 a net gain of 2.37 million users in just one month.

The rapid expansion is linked to major investments by telecommunications operators, who spent over $1 billion in 2025 to deploy more than 2,850 new network sites nationwide. These investments have strengthened coverage, improved network performance, and increased capacity to meet rising demand. Operators have pledged to exceed 2025 investment levels in 2026, with over N1.4 trillion earmarked for network expansion and the gradual rollout of 5G services.

The Federal Government has also prioritised bridging the digital divide, approving the deployment of 4,000 telecom towers in rural communities to improve access to communication services.

For micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs), the internet boom presents significant opportunities. Expanded connectivity enables e-commerce, digital marketing, remote collaboration, online payments, and access to new markets across Nigeria and Africa.

Digital platforms allow small businesses to reach more customers, reduce operational costs, and scale faster than traditional methods.

Dr. Aminu Maida, NCC Executive Vice Chairman, said the telecommunications industry’s investments are crucial to supporting Nigeria’s growing online economy and ensuring network resilience as millions of users increasingly conduct daily activities online.

The surge in internet traffic underscores the growing role of digital infrastructure in Nigeria’s economy. MSMEs that leverage these trends through online services, e-commerce platforms, and digital solutions are best positioned to benefit from the country’s expanding connectivity landscape.

The surge in internet access and usage creates more opportunities for MSMEs to operate digitally enabling e-commerce, digital marketing, remote work, and online payments. Improved connectivity lowers barriers to entry, helps small businesses reach wider markets, and supports innovation, making MSMEs more competitive within Nigeria and across Africa.
Dangote Refinery Partners with Fuel Marketers to Boost Supply and Shield MSMEs
Collaboration aims to diversify channels, reduce risks, and maintain stable fuel distribution amid global volatility

Dangote Petroleum Refinery has partnered with major fuel marketers to strengthen Nigeria’s fuel supply, reduce reliance on a single facility, and improve distribution efficiency. The arrangement, coordinated with regulators, ensures operational flexibility amid global oil market volatility, supports stable pricing, and reinforces supply security, with NNPC acting as a supplier of last resort.

The Dangote Petroleum Refinery has partnered with major fuel marketers to strengthen Nigeria’s fuel supply and reduce risks associated with reliance on a single source, the Major Energies Marketers Association of Nigeria (MEMAN) announced.

Speaking at a MEMAN webinar, Chairman Hubb Stokman said the collaboration aims to improve operational efficiency while mitigating concentration risks in the downstream sector. “Having a refinery that can produce almost everything the country needs is a huge benefit,” he said, adding that working with marketers spreads supply channels and enhances distribution across the country.

The partnership comes amid ongoing global oil market volatility, driven in part by geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. Stokman noted that the Nigerian market has responded positively to the arrangement but emphasized the need for continued agility to navigate fast-moving market conditions.

Fuel pricing in Nigeria continues to follow international trends under the country’s deregulated system, with import parity guiding local prices. The Nigerian Midstream and Downstream Petroleum Regulatory Authority (NMDPRA) ensures a needs-based approach to imports, while the Nigerian National Petroleum Company Limited (NNPC) acts as a supplier of last resort to maintain market stability.

Experts warn that policy inconsistencies and regulatory uncertainties could undermine market confidence. Joe Nwakwue, partner at Zeta Advisory and Consulting, stressed the importance of maintaining a competitive market, noting that imports remain critical for sustaining price stability and mitigating global shocks. He cautioned that rising fuel costs could strain economic growth if unmanaged, recommending targeted, temporary interventions rather than open-ended subsidies.

For micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs), the partnership is particularly significant. Stable fuel supply supports key operational inputs such as transportation, logistics, and distribution essential for retailers, food vendors, transport operators, and other small businesses. By reducing the risk of disruptions, the arrangement helps MSMEs plan costs, maintain service delivery, and sustain growth despite price volatility.

This partnership matters to African MSMEs because stable and reliable fuel supply is critical for their operations, especially for businesses that depend on transportation, logistics, and power generation. By reducing supply disruptions and mitigating price shocks, MSMEs can operate more efficiently, plan costs better, and remain competitive, supporting growth and resilience across Nigeria and the wider African market.
Structural Challenges Continue to Undermine Growth of Nigerian MSMEs
Report highlights financing gaps, infrastructure deficits, low technology adoption, and informality as key challenges

A new policy review by Africari Limited, titled “Reassessment of Nigeria’s National Policy on Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs), 2021–2025 and Strategic Directions for 2026–2030,” identifies persistent structural barriers undermining MSME growth. Despite the sector’s vital role in Nigeria’s economy, challenges such as limited access to long-term financing, weak infrastructure, high informality, low technology adoption, and high business mortality rates continue to stifle small businesses. The report notes that previous government interventions, including credit schemes and entrepreneurship programmes, have not fully addressed these systemic constraints.

A recent policy review has identified significant structural barriers impeding the growth and sustainability of Nigeria’s micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs). The report, titled “Reassessment of Nigeria’s National Policy on Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs), 2021–2025 and Strategic Directions for 2026–2030,” noted that despite the sector’s critical role in the economy, persistent challenges continue to affect small businesses.

The study, conducted by Africari Limited and led by policy researcher Onyinye Ozurumba, highlights limited access to affordable, long-term financing, weak infrastructure, high levels of enterprise informality, low technological adoption, and elevated business mortality rates as key factors undermining MSME performance.

While the government implemented various financial interventions, entrepreneurship programmes, and regulatory reforms during the 2021–2025 policy period, the report found that structural and institutional constraints have prevented sustained enterprise growth.

Many MSMEs continue to struggle to access credit that supports expansion, with most available loans serving short-term liquidity needs rather than capital investment in equipment, technology, or production. As a result, borrowed funds are often used to cover operational expenses such as rent, staff salaries, inventory, and energy costs.

Weak infrastructure, particularly unreliable electricity, inadequate logistics networks, and rising energy costs, further increases operational expenses and reduces competitiveness. Low adoption of digital tools and technology also limits productivity and access to broader domestic and international markets.

The study also pointed to high informality within the sector as a major obstacle. Many MSMEs operate outside the formal economy due to regulatory complexities, administrative costs, and multiple taxation obligations, including VAT, company income tax, stamp duties, import duties, and personal income tax. Businesses that formalize often face additional compliance burdens, discouraging formal operations.

According to the report, these structural challenges contribute to high business mortality rates, with many MSMEs unable to survive beyond the initial years of operation. The study calls for targeted reforms, including improved access to long-term financing, infrastructure development, digital adoption, and regulatory simplification, to strengthen MSME growth and sustainability in Nigeria.

The report underscores that without decisive interventions, Nigeria’s MSME sector which is vital for employment, innovation, and economic growth will continue to face constraints that limit its full potential.

The report concludes that without targeted reforms addressing these structural and institutional constraints, high business mortality rates will continue, preventing Nigerian MSMEs from achieving their full potential.
PAMA Warns Africa Against Overreliance on Raw Material Exports Amid China Duty-Free Policy
Experts urge value addition to maximize benefits of China’s zero-tariff policy for African economies

China’s planned zero-tariff policy for 53 African countries presents a major opportunity for the continent to expand exports. However, PAMA President Mansur Ahmed warns that relying solely on raw material exports exposes African economies to price volatility and low margins. Real gains will come from producing value-added and processed goods, upgrading industries, and developing regional value chains to boost competitiveness, job creation, and sustainable industrial growth.

The President of the Pan-African Manufacturers Association (PAMA), Mansur Ahmed, has cautioned that Africa’s dependence on raw material exports leaves economies vulnerable to price volatility and low-margin trade.

Ahmed’s remarks followed China’s announcement that, effective May 1, 2026, it will remove tariffs on all imports from 53 African countries, expanding duty-free access to one of the world’s largest consumer markets. The move builds on an existing arrangement covering 33 nations and spans agricultural commodities, minerals, and manufactured goods. Last year, bilateral trade between Africa and China surpassed $300 billion.

While the policy could boost short-term exports of oil, minerals, and other raw materials, Ahmed emphasized that long-term gains require value addition. “The zero-tariff window is expected to act as a strategic catalyst for industrial transformation. Manufacturers can upgrade facilities, adopt modern technologies, and meet Chinese market standards,” he said.

Ahmed highlighted potential opportunities in sectors such as processed foods, cocoa derivatives, textiles, and light manufacturing, which could generate higher profit margins, create jobs, and strengthen local supply chains. He also stressed the importance of regional value chains to link inter-African production networks with global markets, improving productivity, reducing costs, and enhancing export competitiveness.

For micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs), the announcement presents both opportunity and challenge. SMEs in agro-processing, light manufacturing, and related sectors could leverage duty-free access to expand exports, scale operations, and access international markets. However, failure to move beyond raw material exports may limit profitability and expose smaller firms to market fluctuations.

Ahmed warned that without deliberate value addition, African exports may remain concentrated in primary commodities, reinforcing dependence on volatile markets and limiting industrial growth. He urged governments, industry associations, and private enterprises to implement policies and strategies that encourage the production of semi-processed and finished goods.

The PAMA president’s comments underscore the need for industrialization and strategic planning to ensure Africa fully benefits from expanding trade opportunities, particularly as duty-free access to China opens new avenues for growth and competitiveness.

This matters to African MSMEs because moving beyond raw material exports opens opportunities for small and medium enterprises to produce processed and value-added goods for international markets. By upgrading production, improving quality, and linking into regional value chains, MSMEs can increase margins, create jobs, expand market access, and reduce vulnerability to price shocks, strengthening their role in Africa’s industrialisation and trade growth.
Nigerian Startup Launches Platform to Simplify Tax Compliance for Businesses
Digital solution automates tax calculations and filings, easing burdens for SMEs and enterprises

TaxStreem has launched a digital platform that automates tax calculations and filings, helping businesses overcome fragmented records and complex regulations. The tool centralizes data, ensures accurate compliance, and stores records for audits, simplifying the administrative burden for companies in Nigeria and potentially across Africa.

A Nigerian tax compliance startup, TaxStreem, has launched a digital platform aimed at automating tax calculations and filings for businesses, addressing persistent challenges in regulatory compliance.

Many businesses in Nigeria struggle with tax obligations due to fragmented financial records, complex regulations, and manual filing processes. TaxStreem’s platform aggregates transaction data from multiple sources including bank accounts, email, WhatsApp Business, and invoicing tools then calculates applicable taxes and files returns with relevant authorities. The system also stores records for audit purposes. For businesses with limited digital infrastructure, transaction data can be uploaded manually using structured templates.

The startup was founded by Kelechi Ibe, a tax lawyer with experience at Udo Udoma & Belo-Osagie, KPMG, and Latham & Watkins, and Sam Ayo, an artificial intelligence and machine learning engineer.

Speaking on the development, Ibe said: “Many businesses struggle with tax compliance because their financial information is spread across different tools and records. TaxStreem brings that information together in one place, automatically calculates the taxes that apply to each transaction, and helps businesses file correctly and on time. Our goal is to make compliance a seamless part of running a business rather than an administrative burden.”

The platform is expected to improve accuracy in tax reporting, reduce administrative workload, and help businesses avoid penalties arising from late or incorrect filings. TaxStreem also indicated that the platform could be deployed in other African markets facing similar challenges, offering a scalable solution for SMEs and larger enterprises across the continent.

By leveraging automation and data integration, the initiative seeks to make tax compliance more efficient, transparent, and manageable, particularly for small businesses that lack in-house accounting or compliance expertise.

The launch underscores the growing role of technology in supporting regulatory adherence and financial management for African businesses, helping MSMEs streamline operations and focus on growth.

For African MSMEs, the platform reduces the time, cost, and complexity of tax compliance, enabling small businesses to focus on growth and operations. By improving accuracy and timeliness in tax filings, MSMEs can avoid penalties, enhance financial planning, and operate more confidently in both local and regional markets.
Nigeria’s Inflation Eases to 15.06% in February 2026, Offering Relief to MSMEs
Falling Energy and Farm Produce Prices Help Moderate Nigeria’s Inflation in February 2026

Nigeria’s headline inflation eased slightly to 15.06% in February 2026, driven by lower farm produce prices and a decline in energy costs. While year-on-year inflation fell compared to February 2025, month-on-month price increases were observed in both urban and rural areas, with variations across states. The data highlights moderation in overall inflation but signals persistent pressures in specific food items and regions.

Nigeria’s headline inflation slowed slightly to 15.06% in February 2026 from 15.10% in January, the National Bureau of Statistics (NBS) reported. The moderation was largely driven by falling farm produce prices, which dropped to 3.7% from 5.10%, and a slowdown in energy costs to 0.26% from 3.13% over the same period.

The Consumer Price Index (CPI) rose to 130.0 in February, a 2.6-point increase from January. Month-on-month inflation accelerated to 2.01% from -2.88%, indicating faster price growth compared with the previous month. Year-on-year, inflation remained 11.21 percentage points lower than February 2025’s 26.27%, showing a significant moderation in price increases.

Food inflation eased on a year-on-year basis to 12.12% in February 2026 from 26.98% in February 2025, though month-on-month food prices rose by 4.69%, driven by staples such as beans, cassava tubers, yam flour, millet flour, crayfish, snails, cowpeas, and ogbono.

Urban inflation increased 2.55% month-on-month, while rural inflation rose 0.71%, reflecting regional variations in price trends.

State-level analysis showed the highest year-on-year all-items inflation in Kogi (23.57%), Benue (22.85%), and Anambra (22.09%), while Katsina (7.78%), Imo (11.66%), and Ebonyi (11.71%) recorded the lowest. For food inflation, Kogi (26.91%), Adamawa (23.12%), and Benue (21.89%) posted the highest year-on-year increases.

The easing of headline inflation provides some relief to micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs), which have been grappling with high input costs and shrinking margins. Lower energy and farm produce prices can help reduce transportation and production expenses for food vendors, retailers, agro-processors, and other small businesses that rely heavily on these inputs. However, rising month-on-month food prices in certain states signal ongoing cost pressures for localized markets.

Economists note that while overall inflation moderation is positive for MSMEs, businesses must remain vigilant, as volatility in specific food items and regional disparities could still affect cash flow and profitability.

The report underscores a general easing of inflationary pressures in Nigeria in February 2026, offering cautious optimism for MSMEs, while highlighting the need for targeted support in states where price pressures remain elevated.

This matters to African MSMEs because inflation directly affects their operating costs, including food, energy, and transportation expenses. Lower inflation in key sectors like farm produce and energy can reduce input costs, improve cash flow, and support business planning. Understanding inflation trends also helps MSMEs make informed pricing, budgeting, and investment decisions, enhancing their resilience and competitiveness in local and regional markets.
She Code Africa and HP Train Women in Digital Skills to Unlock MSME Growth
New Abuja programme equips women with practical tech skills to grow businesses and access digital opportunities.

She Code Africa, in partnership with HP, has launched a digital literacy programme to train up to 300 women in Abuja. The initiative focuses on equipping participants with practical digital skills to support entrepreneurship, remote work, and small business growth.

She Code Africa in collaboration with HP, has launched a digital literacy programme aimed at empowering women with essential technology skills to participate more effectively in Nigeria’s digital economy.

The Abuja-based initiative targets women aged 16–35, particularly those with limited exposure to digital tools, and is expected to support 200–300 participants through hands-on training.

 

Bridging the Digital Skills Gap

The programme addresses a major barrier limiting women’s participation in business and employment: lack of basic digital literacy.

Participants are trained in:

  • Basic computer and smartphone usage

  • Internet navigation and online research

  • Email and productivity tools

  • Social media for business growth

  • Introduction to artificial intelligence tools

This foundational knowledge is critical for enabling women to engage in digital entrepreneurship and modern work environments.

 

Supporting Small Business Growth

The initiative goes beyond technical skills by focusing on practical business applications.

Key benefits for participants:

  • Ability to market products and services online

  • Improved customer communication and visibility

  • Use of digital tools for business operations and efficiency

  • Creation of online presence and brand identity

For many informal businesses, these capabilities can significantly expand market reach and revenue potential.

 

Aligning with Nigeria’s Digital Economy Goals

The programme supports national efforts to accelerate digital inclusion.

  • Contributes to Nigeria’s goal of 95% digital literacy by 2030

  • Aligns with plans to train 30 million Nigerians in digital skills by 2027

  • Builds on previous training of 200 women in Ibadan (2025)

  • Part of a broader effort to close the digital gender gap

 

Expanding Opportunities Beyond Business

Beyond entrepreneurship, the programme opens pathways into:

  • Remote work and freelancing

Uber and Fido Launch Instant Loan Access for Drivers in Ghana
New fintech partnership unlocks quick, collateral-free credit to support gig workers and small business operators.

Uber has partnered with Fido to provide Ghanaian drivers with instant loans of up to GH₵10,000. The initiative offers fast, collateral-free access to working capital, helping drivers manage operational costs and improve income stability.

Uber has partnered with Ghana-based fintech Fido to offer instant loans of up to GH₵10,000 (~$920) to drivers, providing much-needed financial support in a high-cost operating environment.

The partnership enables eligible drivers to access quick, digital loans without collateral or complex paperwork, helping them manage daily business expenses more efficiently.

 

Fast, Flexible Credit for Everyday Business Needs

The loan offering is designed to address liquidity challenges faced by ride-hailing drivers.

  • No collateral required

  • Minimal documentation and fast approval

  • Increasing credit limits based on repayment history

Funds can be used for:

  • Vehicle repairs and maintenance

  • Fuel and daily operating costs

  • Business continuity during income gaps

Addressing Financial Pressure in the Gig Economy

  • Platform commissions impacting earnings

  • Limited ability to save or reinvest in vehicles

The partnership provides a buffer against unexpected costs, helping drivers maintain operations and stabilise income.

 

Beyond Loans: Access to Financial Services

Technology-Driven Lending for Underserved Entrepreneurs

Kenyan wealthtech firm expands beyond entry-level investing with a diversified global fund offering.

Kenyan wealthtech firm Ndovu Wealth has introduced a new multi-asset investment fund aimed at affluent, digitally savvy investors seeking diversified global portfolios.

MTN to Absorb 2,762 IHS Employees as $2.2bn Infrastructure Deal Advances
Telecom giant moves to strengthen infrastructure control while integrating skilled workforce across key African markets.

MTN Group will absorb 2,762 employees from IHS Towers as part of its $2.2 billion acquisition, marking a major shift toward owning telecom infrastructure. The move strengthens MTN’s operational control while preserving jobs and technical expertise across multiple markets.

MTN Group is set to absorb 2,762 employees from IHS Towers as it advances a $2.2 billion acquisition aimed at consolidating telecom infrastructure across Africa.

The deal reflects a broader industry shift, with telecom operators moving toward greater ownership and control of critical infrastructure rather than relying on third-party providers.

 

Workforce Integration and Employee Protection

As part of the transition, MTN has committed to maintaining workforce stability.

Key provisions include:

  • Job continuity for 2,762 employees across multiple markets

  • Guaranteed salaries, bonuses, and core benefits for at least 12 months

  • Alignment of compensation with pre-acquisition packages

  • Smooth integration into MTN’s operational structure

This approach is designed to maintain morale and ensure operational continuity during the transition.

 

Geographic and Talent Distribution

The incoming workforce spans several key markets:

  • Nigeria: 1,559 employees (largest share)

  • Côte d’Ivoire: 158

  • Cameroon: 145

  • South Africa: 123

  • Zambia: 108

  • Additional employees across other regions

Role breakdown:

  • Technical/engineering roles: 1,621 employees

  • Finance: 271

  • IT: 137

  • Human resources: 101

  • Additional support functions across legal and commercial teams

The workforce composition highlights the technical intensity of telecom infrastructure operations.

 

Strategic Shift Toward Infrastructure Ownership

The acquisition signals a major strategic pivot for MTN:

  • Reduced reliance on independent tower companies

  • Greater control over network quality and expansion

  • Improved ability to manage costs and operational efficiency

  • Consolidation of one of Africa’s largest tower portfolios

Upon completion, IHS Towers will become a fully owned subsidiary of MTN.

 

Financial and Operational Context

The deal comes amid evolving market dynamics:

  • IHS reported $166.4 million in staff costs (2025), up 9.2%

  • Nigeria generated $1.07 billion in revenue for IHS (+7% YoY)

  • Growth driven by colocation and new site deployments

  • Portfolio restructuring underway to reduce liabilities before acquisition

Labour relations across markets remain stable, with no major disruptions or strikes reported.

 

Implications for Africa’s Telecom Ecosystem

The transaction reflects broader trends across the sector:

  • Operators seeking greater vertical integration

  • Increasing demand for reliable, scalable digital infrastructure

  • Ongoing restructuring of relationships between telcos and tower providers

This could reshape how telecom infrastructure is financed, managed, and scaled across the continent.

Stronger telecom infrastructure improves connectivity, reliability, and digital access, critical for MSME growth. As networks become more efficient and scalable, small businesses benefit from better digital services, enabling e-commerce, fintech adoption, and broader market reach.

iHatch Cohort 5 focuses on strengthening local ecosystems to unlock startup growth beyond major cities.

Nigeria’s tech regulator, NITDA, is seeking 37 innovation hubs across all states to implement its iHatch incubation programme. The initiative aims to decentralise startup support, improve incubation quality, and expand access to funding opportunities nationwide.

The National Information Technology Development Agency (NITDA), through the Office for Nigerian Digital Innovation (ONDI), has opened applications for the fifth cohort of its iHatch Startup Incubation Programme, in partnership with the Japan International Cooperation Agency (JICA).

The programme is targeting 37 innovation hubs—one in each state and the FCT—to serve as local implementation partners, expanding structured startup support across Nigeria.

EasyBuy has introduced a 10,000-job initiative to onboard business developers across Nigeria, offering training, flexible work, and income opportunities. The programme aims to expand financial inclusion while supporting grassroots entrepreneurship.

Women Push into Nigeria’s Heavy Manufacturing Sector Amid Calls for Inclusive Reforms
Stakeholders Call for Policy Reforms to Break Barriers in Heavy Industry

Nigeria’s heavy manufacturing sector remains largely male-dominated despite women’s growing participation in the broader manufacturing industry. Stakeholders are calling for targeted reforms to address barriers such as limited access to finance, leadership gaps, and structural constraints. Expanding inclusion in heavy industry is seen as critical to driving industrial growth, innovation, and economic competitiveness.

As the world marked International Women’s Day (IWD) on March 8, stakeholders in Nigeria’s manufacturing sector highlighted the need to address persistent gender gaps in heavy industries such as steel, cement, oil servicing, shipbuilding, and large-scale construction.

While women play active roles across Nigeria’s manufacturing landscape, their participation remains largely concentrated in light manufacturing, with heavy industrial sectors still overwhelmingly male-dominated. Industry leaders are calling for deliberate policy reforms to accelerate women’s entry and advancement in these capital-intensive sectors.

Ngozi Oyewole, President of the Commonwealth Business Women Africa (CBW Africa), said that although women contribute significantly to the manufacturing ecosystem, they face major barriers when scaling businesses in heavy industry. She noted that while institutions such as the Bank of Industry have introduced gender-focused financing windows, access remains difficult due to stringent requirements.

“Many women in heavy industry still encounter challenges with collateral thresholds, equity contributions, and long processing timelines attached to large industrial loans,” she said, adding that financing structures often fail to reflect the realities of women entrepreneurs.

Oyewole also emphasised the importance of expanding access to government procurement opportunities, noting that inclusive policies could help women transition from small-scale production to industrial-scale manufacturing. She pointed to ongoing efforts by the Bureau of Public Procurement to develop frameworks that increase women’s participation in federal contracts.

Similarly, Funlayo Bakare-Okeowo, Vice President of the Lagos Chamber of Commerce and Industry (LCCI), said gender disparity in manufacturing leadership reflects deeper structural and cultural barriers. Although women account for about 40% of the manufacturing workforce, their representation in executive roles remains significantly lower.

She attributed this to a “leaky pipeline,” where women’s career progression stalls before reaching leadership levels, as well as a “broken rung” at entry-level management, limiting advancement opportunities. Bakare-Okeowo also noted that women are often concentrated in support functions rather than core operational roles such as engineering and production, which typically lead to leadership positions.

Ekama Akpan, Chief Executive Officer of Showers Group, highlighted additional challenges, including cultural stereotypes discouraging women from technical careers, limited access to industrial land, and workplace environments that are not designed for gender inclusion. She added that infrastructure deficits and unreliable power supply further increase operational costs, placing additional strain on smaller businesses.

Akpan called for gender-sensitive industrial policies, including improved access to financing through credit guarantees and equipment-based lending, as well as the development of dedicated industrial clusters with shared resources and stable electricity.

Stakeholders agree that expanding women’s participation in heavy manufacturing is critical to Nigeria’s industrialisation agenda. They note that inclusive industrial growth can enhance productivity, drive innovation, and strengthen economic competitiveness.

Increasing women’s participation in heavy manufacturing expands access to industrial opportunities, strengthens value chains, and drives inclusive growth. Addressing structural barriers can unlock new markets and improve competitiveness for women-led MSMEs across Africa.
Ghana’s Print Sector Expands as Retail Growth Drives Investment in Advanced Technologies
Rising retail expansion and advertising demand push print firms to adopt advanced technologies for faster, higher-quality, and more durable production.

Ghana’s printing industry is experiencing growth as expanding retail activity and advertising demand drive investment in advanced production technologies. Print firms are upgrading capabilities to deliver faster, high-quality, and durable solutions, positioning themselves to capture opportunities across retail, real estate, and corporate sectors.

Ghana’s visual communications and printing industry is entering a new phase of growth, driven by expanding retail activity, urban development, and rising demand for branding and advertising solutions.

The country’s retail market was estimated at approximately $32 billion in 2023 and is projected to reach $54 billion by 2031, reflecting strong commercial expansion and increasing consumer spending. Outdoor advertising, a key driver of large-format printing, generates around $60 million annually, accounting for more than 20% of total advertising expenditure.

As businesses compete for visibility across storefronts, malls, events, and public spaces, print service providers are investing in advanced production systems to improve turnaround times, expand capabilities, and deliver durable output suited to West Africa’s climate.

Accra-based Chroma Digital Solutions is among the companies leading this shift. The firm recently installed the Canon Colorado M5W wide-format printer, enhancing its ability to produce high-quality retail displays, interior décor, and outdoor signage. The installation marks one of the earliest deployments of the Colorado M5W technology in Africa.

Kwame Owusu-Kwarteng, Operations Manager at Chroma Digital Solutions, said the investment reflects changing client expectations.

He noted that businesses increasingly demand speed, consistency, and the ability to handle diverse print applications. According to
