him, the printer’s UVgel technology enables production of durable prints that withstand heat and sunlight, while features such as white ink, combined matte-and-gloss finishes, and texture printing allow for more advanced applications.

The system’s productivity and efficient ink usage also support cost management, which remains critical in Ghana’s price-sensitive market.

Canon Central & North Africa (CCNA) said Ghana’s evolving economy is prompting print providers to move beyond basic production towards value-added services that support branding, retail experiences, and architectural design.

Tushar Vashnavi, Business Unit Director, B2B, at CCNA, described Ghana as one of West Africa’s most dynamic visual communications markets. He said the expansion of retail, infrastructure, and advertising sectors is driving demand for technologies that improve productivity, enable service diversification, and enhance profitability.

The company added that reliable local technical support and strategic guidance remain essential for successful technology adoption and long-term growth in emerging markets.

For Chroma Digital Solutions, the investment in advanced production capabilities has created new business opportunities and improved service reliability. The company said access to dependable technical support allows it to take on more complex projects and deliver greater value to clients.

As Ghana’s economy continues to diversify, demand for high-quality visual communication is expected to rise across retail, real estate, events, and corporate sectors, positioning technologically advanced print providers to benefit from the industry’s growth.

Growing demand for branding and advertising creates new opportunities for MSMEs in the print and creative sectors. Investing in advanced technology can improve service delivery, expand offerings, and enhance competitiveness in fast-growing markets.
South Africa Inflation Hits Central Bank Target as Rand Strengthens
Easing fuel prices and a stronger rand drive inflation down to target, supporting consumer spending outlook.

South Africa’s inflation slowed to 3.0% in February, reaching the central bank’s target amid declining fuel prices and a stronger rand. While the outlook supports consumer spending and economic stability, policymakers are expected to remain cautious due to global uncertainties.

South Africa’s annual inflation slowed to 3.0% in February, reaching the central bank’s target and declining from 3.5% recorded in January, according to official data released on Wednesday.

The figure came in slightly below economists’ expectations of 3.1% in a Reuters poll, reflecting easing price pressures in the economy.

The moderation in inflation comes as the South African Reserve Bank previously held its key lending rate steady at 6.75% during its January monetary policy meeting.

Market analysts expect the central bank to maintain its current stance at its upcoming March 26 meeting, citing external risks, including geopolitical tensions linked to the U.S.-Israel conflict involving Iran, as factors influencing policy decisions.

The South African rand strengthened in early trading following the release of local economic data, rising about 0.3% to trade at 16.6250 against the U.S. dollar. Investors are also monitoring retail sales data to assess the broader economic outlook.

Data expectations indicate that retail sales likely increased by about 2.5% year-on-year in January, slightly below the 2.6% recorded in December. Economists at Nedbank projected a similar growth rate of 2.4%, noting that consumer spending remains supported by a more favourable inflation environment and improved financial conditions.

The decline in inflation was largely attributed to lower fuel prices. According to Nedbank, petrol prices fell by 3.2% during the month, supported by a stronger rand and stable Brent crude oil prices. This contributed to a significant year-on-year decline of more than 10% in fuel inflation, alongside base effects from the previous year.

In the bond market, South Africa’s benchmark 2035 government bond showed strength in early trading, with yields declining by 8.5 basis points to 8.71%, reflecting improved investor sentiment.

The latest inflation data suggests a stabilising economic environment, which could provide relief for households and businesses.

However, analysts note that global uncertainties and domestic economic pressures remain key factors that could influence monetary policy and growth prospects in the coming months.

Easing inflation and stable interest rates can improve consumer spending and reduce operating costs for MSMEs. However, external risks and modest retail growth mean businesses must remain cautious and adaptable in managing pricing, demand, and financial planning.
Senegal Meets Debt Obligations Amid Fiscal Strain and Unrest
Senegal honours nearly $500 million in debt amid spending cuts and rising domestic unrest.

Senegal avoided a debt default by paying nearly $500 million to bondholders, but spending cuts, delayed payments to other creditors, and rising domestic unrest raise concerns about long-term fiscal sustainability.

Senegal has narrowly avoided a potential debt default after meeting nearly half a billion dollars in external debt obligations, according to reports released on March 14. The development provides short-term relief for the country’s financial standing but has raised fresh concerns about its fiscal sustainability and long-term economic outlook.

The repayment included €380 million transferred by the Central Bank of West African States (BCEAO) to eurobond holders, alongside an additional $33 million paid to investors holding dollar-denominated bonds. These payments enabled Senegal to meet immediate obligations to international creditors and maintain its credibility in global financial markets.

The move comes at a time when several African economies are facing heightened debt pressures due to rising borrowing costs, currency fluctuations, and tighter global financial conditions. By honouring its debt commitments, Senegal has temporarily avoided the reputational and economic consequences of a sovereign default, which could have further restricted access to international capital markets.

However, analysts warn that the country’s approach to meeting these obligations raises concerns about sustainability. Reports indicate that the government has implemented spending cuts and delayed payments to other creditors in order to prioritise its external debt servicing. While this strategy has helped avert immediate default risks, it may create additional fiscal pressures in the near term.

The reliance on such measures is also taking place against a backdrop of growing domestic unrest, which is increasing pressure on public finances and complicating economic management. Rising social and political tensions could further strain government resources, particularly as authorities attempt to balance fiscal discipline with the need to address public concerns and maintain economic stability.

Economists note that continued prioritisation of external debt payments over domestic spending could limit the government’s ability to fund critical sectors such as infrastructure, healthcare, and social programmes. This could, in turn, slow economic growth and reduce the effectiveness of policies aimed at improving living standards and supporting business activity.

The situation in Senegal reflects broader challenges across Africa, where many governments are grappling with high debt servicing costs, limited fiscal space, and external economic shocks. Increasing global interest rates have made borrowing more expensive, while weaker currencies have raised the cost of servicing foreign-denominated debt. At the same time, domestic pressures, including inflation and unemployment, are adding to the complexity of economic management.

Financial experts have stressed the need for a balanced approach to debt management, urging governments to combine prudent fiscal policies with structural reforms that can drive economic growth. This includes improving revenue mobilisation, enhancing export capacity, and strengthening public financial management systems to ensure efficient use of resources.

In Senegal’s case, maintaining debt sustainability will likely require a combination of fiscal discipline, economic reforms, and continued engagement with international financial institutions. Ensuring transparency in debt management and adopting strategies that support long-term growth will be critical to reducing vulnerability to future shocks.

While the latest payments have helped Senegal avoid an immediate crisis, analysts caution that without sustained reforms and improved fiscal resilience, the risk of renewed debt pressures could persist.

Fiscal tightening and economic uncertainty can reduce government spending, delay payments, and weaken demand affecting MSMEs that rely on public contracts and local market activity. Stable public finances are critical for maintaining a supportive business environment and sustaining growth for small enterprises.
Libya Seeks French Support to Reinvest Frozen Sovereign Reserves
Libya engages France to reinvest frozen sovereign funds under UN guidelines to preserve capital and returns.

Libya is negotiating with France to reinvest its frozen sovereign reserves under UN guidelines, aiming to protect the funds from erosion while generating safe returns to support long-term national interests.

Libya is in discussions with France to reinvest its frozen sovereign cash reserves, aiming to prevent further erosion of state assets held abroad under UN sanctions, according to reports from March 12.

The talks focus on applying UN Security Council Resolution 2769, which permits the Libyan Investment Authority (LIA) to place frozen funds in low-risk time deposits. The goal is to preserve capital while securing returns aligned with international market rates.

LIA Chairman and CEO Ali Mahmoud Hassan met with Libya’s ambassador to France, alongside officials from the French foreign and treasury ministries, to explore mechanisms for operationalizing the reinvestment safely. The discussions form part of a broader diplomatic effort, with similar meetings held recently with the UK, Russia, and China to reconcile sanctions compliance with asset preservation.

Libya’s sovereign reserves have been frozen since the 2011 sanctions regime, intended to protect national wealth. However, officials argue that leaving large cash balances idle exposes the funds to erosion over time, undermining their purpose. Reinvesting the assets within the UN framework is seen as a strategy to safeguard Libya’s long-term financial interests.

For Libya, the issue extends beyond technicalities—it is about ensuring that sanctioned but substantial sovereign funds can be preserved, generate returns, and eventually support public welfare, all while adhering to international restrictions.

Securing returns on frozen sovereign assets strengthens national fiscal stability, which can indirectly support local markets, public investments, and a conducive environment for MSMEs. Efficient management of state funds ensures governments can fund infrastructure, services, and economic programmes critical for business growth.
Nigeria’s Capital Market Gains Global Attention Amid Policy Clarity and Market Performance
Global Investor Interest in Nigeria’s Capital Market Signals New Opportunities for MSMEs

Nigeria’s capital market is experiencing a re-rating as global investors reassess the country’s economic prospects, driven by improved market performance, policy clarity, and energy sector reforms. This renewed confidence could increase access to funding and investment opportunities for Nigerian and African MSMEs, supporting growth, expansion, and integration into broader markets.

Nigeria’s capital market is experiencing a re-rating as global investors reassess the country’s economic trajectory and investment potential, the Group Managing Director and CEO of Nigerian Exchange Group (NGX Group), Temi Popoola, said on Monday.

Speaking during a live interview in London amid President Bola Tinubu’s state visit to the United Kingdom, Popoola noted that recent strong market performance and greater policy clarity are reshaping how international investors view Nigeria.

“What we are seeing is a gradual re-rating of Nigeria. Investors are beginning to look at the data more closely, the returns, the reforms, and the improving macroeconomic direction, and that is changing sentiment,” he said.

Popoola explained that Nigeria’s equity market has delivered solid returns in recent months, positioning it more competitively among emerging and frontier markets. The performance, coupled with reforms in key sectors, is helping to recalibrate long-held risk perceptions and attract renewed international interest.

He highlighted progress in Nigeria’s energy sector, including increased domestic refining capacity and ongoing sector reforms, as a factor improving investor confidence. According to Popoola, these developments reduce the economy’s exposure to external oil price shocks and strengthen the country’s investment appeal.

“Global capital responds to clarity and consistency. As those elements become more evident, Nigeria naturally becomes more investable,” he said, stressing that sustained engagement with global financial centres, particularly London, remains essential in connecting the Nigerian market with international investors.

Economists note that a re-rated capital market can have direct implications for micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs). Increased investor confidence in Nigeria encourages foreign portfolio inflows, which can expand financing opportunities for small businesses. Access to equity and debt markets provides MSMEs with alternative funding channels to support growth, modernize operations, and scale across domestic and regional markets.

Improved macroeconomic and sectoral stability also helps reduce input costs and operating risks for MSMEs. For example, reforms in the energy sector could lower production and logistics costs for manufacturing, retail, and agro-processing businesses, strengthening profitability and sustainability.

Popoola’s remarks signal a broader shift in investor sentiment toward Nigeria, underscoring the importance of policy consistency, regulatory transparency, and sectoral reform. For Nigerian and African MSMEs, the re-rating of the capital market represents both an opportunity and a signal to prepare for potential expansion, as greater liquidity and investor engagement may support broader economic growth.

As Nigeria continues to strengthen its capital market and connect with global investors, MSMEs stand to benefit from enhanced access to finance, potential partnerships, and a more predictable business environment, enabling them to compete more effectively within the African and international markets.

Rising global investor interest in Nigeria enhances funding opportunities, improves market confidence, and creates potential partnerships for African MSMEs. Greater capital inflows can help small businesses access financing, scale operations, and integrate into regional and international value chains.
NNPC Ramps Up Crude Supply to Dangote Refinery as Global Oil Price Surge Drives Fuel Costs Higher
Fuel price pressures mount as crude shortages and global tensions strain Nigeria’s energy market

NNPC has increased crude supply efforts to the Dangote refinery as global oil prices surge, aiming to stabilise fuel availability in Nigeria. However, supply shortfalls and reliance on imported crude continue to drive petrol prices higher, intensifying cost pressures on businesses, especially MSMEs.

The Nigerian National Petroleum Company Limited (NNPC) has intensified efforts to boost crude oil supply to the Dangote Petroleum Refinery in a bid to stabilise fuel availability across the country, as global oil prices surge above $108 per barrel. The move comes amid heightened volatility in the international oil market driven by geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, which have disrupted supply flows and increased pressure on domestic fuel prices.

Speaking during a webinar hosted by the Major Energies Marketers Association of Nigeria, the Managing Director of NNPC Retail Limited, Hubb Stokman, stated that the national oil company remains committed to its role as a supplier of last resort, ensuring continuity and stability in petroleum product supply nationwide. He explained that NNPC is working closely with the Nigerian Midstream and Downstream Petroleum Regulatory Authority and other stakeholders to maintain an uninterrupted supply chain for both crude oil and refined products.

Stokman noted that the company is leveraging existing supply channels, including domestic production and imports, in line with the Petroleum Industry Act, to guarantee adequate product availability. He added that NNPC’s collaboration with relevant agencies is critical to sustaining market stability, particularly during periods of global uncertainty.

Despite these efforts, the Dangote refinery has raised concerns over insufficient access to locally sourced crude oil. The company disclosed that it currently receives about five cargoes per month from NNPC, significantly below the 13 cargoes required to sustain its operations. As a result, the refinery has increasingly relied on imported crude, which is priced at international market rates with additional premiums, further increasing production costs.

NNPC officials, however, confirmed that the company is sourcing third-party crude through its global trading network to support the 650,000-barrel-per-day Lekki-based refinery. According to sources, the crude is being supplied at prices competitive with prevailing international rates, dismissing calls for the Federal Government to provide crude at discounted local prices.

Industry sources also indicated that part of the supply shortfall is linked to previously front-sold crude volumes, which have temporarily constrained NNPC’s ability to meet domestic demand. Nonetheless, the company reaffirmed its commitment to supporting local refining and ensuring energy security through alternative sourcing strategies.

Meanwhile, global oil prices have continued to rise sharply, with Brent crude trading above $108 per barrel and U.S. West Texas Intermediate at about $98.50, widening the price gap between both benchmarks. The increase reflects growing concerns over disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz, a critical route for global oil shipments, where ongoing geopolitical tensions have heightened risks to supply.

The surge in crude prices and limited local supply has triggered multiple increases in petrol prices in Nigeria, with pump prices exceeding ₦1,200 per litre in recent days. The development has raised concerns among businesses, particularly micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs), which rely heavily on fuel for operations, logistics, and power generation.

For Nigerian and African MSMEs, the rising cost of fuel is expected to increase operating expenses, reduce profit margins, and potentially lead to higher prices for goods and services. Businesses dependent on transportation, manufacturing, and energy-intensive activities are likely to face the most significant impact, as fuel price volatility continues to shape the broader economic environment.

Rising fuel prices driven by global oil market volatility and local crude supply challenges are increasing operating costs for MSMEs across Africa. Higher expenses in transportation, production, and power supply are squeezing profit margins and may force businesses to raise prices, reduce output, or delay expansion. In the short term, continued instability in fuel supply and pricing could weaken business resilience and slow growth across key sectors.
Nigeria’s Inflation Dips to 15.06% in February, LCCI Sees Cautious Relief for Businesses
Marginal decline offers relief, but rising costs and global uncertainties continue to threaten business stability

Nigeria’s inflation rate eased slightly in February 2026, offering cautious optimism to businesses. However, persistent food inflation, rising monthly price pressures, and global economic risks continue to threaten stability.

Nigeria’s headline inflation rate eased slightly to 15.06 per cent in February 2026 from 15.10 per cent in January, the Lagos Chamber of Commerce and Industry (LCCI) has said. The modest decline follows a significant drop from 26.27 per cent recorded in February 2025, signalling a gradual easing of inflationary pressures in the economy.

LCCI Director General, Dr. Chinyere Almona, said the development offers cautious optimism for businesses and households that have faced high costs and weakened purchasing power over the past year. “From the perspective of the organised private sector, the slight moderation in inflation offers cautious optimism for businesses and households, as high inflation has significantly eroded purchasing power, increased production costs, and weakened consumer demand across several sectors,” she said.

Despite the headline moderation, the Chamber noted that inflationary pressures persist. Month-on-month inflation rose to 2.01 per cent in February after contracting in January, indicating ongoing price pressures. Food inflation remained the primary driver of overall price increases, reflecting structural inefficiencies in the supply chain, high logistics costs, and production constraints.

LCCI also highlighted that a mix of domestic and global risks could reverse the recent gains. Rising geopolitical tensions linked to the Iran conflict in the Middle East were cited as a potential trigger for volatility in global energy markets, which could push up fuel, transportation, and logistics costs. The Chamber further warned that exchange-rate fluctuations could increase the cost of imported raw materials, machinery, pharmaceuticals, and food items.

To mitigate such risks, LCCI urged the government to expand local refining capacity and increase crude supply to domestic refineries, which could cushion businesses from global price shocks.

The modest inflation decline is expected to provide some relief to micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs) across Nigeria and Africa, although cost pressures remain significant. Rising prices for food, transportation, and imported goods continue to affect operating costs, profitability, and pricing strategies for small businesses. Sectors heavily reliant on imported inputs or energy-intensive operations are likely to face ongoing challenges.

Economists and industry stakeholders noted that while the slight drop in inflation is positive, MSMEs will need to manage operational costs carefully and adjust to fluctuating consumer demand as the economy navigates both domestic and international pressures.

Although inflation is easing, MSMEs still face high operating costs and weak consumer demand. Ongoing risks from fuel prices and exchange-rate volatility could limit business recovery and slow expansion across key sectors.
NAGAFF Rebrands Committee to Promote Collaboration and Boost Trade
Trade Advocacy Committee to drive dialogue, port efficiency, and smoother business operations
NAGAFF has rebranded its Trade War Advocacy Committee as the Trade Advocacy Committee to foster collaboration, dialogue, and improved port productivity in Nigeria’s maritime sector.

The National Association of Government Approved Freight Forwarders (NAGAFF) has rebranded its Trade War Advocacy Committee as the Trade Advocacy Committee, signalling a shift toward a more collaborative and solution-focused approach in Nigeria’s maritime sector.

In a press statement on Tuesday, NAGAFF Founder Boniface Aniebonam said the change responds to calls from freight forwarders for a more inclusive and less confrontational framework to address industry challenges. “The newly renamed committee will operate from the association’s headquarters under the Office of the Founder, with a renewed mandate to promote dialogue, industry harmony, and enhanced port productivity,” he said.

Aniebonam emphasized that the committee is not positioned against any stakeholder or institution. Instead, it is “designed to complement government efforts in strengthening Nigeria’s trade ecosystem in line with the Renewed Hope agenda.”

He noted that the rebranding represents a strategic recalibration aimed at fostering cooperation among maritime stakeholders, reducing friction within ports, and advancing a more progressive framework for trade facilitation in Nigeria.

The development is particularly significant for Nigerian and African micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs), which rely on efficient port operations and streamlined freight processes to manage costs, meet delivery timelines, and access international markets.

By promoting dialogue and industry cooperation, the committee’s renewed focus could help ease bottlenecks, improve supply chain reliability, and support smoother trade flows that benefit local businesses.

Efficient and cooperative port operations directly affect MSMEs that depend on imports and exports. Reduced port friction and better trade facilitation can lower costs, shorten delivery times, and enable businesses to compete more effectively in local and international markets.
BII, Deutsche Bank Launch $150m Trade Finance Initiative to Support African SMEs
New risk-sharing initiative targets funding gaps and supports cross-border trade in underserved markets

BII and Deutsche Bank have launched a $150 million trade finance facility to support African SMEs and close the continent’s financing gap. The initiative will improve access to credit and strengthen cross-border trade.

British International Investment and Deutsche Bank have launched a $150 million trade finance facility aimed at improving access to funding for African businesses and addressing the continent’s estimated $100 billion annual trade finance gap.

The initiative, announced on March 16, is designed to support small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) operating in high-risk and underserved markets across Africa, where access to trade finance remains constrained by liquidity challenges and perceived investment risks.

Structured as a risk-sharing facility, the programme will enable local financial institutions to expand their lending capacity and provide more credit to SMEs engaged in cross-border trade. The arrangement is expected to reduce the financial risks associated with international transactions and unlock capital flows across the continent.

Speaking on the partnership, the Global Head of Trade Finance for Financial Institutions at Deutsche Bank, Anand Jha, said the collaboration strengthens the bank’s capacity to facilitate sustainable trade growth in Africa.

The programme will prioritise frontier and least-developed economies, including Zambia, Ethiopia, and Rwanda, where access to trade finance is particularly limited.

Under the facility, local banks will receive enhanced support to finance trade transactions, enabling businesses to import critical goods such as raw materials, industrial equipment, and machinery required for production and expansion.


Also speaking, Managing Director and Head of Financial Services, Debt and Trade Finance at BII, Ndaba Mpofu, emphasised the importance of expanding access to trade finance across the continent.

He added that the initiative would contribute to building a more robust financial ecosystem while strengthening the ability of SMEs to participate in regional and global trade.

Industry stakeholders note that many African banks face persistent challenges in securing guarantees and accessing sufficient capital for trade financing, limiting their ability to support businesses, particularly SMEs.

The new facility is expected to ease these constraints by improving liquidity and enabling financial institutions to extend more short-term credit facilities to businesses involved in import and export activities.

According to the African Export-Import Bank, Africa’s trade finance gap remains a major barrier to economic growth, especially for SMEs that depend on access to credit to scale operations and compete internationally.

The development comes amid growing intra-African trade, which reached $208 billion in 2024, with projections indicating further expansion as access to finance improves and regional trade agreements gain traction.

Analysts say the BII–Deutsche Bank partnership could play a critical role in strengthening supply chains, boosting industrialisation, and enhancing the participation of African businesses in global markets.

By expanding trade finance capacity and supporting local banks, the initiative is expected to improve the flow of goods across borders, reduce transaction bottlenecks, and position African businesses to take advantage of emerging opportunities under regional trade frameworks.

Stakeholders further noted that sustained collaboration between development finance institutions and global banks will be key to closing the continent’s financing gap and supporting long-term economic transformation.

Limited access to trade finance has long restricted MSME growth in Africa. This facility is expected to ease funding constraints, improve cash flow, and enable small businesses to expand, import essential inputs, and compete more effectively in regional and global markets.
FG Promises Improved Power Supply as Gas Constraints Ease
Persistent Outages and Rising Energy Costs Continue to Pressure African MSMEs

Nigeria’s Minister of Power, Adebayo Adelabu, says electricity supply is expected to improve as the government addresses gas shortages that have reduced power generation. The crisis, worsened by a ₦3.3 trillion debt to gas suppliers, has led to widespread outages and increased costs for businesses.

Nigeria’s Minister of Power, Adebayo Adelabu, has said Nigerians will soon experience improved electricity supply as the Federal Government moves to resolve ongoing gas constraints affecting power generation across the country.

Adelabu made this known in his Eid-el-Fitr message, stating that recent government interventions are beginning to address long-standing gas supply challenges that have limited electricity generation. He noted that concrete measures are being implemented to ensure more reliable and sustainable power for homes, businesses, and industries.

The minister added that reforms initiated by President Bola Tinubu are gradually taking effect, expressing optimism that Nigerians will soon witness the benefits of these efforts. He stressed that resolving gas supply issues remains central to stabilising the power sector and improving overall electricity distribution nationwide.

The development follows recent disruptions in the sector after gas suppliers halted supply to thermal power plants over an estimated ₦3.3 trillion debt owed by power generation companies. The situation has worsened electricity shortages, leaving many parts of the country in prolonged blackout conditions.

The Chief Executive Officer of the Association of Power Generation Companies, Joy Ogaji, had earlier warned that the mounting debt across the power value chain could push the sector into a deeper crisis if not urgently addressed. The financial strain has significantly affected the ability of generation companies to sustain operations and meet electricity demand.

Data from the Nigerian Independent System Operator indicates that power generation dropped below 4,000 megawatts in recent weeks, largely due to insufficient gas supply to thermal plants. The data shows that while thermal plants require approximately 1,629.75 million standard cubic feet of gas per day to operate at optimal capacity, actual supply stood at about 692.00 mmscf per day as of February 23, 2026—less than 43 per cent of required volume.

The shortfall has made it difficult for distribution companies to provide stable electricity across their networks, resulting in widespread outages affecting households and businesses alike. Power generation companies have repeatedly appealed to consumers for patience, noting that the gas supply constraints are largely beyond their control.

For African micro, small, and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs), particularly in Nigeria, the ongoing electricity challenges continue to have significant operational implications. Many businesses rely heavily on alternative power sources such as diesel and petrol generators, leading to increased production costs and reduced profit margins. The inconsistent power supply has also disrupted business operations, lowered productivity, and limited the ability of enterprises to scale efficiently.

Adelabu, however, expressed confidence that ongoing reforms and government interventions will begin to yield noticeable improvements in electricity supply in the near term. He noted that the government’s broader reform agenda is designed to improve efficiency across the entire electricity value chain, from generation to distribution.

He also highlighted that recent international engagements by the presidency, including meetings in the United Kingdom, are expected to attract investment into critical sectors such as power, which could further strengthen infrastructure and service delivery.

Despite the current challenges, the minister urged Nigerians to remain patient, describing the situation as part of a transition towards a more reliable and sustainable power system. He emphasised that the difficulties being experienced are temporary and necessary for long-term sector stability.

As efforts continue to address gas supply constraints and financial bottlenecks, stakeholders across the power sector remain cautiously optimistic that improved electricity supply will materialise, providing much-needed relief to households and businesses, including MSMEs that depend on stable power to operate and grow.

Unreliable electricity remains one of the biggest constraints to business growth across Africa, particularly in Nigeria. While the Federal Government’s efforts, led by Adebayo Adelabu under the broader reforms of Bola Tinubu, signal potential improvement, the current situation continues to strain MSMEs.
Ghana Signs MoU with Turkish Firms for Eastern Accra Water Supply Project
New agreement targets growing water demand as urban expansion pressures infrastructure
Ghana has signed an MoU with Turkish firms to develop a major water supply project in eastern Accra, aimed at addressing growing demand and improving infrastructure.

The Government of Ghana has signed a tripartite memorandum of understanding (MoU) with Turkish infrastructure firms Hacıoğlu Holding and Koçak Çevre to advance a major drinking water project in the eastern part of the Greater Accra Region. The agreement marks the beginning of the project’s technical planning phase, aimed at improving water supply in one of Accra’s fastest-growing areas.

Local reports indicate that the project is designed to strengthen potable water access as rapid population growth and urban expansion continue to strain existing infrastructure. Under the MoU, the Turkish firms, led by Yasin Hacıoğlu, Chief Executive Officer of Hacıoğlu Holding, will undertake preliminary assessments and detailed engineering for a proposed treatment plant with a capacity of approximately 300,000 cubic metres of treated water per day.

Ghana’s Minister for Works, Housing and Water Resources, Kenneth Gilbert Adjei, said the initiative is intended to address rising demand for water in the capital’s eastern corridor. He noted that the project remains at a development stage and has not yet reached final contract award.

According to the agreement, Hacıoğlu Holding and Koçak Çevre will be responsible for preparing key project documentation, including feasibility studies, environmental and social impact assessments, as well as technical and financial proposals. Ghana Water Limited, under the supervision of the ministry, is expected to support the process by providing site access, technical data, and facilitating financing arrangements.

The project document identifies the Lower Volta River as the proposed raw water source, with a preferred site downstream of Kpong, near Asutsuare. It also outlines plans for a 90-kilometre transmission pipeline to distribute treated water across the target region.

Officials emphasised that any binding agreement involving foreign participation or financial commitments will be subject to Ghana’s statutory requirements and parliamentary approval. The MoU is valid for two years from the date of signing unless replaced earlier by a formal contract for the project.

Yasin Hacıoğlu stated that the project is being structured under a build-operate-transfer model, which would allow the developers to construct and operate the facility for a defined period before transferring ownership to the government. He added that the initiative reflects a broader commitment to sustainable development and responsible investment in Africa.

The development is expected to have economic implications for businesses, particularly micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs) operating in Accra and surrounding areas. Improved access to reliable water supply could reduce operational disruptions, lower production costs for water-dependent businesses, and support expansion in sectors such as manufacturing, food processing, and hospitality.

As Ghana continues to invest in infrastructure to support urban growth, projects such as the eastern Accra water scheme are seen as critical to improving living standards and enabling business activity in rapidly developing regions.

Reliable water supply is essential for business operations. Improved infrastructure can reduce costs, enhance productivity, and support the growth of MSMEs, particularly in urban and industrial sectors.
Bank of Africa Launches ‘Invest in Morocco’ Initiative to Attract Diaspora Investment
Webinar series targets global investors and Moroccan diaspora to drive growth in key sectors
Bank of Africa has launched the “Invest in Morocco” webinar series to attract diaspora and international investment into key sectors of the Moroccan economy.

Bank of Africa has launched a new initiative titled “Invest in Morocco” aimed at attracting investment from Moroccans living abroad and international investors. The programme, introduced in partnership with Morocco’s 40 Under 40, will be delivered through a series of webinars designed to provide guidance on investment opportunities and processes within the country.

According to reports published on March 18, the initiative seeks to support Morocco’s economic development strategy by promoting investment in priority sectors such as industry, renewable energy, and infrastructure. The webinar series will offer detailed insights into financing options, administrative requirements, and procedures for establishing and operating businesses in Morocco.

Bank of Africa stated that the sessions will be conducted online to ensure accessibility for Moroccan professionals and entrepreneurs in the diaspora, as well as global investors exploring opportunities in the North African market. The programme will include presentations on sector-specific prospects and provide an overview of the country’s investment environment.

The bank noted that the initiative is part of its broader strategy to strengthen economic development and improve investment inflows. It added that the platform is designed to simplify entry into the Moroccan market by addressing common challenges faced by investors, including administrative processes and limited access to local networks.

The programme also aims to strengthen engagement between Moroccan institutions and professionals based abroad by providing a structured channel for communication and collaboration. Officials said the initiative will make it easier for diaspora investors to launch and manage projects in Morocco.

Morocco has in recent years prioritised investment in sectors such as renewable energy, infrastructure, aerospace, and defence as part of efforts to diversify its economy and attract foreign capital. The “Invest in Morocco” initiative is expected to build on this momentum by increasing awareness of opportunities and streamlining the investment process for interested stakeholders.

Bank of Africa, which operates across several African and international markets, said the initiative reflects growing investor interest in Morocco and is expected to contribute to increased capital inflows into the country.

The development is expected to have implications for micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs) across Morocco and the wider African market. Increased investment flows could improve access to financing, create new business opportunities, and strengthen supply chains in key sectors.

For African MSMEs, the initiative highlights the growing role of diaspora capital in driving economic growth and supporting business expansion. By improving access to information and reducing entry barriers, the programme could enable more entrepreneurs to explore cross-border opportunities and participate in emerging markets.
Improved access to investment opportunities and financing can help MSMEs expand operations, enter new markets, and build stronger business networks across Africa and beyond.
African Cocoa Farmers Push for Unified Pricing Model Amid Falling Incomes
Declining Prices and Ineffective Policies Raise Concerns for MSMEs in the Cocoa Value Chain

Adeola Adegoke has called for a unified cocoa pricing model across Africa, warning that current systems are failing to protect farmers from falling prices and market volatility. Despite Africa producing the majority of the world’s cocoa, farmers continue to earn a disproportionately small share of the global chocolate market.

The President of the Cocoa Farmers Alliance Association of Africa (COFAAA), Adeola Adegoke, has called for a harmonised cocoa pricing model across African markets, warning that existing frameworks are failing to deliver meaningful benefits to farmers despite their original intent.

In an open letter addressed to cocoa farmers across the continent, Adegoke said producers are facing declining incomes and growing market uncertainty, even after the global cocoa price rally recorded in 2024. He noted that the recent downturn in prices has affected all cocoa-producing countries in Africa, eroding the gains made during the boom period.

He explained that while governments introduced regulated and semi-regulated pricing systems to protect farmers from volatility, these mechanisms have not achieved their intended outcomes. According to him, prices in many countries continue to adjust rapidly in response to international market fluctuations, undermining efforts to provide stability for farmers.

Adegoke expressed concern that the inability of current pricing models to cushion farmers has weakened confidence in the system and called for a continent-wide reassessment of cocoa pricing structures. He added that the situation highlights a broader disconnect between the global chocolate industry and the welfare of African producers.

Despite Africa accounting for about 70 per cent of global cocoa production, farmers on the continent earn less than six per cent of the estimated $147bn global chocolate market. Adegoke noted that many cocoa farmers continue to face structural challenges, including low incomes, limited access to education, poor infrastructure, inadequate healthcare, and security threats such as illegal mining.

He also questioned the effectiveness of interventions such as the Living Income Differential (LID) introduced by Ghana and Côte d’Ivoire, stating that its impact has not been as strong as expected, particularly in the face of declining global prices. However, he acknowledged the initiative as a positive step, while calling for greater clarity on its implementation and long-term sustainability from institutions such as the Conseil du Café-Cacao and Ghana COCOBOD.

To address these challenges, COFAAA has inaugurated a Global Members Assembly and Empowerment Forum aimed at developing a unified African position on cocoa pricing and farmer welfare. The forum will assess current market developments and propose

actionable solutions to improve income stability for farmers across producing countries.

Adegoke disclosed that the initiative would include participation from key cocoa-producing nations such as Côte d’Ivoire, Ghana, Cameroon, Nigeria, Uganda, and Sierra Leone, with about 20 farmers representing each country alongside expert-led subcommittees.

He added that the forum will also explore support mechanisms, including access to inputs and safety nets to help farmers cope with falling prices.

Public discussions under the initiative are scheduled to commence on March 21, 2026, through a virtual session designed to encourage broader stakeholder engagement across the continent.

For African MSMEs operating within the cocoa value chain including smallholder farmers, processors, exporters, and agribusinesses the ongoing pricing challenges present significant risks. Declining farmgate prices reduce incomes at the production level, which in turn affects supply stability, investment capacity, and overall value chain performance.

The push for a harmonised pricing model reflects growing calls for structural reforms that ensure fair value distribution, improve income security, and strengthen the long-term sustainability of Africa’s cocoa industry.
The concerns raised by Adeola Adegoke highlight deep structural challenges within Africa’s cocoa value chain that directly impact MSMEs across production, processing, and export.
CBN Survey: Rising Loan Rates Spark Credit Cost Concerns
Borrowing costs increase as households express financial strain despite macroeconomic optimism

The CBN survey shows that 42.7 per cent of Nigerians perceive rising loan interest rates, while most respondents prefer lower rates. Despite optimism about the economy, high borrowing costs and persistent price pressures continue to affect households and businesses.

A recent survey by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) has revealed that a growing number of Nigerians perceive an increase in interest rates on bank loans, highlighting concerns over the rising cost of credit in the economy. According to the apex bank’s Consumer Expectation Survey for February 2026, 42.7 per cent of respondents said interest rates on loans had increased over the past three months.

The survey findings reflect mounting pressure on borrowers, particularly as access to affordable financing remains a critical factor for economic activity. A majority of respondents, representing 63 per cent, indicated a preference for lower interest rates, underscoring widespread demand for more accessible and affordable lending conditions across the country.

The report also provides insight into broader consumer sentiment, showing a mixed outlook on the Nigerian economy. The overall Consumer Sentiment Index stood at 0.8 points in February 2026, marking the fourth consecutive month of positive sentiment, although it declined from 2.8 points recorded in January. This suggests that while Nigerians remain cautiously optimistic about the general economic environment, confidence has weakened slightly.

Further analysis shows that optimism is largely driven by perceptions of macroeconomic conditions. The Economic Condition Index rose to 7.2 points, indicating that respondents believe the broader economy is showing signs of improvement. However, this optimism does not extend to personal financial situations. The Family Financial Situation Index dropped to -9.1 points, reflecting widespread pessimism about household finances.

The survey also revealed that the Family Income Sentiment Index stood at 4.3 points, suggesting that some respondents expect improvements in income levels. However, this positive outlook appears insufficient to offset the impact of rising costs and borrowing expenses.

Price pressures remain a significant concern for consumers. The Consumer Sentiment Index on price changes fell sharply to -9.9 points in February from 4.2 points in January, indicating that many respondents perceive prices as high. In addition, the index measuring the average prices of selected goods increased to 23.7 points from 22.6 points, reinforcing the view that the cost of living remains elevated.

For micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs), the perception of rising interest rates presents a major challenge. Many small businesses rely on bank loans and credit facilities to finance daily operations, purchase inventory, and expand production. Higher lending rates increase the cost of borrowing, making it more difficult for businesses to access capital or sustain growth.

In addition, persistent inflation and high prices for goods and services continue to raise operating costs for MSMEs. Expenses related to raw materials, transportation, and energy remain elevated, putting pressure on profit margins. Businesses may be forced to pass on these costs to consumers through higher prices, which could reduce demand, particularly in a market where household purchasing power is already weakened.

The combination of high borrowing costs and weak consumer demand creates a challenging operating environment for MSMEs. Businesses that depend on external financing may delay expansion plans or scale down operations, while those without access to credit may struggle to maintain cash flow.

Economic analysts note that improving access to affordable credit will be critical to supporting MSME growth and sustaining economic recovery. Policies aimed at stabilising interest rates, controlling inflation, and strengthening financial inclusion could help ease the burden on small businesses and encourage investment.

The CBN survey highlights the disconnect between improving macroeconomic indicators and the financial realities faced by households and businesses. While overall sentiment remains positive, rising interest rates and persistent price pressures continue to shape the economic landscape, with significant implications for Nigeria’s MSME sector.

Higher interest rates limit access to credit and increase financing costs for MSMEs. Combined with weak consumer purchasing power and rising operating expenses, this could slow business growth, reduce profitability, and constrain expansion across African markets.
Nigeria’s Cooking Gas Supply Drops 8.5% as Consumption Declines Amid Price Surge
Rising global oil prices and supply disruptions push LPG costs higher across local markets
Nigeria’s LPG supply fell by 8.5 per cent in February 2026, while consumption dropped by 20 per cent as rising global oil prices pushed cooking gas costs higher.

The supply of Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG), commonly known as cooking gas, declined by 8.5 per cent on a month-on-month basis to 4.7 metric tonnes per day in February 2026, down from 5.1 metric tonnes recorded in December 2025. The data was contained in the Nigerian Midstream and Downstream Petroleum Regulatory Authority (NMDPRA) Factsheet report for February 2026 released on Wednesday.

The report also showed a sharp drop in consumption, with daily usage falling by 20 per cent to 4,194 metric tonnes in February 2026 from 5,050 metric tonnes recorded in January 2026. The decline reflects reduced demand as rising prices continue to affect household and business consumption patterns.

Market checks indicate that the drop in supply and consumption is linked to recent tensions in the Middle East, which have driven up global crude oil prices to about $84 per barrel. The disruption in international oil production and supply has led to increased costs of petroleum products, including cooking gas, across Nigeria.

A survey of gas retail outlets in Lagos shows that the price of LPG has risen significantly, with a kilogram now selling between ₦1,050 and ₦1,300 in many locations, while some outlets offer it at higher rates of between ₦1,400 and ₦1,500 per kilogram. The NMDPRA report confirmed that the retail price range for LPG in February stood between ₦980 and ₦1,500 per kilogram, compared to a range of ₦950 to ₦1,550 recorded in January 2026.

In addition to LPG supply trends, the report highlighted a slight decline in overall gas supply metrics. Total wholesale gas supply dropped to 4.771 billion standard cubic feet (BSCF) per day in February 2026 from 4.837 BSCF per day in January. Similarly, the average daily gas supply to the domestic market fell to 1.763 BSCF per day, compared to 1.906 BSCF per day in the previous month.

Industry observers note that the decline in supply and rising prices could continue if global oil market disruptions persist. The Middle East crisis has heightened volatility in energy markets, affecting supply chains and increasing costs for importing and distributing gas products.

The development has direct implications for micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs) across Nigeria and the wider African region. Businesses that rely on LPG for operations, including food vendors, restaurants, caterers, and small-scale manufacturers, are facing higher energy costs, which may affect profitability and pricing.

Rising cooking gas prices are also expected to influence consumer behaviour, as households and businesses adjust consumption levels or switch to alternative energy sources. This shift could affect demand patterns and create additional uncertainty for businesses operating in energy-dependent sectors.

Analysts note that sustained increases in LPG prices may contribute to broader inflationary pressures, particularly in sectors linked to food production and services. As energy costs rise, MSMEs may be forced to pass on additional costs to consumers, potentially reducing demand and slowing business growth.

Higher LPG prices increase operating costs for MSMEs, especially in food and manufacturing sectors. Reduced consumption and rising energy expenses could affect profitability, pricing, and overall business sustainability.
MEMAN Explains Slow Drop in Petrol Prices Amid Market Volatility
Marketers cite cost recovery, inventory levels, and global oil shocks as key drivers of pricing trends
MEMAN says petrol prices rise faster than they fall due to cost recovery, inventory constraints, and global oil market volatility.

The Major Energy Marketers Association of Nigeria (MEMAN) has explained why petrol prices in Nigeria tend to rise quickly but decline more slowly, attributing the pattern to cost recovery pressures, existing stock positions, and global market volatility.

Speaking during a MEMAN webinar on Tuesday, the association’s Chairman, Hubb Stokman, said the trend reflects standard market behaviour rather than a Nigeria-specific issue. He noted that when global oil prices increase sharply, marketers are compelled to adjust pump prices quickly in order to remain operational and sustain supply.

Stokman said the rapid increase in fuel prices is largely driven by the need for marketers to generate sufficient working capital to procure new supplies. According to him, failure to reflect higher costs immediately could prevent marketers from restocking, potentially leading to supply disruptions. He added that similar pricing patterns are observed in other fast-moving consumer goods, although fuel price changes are more visible due to their direct link to global markets.

However, he explained that price reductions tend to occur more gradually when global prices ease. This is because marketers often hold inventory purchased at higher costs and need time to recover those expenses before lowering pump prices. He noted that a gradual price adjustment, sometimes described as a “parachute” approach, helps to stabilise the market and prevent sharp fluctuations that could create uncertainty.

Stokman added that the current global environment, particularly the ongoing tensions in the Middle East, has increased volatility in oil markets, making price movements more pronounced. He emphasised that Nigeria’s petrol pricing remains closely tied to international market dynamics due to the deregulated structure of the downstream sector.

Also speaking, energy expert Joe Nwakwue said Nigeria remains highly exposed to global oil price movements, noting that any change in Brent crude prices is quickly transmitted to the domestic market. He explained that while policies such as the naira-for-crude initiative may help reduce some exposure, global shocks will continue to influence local fuel prices.

Nwakwue stressed the importance of maintaining a competitive and contestable market structure to ensure fair pricing. He noted that allowing fuel imports remains critical in preventing price distortions, as competition from importers can influence pricing decisions by local refiners and marketers.

The explanations come amid growing public concern over persistent fuel price fluctuations, with stakeholders calling for clearer policy direction and measures to reduce the impact on consumers.

For micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs), the slow decline in petrol prices has direct economic implications. Many small businesses depend heavily on fuel for transportation, power generation, and logistics. Rapid price increases immediately raise operating costs, while delayed reductions prolong financial pressure, affecting profitability and pricing decisions.

The continued volatility in fuel prices also affects supply chains and consumer spending, as higher transport costs are often passed on to customers. This can reduce demand for goods and services, particularly in price-sensitive markets, further challenging MSME growth and sustainability.
Delayed fuel price reductions keep operating costs high for MSMEs, affecting margins and pricing. Ongoing volatility in energy costs can disrupt business planning and limit growth across sectors.
Ogun to Host MSME Clinic, Offer Grants & Support
Entrepreneurs to access advisory sessions, grant opportunities, and regulatory guidance
Ogun State will host the third MSME Business Clinic on March 24, 2026, offering entrepreneurs advisory sessions, grant access, regulatory guidance, and a business pitch competition to support growth.

The Ogun State Ministry of Industry, Trade, and Investment will hold the third edition of its Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSME) Business Clinics on Tuesday, March 24, 2026, offering entrepreneurs opportunities to grow their businesses and access financial support. The programme, tagged “MSME Business Clinics 3.0,” will take place at the June 12 Cultural Centre in Kuto, Abeokuta, from 8:00 a.m. to 5:00 p.m.

According to the Commissioner for Industry, Trade, and Investment, Adebola Sofela, the initiative reflects the commitment of Governor Dapo Abiodun’s administration to create opportunities that enable small businesses to thrive. “The initiative forms part of the commitment of the administration of Governor Dapo Abiodun to create opportunities that enable small businesses to grow and thrive,” Sofela said.

The clinic will provide a platform for business owners to engage directly with regulatory, financial, and development institutions. Participants will have the opportunity to interact with agencies such as the National Agency for Food and Drug Administration and Control (NAFDAC), the Standards Organisation of Nigeria (SON), the Corporate Affairs Commission (CAC), the Bank of Industry (BOI), and the Ogun State Agricultural and Multipurpose Credit Agency, among others.

Sofela explained that eligible participants will gain access to grants and business support services. “The programme offers eligible participants the chance to access grants, secure free business name registration with the Corporate Affairs Commission, and receive support for product certification from the National Agency for Food and Drug Administration and Control,” he said.

Entrepreneurs will also receive guidance on exporting their products from the Nigerian Export Promotion Council (NEPC), while tax experts will educate participants on new tax laws from both the Federal and State Revenue Services. “Participants will also benefit from expert guidance on exporting their products from the Nigerian Export Promotion Council, while small business owners will be educated on the new tax laws by the Nigeria Revenue Service and the Ogun State Internal Revenue Service,” Sofela added.

The event will feature one-on-one advisory sessions and a business pitch competition, allowing participants to present their ideas and compete for grant support to scale their operations. Sofela encouraged interested business owners to attend and take advantage of the opportunities provided to enhance their growth prospects.

This initiative aligns with broader efforts across Nigeria to strengthen the MSME sector, which is critical to economic growth, employment creation, and local industrial development. By providing regulatory support, financial access, and technical guidance, the Ogun State MSME Business Clinic aims to empower entrepreneurs and boost the state’s business ecosystem.

The clinic provides crucial support for MSMEs, including access to finance, regulatory compliance, and export guidance, enabling small businesses to expand operations, increase competitiveness, and contribute to economic development.
REA and Lotus Bank Finance N7.4bn Renewable Energy Project
New partnership set to deliver 7MW of power to over 30,000 households and businesses
REA and Lotus Bank have financed a N7.4 billion renewable energy project in Ebonyi, providing 7MW of power to more than 30,000 households and businesses across eight communities.

The Rural Electrification Agency (REA) and Lotus Bank have made a significant stride in advancing renewable energy in Nigeria, as Ventura Logistics Services secured N7.4 billion in financing to deploy renewable power infrastructure across eight sites in Ebonyi State. This marks the first major drawdown from the N100 billion facility recently formalized through a Memorandum of Understanding between REA and Lotus Bank.

The funding, backed by the Distributed Access through Renewable Energy Scale-up Programme (DARES) Performance-Based Grant framework, is expected to deliver approximately 7 megawatts of renewable energy. The initiative is projected to directly benefit more than 30,000 households and businesses across the state.

The deployment will span multiple communities, including Okposi, Mebiokpa, and Ohaozara in Ohaozara Local Government Area; Ebunwana in Edda Local Government Area; as well as Unwana and Afikpo in Afikpo North, and Iboko in Izzi Local Government Area. The project is aimed at providing reliable and sustainable electricity access to underserved areas, enhancing both residential and commercial energy security.

Speaking on the milestone, REA Managing Director Abba Aliyu described the development as a demonstration of the agency’s commitment to forging partnerships that yield tangible benefits for Nigerians. “This project demonstrates how strategic alignment between public and private sector partners can deliver immediate outcomes for communities and businesses,” Aliyu said.

The rapid onboarding of Ventura Logistics Services was facilitated by the structured design of the DARES framework, which employs a performance-based grant model to ensure transparency and measurable results. This approach has been instrumental in giving commercial lenders, such as Lotus Bank, confidence to invest in the renewable energy sector. “The disciplined structure of the DARES framework provides the transparency and assurance in outcomes that commercial lenders like Lotus Bank require to commit capital to renewable projects,” the REA statement added.

The Ebonyi project represents a significant step in Nigeria’s broader energy transition agenda, highlighting how public-private partnerships can accelerate the adoption of renewable solutions while delivering socio-economic benefits. By focusing on underserved communities, the initiative supports local development, reduces dependence on fossil fuels, and provides a model for scalable renewable energy deployment in other states.

Industry analysts note that such investments can create new opportunities for micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs) that rely on reliable electricity for operations, from agro-processing to small manufacturing units. Stable power supply from renewable sources is expected to lower operational costs, improve productivity, and stimulate local economic activities.

Reliable electricity access through renewable energy reduces operational costs and improves productivity for MSMEs, enabling them to expand activities, increase competitiveness, and support local economic growth.
Late Government Payments Deepen Financial Strain on South African SMEs
Unpaid invoices rise to R12.4bn as small businesses face mounting cash flow pressure
Unpaid government invoices in South Africa rose to R12.4 billion in the second quarter of 2025, increasing financial pressure on SMEs and disrupting cash flow across sectors.

South Africa’s small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) are facing increasing financial strain as late payments by government departments continue to rise, according to the latest data from the National Treasury. The report shows that by the end of the second quarter of 2025, a total of 95,399 invoices older than 30 days, valued at R12.4 billion, remained unpaid.

The figures represent a deterioration from the first quarter, when 81,736 invoices were outstanding, marking a 17 per cent increase in unpaid invoices within a single quarter. The total value of delayed payments also rose by six per cent from R11.7 billion to R12.4 billion, highlighting a worsening payment backlog across national and provincial departments.

Industry stakeholders say the trend is raising concerns about the sustainability of current payment practices, particularly for SMEs that rely heavily on timely payments to maintain operations. Lawrance Ramotala, Area Manager at Business Partners Limited, described late payments as a direct threat to business continuity rather than a simple administrative issue. He noted that unlike large corporations, most small businesses lack the financial reserves needed to absorb prolonged delays in receiving payments.

The impact is particularly severe in sectors such as retail, manufacturing, and services, where businesses operate on tight margins and depend on steady cash flow to manage inventory, production, and daily expenses. According to Ramotala, payment delays beyond 30 days disrupt operational planning and create uncertainty that makes it difficult for businesses to function efficiently.

The effects of delayed payments extend beyond individual businesses, creating ripple effects across supply chains. SMEs waiting on payments are often forced to delay settling their own obligations to suppliers, many of whom are also small businesses. This chain reaction contributes to broader liquidity challenges within the economy and weakens already fragile business ecosystems.

Late payments are also affecting workers, as some businesses struggle to meet payroll obligations. Delayed or staggered salary payments can disrupt employees’ ability to cover essential expenses such as rent, school fees, and transportation, increasing financial stress for households. Over time, this can impact staff morale, productivity, and retention, further compounding business challenges.

Persistent delays are also limiting the ability of SMEs to invest in growth. Businesses facing unpredictable income streams are less likely to expand operations, hire new staff, or invest in equipment and inventory. This, in turn, constrains job creation and reduces the contribution of SMEs to economic recovery and development.

Ramotala warned that while South African SMEs have demonstrated resilience, continued delays could push even well-managed businesses into survival mode, with long-term implications for competitiveness and employment. He emphasised that addressing late payment practices is critical to sustaining the sector.

While access to short-term financing and cash flow management tools can help businesses manage temporary gaps, stakeholders note that these measures do not address the root cause of the problem. Analysts have called for stronger enforcement of payment

regulations and greater accountability among government departments and private sector players to ensure timely settlement of invoices.

The growing backlog of unpaid invoices underscores the urgent need for systemic reforms to restore confidence among SME suppliers and protect the millions of jobs they support across South Africa and the wider African economy.

Late payments weaken cash flow, disrupt operations, and limit growth for MSMEs. Addressing payment delays is essential to protect business sustainability, jobs, and economic stability across African markets.
PAFON 3.0 to Spotlight AI, Digital Payments, Financial Inclusion
Forum to Convene Industry Leaders to Shape Nigeria’s Digital Finance Ecosystem

Payments Forum Nigeria (PAFON) will host its third edition, PAFON 3.0, on April 24, 2026, bringing together industry leaders to discuss digital payments, AI, and financial inclusion. According to Chike Onwuegbuchi, the event will foster collaboration between regulators, fintechs, and financial institutions to shape Nigeria’s digital economy.

Payments Forum Nigeria (PAFON), the country’s leading platform for digital finance stakeholders, has announced the third edition of its flagship event, PAFON 3.0, scheduled to hold on April 24, 2026, at the Oriental Hotel.

The event is expected to bring together over 600 professionals and more than 20 expert speakers from across the banking, fintech, telecommunications, and regulatory sectors to discuss emerging trends in digital commerce, cybersecurity, and Artificial Intelligence (AI).

Organisers say the forum will serve as a key platform for collaboration among banks, fintech companies, telcos, and policymakers, with a focus on building a more inclusive and sustainable digital payments ecosystem in Nigeria. As the country continues to lead in digital payment innovation across Africa, this year’s edition will emphasise frameworks that support long-term growth and financial inclusion.

A major highlight of the event will be a session on “Fair Digital Payments as a Catalyst for Deepening Financial Inclusion in Nigeria,” which will explore how technology can expand access to financial services for underserved populations.

Co-Convener of PAFON and Chairman of the Nigeria Information Technology Reporters’ Association (NITRA), Chike Onwuegbuchi, described the forum as a critical policy-industry dialogue aimed at shaping the future of Nigeria’s digital economy. He noted that regulators such as the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), the National Information Technology Development Agency (NITDA), and the Nigerian Communications Commission (NCC) will participate alongside key infrastructure providers.

According to him, the forum seeks to promote a consumer-focused approach that supports both Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs) and everyday users, ensuring that digital payment systems remain accessible, fair, and technology-driven.

Key activities at PAFON 3.0 will include panel discussions on AI and digital commerce, engagement sessions with regulators, product exhibitions and demonstrations by fintech companies and banks, as well as business-to-business networking opportunities for industry players.

The event is powered by TechCastle Foundation and supported by Techeconomy. Through its annual forums, PAFON continues to drive conversations around policy, innovation, and financial inclusion, positioning Nigeria as a key player in the global digital economy.

For African MSMEs, the forum presents opportunities to explore new digital payment solutions, build partnerships, and gain insights into regulatory developments that could shape the future of commerce and financial access across the continent.
PAFON 3.0 highlights how improved digital payments, AI adoption, and regulatory collaboration can help African MSMEs reduce costs, expand market access, and scale more efficiently.
SON Backs National Single Window to Boost Trade Efficiency
Digital Trade Platform Set to Simplify Processes and Improve Market Access for MSMEs

The Standards Organisation of Nigeria (SON) has endorsed the National Single Window (NSW), a digital platform set to launch on March 27, 2026, to streamline Nigeria’s import and export processes. The system will integrate multiple regulatory agencies into a single portal, improving efficiency, transparency, and cargo clearance timelines.

The Standards Organisation of Nigeria (SON) has expressed support for the deployment of Nigeria’s National Single Window (NSW) platform for international trade, as the country moves to modernise its trade processes amid ongoing debates around the initiative.

The NSW, scheduled to commence operations on March 27, 2026, is a digital platform designed to streamline import and export procedures by integrating multiple government agencies into a unified electronic system. The platform will enable traders to submit all required documentation through a single portal, eliminating duplication, improving transparency, and enhancing efficiency across Nigeria’s trade ecosystem.

Director General of SON, Ifeanyi Okeke, said the organisation is collaborating with other regulatory bodies to ensure seamless integration of standards and compliance processes within the new framework. He noted that SON’s role as the national standards body places it at the centre of ensuring product quality, consumer safety, and fair trade practices.

Okeke described the NSW as a major step toward improving ease of doing business in Nigeria, explaining that electronic submission of permits, certificates, and regulatory documents will simplify procedures and accelerate cargo clearance at ports. He added that SON has already aligned its digital systems and regulatory processes with the platform to ensure efficient participation.

According to him, the initiative is expected to significantly reduce cargo clearance timelines, strengthen coordination among government agencies, and enhance Nigeria’s competitiveness in global trade. He also stated that the platform would improve the processing of standards-related approvals and support better compliance with national and international regulations.

The SON boss emphasised that the organisation will continue to engage with key stakeholders, including manufacturers, importers, exporters, and logistics operators, to ensure smooth implementation and promote legitimate trade practices.

For African MSMEs, particularly those involved in import and export, the National Single Window is expected to ease regulatory bottlenecks, reduce delays, and lower transaction costs. By simplifying trade processes and improving transparency, the platform could enhance market access, improve operational efficiency, and support the growth of small businesses engaged in cross-border trade.
The National Single Window will help African MSMEs reduce trade delays, cut compliance costs, and access international markets more easily through a streamlined, transparent digital system.
Exporters Panic Over Nigeria’s Container Shortage
Scarcity of Containers and Shipping Delays Disrupt Export Activities at Lagos Ports
Container shortages and shipping delays at Lagos ports are disrupting exports, threatening Nigeria’s trade gains and increasing costs and risks for MSMEs reliant on international markets.

Exporters have raised concerns over a worsening shortage of shipping containers at Lagos ports, warning that the situation could undermine Nigeria’s growing export sector, valued at $44.06bn as of 2025.

The alarm was raised by the President of the Association of West African Exporters and Marine Professionals (AWAEMAP), Bunmi Olumekun, who said the scarcity of containers, coupled with the reluctance of shipping lines to lift export cargoes, is creating significant disruptions across the trade value chain.

According to him, exporters are increasingly unable to access containers needed to ship goods abroad, while available export cargoes are piling up at terminals within Lagos ports due to a lack of vessels to transport them. He noted that some perishable goods have remained at the ports since December 2025 without evacuation, raising concerns about losses for exporters.

The warning comes despite recent growth in Nigeria’s export performance. Data from the Central Bank of Nigeria shows that total exports rose to $44.06bn in the first nine months of 2025, up from $40.29bn recorded during the same period in 2024, reflecting an increase of $3.76bn.

However, Olumekun alleged that some foreign shipping lines are bypassing Nigerian ports, choosing instead to operate from nearby countries such as Cotonou. He claimed that vessels often arrive in Nigeria to discharge imports but depart empty to pick up export cargoes elsewhere, limiting opportunities for Nigerian exporters.

He further explained that the shortage of containers and vessels has led to congestion at port terminals, with limited space available to accommodate export goods. The situation, he said, is reminiscent of past congestion challenges experienced before the introduction of the electronic call-up system.

The problem is also being compounded by global factors, including disruptions linked to tensions in the Middle East, which have affected shipping routes and led some operators to cancel consignments to certain regions.

An exporter and Managing Director of LWL Concept, Lawal Wasiu, confirmed the challenges, stating that many containers loaded with export goods remain stranded at the ports due to the absence of vessels. He added that some terminal operators have stopped accepting export containers, while transporters are increasingly reluctant to return empty containers because of prolonged delays.

Despite acknowledging improvements in Nigeria’s export sector under President Bola Tinubu, stakeholders warned that the ongoing logistics challenges could reverse recent gains if not urgently addressed.

They have called on the Federal Government to intervene by resolving the container shortage, improving port operations, and ensuring that shipping lines meet their obligations to support export activities.

For African MSMEs, particularly those involved in export trade, the situation poses serious risks, including delayed shipments, increased logistics costs, and potential loss of international market opportunities, all of which could weaken competitiveness and growth across the sector.
Container shortages and shipping delays limit African MSMEs’ ability to export goods, leading to higher costs, lost revenue, and reduced competitiveness in global markets.
Dangote Refinery Considers Fuel Exports Amid Import Licence Controversy
Potential Domestic Fuel Shortages Raise Concerns for Nigerian Economy and MSMEs
The Dangote Refinery is considering exporting all its fuel output amid import licence disputes, raising concerns over potential domestic shortages and higher costs for Nigerian MSMEs.

The Dangote Petroleum Refinery is reportedly considering exporting all petrol, diesel, and jet fuel produced at its 650,000-barrel-per-day facility in Lekki, Lagos State, amid claims that new import licences are still being issued for petroleum products.
