The move has sparked concerns that Nigeria could face fuel scarcity despite the country’s growing domestic refining capacity.

Sources within the refinery alleged that six marketers recently received import licences for petrol, a claim denied by the Nigerian Midstream and Downstream Petroleum Regulatory Authority, which clarified that no new licences have been issued in 2026 and that existing shipments were based on licences granted in late 2025.

A senior refinery official said the export plan is partly motivated by the continued issuance of import licences, arguing that if the government allows imports while global fuel supply is uncertain due to the Middle East conflict, the refinery may prioritise foreign markets. Bloomberg reports indicate growing interest from other African countries, including South Africa, seeking alternative fuel supplies amid global disruptions.

The refinery has historically provided a buffer against energy shocks, with February 2026 figures showing it produced 36 million litres per day, covering the majority of national consumption while imports accounted for only 3 million litres. Industry stakeholders have warned that exporting all output could tighten domestic supply, potentially triggering fuel shortages, price hikes, and long queues at filling stations.

Officials from NMDPRA and industry associations emphasised the importance of prioritising domestic refining to sustain gains achieved in the downstream sector. Ahmed Fashola, Vice President of the Independent Petroleum Marketers Association of Nigeria, noted that the Dangote refinery has shielded the country from global supply disruptions and urged continued support for local refining.

For African MSMEs, the potential export of Nigeria’s domestic fuel output could increase operational costs and disrupt supply chains, as businesses rely heavily on stable fuel availability for transportation, manufacturing, and logistics. Uncertainty in domestic fuel supply threatens production continuity and could weaken the competitiveness of Nigerian and regional MSMEs.

Potential fuel exports from the Dangote Refinery could disrupt domestic supply, driving up energy costs and operational challenges for African MSMEs that rely on stable, affordable fuel for production and logistics.
Ecobank Launches Ellevate 2.0 to Empower Women Entrepreneurs
Enhanced Platform Bridges Financing Gap and Boosts African Trade for Women-Led MSMEs

Ecobank Nigeria has launched Ellevate 2.0, an enhanced platform providing improved financing, digital tools, and access to the African Continental Free Trade Area, aimed at empowering women-led MSMEs to scale businesses, drive innovation, and strengthen economic growth.

Ecobank Nigeria has unveiled the enhanced Ellevate 2.0 initiative, aimed at strengthening the role of female entrepreneurs as key drivers of Nigeria’s economic growth and innovation. The launch, themed “Her Voice. Her Power. Her Growth,” brought together top female business leaders and professionals to mark the transition of Ellevate into a comprehensive, bank-wide programme supporting women-led small and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs).

Speaking at the event, Adenike Ogunlesi highlighted her personal experience with Ecobank, noting that the bank’s support enabled her business growth when other institutions declined financing. She emphasised that women entrepreneurs derive strength from their identity, resilience, and purpose.

The upgraded Ellevate 2.0 platform introduces functional tools including improved credit access, integrated digital payment systems, and the Ecobank Single Market Trade Hub, which connects Nigerian female entrepreneurs to the broader African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA).

Ayo Osolake reiterated the bank’s data-driven belief in the economic potential of women entrepreneurs, describing them as key innovators, job creators, and contributors to national industrial growth.

The launch also featured a panel discussion with industry experts including Bode Abifarin, Titilayo Adesoga, and Subuola Oyeleye, focusing on strategies for scaling businesses amid global economic volatility. Victoria Igun, Ellevate Manager at Ecobank, stressed that the programme is designed to tackle structural barriers, creating pathways for women to build sustainable businesses and achieve lasting impact.

For African MSMEs, Ellevate 2.0 provides increased access to finance, digital tools, and trade networks, helping women-led enterprises expand regionally, strengthen operations, and compete more effectively in the continental market.

Ellevate 2.0 helps African MSMEs led by women access finance, digital tools, and continental trade networks, enabling them to grow, compete, and contribute more effectively to regional economic development.
Experts Urge Suspension of Food Import Tariffs to Curb Rising Inflation
Fiscal Interventions Recommended to Shield Households and MSMEs from Cost-of-Living Pressures

Afrinvest has urged the Nigerian government to suspend tariffs on essential food items and implement fiscal measures to curb rising inflation, protect households, and shield MSMEs from escalating costs and supply chain pressures.

Economic analysts at Afrinvest have called on the Federal Government to implement emergency fiscal measures, including suspending tariffs on essential food items, to prevent a sharp rise in living costs following volatile inflation figures for February 2026.

Although Nigeria’s annual headline inflation slightly eased to 15.06 per cent, monthly inflation spiked 2.0 per cent in February, reversing the 2.9 per cent deflation seen in January. Analysts warn that domestic factors, coupled with rising global crude oil prices approaching $105 per barrel amid the Middle East crisis, could further push energy and food prices upward.

Retail fuel prices have surged, with petrol at N1,350 per litre, diesel at N1,650, and cooking gas at N1,400 per kilogramme in several states. Food inflation rose 3.2 percentage points year-on-year to 12.1 per cent, driven by weak seasonal supplies, high logistics costs, and fasting-period shortages.

Afrinvest recommends immediate government action, including affordable nationwide mass transit, healthcare subsidies for low-income earners, and the suspension of tariffs and related charges on food importers to protect households from worsening price pressures.

The advisory group projects that, if unaddressed, year-on-year headline inflation could rise by 150 basis points to 16.6 per cent, with monthly readings possibly reaching 5.2 per cent, undermining the government’s target of 16.5 per cent average inflation. Analysts note that stabilising the Consumer Price Index will depend on mitigating energy and supply chain challenges in the coming months.

For African MSMEs, high inflation and rising input costs increase operational expenses, reduce profit margins, and threaten business sustainability, making tariff suspensions and fiscal support critical to maintaining affordable goods and stable supply chains.
Suspending food import tariffs helps African MSMEs manage rising input costs, maintain affordable pricing, and sustain operations amid inflationary pressures and supply chain disruptions.

FG Pushes Regional Energy Hubs to Drive Africa’s Mining Industrialisation
Strategic Corridors Seen as Key to Unlocking Mineral Value and Economic Growth

Nigeria is advocating for regional energy and industrial corridors across Africa to boost mining industrialisation, attract investment, and supply critical minerals for global energy transition while fostering regional economic integration.

The Federal Government of Nigeria has urged African nations and the United States to prioritise the development of regional energy hubs to accelerate cross-border mining industrialisation and ensure sustainable supplies of critical minerals for the global energy transition.

Minister of Solid Minerals Development, Dele Alake, made the call during a high-level panel at the Powering Africa Summit in Washington, D.C., highlighting the Lagos–Abidjan and Walvis Bay corridors as strategic frameworks that could boost infrastructure, energy access, regional integration, and industrial growth. Alake cited the Lobito Corridor as a successful model for mineral-led economic transformation.

He stressed that regional corridors would facilitate local beneficiation, technology transfer, manufacturing, and cross-border industrialisation. Alake explained that the development of nuclear or other power sources in one country could service an entire corridor, generating widespread industrial benefits.

The minister noted that global competition for critical minerals such as lithium, cobalt, and nickel is intensifying due to their importance in clean energy technologies, including electric vehicles and renewable energy systems. He emphasised that reforms under President Bola Ahmed Tinubu have strengthened Nigeria’s mining sector by enhancing governance, digitising licensing, improving regulatory frameworks, and ensuring secure mineral tenure for investors.

Alake also highlighted security improvements, including the arrest and prosecution of over 350 suspected illegal miners, signalling that the sector is being sanitised for legitimate operators. Incentives such as tax waivers on imported mining equipment and full repatriation of profits have helped attract over $2.6bn in foreign direct investment in the past two and a half years.

Other panelists, including Sarah Whitten from the Export-Import Bank of the United States and industry leaders from Africa, emphasised the importance of consistent policies and financing structures to mobilise private sector investment in critical minerals projects.

Africa holds around 30 per cent of global mineral reserves but captures a smaller share of mining value due to limited local processing and infrastructure. Analysts argue that the creation of integrated energy and industrial corridors will be critical to increasing local value addition, meeting rising global demand, and supporting sustainable economic growth.

For Nigeria, expanding mining through regional energy hubs aligns with broader efforts to diversify the economy, create jobs, generate foreign exchange, and reduce dependence on oil.

Developing regional energy hubs and mining corridors provides African MSMEs with access to local mineral supply, infrastructure, and industrial clusters, reducing costs, creating jobs, and enabling participation in value-added industries.

Rite Foods Partners to Monetise Plastic Waste in Lagos
‘Waste is Naira’ Initiative Rewards Communities for Plastic Recovery

Rite Foods and RecyclePoint launched ‘Waste is Naira’, a community-driven initiative in Lagos that rewards residents for collecting and returning plastic waste, combining environmental sustainability with economic incentives.

Rite Foods Limited has partnered with RecyclePoint to launch the ‘Waste is Naira’ programme, aimed at removing plastic waste from Lagos communities by incentivising residents to collect and return plastics for cash and rewards.

At the unveiling in Lagos, Eze Ekuma explained that the initiative is part of Rite Foods’ commitment to environmental sustainability. He highlighted the company’s ongoing collaboration with Pop Beach Club to clean coastal areas and noted that the new programme extends waste recovery efforts to hinterland communities.

The community-based project allows participants to earn rewards through a point system, which can be exchanged for cash, household items, or even school fees, effectively monetising plastic waste. Ekuma stated, “Many times, in this programme, many people will be able to pay their students’ school fees by returning the plastic to us. That waste is going to be monetised in a way that people can bring back their waste, and we take the waste, recycle, and reuse.”

RecyclePoint’s Programme Manager, Daniel Oderinde, said the target is to recover 750 tonnes of plastic within the first year, with staged goals of 187 tonnes in three months and 375 tonnes within six months. He noted that the project will also generate jobs, support social initiatives, and foster multi-sector collaboration.

Inflation Above 20% Persists Across Six states and FCT, Despite National Easing.
Persistent high food and energy costs keep inflation above 20% in key states, challenging businesses and households despite a slight national easing.

Inflation in February 2026 remained above 20% in six Nigerian states and the FCT, driven mainly by rising food and local costs, even as the national headline rate eased slightly to 15.06%, signalling persistent regional price pressures despite temporary relief in January.

Inflation remained above 20 per cent in six states and the Federal Capital Territory (FCT) in February 2026, highlighting persistent subnational price pressures despite a slight easing in Nigeria’s headline inflation rate.

Data from the National Bureau of Statistics’ (NBS) latest Consumer Price Index (CPI) report showed that headline inflation slowed marginally to 15.06 per cent in February from 15.10 per cent in January. However, several states continue to experience significantly higher inflation due to food prices and local cost dynamics. Kogi recorded the highest all-items inflation at 23.6 per cent, followed by Benue at 22.9 per cent and Anambra at 22.1 per cent. The FCT posted 21.9 per cent, while Oyo and Akwa Ibom recorded 21.6 per cent and 20.9 per cent, respectively. Adamawa stood at 20.0 per cent.

Food inflation remains a central driver behind the divergence. Kogi recorded food inflation of 26.9 per cent, Adamawa 23.1 per cent, and Benue 21.9 per cent, significantly above the national food inflation rate of 12.12 per cent. Even in states with relatively lower food inflation, such as Anambra at 13.8 per cent and Oyo at 14.9 per cent, overall inflation remained above 20 per cent due to rising costs of transport, housing, and energy.

February also saw a sharp reversal in monthly price trends after declines in January. All six states and the FCT recorded positive month-on-month inflation. Anambra led with a 4.1 per cent rise, followed by Oyo at 4.0 per cent and Akwa Ibom at 3.8 per cent. Abuja and Kogi recorded 2.2 per cent and 2.1 per cent increases, while Benue and Adamawa posted smaller gains of 0.3 per cent and 0.7 per cent. Food prices surged across all states, led by Anambra at 6.6 per cent, Akwa Ibom at 6.5 per cent, and Kogi at 5.9 per cent, suggesting local food supply pressures re-emerged after temporary relief in January.

The NBS noted that the CPI rose to 130.0 in February from 127.4 in January, reflecting a 2.6-point increase within the month. On a year-on-year basis, headline inflation was 11.21 percentage points lower than February 2025’s 26.27 per cent, indicating some moderation. Yet, monthly increases demonstrate that regional price pressures remain strong despite the national slowdown.

Members of the Organised Private Sector (OPS) warned that the marginal easing in headline inflation offers little relief for businesses and households. The President of the Association of Small Business Owners of Nigeria, Dr. Femi Egbesola, said the decline was largely driven by seasonal factors rather than structural improvements in the economy. He added, “The current inflation figures have not translated into relief for small and medium-sized enterprises, which continue to face rising costs and constrained purchasing power.”

The persistent high inflation in key states underscores the challenges faced by both businesses and households, indicating that localized cost pressures may continue to strain economic activities despite national trends suggesting moderation.
Sustained high inflation in key states increases input costs and reduces consumer purchasing power, directly affecting the operations, profitability, and growth of African MSMEs.
Dangote Raises Petrol Price to N1,332 in Fifth March Hike
Dangote refinery drives petrol to N1,332 per litre in fifth March increase, fueling cost pressures for businesses.

Petrol prices in Nigeria have surged to N1,332 per litre following the fifth price hike by the Dangote Petroleum Refinery in March, reflecting sharp market volatility and rising global crude oil costs.

Nigeria’s petrol market is experiencing extreme volatility as the Dangote Petroleum Refinery raises the price of Premium Motor Spirit (PMS) for the fifth time in March, pushing retail prices to N1,332 per litre nationwide. The latest adjustment by MRS Oil Nigeria Plc sets the pump price at N1,332 per litre, with company delivery at N1,290 and self-collection at N1,282, following directives that all loading will be done at the Dangote refinery.

The refinery’s ex-depot gantry price was increased to N1,275 per litre, representing a sharp 2.4 per cent rise from the previous N1,245 per litre earlier in the day and a cumulative 64.7 per cent increase from N774 per litre at the start of March. Coastal prices were also adjusted upward from N1,512,648 per metric tonne to N1,646,748 per metric tonne, reflecting an 8.9 per cent rise.

This rapid succession of hikes underscores Nigeria’s vulnerability to global crude oil price fluctuations and supply chain disruptions, despite the operational capacity of the Dangote refinery, which produces 650,000 barrels per day. The refinery’s pricing reviews are influenced by international market conditions, freight costs, and increased demand from African countries such as South Africa, Ghana, and Kenya, which are seeking alternative fuel supplies due to Middle East disruptions.

The increases are expected to trigger further adjustments at retail filling stations across the country, impacting transport fares, logistics, and industrial production. Diesel prices have also risen, with the gantry price of Automotive Gas Oil (diesel) now at N1,750 per litre, up from N1,500 per litre in a previous hike.

The sustained price pressures highlight ongoing challenges in Nigeria’s downstream petroleum sector, affecting businesses and households and raising concerns over inflationary impacts across the economy.
The repeated petrol price increases escalate operating costs for African MSMEs, particularly in transport, logistics, and manufacturing, directly affecting profitability and supply chain efficiency.

Burkina Faso’s suspension of fresh tomato exports is creating immediate supply and pricing pressures for MSMEs across West Africa, particularly traders, food vendors, and agro-processors who rely on cross-border supply chains.

Burkina Faso’s decision to halt fresh tomato exports is beginning to disrupt core supply channels for MSMEs across West Africa, affecting businesses that depend on consistent access to affordable produce.

While the policy aims to boost local agro-processing and value addition, it is already triggering short-term supply gaps, rising input costs, and operational uncertainty for small businesses across the region.

  • Increased input costs for food vendors and processors

  • Supply shortages affecting daily sales and inventory planning

High-risk MSME segments:

  • Food vendors and restaurants

Key realities:

Countries most exposed:

  • Retain more economic value domestically

  • Strengthen agro-industrial capacity

  • Short-term loss of access to raw materials for traders and resellers

  • Improve inventory planning to manage supply uncertainty

This shift highlights how policy decisions can directly impact MSME supply chains and profitability. For small businesses, resilience will depend on diversified sourcing, local production strategies, and agile pricing models key capabilities for surviving and scaling in evolving African markets.

Dangote Refinery Expands African Fuel Exports, Strengthening Regional Energy Supply
First petrol exports signal a shift toward intra-African energy trade and reduced import dependence.

The Dangote Refinery has exported 456,000 tonnes of refined petroleum products to multiple African countries, marking a major step toward regional energy self-sufficiency and new trade opportunities across the continent.

The Dangote Petroleum Refinery has completed its first major export of petrol, shipping 456,000 tonnes of refined petroleum products across Africa following its ramp-up to 650,000 barrels per day capacity in February.

The exports, delivered to countries including Ghana, Togo, Côte d’Ivoire, Cameroon, and Tanzania, signal a strategic shift in Africa’s energy supply chain, from reliance on overseas refineries to intra-African sourcing and trade integration.

Quick Loans in Nigeria: Understanding Interest Rates, Hidden Costs, and Risks
What entrepreneurs and small businesses need to know before taking instant digital loans

Nigeria’s fast-growing digital lending market offers quick access to cash, but often at high and complex costs. Understanding interest structures, hidden fees, and repayment risks is critical for MSMEs and individuals using these loans for short-term financing.

Nigeria’s digital lending ecosystem has expanded rapidly, offering instant access to credit within minutes. While these loans provide critical support for emergencies and short-term needs, they often come with high interest rates and hidden charges that many borrowers overlook.

For entrepreneurs and small business owners, understanding the true cost of quick loans is essential to avoid cash flow strain and debt traps.

 

Why Quick Loan Interest Rates Are Higher

Digital lenders charge significantly more than traditional banks due to:

  • Unsecured lending risk (no collateral required)

  • High default rates across borrowers

  • Costs of AI-driven credit scoring and instant disbursement systems

  • Premium placed on speed and convenience

Typical comparison:

  • Banks: 25%–30% annually

  • Digital lenders: 15%–30% monthly

 

Understanding How Interest Is Calculated

Many borrowers underestimate costs due to how rates are presented.

Key concepts:

  • Daily/Monthly Rates:

    • Example: 1% daily = 30% monthly ≈ 360% annually

  • APR (Annual Percentage Rate):

    • Reflects the true yearly cost, including fees

  • Example Loan:

    • Borrow ₦10,000 at 20% for 30 days

    • Repay: ₦12,000 (excluding extra fees)

 

Hidden Costs That Increase Loan Burden

The actual cost of borrowing often exceeds the advertised rate.

Common additional charges:

  • Processing/management fees (often deducted upfront)

  • Insurance levies (e.g., credit life insurance)

Real scenario:

  • Loan approved: ₦10,000

  • Processing fee: ₦1,000

  • Amount received: ₦9,000

  • Amount repaid: ₦12,000

Effective borrowing cost becomes significantly higher than advertised.

 

What Determines Your Interest Rate

Digital lenders use risk-based pricing, meaning rates vary per borrower.

Key factors:

  • Credit history (BVN-linked data)

  • Existing loans and repayment behavior

  • User loyalty and repayment consistency

Typical structure:

  • New users:

    • Lower loan limits, higher interest rates

  • Repeat users:

    • Higher limits, reduced rates over time

 

Late Payments and Penalties

Missed repayments can rapidly escalate debt.

Penalty structure:

  • Daily default charges: 1%–2% of outstanding balance

  • Debt can double within weeks or months

  • Long-term impact on credit score and future borrowing ability

 

Red Flags to Watch Before Borrowing

Not all lenders operate transparently.

Warning signs:

  • No clear total repayment amount upfront

  • Changing repayment terms after acceptance

  • Unrealistic loan offers (e.g., high amounts for first-time users)

  • Lack of APR disclosure

 

Best Practices for Safe Borrowing

To avoid financial strain:

  • Focus on total repayment amount, not advertised rates

  • Confirm all fees and deductions upfront

  • Borrow only what you can repay within your next income cycle

  • Use loan comparison platforms to evaluate options

Quick loans can provide fast working capital, but high costs can erode profit margins and destabilise cash flow. For MSMEs, disciplined borrowing and cost awareness are essential to ensure credit supports growth, rather than becoming a financial burden.
Liberia Launches Packaging Center to Boost SME Competitiveness and Exports
New national facility aims to solve packaging challenges limiting small business growth and market access.
Liberia is establishing its first-ever Packaging Solutions Center to help SMEs improve product presentation, reduce outsourcing costs, and compete more effectively in local and international markets.

Liberia is taking a major step to strengthen its SME ecosystem with plans to launch a national Packaging Solutions Center, designed to address one of the most overlooked barriers to business growth, poor product packaging.

Led by the Liberia Special Economic Zones Authority, the initiative will provide local businesses with access to modern packaging services, helping them improve product appeal, meet standards, and expand into new markets.

 

Why Packaging Is a Critical Growth Barrier

For many SMEs in Liberia, product quality is not the problem, presentation is.

Key challenges faced by SMEs:

  • Poor packaging reduces product appeal and shelf visibility

  • Limits access to formal retail and export markets

  • Forces businesses to outsource packaging abroad, increasing costs

This gap has long prevented many businesses from scaling despite having competitive products.

 

What the Packaging Solutions Center Will Offer

The new facility is designed as a shared infrastructure hub for SMEs.

Core features:

  • Access to modern packaging equipment and services

  • Support for product branding and presentation

  • Reduced reliance on outsourcing to countries like Nigeria, India, and China

  • Professional management to ensure efficiency and sustainability

Investment commitment:

  • Approximately $672,000 allocated under Liberia’s 2026 national budget

 

Ecosystem-Wide Collaboration

The initiative reflects a coordinated national effort to support SMEs.

Key stakeholders involved:

  • Liberia Standards Authority

  • Liberia Tourism Authority

  • Liberia Business Association

  • Ministry of Commerce and Industry Liberia

This collaboration aims to align standards, branding, and market access strategies for local businesses.

 

Unlocking Opportunities Beyond Local Markets

Improved packaging has direct implications for growth:

  • Enables SMEs to meet export and retail standards

  • Strengthens brand identity and customer perception

  • Supports entry into sectors like tourism, hospitality, and retail

  • Enhances competitiveness in regional and global markets

 

A Strategic Shift Toward SME-Led Growth

The initiative signals a broader move toward building enabling infrastructure for SMEs, not just providing funding.

By strengthening the link between production and market access, Liberia is positioning its SMEs to scale beyond local markets.

This move highlights how critical non-financial infrastructure like packaging and branding is for business growth. For MSMEs across Africa, improving product presentation can unlock new markets, higher margins, and export opportunities, making it a key lever for scaling sustainably.
New Horizons Expands ICT Training to Ogun, Boosting Digital Skills for MSMEs
New tech hub brings globally recognised digital training closer to entrepreneurs and professionals in Lagos outskirts.

New Horizons Nigeria has launched a new ICT training hub in Ogun State, expanding access to in-demand digital skills for youths and professionals, skills increasingly critical for MSME growth, productivity, and competitiveness.

New Horizons Nigeria has opened a new technology training hub in Akute, Ogun State, aimed at improving access to digital skills for residents in fast-growing communities around Lagos.

The centre is designed to reduce barriers to quality ICT education, enabling entrepreneurs, job seekers, and small business owners to acquire globally recognised tech skills without the need for long-distance travel.

 

Expanding Access to Digital Skills

The new hub will serve multiple underserved communities, including:

  • Akute

  • Alagbado

  • Ajuwon

  • Ojodu-Berger

  • Iju-Ishaga

  • Isheri and surrounding areas

This expansion addresses key challenges such as:

  • High cost of training access

  • Long-distance travel to major cities

  • Limited availability of structured ICT programmes

 

Courses and Certifications Available

Learners will gain access to high-demand digital skills relevant to today’s economy.

Training areas include:

  • Cybersecurity

  • Web and full-stack development

  • Artificial intelligence (AI)

  • Graphic design

Certifications offered:

  • Globally recognised CompTIA certifications (hardware, networking, security)

 

Why Digital Skills Matter for Businesses

The initiative reflects the growing role of technology in business performance.

Key insights:

  • Digital skills now underpin productivity across all sectors

  • ICT capabilities improve efficiency, marketing, and customer reach

  • Tech-enabled businesses are better positioned to scale and compete

New Horizons, part of a global network operating in over 90 countries, already trains over 100,000 students annually, supplying talent to businesses, institutions, and governments.

 

Local Impact and Economic Opportunities

The Akute hub is expected to:

  • Create a pipeline of skilled talent for local businesses

  • Enable individuals to access higher-paying digital opportunities

  • Support remote work, freelancing, and tech entrepreneurship

It also provides a cost-effective alternative to expensive international training, while maintaining global standards.

Access to digital skills is now a core driver of MSME growth. This initiative equips entrepreneurs with tools to digitise operations, reach wider markets, and improve efficiency, while also expanding the local talent pool needed to build and scale businesses in Africa’s digital economy.
Nigeria’s Non-Oil Exports Grow to N12.36tn, But Agricultural Risks Persist
Strong export growth driven by cocoa and agriculture, but limited diversification exposes MSMEs to market volatility.

Nigeria’s non-oil exports rose sharply in 2025, led by agriculture and cocoa exports. However, heavy reliance on a narrow range of commodities and declining performance in key segments like soybeans highlight structural risks for MSMEs.

Nigeria’s non-oil export sector recorded significant growth in 2025, reaching N12.36 trillion, up from N9.09 trillion in 2024, according to data from the National Bureau of Statistics (NBS).

The increase reflects stronger participation in global trade, with agriculture emerging as the primary driver of export expansion.

 

Agriculture Leads Export Growth

Agriculture remains central to Nigeria’s non-oil export performance.

Key highlights:

  • Agriculture contributed 41.04% of total non-oil exports

  • Cocoa alone accounted for 24.61% of total exports

  • Export volume rose by 10%, reaching 8.02 million metric tonnes

This indicates that growth was supported by both:

  • Increased global demand

  • Higher export volumes, not just price gains

 

Concentration Risk Remains High

Despite strong growth, Nigeria’s export base remains narrow and vulnerable.

Major concerns:

  • Heavy reliance on a few commodities:

    • Cocoa

    • Sesame

    • Cashew

  • Limited value addition across export products

  • Exposure to global price fluctuations

Even with updated classification systems, export data still shows a high concentration in primary commodities, increasing risk for exporters.

 

Soybean Decline Signals Structural Weakness

A key weak spot in 2025 was the sharp drop in soybean exports.

Key figures:

  • Export value fell to N47.48 billion

  • Represented just 0.38% of total exports

  • Declined by 62.08% year-on-year

Possible drivers:

  • Domestic production constraints

  • Rising global competition

Despite the decline, Nigeria continues to export soybeans to markets such as:

  • India

  • Canada

  • United States

  • Sri Lanka

  • Gabon

 

What This Means for MSME Exporters

The data reflects both opportunity and risk for small businesses.

Opportunities:

  • Growing global demand for Nigerian agricultural products

  • Increased export volumes signal expanding market access

Challenges:

  • Overdependence on a few commodities

  • Limited processing and value addition

  • Vulnerability to price shocks and supply fluctuations

Rising non-oil exports signal strong growth potential, but sustainability depends on diversification. For MSMEs, the priority is clear: move beyond raw exports into value-added products, expand product lines, and build resilience against global market volatility.
Libya, China Agree on Joint Committee to Boost Cooperation
Move Signals Renewed Strategic Partnership and Reconstruction Push
Libya and China have agreed to form a joint committee to strengthen political and economic cooperation, signalling China’s growing role in Libya’s reconstruction and long-term development.

Libya has agreed with China to establish a joint committee in Beijing, marking a significant step in strengthening political and economic ties as the North African country seeks support for reconstruction and long-term development.

The agreement followed high-level talks between Abdulmajeed Maligata, chairman of the High Committee for Supervising Libyan-Chinese Cooperation, and Chinese officials including Jin Xin of the Communist Party of China’s Central Committee. Libya’s acting Economy and Trade Minister, Suhail Abu Shihah, was also part of the delegation.

The discussions focused on strengthening political coordination, expanding international cooperation, and facilitating the return of Chinese companies to Libya, particularly in infrastructure development and governance support.

The move signals Libya’s intention to deepen its engagement with China not only as a commercial partner but as a strategic ally capable of supporting long-term recovery efforts. It also reflects a broader shift as Libya looks eastward for investment, technical expertise, and diplomatic backing.

China’s renewed engagement follows the reopening of its embassy in Tripoli in November 2025 after more than a decade, paving the way for increased cooperation in trade, investment, and bilateral relations.

The joint committee is expected to serve as a platform for sustained collaboration, as both countries work to revive ties that had weakened during years of instability.
Stronger Libya-China ties could open new trade, infrastructure, and supply chain opportunities for African MSMEs through increased investment, regional connectivity, and market access.
Personal Pension Plan Set to Transform Small Business Retirement — Leadway
Flexible Savings Model Targets Informal Sector and Financial Security
Leadway Pensure says Nigeria’s Personal Pension Plan offers flexible retirement savings for small business owners, aiming to improve financial security despite low participation rates.

Leadway Pensure PFA Limited has stated that Nigeria’s Personal Pension Plan (PPP) is set to reshape how small business owners save for retirement, offering more flexible and accessible options to reduce the risk of old-age poverty.

The pension administrator noted that the initiative is particularly important given Nigeria’s large informal sector, where many entrepreneurs and small business owners lack structured retirement savings.

According to data from the National Pension Commission (PenCom), over 200,000 workers had registered for PPP accounts as of September 30, 2025, but only 7.6 per cent actively funded their accounts during the third quarter, highlighting a significant participation gap.

Speaking on the development, Olusakin Labeodan said the scheme is designed to align with the income patterns of small business owners, allowing them to make contributions at their convenience.

He emphasised that small businesses are the backbone of Nigeria’s economy but often lack structured retirement planning, adding that increased participation in the PPP could help entrepreneurs achieve long-term financial stability and security.

Leadway Pensure expressed optimism that wider adoption of the scheme would not only improve individual financial outcomes but also contribute to broader economic development by promoting savings culture among informal sector participants.

Flexible pension schemes like PPP help African MSME owners build long-term financial security, reduce vulnerability in retirement, and promote sustainable wealth creation beyond active business years.
Nigeria Attracts $2.6bn Mining Investment in Two Years — FG
Alake Pushes Regional Energy Hubs to Accelerate Africa’s Mining Growth
Nigeria has attracted over $2.6bn in mining investments in two years, driven by reforms and a push for regional energy hubs to boost Africa’s mineral industrialisation.

Nigeria has attracted over $2.6 billion in Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) into its solid minerals sector within the past two years, according to the Minister of Solid Minerals Development, Dele Alake.

Alake disclosed this during a panel session at the Powering Africa Summit in Washington, D.C., where he highlighted the impact of reforms under President Bola Tinubu in repositioning the mining sector as a key driver of economic diversification.

He noted that improved governance structures, enhanced regulatory frameworks, digitisation of licensing processes, and ease of doing business have strengthened investor confidence. According to him, these reforms now guarantee secure tenure for mineral title holders, providing the long-term stability required for investment decisions.

The minister also advocated the development of regional energy hubs and industrial corridors across Africa to accelerate mining industrialisation and strengthen the supply of critical minerals needed for the global energy transition. He identified strategic corridors such as the Lagos–Abidjan and Walvis Bay routes as potential catalysts for infrastructure development, energy access, and regional integration.

Alake explained that such corridors could enable local mineral processing, technology transfer, manufacturing, and cross-border industrialisation, adding that developing three to five such hubs across Africa could significantly boost the continent’s industrial capacity.

He further disclosed that the government is expanding geological data systems to support informed investment decisions, while also addressing security challenges through the deployment of Mining Marshals. Over 350 suspected illegal miners have been arrested in the past year, with more than 150 currently facing prosecution.

Reaffirming Nigeria’s openness to investors, Alake outlined incentives including tax waivers on mining equipment imports and full repatriation of profits after due compliance with regulations.

Also speaking at the event, Sarah Whitten of the U.S. Export-Import Bank emphasised the need for long-term policy consistency to unlock private sector investment in critical minerals projects.

The development reflects growing global interest in Africa’s mineral resources, with the continent holding about 30 per cent of the world’s reserves but capturing a smaller share of value due to limited processing and infrastructure.
Increased mining investments and regional industrial hubs can create new opportunities for African MSMEs in supply chains, manufacturing, and services, driving job creation and local economic growth.
Nigeria’s Net Domestic Credit Falls 6.9% to N109.4tn
Decline Raises Concerns Over Business Lending Despite Policy Easing
Nigeria’s net domestic credit fell by 6.9% to N109.4tn, reflecting reduced private sector lending despite efforts to ease monetary policy.

Nigeria’s Net Domestic Credit (NDC) declined by 6.9 per cent year-on-year to N109.4 trillion in January 2026, down from N102.4 trillion in the same period of 2025, according to the latest money and credit report by the Central Bank of Nigeria.

NDC, which measures total bank credit to both the public and private sectors, reflects the overall lending capacity of the financial system. The report showed that credit to the government rose to N34.2 trillion in January 2026 from N25.03 trillion a year earlier, while credit to the private sector declined to N75.2 trillion from N77.4 trillion.

Quarterly data indicates a downward trend through most of 2025. NDC dropped by 4.4 per cent to N100.6 trillion in the first quarter from N105.2 trillion in December 2024, followed by a 2.8 per cent decline in the second quarter to N97.8 trillion. The contraction continued in the third quarter with a 1.1 per cent drop to N96.7 trillion before a slight recovery in the fourth quarter, rising by 2.6 per cent to N99.2 trillion.

Analysts attribute the overall decline to ongoing monetary policy adjustments aimed at easing inflationary pressures. However, concerns remain about the impact on business financing, particularly as credit to the private sector shows signs of weakening.

Commenting on the development, Muda Yusuf, Chief Executive Officer of the Centre for the Promotion of Private Enterprise, described the Central Bank’s decision to reduce the Monetary Policy Rate (MPR) as a positive step. He noted that lower interest rates and reduced Cash Reserve Requirements (CRR) could improve banks’ lending capacity and support business growth.

He added that such measures could stimulate output and job creation but stressed that monetary policy alone is insufficient. According to him, fiscal authorities must complement these efforts by improving infrastructure, strengthening regulatory frameworks, and ensuring macroeconomic stability.

Also speaking, David Adonri, Executive Vice Chairman of High Cap Securities, warned that the continued contraction in credit could limit access to funding for businesses at a time when inflation and weak consumer demand are already affecting economic performance.

The development underscores the delicate balance between controlling inflation and sustaining credit flow to businesses, with implications for economic growth and private sector expansion.
Reduced access to credit limits African MSMEs’ ability to expand, manage cash flow, and invest in growth, especially in an already high-cost economic environment.
Dangote Warns Nigeria, Africa May Bear Cost of Prolonged Iran Conflict
Rising Energy Prices and Inflation Risks Threaten Economic Stability
Dangote warns that prolonged Middle East tensions could drive higher energy costs, inflation, and economic strain across Nigeria and Africa.

Foremost industrialist Aliko Dangote has warned that continued tensions in the Middle East could have severe economic consequences for Nigeria and African economies, despite having no direct involvement in the crisis.

Dangote made the remarks in Lagos after a courtesy visit and Eid-el-Fitr homage to President Bola Tinubu, noting that global economic interdependence means Africa will inevitably feel the ripple effects of geopolitical instability.

He cautioned that if the Iran-related conflict persists, it could trigger rising fuel prices, increased transport costs, and renewed inflationary pressures across the continent. According to him, these developments would deepen existing economic challenges and place additional strain on both governments and households.

Dangote explained that prolonged volatility in global oil markets could lead to higher subsidy costs and unstable government revenues, worsening fiscal pressures. He also highlighted the risk of escalating Africa’s already growing debt burden, which could further limit governments’ ability to respond to economic shocks.

He stressed that rising energy costs would impact virtually all sectors, from small businesses and manufacturing to logistics and household consumption. He noted that some countries are already adopting coping measures such as reduced workdays, energy rationing, and remote work arrangements, which may help manage costs but could also reduce productivity and slow economic output.

Dangote urged global leaders to prioritise de-escalation, emphasising that many Africans rely on daily income and are particularly vulnerable to economic disruptions. He warned that prolonged instability could significantly affect livelihoods across the continent.

On a positive note, Dangote referenced President Tinubu’s recent visit to the United Kingdom, stating that it had opened new investment opportunities and strengthened Nigeria’s economic outlook. He noted that agreements reached, particularly in infrastructure and financing, signal growing international confidence in Nigeria’s reform agenda.

He added that planned investments in key sectors such as ports could improve trade efficiency and support medium-term economic growth. Dangote also expressed optimism that increased investor confidence would attract additional international partnerships and funding opportunities for Nigerian businesses.

The industrialist concluded by reaffirming his support for the government’s reforms, expressing confidence that sustained partnerships and investor confidence would drive long-term economic growth.
Rising energy and transport costs triggered by global conflicts directly increase operating expenses for African MSMEs, reducing margins and slowing business growth.
LBS Warns Weak Governance Threatens Family Business Survival
Poor Succession Planning and Structure Drive High Failure Rates
Lagos Business School warns that weak governance and poor succession planning are driving the high failure rate of family businesses in Nigeria.

Lagos Business School has raised concerns over the high failure rate of family-owned businesses in Nigeria, citing weak governance structures and poor succession planning as major threats to their long-term survival.

The warning emerged from a recent expert session under the school’s Family Business Initiative, where stakeholders examined how governance gaps continue to undermine even profitable enterprises. According to LBS, family businesses dominate Nigeria’s MSME landscape and serve as a major source of indigenous capital and employment, yet many fail to outlive their founders.

Experts at the session noted that while business owners often prioritise growth and profitability, the most critical risk facing family enterprises is leadership transition. Many businesses collapse not because they are unviable, but because they lack structured systems to manage succession, accountability, and clearly defined roles.

Speaking at the event, Okey Nwuke, Director of the Family Business Initiative, described governance as essential to continuity. He warned that businesses operating without formal frameworks are highly vulnerable, regardless of their financial success.

He explained that many family businesses rely on informal, relationship-driven management systems, which can become problematic as the organisation grows. Without clear structures, issues such as role ambiguity, conflict, and poor decision-making often arise.

Also speaking, Igbuan Okaisabor, Chief Executive Officer of Construction Kaiser Limited, emphasised that governance should be treated as a necessity rather than an option. He highlighted cases where poor governance led to the collapse of once-thriving businesses, stressing the need to separate family relationships from business operations and prioritise competence in leadership roles.

Experts further noted that governance extends beyond formal boards to include transparent financial systems, defined responsibilities, and clear expectations for family members involved in the business. Lack of financial transparency, particularly around profit-sharing and reinvestment, was identified as a major source of conflict.

The initiative also underscored the importance of involving independent directors and external advisers to introduce objectivity and align business strategies with long-term growth goals. It emphasised that governance frameworks are most effective when established proactively rather than during periods of crisis.

As part of its efforts to address these challenges, LBS announced plans to host the Third IFBC 2026 Conference on March 26, 2026, at the Ecobank Pan-African Centre, bringing together family business owners, successors, and advisers to explore strategies for building sustainable enterprises.
Strong governance and succession planning are critical for African MSMEs to ensure business continuity, protect generational wealth, and build sustainable enterprises beyond their founders.
Power Crisis Deepens as DisCos Lose N2.4trn to Inefficiencies
Billing Gaps, Debt Burden Worsen Blackouts Across Nigeria
Nigeria’s power crisis is worsening as DisCos record N2.4 trillion in losses from inefficiencies, leading to reduced generation and widespread blackouts.

Nigeria’s electricity crisis has intensified as Electricity Distribution Companies (DisCos) recorded a combined loss of N2.349 trillion over the past two years, driven by billing inefficiencies and weak revenue collection systems.

Data from the Nigerian Electricity Regulatory Commission (NERC) shows that DisCos lost N1.015 trillion in 2024, with losses rising sharply by 31.4 per cent to N1.334 trillion in 2025. A breakdown of the 2025 figures indicates that billing inefficiencies accounted for N649.87 billion, while poor revenue collection led to losses of N684.28 billion.

The financial strain has compounded an already fragile power sector, which is burdened by an estimated N6 trillion debt. Generation Companies (GenCos) are owed significant sums, forcing many to operate below capacity due to reduced gas supply from vendors unwilling to continue deliveries without payment.

As a result, electricity generation has dropped from an average of 4,600 megawatts in 2025 to below 3,500MW in early 2026, triggering widespread blackouts across the country. DisCos have resorted to load shedding, leaving many households and businesses with less than 12 hours of electricity daily, and in some areas, as little as four to six hours.

In Abuja, communities such as Karu and Lokogoma reportedly receive only a few hours of electricity daily, while states under the Abuja

Electricity Distribution Company (AEDC), including Nasarawa, Niger, and Kogi, are also experiencing prolonged outages. Similar conditions persist in Delta State under the Benin Electricity Distribution Company (BEDC), where some communities face days without power despite high estimated billing.

The crisis has been further highlighted by the Presidential Villa’s decision to exit the national grid in favour of a solar hybrid system, underscoring growing concerns about the reliability of public power supply.

Industry experts attribute the losses largely to inadequate metering and lack of transparency. According to Bode Fadipe, without proper metering, electricity consumption cannot be accurately measured, leading to estimated billing and persistent disputes between consumers and service providers.

Consumers have also raised concerns about exploitative billing practices. Chijoke James, Chairman of the Electricity Consumers Association of Nigeria, criticised estimated billing as a major issue, stating that many customers are unwilling to pay for electricity they do not receive.

Experts have called for comprehensive metering across the entire electricity value chain to improve efficiency, transparency, and revenue collection. They also urged stronger policy intervention to address the sector’s financial challenges and restore confidence.

The continued inefficiencies and financial losses within the distribution segment are undermining the stability of Nigeria’s electricity supply industry, raising concerns about the sustainability of power delivery and the broader economic impact.
Unreliable electricity and rising self-generation costs significantly increase operating expenses for African MSMEs, reducing productivity, profitability, and business sustainability.
CPPE Urges Immediate Govt Action Over Rising Energy Costs
Experts Warn Inflation Gains at Risk Amid Global Oil Pressures
CPPE has urged the government to take urgent action to curb rising energy costs, warning that inflation gains remain fragile amid global oil shocks.

The Centre for the Promotion of Private Enterprise (CPPE) has called for urgent government intervention to address rising energy costs, warning that current inflation gains remain fragile amid global oil market pressures.

In a statement, the Executive Director of CPPE, Muda Yusuf, said the ongoing geopolitical tensions involving the United States, Israel, and Iran are increasing pressure on Nigeria’s economy and require immediate policy response to cushion the impact on households and businesses.

Yusuf noted that while Nigeria’s headline inflation eased slightly to 15.06 per cent in February 2026 from 15.10 per cent in January, the improvement is not yet sustainable. He pointed out that month-on-month inflation rose to 2.01 per cent, while food inflation surged to 4.69 per cent, indicating underlying price pressures remain strong.

He explained that Nigeria’s structural dependence on petrol and diesel for power generation—due to unreliable electricity supply—has intensified the impact of global oil price shocks. According to him, unreliable electricity results in annual economic losses estimated between N7 trillion and N10 trillion, while generator usage exceeds N3.7 trillion annually.

The CPPE emphasised that rising energy costs are directly driving increases in production, transportation, and overall consumer prices across the economy.

To address the situation, the organisation recommended several policy measures, including strengthening domestic refining capacity through stable crude oil supply to local refineries such as the Dangote refinery, and expanding affordable public transportation systems to reduce commuting costs.

It also called for the removal of import duties and taxes on renewable energy equipment such as solar panels, inverters, and batteries to encourage alternative energy adoption. Additionally, CPPE urged the suspension of maritime charges to reduce shipping costs and improve trade efficiency.

Yusuf stressed that improving electricity supply remains the most effective long-term solution, advocating investments in power generation, transmission, and distribution infrastructure, alongside support for decentralised energy systems.

He further advised cautious coordination between monetary and fiscal authorities, warning that premature policy easing could worsen inflationary risks. He added that oil revenue gains should be managed prudently to strengthen foreign exchange reserves and support productive sectors of the economy.
Rising energy costs significantly increase operating expenses for African MSMEs, making urgent policy interventions critical to sustain business growth, affordability, and economic stability.
FG Seeks Investment to Drive Climate-Smart Agriculture
Government Targets Resilient Food Systems Amid Climate Risks
The Federal Government is seeking private investment to scale climate-smart agriculture and strengthen food security amid growing climate challenges.

The Federal Government has called on investors, financial institutions, and development partners to support Nigeria’s push toward climate-smart agriculture, aimed at strengthening food security and boosting sustainable production.

The Minister of Agriculture and Food Security, Abubakar Kyari, made the appeal, highlighting the need for increased private sector participation to unlock the country’s vast agricultural potential and address the growing impact of climate change on farming.

According to the government, agriculture accounts for over 24 per cent of Nigeria’s Gross Domestic Product and employs nearly 70 per cent of the labour force. The country’s diverse agro-ecological zones support the production of key commodities such as rice, maize, cassava, cocoa, sesame, sorghum, and horticultural crops for both domestic consumption and export.

However, Kyari warned that climate-related challenges including drought, erratic rainfall, flooding, and desertification are threatening agricultural productivity and food security. He noted that inadequate access to finance remains a major constraint, with total agricultural credit estimated at N3.4 trillion as of April 2025, representing less than four per cent of the sector’s contribution to GDP.

To address these challenges, the government has introduced several initiatives, including the distribution of solar-powered irrigation pumps, development of climate-resilient seeds, and the National Agricultural Mechanisation Programme, which has delivered over 2,000 tractors and 9,000 farm implements.

Other interventions include the Nigeria Postharvest Systems Transformation Programme to improve storage and reduce losses, and the Special Agro-Industrial Processing Zones initiative to enhance value addition and market access.

The government is also strengthening institutions such as the Bank of Agriculture and the National Agricultural Insurance Corporation to reduce investment risks and attract private capital into the sector.

Kyari identified key investment opportunities in irrigation systems, mechanisation, seed development, cold storage, agro-processing, and agricultural logistics. He reaffirmed the government’s commitment to creating a favourable policy environment through initiatives that promote innovation, land security, and climate resilience.

He urged international investors, particularly from the United Kingdom, to explore partnerships and financing models that can support Nigeria’s agricultural transformation and build more resilient food systems.

Increased investment in climate-smart agriculture creates opportunities for African MSMEs across the agribusiness value chain, from farming and processing to logistics and technology-driven solutions.

NALA Enters Nigeria to Lower Cross-Border Payment Costs for SMEs
Fintech expansion enables faster, cheaper international transactions for businesses and trade.

Global fintech company NALA has secured a licence to operate in Nigeria, enabling direct access to the country’s payment system. The move is expected to reduce transaction costs, improve payment speed, and support MSMEs engaged in cross-border trade.

NALA has obtained regulatory approval to operate in Nigeria, marking a significant step toward improving cross-border payment efficiency for businesses.

The licence allows the company to connect directly to Nigeria’s central payment system, removing intermediaries that typically increase costs and slow down international transactions.

 

Faster, Cheaper Cross-Border Payments

For Nigerian businesses, especially MSMEs, the development addresses long-standing payment challenges.

Key benefits include:

  • Reduced transaction fees

  • Faster payment processing times

  • Direct settlement without multiple intermediaries

  • Improved reliability for international transactions

This is particularly critical for businesses involved in:

  • Import/export trade

  • Freelancing and digital services

  • Supplier and vendor payments

 

Supporting SME Growth and Trade

Many SMEs depend on cross-border payments but face barriers such as:

  • High remittance and transfer costs

  • Delays in receiving funds

  • Limited access to efficient payment infrastructure

NALA’s entry introduces more competition and innovation, helping to improve service quality across the ecosystem.

 

Expanding Fintech Infrastructure in Africa

NALA operates across multiple markets with two key offerings:

  • A consumer remittance platform (US, UK, and Europe)

  • A B2B payments infrastructure (Rafiki) for businesses and financial providers

The company’s Nigeria expansion is driven by:

  • Strong diaspora inflows into the country

  • Growing demand for reliable digital payment solutions

Future plans include:

  • Diaspora-focused savings products

  • Investment solutions tailored to African users

 

Implications for Nigeria’s Payment Ecosystem

Nigeria receives billions of dollars annually in diaspora remittances, making it a key market for fintech innovation.

Expected outcomes:

  • Increased competition among payment providers

  • Lower costs for businesses and individuals

  • Improved financial inclusion and access

SEC, NYSC Launch Investment Education Programme to Protect Young Nigerians and Strengthen MSME Financial Literacy

The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the National Youth Service Corps (NYSC) have signed a Memorandum of Understanding to introduce a Community Development Service (CDS) group focused on investment education across Nigeria.

African Angel Networks Could Deploy Up to $80m Annually to Fund Startups — ABAN CEO
Streamlining investment infrastructure could unlock faster, larger early-stage funding across Africa.

The African Business Angel Network (ABAN) says Africa could deploy up to $80 million annually in early-stage funding if structural barriers are addressed, highlighting the critical role of local investors in powering startups and MSMEs.

African Business Angel Network (ABAN) is pushing to strengthen Africa’s early-stage investment ecosystem, with its CEO, Fadilah Tchoumba, stating that the continent could unlock $50–$80 million annually in angel investment by fixing structural bottlenecks.

Angel investors often the first to fund startups, play a critical role in helping businesses move from idea to scalable venture, particularly before venture capital becomes available.

 

The Backbone of Early-Stage Funding in Africa

Since its founding in 2015, ABAN has grown into a network of:

  • Over 5,000 investors

  • More than 60 angel networks

  • Presence across 37 African countries

Collectively, these networks have:

  • Invested $35 million into over 1,200 startups

  • Supported sectors such as fintech, agritech, health, clean energy, and the creative economy

Its Catalytic Africa fund alone mobilised $2.5 million from 200 investors in one year, showing strong local investor participation.

 

Why Early-Stage Capital Still Falls Short

Despite growing interest, angel investment in Africa remains limited due to structural challenges.

Key constraints include:

  • Slow capital deployment (months vs minutes in developed markets)

  • Complex cross-border regulations and currency barriers

  • Lengthy compliance processes like KYC

  • Limited infrastructure for pooling and deploying funds

As a result, even relatively small investments (e.g. $50,000–$200,000) can take months to complete, slowing startup growth.

 

Fixing the System to Unlock Capital

ABAN is working to address these gaps by building practical solutions.

Current efforts include:

  • Creating Special Purpose Vehicles (SPVs) to pool capital across countries

  • Working with policymakers to simplify regulatory frameworks

  • Partnering with banks to improve cross-border payment infrastructure

  • Streamlining compliance processes without compromising standards

If these challenges are resolved, Africa could significantly increase the speed and volume of early-stage funding.

 

Why Local Investors Matter More Than Ever

A key insight from ABAN is that local capital drives startup ecosystems.

According to Tchoumba:

  • Local investors understand markets better

  • Early-stage funding must come from within before global capital follows

  • Foreign investment typically scales businesses but does not start them

This reinforces the idea that “Africa must fund Africa” to build sustainable innovation ecosystems.

 

Closing the Gap: Liquidity and Exit Opportunities

Another major challenge is the lack of exit opportunities for investors.

ABAN is calling for:

  • Greater participation from local corporates

  • Strategic acquisitions of startups by African companies

  • Stronger collaboration between startups and established businesses

Improving liquidity will make angel investing more attractive and sustainable.

Access to early-stage funding remains one of the biggest barriers for MSMEs and startups. Strengthening angel investment can provide the first critical capital needed to launch and grow businesses, while improved infrastructure will enable faster funding, better support, and stronger pathways to scale across Africa.

Co-creation HUB (CcHUB) has launched GATEWAY, a large-scale programme aimed at training 340,000 Nigerians in in-demand digital skills and connecting them to global gig work, with a strong focus on inclusion and job creation.

Happy Pay Raises $5m to Scale Ad-Supported BNPL Model for SMEs in South Africa
New funding backs a zero-interest payment model designed to boost sales and customer access for merchants.

South African fintech Happy Pay has secured $5 million in seed funding to expand its ad-supported buy-now-pay-later (BNPL) platform, enabling SMEs to increase sales while offering customers interest-free payment flexibility.

Happy Pay has raised $5 million in seed funding to scale its innovative ad-supported BNPL platform, positioning itself as a new model for consumer payments and merchant growth in Africa.

The round was led by Partech, with participation from Futuregrowth Asset Management, 4Di Capital, E4E Africa, Equitable Ventures, and Felix Strategic Investments.

 

A New BNPL Model Built for Affordability and Growth

Unlike traditional BNPL providers, Happy Pay operates a merchant-funded, ad-supported model that removes interest and fees for consumers.

Instead of charging users, the platform:

  • Monetises transactions through merchant and brand participation

  • Uses AI to match products with high-intent shoppers

  • Charges merchants only when a sale is completed

This approach shifts the cost of credit from consumers to value creation within commerce.

  • Over 56% of consumers have used BNPL for online purchases

  • Around 41% have used it for travel bookings

Driving Sales and Customer Acquisition for SMEs

Through its platform, businesses can:

  • Increase conversion rates by offering flexible payments

  • Attract new customers through targeted product recommendations

  • Boost average order values

  • Reduce customer acquisition costs

The company already serves over 600,000 users, creating a growing demand base for partner merchants.

 

AI-Powered Commerce and Expansion Plans

The new funding will support:

  • Growth across online and offline retail channels

  • Further development of its AI and advertising infrastructure

This positions the company to scale as both a payments platform and a customer acquisition engine.

Nigeria Seeks $100bn Investment for Reliable Power Supply – FG
Government calls for phased public-private investments to fix generation, transmission, and distribution gaps
Nigeria needs over $100bn in public and private investment to fix its power sector and achieve stable, round-the-clock electricity supply, according to the Federal Government.

The Federal Government has disclosed that Nigeria requires over $100bn in investments across the power sector value chain to achieve reliable, round-the-clock electricity supply.

The Minister of Power, Adebayo Adelabu, made this known during a press conference in Abuja, where he also apologised to Nigerians over the recent decline in electricity supply and assured that corrective measures are underway.

Adelabu said the investment requirement spans generation, transmission, distribution, and gas infrastructure, noting that while the figure is substantial, it can be achieved through phased funding from both government and private sector players.

He explained that generating an additional 20,000 megawatts of electricity would require about $30bn, while transmission infrastructure would need $20bn. Distribution upgrades and gas pipeline investments are estimated at $25bn and $22bn respectively.

Despite ongoing challenges, the minister highlighted recent improvements in the sector, including a peak generation of 6,001MW recorded in April 2025 and a transmission high of 5,801MW in March 2025. Installed capacity has also increased from 13,000MW in 2023 to 14,400MW in 2025.

He noted that financial interventions, including a N4tn debt restructuring plan, have improved sector liquidity, while cost-reflective tariffs for a segment of consumers have boosted revenue from N1tn in 2023 to N2.3tn in 2025.

Adelabu said infrastructure upgrades have reduced grid collapses and increased transmission capacity from 5,000MW in 2023 to 8,500MW, with a target of 15,000MW in the next three years.

He attributed recent power shortages largely to gas supply constraints affecting most of Nigeria’s power plants, noting that only a few plants currently have firm gas supply agreements.

The minister stressed that achieving stable electricity would require sustained investment, improved coordination across sectors, and stronger private sector participation. He added that the government remains committed to gradually expanding access to reliable power through ongoing reforms and infrastructure upgrades.
Reliable power would reduce energy costs, improve productivity, and enable African MSMEs to scale without relying on expensive generators.
Global Trade Moves 500 Billion Tonnes of “Virtual Water” Annually
Trade and Water Sustainability: How Global Commerce Drives Hidden Water Use

A new World Bank report reveals that global trade moves about 500 billion tonnes of “virtual water” annually the hidden water used in producing goods like food, textiles, and industrial products. While trade can improve water efficiency by shifting production to regions with abundant resources, about 20% of water in traded goods comes from water-stressed areas, posing economic and environmental risks.

Global trade moves an estimated 500 billion tonnes of virtual water each year the hidden water embedded in products like food, textiles, and industrial goods highlighting the growing link between international commerce and water sustainability, according to a new World Bank report.

Virtual water represents the freshwater used during production but not present in the final product. For example, producing a single cup of coffee requires about 150 litres of water, while foods such as sugar, milk, and baked goods add even more, meaning a typical breakfast can use more water than many households consume in a day.

The report notes that global virtual water trade accounts for roughly a quarter of total water use worldwide and is about 50 times the weight of goods shipped annually by sea. Over the last 20 years, these flows have grown by approximately 50%, driven by rising incomes, changing diets, and increasingly complex global supply chains.

Trade can enhance water efficiency by shifting production to regions with abundant water. Agricultural trade alone saves around 500 billion cubic metres of water annually, as crops are often produced where water use is more efficient than in importing countries.

Water-dependent sectors, including agriculture, energy, and industry, support roughly 1.7 billion jobs globally, underlining the economic importance of efficient water management.

However, the report warns of uneven benefits. Around 20% of irrigation water embedded in traded goods comes from water-stressed regions, effectively exporting scarce resources and increasing long-term economic risk for those countries.

The World Bank emphasized that trade policies significantly shape water use, with tariffs, subsidies, and regulations influencing competitiveness in water-intensive sectors such as agriculture, textiles, leather, pulp, paper, and chemicals. Policies that support water-saving technologies, such as drip irrigation, smart meters, and wastewater treatment, are critical for improving efficiency.

Governments and private companies are increasingly acting to reduce water risks. Examples include Australia’s water-efficiency labelling scheme and the European Union’s corporate sustainability due diligence rules, as well as multinational firms setting targets to reduce water use in manufacturing and across supply chains. Trade agreements that integrate environmental commitments, such as EU - Chile and Japan - Australia accords, are also helping align trade with water sustainability.

The World Bank stressed that gradual reforms including phased disclosure of water footprints, supply-chain traceability, and investment in water-efficient technologies are needed to protect producers and consumers, especially in developing countries.

Better management of virtual water flows is vital for safeguarding economic growth, jobs, and global water security in the coming decades, the report concluded.

This matters to African MSMEs because many small and medium enterprises rely on water-intensive sectors such as agriculture, food processing, textiles, and manufacturing. Rising global demand for goods can increase pressure on local water resources, raising production costs and operational risks.
SMEs Risk Losing Customers Without Strong Digital Presence
Digital Presence Becomes Key to Survival and Growth for Nigerian SMEs

Nigerian SMEs risk losing customers and growth opportunities if they fail to establish a strong online presence, combining websites and social media to build credibility, visibility, and competitiveness.

Small and medium enterprises (SMEs) in Nigeria face the risk of losing customers and growth opportunities if they fail to establish a robust online presence, according to digital entrepreneur Adedapo Micheal. He emphasized that in today’s economy, online visibility is essential for business survival, as most customers research businesses online before making purchasing decisions.

Micheal, Chief Executive Officer of Dleading Web Design Ltd, explained that businesses without a digital footprint are effectively invisible to a large segment of potential clients. He stressed that digital presence drives trust, customer acquisition, and revenue growth, serving as a company’s first impression.

SMEs often limit their online growth by relying solely on social media, neglecting to own a website, maintaining inconsistent branding, and lacking a clear understanding of their target audience. Micheal advised that while social media supports engagement, websites are crucial as a business’s “digital property,” providing professional credibility, brand control, and improved search engine visibility.

He also highlighted opportunities within Nigeria’s digital economy, noting the increasing demand for skills such as web design, graphic design, video editing, content creation, and digital marketing. Young Nigerians can earn income by offering online services, working with international clients, building personal brands, and creating digital products. Micheal urged consistency, action, and continuous learning, warning against impatience or expecting quick wealth.

For SMEs beginning their digital transformation, Micheal recommended starting with professional social media pages, basic functional websites, clear branding, consistent content, and listings on platforms like Google Business Profile to improve discoverability.

Looking forward, he urged businesses and individuals to embrace emerging digital trends, including e-commerce growth, AI tools in business and content creation, remote work opportunities, and global freelancing. Micheal framed the digital economy as a platform for expansion beyond geographical limitations and a pathway to competitiveness and economic growth.

Through Dleading Web Design Ltd, Micheal aims to help businesses build strong online identities while empowering young Nigerians to leverage the internet as a tool for opportunity, innovation, and economic advancement rather than mere entertainment.

A strong digital presence enables African SMEs to reach wider markets, attract and retain customers, build credibility, compete globally, and leverage digital skills for sustainable growth and revenue generation.
APPON Lagos Hosts Capacity-Building Programme to Strengthen Public Procurement

The Lagos State Chapter of APPON will host its third Capacity Building Programme on April 2, 2026, bringing together policymakers, procurement professionals, and private sector experts to enhance public procurement systems, promote transparency, and drive efficiency in support of sustainable development.

The Lagos State Chapter of the Association of Public Procurement Practitioners of Nigeria (APPON) is set to convene the third edition of its Capacity Building Programme, aimed at improving public procurement systems to support sustainable development.

Ahead of the programme, APPON Lagos highlighted that the training aims to enhance the professional capacity of procurement officers, foster accountability, and ensure that public procurement processes contribute meaningfully to sustainable economic growth.

The event will also provide a platform for sharing best practices and addressing challenges in procurement frameworks.

Participants are encouraged to register in advance to benefit from the insights and discussions, which are expected to have a lasting impact on procurement practices across Nigeria. SMEs Turn to Solar as Fixr Offers Affordable Energy Solutions
Innovative financing drives solar adoption for Nigerian SMEs
Fixr is helping Nigerian SMEs adopt solar energy through accessible financing and turnkey solutions, providing a reliable alternative to costly diesel generators and erratic grid power.

Fixr is helping Nigerian small businesses transition to solar energy as a way to mitigate rising diesel costs and unreliable electricity supply, offering financing solutions that make adoption more accessible. A survey of over 1,000 micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs) in Abuja and Lagos found that while 83.3% of businesses rely on grid electricity, only 17.6% receive it consistently, forcing many to rely on costly diesel generators.

Historically, diesel generators provided a relatively dependable fallback. However, the 2023 removal of fuel subsidies and recent volatility in global oil markets have driven diesel prices from around N774 to over N1,100 within three weeks. For SMEs operating on thin margins, these costs are more than a financial inconvenience they threaten business survival.

Energy disruptions also reduce productivity and limit commercial opportunities. While businesses could theoretically pass costs onto consumers, Nigeria’s highly price-sensitive market often makes this unfeasible, forcing firms to absorb energy costs internally.

Solar energy has emerged as a viable alternative, offering predictable power that neither the grid nor diesel generators can guarantee. However, high upfront costs have historically limited adoption among MSMEs. To address this, Fixr provides innovative financing models, including pay-as-you-go and lease-to-own options, allowing businesses to spread the cost over six to twelve months. Eligible businesses make an initial payment of at least 20%, with financing partners covering the remaining 80%.

Fixr conducts a detailed energy load analysis, often on-site, to ensure accurate system sizing, followed by a credit assessment. Installation is typically completed within 24 hours, minimising operational downtime. Customers also receive access to trained technicians for maintenance and comprehensive warranties up to 25 years for solar panels and five years for batteries.
