However, the IMF cautioned that structural challenges persist. These include uneven distribution of economic growth across regions, ongoing pressure to generate sufficient employment opportunities, and a widening trade imbalance driven by higher import levels relative to exports.

The report underscores the need for continued reforms and targeted policies to ensure that growth is inclusive and sustainable over the long term.

Stable economic growth in Morocco creates opportunities for MSMEs in tourism, manufacturing, and services, while highlighting the need for inclusive policies that support small business expansion across Africa.
Rising Diesel Costs Strain South African Farmers Ahead of Harvest
Fuel shortages and price spikes threaten production as global tensions disrupt supply chains
South African farmers are grappling with rising diesel costs and supply challenges ahead of harvest, driven by global energy disruptions.

Farmers in South Africa are facing mounting pressure from rising diesel costs and supply uncertainties as the harvest season approaches, raising concerns about production and profitability.

Derek Mathews, a farmer in North West Province, said the cost and availability of fuel have become critical challenges as he prepares to harvest sunflower and maize crops on his 1,700-hectare farm. He noted that while prices have surged, securing adequate diesel supplies remains an even greater concern.

The situation has been exacerbated by global energy disruptions linked to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, which has affected key transit routes such as the Strait of Hormuz. As a result, both fuel and fertiliser costs have increased significantly.

Although South African authorities have assured that fuel stocks are sufficient in the short term, farmers report ongoing challenges, including high demand, logistical bottlenecks, and instances of diesel hoarding.

Data from agricultural body AgriSA shows that nearly half of surveyed farmers have struggled to source diesel, with some only able to secure limited quantities. Industry stakeholders say uncertainty around supply has also triggered panic buying.

Diesel prices have risen sharply in recent weeks. Farmers who purchased fuel at around 18 rand per litre earlier in the year are now facing prices as high as 24 rand, with further increases expected. Government projections indicate that wholesale diesel prices could rise to nearly 26 rand per litre.

In response, the government has announced a temporary reduction in the fuel levy for April to cushion the impact of rising prices. However, farmers warn that sustained high fuel costs could significantly erode already thin profit margins.

Despite the challenges, analysts say South Africa’s recent favourable agricultural season may help stabilise food prices in the short term. However, prolonged increases in fuel costs could disrupt this balance and impact future production cycles.

For farmers like Mathews, the outlook remains uncertain, with concerns that continued cost pressures could make staple crop production economically unviable.
Higher fuel costs increase production and logistics expenses, impacting agri-based MSMEs and potentially driving up food prices across the continent.
Zambia Cuts Fuel Taxes to Cushion Rising Energy Costs
VAT zero-rated and excise duty suspended on fuel imports for three months
Zambia has removed VAT and suspended excise duty on fuel imports for three months to reduce the impact of rising global energy prices.

Zambia’s cabinet has approved the zero-rating of Value Added Tax (VAT) and the suspension of excise duty on petrol and diesel imports for a three-month period starting April 1.

The government said the temporary measures are aimed at easing the impact of rising global energy prices on the country’s economy, particularly amid ongoing volatility in international oil markets.

By reducing the tax burden on fuel imports, authorities hope to stabilise domestic fuel prices, lower transportation costs, and ease pressure on businesses and households.

The move comes as many countries grapple with the economic fallout of higher energy costs, which have driven up inflation and operating expenses across multiple sectors.

Zambia’s intervention is part of broader efforts to shield the economy from external shocks while maintaining stability in key sectors reliant on fuel.
Lower fuel costs can reduce operating and logistics expenses for MSMEs, helping businesses manage inflationary pressures and maintain profitability.
World Bank Endorses Nigeria’s Economic Reforms, Boosting Growth Prospects
Global lender commends policy direction, urges stronger oversight and state-level implementation

The World Bank has reaffirmed its support for Nigeria’s ongoing economic reforms, noting steady progress toward recovery. Speaking in Abuja, Country Director Mathew Verghis highlighted the importance of legislative oversight, state-level implementation, and accountability in driving successful development outcomes. He also emphasised the Bank’s continued focus on women’s empowerment and social development programmes as key pillars of long-term growth.

Nigeria’s economic reform agenda has received renewed backing from the World Bank, which described recent policy efforts as a sign of steady progress toward recovery and long-term stability.

Speaking in Abuja during a meeting with the Senate Committee on Capital Market, the World Bank Country Director for Nigeria, Mathew Verghis, commended the country’s reform direction and reiterated the institution’s commitment to supporting its implementation. He noted that Nigeria is increasingly being seen as an example for other countries pursuing similar economic adjustments.

Verghis emphasised that strong legislative oversight remains essential to ensuring accountability and effectiveness in development projects, particularly those funded by international partners. He encouraged lawmakers to maintain a constructive approach in supervising such initiatives to maximise impact.

He also highlighted a strategic shift in how World Bank-supported projects are executed in Nigeria. While the Federal Government remains the borrower, implementation has increasingly been decentralised to state governments, reflecting Nigeria’s governance structure and improving delivery outcomes. According to him, only states that meet specific eligibility criteria are able to access funding, creating an incentive for better governance and project readiness.

In addition, the World Bank reaffirmed its focus on social and inclusive development, particularly programmes aimed at empowering women and improving early childhood development. Verghis described women’s economic participation as a critical driver of sustainable growth, noting that several initiatives are underway to expand access to opportunities, education, and support systems.

Lawmakers also signalled interest in deepening collaboration with the World Bank, with plans to hold further technical sessions to better understand ongoing programmes and strengthen their role in oversight.

The development underscores continued international confidence in Nigeria’s reform trajectory, even as the country navigates broader economic challenges.
Stronger World Bank backing for Nigeria’s reforms can improve access to funding, create more stable business conditions, and open up new growth opportunities for African MSMEs.
Nigeria Risks Losing Regional Trade Dominance Without Deep Seaports
Stakeholders warn that outdated port infrastructure could divert cargo traffic to neighboring countries.

Industry leaders, policymakers, and former President Goodluck Jonathan have cautioned that Nigeria may lose its dominance in regional maritime trade if it fails to invest in modern deep seaports. Despite accounting for over 60% of West Africa’s GDP, only about 25% of regional cargo currently passes through Nigerian ports. Experts stress that without deeper ports capable of handling larger vessels, cargo may increasingly be routed through countries like Togo and Côte d’Ivoire. Ongoing projects such as Ibom and Agge Deep Seaports offer promise, but gaps in infrastructure, connectivity, and digital systems remain critical challenges.

Former Heads of State, port administrators, government officials, state governors, and maritime industry leaders have warned that Nigeria risks losing its position as a regional trade hub if it fails to urgently invest in deep-sea port infrastructure. The warning came during the maiden International Deep Seaport Investment Forum (IDSIF) held in Lagos.

Former President Goodluck Jonathan cautioned that without modern deep-sea ports, large vessels could bypass Nigerian ports entirely, forcing cargo to be diverted through neighbouring countries before reaching domestic markets. He highlighted ongoing and proposed projects, including the Ibom Deep Seaport in Akwa Ibom State and the Agge Deep Seaport in Bayelsa State, as steps in the right direction.

Jonathan also called for collaboration among government, private sector, and international maritime investors, noting the importance of integrating financial institutions into maritime investment strategies.

Nigeria Port Authority (NPA) Managing Director Dr. Abubakar Dantsoho, represented by Ikechukwu Onyemekara, said Nigeria currently handles only 25% of regional cargo despite accounting for over 60% of West Africa’s GDP. He stressed the need to transition from ageing legacy ports to modern deep-sea infrastructure, highlighting opportunities in hinterland connectivity, digital integration, green energy, and future port models.

The NPA noted that the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) would increase cargo volumes, boost export trade, and enhance Nigeria’s potential as a regional hub. New ports must serve as primary “mother-ship hubs,” with legacy ports functioning as feeders to capture both transshipment and domestic cargo.

Minister of Marine and Blue Economy Adegboyega Oyetola, represented by NPA’s Seyi Iyawe, emphasised the importance of green, automated ports and efficient cargo movement, highlighting the launch of the Nigerian Single Window (NSW) system.

Former NDDC Managing Director Timi Alaibe stressed aligning Nigeria’s coastal and maritime infrastructure with global standards, pointing to successful international models such as Abu Dhabi Ports, DP World, and Adani Ports.

Asu Beks, CEO of Maritime Media Limited, convener of IDSIF, noted that while Nigeria has a long coastline and existing ports like Apapa, Tin Can, and Port Harcourt, evolving global shipping trends favour deeper ports capable of handling larger vessels, leaving many of Nigeria’s river ports less competitive.

The forum concluded that urgent investment, regional collaboration, and adoption of global best practices are critical for Nigeria to maintain its strategic position in West Africa’s maritime trade.
Without efficient deep seaports, African MSMEs especially exporters and importers face higher logistics costs, longer delivery times, and reduced competitiveness in regional and global markets.
Cotton Value Addition Could Drive Nigeria’s Inclusive Growth
Cotton Value Addition Set to Boost Jobs and Industrial Growth in West Africa

Major investments in Nigeria’s cotton-to-textile value chain could create 500,000 jobs, empower women and youth, and transform the region’s raw cotton exports into higher-value textile and garment products.

Stakeholders at the just-concluded 14th World Trade Organization (WTO) Ministerial Conference have called for major investments in the cotton, textiles, and garments (CTG) sector to transform economic development in Nigeria and West Africa. The conference brought together ministers, international agency heads, development finance institutions, and private sector partners to explore opportunities for Africa to move up the cotton value chain.

Cotton, primarily grown in northern Nigeria, is a major cash crop but has faced challenges including poor infrastructure, insecurity, and competition from cheap or smuggled polyester fabrics. Currently, about 98% of Nigeria’s and West Africa’s cotton is exported as raw fiber, despite the region’s potential for processing it into textiles and garments.

The event marked the launch of a new phase under the Partenariat pour le Coton (PPC) initiative, focused on mobilising investment to accelerate cotton-to-textile-and-garment transformation. WTO Director-General Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala highlighted that a $5 billion investment could create around 500,000 direct jobs across manufacturing, transportation, and fashion design, with women and youth set to benefit.

The conference also unveiled the Africa Textile Invest platform, designed to provide investors with a single access point for country data, industrial zones, and investment opportunities. UNIDO Managing Director Gunther Berger reaffirmed the agency’s support for industrial capacity development, while ITC Executive Director Pamela Coke-Hamilton stressed that Africa’s cotton future lies in value addition. Afreximbank President George Elombi pledged financial backing, envisioning a shift from raw cotton exports to textile and clothing exports within 15–20 years, boosting income, employment, and livelihoods across the continent.

Investing in cotton value addition creates opportunities for small and medium enterprises across the textile and garment sectors, driving local job creation, industrial growth, and inclusive economic development.
Nigeria’s Oil Reserves Dip as Gas Resources Rise
Nigeria’s Gas Reserves Surge Amid Slight Oil Decline

Nigeria’s oil reserves fell slightly to 37.01 billion barrels in 2026, while gas reserves rose to 215.19 trillion cubic feet, signaling a shift toward a gas-focused energy strategy aligned with the Federal Government’s “Decade of Gas” initiative.

Nigeria’s oil reserves recorded a slight decline in 2026, while gas resources expanded, reflecting a gradual shift in the country’s hydrocarbon profile amid sustained production and exploration success. The Nigerian Upstream Petroleum Regulatory Commission (NUPRC) released the official national petroleum reserves position on Wednesday in Abuja, reporting total oil and condensate reserves at 37.01 billion barrels and total gas reserves at 215.19 trillion cubic feet.

The NUPRC’s Chief Executive, Oritsemeyiwa Eyesan, explained that the marginal 0.74% decrease in oil reserves is due to sustained production and limited new discoveries, while gas reserves rose by 2.21% because of fresh discoveries and improved technical assessments. The reserves life index stands at 59 years for oil and 85 years for gas, indicating the expected duration of resources at current production levels.

Eyesan highlighted that the Petroleum Industry Act, 2021, has strengthened regulatory oversight and created a more conducive investment climate, which supports the growth of both oil and gas reserves. She noted that the increase in gas resources aligns with the Federal Government’s “Decade of Gas” initiative, emphasizing the country’s transition toward cleaner fuel sources.

Despite the slight dip in oil reserves, Nigeria remains among Africa’s top crude holders, while its expanding gas reserves reinforce its position as one of the continent’s leading gas-rich nations. The commission reaffirmed its commitment to enhancing upstream performance, ensuring long-term resource sustainability, and supporting shared economic prosperity.

Nigeria’s growing gas reserves and sustained oil production present opportunities for African MSMEs in energy, manufacturing, and supply chains to access more stable and diversified fuel and industrial inputs, supporting business growth and regional trade.
NAFDAC Flags Counterfeit Infant Food in Lagos, Raises Food Safety Concerns
Fake Cerelac products with altered expiry dates highlight growing risks in Nigeria’s food supply chain.

NAFDAC has warned of counterfeit Cerelac Mixed Fruits and Wheat products circulating in Lagos, urging businesses and consumers to remain vigilant as concerns over food safety and supply chain integrity intensify.

The National Agency for Food and Drug Administration and Control (NAFDAC) has issued a public alert over the circulation of suspected counterfeit Cerelac Mixed Fruits and Wheat in Lagos State. The warning follows a complaint from Nestlé Nigeria and raises serious concerns about the safety of infant nutrition products in the market.

 

Counterfeit Product Detected in Lagos

NAFDAC’s investigation traced the fake product to a retail outlet in Okota, Lagos, where it was found being sold as a Nestlé Spain-manufactured item. Preliminary findings revealed irregularities, including an unusual odour and an altered expiry date, suggesting possible contamination and deliberate mislabelling.

The agency has since removed the affected products from circulation as part of its containment efforts.

 

How to Identify the Fake Product

NAFDAC highlighted a key distinction between genuine and counterfeit packaging. The fake version uses a hyphen (-) to separate the date marking, while the authentic product uses a slash (/). This subtle difference is now a critical verification detail for distributors and retailers.

 

Rising Concerns Over Counterfeit Goods

This incident is part of a broader pattern of counterfeit food and pharmaceutical products in Nigeria. Recent enforcement actions include:

  • Alerts on fake infant formulas such as Aptamil and Cow & Gate

  • Discovery of falsified medicines, including unregistered drug batches

  • Seizure of counterfeit malaria drugs worth over ₦1.2 billion

  • Destruction of expired medical products valued above ₦15 billion

These developments highlight ongoing risks within the supply chain, particularly for essential consumer goods.

 

Regulatory Response and Market Impact

NAFDAC has directed nationwide surveillance to remove counterfeit products and urged businesses to source goods only from authorised suppliers. Distributors, retailers, and healthcare providers are also encouraged to report suspicious products through official channels.

The agency emphasised that counterfeit infant foods pose severe health risks, particularly for vulnerable populations.

Product authenticity and quality control are critical for business credibility and consumer trust. MSMEs in retail and distribution must strengthen supplier verification and compliance to avoid reputational damage, regulatory penalties, and potential health risks to customers.
Afreximbank Anchors $2.5bn Deal to Boost Dangote Refinery Expansion and Africa’s Industrial Growth
Major financing strengthens one of Africa’s largest industrial projects, with implications for energy security, job creation, and MSME development.

Afreximbank has underwritten $2.5 billion of a $4 billion syndicated loan to support the Dangote Petroleum Refinery & Petrochemicals, enabling expansion plans, stabilising finances, and reinforcing investor confidence in Africa’s industrial capabilities.

The African Export-Import Bank (Afreximbank) has underwritten $2.5 billion of a $4 billion senior syndicated loan for Dangote Petroleum Refinery & Petrochemicals. Arranged alongside Access Bank as co-mandated lead arrangers, the five-year facility aims to refinance existing debt, optimise capital structure, and support the refinery’s next phase of expansion.

Scaling Africa’s Largest Refinery

Owned by Dangote Industries Limited and led by Aliko Dangote, the refinery currently processes 650,000 barrels per day, making it the largest in Africa. The new financing will enable capacity expansion to 1.4 million barrels per day, potentially positioning the refinery as the largest globally.

George Elombi of Afreximbank emphasised that the bank’s support reflects a strategic commitment to African-owned industrial projects. Investments of this scale are expected to create jobs, boost government revenues, and enhance long-term economic resilience. Since 2015, Afreximbank has committed about $15 billion to Dangote Group projects, highlighting a deep and sustained partnership.

 

Financial and Operational Support

Beyond the syndicated loan, Afreximbank has provided:

  • A $1 billion working capital facility since production began in February 2024.

  • Advisory services on initiatives such as local-currency crude transactions to reduce foreign exchange reliance.

The syndicated loan drew participation from both African and international financial institutions, signalling strong investor confidence in the refinery as a strategic asset for energy security and industrialisation.

 

Driving Industrial Expansion and Value Chains

Dangote Industries is also advancing expansion across other operations, including:

  • A $400 million agreement with XCMG Construction Machinery Co., Ltd. for operational growth.

  • Plans to increase polypropylene production from 900,000 to 2.4 million metric tonnes annually.

For African MSMEs, a stronger domestic refining and petrochemical sector could reduce fuel import costs, stabilise supply, lower operational expenses, and unlock new opportunities in manufacturing and packaging value chains.

Enhanced local refining capacity strengthens energy supply and reduces costs for small businesses. Expanded petrochemical output can stimulate manufacturing, create new markets, and generate ripple effects that support MSME growth across multiple sectors, reinforcing Africa’s path to industrial self-reliance.

FG Launches OVP-GenU Secretariat to Connect 20 Million Youths to Jobs and Skills
New coordination hub aims to align skills development with industry needs and drive nationwide employment opportunities.

Nigeria has launched the OVP-Generation Unlimited Secretariat to link 20 million youths to skills, education, and jobs by 2030, strengthening workforce development and supporting MSME growth through coordinated public-private partnerships.

The Federal Government has inaugurated the Office of the Vice-President–Generation Unlimited (OVP-GenU) Secretariat to accelerate efforts to tackle youth unemployment. The initiative, supported by UNICEF, aims to connect 20 million young Nigerians to skills, education, and livelihood opportunities by 2030 through a coordinated public-private-youth partnership model.

 

A Central Hub for Youth Development

The new secretariat will serve as a national coordination platform, aligning stakeholders across government, private sector, and development organisations. By focusing on transparency, accessibility, and execution, the initiative aims to ensure that skills development programmes translate into real employment outcomes.

A key priority is aligning training with actual industry and community needs, improving the relevance of skills acquired by young people.

 

Linking Skills to Local Economies

The programme is integrated into the broader Renewed Hope Ward Development Programme, which targets economic growth across Nigeria’s 8,809 wards. By connecting skills development directly to grassroots economies, the initiative is expected to drive more inclusive and localised job creation.

This approach ensures that training is not just theoretical but tied to real economic activities within communities.

 

Data-Driven Skills and Education Planning

A major component of the initiative is a planned national skills census, which will provide data to guide vocational training and education policies. The Federal Ministry of Education is expected to adopt this framework to better align learning systems with labour market demands.

This data-driven approach aims to close the gap between education and employability.

 

Leveraging Existing MSME Platforms

The programme will also build on existing government initiatives, including National MSME Clinics, using shared hubs to expand access to skills training and enterprise development. This integration is expected to scale impact and improve reach across the country.

 

Driving Jobs, Productivity, and Growth

By strengthening coordination and expanding access to opportunities, the initiative positions Nigeria as a leader in youth-focused economic development in Africa. It is designed to not only reduce unemployment but also enhance productivity and support long-term economic growth.

A more skilled workforce improves productivity and reduces hiring challenges for MSMEs. By expanding access to job-ready talent, the initiative strengthens the talent pipeline small businesses need to scale and compete effectively.
Rwanda Secures $250 Million IMF Funding to Support Economic Stability and Growth
New financing will back Rwanda’s reforms and mitigate rising costs in key sectors, boosting resilience for businesses and entrepreneurs.

Rwanda and the International Monetary Fund (IMF) have agreed on a $250 million extended credit facility to support economic policies, manage rising costs, and sustain strong growth in the East African nation.

Rwanda has reached an agreement with the IMF for a 38-month extended credit facility worth $250 million, aimed at strengthening the country’s economic policies and reforms. The IMF’s executive board is scheduled to review and approve the agreement in June, according to Albert Touna Mama, IMF Mission Chief for Rwanda.

Supporting Economic Stability

Finance Minister Yusuf Murangwa highlighted that part of the funding will address rising fuel and fertiliser costs triggered by the Iran conflict. He noted that government intervention is crucial to prevent sharp increases in fertiliser prices, which could negatively affect agriculture and food production.

Rwanda’s economy has shown robust growth, with projections indicating annual GDP expansion exceeding 7% through 2028. The new IMF facility is expected to bolster fiscal stability, maintain macroeconomic balance, and support the implementation of ongoing structural reforms.

 

Implications for Entrepreneurs and MSMEs

The facility will help maintain predictable input costs for key sectors such as agriculture and energy, ensuring that businesses and MSMEs can plan and operate with reduced financial uncertainty. By strengthening the overall economy, the funding also creates a more conducive environment for investment, innovation, and enterprise growth.

Access to stable financing and supportive government policies ensures predictable costs for inputs like fuel and fertiliser. This allows small businesses and entrepreneurs to plan growth, manage expenses, and expand operations, contributing to broader economic resilience and opportunity in Rwanda.
Google for Startups Accelerator Africa 2026 (3-Month Hybrid Program)

Applications are now being accepted for the Google for startups Accelerator Africa 2026, a three-month hybrid accelerator program aimed to promote growth-stage digital firms across Africa. The curriculum brings together the best of Google's products, people, and technology to assist founders in overcoming crucial technological obstacles and scaling long-term, high-impact enterprises.


About the Accelerator.

The Google for startups Accelerator Africa is part of a global network of accelerator programs that promote high-potential entrepreneurs globally. Each cohort brings together 10 to 15 entrepreneurs for an intensive program that includes virtual and in-person workshops, one-on-one technical help, group learning, and concentrated sprint projects.


The program is designed for founders who are developing scalable, profoundly technical solutions, with a focus on addressing real-world business and engineering difficulties.



Programme Structure

Participating startups collaborate with Google experts and industry specialists to address their most critical technical and strategic issues. Founders identify their top challenges at the outset of the program and are assigned to relevant specialists who provide hands-on advice throughout the accelerator.


In addition to customised help, the program offers professional deep dives and workshops on product design, customer acquisition, leadership development, and long-term growth strategy.


Google Product Benefits:

Eligible applicants can obtain early access to specific Google AI products through the Early Access Program and Trusted Tester possibilities.

Startups can also apply for Google Cloud credits to help them create and scale their products.

Participants will also receive free access to Cloud TPUs to help expedite open-source machine learning research.


Benefits

The Google for Startups Accelerator Africa provides equity-free support for the duration of the programme.


Google teams provide focused mentoring to startups, strategic help for company and product strategy, and hands-on technical project partnerships to address hard engineering challenges.

Participants also get access to Google's network of industry experts, unique invites to Google-hosted technical bootcamps when they are available, early access to new AI tools, and product credits to help them grow and innovate.


Who Should apply?

The program is open to growth-stage startups that have demonstrated clear market traction and are developing scalable products or services with large total addressable markets and defensible growth models.

Ideal candidates are highly technical, preferably using technologies like machine learning and artificial intelligence.

Applicants must demonstrate a strong commitment by their CTO and/or core technical staff to actively participate in program sessions and technical engagements.

Google welcomes applications from all qualified entrepreneurs and is dedicated to creating diverse and inclusive accelerator cohorts throughout Africa.

Application Timeline

  • Applications close on March 18, 2026.

  • The programme kicks off in April 2026.

  • Graduation takes place in June 2026.
Call for Proposals: Global Collaboration Action on Climate and Health in Sub-Saharan Africa

Funding Opportunity: Research & Innovation Action under the Horizon Europe / Global Health EDCTP3 framework

Deadline (Stage 1): 4 March 2026

Deadline (Stage 2 – full proposal): 17 September 2026

Total Indicative Budget: €25 million (≈ €5 million per project, ~5 projects expected)


Who can apply?

Eligible applicants must establish a multi-partner research cooperative that includes:


✔ Three independent legal entities from different eligible countries

 ✔ One entity from an EU Member State or affiliated nation (member of the EDCTP Association) 

✔ One entity from a Sub-Saharan African country member of the EDCTP Association.


Universities, research institutes, non-governmental organisations (NGOs), health authorities, corporate sector partners, and foreign organisations can all be part of a consortium. Projects should show scientific brilliance, an implementation strategy, and measurable impact.


Key Dates & Process

Two-Stage Submission Model

Stage 1 – Short Proposal:

  • Deadline: 4 March 2026

  • Submit a concise concept and consortium overview.

Stage 2 – Full Proposal:

  • Deadline: 17 September 2026

  • Invited applicants submit detailed research plans, work packages, budgets, and partnership contributions.

Consortia are strongly encouraged to include financial or in-kind contributions from partners, enhancing impact and resource leverage.

Priority Themes

Proposals should align with themes such as:

  • Climate-sensitive infectious diseases (e.g., vector- and water-borne).

  • Adaptation of health systems for climate resilience.

  • Evidence-based public health interventions.

  • Community health engagement and primary care adaptation.

  • Translating research into policy and practice.

  • Multidisciplinary approaches integrating health, climate, nutrition, WASH, and social dimensions.

Projects are expected to contribute actionable knowledge that enhances health outcomes for vulnerable populations in SSA and supports policy development and implementation.

How to Apply

  1. Visit the EU Funding & Tenders Portal

    • Search for HORIZON-JU-GH-EDCTP3-2026-02-CH-01-two-stage.

  2. Review eligibility, call conditions, and submission requirements on the official portal.

  3. Prepare and submit Stage 1 short proposal by 4 March 2026.

  4. If invited, develop the Stage 2 full application for submission by 17 September 2026.

Proposals should clearly define research objectives, methodologies, impact pathways, and partnership roles.
Call for Applicants: iF Social Impact Prize 2026 (€100,000 Grant)

Applications are now open for the 2026 iF Social Impact Prize, a global initiative launched by iF Design to encourage projects that make a significant contribution to societal improvement. If your work addresses one or more of the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals and is related to design, you are qualified to apply.


Every year, iF Design awards a total of EUR 100,000 to outstanding social impact initiatives that exhibit great societal relevance, sustainability impact, and design-led solutions.


About the iF Social Impact Prize.

The iF Social Impact Prize honours and promotes proven programs that utilise design to address social, environmental, and economic issues. The prize is accessible to organisations, social entrepreneurs, non-governmental organisations (NGOs), and initiatives with projects that are either completed or in process.

Projects may be submitted a maximum of twice with the same concept. Student conceptions and pure theoretical ideas are ineligible.


Why This Matters

Across the globe, social concerns necessitate realistic, well-designed solutions that can scale and persist. The iF Social Impact Prize promotes programs that combine impact and design excellence, providing awareness, credibility, and financial support to expand their reach and effectiveness.


Eligible Focus Areas

Submitted projects must align with at least one of the following UN Sustainable Development Goals:

  1. No poverty

  2. Zero hunger

  3. Good health and well-being

  4. Quality education

  5. Gender equality

  6. Clean water and sanitation

  7. Affordable and clean energy

  8. Decent work and economic growth

  9. Industry, innovation and infrastructure

  10. Reduced inequalities

  11. Sustainable cities and communities

  12. Responsible consumption and production

  13. Climate action

  14. Life below water

  15. Life on land

Eligibility Criteria

  • Eligible projects must already be established and demonstrate a clear link to design, which is one of the five evaluation criteria.

  • Projects must address at least one UN Sustainable Development Goal.

  • Student concepts are not accepted, and the same project may only be submitted twice.

Benefits

  • Projects selected for the iF Social Impact Prize will receive a share of the total EUR 100,000 support fund.

  • In addition, winning projects benefit from international visibility through publication on the iF Design website and presentation to a global design community, the design-interested public, and the media.

  • Each selected project also receives an official iF Social Impact Prize winner certificate.

How to Apply
Applicants are encouraged to review full guidelines and submit their projects via the official iF Social Impact Prize platform.

For more information and application details, visit the official webpage of the iF Social Impact Prize.
Katapult Africa Accelerator Program 2026 ($150,000–$500,000 Investment for Impact Startups)

African startups developing solutions for food systems, agriculture, and climate resilience now have a tremendous potential to expand their influence. Katapult Africa has started applications for its 2026 Accelerator Program, which provides 90 days of intensive support and $150,000 to $500,000 in financing to high-potential impact-driven enterprises.


As global population growth increases pressure on food systems and the environment, Katapult Africa is supporting startups who are leveraging technology to reimagine agriculture, food security, and climate-smart solutions across the continent.


About the Katapult Africa Accelerator

The Katapult Africa Accelerator is a 90-day intensive program designed to help African startups strengthen growth strategy, improve investor readiness, and scale measurable impact.

The program combines:

  • Hands-on workshops

  • Expert-led learning sessions

  • Access to Katapult’s global investor and partner network

  • Tailored impact and business support

Selected startups also receive direct investment ranging from $150,000 to $500,000, enabling them to move faster from innovation to real-world impact.

Who Should Apply

This accelerator is open to innovative African startups working sectors such as:

AgriTech and food systems

  • Climate-smart agriculture

  • Sustainable food production

  • Climate resilience and adaptation

  • Environmental and impact-driven technologies

Startups should demonstrate:

  • Strong founding teams

  • Scalable business models

  • Clear impact on food systems or climate challenges

  • Readiness to grow and engage investors

Both early-growth and scaling startups are encouraged to apply

Key Benefits

Participants in the Katapult Africa Accelerator will receive:

Investment Funding
Selected startups receive $150,000–$500,000 in investment to support growth and scale.

Growth & Investor Readiness Support
Structured sessions focused on business growth, fundraising strategy, governance, and impact measurement.

Global Network Access
Direct connections to Katapult’s ecosystem of investors, corporates, impact partners, and mentors.

Expert Mentorship & Learning
Intensive workshops and one-on-one guidance from experienced operators and impact investors.

Program Duration

  • 90 days of intensive acceleration, workshops, and learning sessions.

Applicants are advised to prepare clear information on:

  • Their solution and market

  • Impact on food systems or climate challenges

  • Business model and traction

  • Team and long-term vision

Why This Opportunity Matters

Africa sits at the center of global food and climate challenges and also at the heart of innovation. The Katapult Africa Accelerator provides not just capital, but the strategic guidance and global exposure African startups need to build solutions that can transform food systems and reduce environmental impact at scale.
STARTS Prize 2026—€30,000 Award for Creative Tech and Innovation Projects (Open to African Innovators)

The STARTS Prize 2026, a prestigious innovation competition supported by the European Commission, is now accepting applications from innovators, researchers, creative professionals, and teams around the world (including Africa) to submit projects at the intersection of science, technology, and the arts.

What is the STARTS Prize?

The STARTS Prize 2026 recognises pioneering collaborations and initiatives that creatively combine science, technology, innovation, and artistic practice to address real-world issues in society, industry, sustainability, and beyond. The award is part of the European Commission's STARTS initiative, which encourages innovation via artistic thought and technological discovery.

Who Can Apply

This open call is global and inclusive — innovators, creators, researchers, companies, teams, and interdisciplinary groups are eligible to submit projects regardless of nationality. There is no geographic limitation, and entries from African innovators and teams are encouraged.

Projects should:

  • Combine technology and arts or creative practice.

  • Demonstrate innovation and impact.

  • Address challenges in fields such as green tech, material research, AI, smart cities, sustainability, HCI, biotech, and citizen empowerment.

  • Show potential to influence perceptions or applications of technology in society and industry.

Prizes & Benefits

  • €20,000 Grand Prize (per category) — top winning projects receive significant cash rewards.

  • Winners and selected projects are showcased at the Ars Electronica Festival 2026 — a major global event for innovation, creativity, and technology.

  • Exposure to international networks of researchers, industry partners, cultural institutions, and funders.

Note: Previous regional editions like STARTS Prize Africa offered a combined €30,000 prize pool with distinct award categories for African innovators.

How to Apply

  1. Visit the official STARTS Prize 2026 open call page:
    https://starts.eu/opportunities/starts-prize-2026/

  2. Review submission guidelines and eligibility criteria on the portal.

  3. Prepare your project submission, including a description, visuals or documentation supporting innovation and impact.

  4. Submit online before the deadline (4 March 2026, 23:59 CET).

Equity-Free £5,000 Funding for African Startups

The Bridge Seed Global Impact Accelerator 2026 is now accepting applications from young African founders (18–35) building early-stage, impact-driven businesses. This three-month accelerator provides £5,000 in equity-free funding, hands-on mentorship, founder training, and access to global networks, without taking any ownership in your startup.

 

What This Opportunity Offers

Bridge Seed Global’s Impact Accelerator is designed to help founders turn promising ideas into commercially viable, scalable businesses that address real social and economic challenges, especially youth unemployment and limited access to early-stage capital across Africa.

 

Significance for African Entrepreneurs

If you’re building an impact-focused startup and need capital, guidance, and exposure without dilution, this program helps you:

  • Secure non-dilutive pre-seed funding

  • Strengthen your business model and growth strategy

Benefits for Selected Startup

  • £5,000 equity-free pre-seed grant (no ownership stake required)

  • World-class mentorship and founder-focused training

Teach For Nigeria Fellowship Program Cohort 2026

Applications are now open for the Teach For Nigeria Fellowship Program Cohort 2026, a transformative leadership initiative that empowers passionate young Nigerians to teach in under-resourced communities and drive long-term improvements in education quality and equity.

The Teach For Nigeria Fellowship is built on the belief that every child deserves access to excellent education, regardless of their background or circumstances. Through this program, emerging leaders gain hands-on experience addressing educational challenges, developing as educators, and contributing meaningfully to community development.

Why This Matters

Nigeria faces persistent educational disparities, with millions of children lacking access to quality learning opportunities. The Teach For Nigeria Fellowship equips committed early-career leaders with the training, coaching, and community support needed to improve learning outcomes, strengthen classrooms, and inspire future generations. By investing in talented individuals who choose to teach where the need is highest, the program helps build a stronger education system and fosters leaders who will influence positive change across sectors.

Who Can Apply

Applications are now open for the Teach For Nigeria Fellowship Program Cohort 2026, a transformative leadership initiative that empowers passionate young Nigerians to teach in under-resourced communities and drive long-term improvements in education quality and equity.

The Teach For Nigeria Fellowship is built on the belief that every child deserves access to excellent education, regardless of their background or circumstances. Who Can Apply

The Teach For Nigeria Fellowship is open to talented Nigerian citizens who are passionate about education and leadership. Eligible candidates should meet the following requirements:

Benefits of the Fellowship

  • Ongoing coaching, mentoring, and peer support throughout the Fellowship

Teach For Nigeria Fellows join a growing network of change agents dedicated to improving education and expanding opportunity for all children. Many alumni continue to lead innovations in education, public policy, social enterprise, and community development after the fellowship.
Call for Applications: SLA BoostHer Programme for Young Nigerian Women (Hands-On Mentorship)

Are you a young Nigerian woman looking to launch a business, advance your career, or gain in-demand skills? The SLA BoostHer Program is now open, offering practical training, mentorship, and support to help you take the next step toward economic independence and professional growth.

The SLA BoostHer Program, delivered by She Leads Africa in collaboration with Jobberman, is a transformative initiative created to empower young Nigerian women entrepreneurs and career builders. Why This Matters

The SLA BoostHer Program bridges this gap by providing practical skills, exposure to industry experts, and a supportive community that enables women to build businesses, secure jobs, and grow confidently in their chosen paths.

Are you a young Nigerian woman looking to launch a business, advance your career, or gain in-demand skills? The SLA BoostHer Program is now open, offering practical training, mentorship, and support to help you take the next step toward economic independence and professional growth. 

Who Can Apply

  • Eligible applicants include young women with innovative business ideas, aspiring entrepreneurs who want to turn their skills into income-generating ventures, and women at different stages of their career journey.

What to Expect

  • The programme also features on-campus and community engagements, creating opportunities for in-person learning at universities and selected locations.

How You Will Learn

Special in-person sessions are also organised to support hands-on learning and deeper connection.

Applications are now open for the International Sustainable Chemistry Collaborative Centre (ISC3) Innovation Challenge 2026, a global competition designed to identify, support, and promote breakthrough innovations in sustainable chemistry.

Chemistry plays a central role in achieving most of the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals, from clean energy and responsible consumption to climate action and sustainable industry. Through this Innovation Challenge, ISC3 seeks to accelerate solutions that deliver environmentally sound, socially just, and economically viable alternatives across critical sectors.

The 2026 edition focuses on chemistry and chemical technology-based innovations in electronics, encouraging solutions that rethink materials, manufacturing processes, product design, and end-of-life management.

Focus Areas

Applicants are invited to submit innovations aligned with one or more of the following thematic areas.

Innovation in Design and Performance

This area covers eco-design approaches that improve durability, repairability, recyclability, and modularity of electronic products through chemical and materials innovation. It also includes design-for-disassembly solutions such as removable or chemically dissolvable adhesives, coatings, and encapsulants, reduced material intensity without performance loss, sustainable solders and solvent systems, and innovations that reduce or replace critical raw materials and toxic metal species using abundant, non-toxic, recycled, or recyclable feedstocks.

Sustainable Manufacturing Approaches
This theme supports low-energy and low-waste fabrication methods such as solution processing, additive manufacturing, and green solvents. It also includes scalable and safer chemical routes that generate less waste, sustainable alternatives to hazardous reagents and byproducts, biodegradable electronic components based on polymers or organic materials, and approaches that address social challenges such as unsafe working conditions and child labour within supply chains.

End-of-Life and Circularity
Innovations under this focus address chemical methods for disassembly, selective separation, and recycling of electronic components, e-waste recycling and recovery of valuable materials, electrochemical recovery of metals such as lithium, cobalt, and gold, mechanical or chemical recycling of plastics in electronics, and the use of recyclable or biodegradable materials that fully mineralise at end of life. Closed-loop systems, prevention of material dissipation, and biomaterial-based conductors or semiconductors are also included.

Alternative and Emerging Materials
This area includes organic and polymer electronics, advanced materials for electronics, two-dimensional materials such as graphene, MoS₂, and MXenes, metal–organic and covalent-organic frameworks for sensing and energy applications, self-healing or stimuli-responsive materials for flexible electronics, and sustainable alternatives to fossil-based plastics used in electronic components such as PVC, ABS, PS, and PC.

Novel Devices
Innovations may also focus on more sustainable energy storage and conversion devices including batteries, supercapacitors, and fuel cells, as well as organic electronics, photonics, optoelectronics, OLEDs, OPVs, perovskite solar cells, and photocatalytic devices.

Eligibility Requirement

  • The challenge is open to innovators and entrepreneurs worldwide who own or represent a sustainable chemistry innovation.

  • Eligible applicants may be affiliated with research institutes, start-ups, small or medium-sized enterprises, or similar organisations involved in developing and implementing sustainable chemistry solutions.

  • Participating enterprises must have no more than 50 full-time equivalent staff and an annual turnover not exceeding €10 million.

  • Enterprises that are majority-owned by large companies are not eligible, except in cases where the innovation leads to the creation of an independent spin-off.

  • Innovators from developing countries, particularly those addressing challenges in developing contexts, are strongly encouraged to apply. Female entrepreneurs are especially encouraged to submit proposals.

Benefits

  • Finalists selected for the ISC3 Innovation Challenge 2026 will receive tailored support through the Global Start-up Service customised support programme, visibility via ISC3 communication channels, networking opportunities through participation in a high-level industry matchmaking event in 2026, and individual and group pitch training to prepare for investor and industry engagement.

  • The total cash prize amounts to €25,000. The overall Innovation Challenge Winner will receive €15,000, while two Special Impact awards will each receive €5,000.

Assessment Criteria
Proposals will be evaluated based on three core criteria: the level of innovation and originality, business viability and market potential, and the sustainability impact of the solution across environmental, social, and economic dimensions.
One Young World & Virgin Unite NewNow Programme 2026

Applications are now open for the NewNow Programme 2026, a global leadership initiative by One Young World, supported by Virgin Unite. The programme identifies and supports exceptional young leaders (ages 18–30) from the Global South who are driving systems-level change through collective action.

Call for CivicHive Civic Tech Fellowship 2026 (West Africa) is open now!!

The CivicHive Civic Tech Fellowship is a 20-week incubation and implementation programme designed to support early-stage civic innovators building tech-enabled solutions to pressing social challenges across West Africa. The fellowship provides a structured pathway for innovators to refine ideas, develop sustainable civic products, and strengthen democratic participation, accountability, and public service delivery.

Nigeria is home to one of the world’s fastest-growing youth populations. In this context, civic technology has emerged as a powerful catalyst for social change, leveraging digital and non-digital tools to bridge gaps between governments and citizens, expand civic space, and strengthen democratic participation.

Thematic Areas

  • Artificial Intelligence for Good: Civic tech solutions that use AI responsibly to address social challenges, strengthen accountability, mitigate bias and harm, and contribute to transparent, inclusive, and effective governance of AI systems.

  • Low-Cost Climate Tech Solutions: Affordable, tech-driven solutions addressing climate adaptation, agriculture, environmental management, food security, and community resilience, particularly in local and resource-constrained contexts.

  • Technology for Social Good (Health, Education, and Justice): Civic innovations that improve access to essential public services, protect rights, close service delivery gaps, and empower citizens to demand accountability and equitable outcomes.

  • Advocacy and Social Movements: Tools that strengthen advocacy, civic organising, collective action, and citizen-led movements, especially in shrinking civic spaces and contexts facing repression or exclusion.

The CivicHive Civic Tech Fellowship exists to identify, nurture, and support the next generation of civic tech leaders responding to these challenges. Over the past five years, 34 CivicHive Fellows have built impactful civic products addressing social challenges across multiple sectors. Alumni projects such as Gavel, Amplify, and Flemer Project have gone on to form strong partnerships with global organisations, consistently citing the CivicHive training as instrumental to their growth. Other notable fellows include GenderMobile, PROMAD, PolicyShapers, JusticePadi, and Urban Alert, whose work continues to shape civic engagement and accountability.

 Benefits

The CivicHive Civic Tech Fellowship will incubate 15 civic tech solutions across West Africa. Selected fellows will receive:

  • Technical, product, and legal support

  • Mentorship from experienced civic tech leaders

  • Access to a regional civic tech network

  • A monthly stipend throughout the fellowship

  • Support to prepare solutions for launch and early scaling

  • Paid trip to attend the 2026 West Africa Civic Tech Conference

Eligibilities

Applicants to the CivicHive Civic Tech Fellowship must meet the following criteria:

  • Be citizens or residents of any West African country,

  • Be individuals or teams working on a civic tech idea or solution,

  • Must have tested the idea or been in operation for 1-3 years.

  • The solution must align with one or more of the fellowship’s thematic areas.

  • Demonstrate a clear public-interest or social impact focus, particularly in strengthening civic space, accountability, participation, or service delivery,

  • Be available to fully participate in the fellowship activities for the duration of the programme,

  • Be open to mentorship, peer learning, iteration, and collaboration within a regional civic tech ecosystem.

VLIR-UOS, in collaboration with Belgian universities, is offering full scholarships to study in Belgium for selected Bachelor’s and Master’s students from 29 eligible countries in Africa, Asia, and Latin America.

This scholarship program, known as ICP Connect, provides an all-inclusive package covering tuition fees, living allowance, travel costs, and insurance, allowing students to focus entirely on their studies while experiencing Belgian culture and world-class academic programs.

VLIR-UOS 2026-2027 Scholarships: Study Levels and Available Programs

The ICP Connect scholarship is available for three study levels:

  1. Professional Bachelors (3 years) – Designed for students who want hands-on, career-oriented education. Programs include:

    • Hotel Management

    • Applied Computer Science

  2. Initial Masters (2 years) – Ideal for students seeking to advance in technical and development-focused fields. Programs include:

    • Agro- & Environmental Nematology

    • Rural Development

    • Aquaculture

    • Epidemiology

    • Food Technology

    • Marine & Lacustrine Science & Management

    • Statistics & Data Science

    • Sustainable Development

    • Transportation Sciences – Road Safety

    • Water Resources Engineering

    • Nutrition & Food Systems

    • Sustainable Land Management

    • Tropical Biodiversity & Ecosystems

  3. Advanced Masters (1 year) – Tailored for professionals and students seeking specialized training and research experience. Programs include:

    • Human Settlements

    • Development Evaluation & Management

    • Governance & Development

    • Globalisation & Development

    • Cultural Anthropology & Development Studies (CADES)

This wide range of programs ensures that students from diverse backgrounds—science, technology, development, and social sciences—can find a course that aligns with their career goals.

Who Can Apply?

The scholarship targets citizens of the 29 eligible countries in Africa, Asia, and Latin America, and applicants must be residing in one of these countries at the time of application. Age limits vary by program level:

  • Bachelor’s/Initial Master’s: 19–35 years (by 1 January of the intake year)

  • Advanced Master’s: Up to 45 years (by 1 January of the intake year)

While the program prefers candidates working in higher education, government, or civil society, strong and motivated profiles from other sectors are also welcome. It is important to note that applicants may only apply for one ICP Connect scholarship per year and must not have previously studied in Belgium or held a Belgian government scholarship in the intake year.

Benefits of the ICP Connect Scholarship

The VLIR-UOS scholarship is fully funded, covering:

  • Tuition fees for the chosen program

  • Monthly living allowance to cover accommodation, meals, and daily expenses

  • Travel expenses to and from Belgium

  • Health and insurance coverage during the study period

This comprehensive support enables students to fully immerse themselves in academic and research activities, participate in international networks, and build lifelong skills and connections.

How to Apply

Applying for the ICP Connect scholarship is a two-step process:

  1. Select a Program – Browse the list of eligible ICP Connect Bachelor or Master programs offered by VLIR-UOS and choose the program that best fits your academic and professional goals.

  2. Submit Your Application – Apply to the host university of the chosen program, indicating in your application that you are also applying for the VLIR-UOS ICP Connect scholarship. Applicants must submit all required documents, including:

    • Certified copies of academic degrees and transcripts

    • Proof of language proficiency (English, French, or Dutch, depending on program requirements)

  3. Curriculum Vitae (CV)

  4. Motivation letter detailing your academic background, professional experience, and future plans

  5. Any additional documents requested by the university

After submission, wait for the selection results from both the university and VLIR-UOS. The process may include interviews or additional assessments, depending on the program.

Deadlines

Application deadlines vary by program but are generally between 1 February and 28 February 2026. Note that the MSc in Tropical Biodiversity & Ecosystems closes on 30 November 2025, so applicants should plan accordingly.

Why This Scholarship Matters

Studying in Belgium offers students the chance to gain an international perspective while accessing top-quality education and research facilities. The ICP Connect scholarship is not just about academic learning—it is also about building networks with fellow students from around the world, participating in multicultural experiences, and developing skills that are highly valued globally. Graduates often return to their home countries ready to contribute to sustainable development, innovation, and leadership in their respective fields.

By supporting talented students from the Global South, the VLIR-UOS ICP Connect scholarship fosters inclusive international collaboration, bridging knowledge gaps and empowering future leaders who can make a difference in their communities.

Final Notes

If you meet the eligibility criteria and aspire to advance your education while gaining international exposure, the VLIR-UOS ICP Connect scholarship is an opportunity not to be missed. Start preparing your application now, choose your preferred program carefully, and ensure all supporting documents are ready before the deadline.
Erasmus Mundus Joint Master (EMJM) Scholarships 2026/2027 - IMAPP

The International Master on Advanced Methods in Particle Physics (IMAPP) has officially opened applications for the 2026/2027 academic year, including the highly competitive Erasmus Mundus Joint Master (EMJM) Scholarships. Announced on 2 January 2026, this call represents a major opportunity for outstanding physics graduates from around the world to pursue advanced training in particle physics within a truly international and research-intensive environment.

IMAPP is a prestigious joint master’s programme delivered by an international consortium of leading universities and research institutions. Now in its fourth year of Erasmus Mundus recognition, the programme continues to attract top-tier students seeking rigorous academic training, cutting-edge research exposure, and strong links to global scientific infrastructures such as CERN and other major laboratories.


About the IMAPP Programme

IMAPP is designed for students holding a Bachelor’s degree in Physics (or an equivalent qualification) who wish to specialise in advanced theoretical, computational, and experimental methods in particle physics. The programme offers a strong interdisciplinary foundation, combining high-level coursework with research-oriented training, preparing graduates for doctoral studies or careers in international research, academia, and high-technology industries.

The first semester of the programme begins in September 2026 at Université Clermont Auvergne, after which students continue their studies across partner institutions within the IMAPP consortium. The programme’s international mobility structure provides students with exposure to diverse academic systems, research cultures, and scientific networks.

Erasmus Mundus Joint Master Scholarships

For the 2026/2027 intake, IMAPP offers Erasmus Mundus Joint Master (EMJM) scholarships to excellent candidates who meet the academic and admission requirements of the programme. These scholarships are funded by the European Union and are intended to support academic excellence, diversity, and global participation.

Key points regarding the scholarships include:

  • An average of 18 scholarships are awarded per intake

  • A maximum of two scholarships per nationality

  • Scholarships cover tuition fees and provide a living allowance

  • Selection is highly competitive and merit-based

The programme has demonstrated consistent growth in scholarship awards:

  • 13 scholarships for the 2023–2025 intake

  • 18 scholarships for the 2024–2026 intake

  • 19 scholarships for the 2025–2027 intake

Application Requirements and Process

Applicants must submit an online application and provide all required information and documentation. In addition to personal and academic details, candidates are required to:

  • Hold a Bachelor’s degree in Physics (or equivalent)

  • Meet all academic admission requirements of the IMAPP programme

  • Provide the email addresses of two referees, who will submit letters of reference directly to the selection committee

Careful attention should be paid to the completeness and quality of the application, as this forms a critical part of the evaluation process.

Important Dates

Applicants are advised to note the following key deadlines:

  • Scholarship application deadline: 15 March 2026

  • Notification of scholarship results: 31 March 2026

  • Start of first semester: September 2026 (Université Clermont Auvergne)

Early preparation is strongly recommended, particularly for securing strong reference letters and ensuring eligibility requirements are met.

Options for Non-Scholarship Applicants

Applicants who are not selected for an EMJM scholarship are still encouraged to pursue IMAPP through alternative pathways. These include:

  • Self-financed enrolment, following the standard admission procedure

  • Application for additional funding opportunities, which are continuously updated on the IMAPP website

Admission deadlines for self-financed students are:

  • EU Bachelor’s degree holders (excluding Germany): 31 December 2026

  • Non-EU Bachelor’s degree holders: 15 May 2026

This flexibility ensures that strong candidates still have multiple avenues to join the programme.

Additional Resources and Information

Prospective applicants who were unable to attend the IMAPP Open Day held on 3 December can access comprehensive recordings and informational videos via the programme’s official YouTube channel. The “Everything about IMAPP” playlist provides insights into the curriculum, partner institutions, student life, research opportunities, and the wider IMAPP academic community.




Why Choose IMAPP?

IMAPP offers a unique academic experience rooted in excellence, international collaboration, and direct engagement with world-leading research institutions. Graduates benefit from strong academic mentorship, global mobility, and professional networks spanning universities, laboratories, and industry partners worldwide. The programme is particularly well-suited for students aiming to pursue PhD studies or advanced research careers in particle physics and related fields.

The Wellington Doctoral Scholarship is a flagship funding opportunity offered by Victoria University of Wellington – Te Herenga Waka to support high-achieving doctoral students from New Zealand and around the world. The scholarship is designed to encourage and sustain excellence in postgraduate research across a wide range of academic disciplines.

Awarded on the basis of academic merit, the scholarship provides generous financial support to enable recipients to focus fully on their doctoral studies while contributing to the university’s vibrant research community.

About Victoria University of Wellington

Victoria University of Wellington is one of New Zealand’s leading research-intensive universities, known for its strong global outlook, academic excellence, and commitment to impactful research. The university supports postgraduate research that addresses local and global challenges and fosters innovation across disciplines.

Through its Faculty of Graduate Research, the university offers structured support for doctoral candidates, ensuring a high-quality research environment and strong academic supervision.

Scholarship Overview

The Wellington Doctoral Scholarship supports full-time doctoral (PhD) study across all major academic areas offered by the University.

Key Scholarship Details

  • Scholarship Level: Doctoral (PhD), fully funded

  • Study Areas Include:

    • Architecture and Design

    • Business and Government

    • Education, Health, and Psychological Sciences

    • Engineering and Technology

    • Humanities and Social Sciences

    • Law

    • Music

    • Science

  • Award Value:

    • NZD $32,000 annual stipend

    • Full domestic tuition fees for up to three years

  • Number of Awards: Approximately 110 per year

  • Tenure: Normally up to three years

Eligibility Criteria

The scholarship is open to domestic and international students who intend to enrol, or are already enrolled, in a doctoral programme at Victoria University of Wellington.

Applicants must:

  • Be graduates of any university within or outside New Zealand

  • Intend to enrol full-time in a PhD programme, or have commenced doctoral study at Victoria University of Wellington

  • Normally hold a Bachelor’s or Master’s degree with academic distinction equivalent to First Class Honours at a New Zealand university

  • Demonstrate strong academic performance and research potential

Applicants who have already been enrolled in a PhD programme at the university for more than six months at the time of the scholarship offer may receive a reduced tenure at the discretion of the University Research Scholarships Committee.

Selection Criteria and Assessment

Scholarships are awarded competitively based on academic excellence and research potential. Selection decisions are made by the Victoria University Research Scholarships Committee, following recommendations from relevant schools and faculties.

Key selection considerations include:

  • Strong academic record and excellent academic references

  • Demonstrated potential for high-quality, original research

  • Record of publication in high-quality journals, where applicable

  • Alignment between the applicant’s research interests and the strategic priorities of the School, Institute, or Centre

  • Strategic considerations such as institutional collaboration or engagement with research and industry partners

Applicants who have completed prior studies outside Victoria University of Wellington must submit certified academic transcripts from all other institutions.

Notification and Offer Process

Offers of the Wellington Doctoral Scholarship are typically made within six weeks of each application deadline. Due to possible offer declines, some applicants may receive notification up to three months after submission.

All applicants will be notified of the outcome by email.

Scholarship Conditions and Expectations

Recipients of the Wellington Doctoral Scholarship are expected to:

  • Maintain full-time enrolment and devote themselves fully to their research

  • Make satisfactory academic progress and submit six-monthly progress reports

  • Sign a Wellington Doctoral Research Scholarship Contract

  • Act as ambassadors for Victoria University of Wellington when requested

Limited paid employment of up to 600 hours per calendar year may be approved with supervisor and Head of School support.

Scholarships may be suspended or terminated if recipients fail to meet academic progress requirements or breach scholarship conditions.
Government of Ireland International Education Scholarship (GOI-IES) Program

Overview

The Government of Ireland International Education Scholarship (GOI-IES) programme supports high-achieving international students who wish to pursue full-time postgraduate study in Ireland at NFQ Level 9 or 10 (master’s, postgraduate diploma, or PhD).

The programme is funded by the Government of Ireland, delivered in partnership with Irish higher education institutions (HEIs), and administered by the Higher Education Authority (HEA).  

Each year, 60 one-year scholarships are awarded to outstanding students from outside the EU/EEA, Switzerland, and the United Kingdom. The 2026 call will open on 29 January 2026.

What the Scholarship Includes

Each Government of Ireland International Education Scholarship provides comprehensive financial and institutional support for one academic year:

  • €10,000 stipend to contribute toward living costs

  • Full tuition fee waiver for the scholarship year, provided by the host HEI

  • Access to world-class Irish higher education and research environments

  • Recognition as a Government of Ireland scholar, enhancing academic and professional profiles

The scholarship applies to one year only, even if the programme of study is longer in duration.

Eligibility Criteria

To be eligible for the GOI-IES programme, applicants must meet all of the following conditions:

  • Be domiciled outside the EU/EEA, Switzerland, and the United Kingdom

  • Apply for admission to a master’s, postgraduate diploma, or PhD programme at an eligible Irish HEI

  • Hold a conditional or final offer of admission at the time of submitting the scholarship application

  • Submit proof of admission as part of the online application

  • Not have previously held a GOI-IES scholarship

The following applicants are not eligible:

  • Russian or Belarusian nationals

  • Applicants submitting more than one application in the 2026 cycle

Applicants are strongly advised to confirm course eligibility directly with their chosen HEI. Contact details for eligible institutions are listed in Appendix 1 of the 2026 GOI-IES Call document, available on the programme webpage.

How to Apply

Applicants must carefully review the official call documentation before submitting an application. The process is fully online.

Key application requirements include:

  • Submission via the 2026 GOI-IES online application portal

  • A maximum of one application per candidate

  • Upload of two references through the portal

  • Upload of proof of admission from an eligible HEI

Once submitted, applications cannot be edited or withdrawn. Late submissions are not accepted. Candidates are encouraged to apply well in advance of the deadline to avoid technical issues caused by high server traffic.

A sample application form is provided for guidance only and should not be submitted.

Assessment and Selection Process

Applications are assessed through a competitive, multi-stage process. Candidates are expected to demonstrate:

  • Outstanding academic achievement

  • Excellent communication skills

  • Active participation in extracurricular activities, such as:

    • Community or humanitarian engagement

    • Arts, culture, or sport

    • Leadership, advocacy, or public service

  • A clear and compelling rationale for studying in Ireland, including how the scholarship aligns with long-term academic and professional goals

Eligible applications are first reviewed by the relevant HEIs for eligibility and strategic alignment. Shortlisted applications are then evaluated by an independent panel of assessors.

About the scholarship

By 2030, more than two billion young people around the world will be shaping the future of their economies. This presents an exciting opportunity for innovation, entrepreneurship, and sustainable growth, especially in emerging and frontier markets. Young entrepreneurs are already at the forefront of this change, building businesses that respond to local challenges and create lasting impact in their communities.

Entrepreneurs play a vital role in strengthening local economies. By developing new products and services, growing innovative business ideas, and expanding into new markets, they help create more resilient and dynamic business environments. However, many young entrepreneurs still face barriers such as limited access to networks, resources, and international exposure.

To help address these challenges, the Netherlands Ministry of Foreign Affairs, through the Dutch Enterprise Agency (RVO), partners with One Young World to offer an annual scholarship for young entrepreneurs and business leaders. The scholarship is designed to support promising changemakers who are building scalable solutions to sustainability challenges and are ready to take their impact to the next level by connecting with a global community.

Scholarship recipients will attend the One Young World Summit 2026 in Cape Town, where 2,000 young leaders from over 190 countries come together to share ideas, learn from global experts, and collaborate on solutions to today’s most pressing challenges. The Summit offers an inspiring environment to grow professionally, strengthen entrepreneurial skills, and build meaningful international connections that can support long-term success.

Join us

Expectations of selected scholars

The African Union (AU) Digital and Innovation

The African Union (AU) Digital and Innovation Fellowship is a prestigious 12-month immersive programme designed to empower Africa’s brightest youth innovators to drive digital transformation, good governance, and public sector innovation from within African Union institutions.

The programme will select 12 Fellows to work at the intersection of technology, data, innovation, and governance. Fellows are placed within AU institutions, where they collaborate directly with AU staff to identify challenges and co-develop impactful digital solutions.

What the Fellowship Offers:

  • Capacity Building and Professional Development: Tailored training to strengthen technical, leadership, and innovation skills for public sector transformation.

  • Immersive Institutional Experience: Hands-on collaboration with AU institutions to deliver real-world digital impact.

  • Financial Support:  Monthly stipend of €2,500, Equipment support of up to €800, Individual learning budget of €500

  • International and Continental Exposure: Two in-person bootcamps in Addis Ababa, Mid-term delegation visit to Germany, Engagement with African and global policy and innovation actors

  • Sustainable Institutional Impact: Fellows embed tools, practices, and knowledge that continue to add value beyond the fellowship period.

Applicants must:

  • Be a citizen of an AU Member State and based in Africa

  • Be under 35 years at the time of application

  • Hold a bachelor’s degree or equivalent

  • Demonstrate strong problem-solving and innovation skills

  • Have experience with technology or data-driven projects

  • Commit fully to 12 months, including relocation to Addis Ababa

  • Be proficient in English (other AU languages are an added advantage)

Opening: February 28

The fellowship follows a structured selection and placement process aligned with AU institutional needs.


Prepare your application materials


Before applying, gather all required documents. You will need to submit your current CV highlighting relevant experience in technology, data, or digital innovation, a scan of your national ID or passport, and a brief of previous digital projects (if available).


Fill out the application form


Applications must be submitted through the Job Postings page. This is the only way to be considered for the fellowship. Make sure your application is complete and accurate. If you need guidance during the process, you can reach out to our team at shc@enpact.org.

Check your e-mail


You will receive an e-mail with the status of your application within three weeks. The e-mail will notify you if you are shortlisted so that you can prepare for a quick interview or share feedback in case we are unable to accommodate you in the programme. Please make sure to also regularly check spam.

Switzerland-specific advice on regulations and tax, easy access to the EU market, and strong trade links.
