Federal Government Enterprise and Empowerment Programme (GEEP) 2026 ( N300,000 Interest-Free Loan)

The Government Enterprise and Empowerment Programme (GEEP) is a flagship financial inclusion initiative of the National Social Investment Programme Agency, designed to support micro- and small-scale entrepreneurs across Nigeria. The programme provides zero-interest, collateral-free loans to help economically active Nigerians expand their businesses, improve productivity, and build sustainable livelihoods.

GEEP focuses on reaching people who are often excluded from formal banking systems, including smallholder farmers, petty traders, artisans, and market women. Through its loan schemes, beneficiaries can access affordable capital to grow their enterprises without the burden of high interest rates.


The Government Enterprise and Empowerment Programme (GEEP) is a flagship financial inclusion initiative of the National Social Investment Programme Agency, designed to support micro- and small-scale entrepreneurs across Nigeria. The programme provides zero-interest, collateral-free loans to help economically active Nigerians expand their businesses, improve productivity, and build sustainable livelihoods. 

GEEP focuses on reaching people who are often excluded from formal banking systems, including smallholder farmers, petty traders, artisans, and market women. Through its loan schemes, beneficiaries can access affordable capital to grow their enterprises without the burden of high interest rates.

Eligibility

  • The programme is open to Nigerians who are engaged in income-generating activities.

  • This includes small business owners, traders and artisans, market women, and smallholder farmers who require financial support to scale their operations or stabilise their businesses.

 

Benefits

  • FarmerMoni is designed for smallholder farmers and offers interest-free loans of up to ₦300,000. The facility helps farmers access essential inputs such as seeds, fertilisers, equipment, and basic farm tools. The goal is to boost agricultural productivity, strengthen food security, and improve farmers’ incomes.

  • TraderMoni targets petty traders, artisans, and small-scale entrepreneurs. It provides quick and accessible loans of up to ₦100,000, enabling beneficiaries to restock goods, improve cash flow, and gradually expand their businesses.

  • MarketMoni focuses on market women and offers interest-free loans of up to ₦100,000. With access to affordable credit, beneficiaries can increase their working capital, restock inventory, and stabilise daily trading activities.

Required Documents

  • Applicants are expected to provide basic personal and business information.

  • This includes full name, phone number, National Identification Number (NIN), state and local government area, nature of business or farming activity, a passport photograph, and a valid means of identification.

  • Details of a guarantor are also required. This covers the guarantor’s full name, phone number, email address, and residential address.

Petty Traders Grant 2026 Opens Third Round with ₦10,000–₦100,000 Non-Repayable Support for Small Businesses

The Petty Traders Grant Support Programme 2026 has opened applications for its third round, offering financial support to small-scale business owners across Nigeria.

The community-driven initiative is designed to empower both aspiring and existing petty traders by providing seed capital for small businesses, such as buying and selling goods or offering basic services. For traders already operating, the grant serves as additional funding to strengthen and expand their businesses.

 

Under the programme, eligible applicants can receive between ₦10,000 and ₦100,000 in non-repayable grants to help generate sustainable income. Unlike many funding opportunities, business registration is not required, and the application process involves minimal documentation. As long as applicants fall within the specified age bracket and reside in Nigeria, they are eligible to apply.

The grant targets entrepreneurs who need start-up capital as well as small business owners seeking recapitalisation to grow their enterprises. Beyond financial support, beneficiaries will also receive business growth guidance aimed at helping them sustain and improve their livelihoods.

The Petty Traders Grant Support Programme 2026 has opened applications for its third round, offering financial support to small-scale business owners across Nigeria. 

The community-driven initiative is designed to empower both aspiring and existing petty traders by providing seed capital for small businesses, such as buying and selling goods or offering basic services. For traders already operating, the grant serves as additional funding to strengthen and expand their businesses.

Under the programme, eligible applicants can receive between ₦10,000 and ₦100,000 in non-repayable grants to help generate sustainable income. Unlike many funding opportunities, business registration is not required, and the application process involves minimal documentation. As long as applicants fall within the specified age bracket and reside in Nigeria, they are eligible to apply.

The grant targets entrepreneurs who need start-up capital as well as small business owners seeking recapitalisation to grow their enterprises. Beyond financial support, beneficiaries will also receive business growth guidance aimed at helping them sustain and improve their livelihoods.

To qualify, applicants must be between 18 and 55 years old and either own a small business or have a viable business idea. They must provide a valid National Identification Number slip, details of one contact person, and a verifiable residential address. Applicants are also required to have a valid 10-digit bank account number and reside in Nigeria. The programme is open to individuals with or without Corporate Affairs Commission registration.

Applicants are advised to submit only one application, as multiple entries may lead to disqualification.

Applications are now open for the KPMG Female Founders Africa Competition, a prestigious platform designed to identify and support outstanding women entrepreneurs building innovative and scalable businesses across Africa.

The competition forms part of KPMG’s broader commitment to promoting diversity in entrepreneurship and strengthening the ecosystem for women-led ventures. Selected founders gain valuable exposure, expert feedback, and access to influential networks that can accelerate business growth locally and globally.

About the Competition

The KPMG Female Founders Africa Competition seeks to spotlight innovative businesses founded and led by women. The programme provides founders with the opportunity to pitch their ventures to industry experts, investors, and business leaders while benefiting from mentorship, strategic visibility, and potential advancement to further stages of competition.

Beyond recognition, the competition helps founders refine their business models, strengthen investor readiness, and expand regional and global networks.

Eligibility Criteria

To be eligible for the KPMG Female Founders Africa Competition, applicants must meet the following criteria:

  • The applicant must be a female founder or co-founder of the business.

  • The business must be legally registered and operating within an African country.

  • The founder must hold a significant leadership role within the company and be actively involved in decision-making.

  • The business should be innovative, scalable, and demonstrate strong growth potential.

  • The company must have a viable product or service already in the market (beyond the idea stage).

  • The business should demonstrate traction, which may include revenue generation, customer growth, partnerships, or investment raised.

  • The founder must be available to participate in pitching sessions and related competition activities if shortlisted.

Why You Should Apply

Participating in the KPMG Female Founders Africa Competition offers more than just recognition. It provides:

  • Exposure to investors and strategic partners

  • Expert insights to strengthen your business strategy

  • Enhanced credibility that can open doors to funding and partnerships
WE Empower UN SDG Challenge 2026: Funding for Women Social Entrepreneurs

The WE Empower UN SDG Challenge 2026 invites women social entrepreneurs driving change in their communities and advancing the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) to apply for a global competition that combines funding, training, and international exposure.

Selected awardees will be notified by mid‑July 2026 and will travel to New York City during Climate Week and the 81st UN General Assembly (September 19–26, 2026). Participants will engage in a high-impact agenda, including:

  • Pitch workshops and mentorship sessions tailored by leading partners.

  • Curated networking with global leaders in business, policy, and philanthropy.

  • The WE Empower Pitch Challenge, hosted by philanthropist and Vital Voices Board Member Diane von Furstenberg, where awardees present their enterprises to a distinguished panel for grant funding.

Due to evolving U.S. visa restrictions, the program may alternatively be held at the UN’s second headquarters in Geneva during June 14–20, 2026.

Eligibility Criteria

To participate, applicants must:

  • Identify as a woman entrepreneur.

  • Lead decision-making within their organization or business.

  • Have at least three years of operational experience as of August 1, 2026.

  • Employ a minimum of three full-time staff or equivalents.

  • Generate at least USD $75,000 in annual revenue (for nonprofits, this includes grants, donations, and fundraising).

  • Be professionally proficient in English.

  • Be at least 21 years old by August 1, 2026.

  • Be available to travel and participate in all events during the program dates.

Why It Matters for African Women Entrepreneurs

This challenge provides funding, global visibility, and strategic mentorship that can accelerate business growth, strengthen organizational impact, and position African women-led enterprises as leaders in advancing sustainable development locally and internationally.

The WE Empower UN SDG Challenge 2026 is a unique opportunity for women social entrepreneurs to access capital, gain international exposure, and network with global stakeholders committed to sustainable development.
SAB Foundation Social Innovation and Disability Empowerment Awards 2026

The SAB Foundation is calling for applications to its Social Innovation and Disability Empowerment Awards 2026. These awards support entrepreneurs, innovators, and social enterprises in South Africa whose products, services, or business models address real social and economic challenges. The programme focuses on creating impact for women, youth, people living in rural areas, and persons with disabilities.
The awards provide funding alongside tailored mentorship, technical support, and business development assistance to help early-stage ventures grow and scale. Since 2015, the programme has invested over R88 million, supporting more than 200 innovators and creating over 4,000 jobs, impacting more than 16,000 livelihoods.

Award Categories

  • Social Innovation Awards: For innovations that solve social challenges in areas such as health, housing, rural livelihoods, energy, water, and financial inclusion.

  • Disability Empowerment Awards: For innovations that empower persons with disabilities and enhance their access to economic opportunities.

Eligibility Requirements

  • Open to South African citizens aged 18 and above.

  • Applicants can be individuals, teams, institutions, or social enterprises.

  • Innovations must be original, demonstrate potential as a sustainable business, and have progressed beyond the “blue-sky” stage with some evidence of investment, planning, or prototype development.

  • Previous recipients of the Tholoana Enterprise Programme are ineligible.

Why It Matters for African MSMEs and Social Entrepreneurs
These awards provide funding, mentorship, and business support, helping entrepreneurs refine their solutions, scale impact, and enhance livelihoods in under-served communities. They offer an opportunity to access a network of investors, technical experts, and peers, while strengthening business viability and social outcomes. For innovators tackling systemic barriers—particularly in sectors like health, education, energy, and financial inclusion—the programme can accelerate growth, improve market reach, and foster sustainable social impact.


The SAB Foundation Social Innovation and Disability Empowerment Awards 2026 offer a platform for early-stage innovators to transform ideas into tangible solutions, drive social change, and strengthen the South African entrepreneurial ecosystem. Interested applicants are encouraged to submit their proposals before the March 16 deadline to secure funding, mentorship, and visibility at the national level.

The United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) has announced the launch of the UNESCO-Aschberg Programme for Artists and Cultural Professionals – Fuelling Creative Futures, a global initiative designed to strengthen the protection of artists’ rights and promote artistic freedom across diverse cultural contexts. The programme includes a dedicated funding track for Civil Society Organisations (CSOs), recognizing their critical role in advocating for creative communities and shaping cultural policy environments.

This initiative focuses on supporting forward-thinking projects that address challenges faced by artists and cultural professionals, particularly in regions where freedom of expression, fair working conditions, and access to opportunities remain constrained. By providing financial assistance and institutional support, UNESCO aims to foster ecosystems where creativity can thrive safely and sustainably at local, national, regional, and international levels.



At its core, the programme is built around two fundamental pillars:

  • Improving the professional status and working conditions of artists

  • Safeguarding artistic freedom as a cornerstone of democratic and cultural development

Artistic expression is increasingly recognized as not only a cultural asset but also a driver of economic growth, social cohesion, and innovation. However, many artists continue to face systemic barriers such as inadequate legal protections, censorship, limited funding, and lack of institutional recognition. The UNESCO-Aschberg Programme seeks to address these gaps by empowering organizations working directly within creative sectors.

Financial support is available for projects that introduce innovative approaches to strengthening artist ecosystems and defending creative rights. Eligible initiatives may operate across multiple levels, from grassroots community programmes to international collaborations.

Eligible project types include:

  • Capacity Building Initiatives
    Training programmes, workshops, and professional development opportunities that enhance artists’ skills, knowledge, and sustainability.

  • Advocacy Campaigns
    Policy engagement, awareness campaigns, and stakeholder mobilization aimed at improving legal and institutional frameworks for artists.

  • Monitoring and Research Projects
    Studies, data collection, and reporting efforts that track the status of artistic freedom, working conditions, and sector challenges.

  • Artist Rights Protection Programmes
    Legal support mechanisms, rights education, and protection initiatives addressing violations or vulnerabilities faced by creatives.

  • Artistic Freedom Promotion Activities
    Programmes that create safe spaces for expression, dialogue, and cultural participation while countering censorship and restrictions.


A Strategic Investment in the Global Creative Economy

The creative and cultural industries contribute significantly to employment and GDP worldwide, yet they remain under-supported in many regions. UNESCO’s programme reflects a growing global recognition that investing in artists is investing in economic resilience, innovation, and cultural identity.

By strengthening civil society actors, the initiative also encourages cross-sector collaboration between governments, private sector stakeholders, and creative communities to build more inclusive cultural economies.

LEAP Africa Leadership Accelerator Programme 2026 for African Women (Free Online Course+ Three-Month Intensive Mentorship)

The LEAP Africa Leadership Accelerator Programme is structured to support women at critical stages of their professional journey. By focusing on leadership capability, workplace challenges, and personal growth, the programme prepares participants to navigate complex professional environments and take on greater responsibility and influence.

Why This Matters

Despite growing participation of women in the workforce, leadership representation across Africa remains disproportionately low. The LEAP Africa Leadership Accelerator Programme addresses this gap by providing women with targeted leadership training, exposure, and mentorship, strengthening the pipeline of women leaders across sectors.

Programme Phases

Free Online Course
The programme begins with a free online course made up of 10 short-paced modules completed over a three-week period. The course addresses key issues affecting women in the workplace and factors that limit professional advancement. The modules were developed based on needs assessments conducted with over 200 respondents, ensuring the content reflects real challenges faced by African women in professional settings.

One-Week Paid Intensive Bootcamp
Participants who successfully complete the online course within the three-week timeframe will be eligible for a one-week physical bootcamp. The bootcamp brings together high-potential African women aged 23 to 35 who are seeking to advance their careers and secure leadership roles. The experience focuses on practical leadership skills, peer learning, and immersive development sessions.

Capstone Projects
After the bootcamp, participants will design and implement a capstone project aligned with their leadership goals. These projects serve as a practical application of the skills and insights gained during the programme. Completed projects will be showcased across LEAP Leadership Institute communication channels to inspire other women professionals and promote leadership action.

Three-Month Intensive Mentorship
Each participant will be paired with an accomplished mentor for a three-month mentorship period. Mentor matching is based on the individual needs identified by participants during onboarding. Mentorship sessions will take place once or twice monthly, focusing on career development, leadership growth, and personal advancement. Monthly check-ins will ensure mentorship goals and outcomes are achieved.

Who Should Apply


The programme is open to high-potential African women aged 23 to 35 who are currently working in corporate or entrepreneurial environments and are motivated to grow into leadership roles.

The Wells Mountain Initiative (WMI) is a public charitable foundation based in Bristol, Vermont, USA, which has been approved under the United States Internal Revenue Service under Section 501(c)(3). WMI works to create social change through education and community building by providing bright young people in the developing world with student scholarships and skills training, building a network of grassroots leaders who are catalyzing community transformation.

The Wells Mountain Initiative, or WMI, is much more than just a financial aid programme. It is a global movement designed to create social change from the ground up. Founded in 2005 by Tom Wells, the organization started with a simple idea. That idea is that the best people to solve problems in developing countries are the people who actually live there. While many other scholarships take the brightest students away to study in wealthy Western nations, WMI encourages them to stay. By supporting students as they study in their home countries, the organization ensures that local talent and skills remain where they are needed most.

Wells Mountain Initiative (WMI) fosters social change at the local level by building a global network of grassroots leaders who are catalyzing community transformation across 55 developing countries. They support young people who have proved to be unstoppable in the face of adversity and are deeply committed to leading change in their community. They fund their undergraduate studies in high-need sectors, help them lead social service projects during their academic career and stand alongside them to address any challenges so that they successfully complete their studies.

One of the most important parts of WMI is its lifelong network. The support does not stop when a student graduates. Instead, it grows into a global family of leaders who work together. They share ideas on how to fix problems like climate change, healthcare, and education. Through special conferences and local fellowships, scholars from different countries connect and mentor each other. This creates a lasting bond that goes beyond just a classroom education.

A core value of this organization is the idea of giving back. WMI scholars participate in a programme called Educate Your Own. This is where older graduates help raise money or provide support for the next generation of students. Every scholar also promises to do at least 100 hours of community service every year. This ensures that even while they are still in school, they are already making a positive difference in the lives of their neighbours. The programme challenges these scholars to think critically about their surroundings and to become proactive problem-solvers rather than passive observers. This culture of accountability and action ensures that every dollar invested in a student’s education pays dividends in the form of improved local schools, better healthcare delivery, and stronger local economies.

 Benefits

  • Financial support for university tuition and related educational fees.

  • Coverage of essential academic costs, which may include books, materials, and other approved educational expenses.

  • Guidance and support from the Wells Mountain Initiative team throughout the duration of the undergraduate programme.

  • Recognition as a WMI Scholar, a prestigious international distinction for community-minded leaders.

  • Membership in a global network of motivated students and professionals from over 40 developing countries.

  • The chance to make an impact in your home community through organized volunteerism and local projects.

 Eligibilities

  • Applicants must be 35 years old or younger by August 1, 2026.

  • Completed or is in the final year of secondary education, with above-average to excellent grades.

  • Applying to university or currently enrolled and taking classes towards completion of their first undergraduate degree.

  • Will be studying in their home country or another country in the developing world.

  • Planning to live and work in their own country after graduation.

  • Participated in volunteer service before applying for this scholarship and is committed to volunteering within their community during their studies.

  • Have some funding available for their education but will not be able to go to university or continue without assistance.

Application Process

  • Click the Apply Now button and create a free account on the Kaleidoscope application portal using a valid email address.

  • Submit a clear, professional personal headshot photo.

  • A personal statement (350–500 words) describing your background, family, and experiences.

  • Write the required essay (up to 1,000 words) answering the specific prompt about addressing a community challenge with a $1,500 grant.

  • Submit names and working email addresses of two recommenders.

  • Upload a national ID or alternative form of ID.

  • Submit grade transcripts from schools attended and national exam results if applicable.

  • Ensure all documents are in English or accompanied by an official, certified English translation.

  • Review your application for originality, ensuring no plagiarism or AI-generated content is used in your essays.
UNIDIR Women in AI (Artificial intelligence) Fellowship 2026

Artificial intelligence (AI) has quickly become an omnipresent governance challenge, including within the arena of international security and disarmament. Discussions around AI today extend far beyond the Group of Governmental Experts on Lethal Autonomous Weapon Systems, with the issue becoming increasingly prominent across a wide range of UN cybersecurity, disarmament and counter-terrorism processes.

However, a common feature of international security processes is the under-representation of women. UNIDIR research shows that, on average, only a third of the diplomats accredited to arms control, non-proliferation and disarmament forums are women.

As a result, these forums not only lack equitable participation, but they also become susceptible to oversights around the gender-specific impacts of weapons and violence. Their research also highlights that effective capacity-building may be one measure to help rectify this vis-à-vis governance of AI.

To redress gender inequalities and promote inclusive and effective policymaking, UNIDIR is convening the 2026 edition of the Women in AI Fellowship. This is designed as a capacity-building intervention that endows women diplomats with up-to-the-minute knowledge of the policy, legal and technical aspects of AI. Women in AI Fellows acquire the know-how, skills, resources and networks required to engage effectively in multilateral AI discussion in the field of international peace and security.

The Women in AI Fellowship comprises a week-long, in-person training in Geneva. The fellowship is structured around three key pillars for a holistic and effective programme of learning:

Education:

UNIDIR provides the fellows with a substantive introduction to AI technology, including its strengths and limitations; an overview of the possible and observed use cases of AI in security applications; related international security concerns surrounding AI; background to and current multilateral discussions around AI policy and governance; and insights into the links between military AI and considerations of law, ethics and gender. Learning activities are led by UNIDIR researchers in collaboration with relevant experts and stakeholder organisations.

Exploration:

Apart from lectures, UNIDIR facilitates the fellows’ interactions with stakeholders engaging in practical applications of AI in technical and academic settings. This will allow fellows to receive targeted briefings and to gain a better sense of the ecosystems involved in AI design, development, deployment and governance.

Networking:

UNIDIR also streamlines fellows’ engagements with relevant stakeholders to enable the forging of strong connections with the diplomatic community, the private sector and civil society.

This includes:

a) the organization of a reception with the diplomatic community in Geneva and

b) a series of virtual meetings with a group of multi-sectoral experts working at the intersections of gender, security and technology.

The fellows also benefit from and become part of the UNIDIR Alumni Network and the UNIDIR Expert Network on the Governance of Artificial Intelligence in the Military Domain.

 Benefits

  • Intensive In-Person Training (Geneva)

  • Comprehensive Education on AI and Security

  • Practical Exposure and Exploration

  • High-Level Networking Opportunities

  • Long-Term Professional Community

  • The fellowship is delivered free of charge. Financial support for travel to and accommodation in Geneva for the week-long training programme will be available for a select number of participants. Fellows from developing countries will be prioritized for this support.

 Eligibilities

  • The fellowship is open to women diplomats and government representatives who:

  • are junior to mid-career level

  • are representatives of UN Member States

  • have a strong command of both written and spoken English

  • have an interest in security, technology and/or gender
The KCFP Climate Resilience Fellowships mentors and supervisors in Online 2026

The KCFP Climate Resilience Fellowships offer a one-year professional development program for frontline climate adaptation professionals. The ACU is seeking academic supervisors and professional mentors to support those completing this fellowship.

Commonwealth Small Island Developing States (SIDS) are among the most vulnerable countries in the world, facing numerous economic, social and environmental development challenges, all exacerbated by climate change.

Building resilience to the trends and events associated with climate change is critical to addressing these vulnerabilities and requires adaptation. Effective adaptation action is informed by a knowledge of climate change, its impacts and effective solutions, in addition to harnessing collective leadership and capacities for action among various sectors and stakeholders.

The King’s Commonwealth Fellowship Programme Climate Resilience Fellowships, inspired by His Majesty King Charles III, will provide professionals working on the frontline of climate adaptation from across the public sector, civil society, and the private sector in Commonwealth SIDS with an accredited one-year professional development programme focused on climate adaptation practice.

The Association of Commonwealth Universities (ACU) is seeking academic supervisors and professional mentors to support Fellows completing a practical, workplace-based applied project as part of a postgraduate certificate in climate adaptation practice in Commonwealth Small Island Developing States (SIDS). The postgraduate certification is being delivered as part of the KCFP.

Academic supervisors will be university-based climate adaptation-focused academics. Professional mentors will be leaders within NGOs, government agencies, or private organisations working within climate adaptation practice.

The supervisors and mentors will support high-quality, student-led climate adaptation projects in Commonwealth SIDS by providing expert guidance, ensuring alignment with National Adaptation Plans (NAPs), and flagging potential risks, ultimately helping students to design and deliver locally relevant solutions that strengthen resilience to climate impacts.

The Association of Commonwealth Universities (ACU) is seeking academic supervisors and professional mentors to support Fellows completing a practical, workplace-based applied project as part of a postgraduate certificate in climate adaptation practice in Commonwealth Small Island Developing States (SIDS). The postgraduate certification is being delivered as part of the KCFP.

Academic supervisors will be university-based, climate adaptation-focused academics. They will supervise the main assessment submission.

Professional mentors will be leaders within NGOs, government agencies, or private organisations working within climate adaptation practice. They will support successful practical implementation of the project.

Benefits

  • Fully Funded Fellowship – covers programme costs, travel, accommodation, and living allowance.

  • Hybrid Format – Fellows continue their current employment while engaging in a one-year programme combining online learning, project-based work, and mentorship.

  • Mentorship & Supervision – guided by academics and climate resilience experts from King’s College London and partner institutions.

  • Capacity Building – strengthens leadership in climate adaptation and resilience, equipping fellows with practical tools to address urgent climate challenges.

  • Networking Opportunities – connects fellows with peers across Commonwealth Small Island Developing States (SIDS), building long-term collaborative networks.

  • Applied Impact – fellows implement practical resilience solutions in their home countries, ensuring direct community benefit.

Eligibilities

The ACU reserves the right to request evidence of employment status, if appropriate and necessary to do so.

The ACU is proud to be an equal opportunity workplace and encourages applications from all individuals regardless of age, disability, sex, gender reassignment, sexual orientation, pregnancy and maternity, race, religion or belief and marriage and civil partnerships.

Supervisors

To be eligible for this post as an academic supervisor, you must be employed at an ACU member university.

The ideal candidate should have a research background in the following categories or similar:

  • Climate science

  • Adaptation planning

  • Environmental policy

  • Earth and environmental sciences

  • Disaster risk reduction

  • Renewable energy

  • Community-based adaptation

Mentors

To be eligible as a professional mentor, you must be an employee of a registered organisation or registered as self-employed. Ideal mentors should combine geographic and cultural insight, technical expertise, practical experience, and place-based expertise.

For example:

  • Practitioners – professionals from NGOs or government agencies implementing National Adaptation Plans (NAPs); private sector leaders in sustainable agriculture, water security, or green infrastructure.

  • Local and indigenous – community leaders or elders with expertise in traditional resource management, coastal resilience, and nature-based solutions; representatives from indigenous organisations active in climate advocacy. 

  • Cross-cutting profiles – community leaders or elders with expertise in traditional resource management, coastal resilience, and nature-based solutions; representatives from indigenous organisations active in climate advocacy.

Turkey Scholarships is a government-funded higher education scholarship programme provided by the Republic of Turkey for international students. The primary purpose of Turkey Scholarships is to provide equal opportunities to successful students around the world in order to receive them. More than just a financial aid package, this government-funded initiative is designed to foster a global network of “future leaders” who are committed to strengthening international cooperation and building mutual understanding between societies.

For many years, Turkey has been providing scholarship opportunities to international students and researchers from all over the world. Higher education scholarship programmes provided by Turkey were branded as “Turkey Scholarships” in 2012, and studies were started to be carried out under the coordination of the Presidency for Turks Abroad and Related Communities (YTB). Turkey Scholarships are carried out in cooperation with all relevant institutions, such as YÖK, Yurt – Kur, the General Directorate of Migration Management and the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

Turkey has rapidly transformed into a global hub for higher education, hosting over 150,000 international students from 165 different countries. The 2026 scholarship cycle continues this momentum by offering access to nearly all academic disciplines—ranging from advanced engineering and basic sciences to social sciences, humanities, and arts. The programme is unique because it integrates university placement directly into the scholarship application, allowing candidates to bypass the traditional, separate university admission processes. This streamlined approach ensures that high-achieving students are matched with departments and institutions that best fit their academic profiles and career ambitions.

With over 125,000 applications received annually, the competition for the roughly 5,000 seats awarded each year is intense. This competitive nature underscores the global recognition of Turkish degrees, which are increasingly valued for their international standards and compatibility with European higher education systems (Bologna Process). Whether it is a researcher conducting postdoctoral work or an undergraduate student starting their journey, the programme offers a transformative experience at the crossroads of Europe and Asia.

Summer Student Fellowship Program 2026 in Germany

Each summer between July and September, the HZDR offers the possibility to join our research groups and participate in their activities.

This offer applies to international senior university students of natural sciences, life sciences, medicine, technology and related areas.

Their summer student programme is addressed to senior university students of natural sciences, life sciences, medicine, technology and related areas who want to join our research teams in Dresden some time between July and September. Research at the HZDR has many facets, providing students with the unique opportunity of working in natural sciences, life sciences, medicine or technology while focusing on fundamental or application-driven research and development. Summer students will be an integral part of research groups. The Gruber Foundation Fellowship in Astrophysics 2026

The International Astronomical Union (IAU) is pleased to invite applications for the 2026 Gruber Foundation Fellowship in Astrophysics, a prestigious programme supported by The Gruber Foundation (GF). This fellowship is designed to support early-career astronomers and promote the advancement of cosmology and other branches of astronomy. They encourage all IAU members to inform eligible colleagues about this excellent opportunity.

The Fellowship will be awarded to highly promising early-career astronomers working in any field of astrophysics, including theoretical, observational, or experimental research. The Gruber Foundation provides funding of US$ 75,000 per year, typically divided among three recipients as a research grant to cover travel and research expenses in support of a postdoctoral appointment at a host institution. The postdoctoral appointment for the 2026 Gruber Foundation Fellowship in Astrophysics must begin no later than 1 October 2026. The fellowship may be combined with other awards.

The IAU is the international astronomical organization that brings together about 13,000 distinguished astronomers from around the world. Its mission is to promote and safeguard the science of astronomy in all its aspects, including research, communication, education, and development, through international cooperation. The IAU also serves as the internationally recognized authority for assigning designations to celestial bodies and their surface features. Founded in 1919, the IAU is the world’s largest professional body for astronomers.

The Gruber Foundation (TGF) has created the GF Fellowship Programme with the aim of promoting the science of cosmology and other branches of astronomy. The IAU Executive Committee recognizes this contribution to our science and shares the desire of TGF to give this distinction the highest possible scientific standing and international character. Accordingly, at its 73rd meeting in Manchester, UK, in August 2000, the IAU EC decided to collaborate with TGF in the nomination of candidates and the selection of recipients, effective as of 1 January 2001.

TGF provides an amount of $75,000 (US) for Fellowships every year. The awarded Fellowship amount is intended as a research grant covering research-related expenses, supplementing a postdoctoral appointment at a host institution starting no later than October 1st of the award year. The Fellowship will be awarded to extremely promising, early-career astronomers, working in any field of astronomy, either theoretical, observational, or experimental. The total grant will be divided typically between three independent awardees. The Fellowship may be held in conjunction with any other fellowship or award.

 

 Benefits

  • The total grant (US$ 75,000) will be divided typically between three independent awardees (US$ 25,000 each).

  • The grant will be paid directly to the recipient(s).

  •  The proposed budget should cover only the period for which the applicant has confirmed employment at the time of application.

  •  The fellowship cannot be used to extend an existing postdoctoral position beyond the confirmed period of employment.

  •  A small portion of the grant (up to 10%) may be used to supplement salary if the host institution fully covers all applicable salary-related costs (pensions, health insurance, etc.), subject to approval by the IAU General Secretary. A detailed justification must be provided.

The grant may be used for research-related purposes such as:

  •  Travel for research visits, observing runs, or conferences

  •  Equipment or software

  •  Open-access publication costs

Each budget item should be clearly described and justified.

 Eligibility

Eligible applicants are early-career astronomers within 3 years of receiving their PhD at the time of application, meeting one of the following conditions:

  • Final-year PhD students with a confirmed contract offer for a postdoctoral position. The PhD must be successfully defended by the fellowship start date (no later than October 1 of the award year).

  • Early-career astronomers in a postdoctoral appointment with at least one year of confirmed employment remaining from the fellowship start date.

  • Astronomers transitioning to a new postdoctoral position with a contract offer starting no later than 1 October of the award year.

The 3-year eligibility period is measured from the PhD defense date to the fellowship start date. Officially documented career breaks (e.g., parental leave, medical leave, or mandatory military service) do not count toward this limit. There are no limitations on nationality, but, while the scientific quality of the application is the prime criterion, preference will be given to applicants from countries in difficult economic conditions.

  • Host Institution Requirement: The host institute for the postdoctoral appointment must be a center of excellence in the applicant’s field of research and located in a country different either from that where the PhD was awarded or from where the current post-doc contract is being completed (in the case of a second or subsequent post-doc). While the IAU Secretariat will administer the grant, the host institute should agree to offer basic salary and research facilities, since the GF grant is intended to cover primarily travel and research expenses. Upon agreement between the host institute and the IAU General Secretary, a small part of the grant (up to 10%) may be used to complement the basic salary, on the condition that the host institute covers the salary overheads (social benefits, salary and income taxes, personal insurance, etc.) and all other similar legal obligations valid in the country where the host institute is located. A detailed justification must be provided. The grant will be handed out directly to the selected applicant(s).

Application Process

Applications for the TGF Fellowship open on 1 December each year. Applications can be submitted via the dedicated online application form made available on 1 December every year.

Applications should contain:

  • The applicant’s full name, address, email address, and current institute address.

  • The applicant’s curriculum vitae and list of publications.

  • The applicant’s research project to be carried out at the desired host institute (up to 5 pages + references, text in Times 12 or equivalent font).

  • The starting date and period during which the fellowship grant will be used by the awardee at the host institute; in principle, this period should be 1 year, up to 2 years at most.

  • A draft budget for the use of the grant by the awardee at the host institute (e.g., travel for research visits, observing runs, and conferences; equipment; software; or open-access publication costs, etc.).

  • A letter of appointment to a postdoc position from the director of the desired host institute, with particular attention to salary issues (salary amount, overheads, etc., to be covered by the host institute).

  • Names and contact information for two reference persons.

Note: Two reference letters from people who have worked or collaborated with the applicant at some stage of the applicant’s career must be uploaded directly by the reference persons themselves, keeping in mind the application deadline. After submission of an application, the applicant will receive a link to distribute to these reference persons. After submission of these two letters, they will automatically be linked to the applicant’s submitted application and will be considered complete.

Deadline

The complete applications for the TGF Fellowship should be uploaded no later than 1 March at 11:59 PM Paris Time (UTC+1).

Applications received after that date or incomplete applications will not be considered. In order to give your references time to complete their letters of reference, please be sure to submit the application well before the deadline.

Queries should be addressed to iau-general.secretary@iap.fr.

Applicants will be notified of the outcome of the selection before the end of May.

The name of the selected TGF Fellow and that of the host institute will be announced on the IAU website.

  • Business strategy & leadership

  • Financial accounting & budgeting

  • Marketing & customer engagement

  • Operations & team management

  • Innovation and digital transformation

These courses are curated to support women in gaining practical skills that can be applied immediately to real business challenges.

Benefits of Participation

  • 100% free access to business training resources

  • Learn at your own pace (self-paced online learning)

  • Earn professional certificates upon course completion

  • Develop skills to grow and manage your business more effectively

  • Network and learn alongside a global community of women leaders

  • Boost your business credibility with recognised certificates

How to Apply / Get Started

  1. Visit the 10,000 Women programme page on Coursera

  2. Browse the available courses and select the ones most relevant to your needs.

  3. Click Enrol to begin; no payment is required.

Training is flexible and self-paced, so you can balance studies with your work and other commitments.

Small and growing businesses, especially those led by women, often lack access to formal business training and capacity development. The Goldman Sachs 10,000 Women programme helps bridge that gap by offering practical, globally relevant courses that build confidence, strategic insight, and operational effectiveness, all at no cost.

This training is an excellent opportunity to develop:

  • Business planning skills

  • Financial management confidence

  • Marketing and digital engagement knowledge

  • Leadership and decision-making capabilities

which are essential for sustainable business growth.
West Africa Civil Society Institute (WACSI) Research Fellowship 2026

Applications are open for the WACSI Research Fellowship 2026. The West Africa Civil Society Institute (WACSI) invites young researchers from Ghana, Guinea Conakry, Liberia, Nigeria and Senegal to apply for its 2026 Research Fellowship, running from May to October 2026. This six‑month programme strengthens the research, analytical and documentation skills of emerging scholars while contributing to WACSI’s mission of enhancing civil society in West Africa.

This year’s fellowship prioritises indigenous knowledge in biodiversity and conservation, encouraging research that highlights community practices, CSO contributions and replicable models that advance development across the region.

Benefits

Active participation in this fellowship offers fellows with a keen interest in biodiversity and conservation access to a wide range of benefits to help successfully conduct their research or engage in a knowledge documentation project. Some specific benefits include but are not limited to the following:


Opportunity to publish in a well-read and globally acclaimed platform dedicated to research on civil society in West Africa.

Production of at least one publishable research report or policy document on biodiversity and conservation. This can be either an issue paper, an Op-Ed and/or blog articles.

Fellows will be assigned to mentors who will guide them throughout the fellowship.

The research findings and the fellows’ experience will be used to promote good practices in West Africa and beyond.

Research fellows will receive monthly stipends and a research allowance from the institute.

Fellows are provided with a wealth of opportunities, skills and knowledge to better plan for or advance their future careers.

Candidates would develop at least one WACSeries publication that will be published on the institute’s website.

Free access to WACSI’s publications.

Eligibility

  • The fellowship is open to young researchers from the following countries:

    • Ghana

    • Guinea-Conakry

    • Liberia

    • Nigeria

    • Senegal

  • Current staff of organisations that are part of the capacity-strengthening programme for organisations in the biodiversity sector are encouraged to apply

  • Those interested in honing their research skills and who may not necessarily be among the organisations in the above-mentioned programme are also encouraged to apply.

  • Contributors must show a strong correlation between their intended topic of interest and its relevance and/or applicability within the biodiversity and conservation sector.

  • Applicants must demonstrate strong analytical skills and the ability to perform independent research.

  • Applicants must be able to communicate fluently either in English and/or French.

Application

Interested candidates should submit the following:

  • A motivation letter stating their duration and theme of interest.

  • A curriculum vitae.

  • A synopsis of not more than one page on the specific theme of interest to the potential fellow. This should articulate how the candidate intends to complete the fellowship and meet up with all deliverables within the fellowship duration.

  • Sample of an authored article or paper. Provide evidence of any published works if available.

Applications should be submitted via email to Mabel Shu (mlum@wacsi.org) and cc Jimm Chick Fomunjong (jcfomunjong@wacsi.org) and Nancy Kankam Kusi  (nkusi@wacsi.org).

UniPods AI Solutions for African Program 2026 (Fully Funded 12-Month AI Innovation Journey)
UniPods AI Solutions for African Program

African students, graduates, and early-stage innovators with an interest in artificial intelligence can now apply for the UniPods AI Solutions for African Program 2026, a fully funded 12-month AI training and innovation initiative designed to build next-generation tech leaders across the continent.

The programme integrates advanced AI education with practical innovation development, leveraging the Global Consumer Intelligence (GCI) Course offered by the University of Tokyo. The GCI course provides foundational and applied knowledge in data science, AI systems, and real-world digital problem-solving.

The initiative aims to strengthen Africa’s AI ecosystem by equipping participants with the technical skills and entrepreneurial capacity to develop scalable, AI-driven solutions tailored to both local and global markets.

What the Program Offers

Selected participants will undertake a structured 12-month learning and innovation journey that combines rigorous academic exposure with practical solution-building.

Advanced AI and Data Science Training
Participants will receive technical training through the GCI curriculum, covering:

  • Data analytics fundamentals

  • Machine learning models

  • AI-driven consumer intelligence

  • Applied computational techniques

Innovation and Commercial Development Support
Beyond technical skills, the programme emphasizes translating ideas into viable products by focusing on:

  • Identifying real market problems within African economies

  • Developing working prototypes

  • Achieving product-market fit

  • Designing commercialization and scaling strategies

International Academic Collaboration
Through collaboration with the University of Tokyo, participants benefit from globally recognized research methodologies, structured innovation frameworks, and exposure to international best practices in AI application.

Fully Funded Participation
The programme covers core training costs, ensuring that selected participants can access world-class AI education and mentorship without financial barriers.

Who Should Apply?

The programme is ideal for:

  • African university students and recent graduates

  • Early-stage tech innovators and startup founders

  • Data and technology enthusiasts seeking structured AI training

  • Individuals passionate about solving African challenges using AI

Applicants are expected to demonstrate strong interest in artificial intelligence, innovation, and technology-driven problem-solving.

About the Global Consumer Intelligence (GCI) Course

The GCI course focuses on applying AI and data science techniques to understand consumer behavior and market systems. It blends technical depth with practical business application, making it particularly suitable for innovators interested in deploying AI beyond theory into scalable ventures.

Brief Summary

The UniPods AI Solutions for African Program 2026 is a fully funded 12-month AI innovation initiative offering advanced training through the University of Tokyo’s Global Consumer Intelligence course. The programme combines technical AI education with practical product development support to strengthen Africa’s digital ecosystem. It targets students, graduates, and early-stage innovators seeking to build scalable AI-driven solutions.

Artificial intelligence is transforming industries globally, yet Africa faces a significant AI skills gap. By training innovators in applied AI and commercialization strategies, the programme supports the creation of tech-driven startups, enhances digital competitiveness, and enables MSMEs to leverage AI for efficiency, customer insights, and market expansion. Strengthening local AI talent directly contributes to job creation, productivity growth, and digital trade participation.

The Africa–Europe Digital Innovation Bridge (AEDIB) Joint Innovation Facility (JIF) 2026 is now accepting applications from Africa–Europe consortia developing commercially viable, digitally enabled solutions with strong climate-positive impact.

The facility provides up to €200,000 in non-dilutive funding to support scaling, market expansion, and cross-continental collaboration.

  • Product refinement and technical optimisation

  • Pilot expansion and validation

  • Market testing and commercial deployment

  • Partnership strengthening and ecosystem integration

Applications must be submitted by Africa–Europe consortia with complementary expertise and clearly defined governance structures.

Eligible partners may include:

  • Startups and SMEs

  • Technology providers

  • Research and innovation institutions

  • Ecosystem enablers or industry partners

Applicants must provide clear implementation plans, defined roles, measurable outcomes, and impact indicators.

Selection Criteria

Applications will be assessed based on:

  • Technological innovation

  • Climate relevance and measurable environmental impact

  • Market scalability and commercial potential

  • Strength of the Africa–Europe partnership

  • Feasibility and clarity of implementation plan
M4D Open Innovation Challenge 2026 — Up to USD 250,000 in Awards

The M4D Open Innovation Challenge 2026 is now accepting applications from innovators building scalable, technology-driven solutions to tackle pressing global development challenges. Selected applicants can receive up to USD 250,000 in awards, alongside access to strategic support designed to accelerate large-scale impact.

This challenge targets high-potential innovations that move beyond ideas and prototypes into commercially viable, scalable solutions capable of delivering measurable social or environmental outcomes.

Funding and Acceleration Support

  • Product refinement and technical enhancement

Beyond funding, participants may gain access to:

Sustainable Energy Accelerator (SEA) Cohort 3 for African Clean Energy Startups

Applications are now open for Sustainable Energy Accelerator (SEA) Cohort 3, a structured acceleration programme supporting startups building scalable solutions in renewable energy and climate innovation.

As Africa confronts widening electricity access gaps, rising energy costs, and increasing climate pressures, early-stage and growth-stage ventures are playing a critical role in delivering decentralised, affordable, and sustainable energy systems. SEA Cohort 3 is designed to strengthen these ventures by equipping founders with the tools, networks, and strategic support needed to scale sustainably.


Programme Structure and Support

SEA Cohort 3 offers a structured acceleration pathway tailored to clean energy startups operating in areas such as solar, mini-grids, energy storage, energy efficiency, clean cooking, and other sustainable power technologies.

1. Structured Business Acceleration

Participants receive hands-on advisory support to strengthen their core business foundations, including:

  • Business model validation and refinement

  • Revenue model optimisation and pricing strategies

  • Customer acquisition and retention planning

  • Financial forecasting and cost structure optimisation

  • Operational efficiency improvement

The goal is to ensure participating startups are commercially viable and strategically positioned for sustainable growth.

2. Investment Readiness and Capital Positioning

Access to capital remains one of the biggest barriers for climate-tech startups in Africa. SEA helps founders prepare for investor engagement through:

  • Pitch deck development and storytelling refinement

  • Financial documentation and due diligence preparation

  • Impact metrics articulation and ESG positioning

  • Growth strategy mapping and expansion modelling

  • Investor engagement and fundraising readiness support

This component strengthens founders’ ability to attract both local and international climate-focused investors.

3. Technical and Regulatory Advisory

Clean energy startups often operate within complex regulatory and infrastructure environments. SEA provides guidance on:

  • Product-market fit validation

  • Regulatory compliance and licensing considerations

  • Technology optimisation and performance improvements

  • Market entry and cross-border expansion strategies

4. Ecosystem Access and Visibility

Selected ventures benefit from increased exposure within the climate-tech ecosystem, including:

  • Connections to energy sector stakeholders

  • Visibility among climate and impact investors

  • Access to potential corporate and development partners

  • Networking within Africa’s clean energy and sustainability community


    Who Should Apply

SEA Cohort 3 is ideal for:

  • Renewable energy startups

  • Climate-tech innovators

  • Clean energy product and service providers

  • Founders working on distributed power, storage, mini-grids, or clean cooking

  • Early-stage and growth-stage ventures seeking structured acceleration

Applicants should demonstrate:

  • A clearly defined energy challenge

  • A viable and scalable solution

  • Evidence of traction (pilots, customers, partnerships, or revenue)

  • Commitment to scaling impact within African markets

    Why This Matters for Africa’s Energy Ecosystem

Millions across Africa still lack reliable access to electricity. Private-sector innovation is central to bridging this gap while supporting climate mitigation and adaptation goals.

By strengthening clean energy entrepreneurs, SEA contributes to:

  • Expanding energy access in underserved communities

  • Accelerating renewable energy adoption

  • Driving green job creation

  • Supporting climate resilience and decarbonisation

  • Improving long-term energy affordability

Programmes like SEA play a catalytic role in building Africa’s renewable energy ecosystem while preparing startups for sustainable growth and investment readiness.

Key Dates

  • Application Deadline: 03/05/2026

  • Programme Start Date: March 2026

One Acre Fund Future Leaders Program (12-Month Training )
Kenya, Rwanda, Uganda, Tanzania, Zambia, Ethiopia, Burundi, Malawi

The Future Leaders Program is a 12-month, full-time leadership development initiative designed for high-potential professionals who are passionate about driving measurable social impact.

The programme provides participants with hands-on experience working on some of One Acre Fund’s most impactful operational and strategic initiatives, combined with structured mentorship and professional development. It offers an accelerated pathway for professionals seeking to align their career growth with meaningful impact in agriculture and rural development across Africa. 

The Future Leaders Program immerses participants in real-time problem-solving within large-scale agricultural and social enterprise operations. Fellows work closely with cross-functional teams and senior leaders, contributing to initiatives that improve farmer livelihoods, strengthen systems, and scale impact.

Participants are expected to translate strategic priorities into actionable project plans, build systems and partnerships from the ground up, and use data-driven insights to inform decision-making.

 

Eligibility Requirements

Applicants must:

  • Have the legal right to work in one of One Acre Fund’s countries of operation: Kenya, Rwanda, Uganda, Tanzania, Zambia, Ethiopia, Burundi, or Malawi. The organisation does not sponsor work permits.

  • Hold a bachelor’s degree or higher qualification.

  • Possess 2–4 years of relevant professional work experience.

  • Demonstrate working proficiency in English.

  • Fluency in one of the programme country languages, such as Swahili, Kinyarwanda, Kirundi, Amharic, Chichewa, or French, is desirable.

Desired Competencies

Successful candidates should demonstrate:

  • Strong project management and stakeholder engagement skills.

  • The ability to convert strategy memos into structured and executable project plans.

  • Experience in designing systems, processes, and partnerships from inception.

  • Strong analytical skills, including the ability to use data and sound business judgement to guide decisions.

  • While prior experience in social enterprises, agriculture, or rural development is advantageous, it is not mandatory.

Program Benefits

Participants in the Future Leaders Program receive:

  1. Direct mentorship from senior leadership within One Acre Fund.

  2. Hands-on experience working on high-priority strategic and operational initiatives.

  3. Leadership development support and professional training.

  4. Exposure to large-scale agricultural and social enterprise programmes.

  5. Opportunities to build strong networks within the development and impact sector.

  6. A potential pathway to permanent employment opportunities within One Acre Fund upon successful completion of the programme.

Important Note

One Acre Fund cannot support work permit applications. All candidates must already have the legal right to work in one of the programme countries at the time of application.
STREAMING Project Open Call for Digital Training in Sustainable Food Trade

The STREAMING Project (Sustainable Trade Regimes with Europe and Africa through Mapping Innovation, New Technology and Growth Mindset) has launched an Open Call for Knowledge Transfer, inviting organisations across Africa and Europe to apply for funding to develop practical digital training programmes that strengthen sustainable and inclusive food trade systems. The initiative aims to bridge the gap between research and real-world application by supporting interactive, practice-orientated online courses tailored for farmers, food processors, start-ups, and entrepreneurs.

Overview of the Funding Opportunity

  • Grant Amount: Up to €25,000 per project (approximately ₦13 million+)

  • Total Budget: €275,000 to support up to 11 projects

  • Project Duration: Typically 8–12 months

  • Application Deadline: 2 March 2026 (23:59 CET)

  • Funded By: European Union under the Horizon Europe programme

What the Call Supports

The call focuses on developing digital training modules, tools, and courses that translate research and innovation into practical skills for stakeholders within food systems. Proposed solutions should be:

  • Accessible and user-friendly

  • Interactive and practice-oriented

  • Relevant to real-world agricultural and food value chain challenges

Priority Themes

Proposals should address innovations and training related to:

  • Traceability in food supply chains

  • Sustainable farming systems

  • Food safety and quality standards

  • Nutrition and micronutrient deficiencies

  • Food loss and waste reduction

  • Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)

  • Scaling innovations across food value chains

Who Can Apply

Eligible applicants include legally registered entities such as:

  • Universities and research institutions

  • NGOs and civil society organisations

  • Innovation hubs, incubators, and industry partners

  • Business schools and vocational institutions

  • Consortia of up to three entities from Africa and/or Europe

At least 60% of the total funding is reserved for African-based organisations, and women-led organisations or projects supporting women farmers and entrepreneurs are strongly encouraged to apply. The call is open globally and is not restricted to existing STREAMING partner countries

How to Apply

Application materials, including the Open Call text, proposal template, Declaration of Honour, consortium agreement, and guidance documents, are available on the official STREAMING webpage.

Applicants are strongly advised to review all documentation carefully before preparing submissions.

Plus Incubation Hub has officially launched The Plus Factor Grant Program, a high-impact entrepreneurship support initiative designed to identify, nurture, and fund promising founders across Africa. The programme provides non-dilutive grant funding, structured mentorship, innovation support, and market access to help entrepreneurs build scalable, market-relevant businesses.

This initiative targets founders who have strong ideas or early traction but face barriers such as limited access to capital, weak market linkages, and insufficient innovation support.

According to Eunice Alabi, programme lead, the vision goes beyond capital injection:

“The Plus Factor Grant Program is not just about funding; it is about backing entrepreneurs with the clarity, readiness, and support required to build sustainable businesses. We are intentionally combining finance, innovation, and market access to create real, measurable impact.”

About the Program

The Plus Factor Grant Program is a Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) initiative of Plus Incubation Hub, implemented by the School of Grant Success.

The programme runs over an 8-week implementation period, featuring a transparent, competitive, multi-stage selection process designed to ensure fairness, accountability, and quality evaluation.

The initiative is sector-agnostic, welcoming entrepreneurs across various industries.

Who Should Apply?

The programme is open to:

  • Idea-stage entrepreneurs with innovative, market-relevant business ideas

  • Early-stage startups with pilots, early customers, or initial traction

  • Growth-stage businesses ready to scale, expand markets, or strengthen operations

Eligibility Criteria

To qualify, applicants must:

  • Be a founder or co-founder of a business or startup

  • Have an operational business or a validated business idea

  • Demonstrate commitment to growth and scalability

  • Be available to fully participate in all program activities

  • Submit a complete application before the deadline

Applications that do not meet these requirements will be disqualified at the initial screening stage.

Selection Process & Evaluation Criteria

The programme follows a structured three-stage selection process:

Stage 1: Eligibility & Alignment Screening

Applicants are assessed on:

  • Application completeness

  • Entrepreneurial motivation and vision

  • Business relevance and feasibility

Stage 2: Business, Market & Innovation Assessment

Evaluation focuses on:

  • Market opportunity and demand

  • Innovation and differentiation

  • Business model sustainability

  • Growth and scalability potential

  • Founder capacity and leadership

Stage 3: Final Pitch & Evaluation

Finalists will pitch before an expert panel and will be assessed on:

  • Market and financial readiness

  • Effective use of grant funding

  • Innovation impact

  • Pitch clarity and execution

  • Inclusion, youth, gender, and social impact considerations

What Selected Beneficiaries Will Receive

Successful entrepreneurs will benefit from:

Non-Dilutive Grant Funding

  • 1st Place: ₦1,000,000

  • 2nd Place: ₦500,000

  • 3rd Place: ₦250,000

  • 4th Place: ₦150,000

  • 5th Place: ₦100,000

Additional Support

  • Business mentorship and pitch support

  • Market access and visibility opportunities

  • Innovation and growth readiness support

  • Investment readiness training

Program Timeline

  • Applications Open: Now

  • Application Deadline: 15 March 2026

  • Program Duration: 8 weeks of selection, evaluation, and training

The Plus Factor Grant Program is positioned as more than a funding opportunity. It is a structured pathway for African founders ready to refine their ideas, strengthen their business models, and scale with clarity, support, and measurable impact.

Innovation Grants under the Scholars Entrepreneurship Fund (SEF 2.0)

The Mastercard Foundation Scholars Program at the University of Rwanda is inviting applications for Innovation Grants under the Scholars Entrepreneurship Fund (SEF 2.0) – Phase Two (2025–2026).

SEF 2.0 is an initiative of the Mastercard Foundation implemented through the Mastercard Foundation Scholars Program at the University of Rwanda, in partnership with BAG Technologies. The Fund is designed to nurture and scale innovative, entrepreneurial, and impactful student-led solutions that respond to pressing community and national development challenges.

Shortlisted teams will participate in the HATANA Innovation Bootcamp (23–28 March 2026 in Kigali), where they will receive intensive entrepreneurship training, mentorship, and pitch development support and compete for grant funding of up to USD 5,000 to support prototype development and venture implementation.

To be eligible, applicants must:

  • Be undergraduate students at the University of Rwanda

  • Apply as a team (individual applications are not accepted)

  • Form teams of 3–5 members

  • Include at least one Mastercard Foundation Scholar enrolled at the University of Rwanda

  • Ensure at least 70% of team members are female

  • Demonstrate inclusivity where possible (e.g., youth with disabilities, refugees, international students)

Teams may apply with or without a legally registered business idea.

Project Requirements

Proposed ventures must:

  • Address at least one Sustainable Development Goal (SDG)

  • Demonstrate alignment with Rwanda’s NST2 and/or Vision 2050 priorities

  • Show innovation, originality, and clear potential for scalability

  • Demonstrate feasibility and prototype development potential

  • Be prepared to fully participate in the HATANA Innovation Bootcamp

Grant Funding:

Winning teams may receive innovation grants of up to USD 5,000 to support prototype development, implementation, and scaling of their ventures.
Dsm-Firmenich Progress Foundation Scholarship 2026 – One Young World Summit

The dsm-firmenich Progress Foundation is offering five outstanding young leaders the opportunity to participate in the One Young World Summit 2026, taking place in Cape Town, South Africa, from 3 to 6 November 2026.

The scholarship supports young entrepreneurs who are creating measurable impact in their communities, particularly in areas aligned with the foundation’s three priority focus areas: fighting malnutrition, strengthening community resilience, and protecting and restoring ecosystems.

Selected scholars will join the global One Young World community and participate in high-level discussions, leadership sessions, and networking engagements alongside the dsm-firmenich delegation.

Eligibility Criteria:

Candidates must:

  • Be aged 18–30 (applicants over 30 may be considered if they demonstrate exceptional impact)

  • Reside in Africa, Asia-Pacific, or Latin America

  • Have 3–5 years of business experience, preferably running their own enterprise

  • Be making measurable impact in one of the three focus areas

Selected scholars will receive:

  • Full access to the One Young World Summit 2026

  • Hotel accommodation in Cape Town (31 October – 8 November 2026)

  • Return economy flights to and from Cape Town

  • Breakfast at the hotel and lunch and dinner during core Summit days

  • Ground transportation between accommodation and Summit venue

  • Pre- and post-programme engagement in Cape Town

  • Opportunity to join the dsm-firmenich delegation during the Summit

EEP Africa Supports early-stage, commercially viable clean energy solutions in Southern and Eastern Africa to boost climate resilience and energy access.

Who can apply?

Private companies, start-ups and social enterprises from any country in the world with early-stage projects in one or more of the EEP Africa target countries (Botswana, Burundi, Comoros, Eswatini, Kenya, Lesotho, Madagascar, Malawi, Mauritius, Mozambique, Namibia, Rwanda, South Africa, Tanzania, Uganda, Zambia and Zimbabwe) are welcome to apply.

All applicants need to have a strong local presence in the country of application. The applicant should be based in the country, be locally led, or be an international company with strong local partnerships.

All applicants must be registered legal entities for at least six (6) months prior to the Expression of Interest opening date.

EEP Africa does not fund NGOs, charities, research institutions, or government institutions (e.g., government ministries, UN agencies, and national/provincial/district departments). However, these types of organisations can serve as project partners.

All applicants must meet the eligibility criteria defined in the CfP2026 Application Guidelines.

What type of projects is EEP Africa looking for?

EEP Africa supports early-stage, locally driven innovative clean energy projects that are in active development. All applications need to demonstrate a clear need and additionality for EEP Africa financing.

Funding is provided to feasibility studies, pilot, replication or scale-up projects.

All project activities need to have a clear path to profitability with a reasonable expectation of further investment and sustainable commercialisation.

EEP Africa is technology agnostic as long as solutions are based on renewable energy sources (solar, wind, biomass, geothermal, hydro, and other renewable energy sources) or energy efficiency.

For the CfP2026, EEP Africa limits applications from Kenya, Botswana, Mauritius, Namibia and South Africa to projects that focus on e-waste management, specifically e-waste generated from the clean energy sector. See Annex II of the CfP2026 Guidelines for more details.

The projects should fall under at least one of the following groups of technologies:

  • Residential electricity access

  • Productive uses of energy (commercial and/or institutional)

  • Mini grids (AC or DC)

  • Power generation

  • Clean cooking (residential and/or institutional, stoves and/or fuels)

  • Mobility (electric and/or fuels)

  • Energy efficiency & storage

What kind of financing is available?

Applicants can apply for grants and repayable grants between EUR 200,000 and 500,000. The minimum required co-financing is 30-50% of the total project budget, depending on the project phase and company maturity. Furthermore, the Investment Committee may determine additional repayability of funds.

How to apply?

The application process is a two-stage process: Expression of Interest (EoI) and a detailed Full Proposal. After the EoI has been submitted, EEP Africa will invite longlisted applicants for pitching sessions. Based on the pitch interviews, applicants are shortlisted to proceed to the full proposal stage. For an indicative timeline, please scroll down.

Please carefully read the CfP2026 Application Guidelines before beginning the application process. The guidelines and other documents listed below provide detailed information on eligibility and evaluation criteria.

  • Application Guidelines: Call for Proposals 2026

  • NDF Environmental & Social Policy (PDF)

  • NDF Policy on Anticorruption and Integrity (PDF)

  • Project Implementation Manual 2026

  • FAQ – Frequently Asked Questions (PDF)

How to submit your application?

IMPORTANT! Please note that all proposals must be submitted through the EEP Africa online application platform. Proposals that are sent through e-mail or social media channels will not be considered.

Please register for the EEP Africa online application platform. During the application process, you can save your edits and continue to work on your application by signing in with the same account. Remember to save your edits each time you work on it. Once you have fully completed your application (expression of interest), submit it to EEP Africa by pressing ‘Submit’. After submission, changes to the Expression of Interest are no longer possible.
HealthX Incubation Program by Evon Labs

Evon Labs, a subsidiary of Evon Medics LLC, is now accepting applications for HealthX, a 12-week incubation programme designed for idea-stage and early-stage founders building innovative healthcare solutions.

Why it matters


HealthX equips founders with the tools, mentorship, and structured support needed to turn healthcare ideas into viable solutions. By participating, founders gain guidance on product development, regulatory compliance, and venture fundamentals, increasing their chances of success in the complex healthcare ecosystem.

Eligibility Criteria


Applicants should:

  • Be founders or co-founders of an early-stage healthcare startup or have a healthcare-focused idea

  • Be committed to dedicating time and effort to the 12-week programme.

  • Demonstrate passion for improving healthcare outcomes through innovation

  • Be open to mentorship and collaboration with other founders

Benefits


Participants in HealthX will receive:

  • Mentorship from experienced healthcare and startup professionals

  • Support on product development, regulatory requirements, and business foundations

  • Access to a network of healthcare innovators and investors
